The post also advocates creating detailed imaginary user personas to ground design decisions in empathy rather than programmer assumptions. Together, these techniques form a six-step process for designing software that feels natural and guides users toward their goals.
key_points: Feature-driven design leads to programs that have all the right capabilities but don't flow — users can't discover how to accomplish their goals
Activity Based Planning reframes design around what users are trying to do, which naturally suggests features and priorities that a feature list wouldn't reveal
Observing real Excel users revealed that list-keeping was far more common than financial modeling, leading to major features like AutoFilter and multi-user editing
Lotus Improv failed because it optimized for complex multidimensional models while making the most common activity — keeping lists — nearly impossible
Creating detailed imaginary user personas prevents programmers from overestimating user sophistication and grounds design decisions in empathy
money_quotes: People sit down and they don't know what it does, and they don't know how to accomplish what they want.
The key insight is to figure out the activity that the user is doing, and focus on making it easy to accomplish that activity.
Instead of thinking about your program like a programmer, you're thinking about it like the user.
We only had to watch about five customers using the product before we realized that an enormous number of people just use Excel to keep lists.

Improv is now a footnote in history. Search for it on the web, and the only links you'll find are from very over-organized storeroom managers who have, for some reason, made a web site with an inventory of all the stuff they have collecting dust.
One of the best ways to decide which features get done, and which features get left out, is to evaluate which features support the most important user activities.
Thinking about a 'real' person gives you the empathy you need to make a feature that serves that person's need.
Good UI sells software, but it also makes people happy, because people are happy when they accomplish the task they wanted to accomplish.
posts.key_insight: Great software design starts not with a list of features but with understanding the specific activities users are trying to accomplish, then building the interface to guide them through those activities naturally.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky comments on three unrelated topics: the failure of Raging Search (an AltaVista spinoff) to match Google's search quality despite copying its design, Juno's invasive privacy policy that tracks extensive user data for millions of Americans, and a brief personal note about his UI book being completed. He reflects on whether he would have refused to implement Juno's tracking features on principle had he still worked there, but honestly admits his integrity was never actually tested since he had already quit.
key_points: Copying a product's design (as Raging Search did with Google) doesn't replicate the underlying quality that makes it work
Juno's privacy policy represents a troubling erosion of user privacy by combining personal identity with detailed browsing data
Spolsky questions whether he would have taken a principled stand against implementing invasive tracking, but admits he never had to face that test
Even principled refusal by employees rarely changes corporate decisions — management would simply pressure dissenters into compliance
money_quotes: This steady erosion of privacy dismays me.
The Juno database will have your name and address and the web pages you visited, for 3 million Americans who use the service every month.
I would like to think that I would have refused on principle, even if it cost me my job.
Then again, it's hard to say "I would have taken a stand," since I wasn't really there, and I didn't really take a stand.
Lucky for me, I quit way back in November, and so my integrity never stood the test.
Management would just gather us all in a room and scream at us.
posts.key_insight: It's easy to claim you would have taken a principled stand against unethical practices when you never actually had to face the choice.

posts.summary: This is a brief link post where Spolsky promotes two other pieces of content. He teases his 'Strategy Letter I' which categorizes startups into two types — 'Ben and Jerry's' companies versus 'Amazon' companies — and warns that not knowing which type you are leads to strategic mistakes. He also links to a reader suggestion that he finds deceptively appealing but potentially problematic.
key_points: Startups fall into two fundamentally different categories that require different strategies
Misidentifying your startup type leads to compounding strategic errors
Ideas that sound good at first glance can turn out to be minefields upon reflection
money_quotes: If you don't know which one you are, you're going to make lots of mistakes and you won't even know why.
It sounds good at first, but after a minute you realize it might be a minefield.
posts.key_insight: Startups must correctly identify their fundamental strategic type — growth-focused or profitability-focused — because the wrong self-diagnosis leads to invisible but compounding mistakes.

posts.summary: A reader asks Joel about a spreadsheet feature called 'auto motion' from an old DEC PDP/11 program that would let the Enter key follow the direction of your last arrow key press. Joel explains why he opposes this feature for Excel: it creates an invisible mode where the Enter key behaves differently based on a prior action the user may have forgotten. He argues this leads to confusion, data entry errors, and accidental overwrites when users aren't watching the screen.
key_points: Invisible modes—where behavior changes without visible on-screen indication—are a major source of user confusion
Consistency of basic controls like the Enter key matters more than flexibility for power users
Features that seem clever in isolation can cause real data loss when users look away from the screen during data entry
A feature being 'wonderful' for one knowledgeable user doesn't make it good design for a broad user base
money_quotes: It's important that things (especially the enter key) behave in the same way instead of getting into invisible modes.
Nothing on the screen tells you why Enter is going up now, and you may just decide it's a bug and get frustrated.

You'll get especially frustrated if you look away from the screen and type in a bunch of numbers, only to look back and find out that not only are they in the wrong cells, but you have accidentally overwritten something interesting you used to have there.
Ah, you're trying to trick me into recommending a feature that I don't approve of.
Many people will see this behavior and they won't realize why the Enter key suddenly wants to go UP instead of DOWN.
posts.key_insight: Features that introduce invisible modes—where controls behave differently based on forgotten prior actions with no on-screen indication—create confusion and data loss, no matter how clever they seem.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that every company must make a fundamental strategic choice: grow slowly and organically (the 'Ben and Jerry's' model) or pursue rapid growth fueled by massive capital (the 'Amazon' model). The right choice depends on market conditions — if you're entering a market with network effects, no competitors, and lock-in potential, you must go big fast or lose to someone who does. If you're entering an established market, organic growth is more sensible and sustainable. He compares the two models across several dimensions including capital requirements, corporate culture, risk tolerance, and ability to learn from mistakes. The worst strategic error is failing to choose between the two approaches, or choosing one while executing the other. Both models can work, but mixing them leads to incoherent strategy and failure.
key_points: Companies in markets with strong network effects, lock-in, and no existing competition must pursue aggressive growth or risk being permanently locked out by a faster competitor.

Organic growth preserves corporate culture and allows mentoring, while hypergrowth makes it impossible to transmit values to new hires — as Netscape demonstrated growing from 5 to 2,000 employees in a year.
The Ben and Jerry's model offers near-certain modest success with low risk, while the Amazon model offers a tiny chance of massive success against a high probability of total failure.
Money can substitute for time in specific ways (premium office space, high salaries, consultants, concierge services), but only Amazon-model companies can afford to do this.
The worst strategic mistake is choosing one model but executing the other — especially claiming to be an Amazon company while penny-pinching like a Ben and Jerry's company.
money_quotes: No matter what else you do, you absolutely must figure out which camp you're in, and gear everything you do accordingly, or you're going to have a disaster on your hands.
It's not impossible to displace a competitor, if that's what you want to do.
As soon as you try to grow so fast that mentoring and coaching is impossible, you are simply going to stop transmitting those values.
A company that is growing too fast will simply not notice when it makes a big mistake, especially of the spend-too-much-money kind.
As a rule of thumb, you can make a nice place to work, or you can promise people they'll get rich quick. But you have to do one of those, or you won't be able to hire.
You have a tiny chance of becoming a billionaire, and a high chance of just failing.
The worst thing you can do is fail to decide whether you're going to be a Ben and Jerry's company or an Amazon company.
Amazon companies absolutely must substitute cash for time whenever they can.
posts.key_insight: Every company must deliberately choose between organic profitability and capital-fueled hypergrowth based on its market conditions, and then execute consistently within that chosen model — the fatal mistake is mixing the two.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief follow-up post where Joel notes that his strategy letter resonated widely with readers. He points readers to a response written by a former Netscape employee ('Netscapee') that offers an insider perspective on his arguments. The post is essentially a link referral rather than a substantive essay.
key_points: Joel's strategy letter generated significant reader response and discussion
Former Netscape employees ('Netscapees') engaged with his arguments from an insider perspective
The post serves as a curated pointer to community discussion rather than original analysis
money_quotes: My strategy letter seems to have hit a nerve.
posts.key_insight: This micro-post illustrates Joel's practice of engaging with reader responses and directing his audience to relevant insider perspectives that validate or extend his arguments.

posts.summary: This is not a substantive blog post but rather a brief announcement linking to a separate strategy letter about the Chicken and Egg problem in technology platforms. The entire post is a single sentence directing readers to another article. There is no argument or analysis contained within this post itself.
key_points: The post serves as a pointer to a longer strategy letter published elsewhere on the site
The referenced article discusses the Chicken and Egg problem in platform/product strategy
This is characteristic of early blog-era link posts common in the 2000s
posts.key_insight: This is a navigation post with no standalone content to analyze.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that the chicken-and-egg problem — where platforms need software to attract users and users to attract software — is the most important strategic challenge in technology. He traces the history of PC platforms to show that backward compatibility is the key to solving this problem: DOS succeeded because it could run CP/M software, Windows 3.x succeeded because the 80386 let it run DOS programs properly, and Windows 95 obsessively preserved compatibility with older software. He extends this principle beyond platforms to bill presentment startups, showing how PayMyBills.com solved their chicken-and-egg problem by providing a 'backward compatibility mode' of physically scanning paper bills. The conclusion is clear: without a backward-compatibility strategy that dissolves the chicken-and-egg problem, new platforms and services will fail.
key_points: The chicken-and-egg problem is the defining strategic challenge for platforms: no users without software, no software without users, creating a 'Gordian Death Spiral'
DOS won the early PC OS war not through marketing muscle but because Tim Paterson built CP/M compatibility into it — WordStar was ported by changing a single byte
Windows 3.x succeeded only at version 3.0 because the Intel 80386 finally allowed it to run multiple DOS programs respectably in windows, not because Microsoft needed five tries to 'get it right'
Microsoft added specific code to Windows 95 to detect SimCity and work around its memory bug — exemplifying the backward-compatibility obsession that drove adoption

PayMyBills.com solved bill presentment's chicken-and-egg problem by providing a 'backward compatibility mode' — physically scanning paper bills — while Microsoft's TransPoint failed by requiring merchants to integrate first
money_quotes: Nobody is going to buy your platform until there's good software that runs on it, and nobody is going to write software until you have a big installed base.
The only thing a software product guide tells you is that there is no software available for the system. When you see one of these beasts, run fleeing in the opposite direction.
Steve Jobs practically made a career out of not understanding the chicken and egg problem, twice.
WordStar was ported to DOS by changing one single byte in the code. Let that sink in.
DOS was popular because it had software from day one.
On beta versions of Windows 95, SimCity wasn't working in testing. Microsoft tracked down the bug and added specific code to Windows 95 that looks for SimCity.
Their business plan requires 93,000,000 humans to cooperate with their crazy business scheme before it actually works.
Any business plan that calls for making a computer that doesn't run Excel is just not going anywhere.
posts.key_insight: The only reliable way to solve a chicken-and-egg platform problem is to provide backward compatibility with the incumbent, giving your new platform an instant library of existing software or content from day one.

posts.summary: This post compiles reader responses to Spolsky's earlier essay arguing against rewriting code from scratch. Seth Gordon offers tips for reading others' source code, and Spolsky advises that reading code requires deliberately slowing down because code is far denser than natural language. Lou Montulli, a founding Netscape engineer and creator of Lynx, provides a firsthand account of how Netscape's decision to rewrite everything contributed to the company's downfall. Montulli confirms that years of accumulated bug fixes and protocol handling in ugly-looking code represented irreplaceable institutional knowledge. The post reinforces Spolsky's core thesis that rewriting from scratch destroys hard-won lessons embedded in existing code.
key_points: Reading code requires deliberately slowing down because it is far denser than natural language and contains implicit dependencies that must be traced
Many programmers lack the patience to read existing code carefully, which leads them to rewrite from scratch — but reading is still faster than rewriting
Lou Montulli, a Netscape founder, resigned partly because of the decision to rewrite everything, validating Spolsky's argument with a firsthand account
Ugly code that handles edge cases (like 5,000 lines for 60 FTP server types) represents years of accumulated knowledge that cannot be easily replicated
Netscape's rewrite decision was driven by real problems (Java integration, rapid development) but the response of starting over was disproportionate
money_quotes: I think the best way to read somebody else's code is just to SLOW DOWN… it's like deciphering a code, not like reading.
Code is much more dense than English, and contains 'secrets' that need to be deciphered by looking elsewhere.
It takes some skill to learn how to read code slowly and carefully, and many programmers are not patient enough (so they wind up rewriting the code from scratch).
But you have to remember that it's still faster to read than to rewrite!
It had taken 3 years of tuning to get code that could read the 60 different types of FTP servers, those 5000 lines of code may have looked ugly, but at least they worked.
I was unable to supply enough visions of doom to divert the effort.
There was good reason for a large change, but rewriting everything was a bit overboard to say the least.
posts.key_insight: Reading existing code demands patience and deliberate slowness, but it is always faster than rewriting — and ugly, battle-tested code embodies years of hard-won knowledge that a rewrite will inevitably lose.

posts.summary: The post shares an email from Seth Gordon comparing the practice of reading source code to studying the Talmud. Gordon, who had spent time at an Israeli yeshiva before becoming a technical writer, noticed the parallel while deciphering a complex yacc-and-Perl codebase with a co-worker. He offers four Talmud-study tactics applicable to code reading: working in pairs, arguing about interpretations, skipping difficult sections to return later, and reading at both micro and macro levels.
key_points: Pair reading of code, like paired Talmud study, forces you to articulate your understanding and catch misinterpretations
Arguing about what code means leads to deeper understanding — demand explanations rather than accepting assumptions
Non-linear reading is valid: skip confusing sections, understand the surrounding context, then return with better context
Code must be read at two levels simultaneously — line-by-line detail ('inside') and broader structural meaning ('outside') — to avoid both missing the forest and fooling yourself with guesses
money_quotes: It's just like reading Talmud.
Work in pairs, thinking out loud to one another.
If your partner says "this means X", and you either don't understand why or you have another opinion, demand an explanation.
Sometimes, when dealing with a chunk of text, it's easier to figure out the middle after you understand what's on both ends.
If you only read inside, you can miss the forest for the trees; if you only read outside, you can fool yourself by making broad guesses and not verifying them with details.
posts.key_insight: Reading code effectively requires the same disciplined interpretive techniques used in Talmudic study: collaborative argumentation, non-linear navigation, and simultaneous close and broad reading.

posts.summary: This is a brief teaser post promoting Joel's third strategy letter about barriers to switching. Joel argues that understanding barriers of entry is critical when trying to win customers from competitors, and that most people underestimate how significant these barriers are. He warns that failing to address switching costs will result in business failure.
key_points: Understanding barriers of entry is essential when competing for users of rival products
Most people underestimate how difficult it is to get users to switch products
Failure to address switching barriers leads to business failure
money_quotes: When you're trying to get people to switch from a competitor to your product, you need to understand barriers of entry, and you need to understand them a lot better than you think, or people won't switch and you'll be waiting tables.
posts.key_insight: Most companies fatally underestimate the switching costs that prevent users from adopting their product over a competitor's.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that winning customers from an established competitor requires systematically eliminating every barrier to switching, not just the obvious ones. Using Microsoft Excel's conquest of Lotus 123 as the central case study, he shows how each barrier removed roughly doubles potential conversions. The critical non-obvious insight is that the fear of switching *back* is often the biggest barrier to switching *in* — people won't try your product if they feel trapped. Excel's tipping point came not when it could read Lotus files, but when it could write them, letting users participate in the Lotus ecosystem while using Excel. Spolsky argues that making it easy for customers to leave your product is counterintuitively the best way to get them to arrive. Trying to lock in customers before they've even switched to you only locks them out.
key_points: Barriers to switching are cumulative — with 8-9 barriers, virtually no one will overcome all of them, so eliminating even one barrier can double your conversion rate.
The most overlooked barrier isn't the difficulty of switching in, but the fear of how hard it will be to switch back out if the new product doesn't work.
Excel's tipping point came with version 4.0, which could write Lotus 123 files — not just read them — allowing users to participate in the existing ecosystem.
Attempting to create lock-in before you've won the customer is counterproductive; it becomes lock-out instead.
Companies in competitive markets must honestly acknowledge they need people to switch from a dominant competitor rather than pretending the competitor doesn't matter.
money_quotes: The only strategy in getting people to switch to your product is to eliminate barriers.
Eliminating just one barrier will likely double your sales.
That's the barrier to entry. Not how hard it is to switch in: it's how hard it might be to switch out.
Try to lock them in before they become your customers, and you'll just lock them out.
Nobody wants to switch to a product that is going to eliminate their freedom in the future.
The mature approach to strategy is not to try to force things on potential customers.
Most managers only think about strategy one step at a time, like chess players who refuse to think one move ahead.
Shakespeare and Company on Manhattan's Upper West Side did not close because Barnes and Nobles had cheaper prices, it closed because Barnes and Nobles had more human beings in the building.
posts.key_insight: The biggest barrier preventing customers from switching to your product isn't the cost of switching in — it's the fear of how hard it will be to switch back out if things don't work.

posts.summary: This is a very brief link post where Joel Spolsky points readers to a separate article he wrote about REALBasic. He notes that REAL Software is a particularly interesting case study because all three of his previously published strategy letters apply directly to the company. The post itself contains no substantive argument beyond this teaser.
key_points: Joel wrote a dedicated analysis of REALBasic as a company/product
He considers REAL Software a compelling example because all three of his strategy letters apply to it simultaneously
The post serves purely as a pointer to the full article rather than containing standalone analysis
money_quotes: REAL Software is a particularly interesting example because all three of my strategy letters apply to them very directly!
posts.key_insight: REAL Software serves as a case study where multiple strategic frameworks about software business converge.

posts.summary: Joel argues that REALBasic is tantalizingly close to being the ideal cross-platform development tool but falls short due to gratuitous incompatibilities with Visual Basic. He establishes that managed-memory languages like VB are far more productive than C++ for GUI work, and that applications must use native widgets to look professional. Given Windows' 90-95% market dominance, Mac ports only make economic sense if they cost less than 10% of the original development effort. REALBasic could unlock a flood of Mac ports if it achieved near-perfect VB compatibility, but unnecessary differences in syntax and function names create pointless obstacles. He urges REALBasic's developers to prioritize VB source compatibility above all else, arguing that Apple itself should fund this effort.
key_points: Managed-memory languages like VB make programmers significantly more productive than C++ for GUI work, though C++ is still needed for performance-critical code paths
Applications must use native widgets and platform conventions to look professional — users judge software harshly when it violates UI expectations
Mac ports only make financial sense when they cost less than 10% of the original Windows development cost, given the ~10x difference in market size
REALBasic is extremely close to enabling cheap VB-to-Mac porting but is undermined by gratuitous incompatibilities like renaming LCase to LowerCase
Apple should pay REALBasic to achieve VB compatibility, as it would solve the Mac's chicken-and-egg software availability problem
money_quotes: My time writing Visual Basic was ALWAYS significantly more productive.

There are a lot of C++ only programmers who think of Basic as a toy language and disdain it. They think that they are cool coding jocks because they don't need wimpy languages. Frankly, they are wrong.
If you violate these expectations, they will feel like your program is a piece of junk.
When programmers try to reinvent their own widgets (viz. Netscape 6), users will think the program looks clunky.
I don't know how many people have told me that they tried various applications which were written in Java and thought that they felt unprofessional.

There are functions which have been in Basic since time immemorial, like LCase, which REALBasic calls 'LowerCase' for absolutely no good reason… except to serve as an obstacle to porting to or from VB.
If Apple has any sense at all (and they don't), they would PAY REALBasic to be more VB compatible.
posts.key_insight: Cross-platform development tools succeed or fail based on whether they make porting cheap enough to justify the smaller platform's market size, and REALBasic's gratuitous incompatibilities with Visual Basic squander an enormous opportunity.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky pushes back against the common complaint from software CEOs that they can't find good programmers. He argues this claim is 'baloney' and that the labor market is functioning normally — there are plenty of programmers available if companies know how to attract them. The post frames the hiring problem as one of approach rather than supply, promising to explain the programmer's perspective on recruitment.
key_points: The 'talent shortage' in software is overstated — CEOs who claim they can't hire are misdiagnosing the problem
The labor market for programmers is clearing normally in microeconomic terms; supply exists but companies fail to access it
Hiring success depends on understanding the programmer's perspective and what attracts them to a company
money_quotes: Ask any software CEO these days what their biggest problem is, and they'll usually complain about how hard it is to find good programmers.
Frankly, this is baloney.
In microeconomic terms: the market is clearing.
There are hundreds of thousands of programmers out there, and you can hire them, if you know how.
posts.key_insight: The programmer hiring crisis is not a supply problem but a failure by companies to understand what programmers want and how to attract them.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky argues that the supposed programmer shortage is a myth — it's simply a matter of companies refusing to meet market prices or failing to make themselves attractive employers. He applies basic microeconomic principles: markets clear when buyers accept market prices. Since money isn't most programmers' top motivator, companies can reduce their cash outlay by offering non-monetary benefits that matter more. He proposes a three-pronged approach: create an attractive workplace with private offices and good amenities, eliminate hiring obstacles like slow recruiters and relocation headaches, and provide perks that feel more valuable than they cost.
key_points: The programmer shortage is a pricing problem, not a supply problem — the labor market clears when employers accept market rates.
Software companies have effectively unlimited hiring needs because each additional programmer can generate more revenue through new features and improvements.
Money is not most programmers' top motivator; workplace quality, autonomy, interesting work, and learning opportunities matter more.
Companies should eliminate hiring friction — slow recruiters, failure to communicate decisions, and unresolved relocation concerns all drive candidates away.
Perks like private offices, free drinks, and extra vacation time cost far less than their perceived value, making them efficient substitutes for higher salaries.
money_quotes: Frankly, this is baloney. In microeconomic terms: the market is clearing.
You can hire programmers if you are willing to pay at or above the market price.
The trick is that money is not everyone's number one motivator, in fact, it's not even the number one consideration for most people.

People spend most of their waking hours at work, and they are going to take it personally, and they are not going to be willing to suffer throughout their career just for a little extra cash they can spend when they retire.
Work needs to be rewarding and pleasant. Doing things that make work rewarding and pleasant is the most important part of attracting people.
Cubicles have become such an icon of nasty workplaces that it's shocking that the companies who manufacture them still have the chutzpah to pretend that they're efficient, productive, and pleasant.
I don't know how many companies I've seen that have disorganized recruiters who just forget candidates and don't call them back.
Giving somebody six weeks instead of three weeks vacation is like paying them about 6% more salary.
posts.key_insight: The programmer shortage is not a supply problem but a demand-side failure: companies that won't pay market rates or invest in making work attractive and frictionless have only themselves to blame.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky dismisses Microsoft's .NET announcement as worse than vaporware, arguing that even the conceptual substance behind it is lacking. He expresses frustration that the tech industry and media have been 'duped' into treating .NET as revolutionary. The post is a brief teaser directing readers to his longer article 'Microsoft Goes Bonkers' for the full argument. He also notes he's been on vacation, explaining the gap in posts.
key_points: Microsoft .NET is characterized as worse than vaporware — even the 'vapor' (the conceptual vision) is absent
The tech industry has been uncritically credulous in treating .NET as revolutionary
Joel directs readers to his longer-form critique 'Microsoft Goes Bonkers' for the detailed argument
money_quotes: It's not Microsoft .NET per se that gets me all riled up, it's that everyone has been duped into thinking that this is some kind of revolutionary new thing when it's really one level worse than vaporware: even the vapor isn't there.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's .NET announcement was worse than vaporware because it lacked even a coherent vision behind the hype, yet the industry accepted it uncritically as revolutionary.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky dissects Microsoft's .NET announcement as empty vaporware, arguing the white paper contains no concrete ideas or implementable features, only abstract buzzwords and meaningless generalizations about 'empowerment' and 'productivity.' He explains that Microsoft's smart thinkers have ascended too far up the abstraction ladder, producing a vision so vague it becomes a Rorschach test onto which readers project their own ideas. The only discernible concrete proposal — software as subscription — serves Microsoft's revenue needs rather than customer needs. He notes that the actual .NET SDK contains only incremental improvements (SOAP, C#, updated ASP/ADO) rebranded under a revolutionary-sounding marketing umbrella. Spolsky concludes that Microsoft's marketing team simply slapped a unifying theme on routine product updates to manufacture the appearance of innovation.
key_points: The .NET white paper is worse than traditional vaporware because it doesn't even describe concrete features — just abstract, unfalsifiable 'benefits' that no one can criticize or implement
Over-abstraction is dangerous: smart thinkers who keep climbing the abstraction ladder eventually 'run out of oxygen' and produce frameworks that sound profound but mean nothing
Microsoft's real motivation behind .NET is enforcing subscription-based software licensing, which customers have repeatedly rejected for nearly a decade
Truly revolutionary innovations are rare and unlikely to emerge from within a large corporation where good ideas from rank-and-file programmers get buried
The actual .NET SDK contains only incremental improvements to existing products (SOAP, C#, ASP, ADO) dressed up with revolutionary marketing language
money_quotes: With vaporware, you promise all kinds of features and products that you simply can't sell because you don't really have them. But .NET is worse than vaporware.
And if you go too far up, abstraction-wise, you run out of oxygen.

Sometimes smart thinkers just don't know when to stop, and they create these absurd, all-encompassing, high-level pictures of the universe that are all good and fine, but don't actually mean anything at all.

This stuff is so abstract it's impossible to criticize. Who doesn't want an operating system that supports productivity? Great feature! Get me one of those spiffy new operating systems with the productivity feature!
Microsoft desperately wants to believe that it can manufacture revolution, but even in the Cambrian explosion of the Internet, there are only a handful of truly revolutionary ideas per year.
It almost seems as if Microsoft .NET doesn't fill a single customer need, it only fills Microsoft's need to find something for 10,000 programmers to do for the next 10 years.
They are playing the trick of psychic hotlines and newspaper horoscopes: by feeding you cloudy, meaningless generalizations, you fall into their trap of thinking that they read your mind.

What we have here, ladies and gentlemen, is a pure marketing team that looked around at all the upcoming releases, decided they need a 'theme' to make Microsoft look like Big Revolutionary Innovators, and ordering everyone to call their next thing '.NET'.
posts.key_insight: When smart people abstract too far from concrete problems, they produce grandiose visions that sound revolutionary but contain no implementable ideas — and marketing departments exploit this vagueness to manufacture the illusion of innovation.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky puts out a call to readers for firsthand experiences with offshore software development, specifically US-India development teams. He notes the common narrative that India offers low salaries and high skills, but says he hasn't seen a rigorous general analysis of whether it actually works. Having worked on such a team himself, he wants honest accounts from both sides to write a thorough article on the topic.
key_points: Offshore development (particularly US-India) was becoming a hot topic in 2000, driven by the salary differential and available talent
Joel acknowledges the lack of rigorous, honest analysis beyond rumors and anecdotes about whether offshoring actually delivers on its promises
He explicitly asks for brutal honesty about costs, savings, problems, and successes from people with direct experience
money_quotes: I've heard a lot of rumours, and I've worked on one such team myself, but I've never seen a more general analysis of whether it can be made to work.
This site is not known for pulling punches.
posts.key_insight: Despite growing enthusiasm for offshore software development, there was a notable absence of honest, experience-based analysis of whether it actually delivers on its promises of cost savings and quality.

posts.summary: Joel responds to an anonymous Microsoft insider's reaction to his earlier critique of Microsoft .NET. He argues that .NET is not a real product or coherent technical vision but rather marketing blather designed to create the illusion of a grand strategy. He compares it to the previous substance-free 'Windows DNA' campaign. Joel contends that Microsoft's marketing people are allowed to write white papers pretending engineers are building features that are really just wishful thinking.
key_points: Microsoft .NET is criticized as a marketing umbrella term rather than a genuine technical product or vision
The .NET branding follows the same pattern as the failed 'Windows DNA' campaign — repackaging existing products under a buzzword
Microsoft's marketing teams write white papers that overstate what engineering is actually building, creating a gap between vision and reality
money_quotes: It's not that Microsoft doesn't have any new products that they're calling .NET; it's that the concept .NET itself is just marketing blather designed to make it look like there's a grand vision at Microsoft, when all that you have is a handful of upgraded products.
The trouble is, at Microsoft, these marketing guys are allowed to write white papers and pretend that the engineers are really working on that telepathic user interface they asked for.
posts.key_insight: Grand-sounding technology branding like .NET can be hollow marketing that repackages existing products under the illusion of a unified vision.

posts.summary: An anonymous Microsoft employee responds to Joel's earlier critique of .NET, confirming from the inside that even Microsoft staff don't understand what .NET is supposed to be. The employee, who works on Passport (Microsoft's flagship 'web service' example), describes a year of management trying to explain the NGWS/.NET vision without ever defining a clear goal. Through a series of increasingly circular conversations with colleagues, the employee demonstrates that every attempt to define 'web service' collapses into descriptions of things that already exist. The employee concludes that .NET has become 'all things to all people' — a branding umbrella for every product shipping in the next two years, masking a deeper problem of missing focus and direction at Microsoft.
key_points: Even insiders working on Microsoft's showcase .NET product (Passport) couldn't articulate what .NET actually was or how it differed from existing work
Management spent nearly a year pushing the NGWS/.NET vision without ever defining a concrete goal — 'the proverbial answer in search of a question'
C# is dismissed as an unfinished Java copy lacking a mature standard library and years of real-world testing
.NET had become a catch-all umbrella brand for every Microsoft product shipping in the next two years, diluting any coherent meaning
The employee suspects .NET is really a substitute for genuine strategic focus — a rallying point to paper over directionlessness
money_quotes: Management spent nearly a year explaining how everyone needed to focus on NGWS and how we could all fit into the vision – without ever describing the goal.
It was the proverbial answer in search of a question.
All of a sudden it has a new name, seemingly an attempt to hide the fact that it still has no body.
I've asked around how this new .NET plan differs from everything we've been working on the past two years and haven't been given a decent answer.
I don't think we know what .NET is. It is becoming all things to all people and the vision continues to grow.
Perhaps .NET is nothing more than a hopeful solution to a deeper problem…a lack of focus in the company – a missing goal and rallying point.
Unfortunately, we need a lot more than hand-waving right now.
posts.key_insight: When a company's grand strategic vision cannot be clearly explained even to the people building it, the vision is likely serving as a substitute for genuine strategic direction rather than providing one.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky raises alarm about Microsoft Passport, arguing it's a thinly disguised attempt to build the world's largest consumer database for profit while being marketed as a consumer benefit. He links to a longer article detailing the privacy threats. He also includes a humorous aside mocking a Concorde passenger's misunderstanding of basic probability — the gambler's fallacy that a prior crash makes a second crash less likely.
key_points: Microsoft Passport poses a major privacy threat by centralizing consumer data under Microsoft's control
Microsoft is successfully marketing a data-mining tool as a consumer benefit, and people are falling for it
The Passport system will make existing cookie-based tracking seem trivial by comparison
People commonly misunderstand probability, believing past events influence independent future outcomes (the gambler's fallacy)
money_quotes: It seems to me like a fairly blatant attempt to build the world's largest, richest consumer database, and then make fabulous profits mining it.
The scariest thing is that Microsoft is advertising Passport as if it were a benefit to consumers, and people seem to be falling for it!
It's a terrifying threat to everyone's personal privacy and it will make today's 'cookies' seem positively tame by comparison.
I'm not a statistician, but I do know that the probability of the Concorde crashing twice is infinitesimal.
Even if you don't know basic probability, do you really think that the Concorde you're flying on knows about the other Concorde that crashed?
posts.key_insight: When a company packages mass data collection as a consumer convenience, people willingly surrender their privacy — a pattern Joel identified in 2000 that would define the next two decades of tech.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky briefly notes responses to his earlier article criticizing Microsoft Passport. He observes that the only defender of Passport was a long-time Microsoft employee, suggesting that extended time within Microsoft's culture creates a perspective bubble that diverges from how the broader world views their products.
key_points: The only defense of Microsoft Passport came from a 10-year Microsoft veteran
Spolsky suggests that long tenure at Microsoft creates an insular worldview
Even personally trusted and respected colleagues can have blind spots shaped by corporate culture
money_quotes: the only person who defended Passport was a 10-year Microsoft employee — a person who I know, trust, like, and respect, but who, I'm afraid to say, has been living in Redmond too long to see things the way the rest of the world sees them.
posts.key_insight: Working inside a large tech company for too long can create a perspective bubble that prevents you from seeing your products the way the outside world does.

posts.summary: Spolsky shares reader responses to his earlier article criticizing Microsoft Passport, a centralized authentication system. The overwhelming majority of respondents opposed Passport, with only one supporter — a Microsoft employee. Readers raised concerns about privacy surveillance, Microsoft leveraging its software ecosystem to force Passport adoption, and parallels to existing ad network tracking. The lone defender argued that centralized identity solves real usability problems like password fatigue and that competitive markets would prevent abuse. Spolsky frames the lopsided response as evidence of Microsoft's internal 'Reality Distortion Field.'
key_points: Reader responses to Spolsky's Passport critique were overwhelmingly negative, with only one Microsoft employee defending the system
Readers warned that Microsoft could use its control of tools like FrontPage to force third-party websites into the Passport network, extending surveillance far beyond Microsoft properties
Multiple respondents drew parallels between Passport and existing ad network tracking via cookies, arguing this was an extension of already-eroded web privacy
The sole Passport defender argued centralized identity solves real UX problems like password fatigue and that market competition prevents price discrimination abuse
Spolsky uses the response ratio to illustrate how disconnected Microsoft's internal culture was from external developer and user sentiment
money_quotes: Out of the dozens of emails I received, only one supported Passport, and that was from a Microsoft employee. Which just goes to show how powerful the Microsoft Reality Distortion Field gets in Redmond.
It's not just Microsoft sites, they'll know all about you if you visit competitors, mom-n-pop shops, porn sites, etc etc.
What Microsoft is doing is what every ad banner network has been doing for ages: spying on you.
Ever since the cookie was integrated into most browsers, there has been zero privacy on the net, ethical or not.
Do you think the alternative: typing all of your information every time you log into a site and having so many passwords to remember is acceptable?
Someone has to solve this problem. If you think you know a better way, then that would be a much more interesting article.
posts.key_insight: The near-unanimous opposition to Microsoft Passport from outside Redmond revealed how corporate echo chambers can blind companies to legitimate privacy concerns about centralized identity systems.

posts.summary: Joel briefly mentions experimenting with WAP (Wireless Application Protocol), the early mobile web technology for cellphones, and finds it disappointing. This is essentially a short link post directing readers to a fuller article about the wireless web's shortcomings.
key_points: WAP, the early mobile web protocol, failed to impress despite industry hype
The wireless web in 2000 was not ready for practical use
money_quotes: I've been playing with WAP, the new web-like service for cellphones. Conclusion: I'm underwhelmed.
posts.key_insight: Early mobile web technology (WAP) was overhyped and underdelivered on its promise of bringing the internet to cellphones.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky recounts his experience as an early adopter of Verizon's Wireless Web (WAP) service on his cellphone, detailing the painful ordering process, the severe limitations of typing and reading on a four-line screen, and the shockingly high costs. He compares the nascent wireless web to the early days of Gopher and Mosaic, acknowledging a similar gut feeling about its potential, but argues that unlike the web revolution, wireless web is merely an incremental step. He warns that cellular providers' exorbitant airtime charges threaten to strangle the service before it can grow, and criticizes providers who restrict access to only a handful of paid partner sites.
key_points: The WAP wireless web experience is severely hampered by tiny screens, painful text input, and frequent disconnections that waste paid airtime minutes.
Cellular providers pursuing walled-garden approaches (restricting access to paid partner sites) are repeating the same mistake AOL made before the open Internet rendered it obsolete.
Unlike the early web which had flat-rate unmetered access, wireless web's per-minute airtime charges create a financial barrier that could kill adoption before the ecosystem matures.
The WAP protocol itself is largely irrelevant to developers since gateways translate WAP to HTTP, but the real challenge is redesigning content for tiny screens using WML.
Despite its current limitations, the wireless web gives Spolsky the same gut feeling he had when first seeing Mosaic — but he questions whether an incremental improvement can spark a revolution.
money_quotes: Whenever you order something and they say that the service will start 'within x days', you can tell that you discovered a business that has a turd-drop process in place.
The unbelievable stupidity of the cellular providers that haven't learned this lesson and are trying to create their own private micro-sub-set of the wireless web is outrageous.
Do not use their service unless you're the kind of person who would consider buying Internet access that only let you go to a dozen web sites.
The web was truly a revolution in the way that the printing press was a revolution, but wireless web seems to be just an incremental step, not a revolution.
I'm starting to think that the high price charged by the cellular providers is going to choke off the oxygen to this fledgling service before it can get started.
If you think you can make a profit in a wireless world in which the telcos are sucking out all the oxygen, well, you had better bring a spacesuit.
Yep, that's right, my phone bill was $284 for the first month of playing around with this service, and I didn't even play with it that much.
posts.key_insight: Exorbitant per-minute pricing by cellular providers threatens to suffocate the wireless web before it can achieve the critical mass that flat-rate pricing enabled for the desktop web.

posts.summary: This is a very brief announcement post where Joel shares free desktop wallpaper pictures he took during the summer in East Hampton. There is no substantive argument or technical content. It is essentially a personal share of photography for readers to download and use as desktop backgrounds.
key_points: Joel offered free high-resolution desktop wallpaper photos to readers
The photos were taken during summer in East Hampton
The post serves as a brief personal/lifestyle aside rather than a technical essay
money_quotes: Stuck behind a desk? Brighten your monitor a bit with these high resolution digital pictures I took this summer in East Hampton.
posts.key_insight: A minor personal post sharing desktop wallpaper photos with no technical or business content.

posts.summary: This is a brief follow-up post where Joel references his earlier Strategy Letter I, in which he used Wordsworth.com as an example of a company that failed despite being an early mover in online bookselling. Joel notes that someone from Wordsworth wrote back to respond to his characterization, and he links to their response. He also mentions a friend opening a small bookstore with an accompanying blog.
key_points: Early movers without lock-in and network effects can still lose to later entrants like Amazon
Wordsworth.com preceded Amazon by two years but failed to achieve widespread recognition
The post demonstrates Joel's practice of engaging with and linking to reader responses
money_quotes: If you're going into a market with no existing competition, lock-in, and network effects, you better use the Amazon model, or you're going the way of Wordsworth.com, which started two years before Amazon, and nobody's ever heard of them.
posts.key_insight: Being first to market means nothing without a strategy for lock-in and network effects, as demonstrated by Wordsworth.com losing to the later-arriving Amazon.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky follows up on his Strategy Letter I by sharing a response from Wordsworth.com, an online bookstore that predated Amazon by two years but failed to capitalize on the opportunity. Sanj Kharbanda from Wordsworth admits they had the vision but didn't execute, partly because they feared losing their independent community store ethos. Spolsky uses this as a launching point to argue that independent bookstores, both online and physical, often fail because they don't do what it takes to compete. He uses the example of Shakespeare and Co. losing to Barnes & Noble on the Upper West Side, arguing the indie store offered nothing compelling enough to survive. Spolsky acknowledges the cultural value of independents but contends that megastores like B&N actually democratized access to books across America. The post is a pragmatic, if somewhat bittersweet, case for why execution and competitive positioning matter more than being first or having good intentions.
key_points: Wordsworth.com had a two-year head start over Amazon but failed to execute on its early vision, proving that first-mover advantage without execution is worthless
Independent bookstores often fail not because of unfair competition but because they don't offer enough differentiation to justify their limitations in selection, space, and convenience
The people who love books enough to run indie bookstores are often not the best businesspeople, creating a fundamental tension between passion and competitiveness
Big-box bookstores like B&N actually democratized access to books, making diverse selections available in smaller cities and at convenient hours
Switching affiliate links from Amazon to Wordsworth killed Doc Searls' referral revenue, demonstrating the power of Amazon's lock-in and network effects
money_quotes: Our error, we had the vision we just did not execute.
I suspect that is because the types of people who love books enough to be indie booksellers are not necessarily the best businesspeople.
I would miss them, if I could think of a single thing they offered that B&N didn't.
Doc Searls changes his bookstore from Amazon to Wordsworth and sees his revenue plummet to $0.
I love the fact that B&N means that worried gay teenagers can read XY magazine, even if they live in Kansas City.
I love the fact that I can buy an XML reference manual 11:30 PM in my neighborhood.
posts.key_insight: Having the vision without the execution is worthless — independent businesses that can't articulate a concrete competitive advantage over well-capitalized incumbents are doomed regardless of how much cultural goodwill they carry.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky introduces his Joel Test, a quick 12-step checklist for evaluating software team quality, positioning it as a practical alternative to the complex SEMA methodology. He notes it takes only 3 minutes compared to years of study. The post also mentions a Russian translation of his UI book and praises Windows 2000's automatic handling of international character encodings.
key_points: Existing software team evaluation methods like SEMA are too complex and time-consuming for practical use
Simple, fast heuristics for measuring software team quality can be more useful than rigorous but inaccessible methodologies
Unicode and modern internationalization support in Windows 2000 represented a meaningful leap in usability for multilingual content
money_quotes: It's a fairly esoteric system for measuring how good a software team is. It will take you about six years just to understand that stuff.
So I've come up with my own, highly irresponsible, sloppy test to rate the quality of a software team.
The great part about it is that it takes about 3 minutes. With all the time you save, you can go to medical school.
posts.key_insight: A simple, fast heuristic for evaluating software teams is more practically valuable than a rigorous but inaccessible methodology.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky presents a simple 12-question yes/no test for evaluating the quality of a software development team, as a practical alternative to complex methodologies like SEMA. The test covers fundamentals like source control, one-step builds, daily builds, bug tracking, specs, quiet offices, good tools, testers, coding interviews, and hallway usability testing. He argues that most organizations score only 2 or 3 out of 12, while companies like Microsoft consistently score 12. Each question is explained with practical rationale and real-world anecdotes, including Microsoft Word's 'infinite defects methodology' disaster. The test is designed to be quick and actionable, giving teams and job candidates a fast way to assess engineering maturity.
key_points: A simple yes/no checklist can effectively measure software team quality without complex metrics, with scores below 10 indicating serious problems
Fixing bugs before writing new code is critical because bug-fixing costs grow exponentially over time and make schedules unreliable
Programmer productivity depends heavily on uninterrupted flow state, making quiet private offices far more productive than open-plan layouts
Specs, schedules, and process discipline aren't bureaucratic overhead — they prevent the kind of chaos that killed Netscape and plagued early Microsoft Word
Hiring programmers without having them write code is as absurd as hiring a magician without seeing tricks or a caterer without tasting food
money_quotes: A score of 12 is perfect, 11 is tolerable, but 10 or lower and you've got serious problems.
The schedule was merely a checklist of features waiting to be turned into bugs. In the post-mortem, this was referred to as 'infinite defects methodology'.
In general, the longer you wait before fixing a bug, the costlier (in time and money) it is to fix.
Writing specs is like flossing: everybody agrees that it's a good thing, but nobody does it.

If a coworker asks you a question, causing a 1 minute interruption, but this knocks you out of the zone badly enough that it takes you half an hour to get productive again, your overall productivity is in serious trouble.
Top notch development teams don't torture their programmers.
Would you hire a magician without asking them to show you some magic tricks? Of course not.

If your team doesn't have dedicated testers, at least one for every two or three programmers, you are either shipping buggy products, or you're wasting money by having $100/hour programmers do work that can be done by $30/hour testers.
posts.key_insight: Software team quality can be reliably assessed with 12 simple yes/no questions covering engineering fundamentals, and most teams fail badly because they neglect basic practices like bug tracking, specs, and quiet working conditions.

posts.summary: Joel shares two brief updates: he's setting up a new software company (temporarily called PaxDigita) in New York City and comments on the state of startup office space. He also highlights that four of the most influential UI designers—Norman, Laurel, Tognazzini, and Nielsen—are now all working together at the Nielsen|Norman Group, comparing it to a historic intellectual dream team.
key_points: Startup office spaces in NYC were cramped and poorly named ('cages' and 'bullpens'), making it hard to attract top talent.
The four greatest UI design thinkers—Norman, Laurel, Tognazzini, and Nielsen—have converged at one group, which Joel sees as a historic event.
Joel recommends four essential UI design books as required reading for every software engineer.
The physical workspace matters for recruiting top talent—terminology and environment send signals about company culture.
money_quotes: Can you imagine trying to hire the world's top computer scientists to work in 'cages'?
Imagine the Institute for Advanced Study in the forties when Einstein and Feynmann were there. Now imagine if Newton and Kepler were there, too, and you get some idea of how exciting this is.
These four books are the 'Great Books' of UI design and should be required reading for every software engineer and UI designer.
If you asked experienced UI designers to list the world's top names in UI design, you would get surprisingly similar results. Norman. Laurel. Tognazzini. Nielsen.
It seems to have escaped everyone's notice that all four are working together now.
posts.key_insight: The physical environment you offer talent matters as much as the work itself, and the convergence of top minds in a field is a rare event worth paying attention to.

posts.summary: In this brief, humorous post, Spolsky jokes that Verizon staged a strike just to teach people how to pronounce their new corporate name. He then makes a sharp observation about corporate rebranding: when companies change their names, it's because their old brand has become a liability through years of poor customer service. He closes with a self-deprecating punchline, announcing that his own company PaxDigita has been renamed to Fog Creek Software — implying the same logic applies to himself.
key_points: Corporate rebranding signals that the old brand name has become a liability rather than an asset
Companies like BellAtlantic and GTE accumulated 'negative brand equity' through years of poor customer service
Spolsky announces the renaming of his company from PaxDigita to Fog Creek Software, using self-deprecating humor to underscore his own point
money_quotes: Whenever a company changes its name, the only thing that it logically means is that they concluded that their old brand name was a liability, not an asset.
BellAtlantic and GTE have spent so long pissing off so many people with such bad customer service that their names had negative brand equity.
On a completely unrelated subject, my company PaxDigita is now known as Fog Creek Software.
posts.key_insight: Corporate rebranding is an admission that the old name has accumulated negative brand equity, usually through poor customer experience.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky briefly reviews Tom DeMarco's essay collection 'Why Does Software Cost So Much?', recommending it based on DeMarco's credentials as co-author of Peopleware. He considers Peopleware the best book ever written about software management and praises DeMarco as a genius of software project management.
key_points: Peopleware is endorsed as the all-time best book on software management
Tom DeMarco is praised as the world's leading genius of software project management
About half the essays in the collection are highlighted as proving DeMarco's expertise
money_quotes: Peopleware, which is the all-time best book about software management ever written.
About half the essays prove that Tom DeMarco is simply the world's leading genious of software project management, and you shouldn't skip this book.
posts.key_insight: Tom DeMarco's writings, especially Peopleware, represent essential reading for anyone involved in software project management.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Joel Spolsky promotes two things: a new article called 'Three Wrong Ideas from Computer Science' that challenges established CS orthodoxy, and the launch of his consulting company Fog Creek Software in New York City. The post serves primarily as a teaser for the longer article, promising that three widely-accepted computer science ideas are actually wrong. It also marks the founding moment of Fog Creek Software, which would go on to create products like FogBugz, Trello, and Stack Overflow.
key_points: Joel teases an article arguing that three fundamental computer science ideas are wrong and gaining recognition as such
Fog Creek Software is announced as a new consulting company offering high-end custom software development in NYC
The post reflects Joel's contrarian approach to conventional wisdom in software engineering
money_quotes: Not to rain on everybody's parade, but there are three important ideas from computer science which are, frankly, wrong, and people are starting to notice.
Ignore them at your peril.
posts.key_insight: This brief post captures two defining aspects of Joel Spolsky's career: his willingness to challenge established computer science orthodoxy and his entrepreneurial ambition in launching Fog Creek Software.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky argues that three widely-accepted ideas from computer science are fundamentally wrong: that search should maximize results, that anti-aliased text looks better, and that network resources should behave like local ones. He points out that Google succeeded by focusing on ranking rather than recall, that anti-aliased body text looks blurry compared to pixel-fitted fonts like Verdana, and that network transparency abstractions like RPC and DCOM dangerously hide latency, availability, and reliability concerns. The post challenges the gap between academic ideals and practical engineering reality.
key_points: Search engines like Altavista failed by optimizing for recall rather than relevance; Google's PageRank succeeded by focusing on sorting results, not finding more of them.
Anti-aliased text looks worse for sustained reading at screen resolution; fonts like Verdana that embrace the pixel grid are more readable than anti-aliased high-resolution fonts.
Network transparency abstractions like RPC and DCOM hide critical differences between local and remote resources: availability, latency, and reliability.
Practical engineering must account for real-world constraints rather than pursuing theoretically elegant abstractions that ignore them.
Microsoft's ClearType, despite being a newer approach to anti-aliasing, still produces blurry text on LCD screens.
money_quotes: By reducing the search from one billion to one million pages, Altavista has done absolutely nothing for me.
The real problem in searching is how to sort the results.
Ranking the pages in the right order was more important than grabbing every possible page.
If you try to read a paragraph of antialiased text, it just looks blurry. There's nothing I can do about it, it's the truth.
Instead of creating a high-resolution font and then trying to hammer it into the pixel grid, they finally accepted the pixel grid as a 'given' and designed a font that fits neatly into it.
Using programming interfaces that hide all this stuff from you is a great way to make a lousy software program.
The next time someone tries to sell you a programming product that lets you access network resources the same way as you access local resources, run full speed in the opposite direction.
posts.key_insight: Elegant computer science abstractions that hide real-world complexity — whether in search, rendering, or networking — produce worse outcomes than pragmatic solutions that embrace the underlying constraints.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky outlines his initial compensation plan for Fog Creek Software, built around transparency and egalitarianism. Programmers are placed into six levels (9-14) based on experience, technology exposure, and architectural skills, with fixed salaries per level. Bonuses are distributed as an equal percentage of salary company-wide rather than individually, and stock options are weighted toward early employees who take the most risk. To handle competitive hiring situations without breaking internal equity, Spolsky introduces a one-time 'startup bonus' as a flexible lure. He explicitly rejects individual salary negotiation, believing transparency and fairness will prevent resentment. The post was later completely rewritten based on reader feedback.
key_points: Salaries are fixed per level with no individual negotiation — everyone at the same level earns the same amount, and the schedule is transparent to all employees
Bonuses are company-wide and proportional: if one person gets 11%, everyone gets 11%, rejecting individual performance bonuses
Stock options are front-loaded to reward early employees who take the most risk joining a small company
A one-time 'startup bonus' serves as the only flexible tool to compete for candidates with higher outside offers, preserving salary equity
The six-level system ranges from high school interns (Level 9) to industry legends like Bjarne Stroustrup or Linus Torvalds (Level 14)
money_quotes: Salary is intended to be very egalitarian, fair, and transparent.
It is our explicit philosophy that bonuses are rewarded based on the performance of the entire company, not the individual.
There is no penalty for quitting the company before bonuses are paid: if you do this, you'll still get your prorated bonus for the part of the year that you worked.
This policy is to prevent getting a wave of people quitting every January and screwing up our lives.
If we're having trouble hiring because of low salaries, we'll raise the salaries for everyone, even the people we've already hired.

We think it will be extremely valuable to maintain salaries, bonuses, and benefits for programmers at equal levels rather than negotiating individually with everyone and then having people upset that they are not getting a 'fair' salary.
This level is reserved for hiring people like Bjarne Stroustrup or Linus Torvalds, or promoting geniuses to an independent research position.
posts.key_insight: Transparent, egalitarian compensation with no individual negotiation prevents internal resentment, and one-time signing bonuses can handle competitive offers without breaking salary equity.

posts.summary: Joel briefly announces he's designing Fog Creek Software's programmer compensation package, linking to a detailed article and reader feedback. The bulk of the post is a rant about Microsoft Word's terrible HTML export, which generates bloated, non-standard markup full of proprietary formatting. He describes spending 30 minutes manually cleaning up Word's output, including stripping smart quotes that break on non-Windows platforms. He recommends a tool called 'Demoroniser' for fixing Word's HTML mess.
key_points: Joel is publicly designing Fog Creek's compensation system and soliciting reader feedback, showing his characteristically transparent approach to company-building.
Microsoft Word's 'HTML as native format' claim is misleading—it generates bloated, proprietary markup that requires extensive manual cleanup.
Smart quotes from Word are not valid HTML and cause cross-platform display issues, representing a subtle but pervasive compatibility problem.
Even routine tasks like publishing a blog post can be derailed by poor tool interoperability.
money_quotes: Unfortunately, Word, which "uses HTML as its native format," made a mess of it.
The "HTML" that it generated was full of unnecessary formatting crap that only Word cares about.
Word uses smart quotes, and it leaves them in the doc, even though they are not a part of HTML and they don't show up right on non-Windows platforms.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft Word's claim to use HTML as a native format masks the reality that its output is bloated, proprietary markup that fails basic cross-platform compatibility.

posts.summary: Joel notes that his previous post on compensation policy generated significant reader feedback, leading him to extensively rewrite the policy. He draws a parallel between this collaborative refinement process and open source software development, except applied to company-building principles rather than code. He describes the concept of an 'Open Blueprint Company' where the blueprint for running a software company is developed publicly with community input.
key_points: Reader feedback led to a substantial rewrite of Fog Creek's compensation policy while preserving the core idea
Developing company policies in public with community feedback mirrors the open source development model
Joel introduces the concept of an 'Open Blueprint Company' — open-sourcing the organizational design rather than code
money_quotes: The process feels a lot like open source.

It's not really the source code in this case, it's the blueprint for making a software company which I'm developing, in the open, with feedback and enhancements sent in by a community of contributors.
posts.key_insight: The open source model of collaborative, public iteration can be applied not just to code but to the very blueprint for how a software company operates.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky outlines Fog Creek Software's compensation system, built around a transparent professional ladder (levels 8-15) adapted from Microsoft's system. The ladder provides a career path for engineers who don't want to move into management, with levels determined by experience, programming maturity, and strategic role. Compensation is deliberately simple and public: fixed salaries per level, no bonuses (replaced by company-wide profit sharing), generous benefits, and stock options weighted toward early employees. The system prioritizes fairness and transparency, with the explicit philosophy that anyone should be able to figure out what their coworkers earn.
key_points: The professional ladder (levels 8-15) creates a promotion path for engineers who don't want to manage people, avoiding the common trap of forcing good programmers into management roles.
Compensation transparency is a core principle: salary rules, levels, and the formula are all public, rejecting the common practice of salary secrecy that breeds politics and resentment.
Traditional bonuses are rejected as ineffective — too expected and too temporally removed from actual work — replaced by company-wide profit sharing that rewards collective performance.
Performance reviews are internal management tools for ensuring correct leveling, not carrot-and-stick incentive systems for grading employees.
Salary is set by level with no within-level variation, using market signals (offer acceptance rates) rather than negotiation to keep compensation fair and competitive.
money_quotes: Fog Creek explicitly recognizes that many good software engineers have no desire whatsoever to do 'management' or to take on a formal personnel management role.
We want to avoid the trap that many companies fall into of forcing good programmers to stop programming and start managing people, if that's not what they want to do.
Many companies try to obfuscate the rules they use for determining compensation in hopes that they won't get caught paying some people too much and others too little.
This transparency policy is a good thing that forces Fog Creek management to keep things fair.
Bonus plans have too many problems. They've become like tips in restaurants: everyone expects one, so they can no longer be used well to reward good performance.

I hate companies that say 'we're only going to pay you half what you're worth, but the stock options will make you a zillionaire when we go public!' Fog Creek might not go public. You might not become a zillionaire.
It's impossible to use hard-and-fast metrics to decide where someone fits in, and these kinds of metrics tend to accidentally incentivize the wrong things.
Nothing is carved in stone except the principle that within each group, people should be true peers.
posts.key_insight: Fair compensation requires radical transparency — public rules, fixed salaries per level, and no individual bonuses — because secrecy and discretion inevitably breed politics and resentment.

posts.summary: This is a brief, lighthearted personal post with two unrelated musings. Spolsky recommends Patagonia Capilene T-shirts as an affordable alternative to expensive Prada shirts. He then jokes about his fantasy next business venture: an absurdly upscale mustard and mayonnaise shop in the Hamptons. The post is pure whimsy with no software or tech content, offering a glimpse into Spolsky's humor and personality.
key_points: Spolsky recommends Patagonia Capilene Silkweight T-shirts as a comfortable, affordable alternative to designer clothing
He satirizes upscale retail culture by imagining an artisanal mustard and mayonnaise shop with a fake heritage brand name
The post captures the casual, personal blogging style common in early 2000s tech blogs where authors mixed life observations with professional content
money_quotes: I'm sick of computers. I think my next venture will be a very tastefully decorated shop, lots of wood and bronze, that only sells mustard and mayonnaise.
It will be called "Smith, Smith, Smith, and Son: Purveyors of Fine Mustards and Mayonnaises Since 1804".
We will have Lime-Basil Aioli, (which real foodies know does not involve limes).
posts.key_insight: Even prolific tech bloggers occasionally just want to talk about t-shirts and fantasy condiment shops.

posts.summary: This is a brief blog update post from Joel Spolsky covering two topics: a scathing review of DigitalConvergence's 'Cat' product (a barcode scanner peripheral), which he considers a terrible business idea, and an announcement that Fog Creek Software has moved into a new office near South Street Seaport in Manhattan. The post is primarily a link to a longer review and a short office update with photos.
key_points: Joel published a review harshly criticizing DigitalConvergence's Cat product as a fundamentally flawed business idea
Fog Creek Software moved into a new office near South Street Seaport with windows, a kitchen, and red walls
The post reflects Fog Creek's early days as a small startup establishing its physical presence in New York City
money_quotes: The number of dumb things going on here exceeds my limited ability to grok all at once.
I'm a bit overwhelmed with what a feeble business idea this is.
posts.key_insight: A brief diary-style update capturing Fog Creek Software's early startup days, combining product criticism with the milestone of getting a real office.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky tears apart the CueCat, a barcode scanner mailed free to Wired subscribers that lets users scan barcodes in magazine ads to visit websites. He argues the device fails on three fronts: it solves no real problem since typing a URL is trivially easy, it suffers from a fatal chicken-and-egg problem where neither advertisers nor consumers have incentive to adopt first, and it follows a pattern of similarly doomed ideas Wired had already promoted and abandoned. He estimates the company DigitalConvergence is burning money at unsustainable rates with 200 employees and massive distribution costs. A postscript confirms the company laid off most employees less than a year later.
key_points: The CueCat solves no real consumer problem — typing a short URL is trivially easy, and the device offers zero benefit to the end user
The product suffers from a classic chicken-and-egg problem: advertisers won't add barcodes without an installed base, and consumers won't install without barcodes to scan
The company's burn rate is staggering — 200 employees plus millions in manufacturing and postage for a free device most recipients will throw away
Products that provide no consumer benefit cannot achieve adoption no matter how much money you spend on distribution
Wired had already promoted and abandoned a similar URL-from-ads technology just two months prior, suggesting the entire category is doomed
money_quotes: The number of dumb things going on here exceeds my limited ability to grok all at once.
If you invent something that doesn't solve a problem, it better be entertaining.
There is no possible benefit to the consumer, so no consumer in their right mind will use this.

This might be a decent business if everybody had cats installed, and it would be a great business if every ad, everywhere, had barcodes, but, uh, they don't, and they don't, so it's not such a great business.
So if Wired thinks they are going to impress their advertisers by showing them how 13 people scanned the page and went to the web site, they're in for a rude awakening.

If you're working for a company that's spending millions of dollars trying to get people to do something with absolutely no benefit to them, and which suffers from chicken and egg problems, don't be counting your stock options just yet.
posts.key_insight: No amount of money spent on distribution can save a product that provides zero benefit to the consumer and suffers from a chicken-and-egg adoption problem.

posts.summary: Joel discusses two unrelated topics: his frustration with Register.com's URL forwarding service that injects ads into his site, and his positive experience setting up a Cobalt Qube server for Fog Creek Software. He used JavaScript to work around Register.com's ad injection and notes the irony that it might discourage people from using the service. On the Cobalt Qube, he observes that while the hardware is no longer a bargain compared to a Linux PC, it sells well because the software makes setup drastically easier, predicting Cobalt will eventually become a pure software company.
