key_points: Register.com's 'free' URL forwarding service frames your site with their ads, which Joel considers deceptive and works around with JavaScript
The Cobalt Qube replaced days of Linux configuration work with about 3 hours of setup for equivalent server functionality
The Qube's hardware price advantage disappeared, but its software ease-of-use kept it competitive, demonstrating that software adds value faster than it adds cost
Joel predicts Cobalt will outsource hardware and become a pure software company based on where the real value lies
money_quotes: Software adds value faster than it adds cost.
Their free redirection service frames your site with a big fat ad. Lame.

Now I'm kind of hoping that people mistakenly believe that if you use register.com, your web page will have a big register.com ad. That would teach them to sneak their nasty ads in where they don't belong!
All told, we got all this stuff running with about 3 hours of work between us… compared to several days of work when I set up the same thing for myself on a new Linux box at home.
Prediction: Cobalt will outsource all the hardware stuff and become a pure software company.
posts.key_insight: Software's ability to simplify complex tasks adds value far faster than it adds cost, making it the real competitive advantage over hardware.

posts.summary: This is a very brief link post where Joel Spolsky piles on criticism of the CueCat barcode scanner product. He references his earlier detailed critique of the device and adds a new data point: the company behind CueCat failed to keep customer data private. The post implies that poor security practices are yet another reason to avoid the product.
key_points: CueCat already had numerous problems that Joel had previously documented
The company behind CueCat suffered a customer data privacy failure
Security incompetence compounds an already flawed product concept
money_quotes: As if there weren't enough reasons not to use Cue Cat, the company can't even keep it's customer data private.
posts.key_insight: A product that was already ill-conceived becomes indefensible when the company behind it can't even protect its users' data.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky criticizes non-compete clauses in employment contracts, particularly the aggressive two-year versions some companies are imposing. He reveals that a lawyer admitted the goal is to create a 'united front' so employees have no choice, and positions Fog Creek's refusal to use non-competes as a competitive hiring advantage. He also humorously catalogs the overwhelming bureaucratic burden of incorporating a new company, describing weeks of paperwork just to create a company that 'doesn't do anything.'
key_points: Two-year non-compete clauses are unreasonable and effectively prevent employees from working in their field
Lawyers representing employers openly admit the strategy is to make non-competes universal so workers lose bargaining power
Refusing non-competes becomes a competitive advantage in hiring talent
The administrative overhead of starting a company is absurdly large relative to doing any actual work
money_quotes: Does your employment contract include a non-compete clause?
That's nuts. What am I supposed to do? Go two years without a job? Go back to graduate school?
We're trying to get all the employers to put them in. Provide a united front. So that employees don't have any choice.
I'll hire you even if you don't want to sign a restrictive non-compete clause that makes you scared to ever look for another job in your field.
Over the last four or five weeks I've been shocked, shocked at how much work it takes just to create a company that doesn't do anything.
posts.key_insight: Non-compete clauses benefit employers collectively at employees' expense, and refusing to use them is both ethically right and a smart hiring strategy.

posts.summary: This is a very brief blog post serving as a teaser for an upcoming series on writing specs, combined with a lighthearted anecdote about complaining to Starbucks customer service. Joel wrote to Starbucks about the grease pencils leaving black residue on his hands when he picks up his coffee. He received a prompt, professional reply from their customer relations team. The post is essentially filler content with a self-deprecating joke about drinking too much coffee.
key_points: Joel announces an upcoming series on how to write specs
He shares an anecdote about writing to Starbucks customer service about grease pencil residue on coffee cups
Starbucks responded promptly and professionally, forwarding his suggestion to operations
Joel jokes that excessive coffee consumption drives people to write complaint letters
money_quotes: See what drinking too much coffee makes you do?
posts.key_insight: This is a minor transitional post teasing upcoming spec-writing content, padded with a humorous customer service anecdote.

posts.summary: In this very brief post, Spolsky makes a humorous observation about Barbra Streisand dropping the 'a' from her first name in 1960, nearly 40 years before the internet existed. He jokes that the unusual spelling makes her much easier to find in search engines and on Amazon. He sarcastically credits her with 'incredible foresight' for inadvertently optimizing her name for digital searchability decades before it would matter.
key_points: Unique name spellings provide a significant advantage in digital search and discovery
Decisions made long before a technology exists can accidentally become perfectly suited to it
The internet rewards distinctiveness and uniqueness in ways no one could have predicted
money_quotes: In 1960, almost 40 years before the Internet came along, Barbra Streisand drops the "a" from her first name.
Of course, with the unusual spelling, it's much easier to find her in search engines, on Amazon, etc.
That woman has incredible foresight.
posts.key_insight: Unique, distinctive naming — even if accidental — turns out to be a powerful advantage in the age of search engines and digital discovery.

posts.summary: Joel introduces a series on functional specifications, noting that his Joel Test revealed specs as a major pain point for developers. He draws an analogy between writing specs and flossing — universally acknowledged as important but rarely practiced. The post serves as a teaser for a multi-part deep dive into functional specifications, starting with making the case for why they matter.
key_points: Writing specs was one of the biggest sore points revealed by The Joel Test
There is a universal gap between knowing specs are important and actually writing them
The series promises to cover the what, why, and how of functional specifications
money_quotes: It seems that specs are like flossing: everybody knows they should be writing them, but nobody does.
posts.key_insight: The gap between knowing you should write specs and actually doing it is one of the most common failures in software development.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky argues that writing functional specifications is essential for any non-trivial software project, yet most teams skip them. He presents three major reasons specs matter: they force you to design the program upfront in a medium where iteration is cheap (words vs. code), they serve as a single communication document for every stakeholder, and they make scheduling possible. Through a parable of two programmers — Speedy who codes first and Mr. Rogers who specs first — he demonstrates that skipping specs leads to more time spent and worse designs. He concludes that the real reason people avoid specs is not time savings but an aversion to writing, which can be overcome with practice.
key_points: Designing in a human language (specs) allows rapid iteration in minutes, whereas designing in code takes weeks and creates emotional attachment to potentially wrong solutions
Specs serve as a single source of truth that eliminates ad-hoc communication between programmers, QA, tech writers, marketing, and customers
Without specs, scheduling is impossible, and unresolved design debates accumulate as deferred decisions that threaten project delivery
The real barrier to writing specs is not time but people's reluctance to write, which is a skill that improves with practice
Specs force you to nail down every detail — including copy, edge cases, and small design decisions — that would otherwise be discovered late in development
money_quotes: Failing to write a spec is the single biggest unnecessary risk you take in a software project.
Programmers and software engineers who dive into code without writing a spec tend to think they're cool gunslingers, shooting from the hip. They're not. They are terribly unproductive.

When you design your product in a human language, it only takes a few minutes to try thinking about several possibilities, revising, and improving your design. But when you design your product in a programming language, it takes weeks to do iterative designs.
A programmer who's just spent 2 weeks writing some code is going to be quite attached to that code, no matter how wrong it is.
The QA people are really testing the program against the program rather than the program against the design, which would be, um, a little bit more useful.
Decisions in 10 minutes or less, or the next one is free.
If you notice that your company is constitutionally incapable of making decisions, you might as well close down now and return the money to the investors, because you ain't never gonna ship nothing.
Writing is a muscle. The more you write, the more you'll be able to write.
posts.key_insight: Writing specs forces design iteration in a medium where changes cost minutes instead of weeks, preventing the sunk-cost attachment that leads programmers to ship compromised architectures.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Joel introduces part two of his four-part series on writing functional specifications. He emphasizes that the most important aspect of product design is nailing down the user experience—defining the screens, how they work, and what they do. The post serves primarily as a teaser and navigation pointer to the full article.
key_points: Functional specifications should focus on defining the user experience above all else
Product design means nailing down screens, workflows, and functionality
This is part of a structured four-part series on writing specs
money_quotes: When you design a product, inside and out, the most important thing is to nail down the user experience.
What are the screens, how do they work, what do they do.
posts.key_insight: The core purpose of a functional specification is to define and nail down the user experience before building anything.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky defines what belongs in a functional specification, distinguishing it from technical specs by its exclusive focus on the user experience rather than implementation details. He walks through the essential components: a disclaimer, a single named author, realistic user scenarios, explicit nongoals, an overview, exhaustive details covering every edge case, open issues, and audience-specific side notes. He emphasizes that details are the most important part because they force decisions that directly translate to code. Finally, he argues that specs must be living documents, continuously updated throughout development rather than frozen and thrown over the wall in a waterfall process.
key_points: Functional specs describe the product entirely from the user's perspective, while technical specs cover implementation — nail down user experience before debating programming languages or architecture.
Every spec should have a single named author who takes ownership and responsibility, not a committee — group writing produces worse results and diffuses accountability.
Realistic user scenarios with fictitious but stereotypical users are essential to avoid designing products that don't match real-world usage.
Exhaustive details and edge cases are the most important part of a spec because they force decisions that someone will have to make anyway when writing the code.
Specs must be living documents updated throughout development — the waterfall approach of writing once and throwing it over the wall is why specs get a bad reputation.
money_quotes: There's no use arguing about what programming language to use before you've decided what your product is going to do.
Leave the group writing to the management consulting firms with armies of newly minted Harvard-educated graduates who need to do a ton of busywork so that they can justify their huge fees.
People should take responsibility and ownership of the things that they specify.
The more vivid and realistic the scenario, the better a job you will do designing a product for your real or imagined users.
If you don't decide, you can't write the code. The spec needs to document the decision.

You will have enough problems resolving the new issues that come up when the programmers try to implement the code, without having old open issues around that you knew about in advance and could have solved then.
Excuse me. Maybe your specs are out of date and don't reflect the product. My specs are updated frequently.
posts.key_insight: A functional spec's primary value is forcing every decision about user-facing behavior to be made explicitly and early, because each detail corresponds to code someone will have to write and a decision someone will have to make.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that the program manager role is critically undervalued at most companies. He observes that Microsoft teams with strong program managers (Excel, Windows 95, Access) shipped more successful products than teams where developers ignored weak program managers (MSN 1.0, Windows NT 1.0). The post serves as an introduction to part three of his series on writing specs, which covers the program manager role in detail.
key_points: Most companies lack the program manager role entirely, which Spolsky sees as a significant gap
Microsoft teams with strong program managers consistently produced more successful products
Weak program managers get ignored by developers, leading to worse outcomes
Program managers own the spec-writing process, making them central to product definition
money_quotes: Most companies don't even have the concept of program manager. I think that's too bad.
The groups at Microsoft with strong program managers had very successful products: Excel, Windows 95, and Access come to mind.
Other groups were run by developers who generally ignored the program managers (who weren't very good anyway, and probably deserved to be ignored), and their products were not as successful.
posts.key_insight: The quality of a team's program managers—the people who own the specs—is a strong predictor of product success, and most companies hurt themselves by not having the role at all.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky explains the origins of Microsoft's 'program manager' role and argues it's essential for successful software products. The role evolved from Charles Simonyi's failed 'master programmer' concept, where one senior developer directed junior 'code slaves.' After that system was discredited, Jabe Blumenthal reinvented the program manager as someone who owns design and specifications rather than code. Spolsky argues program managers should be technical but not coders, should never have developers reporting to them, and should rely on persuasion rather than authority. He credits strong program management for the success of products like Excel, Windows 95, and Access.
key_points: The program manager role at Microsoft evolved from a failed 'master programmer' hierarchy into a design and spec ownership position that coordinates across teams.
Program managers should be technical but are a separate career path from coding — promoting good coders into the role is a Peter Principle mistake.
Program managers should have no direct reports; their power should come from persuasion and consensus-building, not authority, which produces better products.
The role solves real problems: bridging the gap between technical elegance and marketability, writing clear specifications, and providing product direction.
Microsoft teams with strong program managers (Excel, Windows 95) shipped more successful products than teams where developers ignored or marginalized them.
money_quotes: Nobody wants to be a code slave. The system didn't work at all.

Rewarding good coders by promoting them to a different position, one that involves writing English, not C++, is a classic case of the Peter Principle: people tend to be promoted to their level of incompetence.
If any of these people had reported to me, the product wouldn't have been as good.
If I wanted people to do something, I had to convince them that it was the right thing to do.
I had no choice but to build consensus. This form of decision making was the best way to get the right thing done.
Program managers are the glue of software teams. Charisma is crucial.
All program managers need to be very technical, but they don't have to be good coders.
posts.key_insight: The best product decisions come from technically skilled program managers who own the spec but have no authority over developers, forcing them to build consensus rather than dictate.

posts.summary: Spolsky addresses the common complaint that nobody reads specifications, even when teams take the time to write them. He describes the typical bureaucratic scenario where months are spent writing boring specs that end up shelved and ignored during actual implementation. While he acknowledges that the writing process itself has value by forcing people to think through issues, the resulting cynicism from unread specs makes the effort feel wasted. He teases his fourth article on functional specifications, which focuses on how to get people to actually read specs.
key_points: The biggest problem with specs isn't writing them—it's that nobody reads them, which breeds cynicism among spec writers
At bureaucratic companies, specs become shelf decorations rather than living documents that guide implementation
The process of writing a spec has inherent value because it forces people to think through issues, even if the document itself goes unread
Specs fail not because the concept is wrong but because they are written in a mind-numbing way that discourages reading
money_quotes: The biggest complaint you'll hear from teams that do write specs is that 'nobody reads them.'
Engineers use stacks of 4-inch thick specs to build extensions to their cubicles.
Once the spec is done, it goes up on the shelf, never to be taken down again, and the product is implemented from scratch without any regard to what the spec said.
Nobody read the spec, because it was so dang mind-numbing.
The very process of writing the spec might have been a good exercise, because it forced everyone, at least, to think over the issues.
posts.key_insight: Specifications fail not because they're unnecessary but because they're written to be boring—the real challenge is making specs that people will actually want to read.

posts.summary: In the final installment of his spec-writing series, Spolsky addresses why specs go unread and offers practical rules for writing specs people will actually read. He argues that the biggest problem isn't the absence of specs but that they're written in dense, boring, academic prose that nobody wants to read. His five rules — be funny, write for human brains not compilers, keep it simple, review repeatedly, and avoid templates — all serve the goal of making specs enjoyable and understandable. The core insight is that a spec is fundamentally a piece of writing meant to be read by humans, and should be treated with the same craft as any good prose.
key_points: Specs fail not because teams don't write them, but because they're so boring and dense that nobody reads them, leading to the same arguments specs were supposed to prevent.

Programmers write specs like code — optimized for technical correctness rather than human comprehension — but specs need to be written so human brains can 'compile' them, using stories and motivation before details.
Humor, specific examples, simple language, and visual elements like screenshots make specs readable and prevent the 'brain seeping out through eye sockets' problem.
Templates are harmful because they accumulate mandatory sections that make specs daunting to write and tedious to read, turning a communication tool into a bureaucratic exercise.
Human brains understand information differently from computers: they need context, narrative, and motivation before details, not top-down formal definitions.
money_quotes: The very process of writing the spec might have been a good exercise, because it forced everyone, at least, to think over the issues. But the fact that the spec was shelved (unread and unloved) when it was completed makes people feel like it was all a bunch of work for naught.
As a spec-writer, you have to trick people into reading your stuff.
Everybody has funny ideas all the time, they just self-censor them because they think that it's 'unprofessional.' Feh. Sometimes you have to break the rules.
Writing a spec is like writing code for a brain to execute.
Humans don't want to have to decode something, they just want to read it in order and understand it.
Our brains have evolved to understand stories.

People use words like 'utilize' because they think that 'use' looks unprofessional. Any time somebody tells you that you shouldn't do something because it's 'unprofessional,' you know that they've run out of real arguments.
Avoid the temptation to make a standard template for specs. Have you ever heard of a professor passing out templates for students to write their college papers?
posts.key_insight: A spec is only as good as the number of people who actually read it, so writing specs is fundamentally a communication challenge that demands clear, entertaining prose rather than formal technical correctness.

posts.summary: This is a very brief meta-post where Joel notices a spike in his blog's readership and investigates the source. He discovers that The Motley Fool linked to his site, explaining the traffic increase. The post is essentially a public note-to-self about referral traffic.
key_points: Joel tracked his readership using Userland's 'most read sites' ranking
A link from The Motley Fool drove a significant traffic spike to Joel on Software
Early blogging culture involved publicly acknowledging and investigating inbound links
money_quotes: Somebody must be linking to this site; my readership is up dramatically since yesterday.
posts.key_insight: A brief housekeeping post reflecting the early blogosphere's culture of tracking and acknowledging referral traffic sources.

posts.summary: This brief post contains two short items. First, Joel notes that when Atomz.com started charging $100 for three months of search service, he switched to Google in 20 minutes, illustrating how easily replaceable commodity services are when free alternatives exist. Second, he makes a tongue-in-cheek joke connecting a new hire at Fog Creek Software with Nortel's stock plunge, humorously implying causation.
key_points: Commodity web services with free alternatives have zero pricing power — users will switch instantly rather than pay
Google's free search offering made paid search providers like Atomz immediately obsolete
Joel uses humor to celebrate new hires and build Fog Creek's brand as a desirable employer
money_quotes: When Atomz asked me to start paying ($100 for three months), it took me 20 minutes to switch over to Google. Kiss them goodbye.
posts.key_insight: When a free, superior alternative exists, charging for a commodity service is a death sentence — users will leave in minutes.

posts.summary: Spolsky examines the business model of offering free services with the hope of converting users to paid plans through 'stealth lock-in.' He uses his experience with Atomz.com's search service as a case study, noting they offered free indexing up to 500 pages before pushing expensive upgrades. He argues that the success of this model depends entirely on how much switching cost the service creates. Companies with high lock-in like email providers can succeed, but commoditized services with no switching costs are doomed to fail.
key_points: Free-to-premium business models depend on lock-in: how costly it is for users to switch away determines whether the model works
Atomz.com's search indexing service had virtually no lock-in, making their $100/quarter upgrade easy to reject by switching to Google in 20 minutes
Email services like Juno have genuine lock-in because changing email addresses has real social costs (business cards, contacts)
Online storage companies offering free space with paid upgrades have zero lock-in due to low barriers to entry and intense competition
money_quotes: I hate being tricked into upgrading from a 'free' service to a 'premium' service.
Atomz.com is hoping to achieve what I call 'stealth lock-in'. They don't even want to you realize that you're becoming dependent on a service.
In the future, when you're hooked, they can start charging you a bundle, and you'll pay them just to avoid the cost of switching.
The question here is: how much lock-in do these companies really have? That's what is going to determine whether their plans work or fail.
Besides the fact that there is almost no barrier to entry, incredible competition, and the service is stupid to begin with, these companies are simply not going to succeed, period.
Lock-in? Well, I'm afraid not. It took me 20 minutes to switch over to Google. Kiss them goodbye.
posts.key_insight: A free-to-premium business model only works when the service creates genuine switching costs; without real lock-in, users will simply leave when asked to pay.

posts.summary: Joel critiques the Enigma Browser, a supposed lightweight web browser that claims to be only 33K in size. He exposes that it simply embeds the Internet Explorer ActiveX control, meaning it still relies entirely on IE's rendering engine. He points out that IE itself is only 59K wrapping the same control, making Enigma's size claims meaningless. The browser also requires the Visual Basic runtime (1.32 MB) and other components, further undermining its 'lightweight' marketing. Joel is incredulous that Dr. Dobb's Journal reported on it without catching the deception.
key_points: Enigma Browser's 33K size is misleading because it simply wraps the Internet Explorer ActiveX control, which does all the actual work
Internet Explorer itself is only a 59K wrapper around the same control, so Enigma saves a trivial 26K while removing features
The browser requires the Visual Basic runtime (1.32 MB) and additional components, making its total footprint larger than claimed
The Enigma author's criticism of IE as 'clunky and inefficient' is hollow since Enigma literally uses IE under the hood
Joel faults Dr. Dobb's Journal for failing to scrutinize an obviously misleading technical claim
money_quotes: These guys have written a 'web browser' which they say is only 33K. But it's really nothing than a little program which embeds the Microsoft Internet Explorer control.
The joke is, Internet Explorer itself is nothing but a 59K program that embeds the Internet Explorer control. Wooooo, they saved 26K.
When you look closely at Enigma, you still need Internet Explorer, you're still using Internet Explorer, but you don't get any of the features.
Come on, this is lame. I can't believe nobody at Dr. Dobbs Journal caught them on this lie.
posts.key_insight: Claiming software is lightweight by offloading all functionality to an embedded system component is dishonest accounting, not engineering achievement.

posts.summary: Spolsky responds to Jakob Nielsen's claim that Flash is '99% bad,' partially agreeing that Flash reduces usability but pushing back on Nielsen's absolutist stance. He argues that usability, while important, is not always the top priority and shouldn't be treated as the only metric that matters. Using a vivid analogy about singles bars, he illustrates that optimizing purely for usability can strip away the qualities that actually attract people.
key_points: Flash does reduce usability, confirming Nielsen's technical criticism
Usability is important but not the supreme design value that trumps all others
Optimizing purely for usability can produce sterile experiences that nobody wants
Appeal, atmosphere, and fun are legitimate design goals that sometimes conflict with usability
money_quotes: Flash always reduces usability.
Every time I read Jakob Nielsen, I get this feeling that he really doesn't appreciate that usability is not the most important thing on earth.
Usability is simply not everyone's number one priority, nor should it be.
You get the feeling that if Mr Nielsen designed a singles bar, it would be well lit, clean, with giant menus printed in Arial 14 point, and you'd never have to wait to get a drink.
But nobody would go there, they would all be at Coyote Ugly Saloon pouring beer on each other.
posts.key_insight: Usability is necessary but not sufficient — designing purely for usability while ignoring appeal and experience produces products nobody actually wants to use.

posts.summary: This is a brief administrative post where Joel announces the creation of a discussion group for his blog. He seeds the first discussion topic with a provocative Bill Gates quote from the New York Times about the misguided nature of donating computers to impoverished African villages instead of addressing basic health and survival needs. The post is essentially a launch announcement paired with a conversation starter rather than an essay.
key_points: Joel launched a discussion forum for his blog hosted on Philip Greenspun's platform
The forum was open to all without requiring membership
He chose a Bill Gates quote about technology vs. poverty as the inaugural discussion topic, highlighting the disconnect between tech solutions and basic human needs
money_quotes: The mothers are going to walk right up to that computer and say, My children are dying, what can you do?
They're not going to sit there and like, browse eBay or something. What they want is for their children to live.
Do you really have to put in computers to figure that out?
posts.key_insight: Technology solutions are meaningless when people's basic survival needs aren't met first.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky pushes back against Steve McConnell's call for government-type certification of software engineers, as proposed in McConnell's book 'After the Gold Rush.' While acknowledging McConnell's previous work as excellent, Spolsky finds the certification idea deeply misguided. He cites Tom DeMarco's critique that certification primarily benefits the certifiers, not society. Spolsky drives his point home by noting that Shawn Fanning, who created Napster at 18, would never have been certified under such a scheme. He concludes bluntly: don't buy the book.
key_points: Government-style certification of programmers would serve the interests of certifiers more than the profession or society
Some of the most impactful software has been written by people who would never pass formal certification (e.g., 18-year-old Shawn Fanning writing Napster)
Even respected authors like McConnell can advocate for fundamentally wrong-headed ideas
Professionalism in software engineering cannot be reduced to credentialing
money_quotes: When, not if. Yikes.
Certification is not something we implement for the benefit of the society but for the benefit of the certifiers.
Would 18-year-old Shawn Fanning, who wrote Napster and changed the world, have been certified?
Don't Buy 'After the Gold Rush.' It's wrong.
The new Robert Jordan book is about to ship. Buy that instead.
posts.key_insight: Formal certification of programmers would gate-keep the profession in ways that exclude the unconventional talent responsible for some of software's most transformative innovations.

posts.summary: This is a brief promotional announcement for Fog Creek Software's consulting services. Joel announces that Fog Creek is expanding and invites readers to consider hiring them for custom software development. The post is essentially an advertisement rather than a substantive blog entry.
key_points: Fog Creek Software was expanding its business in late 2000
The company offered custom software development consulting services
Joel used his blog as a marketing channel for his company
money_quotes: From the blatant self-promotion department: Fog Creek Software is expanding!
posts.key_insight: A 26-word self-promotional note announcing Fog Creek Software's expansion and consulting availability, notable mainly as a historical marker of the company's early growth.

posts.summary: Spolsky responds to Phil Greenspun's article on managing software engineers, agreeing with most of it but pushing back on the glorification of 70-hour work weeks. He argues that nice office environments attract top talent not because programmers want to live at work, but because they prefer employers who treat them well. He points to Netscape's burnout culture as evidence that death marches destroy companies. The post also includes personal updates about new hardware purchases and a heartfelt reflection on the privilege of voting.
key_points: Nice office environments work as a recruiting tool because top programmers choose employers who treat them well over competitors who 'treat them like veal'
70-hour work weeks create short-term burnout companies — they may look successful initially but destroy companies over time, as Netscape demonstrated
Greenspun's article on managing engineers is 'almost right' but fatally wrong on its enthusiasm for overwork
Spolsky expresses deep personal gratitude for democratic participation, framing voting rights through a historical lens as a Jewish American
money_quotes: It works because if you make a nice environment, the best programmers will want to work for you, not for your competitors who treat them like veal.
Phil thinks it's great when people work 70 hour weeks. I think that's a way to make a short term burnout company.
Look at Netscape, a company that was famous for deathmarch after deathmarch, and for burning out individual programmers, and indeed for burning out the whole damn company.
Standing in line with a bunch of fellow citizens doing our civic duty almost makes me tear up.
Democracy is something we take for granted, but… think of it from a historical point of view. They let me — a Jew — vote! For 2000 years that was unheard of.
posts.key_insight: Creating a great work environment attracts top talent not by making people want to live at work, but by signaling that you value their wellbeing — and 70-hour weeks signal the opposite.

posts.summary: Spolsky comments on the 2000 US presidential election's Florida ballot controversy, arguing it was essentially a usability bug that may have determined the presidency. He connects the confusing butterfly ballot design to his own principle that non-trivial program models won't match user expectations. He also criticizes the Florida Department of State's website for buckling under election-night traffic due to unnecessary GIFs and a Java applet, when simple optimization could have solved the problem.
key_points: The Florida butterfly ballot is a real-world example of how UI design failures can have enormous consequences beyond software
When you do something even slightly complicated, users will assume you did the simple thing, leading to errors
Florida's election website failed under load due to gratuitous visual elements that any competent web developer would have removed
money_quotes: Is Bush going to actually be the next president because of a usability bug?
If your program model is nontrivial, it's probably not the user model.
Any time you do something even a little bit complicated, some part of your user population is going to be confused, because they thought that you were doing the simple thing.
Obviously, usability matters for more than just web sites.
They've got 4 or five stupid GIFs and a gratuitous scrolling Java applet on that damn page. No wonder you can't handle the load.
posts.key_insight: Usability failures aren't just software annoyances — they can have real-world consequences as serious as determining a presidential election.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares three brief observations: praise for Transmeta's CPU design philosophy of embracing x86 compatibility and tackling hard problems rather than building to flip, a humorous aside about the misery of Denver airport and United Airlines, and commentary on corporate rebranding—specifically Borland's pointless name change to Inprise and back. He connects these threads with a broader theme about sustainable competitive advantage versus short-term thinking, and predicts that companies with poor reputations will increasingly resort to name changes.
key_points: Transmeta was right to accept that the era of inventing new instruction sets was over—x86 compatibility is non-negotiable for PC CPUs
Companies that tackle genuinely hard, long-term problems (like Transmeta challenging Intel) are more admirable than 'build to flip' dot-coms with no sustainable competitive advantage
Corporate name changes only make sense when the existing brand has become a liability due to poor reputation, as with ValuJet becoming AirTran
Many dot-com failures stemmed from being built in three months with no barriers to entry or sustainable advantage
money_quotes: If you want to design a CPU for PCs, and you want people to use it, you no longer have the luxury of inventing your own instruction set. You have to run x86 code. Period.
This is not a 'build to flip' company. They don't mind going after Intel, which is very brave of them.

So many of the dot coms that are blowing up right now were built in about three months and never had any sustainable competitive advantage, let alone barriers to entry, and it's no wonder that they're doing so badly.
When I say 'I like Transmeta,' I don't mean as an investment. Intel is probably going to squash them like a bug without even doing anything.
The only time a company would want to change its name, from something people recognize to something completely new, would be if it had such low brand equity that the old name was a liability.

You'll see a lot of name changes among local phone companies, cable television companies, and airlines over the next few years, as the shakeout for years of abysmal customer service starts to catch up with them.
posts.key_insight: Companies that invest in solving genuinely hard problems build sustainable competitive advantages, while those built quickly without barriers to entry—whether dot-coms or rebranded corporations—are destined to fail or endlessly reinvent themselves.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post where Joel Spolsky shares that he was quoted in the French newspaper Le Monde. The quote, translated from French, says 'Bush will really end up being the first president elected thanks to a usability bug.' This references the 2000 US presidential election controversy involving the confusing butterfly ballot design in Palm Beach County, Florida. Joel frames the election outcome as fundamentally a UI design failure.
key_points: The 2000 US presidential election outcome was influenced by poor ballot design — a usability problem
Joel connects software UI design principles to real-world consequences far beyond technology
The butterfly ballot is characterized as a 'bug' rather than a political issue, reframing the controversy through a technologist's lens
money_quotes: Bush will really end up being the first president elected thanks to a 'usability bug'
posts.key_insight: Bad UI design can have consequences far beyond software — even deciding a presidential election.

posts.summary: Joel critiques Dell's website for forcing users into arbitrary buyer categories (home, small business, large business) that don't match how customers actually think about purchasing computers. He walks through the frustrating experience of trying to find a small Linux server on Dell's site, encountering dead ends, irrelevant Air Force pages, and useless press releases. He contrasts Dell's approach with Apple and IBM's hardware pages, which organize by product rather than customer segment. The core argument is that Dell organized its website around its internal corporate structure rather than around what users actually want to do.
key_points: Dell's website forces users to self-categorize into buyer segments (home, small business, large business) that are meaningless to the customer and likely exist to enable price discrimination
Finding specific configurations like a small form factor Linux machine is nearly impossible because there's no way to search or filter by OS or form factor
Dell's site search is broken — searching for 'Linux' leads to a US Air Force procurement page rather than Linux-compatible products
Apple and IBM demonstrate better approaches by organizing around products and letting users browse by what they want to buy, not who they are
Companies should not mirror their internal org chart onto their customer-facing website — the program model must match the user model
money_quotes: I want a PC. What difference does it make whether I'm a home buyer or a small business buyer?
I suspect that they are asking me this because they want to charge businesses more than homes, and large businesses even more. To defeat their system, I choose 'home.'
Thanks, guys, I'm trying to buy a computer here.

If Dell is really 'building one for me,' I don't see why there are so many confusing product lines and why Dell insists on putting their customers into little boxes based on their arbitrary distinctions.
I'm sure it made sense for Michael Dell to organize his executives according to target markets, but it makes no sense to organize the web site this way.
The first principle of usability says that you have to bring the program model in line with the user model. Figure out what the user expects to see on the first page, and make that the first page.
posts.key_insight: Companies should organize their websites around what users want to accomplish, not around their own internal corporate structure — the program model must match the user model, not the org chart.

posts.summary: This is a brief meta-post where Joel Spolsky lists the 44 countries from which Joel on Software has subscribers. It serves as a milestone marker celebrating the blog's international reach relatively early in its history. There is no argument or analysis—it is purely a factual enumeration. The post implicitly signals pride in the blog's growing global audience.
key_points: Joel on Software had subscribers in 44 countries as of November 2000, demonstrating early global reach for a programming blog.
The list spans every inhabited continent, from Western Samoa to Iceland to Botswana.
The post functions as a brief community milestone rather than a substantive essay.
money_quotes: Joel on Software has subscribers in 44 countries.
posts.key_insight: Even in 2000, a niche English-language software blog could attract a genuinely global readership across 44 countries.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post consisting of a single rhetorical survey question directed at readers, implying that Netscape 6 shipped with obvious, critical bugs. Joel uses the format of a reader poll to make a pointed critique of Netscape 6's quality at launch, suggesting that show-stopper bugs were so prevalent they could be found almost immediately.
key_points: Netscape 6 launched with easily discoverable critical bugs
The rhetorical framing ('how long') implies the bugs were found nearly instantly by anyone who used it
Joel uses humor and audience participation to make his quality critique more devastating than a straightforward review
money_quotes: How long did it take you to find the first 3 show-stopper bugs in Netscape 6?
posts.key_insight: When show-stopper bugs are so obvious that finding three is a matter of minutes, the product should not have shipped.

posts.summary: This is a very brief post where Joel notes he has returned from a weekend in Boston and observes that discussion around Netscape 6 has been overwhelmingly negative. He acknowledges the bias in the feedback, since he had specifically asked for bug reports, which naturally skews responses toward complaints.
key_points: Public reception of Netscape 6 was overwhelmingly negative
Soliciting bug reports creates selection bias toward negative feedback
Joel shows awareness that the framing of a question influences the responses you get
money_quotes: The discussion on Netscape 6 seems to be pretty universally negative.
I know, I know, I asked for bug reports, so it's not exactly fair.
posts.key_insight: Asking for bug reports inherently biases feedback toward negativity, making it an unreliable gauge of overall reception.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky covers several topics in this blog update: he introduces his new article on bug tracking, commenting that most teams fail at this basic practice. He takes a victory lap over Netscape 6's poor reception, arguing it validates his earlier warnings about their rewrite decision. He also shares a link to The Autodesk Files, a bootstrapping story from the pre-VC era, and describes his experience installing Mailman for mailing list management, taking a jab at open source software's hidden costs.
key_points: Bug tracking databases are a hallmark of good software teams, yet most programmers wrongly believe they can remember all their bugs or track them on post-it notes
Netscape 6's failures serve as a case study for ignoring sound software development practices, particularly the dangers of full rewrites
Open source software has hidden costs in time and effort that undermine its 'free' label
The Autodesk Files illustrates how major software companies were bootstrapped before venture capital dominated the industry
money_quotes: I was born with only two bug-storing-slots in my brain. At any given time, I can only remember two bugs.
If you ask me to remember three, one of them will fall on the floor and get swept under the bed with the dust bunnies, who will eat it.
Keeping a database of bugs is one of the hallmarks of a good software team. I never cease to be amazed at how few teams are actually doing this.
One of the biggest incorrect facts that programmers consistently seem to believe is that they can remember all their bugs or keep them on post-it notes.
Netscape 6 is practically an advertisement for what happens if you don't read Joel on Software and do what I tell you.
Like most open source software, "it's only free if your time is worthless."
posts.key_insight: Even the most basic software engineering practices like bug tracking are widely neglected, and ignoring established development wisdom — whether about rewrites or process discipline — leads to predictable failures.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky uses the release of Netscape 6.0 as a case study in software development mistakes, arguing it validates several points he's made on his blog. He criticizes Netscape's decision to rewrite their browser from scratch, which cost them three years and resulted in a buggier product. The reimplementation of native UI widgets broke countless user expectations around keyboard shortcuts, context menus, and accessibility. He argues the release demonstrates failures in testing, scheduling, and UI consistency. The only bright spot he identifies is Netscape's improved Sidebar metaphor compared to Outlook's confusing equivalent.
key_points: Rewriting code from scratch cost Netscape three years and produced a buggier codebase than the original, disproving the assumption that rewrites yield cleaner code
Reimplementing native UI widgets for cross-platform compatibility broke fundamental user expectations like right-click menus, keyboard shortcuts, and accessibility settings
Old code doesn't rust — it improves over time as bugs are fixed, and ugly-looking code often encodes years of hard-won edge case handling
Without a prioritized schedule, programmers gravitate toward fun features like skinnable themes instead of important ones like standards compliance
Microsoft learned the lesson of UI consistency years earlier by making Excel emulate Lotus 123 keystrokes and Word emulate WordPerfect
money_quotes: This one decision cost Netscape 3 years. That's three years in which the company couldn't add new features, couldn't respond to the competitive threads from Internet Explorer, and had to sit on their hands while Microsoft completely ate their lunch.
The sheer volume of bugs, it seems, proves that rewriting code from scratch does not make for a better code base, it makes it worse.
Old code doesn't rust, it gets better, as bugs are fixed.
It had taken 3 years of tuning to get code that could read the 60 different types of FTP servers, those 5000 lines of code may have looked ugly, but at least they worked.
Without a prioritized schedule, programmers will do the fun features first, not the important ones, where 'fun' is very loosely defined.
For vision-impaired users who need to set fonts very large or use high-contrast color schemes, it's a reason not to use Netscape.
posts.key_insight: Netscape 6.0's failure demonstrates that rewriting software from scratch destroys accumulated bug fixes and institutional knowledge, while reimplementing platform-native UI widgets alienates users by breaking the consistency expectations they've built over years of use.

posts.summary: Joel announces the launch of FogBUGZ, Fog Creek's first product—a bug tracking tool designed around usability and sociology rather than feature bloat. He argues that most bug tracking software fails not because of missing features but because it's too cumbersome for teams to actually use. The key insight is that reducing friction by even 10% can double adoption, so FogBUGZ was designed with minimal required fields and a viral adoption model. Rather than requiring top-down organizational buy-in, one person can start using it and it naturally spreads to the whole team. He briefly praises Google's new navigation bar beta as well.
key_points: Many development teams buy bug tracking software but never actually use it, due to usability and sociology problems rather than missing features
Making a task 10% easier can double the number of people who accomplish it—removing two fields from a signup page dramatically increased signups
FogBUGZ was designed to be viral: one person starts using it as a to-do list, then it organically spreads to the whole team without requiring top-down mandates
Overengineered 'defects management' tools with too many fields discourage bug reporting and push people toward workarounds like email and post-it notes
Change is hard—successful tools work with human behavior rather than trying to force new workflows on entire teams at once
money_quotes: Lots of companies spend hundreds of thousands of dollars on overengineered 'defects management' tools which nobody ever uses.
Usability theory holds that if you make a task 10% easier, you double the number of people that can accomplish it.
I've always felt that if you can make it 10% easier to fill in a bug report, you'll get twice as many bug reports.
The more fields you have to fill out, the less likely you are to use the bug database to report a bug, and the more likely you are to work around the system by sending email or making a post-it note.
It's difficult to get people to learn new habits and new ways of working, and it's impossible to inflict a new style of working on an existing group of developers all at once.
FogBUGZ reflects our attention to usability and sociology, not just technological 'gee whiz' gadgetry.
posts.key_insight: Bug tracking tools fail not from lack of features but from too much friction—reducing barriers to entry and designing for viral, bottom-up adoption matters far more than comprehensive functionality.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces that Fog Creek Software, being too small to afford any sales staff, will rely on web promotion and a referral program to sell consulting services. He offers a 5% referral fee (up to $5,000) to anyone who connects Fog Creek with a paying client. The post is a characteristically self-deprecating look at bootstrapping a tiny software company, acknowledging the financial constraints while still pushing forward with creative solutions.
key_points: Fog Creek Software is too small to afford any dedicated sales capacity, not even a 'sales cat'
Joel plans to use his blog and website as the primary marketing channel for consulting services
A referral program offering 5% of revenue (up to $5,000) is introduced as a grassroots sales strategy
The post candidly reveals the bootstrapping realities: the lawyer exists only because insurance requires one
money_quotes: Fog Creek Software can't afford a sales team, or even a sales person, or in fact, a sales cat.
I'll promote them on my web site! Surely somebody out there wants to hire smart programmers and architects.
You can afford a lawyer? No, but we couldn't afford to buy errors and omissions insurance if we didn't have a lawyer, so it's the lesser of two costs.
Start thinking of all your friends who are CTOs at wealthy startups and Fortune 500 corporations that need a small but crack team of software wizards.
posts.key_insight: When you can't afford traditional sales, turning your audience into your sales force through referral incentives and content marketing is a scrappy but effective bootstrapping strategy.

posts.summary: This is a brief promotional announcement from Joel Spolsky seeking referrals for potential clients of Fog Creek Software. The post offers up to $5,000 as a finder's fee for anyone who refers a client that hires the company. At only 17 words of content, it functions as a classified ad rather than a blog post. It reflects the early bootstrapping phase of Fog Creek Software when Joel was actively seeking consulting clients to sustain the business.
key_points: Fog Creek Software was actively seeking new clients through referral incentives during its early days
The $5,000 finder's fee suggests the company was pursuing substantial consulting contracts
Joel used his blog as a direct marketing channel for his business, not just for thought leadership
money_quotes: Know somebody who might hire Fog Creek Software? We'll pay up to $5000 as a finder's fee.
posts.key_insight: In its early days, Fog Creek Software relied on referral-based business development, using Joel's blog as a direct channel to find consulting clients.

posts.summary: Joel announces that his web series 'UI Design for Programmers' is being published as a print book by Apress. The print version will include expanded content with five bonus chapters, additional illustrations, and a foreword by Dave Winer. He expresses enthusiasm for the publisher Apress, noting that its founder Gary Cornell has deep programming knowledge.
key_points: Joel's 'UI Design for Programmers' web series is being adapted into a print book with significant additional content
Joel values publishers whose leaders have genuine technical expertise, citing Apress founder Gary Cornell as a rare example
The book will feature a foreword by Dave Winer, a notable figure in the blogging and software world
money_quotes: the founder, Gary Cornell, actually knows more about programming than I do, a rare trait in a computer books publisher.
posts.key_insight: Joel values technical credibility in publishing, preferring publishers whose leadership genuinely understands programming.

posts.summary: This is not a traditional blog post but a brief notice announcing that Joel on Software is closed for the day in observance of World AIDS Day and Day Without Art/Weblogs on November 30, 2000. Joel links to three related initiatives and suspends his blog in solidarity. The post consists of only 17 words of original content plus three hyperlinks.
key_points: Joel participated in 'Day Without Weblogs,' a coordinated blogger protest/awareness campaign for World AIDS Day
The post demonstrates early blogger culture and collective action in the year 2000
Joel chose solidarity and silence over content creation as a form of awareness-raising
money_quotes: Joel on Software is closed for the day.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the most meaningful thing a writer can do is choose not to write.

posts.summary: Spolsky recounts his experience at Viacom in 1995 struggling with Illustra, an expensive proprietary database tool whose crashes couldn't be debugged without source code access. This experience taught him that for mission-critical systems, you should either use tools you deeply trust or tools you can fix yourself. He applies this lesson to FogBUGZ by changing its license to allow customers to view and modify the source code, while still charging for the software. He argues this hybrid approach — commercial software with source access — gives customers the safety net of open source without requiring his small company to give up its revenue model.
key_points: When a critical proprietary tool fails and you lack source access, you're completely helpless — this is an unacceptable risk for mission-critical systems
A good architect only uses tools that can either be trusted (proven reliable) or fixed (source available) — anything else is too risky to bet your career on
For your most important differentiating work, use tools one level lower in abstraction than ideal so you maintain control over what matters most
Open source's greatest virtue isn't price but the ability to debug and fix problems yourself when things go wrong
Commercial software can adopt key benefits of open source (source access, accepting patches) without fully embracing the open source model
money_quotes: A good architect only uses tools that can either be trusted, or that can be fixed.
Without the source code, you are up the proverbial tata without a tutu.
For your most important, mission critical stuff, you have to use a tool that is one level lower in abstraction than ideal.
You can bet your career on the Oracle DBMS, because it just works and everybody knows it. And you can bet your career on Berkeley DB, because if it screws up, you go into the source code and fix it.
There are very few magical, trade-secret algorithms in any software.
At least, if you had the source code, if Illustra crashes, well, it falls into your lap in the debugger and you can try to fix the bug.
You probably don't want to bet your career on a non-open-source, not-well-known tool.
We don't claim to be open source but we can make sure that FogBUGZ is a safe decision to make, by adopting two or three nice features from the open source world.
posts.key_insight: A good software architect only bets on tools that are either proven trustworthy or come with source code that can be fixed — anything else is an unacceptable career risk.

posts.summary: This short blog post covers three unrelated topics from Joel's daily work life at Fog Creek Software. He enthusiastically recommends SGI's 1600x1024 flat-screen monitor, now available at a reduced price of $1500. He shares an amusing anecdote about a coworker typing on a 1940s Smith-Corona typewriter found in the office closet. Finally, he discusses the logistical challenges of moving Fog Creek to a new office, including a two-week gap without office space and the technical difficulty of migrating live servers without downtime.
key_points: SGI's high-end flat-screen monitor dropped from $2995 to $1500, making it accessible to enthusiasts
The humorous contrast between cutting-edge flat screens and a 1940s typewriter highlights the range of technology in their office
Moving a live web server, mail server, and Mailman installation to a new IP without downtime is a significant technical challenge
Fog Creek strategically timed their office move to coincide with the holiday period to minimize disruption
money_quotes: Once you have one of these, you can just drop dead on the spot, because it can only go downhill from there.
Not only doesn't this thing have Windows, but there's not even a decent web browser that runs on it.

Meanwhile I'm figuring out all the tricks you need to do to move a 'live' web server, mail server, and (this is the hard part) Mailman installation to a new machine with a different IP address without downtime.
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted snapshot of early-2000s startup life at Fog Creek, mixing hardware enthusiasm, office humor, and the unglamorous operational realities of running a small software company.

posts.summary: This is a very brief follow-up post noting that Dell fixed their website search engine after Joel's earlier complaint, while Starbucks still hasn't addressed his gripe about their use of grease pencils for marking cups. Joel uses the contrast to highlight the difference between companies that respond to usability criticism and those that don't.
key_points: Dell responded to public criticism by fixing their search engine within weeks
Starbucks continued using grease pencils despite Joel's earlier complaint about the practice
Public criticism from influential voices can sometimes drive rapid change in tech companies
money_quotes: So why is Starbucks still using their damned grease pencils?
posts.key_insight: Tech companies may respond quickly to public usability complaints, but entrenched offline processes are far harder to change.

posts.summary: Joel shares a brief personal update about his day spent on system administration tasks at Fog Creek Software. He's migrating servers, moving email systems, and dealing with the frustrations of command-line configuration. He also announces that his UI book manuscript has been submitted to the publisher, leaving him with a rare free weekend.
key_points: System administration work consumes significant time even for software company founders, often involving tedious trial-and-error with configuration tools
Fog Creek was in a period of infrastructure migration, moving FogBUGZ demos and email to new servers in preparation for an office move
Joel's book 'UI for Programmers' (later published as 'User Interface Design for Programmers') reached the publisher and tech reviewer stage
money_quotes: I'd like to think that I'm learning things, but really I'm just spending a lot of time trying different command-line arguments to ./configure.
I have nothing to do this weekend!
posts.key_insight: Even software company founders spend unglamorous days wrestling with server administration and ./configure scripts rather than building products.

posts.summary: Spolsky highlights the CueCat, a barcode reader device by Digital Convergence, as a spectacular example of dot-com era waste. He notes that Wired magazine has finally caught up to what was already obvious: nobody wants the product. Despite 265 employees and an estimated $190 million in venture capital, not a single person has written to defend the device. Spolsky marvels at how an entire company could fail to notice they were building something with zero demand.
key_points: The CueCat was a consumer barcode reader that no one wanted, yet the company employed 265 people and raised $190M in VC funding
Not a single person wrote in to defend the product, validating Spolsky's earlier critique — the only positive use was as a hacked cheap barcode reader
The disconnect between massive investment and zero consumer demand exemplifies dot-com bubble excess
Large organizations can develop collective blindness to the fundamental viability of their own product
money_quotes: Digital Convergence's completely moronic ":CueCat" is simply the most expensive, most meritless idea since Ishtar.
This company now has 265 employees, all for a product that nobody wants.
Which makes me wonder how not one of those 265 geniuses in Dallas noticed that their company was working on a product which not a single person wants.
265 people, assuming a conservative cost of, say, $50,000 each, costs over $13m a year just in salary.
The rumors are that the company raised $190,000,000 in VC on this little boondoggle.
I think a few investors are going to be very, very pissed when the whole thing goes up in smoke.
posts.key_insight: A company can burn through hundreds of millions of dollars building a product that literally no one wants, and somehow 265 employees can all fail to notice.

posts.summary: Joel shares two brief updates: his frustration with internet providers who can't even send email (preferring to fax quotes), and his excitement about a new wireless modem that lets him post from a cafe. The irony of an internet provider unable to use email drives his purchasing decision away from Qwest. He highlights SAVVIS as the likely winner for their office T1 line. The post captures a moment in early 2000s tech where wireless internet access from a cafe felt genuinely novel.
key_points: Internet providers soliciting T1 business couldn't manage to send a quote via email, insisting on fax instead
Joel uses a company's inability to use its own product category as a disqualifying criterion
Early wireless broadband (128kbps Ricochet modem) represented an exciting new frontier of mobile connectivity
money_quotes: Fax? Hello, people, you're Internet providers. Why not email me a quote?
I don't know why I would want to buy T1 access from an Internet company that doesn't know how to send an email.
posts.key_insight: If a company can't competently use the very product it sells, that tells you everything you need to know about whether to buy from them.

posts.summary: Joel reviews the Ricochet wireless modem, a 128kbps wireless internet service available in Manhattan for $80/month. He finds the speed impressive for its era, comparable to dual-channel ISDN, though signal strength varies by location. The service works well near major avenues but poorly behind thick masonry. He considers equipping his entire team with the modems as a temporary solution while waiting for a T1 line installation. The post ends with a sobering postscript: Ricochet shut down in 2001 after spending $500 million and attracting only 51,000 subscribers.
key_points: The Ricochet wireless modem delivered genuine 128kbps speeds in Manhattan, comparable to dual-channel ISDN, at a time when wireless internet was expensive and slow
The new economy created absurd outsourcing chains: Juno outsourced hardware to Metricom, Metricom outsourced billing to Juno, and Juno outsourced customer service to Canada
Installation was almost easy but required insider knowledge from Joel's time as a Juno programmer to resolve an obscure account-activation quirk
Coverage was the fundamental limitation — the service only worked in downtown areas of 10 cities, using short-range transmitters mounted on light poles
The business was catastrophically unviable: $500 million spent, 51,000 subscribers, requiring roughly $30,000 per subscriber to break even
money_quotes: The one thing about the new economy is that nobody wants to get their hands dirty.
Juno doesn't want to get their hands dirty with messy hardware and infrastructure, and Metricom doesn't want to get their hands dirty with customer service and billing ('ew').
I think that if I didn't have this 'inside information' I would have been pretty frustrated, and I would have had to call Juno tech support.
They spent $500,000,000 and went out of business owing almost a billion dollars, with 51,000 subscribers.
They would have had to make about $30,000 per subscriber for the business idea to have worked.
In the past couple of years, the places where I want wireless access are not downtowns.
posts.key_insight: A technically impressive product can still fail catastrophically when the infrastructure costs vastly outstrip the addressable market — Ricochet needed $30,000 per subscriber to survive but charged $80/month.

posts.summary: Joel shares his excitement about Dave Winer writing a foreword for his book, expressing deep gratitude. He then humorously describes his day as a battle with his copy editor over comma placement, illustrating the back-and-forth with Word's revision tracking feature.
key_points: Dave Winer wrote a foreword for Joel's book that Joel considers perfect
Joel is in the editing phase of book publication, dealing with copy editing
The comedic tug-of-war over commas illustrates the tension between author voice and editorial convention
money_quotes: My copy editor keeps deleting my commas. I keep putting them back.
That's the theme of the day!
posts.key_insight: Even in professional writing, the editing process becomes a humorous battle of wills between author and copy editor over minor stylistic choices like comma placement.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post where Joel Spolsky puts out a call on behalf of his publisher seeking an author to write a book about programming for accessibility. He asks qualified or interested people to contact him so he can connect them with the publisher. At just 34 words, this is essentially a classified ad rather than a substantive blog post.
key_points: Joel's publisher was actively seeking an author for a book on accessibility programming, suggesting industry interest in the topic circa 2000
Joel used his blog's readership as a recruiting channel for his publisher, demonstrating the platform's reach even in its early days
The post reflects that accessibility in software was recognized as a topic deserving dedicated book-length treatment at the turn of the millennium
money_quotes: My publisher is looking for someone to write a book about programming for accessibility.
posts.key_insight: Even in 2000, accessibility in software development was recognized as an important enough topic to warrant a dedicated book, though finding qualified authors proved challenging enough to require a public call.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief personal post where Joel Spolsky notes that it is snowing in New York City and shares a link to some photos he took. There is no substantive argument or technical content. It is essentially a micro-blog entry from the early days of his blog.
key_points: Joel documents a snow day in New York City with photos
The post reflects the early blogging era when personal micro-updates were common
money_quotes: It's snowing today in New York City.
posts.key_insight: This is a trivial personal update with no technical or professional insight, characteristic of early-2000s blogging culture.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post where Spolsky references his earlier advice against signing non-compete agreements and provides a link to an external example illustrating why. The post serves as a follow-up validation of his prior stance, using a real-world case as evidence. It contains no substantive argument of its own beyond connecting these two references.
key_points: Spolsky reiterates his stance that developers should never sign non-compete agreements
He uses a real-world example to validate his earlier advice
money_quotes: Remember how I said you should never sign non-compete agreements? Here's why.
posts.key_insight: Non-compete agreements are harmful to employees, and real-world cases consistently demonstrate why developers should refuse to sign them.

posts.summary: This is a brief teaser post directing readers to Joel's longer article 'Big Macs vs. The Naked Chef.' It poses the question of why the biggest IT consulting companies consistently produce poor work, and why promising upstart consultancies inevitably decline into mediocrity after initial success. The full argument is contained in the linked article rather than this post itself.
key_points: Large IT consulting firms paradoxically do some of the worst work despite their size and resources
Upstart consulting companies follow a predictable arc: early spectacular successes, rapid growth, then decline into mediocrity
The tension between scaling a services business and maintaining quality is presented as a central puzzle
money_quotes: Why is it that some of the biggest IT consulting companies in the world do the worst work?
Why is it that the cool upstart consulting companies start out with a string of spectacular successes, meteoric growth, and rapidly degenerate into mediocrity?
posts.key_insight: There is an inherent contradiction between scaling a consulting business and maintaining the quality of work that made it successful in the first place.

posts.summary: Spolsky draws an analogy between McDonald's and IT consulting firms to explain why large consulting companies produce mediocre work. McDonald's succeeds by creating exhaustive rules that allow unskilled workers to produce consistent (if low-quality) results, while brilliant chefs like Jamie Oliver create superior food through improvisation and talent. The same pattern plays out in IT: talented programmers start consulting firms, grow too fast, hire less talented people, create rigid methodologies to compensate, and eventually become the mediocre incumbents they once displaced. Spolsky argues that methodologies are a ceiling on quality, not a floor, and that the only real solution is to hire talented people and grow slowly enough to maintain quality.
key_points: Rigid methodologies produce consistent but mediocre output because they substitute rules for talent, and rules can't handle improvisation or changing conditions

IT consulting firms follow a predictable lifecycle: a talented founder builds something great, hires cheap labor, creates a methodology to compensate, and degrades into the same mediocrity they originally disrupted

The scalability problem is fundamental: you either hire equally talented people (who will demand equal compensation) or hire cheaper people and try to encode talent into rules (which produces inferior results)
Methodologies fail hardest when conditions change, because rule-followers can't adapt while talented people can learn anything
The alternative to scaling through methodology is to stay small, hire only the best, and choose business models (like software) that scale without requiring proportional headcount growth
money_quotes: The secret of Big Macs is that they're not very good, but every one is not very good in exactly the same way.
McDonald's real secret sauce is its huge operations manual, describing in stunning detail the exact procedure that every franchisee must follow in creating a Big Mac.

An automaton chef who is merely following instructions might be able to produce a given dish when everything is working perfectly, but without real talent and skill, will not be able to improvise, which is why you never see jicama at McDonald's.

All the IT service companies get greedy and try to grow faster than they can find talented people, and they grow layers upon layers of rules and procedures which help produce 'consistent,' if not very brilliant work.

The brilliant founders of these companies could certainly adjust to the new world: they are talented computer scientists who can learn anything, but the company they built can't adjust because it has substituted a rulebook for talent, and rulebooks don't adjust to new times.

Beware of Methodologies. They are a great way to bring everyone up to a dismal, but passable, level of performance, but at the same time, they are aggravating to more talented people who chafe at the restrictions that are placed on them.
We are perfectly happy to stay small if we can't find enough good people.
posts.key_insight: Methodologies are an attempt to scale talent through rules, but they produce a ceiling of mediocrity because rules cannot substitute for improvisation, and they drive away the talented people who could actually do great work.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky lambasts ADP's web programming quality, detailing how their 401K site's misuse of the tabindex attribute locks users out of their accounts through normal keyboard navigation. He extends the criticism beyond one page to ADP's entire web platform, describing how their payroll product EasyPayNet was riddled with JavaScript bugs that corrupted employee data, locked users out, and made forms unusable due to overly clever validation logic. Fog Creek switched to Intuit QuickBooks as a result. He also warns against SMC Networks' wireless products, which barely functioned at short range.
key_points: ADP's misuse of HTML's tabindex attribute created a trap where normal tab-and-space keyboard navigation locked users out of their accounts, requiring a new PIN sent by postal mail
Overly clever JavaScript validation can make forms completely unusable when controls have interdependencies that prevent users from making sequential changes
Even the most trivial usability testing would have caught these bugs, indicating ADP did none whatsoever
Bad UI on business-critical software like payroll has real consequences — corrupted employee data and inability to pay people
money_quotes: Because the morons who wrote that page are using the "tabindex" attribute without understanding it, this actually activates a button way down at the bottom of the page which locks you out of your account and mails a new PIN to you via snail mail.
This kind of horrific programming shows evidence of no usability testing whatsoever.
The most trivial usability test would uncover this.
Way-too-clever JavaScript validation routines made it impossible to change values on certain forms.
The UI was full of stupid JavaScript bugs that screwed up my payroll every single time I tried to pay people.
It copied employee's data on top of other employee's data.
posts.key_insight: Enterprise software that skips even basic usability testing creates infuriating traps for users, and when that software handles critical functions like payroll, the consequences are not merely annoying but actively harmful.

posts.summary: This is a very brief announcement post where Joel Spolsky shares that his book has appeared on Amazon.com, though it won't ship until March. He notes that the cover image shown is just a mockup and not the final design. The post is essentially a quick personal update with no substantive argument or analysis.
key_points: Joel's book appeared on Amazon before its March shipping date
The cover image on Amazon was a preliminary mockup, not the final design
money_quotes: How exciting, my book is already on Amazon!
posts.key_insight: A brief personal announcement about Joel's upcoming book appearing on Amazon ahead of its release date.

posts.summary: Spolsky explains why breaking the build in source control is a serious problem that can halt an entire team's productivity. He advocates for daily builds as a safeguard against unnoticed breakages. He recommends scheduling builds at lunchtime so developers can check in code beforehand and resolve any failures when they return. He shares the Excel team's practice of making the person who broke the build responsible for babysitting future builds as both punishment and education.
key_points: A single developer's forgotten checkin can block the entire team from working
Daily builds serve as an early warning system to catch breakages before they compound
Scheduling builds at lunchtime creates a natural workflow: checkin, break, then fix or continue
Rotating build babysitting duty through the person who broke it teaches everyone the build process
money_quotes: Breaking the build is so bad (and so common) that it helps to make daily builds, to insure that no breakage goes unnoticed.
