They've added a new source file, and everything compiles fine on their machine, but they forgot to add the source file to the code repository. So they lock their machine and go home, oblivious and happy. But nobody else can work, so they have to go home too, unhappy.
On the Excel team we had a rule that whoever broke the build, as their "punishment", was responsible for babysitting the builds until someone else broke it.
This was a good incentive not to break the build, and a good way to rotate everyone through the build process so that everyone learned how it worked.
If the build failed, you fix it, but everybody can keep on working with the pre-build, unbroken version of the source.
posts.key_insight: Daily builds with social accountability mechanisms like rotating build babysitting duty prevent broken builds from silently derailing team productivity.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky argues that daily builds are essential for productive software development teams. He traces the concept from the personal productivity gains of faster compilers (the Edit-Compile-Test loop) to the team-level Report-Fix-Retest loop, showing how both benefit from speed. Daily builds should be automatic, scheduled, and compile every version from a clean checkout. The practice catches broken builds quickly, keeps testers supplied with fresh code, enables binary search through build history to find bugs, and ensures no single person holds institutional knowledge about the build process. Joel draws on his experience at Microsoft Excel and Juno to provide practical implementation advice.
key_points: The Edit-Compile-Test loop is fractal: the same principle that makes fast compilers boost individual productivity applies at team scale through the Report-Fix-Retest loop
Daily builds must be automatic, daily (or more frequent), and complete — building every version from a clean checkout, not relying on incremental compilation
The build process should be fully scripted end-to-end so no institutional knowledge lives in one person's head
Broken builds should stop the team until fixed, and the person who broke it should babysit builds until someone else breaks them
Maintaining an archive of daily builds enables binary search to pinpoint exactly when a bug was introduced
money_quotes: A crucial observation here is that you have to run through the loop again and again to write a program, and so it follows that the faster the Edit-Compile-Test loop, the more productive you will be, down to a natural limit of instantaneous compiles.
This is the equivalent of the Blue Screen of Death for an entire programming team, and happens a lot when a programmer forgets to add a new file they created to the repository.
You never get into a situation where you can't release a product because only Shaniqua knows how to create the installer, and she was hit by a bus.
There is nothing worse for your sanity then when you are trying to ship the code, and there's one tiny bug, so you fix that one tiny bug right on the daily build server and ship it.
As a golden rule, you should only ship code that has been produced by a full, clean daily build that started from a complete checkout.
Whoever broke the build became responsible for babysitting builds until somebody else broke it.
posts.key_insight: The speed of feedback loops at every scale — from compiler speed for individual developers to daily builds for entire teams — is the primary determinant of software development productivity.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief placeholder post where Joel Spolsky announces he is going on vacation for a week. He humorously addresses his readers as if they were children being left with a babysitter, telling them to behave. There is no substantive technical or business content. It reflects the casual, personal tone common in early blogging culture where authors maintained a conversational relationship with their audience.
key_points: Joel announces a one-week vacation break from blogging
He uses a playful parent-child metaphor to address his readership
The post illustrates the informal, personal nature of early 2000s blogging culture
money_quotes: Now behave and don't give the babysitter a hard time or I'll dock your allowance.
posts.key_insight: A throwaway vacation notice that demonstrates the personal, conversational relationship early bloggers maintained with their regular readers.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces his return to blogging and shares that his company Fog Creek Software has moved into a new office in a Manhattan townhouse. He highlights the office's garden and wireless networking setup for outdoor work.
key_points: Fog Creek Software relocated to a Manhattan townhouse office
The office features a garden for outdoor work
Wireless networking was set up to enable working outside in summer
money_quotes: we got wireless networking so we can work outside in the summer
posts.key_insight: A brief update post announcing Fog Creek Software's move to a new Manhattan office with outdoor workspace.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky introduces the concept of task allocation in software team management, arguing it's a critical skill that managers must master. He uses the Hebrew colloquialism 'file dumping' to describe assigning work to people. The post serves as a teaser for his longer article 'Human Task Switches Considered Harmful,' which argues that programmers should only work on one task at a time. He contends that proper task allocation can yield incredible productivity benefits, while poor allocation leads to organizational paralysis.
key_points: Task allocation is one of the first and most important skills for managers of programmers to master
Assigning work correctly can yield incredible productivity benefits; doing it wrong creates organizational dysfunction where nothing gets done
Each programmer should only be working on one thing at a time to avoid harmful context switching
money_quotes: When you're managing a team of programmers, one of the first things you have to learn to get right is task allocation.
How you decide which files to dump in which laps is one of the areas where you can get incredible productivity benefits if you do it right.
Do it wrong, and you can create one of those gnarly situations where nobody gets anything accomplished and everybody complains that 'nothing ever gets done around here.'
posts.key_insight: Programmers should work on only one task at a time, because poor task allocation and context switching destroy team productivity far more than most managers realize.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that assigning programmers multiple tasks simultaneously destroys productivity due to the enormous cost of context switching. Using a CPU multitasking analogy, he demonstrates that sequential processing yields faster average completion times than multitasking, and that the penalty grows as task-switch time increases. He then maps this to human programmers, arguing that the mental state required for programming makes context switches extraordinarily expensive — on the order of hours, not seconds. His conclusion is that managers should never assign more than one task at a time and should view their role as removing obstacles so programmers can focus deeply.
key_points: Sequential processing produces lower average completion times than multitasking, even with zero-cost task switches
The penalty for multitasking grows proportionally with task-switch duration, making it catastrophic for high-switch-cost work
Programming requires holding vast mental state in short-term memory, making human task switches take hours rather than seconds
Managers should ensure each programmer works on exactly one thing at a time and should absorb interruptions themselves
A programmer who 'starves' one task to focus on another is actually being more productive than one who tries to juggle both
money_quotes: A programmer coding at full throttle is keeping zillions of things in their head at once: everything from names of variables, data structures, important APIs, the names of utility functions that they wrote and call a lot, even the name of the subdirectory where they store their source code.
The human brain seems to move it out of short-term RAM and swaps it out onto a backup tape where it takes forever to retrieve.

I feel like when I have two programming projects on my plate at once, the task switch time is something like 6 hours. In an 8-hour day, that means multitasking reduces my productivity to 2 hours per day.
You should never let people work on more than one thing at once.
Good managers see their responsibility as removing obstacles so that people can focus on one thing and really get it done.
If you give somebody two things to work on, you should be grateful if they 'starve' one task and only work on one, because they're going to get more stuff done and finish the average task sooner.
posts.key_insight: Because programming requires holding enormous mental state in memory, human task-switching costs are so high that managers should never assign a programmer more than one project at a time.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief link post where Joel Spolsky shares a link to Michael's new weblog about technical interview questions. There is no substantive argument or analysis — it is simply a pointer to another resource. The entire post is only 9 words plus a hyperlink.
key_points: Joel signals interest in the topic of technical interviewing, which would become a major theme of his later writing
The post reflects the early blogging era's convention of short link-sharing posts
posts.key_insight: This micro-post is a simple link share with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: Joel provides a casual update on Fog Creek Software's early days, where the team is simultaneously renovating their office space and preparing to ship FogBUGZ 2.0. He contrasts their scrappy, bootstrap approach with venture-capital funded startups. He outlines their beta testing philosophy—limiting testers to committed installers and providing real-time phone support—as a direct counter to Netscape's approach of ignoring beta feedback. He also teases an upcoming unannounced product.
key_points: Fog Creek's bootstrap mentality meant founders personally renovated their office, contrasting with VC-funded competitors
FogBUGZ 2.0 beta testing strategy involves selecting committed testers and providing dedicated real-time support during installation
Joel emphasizes listening to customers obsessively, even soliciting feedback from people who tried the demo but didn't buy
Free upgrades for existing 1.0 customers signals a customer-loyalty-first pricing approach
money_quotes: You venture-capital funded wimps probably never had to touch spackle.
I slammed Netscape for using the entire world as beta-testers, and then ignoring the bug reports that came in.
We're inviting a select group of people to install the beta, and asking them to tell us when they will be installing it.
During that time, we will literally be standing by the phone on our toll-free line to help them with any problems that come up.
I'm obsessive about listening to customers.
posts.key_insight: A bootstrap startup's competitive advantage lies in doing things that don't scale—like personally supporting every beta tester—which VC-funded companies are too removed to bother with.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky critiques a Jakob Nielsen article on retaining key staff, finding it devoid of actionable content despite multiple readings. He notes that Nielsen's piece merely lists approaches and dismisses them without offering alternatives. Spolsky then redirects readers to his own writings on attracting and managing programmers, as well as the book Peopleware, which he considers the definitive resource on the topic. The post is essentially a brief rebuttal paired with a curated reading list.
key_points: Jakob Nielsen's article on staff retention is criticized for listing ideas only to assert they don't work, without offering solutions
Spolsky positions his own articles on attracting and managing programmers as superior alternatives
Peopleware is recommended as the essential book on managing technical staff, with Spolsky claiming managers who follow it have virtually no turnover
money_quotes: I read the article again and again and it just doesn't say anything.
All it does is list a bunch of things and then asserts that they don't work.
Managers who understand and implement the philosophies of Peopleware have virtually no turnover.
posts.key_insight: Advice on retaining talent is worthless if it only enumerates what doesn't work without offering a constructive alternative.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky pays tribute to Philip Greenspun, acknowledging him as a key inspiration for Fog Creek Software. He describes Greenspun's company arsDigita as a consulting firm distinguished by its personal voice and commitment to education. Joel notes that Greenspun has departed from arsDigita, which he sees as a significant loss.
key_points: Philip Greenspun was a key inspiration to many in the tech community, including Joel's own company Fog Creek
arsDigita distinguished itself from other internet consulting companies through two qualities: personal voice and education
Greenspun's departure from arsDigita represents a meaningful loss for the company and community
money_quotes: He started a company called arsDigita, which was really just another consulting company for the Internet, but it had two things which made it uniquely different. It had a personal voice, and it had education.
But now it doesn't have Philip, and that's a real bummer.
posts.key_insight: What makes a company truly distinctive is not its business model but the personal voice and educational mission of its founder — qualities that leave when the founder does.

posts.summary: Spolsky reflects on how arsDigita, Philip Greenspun's internet consulting company, inspired Fog Creek Software through its authentic personal voice and educational mission. He traces his own discovery of the early web through Greenspun's captivating personal writing style, then analyzes what arsDigita got right (personal voice) and wrong (consulting-only model, anti-Microsoft religious bigotry). The post becomes an elegy for arsDigita after it took venture capital, grew rapidly, lost Greenspun, and replaced its distinctive voice with generic corporate marketing speak. Spolsky uses the metaphor of spring and youthful optimism to mourn the loss of authenticity that comes with institutional maturity.
key_points: A personal, human voice on the internet is what makes companies relatable and compelling — more than open source, community, or technology choices

Consulting doesn't scale the way software licensing does: consulting yields 10-20% margins while each marginal software unit sold is 100% profit, explaining why software companies are valued ~25x higher
Technology bigotry (refusing to use Microsoft products on principle rather than pragmatic grounds) is a form of religious thinking that gets in the way of doing good work
Taking venture capital forced arsDigita to grow from 5 to 250 people, which led to boring old-school management replacing the authentic voice with corporate happy-talk
The transition from scrappy authenticity to corporate maturity is a recurring tragedy in tech companies
money_quotes: What excites me was the way arsDigita had a personal voice on the Internet that made it possible to relate to it in a human way, which is what people want to do, since we're humans.
A consultant working on a project makes ten or twenty percent profit. If you license software, each marginal unit you sell is 100% profit.
I don't believe anything. Gets in the way of learning.

If you say, 'we know Unix better, so we get better results with Unix,' you're a scientist. If you say, 'we don't use any Microsoft crap, it all sucks' you're just a fanatic, and it's getting in the way of doing good work.
We don't have venture capital; we have money.
If you have a voice on your website, they will care.
Maturity is kind of sad.
posts.key_insight: A company's authentic personal voice is its most valuable and fragile asset — venture capital, rapid growth, and professional management almost inevitably destroy it.

posts.summary: Spolsky sets up a common complaint about software bloat by noting that Excel grew from 15MB to 146MB between 1993 and 2000. He frames the tenfold increase as seemingly damning evidence of sloppy programming, then immediately subverts the reader's expectation by linking to an article that argues the opposite. The post is a teaser that challenges the knee-jerk reaction that growing software size equals poor engineering.
key_points: Excel grew from 15MB to 146MB between version 5.0 (1993) and Excel 2000, nearly a tenfold increase
The instinctive reaction is to blame 'sloppy Microsoft programmers' for bloated software
Spolsky deliberately sets up this assumption only to immediately refute it, linking to his longer essay on why software bloat isn't what it seems
money_quotes: It was positively huge: it required a whole 15 megabytes of hard drive space.
In those days we could still remember our first 20MB PC hard drives (around 1985) and so 15MB sure seemed like a lot.
Dang those sloppy Microsoft programmers, right? Wrong.
posts.key_insight: The instinctive assumption that growing software size proves sloppy programming is wrong, and the real story behind software bloat is more nuanced than it appears.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky dismantles the common complaint about 'bloatware' by showing that software isn't actually getting bigger in economic terms — Excel 2000 costs $1.03 in hard drive space versus $36 for Excel 5.0 in 1993 dollars. He argues that modern operating systems only load the pages actually used, making executable size largely irrelevant to performance. He then attacks the '80/20 myth' — the belief that you can build a lite product with 20% of features and capture 80% of the market — by pointing out that every user needs a different 20%. Companies that ship lite word processors consistently fail because the missing features are always someone's dealbreaker.
key_points: Software size measured in dollars of hard drive space has actually decreased dramatically over time, making bloatware complaints economically unfounded
Modern operating systems use demand-paged virtual memory, so large executables don't consume resources for unused code paths
The 80/20 rule is misleading for software features because every user relies on a different 20%, so cutting 80% of features alienates nearly everyone
Developer time spent shrinking code size produces imperceptible benefits to users while delaying valuable features
Dozens of companies have tried shipping 'lite' products and consistently failed because real user needs are large and varied
money_quotes: In real terms, it's almost like Excel is actually getting smaller!
Hard drive space is down to $0.0071 per megabyte and still plummeting like a sheep learning to fly by jumping out of a tree.
I'm starting to suspect that fretting about bloatware is more of a mental health problem than a software problem.

If programmers don't have to worry about how large their code is, they can ship it sooner. And that means you get more features, and features make your life better (when you use them) and don't usually hurt (when you don't).

A lot of software developers are seduced by the old '80/20' rule. It seems to make a lot of sense: 80% of the people use 20% of the features. So you convince yourself that you only need to implement 20% of the features, and you can still sell 80% as many copies.
Unfortunately, it's never the same 20%. Everybody uses a different set of features.
Mozilla is not big because it's full of useless crap. Mozilla is big because your needs are big. Your needs are big because the Internet is big.
posts.key_insight: Bloatware complaints ignore the economics of plummeting storage costs, and the 80/20 strategy fails because every user depends on a different 20% of features, making 'lite' products unsellable.

posts.summary: This short post addresses two unrelated topics. First, Joel responds to a question about why Excel requires so much disk space, explaining that the bloat comes from shared Office components and bundled applications like Internet Explorer, not from Excel itself. He dismisses the concern, noting that Office takes up only 1% of his disk space. Second, he shares an amusing observation about deceptive advertising, referencing a Simpsons writer's concept of ads where the 'word-to-falsehood ratio approaches one,' and teases a piece of junk mail from Earthlink.
key_points: Excel's large install size comes primarily from shared Office components and bundled applications like IE, not from Excel's own code (the EXE is under 7 MB)
Software bloat concerns are often overblown relative to available disk space — 190MB was only 1% of his disk at the time
Deceptive marketing can be so concentrated that nearly every word in an ad contains a falsehood
money_quotes: The actual Excel EXE itself is under 7 MB.
But who cares? It's a great app and it's 1% of my disk space.
George Meyer, one of the writers on the Simpsons, likes to collect examples of advertising in which 'the word-to-falsehood ratio approaches one.'
It's not from the country, there is no crock. Two words, two lies.
posts.key_insight: Software bloat is often misattributed to the core application when the real culprit is shared components and bundled dependencies, and the practical impact on users is typically negligible.

posts.summary: Joel catalogs twelve distinct deceptions on a single piece of Earthlink junk mail designed to mimic a FedEx express delivery. He systematically dissects every element—from fake tracking numbers and forged handwriting fonts to a pointless piece of clear plastic—showing how direct mailers will use any trick to get recipients to open their mail. He contrasts these tactics with Earthlink's stated corporate value of 'complete honesty and integrity in everything we do.' The post uses humor and detailed forensic analysis to highlight the fundamental dishonesty baked into direct mail marketing.
key_points: The Earthlink mailer uses at least twelve distinct deceptions to disguise junk mail as an urgent express delivery, including fake tracking numbers, forged signatures, and misleading weight limits
Direct mail's core problem—99% gets thrown away unopened—drives marketers to increasingly dishonest tactics to trick recipients into opening envelopes
The biological concept of Batesian mimicry perfectly describes how junk mail imitates legitimate express mail packaging to exploit recipients' trust
Corporate mission statements about honesty and integrity ring hollow when the company's own marketing department employs systematic deception
Each individual lie seems small, but the cumulative effect reveals a marketing culture where deception is the default rather than the exception
money_quotes: Two words, two lies.
The marketing slimeballs at Earthlink must have drooled at the thought of old Marge in a nursing home, practically falling over with excitement that she got an urgent express letter.
Sorry, folks, for $0.23 you don't get tracking.
People won't open it unless it's from someone they know, and everyone knows Sky Dayton!

The clear plastic serves no purpose whatsoever except to imitate FedEx envelopes which have the same plastic to hold the mailing manifest, much like those little butterflies which have fierce markings in attempt to convince butterfly-eaters that they themselves are fierce.
The biggest problem with direct mail (what we normal people call 'Junk Mail') is that 99% of it (literally) gets thrown away unopened, Batesian mimicry or not.
To combat this, direct mailers will do anything to get you to open their junk, no matter how dishonest.
We require complete honesty and integrity in everything we do.
posts.key_insight: The desperation of direct mail marketing—where 99% of pieces are discarded unopened—creates a race to the bottom in honesty, producing elaborate deceptions that directly contradict companies' stated values.

posts.summary: Joel announces the release of FogBUGZ 2.0 and a complete redesign of the Fog Creek website. The post is a brief, informal update expressing exhaustion and satisfaction after shipping.
key_points: FogBUGZ 2.0 shipped as a major release
Fog Creek website was completely redesigned simultaneously
Shipping software brings a mix of exhaustion and satisfaction
money_quotes: Feels good to get it out the door!
posts.key_insight: Shipping software is exhausting but deeply satisfying.

posts.summary: This is a brief personal post where Joel Spolsky asks his blog readers for recommendations on renting a luxury villa in Greece for a family vacation. He specifies requirements including sleeping capacity for 10, proximity to a beach, and a self-catered kitchen for kosher meals. The post is notable mainly as an example of how early bloggers used their platforms as personal bulletin boards, leveraging their audience for crowdsourced advice.
key_points: Joel uses his popular tech blog as a personal channel to crowdsource vacation recommendations
The post reveals personal details about Joel's family size and kosher dietary observance
Demonstrates the early-2000s blogging culture where personal and professional content freely mixed
money_quotes: We're looking to rent a luxury villa on an island in Greece, walking distance to a nice beach, for a week or two in July/August, for the family.
posts.key_insight: This is a purely personal post with no technical or professional content, illustrating how early influential tech bloggers mixed personal requests with their professional writing.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post consisting of a single sentence quoting Earthlink's response about aiming for an 'effect of verisimilitude,' with a link to an external source. The post is too short to contain a substantive argument or conclusion. It appears to be a quick link-blog entry, common in early 2000s blogging, where Joel shared an amusing or noteworthy quote with minimal commentary.
key_points: A one-line link-blog entry sharing an Earthlink quote about 'verisimilitude'
Reflects the early blogging style of short, link-driven posts with little original commentary
money_quotes: We're shooting for an 'effect of verisimilitude'.
posts.key_insight: This micro-post is a link-blog artifact from early 2000s blogging culture, too brief to convey a substantive insight.

posts.summary: Joel shares results from an A/B test on FogBUGZ's demo signup page, where half of visitors were asked for an email address and half weren't. He found that simply requiring an email address caused about half of potential customers to abandon the signup process. Based on this, they removed the email requirement entirely. He connects this to a broader usability principle: even small reductions in friction can dramatically increase completion rates.
key_points: An A/B test showed that requiring just an email address cut demo signups by roughly 50%
Even minimal friction in signup flows causes massive user drop-off due to privacy and spam concerns
Reducing difficulty by even a small amount tends to double the number of people who succeed with a task
Joel also quotes Mark Newman dismissing CMM (Capability Maturity Model) as primarily a vehicle for consultants to sell books and seminars
money_quotes: About half of the people gave up when asked to type in an email address.
Reducing difficulty by even a small amount tends to double the number of people who succeed with a task.
We changed the demo signup so that 50% of the guinea pigs, er, potential customers had to provide an email address and 50% didn't.
Many of the free software demos you find on the web require a complete name, address, where did you hear about us, birthday, mother's driver's license ID, etc.
I view it primarily as a means for high-priced consultants to hold seminars and sell books, not as a process to improve software.
posts.key_insight: Even the smallest friction in a user flow — like asking for an email address — can cut your conversion rate in half, so remove every unnecessary barrier.

posts.summary: Spolsky criticizes 'architecture astronauts' who fixate on technical architecture patterns while completely missing the user value that makes products successful. He uses Napster as an example: its value wasn't peer-to-peer technology, but the ability to instantly find and listen to any song. He observes how this architectural fixation spawns an entire ecosystem of hype — conferences, VC funds — followed by predictable backlash, all disconnected from what actually matters to users.
key_points: Architecture astronauts strip away user value and fixate on technical patterns, missing what makes products actually compelling
Napster's success was about instant music access, not peer-to-peer architecture — but the tech industry obsessed over the latter
Architectural hype cycles (conferences, VC funds, backlash articles) are self-reinforcing and disconnected from real product value
money_quotes: Your typical architecture astronaut will take a fact like 'Napster is a peer-to-peer service for downloading music' and ignore everything but the architecture, thinking it's interesting because it's peer to peer, completely missing the point that it's interesting because you can type the name of a song and listen to it right away.

Suddenly you have peer-to-peer conferences, peer-to-peer venture capital funds, and even peer-to-peer backlash with the imbecile business journalists dripping with glee as they copy each other's stories.
posts.key_insight: Products succeed because of the problems they solve for users, not because of the architectural patterns they employ — but the tech industry chronically confuses the two.

posts.summary: Spolsky mocks IT research firms like Forrester and Yankee Group for publishing inflated market projections that startups use to justify unrealistic business plans. He argues their growth extrapolations are essentially fabricated, based on minimal actual data projected forward with absurd growth rates. He illustrates how these projections enable a cycle where startups raise VC money based on capturing a tiny percentage of a fictional market, only to fail spectacularly. He closes with a cautionary note about venture capital, citing Philip Greenspun being pushed out of his own company by the VCs he brought in.
key_points: IT market research firms produce unreliable growth projections extrapolated from minimal data points
Startups use these inflated projections in business plans to justify raising venture capital with the 'just 1% of the market' argument
The cycle of VC-funded startups built on fantasy market projections leads predictably to failure
Venture capital comes with loss of control, as illustrated by Greenspun being ousted from ArsDigita
money_quotes: I hate those cheesy market research companies who come up with ridiculous numbers and extrapolations (suitable for business plans) that make no sense, which, frankly, they pulled out of their tokhes.

They always have these lame bar charts where they only have data for this year, then they extrapolate out four years assuming a typical 736.13% rate of growth, a number they divined by rolling dice and tossing bones about haphazardly.

So you show the stupid veecees the report from Forrester and they give you enough money to hire 250 people, and two years later you've got $254 in revenue from selling tomatoes you grew in the company backyard.
If your business plan includes projections from IT research firms, you better start planting tomatoes.
I was pushed out of ArsDigita by the venture capitalists and managers that I brought in.
posts.key_insight: Market projections from IT research firms are fabricated nonsense that fuel a destructive cycle of VC-funded startups built on fantasy numbers.

posts.summary: This is not a standalone blog post but rather a brief link/pointer to Joel Spolsky's full article 'Don't Let Architecture Astronauts Scare You,' which critiques Groove and similar products for being over-architected. The actual content is just a one-line reference to the full article about Architecture Astronauts.
key_points: This entry serves as a navigational pointer to a longer article about Architecture Astronauts
The linked article questions whether Groove's designers have over-engineered their product
money_quotes: Are the Groove designers Architecture Astronauts?
posts.key_insight: This is a stub post linking to Spolsky's influential 'Architecture Astronauts' essay rather than a substantive blog entry itself.

posts.summary: Spolsky shares anecdotal experience comparing pair programming with traditional solo development plus code review at Fog Creek. After trying pair programming on a client project, the team found they moved faster working individually on CityDesk. He acknowledges the irony of using anecdotes as data, but argues that lightweight code review using CVS/FogBUGZ integration provides the quality benefits of pair programming without the productivity cost.
key_points: Pair programming provided 'continual code review' benefits but at significant productivity cost compared to solo work
Solo developers with tooling-assisted code review moved faster, 'blasting through dozens of fixes per person' per day
Lightweight code review (10 minutes total) caught problems while being far cheaper than full-time pairing
Good tooling (CVS/FogBUGZ integration with two-click graphical diffs) makes code review nearly frictionless
money_quotes: I have to say that we're moving even faster without pairing up, literally blasting through dozens of fixes and small features a day per person.
Q: To a Political Scientist, what is the singular of the word "data"? A: "Anecdote"
I've gathered some data which shows that the benefits of pair programming are not enough to offset the loss in productivity.
Today I spent about 10 minutes total doing code reviews (found some problems, even) which is really cheap compared to pair programming.
The 'continual code review' made things go very quickly.
posts.key_insight: Lightweight, tool-assisted code review captures most of pair programming's quality benefits at a fraction of the productivity cost.

posts.summary: Spolsky coins the term 'Architecture Astronauts' to describe technologists who get so absorbed in abstract architectural patterns that they lose sight of what makes products actually useful. He argues that Napster succeeded not because of its peer-to-peer architecture but because users could instantly find and listen to songs. He criticizes the hype cycles around technologies like .NET, Jini, SOAP, and Java, pointing out that their grandiose white papers promise revolutionary change while delivering incremental infrastructure improvements. The core message is that architecture should serve user needs, not exist for its own sake.
key_points: Excessive abstraction leads to meaningless generalizations — when you go too far up, you 'run out of oxygen' and lose touch with concrete problems users actually have.
Napster's success was about letting people type a song name and listen to it immediately, not about peer-to-peer architecture — products win on features, not architecture.
Technologies like SOAP, .NET, Jini, and Java are fine architectures but are wildly overhyped with utopian promises that amount to incremental wire-format improvements.
Architecture Astronauts solve problems they know how to solve, not problems that are useful to solve — a critical distinction that separates useful engineering from intellectual self-indulgence.
The bombastic, utopian language in technology white papers is a reliable signal that you're being sold architecture hype rather than genuine user value.
money_quotes: When you go too far up, abstraction-wise, you run out of oxygen.
It's very hard to get them to write code or design programs, because they won't stop thinking about Architecture.
They tend to work for really big companies that can afford to have lots of unproductive people with really advanced degrees that don't contribute to the bottom line.
If Napster wasn't peer-to-peer but it did let you type the name of a song and then listen to it, it would have been just as popular.

That's one sure tip-off to the fact that you're being assaulted by an Architecture Astronaut: the incredible amount of bombast; the heroic, utopian grandiloquence; the boastfulness; the complete lack of reality.
Remember that the architecture people are solving problems that they think they can solve, not problems which are useful to solve.
Tell me something new that I can do that I couldn't do before, O Astronauts, or stay up there in space and don't waste any more of my time.
posts.key_insight: Grand architectural abstractions are worthless if they don't enable something concretely new and useful for actual users — judge technology by what it lets people do, not by the elegance of its architecture.

posts.summary: Joel shares a brief debugging tip about internationalization testing. He describes setting his laptop's regional settings to France permanently to catch i18n bugs in Fog Creek's applications. He notes surprising differences in number and date formatting across locales and argues that web applications should respect browser locale settings rather than server settings.
key_points: Setting your development machine to a foreign locale is an effective way to discover internationalization bugs
Number and date formatting varies dramatically across locales (e.g., France uses spaces as thousands separators and commas for decimals)
Web applications should respond to the browser's locale settings, not the server's locale
money_quotes: I finally decided to permanently set my regional settings to France to smoke out the last of the internationalization bugs in our applications.
Did you know that in France large numbers are written with spaces as the thousands separator? For example 1 000 000 is a million. And 3,14 is pi. And 4/1 is January fourth.
Even web-based applications should be written to respond to locale settings.
FogBUGZ shows you dates according to the web browser's settings, not the web server.
If you set your computer's regional settings to Denmark, Google appears in Danish.
posts.key_insight: Permanently running your development machine in a foreign locale is a simple but effective way to catch internationalization bugs before your international customers do.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky responds to a Groove Networks designer who objected to being called an 'Architecture Astronaut.' While acknowledging Groove's elegant peer-to-peer architecture that improves on Lotus Notes by eliminating server bottlenecks, Spolsky argues that architecture alone doesn't sell products — killer apps and compelling features do. He draws parallels to Notes, which succeeded as an email client despite being sold as a groupware platform, and to NeXT and Be, which had great architectures but failed commercially. His central argument is that Groove needs to demonstrate specific, compelling use cases rather than relying on 'third parties will build things' as a strategy.
key_points: Groove's peer-to-peer architecture is essentially 'Notes done right' by eliminating the server bottleneck that plagued large Notes installations
Lotus Notes was the most misunderstood product in history — sold as groupware but bought and used as email, which ironically didn't work well enough
Architecture doesn't sell products; features and killer apps do, as demonstrated by the commercial failures of NeXT and Be despite superior architectures
Relying on third parties to build compelling apps on your platform is a weak strategy unless you can articulate what those killer apps will be
Repeatedly hearing the same contrived example use case (like GPS coffee shop coupons) is a warning sign that a platform lacks genuine real-world demand
money_quotes: It's the applets that are going to sell Groove, not the architecture.
You can build the best dang computer in the world with a killer architecture, but if it doesn't run Microsoft Excel, nobody wants one.
When you say 'it's a platform, third parties will build things,' you have to explain what KIND of things and why THEY will be killer apps that sell the architecture.
Notes was the most misunderstood product in history.
It was being sold as a groupware platform, but in reality, it was being bought (and used) as an email program.
It amuses me, because it solves the one problem that coffee shops DON'T have, namely, advertising to people who are standing right in front of the store!
If you want it to be The Next Great Thing it has to be more than architecture, it has to enable things that people really need.
posts.key_insight: Great architecture is necessary but insufficient for product success — what matters is whether the platform enables specific, compelling features that solve problems people actually have.

posts.summary: Joel discovers Ev Williams' idea for an 'Alias Manager' to handle team email aliases and realizes FogBUGZ nearly does this but would need about $10,000 to add the feature. He asks readers whether such a simple, cheap tool already exists and whether they need one. The responses reveal a gap in the market: expensive CRM packages exist but are overkill for small businesses, while some open source options like RT are available.
key_points: Managing shared email aliases (like support@company.com) is a common team coordination problem that lacks simple, affordable solutions
Joel estimates the cost to add alias management to FogBUGZ at $10,000, showing his habit of quantifying feature investment
The market gap exists between overbuilt expensive CRM packages and bare-bones open source tools, with nothing simple and cheap for small businesses
money_quotes: It would take about $10,000 of investment to add this feature to FogBUGZ.
There are a lot of expensive CRM packages. That's not what I'm talking about, they are too complicated and too expensive for small business.
posts.key_insight: Small businesses often fall into a gap between expensive enterprise CRM tools and bare-bones open source options when they need simple shared email management.

posts.summary: Joel shares a brief anecdote about an attempted break-in at Fog Creek Software's office, where a burglar apparently fled upon realizing someone was inside. He describes the police response and pivots to a sarcastic note about EarthLink's SEC investigation for allegedly bilking investors, linking it to his earlier writings about the company's questionable ethics.
key_points: Fog Creek Software experienced an attempted burglary, with evidence of forced entry but nothing stolen
Joel takes a sarcastic jab at EarthLink, connecting SEC fraud allegations to his previous criticisms of the company's honesty and integrity
The post is a casual diary-style update mixing personal office anecdotes with industry commentary
money_quotes: They must have run away when they realized I was here.
The police came and searched the place, guns drawn. Very NYPD Blue.
That company is just all about honesty and integrity.
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted diary entry that uses humor to recount an office break-in and takes a parting shot at EarthLink's ethical troubles.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky briefly introduces the concept of 'eating your own dog food' — the practice of using your own product internally. He recounts taking home a build of CityDesk, his content management product, believing it was nearly ready to ship, and attempting to build a site with it. The post implies that dogfooding revealed the product wasn't as close to shipping as he thought. The entry is extremely brief and serves more as a teaser linking to a longer article on the topic.
key_points: Dogfooding (using your own product) is a powerful quality assurance practice that reveals issues other testing methods miss
Joel thought CityDesk was about 3 weeks from shipping before he tried using it himself, implying dogfooding revealed otherwise
The gap between developer confidence in a product and actual user experience can be significant
money_quotes: Eating your own dog food is the quaint name that we in the computer industry give to the process of actually using your own product.
I had forgotten how well it worked, until a month ago, I took home a build of CityDesk (thinking it was about 3 weeks from shipping) and tried to build a site with it.
posts.key_insight: Actually using your own product reveals quality issues that no amount of internal testing or developer confidence can substitute for.

posts.summary: Spolsky reflects on the dot-com bust and pivots to discussing 'eating your own dog food' — the practice of using your own software product. After the euphoria and schadenfreude of watching inflated startups fail, he describes how taking a build of CityDesk home and actually trying to use it revealed 45 bugs in a single afternoon, including showstoppers that formal testing had missed. He contrasts this with Juno Online Services, where employees used Microsoft Outlook instead of their own email product, leading to a terrible user experience that couldn't compete with AOL. The post argues that dogfooding is essential for building good software and that sustainable, customer-focused businesses will outlast the venture-capital-fueled chaos of the dot-com era.
key_points: Formal QA testing (pulling down every menu) missed critical showstoppers that two hours of actual product usage immediately revealed — 45 bugs in one Sunday afternoon.
Dogfooding not only finds bugs but inspires productivity improvements: Spolsky wrote an automatic page-import subroutine that reduced a full day's work to half a minute.
Juno Online Services failed to eat its own dog food — 175 of 178 employees used Outlook instead of Juno — which meant the product's poor experience went unfixed while AOL gained market share.
The dot-com bust, while initially entertaining as schadenfreude, revealed that 'Get Big Fast' without sustainable business fundamentals was never a real strategy.
In the post-dot-com economy, the path forward is listening to customers, using your own product, and focusing on staying in business rather than chasing venture capital.
money_quotes: Five hundred 31-year-olds in Dockers discovered that just copying Jeff Bezos wasn't a business plan.
All the testing we did, meticulously pulling down every menu and seeing if it worked right, didn't uncover the showstoppers that made it impossible to do what the product was intended to allow.
Trying to use the product, as a customer would, found these showstoppers in a minute.
Hey, I'm a programmer! In the time it took me to import one page and adjust the pictures, I could write a subroutine that does it automatically!

Sometimes you download software and you just can't believe how bad it is, or how hard it is to accomplish the very simple tasks that the software tries to accomplish. Chances are, it's because the developers of the software don't use it.
The president was very insistent that we show popup ads at six different points in time, until he got home and got six popup ads, and said, 'You know what? Maybe just two popups.'
We didn't eat our own dog food, because it was disgusting, and management was sufficiently dysfunctional that we simply were not allowed to fix it, and at least make it tolerable to eat.
posts.key_insight: Using your own product as a real customer would is the fastest and most effective way to find critical usability problems that no amount of formal testing will uncover.

posts.summary: Spolsky responds to a critic who argued that his 'eat your own dog food' philosophy is a barrier to proper usability testing. He clarifies that dogfooding and usability testing are not mutually exclusive, pointing out that he explicitly includes hallway usability testing in The Joel Test and devotes a chapter to it in his book. He dismisses the critique as a logical fallacy, noting that advocating for one practice doesn't mean opposing another.
key_points: Dogfooding and usability testing are complementary practices, not competing ones
Hallway usability testing is already one of the 12 requirements of The Joel Test
The critic's argument commits a logical fallacy by treating two compatible practices as mutually exclusive
Microsoft, cited as a counter-example, actually does both dogfooding and extensive usability testing
money_quotes: Just because I said 'eat your own dog food' doesn't mean I don't advocate usability testing. It's just a logical fallacy.
They could have said 'Eat Your Own Dog Food, but Not If It Means The Dog Will Go Hungry.' It just doesn't follow.
Sorry, you can't read it, he works for Giga and they charge for their ideas.
posts.key_insight: Advocating for dogfooding does not imply opposing usability testing — the two practices are complementary, and treating them as mutually exclusive is a logical fallacy.

posts.summary: Joel shares a usability anecdote from Mark Hurst's talk at the Reboot conference in Denmark. When checkout screens prompt for a username and password, some novice users type their AOL credentials, because they don't read the specific instructions and assume the simplest mental model — that one password works everywhere. Joel connects this to his broader principles that users don't read on-screen text and that they assume the simplest possible program model until proven wrong.
key_points: Users don't read instructional text on screens, even when it's directly relevant to what they need to do
People default to the simplest mental model — assuming their AOL password works on every site — until experience teaches them otherwise
Usability failures often stem from designers assuming users will read and process on-screen instructions
money_quotes: When your checkout screen prompts for a username and password, some newbie users will type in their AOL username and password.
More proof that people don't read.
People will assume the simplest possible program model — i.e., that their new AOL password works everywhere — until they learn otherwise.
posts.key_insight: Users don't read instructions and will always assume the simplest possible mental model for how software works, which designers must account for.

posts.summary: Spolsky shares a humorous anecdote from Jef Raskin about a CEO's unfortunate email address, then pivots to criticize usability experts who attack Microsoft Office products without offering specific improvements. He argues that designing UIs for complex software like Word and Excel is inherently difficult, and that experts who merely point out problems without proposing solutions are being disingenuous. He compares this behavior to Scott McNealy's crowd-pleasing Microsoft jokes at Sun conferences.
key_points: Usability experts who criticize Microsoft Office are often pandering to audiences rather than offering genuine insight
Designing UIs for complex software is extremely hard, and even the best designs will frustrate users
Criticism without specific alternative proposals is intellectually dishonest
Attacking the market leader is an easy applause line, not a meaningful contribution
money_quotes: Whenever usability experts point to Microsoft Office products as an 'example' of horrendous usability, they are usually being disingenuous and just pandering to the crowd.
The dishonest part is that they never seem to offer any specific improvements.

Designing UIs for a program as complex as Word or Excel is extremely hard and the very best designs are still often difficult to use, and that applies to any program with a reasonable level of complexity.
Before you hand-wave about a bad UI, explain what you would do differently and tell us why that would be easier to use.
Now say something interesting without just making fun of the fat boy.
posts.key_insight: Criticizing the usability of complex software is cheap and easy; the real challenge is proposing specific, better alternatives.

posts.summary: Joel describes his experiment with enforcing a strict 9-to-5 workday, inspired by Tom DeMarco's book 'Slack' and its advocacy for shorter workdays. He theorizes that limiting work hours forces you to eliminate wasted time. He also reviews 'Slack' itself, praising its core idea but criticizing it as a book that should have been an article, with diminishing returns after the first few chapters. The post ends with a tech interview puzzle and a self-deprecating note about his new schedule slowing down side projects.
key_points: Forcing yourself to leave after 8 hours may train you to stop wasting time during the day
DeMarco's 'Slack' advocates for 7.5-hour workdays, reinforcing ideas from 'Peopleware' about knowledge work
Lister's Law: you can't think faster, so factory-style productivity pressure doesn't work for knowledge workers
'Slack' is criticized as a one-idea book padded into book length — it should have been an article
money_quotes: If you force yourself to leave work after 8 hours, eventually you train yourself not to waste time during the day.
Slack itself is one of those books that should have been an article.
You can't think faster, so trying to force knowledge workers to work "faster" in the same way you try to get Nike shoe makers to make more shoes per hour will never work.
It'll never last.
It devolves into a typical lightweight bizniss who-moved-that-cheese kind of book about Managing Change, albeit an intelligent one.
posts.key_insight: Knowledge work productivity comes not from longer hours but from disciplined, focused shorter ones — you can't think faster, only think better.

posts.summary: Joel announces that his blog has migrated to a new server hosted inside Fog Creek's offices. The migration process revealed numerous hardcoded URLs throughout the Manila content management system, requiring manual fixes. He notes the irony that while the site looks identical to readers, significant behind-the-scenes work was needed to make it portable. The post highlights the practical challenges of moving a web application between servers when URLs are embedded throughout the codebase.
key_points: Joel migrated his blog to a self-hosted server inside Fog Creek's offices, moving away from external hosting
The Manila CMS had hardcoded URLs scattered throughout, making the migration tedious and error-prone
He emphasizes the importance of making sites portable by eliminating hardcoded URLs
Static assets were still pointing to UserLand's servers and needed manual adjustment
money_quotes: I know, it looks the same to you … but this version of Joel On Software is actually running on a new server inside the Fog Creek offices.
I've been having a lot of fun (!) discovering all the places in Manila where URLs are hardcoded.
I'd like to get rid of as many hardcoded URLs as possible to make sure the site stays portable.
posts.key_insight: Hardcoded URLs throughout a web application create hidden migration costs that only become apparent when you try to move the system to a new server.

posts.summary: Joel briefly mentions MP3pro, a new audio format that achieves half the file size of MP3 with better quality while maintaining backward compatibility with existing MP3 players. He notes it eliminates Windows Media's technical advantage. He dismisses a Gartner analyst's concern about record label adoption as irrelevant. The post ends with an unrelated note about salvaging a vintage Bang & Olufsen stereo receiver.
key_points: MP3pro offered 50% size reduction over MP3 with higher quality and backward compatibility
The format neutralized Windows Media's technical advantages over MP3
Joel dismisses the importance of major record label buy-in for audio format success, siding with user adoption over industry gatekeepers
The post reflects Joel's characteristic interest in consumer technology and contrarian takes on industry dynamics
money_quotes: I've been playing with MP3pro, a new file format that is exactly half the size of MP3s, with higher sound quality.
The even neater part is that you can play MP3pro's with your old MP3 player.
And it eliminates Windows Media's technical advantage.
Hello? Who cares what record companies do?
Remember RCA? I thought they were long gone.
posts.key_insight: Backward compatibility and technical merit matter more than industry gatekeeper approval when it comes to technology adoption.

posts.summary: This is not a blog post but a brief administrative notice informing readers that Joel on Software has moved to a new server. Joel asks readers to update their bookmarks to the new URL. He notes the old URL will continue working but will eventually redirect.
key_points: Site migrated to new server at joelonsoftware.com
Old URLs will continue working temporarily before redirecting
money_quotes: The old URL will continue to work for a long, long time, but will eventually just redirect you.
posts.key_insight: This is a minor administrative notice about a server migration, not a substantive blog post.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the publication of his book 'User Interface Design for Programmers' in print form through Apress. The book originated as a series of articles on his blog. The printed version includes bonus chapters not available in the web version, along with additional illustrations and professional design work by Apress.
key_points: Joel's UI design book has been published in print by Apress
The book originated as web content on Joel on Software
The print edition includes bonus chapters and more illustrations not found online
money_quotes: The printed version of the book has several bonus chapters which are not on the web version.
posts.key_insight: Joel transitioned his popular web-based UI design content into a published book with additional material, demonstrating the viability of blog-to-book publishing.

posts.summary: Spolsky reviews Michael Porter's Competitive Strategy and applies its framework to Microsoft's antitrust behavior. He argues that Microsoft's refusal to appear contrite about antitrust violations is a deliberate strategic choice, not delusion. By maintaining an aggressive posture, Microsoft preserves the 'fear factor' that discourages VCs and startups from entering its markets. Spolsky draws a parallel to IBM's decline, where consent decree oversight made IBM ineffectual against competitors like young Microsoft. The post demonstrates how academic business strategy frameworks illuminate real-world tech industry dynamics.
key_points: Porter's framework suggests analyzing how regulatory constraints like antitrust affect a competitor's willingness to react to smaller rivals
Microsoft's refusal to concede any wrongdoing in its antitrust case is a calculated strategy to maintain fear among potential competitors and VCs
If Microsoft showed any contrition, it would signal to startups and investors that they could safely enter Microsoft's markets without being crushed
Microsoft learned from IBM's example: consent decree oversight paralyzed IBM and allowed smaller companies to compete against it effectively
The Netscape case served as a deliberate warning to VCs like Kleiner Perkins to stay out of Microsoft's territory
money_quotes: Lemming VCs generally have a policy of automatically saying 'no' to any investment that could be seen as competing with Microsoft or which might prod Microsoft to create a new product in response.
This herd fear of Microsoft in the software industry is enormously beneficial to Bill … lots of potential competition never gets off the ground because of fear that Microsoft will squash it.

If Microsoft were to act contrite and concede even the slightest point in the Justice Department's anti-trust case, hundreds of independent software companies and VCs would see this as their opportunity to enter Microsoft's markets.

Everything IBM did had to be reviewed by internal consent decree committees to make sure it wasn't monopolistic, and it was no problem for young companies (like, um, Microsoft) to take big bites out of IBM's ankles without provoking a reaction.
Microsoft's squashing of Netscape could well have been intended as a stern warning across the bow of Kleiner Perkins to stay the hell out of their garden.
It's a brilliant book and it is considered the standard text on the subject, read by freshly hatched management consultants worldwide. But it's drier than school cafeteria chicken.
Every sentence makes me look up at the ceiling, say 'Ah-ha!' and rethink some aspect of how the software industry works in a new light.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's seemingly delusional refusal to acknowledge antitrust wrongdoing is actually a rational strategy to preserve the fear that keeps competitors and investors from challenging its dominance.

posts.summary: This is a teaser post that introduces the common fear in the software industry about Microsoft entering a product space and eliminating profitability. The brief excerpt sets up a longer article exploring how executives frequently kill promising product ideas due to fear of Microsoft competition. The full argument is contained in the linked article rather than this stub post.
key_points: Fear of Microsoft competition was a dominant force in software product decision-making
Executive risk aversion frequently killed promising new product ideas
The 'what if Microsoft enters this space' question was pervasive enough to be recognized as a pattern
money_quotes: If you've been working in the software industry, you've probably seen zillions of good new product ideas quashed by some executive who asks, 'what if Microsoft enters this space and makes all the profits go away?'
posts.key_insight: Fear of competing with Microsoft was a reflexive excuse used by risk-averse executives to kill promising product ideas before they could be explored.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky writes a tribute to Adam Bosworth, praising him as one of the most influential software designers in the industry. He traces Bosworth's career from Microsoft Access through ODBC, Internet Explorer's DOM, SOAP, and XML. Joel uses Bosworth's example to illustrate the kind of talent Fog Creek Software aspires to attract, and urges readers to carefully study Bosworth's recent writing on n-tier architectures and the real-world limitations of SOAP. The post also touches on how seemingly minor UX problems—like the inability to show progress indicators in web browsers—can have outsized business consequences.
key_points: Adam Bosworth exemplifies the ideal program manager: fast-thinking, consistently correct, but also willing to listen and change his mind without ego
Bosworth's track record of shipping world-shaping software (Access, ODBC, IE's DOM, SOAP, XML) is unmatched
The typical SOAP demos showing stock quote services are unrealistically simplistic and don't reflect real-world scaling challenges
Small UX failures like missing browser progress indicators during long server tasks can threaten entire industries
Fog Creek Software's hiring ambition is explicitly benchmarked against attracting people of Bosworth's caliber
money_quotes: He is an incredibly fast thinker and incessantly right.
Unlike most fast-thinkers who are always right, he also knows how to listen, and if he discovers he was wrong, he'll change his mind without emotional attachment to his old ideas.
Adam has designed and shipped more world-shaping software than anyone else I can think of.
One of the goals of my own company, Fog Creek Software, is to create a place where we stood a snowflake's chance in hell of getting superstars like Adam Bosworth to work.
Understand why all the six hundred demos you've seen of a SOAP service that returns stock quotes are unrealistically simplistic and don't scale up to the real world.

Think about how the fact that it's almost impossible to display a progress indicator in a web browser while the server does some 2 minute task came as close to killing the entire online travel-agency industry as Nikita Khrushchev came to destroying the world by putting missiles in Cuba.
posts.key_insight: The best software leaders combine relentless intellectual speed with genuine openness to being wrong, and even tiny UX shortcomings in platforms can have industry-threatening consequences.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky criticizes tech companies for using meaningless corporate jargon on their websites instead of clearly explaining what their products do. He singles out KnowNow and GrandCentral as examples of companies whose homepages are completely inscrutable, even to a veteran software designer. He argues that if he can't understand what a product does after twenty years in software, the companies are failing at basic communication and hurting their own products.
key_points: Tech company websites frequently use vague, buzzword-laden descriptions that fail to communicate what their products actually do
If your target audience (experienced software professionals) can't understand your product description, you have a communication problem that's hurting sales
Corporate jargon like 'Internet-scale event routing solutions' and 'enabling the real-time enterprise' are meaningless to the people making purchase decisions
Companies inflate simple facts with unnecessary complexity — 'four main categories of functionality' instead of just 'four features'
money_quotes: When I go to a company's web site and it says that their product 'provides Internet-scale event routing solutions that seamlessly integrate information among Web services, applications and users,' I want to barf.
I'm a software designer! I'm your target audience, and I can't make head or tail of it!
I have a feeling that both of these products are interesting and useful. But listen, bubbie: if I can't understand it, and I've been writing software for twenty years, then I don't know who will.
You're ruining your products because you can't explain in two sentences what they do in a way which would be meaningful to the people who are going to make the purchase decision.
I think that means 'Grand Central has four features.' Maybe somebody thought that it didn't sound cool enough that way.
posts.key_insight: If you can't explain your product in plain language that your target audience understands, your marketing is actively sabotaging your product.

posts.summary: This is a very brief post consisting of a single self-quote from Joel's longer article 'Good Software Takes Ten Years. Get Used To It.' The quote criticizes the software industry practice of adding and removing features as a way to justify new paid versions, using Microsoft's Clippy as the canonical example of a pointless feature addition.
key_points: Software companies struggle to justify new versions when they run out of meaningful features to add
The cycle of adding then removing unwanted features (like Clippy) is a cynical revenue strategy
Customers see through the tactic of charging for both additions and removals of features
money_quotes: Now the trouble comes when you can't think of any new features, so you put in the paperclip, and then you take out the paperclip, and you try to charge people both times, and they aren't falling for it.
posts.key_insight: When software companies run out of genuine improvements, they resort to churning features in and out to justify new paid releases, and users recognize the ploy.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief status update where Joel announces his return from a break and mentions being overwhelmed by accumulated email. There is no substantive argument or technical content. It serves purely as a placeholder post signaling his presence back on the blog.
key_points: Joel returned from an absence and posted a minimal update
Email backlog is referenced as the main challenge upon return
money_quotes: I'm back! … still trying to dig out from under a big pile o' email.
posts.key_insight: This is a trivial status update with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post for FogBUGZ 2.03, a bug-fix release from Fog Creek Software. Joel notes that all known bugs have been fixed in this release. He directs customers who didn't receive upgrade notifications to contact him. The post primarily serves to promote his longer article 'Hard-assed Bug Fixin'' which details their bug-fixing process.
key_points: FogBUGZ 2.03 is a strictly bug-fix release with all known bugs resolved
Fog Creek proactively notified customers by email about the upgrade
The post links to a longer article detailing their rigorous bug-fixing methodology
money_quotes: This is strictly a bug-fix release in which we have fixed all known bugs.
posts.key_insight: A short product announcement emphasizing Fog Creek's commitment to shipping bug-free software and their transparency about the bug-fixing process.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that fixing bugs should be treated as an economic decision, not a moral imperative. He lays out a framework for calculating whether a bug fix is worth the cost by comparing the value of the fix against the cost of developer time. Using Fog Creek's own experience with FogBUGZ, he demonstrates how they calculated the ROI of a two-week bug-fixing sprint: $48,000 in developer time for $150,000 in tech support savings. He advocates for systematic bug collection, charging tech support costs back to product units to create proper incentives, and making data-driven decisions about which bugs to fix. Despite the economic framing, he concludes by erring on the side of quality, noting the hidden value of a rock-solid product.
key_points: Fixing bugs is only worthwhile when the value of the fix exceeds the cost of fixing it, and developers should make this calculation explicitly rather than treating all bug fixes as inherently good.

Companies should charge tech support costs back to product units to create the right economic incentives for fixing bugs, rather than making tech support a self-funding operation that hides the true cost of bugs.
Systematic crash reporting and treating every tech support call as evidence of a bug are essential for discovering which bugs actually matter to users.
Fog Creek calculated their bug-fixing sprint as a 3:1 ROI ($48K spent for $150K in tech support savings alone), demonstrating how to justify quality work in business terms.
Despite the ruthlessly economic framing, there is hidden value in high-quality products that is hard to quantify — employee pride, customer loyalty, and reputation.
money_quotes: Fixing bugs is only important when the value of having the bug fixed exceeds the cost of the fixing it.
By moving the economic burden of bugs onto the users themselves, you lose what limited ability you might have had to detect the damage they were causing.
It's hard to overcome the reputation of having a buggy product.
If you have to decide between fixing the bug for OS/2 guy and adding a new feature that will sell 20,000 copies of your software to General Electric, well, sorry, OS/2 guy.
When you wait long enough and have millions of users and religiously collect their stack dumps, you will find crashes in places like that and you won't believe your eyes.
I believe that there is a lot of hidden value to producing very high quality products that is not very easy to capture.
A single tech support call about the video drivers would blow away the profit you could make from twenty copies of your product.
posts.key_insight: Bug fixing should be driven by economic analysis — calculate the cost of fixing versus the cost of not fixing — but when in doubt, err on the side of quality because reputation and pride have real if hard-to-measure value.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky enthusiastically recommends VMWare for software configuration testing, describing how he set up dozens of virtual operating system configurations on a single machine. He explains how virtual machines allow testing complex installation scenarios across different Windows versions, running multiple servers simultaneously, and using 'undoable hard drives' to quickly reset to pristine states. He argues that every tester, developer, and support person should use it, while noting a minor complaint about unformatted virtual hard drives making initial OS installation tedious.
key_points: VMWare virtual machines enable testing software installations across dozens of OS configurations on a single physical computer
The 'undoable hard drives' feature is critical for testing installers on pristine systems, preventing the common mistake of shipping software that only works on developer machines
Virtual machines serve multiple roles beyond testing: safe evaluation of beta software, product support assistance, and cross-platform software usage
Even powerful tools have friction points — setting up NT 4.0 from scratch took three days due to undocumented FAT16 formatting requirements
money_quotes: Testing on pristine computers is essential. Otherwise you may ship an installer which works perfectly on your developers' computers, because they have all kinds of goodies loaded, but which simply does not work on typical users' computers.
This is such an excellent product that I think every tester, developer, and product support person should have it.
It's also a great way to try out scary software (like Windows XP, Office XP, or the .NET Beta) without messing up your primary computer.
Every time I ran SETUP and it didn't work, with one click I could wipe out all the changes I had made to the hard drive and go back to a pristine Windows installation.
It took me almost three days to install an NT 4.0 system from scratch, because I had the hardest time figuring out that the NT 4.0 installer requires a FAT 16 formatted hard drive.
posts.key_insight: Virtual machines are indispensable for software testing because they let you verify installations on pristine systems — catching the bugs that hide on developer machines loaded with dependencies.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky recommends Rick Chapman's 'The Product Marketing Handbook for Software' as an essential resource for software developers who lack marketing knowledge. He discovered the book through a book swap with the author and read it in a single weekend. Joel describes it as a comprehensive brain dump from a channel marketing veteran, covering everything from retail distribution deals to UPC codes. He considers it indispensable for anyone thinking of creating shrink-wrap software.
key_points: Technical founders often have deep product design skills but lack marketing knowledge, creating a critical gap
Channel marketing for shrink-wrap software involves complex logistics like SPIFs, UPCs, and retailer deals that most developers never consider
Practical, experience-based marketing knowledge is more valuable than theoretical frameworks for software entrepreneurs
money_quotes: I found a great resource for someone like me who knows how to design a software product and get it built, but doesn't know the first thing about marketing it.
It's the education you would receive if you captured a veteran of channel marketing and gave him a truth drug.
It's a complete braindump covering just about everything you should know if you're thinking of creating shrink-wrap software.
posts.key_insight: Software developers who can build great products still need to fill their marketing knowledge gap, and learning the practical realities of channel distribution is essential for shipping shrink-wrap software.

posts.summary: Joel discusses two mundane office topics: choosing a firewall appliance and dealing with aggressive telemarketing from MCI. The bulk of the post focuses on his frustrating experience with MCI telemarketers who exploited a legal loophole requiring customers to use the exact phrase 'please put me on your do-not-call list' before they would stop calling. Joel questions the business logic of deliberately annoying potential customers and promotes New York State's do-not-call registry as a solution.
key_points: MCI telemarketers were trained to ignore removal requests unless the customer used the legally exact phrase 'please put me on your do-not-call list'
Joel questions the management logic behind policies that deliberately antagonize potential customers
New York State offered a do-not-call web form that could result in $2000 fines for violators
The post captures a pre-national-Do-Not-Call-Registry moment in telemarketing regulation history
money_quotes: Hi, this is your local phone company,' they lied.
Apparently the telemarketers are now being trained that unless they hear this magic phrase word for word, they should keep calling you.
Why do they want to call me if they know it makes me angry? What kind of company would do that?
It can't possibly help and can only hurt.
If enough people (and states) figure this out, maybe one day telemarketing will be worthless and nobody will bother any more.
posts.key_insight: Companies that optimize for exploiting legal loopholes to keep annoying customers are engaging in self-defeating management logic that can only damage their brand.

posts.summary: Spolsky discusses research from the Journal of Experimental Psychology that supports his long-held claim that software developers cannot effectively multitask. He argues that task switching between projects incurs massive overhead, and that developers assigned to multiple simultaneous projects produce far less than if they worked sequentially. The real reason managers allow multitasking despite its productivity cost is that it obscures unrealistic schedules — working on three projects at once creates the illusion they'll all finish in a month. In reality, the work takes far longer and some tasks may never get completed at all.
key_points: Academic research confirms that rapid task switching incurs significant cognitive overhead, even beyond what the study directly measured.
Developers alternating between two major projects, even on different days, produce less total output than working sequentially.
Teams often take weeks to reach full productivity when starting a new project after finishing an old one.
Managers assign simultaneous projects not because it's efficient, but because it hides the fact that the schedule is unrealistic.
Multitasking creates an illusion of parallel progress while actually extending timelines dramatically.
money_quotes: The overhead of task switching can be huge.
Developers who have two major projects to work on, even if they alternate every other day, will not get as much done as if they do them sequentially.
Despite the fact that the multitasking dramatically reduces productivity, they're willing to do it because it obscures the schedule.

It seems like each of those tasks takes about a month. Do them in sequence, and three months will elapse. Do them at the same time, and it's possible to deceive yourself that only a month will elapse.
In reality, six months will elapse and one task won't even get done.
This just proves that the schedule is completely unrealistic.
posts.key_insight: Multitasking doesn't save time — it destroys productivity while creating the comforting illusion that unrealistic schedules are achievable.

posts.summary: This is a brief link post where Spolsky shares an interview he did with WebWord.com. The quoted excerpt emphasizes that building popular software requires far more than just writing code. He lists the many non-coding tasks that consume most of a software team's time, from documentation to usability testing to hiring. The core message is that software success depends on attending to a broad range of activities beyond programming itself.
