key_points: Building popular software requires dealing with many tasks beyond writing code, including manuals, icons, testing, and recruiting.
Non-coding activities consume most of a software team's time and energy.
Success in software is about the full product experience—usability, documentation, and people management—not just technical implementation.
money_quotes: If your software is going to be popular, you are going to have to deal with a million other things.

Writing manuals, creating good looking icons if you have a GUI, testing, recruiting good programmers, making a product that people want, usability testing, dealing with Norman the Cranky Tester, and about 100 other things that take up most of your time.
posts.key_insight: Writing code is only a small fraction of what it takes to ship popular software; the hundred other tasks around it are what consume most of your time.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to Joel's 'Ask Joel' column where he answers reader questions. The questions cover topics including learning C++ programming, tech companies in New York, Fog Creek's shrink-wrap software strategy, and Microsoft's use of Visual SourceSafe. The post is essentially a teaser with no substantive content of its own.
key_points: The post is a link/teaser to Joel's Q&A column 'Ask Joel'
Reader questions span education, NYC tech scene, business strategy, and development tools
No substantive arguments or insights are made in this particular post
posts.key_insight: This is a navigational post pointing readers to Joel's Q&A column rather than a standalone essay with its own argument.

posts.summary: In this mailbag-style post, Joel Spolsky responds to reader questions covering computer science education, software licensing, the tech job market during the dot-com crash, bootstrapping a software company, and version control tools. He shares candid details about Fog Creek's finances, revealing that FogBUGZ covers non-salary expenses while they bootstrap toward profitability with CityDesk. He advises a new grad to take a New York banking job and wait for better opportunities, and counsels an aspiring entrepreneur on the realities of building a product company from consulting revenue. The post offers a transparent window into the early-stage struggles and strategy of a small software company during the 2001 recession.
key_points: Computer science education should emphasize practical skills like database design, UI/UX, writing functional specs, debugging, and especially writing — because great programmers are great communicators.
During the 2001 dot-com crash, interesting R&D software jobs in New York City were scarce, but Microsoft offered valuable learning opportunities despite its suburban location.

Fog Creek's bootstrapping strategy involved using consulting revenue to fund product development, shipping FogBUGZ as a low-risk first product because they already had it, and conserving marketing spend for their bigger bet, CityDesk.
FogBUGZ succeeded in a crowded market of 1000 competitors by focusing on simplicity ('painless bug tracking') and fanatical customer service where callers spoke directly to programmers.
Joel recommends CVS over Visual SourceSafe, citing SourceSafe's poor reputation for data reliability.
money_quotes: Every local programmer I know complains all the time about how hard it is to get interesting software jobs that aren't at a bank.
The good thing about working for Microsoft is that Microsoft is one company that really knows how to make software, and you'll learn a lot that will help you wherever you go.
I think in your place I would come to the city and spend some time hopping around, waiting for something interesting to show up. Pay your dues at the banks and media companies like the rest of us!

It has more than paid for its own R&D costs, so now it feels like one of those mystical things you hear about on late-night TV that you set up and get a nice check every month in exchange for doing nothing.
There are about 1000 bug tracking packages on the market (from companies who are probably selling it for the same reason as we are — because they have it.)

People are also happy because we're a small company that provides fanatical customer service… when you find a bug and call our toll-free number, you'll speak to a programmer and probably get a bug fix shipped to you that day.

We hope to be able to demonstrate that x dollars spent on marketing results in x times y dollars in revenue. At this point, if y > 1, we can make a strong argument to raise money for marketing without giving up very much equity.
posts.key_insight: A bootstrapped software company can succeed even in a crowded market and a recession by shipping what it already has, providing exceptional customer service, and patiently reinvesting revenue rather than chasing outside funding.

posts.summary: This brief post shares a reader's account of Microsoft's internal source control practices during a 1997 internship. Ron Vogl describes how Microsoft used two competing tools: SourceSafe and an internal tool called Source Library Manager (SLM). Joel uses this anecdote to make a pointed observation: since Microsoft famously practices 'eating your own dog food,' the fact that many teams refused to use their own SourceSafe product speaks volumes about its quality.
key_points: Microsoft internally used both SourceSafe and an internal tool called SLM (Source Library Manager) in roughly equal amounts as of 1997
SLM was a command-line tool with a mix of SourceSafe and CVS-style workflows
Microsoft's own teams avoiding their own source control product is a damning signal about its quality, given their dogfooding culture
money_quotes: Given how devoted Microsoft is to eating your own dog food, the fact that there are still groups that don't use SourceSafe says something about how bad that product must be.
posts.key_insight: When a company famous for dogfooding its own products has teams that refuse to use one of them, that's the strongest possible indictment of the product's quality.

posts.summary: Joel shares a link to a presentation by Microsoft developer Mark Lucovsky about the Windows NT development process, highlighting the staggering scale of the codebase. He uses the massive numbers — 29 million lines of code, 50 gigabytes of source, 8-hour builds — to put everyday source control complaints in perspective. The brief post implicitly argues that neither common open-source nor Microsoft's own consumer-grade tools could handle development at this scale.
key_points: The Windows NT codebase comprised 29 million lines of code and 50 gigabytes of source files, illustrating enterprise-scale software complexity.
Even Microsoft didn't use their own SourceSafe product for a project of this magnitude, implying its inadequacy for large-scale development.
Joel suggests that CVS, the dominant open-source version control system at the time, would also fail at this scale.
The post highlights how extreme scale in software development demands specialized, purpose-built infrastructure.
money_quotes: 29 million lines of code. Source code takes up 50 gigabytes. Build takes 8 hours on a 4 CPU machine.
No, they don't use SourceSafe.
But I don't think CVS could handle it, either.
posts.key_insight: The sheer scale of large software projects like Windows NT exposes the limits of standard version control tools, requiring purpose-built infrastructure that most developers never encounter.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky provides a real-time personal account from Manhattan on September 11, 2001. Writing from the Upper West Side, about 5 miles from Ground Zero, he describes the surreal quiet of the city, streams of people walking home in business clothes, and the complete shutdown of mass transit. He recounts friends scattered across the city trying to get home, and ends with a sobering conversation with a policeman about the hundreds of first responders killed in the collapse.
key_points: Blood donation centers were immediately overwhelmed, turning away volunteers
The city's transportation infrastructure completely shut down, stranding thousands and forcing people to walk miles to get home
Despite the horror downtown, life continued in strange normalcy uptown — going to dinner, trying to get home
A policeman reported 400 police and firefighters killed while rescuing people inside the buildings, and shared the haunting memory of hearing trapped officers' screams over the radio
money_quotes: Up here on the Upper West Side, about 5 miles north of Ground Zero, I saw streams of people in business clothes walking home.
I saw people planning to walk all the way up to the George Washington bridge and then across to New Jersey where they lived.
The streets were very quiet around here, there was very little traffic.
He said he will always remember the screams he heard from trapped police over the radio.
He said that 400 policemen and firemen were killed when the buildings collapsed. They were in there rescuing people.
posts.key_insight: A firsthand account of September 11th from a New Yorker capturing the eerie calm, logistical chaos, and human toll as the city tried to process an incomprehensible event in real time.

posts.summary: Written the day after the September 11, 2001 attacks, this brief, poignant entry captures a moment of stillness in New York City. Spolsky observes the eerie beauty of perfect weather contrasted with the dust cloud still visible in the southern sky. He notes an elderly couple standing on a corner, holding hands and looking skyward, simply grateful to be alive. The post says everything by saying almost nothing.
key_points: The contrast between perfect weather and catastrophic destruction creates an unsettling dissonance
Small human gestures — holding hands, standing still — become profound in the aftermath of tragedy
The brevity itself is the message: some events render commentary inadequate
The dust in the southern sky serves as both literal observation and metaphor for what was lost
money_quotes: Quiet day.
The southern sky is still filled with white dust.
They weren't going anywhere, just standing outside looking at the sky, happy to be here.
posts.key_insight: In the face of catastrophe, the most powerful writing is simple witness — observing the dust in the sky and an old couple holding hands says more than any analysis could.

posts.summary: Spolsky criticizes Gartner's recommendation that Microsoft completely rewrite IIS to address security vulnerabilities. He argues that Gartner falls into the common trap of believing new code is inherently less buggy than old code. He points out that IIS has been battle-tested for six years across millions of servers, and a rewrite would simply introduce a fresh set of unknown bugs. He dismisses the recommendation as reflecting the mindset of immature developers who lack real software experience.
key_points: Completely rewriting software does not make it less buggy — it simply trades known bugs for unknown ones
Battle-tested code that has been publicly deployed for years has had its bugs discovered and fixed through real-world usage
Institutional knowledge about past bugs is fragile — team members who fixed old bugs may no longer remember them or even be on the team
The desire to rewrite from scratch is a hallmark of inexperienced developers
money_quotes: Gartner seems to suffer the common but moronic fallacy that new or "completely rewritten" code is somehow less buggy than old code.
Completely rewriting it would just introduce another set of bugs that would take another few years to find.
IIS has been publically tested, for about six years now, on millions of web servers and with thousands of hackers trying to find bugs.
Chances are that nobody on the current IIS team even remembers the bugs they fixed five years ago.
Completely rewriting code is a big-time mistake common of immature developers with no real software experience.
posts.key_insight: Rewriting battle-tested software from scratch doesn't eliminate bugs — it merely replaces known, fixed bugs with a fresh crop of unknown ones.

posts.summary: This is a brief transitional post where Joel Spolsky explains his recent silence on the blog, attributing it to intense work at Fog Creek Software preparing for the beta release of CityDesk. He announces his intention to write about the design and development process behind CityDesk, linking to the first installment of that series.
key_points: Joel had been quiet on his blog due to focused product development work at Fog Creek
CityDesk was being prepared for its beta release and was considered Fog Creek's flagship product
Joel planned to document and share the design and development process behind CityDesk
money_quotes: I've been rather quiet lately on this weblog — mainly because we've been working so hard at Fog Creek getting ready for the beta of CityDesk, our flagship product.
posts.key_insight: This is a placeholder announcement post with no substantive argument, serving only to flag an upcoming series on CityDesk's development.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky introduces CityDesk, a Windows-based content management system built by Fog Creek Software. The product separates content from design, allowing non-technical users to manage websites using a WYSIWYG editor without knowing HTML. CityDesk supports multi-language publishing, regional editions, scheduled publishing, and multiple output formats from a single content source. The post serves as a product announcement ahead of its October 2001 beta launch, emphasizing ease of use backed by a robust database engine.
key_points: CityDesk separates content from presentation, allowing site-wide design changes from a single template modification
The product targets non-technical users who need to manage frequently-updated websites without HTML knowledge
Built-in multi-language support includes a split-screen translation interface with warnings for untranslated content
Multiple output formats (web, printer-friendly, Palm Pilot, large-font, print) can be generated from a single article entry
A database engine enables simultaneous multi-user editing without conflicts
money_quotes: With CityDesk, people who don't know anything about HTML or web servers can easily add, edit, and remove articles from a web site, using a program that is as easy as a word processor.
The real power in CityDesk comes from the fact that you only design the formatting of your site once.
Because CityDesk keeps the text of the articles separate from their design, you can change the design in one place, and every article on your site changes accordingly.
You can even have a whole newsroom banging away at the same time; even a virtual newsroom, with contributors all over the Internet!
Just write the article and set the date range, and CityDesk will never publish it before or after those dates. This is a good way to keep fresh content on your web site when you're on vacation.
You only have to type in the article once, and it will automatically be published in each and every format without any additional work.
posts.key_insight: A content management system that strictly separates content from presentation empowers non-technical users to maintain complex, multi-format websites without understanding the underlying technology.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky describes the final days before shipping CityDesk's beta, noting that the team is on schedule thanks to estimates made months earlier. He contrasts the calm, quiet reality of a well-managed ship cycle with Hollywood's frantic last-minute coding trope. The post emphasizes that good teams run out of work before the deadline rather than scrambling to finish. He also argues strongly for reaching zero known bugs before releasing a beta, explaining that known bugs waste beta testers' time and goodwill.
key_points: Well-estimated schedules lead to quiet, calm ship days rather than last-minute scrambles
Teams that write code until the last minute produce poor-quality software
Reaching zero known bugs before a beta release is essential to get useful feedback from testers
Known bugs in a beta waste tester goodwill and prevent them from finding unknown issues
Regular schedule check-ins with revised estimates and feature cuts keep projects on track
money_quotes: On good teams, the days before shipping just get quieter and quieter as programmers literally run out of things to do one at a time.
No code works the way you expected it to work the first time, and if you're writing code up until the last minute, it's going to suck.
It is crucial to get to zero known bugs before releasing a beta.
It is ok to have bugs in the beta, just not known bugs.
If you don't, you'll waste a lot of time during the beta reading 200 emails about a bug that you already knew about.

A common misconception, I assume popularized by Hollywood, is that as you get closer to shipping software, activity becomes frenetic as everybody scrambles to finish all the things that need to be done in time for the deadline.
posts.key_insight: A well-run software project gets quieter as the ship date approaches, and releasing a beta with zero known bugs is essential to getting valuable feedback from testers.

posts.summary: This is a very brief announcement post where Joel mentions ongoing work on CityDesk and introduces a new discussion board for the Joel on Software community. He notes the board isn't finished yet and warns that early messages may be deleted when the final version launches.
key_points: Joel is actively developing CityDesk, Fog Creek's content management product
A new community discussion board is being launched at discuss.fogcreek.com
The board is released in an incomplete state, inviting early participation despite impermanence
money_quotes: It's not totally done yet, but feel free to start playing with it.
Messages you post will probably disappear in a couple of days when I put up the final version.
posts.key_insight: A minor announcement post reflecting Joel's willingness to ship early and iterate, even at the cost of losing early user-generated content.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky recounts his frustrating three-day attempt to install discussion board software for CityDesk beta testers. After trying multiple packages — Discus, IdealBB, Manila, and Lusenet — each with significant drawbacks ranging from buggy code to excessive complexity, he gave up and wrote his own simple discussion board in two hours using ASP and VBScript. Along the way, he articulates his design philosophy: for a discussion board serving mostly casual users, learnability and simplicity should trump power-user features. The post doubles as a critique of how difficult server software installation remains compared to desktop software.
key_points: Server software installation is far harder than desktop software due to permissions, dependencies, database servers, and configuration — and neither free nor commercial software solves this well.
When designing for casual or occasional users, learnability and simplicity must take priority over power-user features and usability optimizations.
Most existing discussion boards inherit from BBS culture and are cluttered with features that serve power users while alienating novices.
Requiring membership to post is enough to turn away 90% of casual visitors who might otherwise contribute.
Sometimes building a minimal custom solution is faster and more effective than wrestling with existing packages that don't fit your needs.
money_quotes: Installing server software is just much harder than installing Windows software.
In designing a UI for anything, the very first question you always want to ask is: who is the user?
For casual users, learnability and simplicity are more important than usability and power.
All of these features are neat for power users but they just clutter up the interface for novices.

It's not uncommon for a program to start out simple and evolve to be more complicated, and you can do this without hurting 'average' usability because your users are getting more experienced on average.
Manila requires membership to post a message, and in my experience that is enough to turn away 90% of the casual visitors who might otherwise use the discussion board.
I realized that the software I want consists of exactly one table, and I could write the thing myself in less time than it took me to install some of these packages.
posts.key_insight: When your users are casual and occasional, ruthlessly prioritize simplicity over features — and if no existing tool embodies that philosophy, building your own minimal solution may be faster than fighting the wrong tool.

posts.summary: This short blog post covers three topics: a teaser for Joel's article defending Not-Invented-Here Syndrome in great teams, an announcement of Fog Creek's new discussion board, and a reflection on UI design trade-offs when using underline-styled edit boxes instead of standard HTML input boxes. Joel acknowledges that replacing standard form controls with underlines reduces learnability but admits he finds them too visually appealing to revert. The post illustrates Joel's characteristic tension between advocating UI consistency principles and occasionally breaking them for aesthetic reasons.
key_points: Great programming teams often reject external code not out of arrogance but because maintaining quality and shipping on time requires owning your dependencies.
Standard HTML widgets provide consistency and learnability, which are core usability principles.
Aesthetic preferences can conflict with usability best practices, and even advocates of consistency sometimes choose style over convention.
Joel openly admits the hypocrisy of violating his own UI consistency guidelines because the underline design looks cool.
money_quotes: When you're working on a really, really good team with great programmers, everybody else's code, frankly, is bug-infested garbage, and nobody else knows how to ship on time.
I like to make fun of designers who reinvent common widgets simply because they can.
The big advantage of using a standard HTML edit box is consistency — more people will recognize it and know how to use it.
It probably does reduce learnability, marginally, to use underlines for edit boxes instead of boxes.
But then again, they're really cool looking and I can't bear to switch them back to boxes.
posts.key_insight: Even principled advocates of UI consistency will bend their own rules when aesthetics prove too compelling to resist, revealing the eternal tension between usability dogma and design taste.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky challenges the conventional wisdom that code reuse is always good and reinventing the wheel is always bad. He draws on his experience at Microsoft, where the Excel team's refusal to take on external dependencies allowed them to ship on time with high-quality code. He extends the argument beyond software to business generally, arguing that outsourcing core functions leads to loss of control and competitive disadvantage. His conclusion is that core business functions should always be done in-house, while non-core functions can be safely outsourced.
key_points: The Excel team's 'find the dependencies and eliminate them' philosophy led to consistently on-time shipping and high-quality code, despite seeming wasteful
When you have a great team, external code is often lower quality and introduces risks you can't control — growing your own lavender matters when you're a great chef
The dotcom-era vision of fully virtual companies that outsource everything fails because the market pays for value added, and outsourcing critical functions means losing control
The rule is simple: if it's a core business competency, build it yourself; if it's not, outsource it
Companies like Amazon and Google gain competitive advantages precisely because they build core infrastructure themselves rather than using off-the-shelf solutions
money_quotes: Find the dependencies — and eliminate them.
When you're working on a really, really good team with great programmers, everybody else's code, frankly, is bug-infested garbage, and nobody else knows how to ship on time.

When you're a cordon bleu chef and you need fresh lavender, you grow it yourself instead of buying it in the farmers' market, because sometimes they don't have fresh lavender or they have old lavender which they pass off as fresh.
If it's a core business function — do it yourself, no matter what.

Two yuppies in a living room buying an e-commerce engine from company A and selling merchandise made by company B and warehoused and shipped by company C, with customer service from company D, isn't honestly adding much value.

If you outsource fulfillment, and your fulfillment partner has a different idea about what constitutes prompt delivery, your customers are not going to be happy, and there's nothing you can do about it.
The only exception to this rule, I suspect, is if your own people are more incompetent than everyone else, so whenever you try to do anything in house, it's botched up.
posts.key_insight: The conventional wisdom that code reuse is always superior to building in-house is wrong — core business functions should always be built internally because dependencies sacrifice control, quality, and competitive advantage.

posts.summary: This is a short status update post from Joel Spolsky covering several operational issues at Fog Creek Software. The server keeps crashing due to overheating, prompting an upgrade to Dell rack servers. A mailing list bug caused duplicate emails during the crashes. The CityDesk beta has launched and is already surfacing bugs that months of internal testing missed. The discussion forums have a clever trick for tracking unread topics without server-side state.
key_points: Fog Creek's home-built server kept crashing from overheating, highlighting the limits of DIY infrastructure for a growing company
The CityDesk beta immediately surfaced bugs that months of internal testing and 329 bug fixes had missed, validating the importance of external beta testing
A stateless UI trick for tracking read/unread forum topics demonstrates clever engineering within constraints
money_quotes: I guess it's time to replace it with a real industrial strength server…
We've been banging on this dang thing for months. We fixed 329 bugs. Then Brad W. downloads it, plays for ten seconds, and finds another.
posts.key_insight: No matter how thoroughly you test internally, real users will find bugs within seconds of touching your software, which is exactly why you do a beta.

posts.summary: This is a brief status update post where Joel asks readers for recommendations on GUI automated testing tools for CityDesk, their Windows desktop product. He wants to build an automated test suite to address regression bugs during the beta period. He also notes being buried in deferred work now that the beta has shipped.
key_points: Regression bugs were a real problem: they were breaking two things for every one they fixed, motivating investment in automated testing
Joel wanted affordable testing tools, explicitly ruling out expensive enterprise solutions that require sales demos
Shipping a beta creates a backlog of deferred work that hits all at once afterward
money_quotes: I'm going to take advantage of the CityDesk beta to develop an automated test suite (so we don't keep breaking 2 things for every 1 thing we fix!)
It needs to support Windows apps and be the sort of thing that doesn't involve a salesperson visiting your office with a $6000 projector for the demo (in other words, not too expensive.)
Now I'm snowed under with all the stuff I was deferring until the beta shipped
posts.key_insight: Automated testing becomes urgent when regression bugs outpace fixes, but small companies need affordable tools without enterprise sales overhead.

posts.summary: In this brief update post, Joel Spolsky defends his choice to use Visual Basic despite its reputation as an uncool language, arguing that productivity matters more than syntax aesthetics. He provides updates on CityDesk beta testing, explaining delays are due to bug fixing to avoid duplicate feedback. He also mentions refinements to his discussion board, including anti-spam measures for email addresses.
key_points: Visual Basic's lack of curly braces makes developers feel uncool, but productivity should outweigh peer perception
CityDesk beta releases are being deliberately delayed to fix known bugs before expanding the tester pool
Discussion board improvements include protecting user email addresses from spam harvesters
money_quotes: The worst part about coding in Visual Basic is that people think you're not cool because your code doesn't have {'s and }'s.
I can live with the shame if it means I'm more productive.
posts.key_insight: Choosing a programming language for productivity over perceived coolness is a pragmatic trade-off worth making.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky explains why Fog Creek chose Visual Basic for CityDesk's GUI development, arguing that top-notch C++ programmers become extraordinarily productive when working in VB. He divides programmers into three tiers based on their ability to handle pointers, then argues that the single most impactful language feature for productivity is managed memory, not OOP or any other paradigm. He acknowledges VB's limitations but notes the ability to drop into C++ for performance-critical code makes it a practical choice. He predicts C# will eventually succeed VB in the Windows GUI space, while dismissing Java for desktop apps due to its non-native feel.
key_points: Programmers fall into three groups: those who can't program, those who can but not with pointers, and the elite few who handle pointers and indirection naturally.
The only language feature that genuinely improves productivity is managed memory (garbage collection or reference counting), not OOP, UML, Java, or any other fad.
Expert C++ programmers writing in VB become 'super-efficient coding machines' producing clean, object-oriented code without wasting time on low-level plumbing.
VB's ability to drop into C/C++ for performance-critical inner loops provides an escape hatch that makes it viable for commercial applications.
Java's GUI libraries produce applications that look and feel non-native, making it unsuitable for desktop software where user experience is the competitive advantage.
money_quotes: For some reason, some people just do not seem to be able to write code with pointers in it. They were born without the part of the brain that does indirection, I guess.

What I wondered was, what happens if you take top-notch C++ programmers who dream in pointers, and let them code in VB. What I discovered at Fog Creek was that they become super-efficient coding machines.
The only thing that improves your programming productivity is using managed code – that is, using a language in which memory management is automatic.
I don't care how you do it, just let me concatenate strings without thinking about where the new bigger string will go and I'll be happy.
And the worst part about coding in VB is that people think you're not cool because your code doesn't have {'s and }'s. I can live with the shame if it means I'm more productive.
Every Java app LOOKS like a Java app, takes forever to launch, and just doesn't feel completely native.
At some point, you have to stop debating and write code!
posts.key_insight: The biggest productivity gain in programming comes not from paradigms or methodologies but from automatic memory management, and skilled programmers should use the highest-level tool that gets the job done rather than chasing language prestige.

posts.summary: This is an ultra-brief link post where Joel Spolsky shares a link to Orisinal, a collection of Flash games by Ferry Halim, calling them some of the nicest he has ever seen. The post contains no argument or analysis — it is simply a recommendation. At only 9 words, it reflects the casual, link-blog style common in early 2000s blogging.
key_points: Joel shares a link to Ferry Halim's Orisinal Flash games collection
The post is a pure link recommendation with no further commentary or analysis
money_quotes: Some of the nicest flash games I've ever seen.
posts.key_insight: A minimal link-blog post recommending Ferry Halim's Flash games, with no substantive argument or insight.

posts.summary: Joel provides a brief update on the CityDesk beta testing process, which is going well in the sense that they're finding lots of bugs. They released to about 100 testers and received most bug reports within 24 hours, primarily configuration issues related to different Windows environments. He outlines their plan to iterate with progressively larger beta groups before a December 1st release.
key_points: Most beta bug reports arrived within the first 24 hours of release, suggesting initial tester enthusiasm is critical to capture
The majority of bugs were configuration issues — different IE versions, font settings, and OS differences between Windows 2000 and NT 4.0
They used multiple feedback channels: discussion groups, email, in-app feedback, and automatic crash reporting integrated with FogBUGZ
They deliberately limited beta size to ~100 people because handling feedback from more would have been unmanageable for a small company
money_quotes: Our beta is going extremely well, meaning, we're finding lots of bugs.
It would be almost impossible for a small company to find these kinds of bugs without beta testers.
And it would have been impossible for us to handle the feedback from more than about 100 people!
People have funny versions of Internet Explorer. People have their computers set to display everything in large fonts.
If CityDesk crashes, it offers to send a bug report to us, which goes right into FogBUGZ.
posts.key_insight: A successful beta is one that finds lots of bugs, and small companies should deliberately limit beta group size to what they can actually handle rather than casting a wide net.

posts.summary: Spolsky presents a comprehensive guide to user interface design aimed at programmers, built around one central axiom: a UI is well-designed when the program behaves exactly how the user expected it to. Drawing from psychology research on learned helplessness, he argues that users' happiness depends on feeling in control of their environment, and that tiny frustrations from unexpected behavior accumulate into genuine unhappiness. He covers practical principles including matching the program model to the user model, minimizing unnecessary choices, using affordances and metaphors, maintaining consistency with platform conventions, designing for users who don't read and can't precisely control the mouse, and using activity-based planning. The book concludes with a six-step design process: invent imaginary users, identify activities, understand user models, sketch designs, iterate, and test with real humans.
key_points: The cardinal axiom of UI design is that a program should behave exactly how the user expected it to, based on their mental model formed from prior experience with other software and real-world analogies.

Every option you provide forces the user to make a decision — designers should make hard choices themselves rather than abdicating responsibility by exposing configuration that users don't care about.

Consistency with platform conventions matters more than creativity or correctness — millions of users trained on existing software will expect your program to work the same way, and deviating from that causes real frustration.
Users don't read instructions, can't precisely control the mouse, and can't remember things — design must accommodate these constraints rather than assuming attentive, skilled, motivated users.

Activity-based planning (designing around what users are trying to accomplish rather than around feature lists) produces dramatically better software, as demonstrated by Excel 5.0's discovery that most users just kept lists.
money_quotes: A user interface is well-designed when the program behaves exactly how the user thought it would.
Every time you provide an option, you're asking the user to make a decision.
It is the height of arrogance for a software designer to inflict a choice like this on the user simply because the designer couldn't think hard enough to decide which option is really better.
Your emotions don't seem to care about the magnitude of the event, only the quality.
The more you feel that you can control your environment, and that the things you do are actually working, the happier you are.
Being inconsistent is almost always more work than just acting like your platform expects you to act.

Using a command line interface is like having to learn Korean to order food in a Seoul branch of McDonalds. Using a menu based interface is like being able to point to the food you want and nod your head vigorously.
There is a much worse kind of arrogance in software design: the arrogant assumption that 'my software is so damn cool, people are just going to have to warp their brains around it.'
posts.key_insight: User happiness stems from feeling in control of their environment, so UI design must prioritize matching the user's mental model over technical elegance, creative expression, or feature richness.

posts.summary: Joel shares a brief update linking to his CityDesk development series about dealing with buggy third-party code libraries. He also recommends a Harvard philosophy professor's guide on writing, arguing that its advice about writing for a 'lazy, stupid, and mean' reader is the best guidance programmers can follow when designing and documenting their code.
key_points: Third-party code dependencies can introduce crashing bugs that are unacceptable in your own shipped product
Good writing advice from other disciplines (philosophy) applies directly to programming documentation
Writers should assume their reader is lazy, stupid, and mean — meaning they won't work to understand you, need simple explanations, and will interpret ambiguity uncharitably
money_quotes: For the umpteenth time, I found myself dependent on a code library which had a crashing bug that was unacceptable in code I shipped.
What are you supposed to do if you're the chef at Les Halles and your fishmonger is giving you smelly fish?
Pretend that your reader is lazy, stupid, and mean.
He's lazy in that he doesn't want to figure out what your convoluted sentences are supposed to mean, and he doesn't want to figure out what your argument is, if it's not already obvious.
I can think of no better advice for the kind of writing that programmers should be doing in designing and documenting their code.
posts.key_insight: Programmers should write documentation and design specs as if their reader is lazy, stupid, and mean — making arguments obvious, explanations simple, and language unambiguous.

posts.summary: Joel briefly notes that Fog Creek is releasing a new beta of CityDesk while simultaneously migrating servers, acknowledging this is risky timing. He warns that discussion groups may be unreliable during the transition. He expresses frustration with DNS caching behavior, where IP address changes take far longer to propagate than the configured TTL should allow.
key_points: Fog Creek is releasing a new CityDesk beta while simultaneously migrating servers, which Joel recognizes as risky
DNS TTL settings are often ignored by intermediate resolvers, causing stale IP addresses to persist for days
Joel shows self-awareness about combining two risky operations at once
money_quotes: Is that asking for trouble, or what?
I hate the way that when I change a server's IP address, even though our TTL is set to 15 minutes, people are still using the old IP address almost a week later.
posts.key_insight: DNS TTL settings are frequently ignored by intermediate caching resolvers, making server migrations unpredictably slow to propagate.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post where Joel Spolsky recommends a taskbar utility created by David Bau, a former Internet Explorer developer. The tool combines search, address bar, and calculator functionality into a single taskbar widget. The post is essentially a link recommendation with minimal commentary, offering just a one-sentence description and download link.
key_points: Joel highlights a multi-purpose taskbar utility that combines search, address bar, and calculator functions
The tool was built by David Bau, whose credibility is established by his background on the Internet Explorer team
The post exemplifies early 2000s blogging style: short link-sharing posts with brief personal endorsement
money_quotes: David Bau, a veteran of the Internet Explorer development team, wrote an incredibly cool search utility/address bar/calculator for your taskbar.
posts.key_insight: A brief link recommendation post highlighting a developer tool, representative of early blogging as a link-sharing medium.

posts.summary: Joel announces the migration of Joel on Software from UserLand Manila to his own company's product, CityDesk, a desktop content management tool. He explains the practical differences between the two approaches: Manila dynamically generated pages from a database, while CityDesk produces static HTML files uploaded via FTP. The migration involved tedious manual copy-and-paste work, which doubled as thorough testing of CityDesk. He emphasizes his commitment to avoiding linkrot by implementing redirects for all old URLs. The post also briefly teases a reader question about 'hit and run management' style.
key_points: Joel migrated his blog from UserLand Manila to CityDesk, his own company's product, practicing what he preaches about eating your own dogfood
CityDesk uses a static HTML generation model (desktop app producing files uploaded via FTP) rather than Manila's dynamic database-driven page rendering
The painful manual migration process served as a rigorous real-world test of CityDesk's capabilities
Joel prioritized URL preservation by implementing redirects to prevent linkrot during the migration
money_quotes: My company's product CityDesk is in beta, and I'm a strong believer in eating your own dogfood.
CityDesk is a desktop application — I'm running it on my own Windows 2000 laptop. It produces plain ol' static HTML files which are then ftp'ed to a generic web server.
Porting Joel on Software to CityDesk involved a lot of manual copying-and-pasting — something I never would have had the patience for if it wasn't for the opportunity to thoroughly test CityDesk.
I'm bending over backwards not to create linkrot — all old links to Joel on Software stories have been replaced with redirects, so they should still work.
Joel on Software has always been free and ad-free, thanks to sponsorship from UserLand and from Fog Creek Software.
posts.key_insight: Using your own product (dogfooding) turns tedious migration work into valuable product testing, aligning business incentives with quality assurance.

posts.summary: Joel briefly describes a bug that only affected users running Hebrew or Greek versions of Windows 2000. With the help of beta testers, developer Michael tracked it down to a library using Chr(255) as a separator character, which got remapped through non-Latin code pages. They used VMWare to install Hebrew Windows 2000 for debugging. The post illustrates how character encoding assumptions can cause subtle, locale-specific bugs.
key_points: A library's use of Chr(255) as a separator character caused bugs on non-Latin Windows locales
The bug only manifested on Hebrew and Greek versions of Windows 2000 due to code page character remapping
VMWare was used as a practical tool for reproducing locale-specific bugs without dedicated hardware
Beta testers were essential in identifying and tracking down the internationalization issue
money_quotes: One of the libraries we were using was using Chr(255) as a separator character in lists.
This got mapped through the Hebrew and Greek code pages to a different character (y umlaut was mapped to plain y).
posts.key_insight: Using high-ASCII characters as sentinel values in code is fragile because code page mappings differ across locales, causing subtle bugs for international users.

posts.summary: Joel provides a brief development update on CityDesk Beta 3, describing the process of squashing difficult bugs before release. The team fixed issues ranging from mysterious crashes to HTML corruption caused by a Microsoft library function they had to reimplement. Despite declaring the beta ready, they had to release it four times in one day as show-stopper bugs kept appearing. The post captures the unglamorous reality of shipping software.
key_points: The team fixed deeply challenging bugs including non-reproducible crashes, HTML corruption from a Microsoft library, and internationalization issues
Sometimes you have to reimplement vendor library functions when they're the source of bugs you can't otherwise fix
Beta 3 was released four times in a single day as new show-stopper bugs kept surfacing after each release
Performance was improved by caching CRCs for incremental publishing
money_quotes: For this beta we found, and fixed, some of the nastiest, hairiest, scariest bugs that we thought we'd never be able to fix.
That bug, incidentally, was in a Microsoft library function, which we reimplemented ourselves.
We managed to release Beta 3 today – four times!
Scary little show-stopper bugs kept popping up.
Fog Creek Software: "We Make Lots of Bugs, But At Least We Fix Them!"
posts.key_insight: Shipping software is a messy, iterative process where fixing the last bugs often reveals new ones, and sometimes the bugs are in someone else's code that you have to rewrite yourself.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares updates on three topics: soliciting book recommendations for software management, celebrating CityDesk's ease-of-use as validation of avoiding venture capital influence, and enthusiastically agreeing with Alan Cooper's critique of web browsers as a retrograde UI architecture. He argues that browsers are essentially a revival of IBM's ancient CICS mainframe terminal model — server-rendered forms with minimal client-side intelligence. The post reflects Spolsky's broader philosophy that good software requires rich client-side UI, not thin browser-based interfaces. He credits CityDesk's usability to building a desktop application in Visual Basic rather than following the web-based content management trend.
key_points: CityDesk's ease-of-use came from avoiding VC pressure to copy competitors' web-based approaches and instead building a desktop application
Alan Cooper argues browsers are architecturally retrograde, reviving a 25-year-old IBM batch-processing model that was abandoned because it was bad
From a UI architecture perspective, web browsers are essentially CICS (IBM mainframe terminals) with fonts — server sends a form, user fills it in, sends it back
Being outside Silicon Valley's echo chamber allowed Fog Creek to make genuinely different (and better) product decisions
money_quotes: We're thanking our lucky stars that we didn't have a bunch of stupid venture capitalists forcing us to copy all the other content management companies.
We're grateful that we're not in Silicon Valley where everyone meets at Bucks and Stanford University seminars and copies each other's bad ideas.
CityDesk is the easiest content management software they've ever seen, full stop.
We have stepped backward in terms of user interface, capability, and the breadth of our thinking about what we could do as a civilization.
The browser is a very weak and stupid program because it was written as essentially a master's thesis inside a university and as an experiment.
From a UI architecture perspective, browsers are CICS + fonts.
The user suffers because the UI is abysmal and the programmer suffers because you can't MAKE a good UI no matter how hard you try.
posts.key_insight: Web browsers represent an architectural regression to mainframe-era terminal computing, and building rich desktop applications instead of browser-based ones produces fundamentally better user experiences.

posts.summary: Spolsky challenges Postel's Law—the principle that programs should be liberal in what they accept—arguing that HTML's evolution proved this approach flawed. He contends that stricter APIs and development environments produce more robust software. Using Java's fixed data type sizes as a positive example and citing Russian Field Marshal Suvorov's maxim about hard drills, he advocates for developing under the harshest possible conditions so that software handles real-world edge cases effortlessly.
key_points: Postel's Law ('be liberal in what you accept') led to the mess of HTML parsing and is not a sound engineering principle
Stricter APIs and compilers catch problems early, producing code that works reliably across diverse environments
Java's decision to fix data type sizes was superior to C's loose specification approach
Developers should intentionally use extreme, unusual system configurations during development to surface hidden assumptions
Robustness comes from training under harsh conditions, not from tolerating sloppy inputs
money_quotes: People understand instinctively that the best way for computer programs to communicate with each other is for each of the them to be strict in what they emit, and liberal in what they accept.
I think that the evolution of HTML has proven that this isn't such a great idea.
The stricter the API is about its input, the more likely the code is going to work in funny situations.
A hard drill makes an easy battle.
You want your compiler and your development environment to be as strict as possible.

You want to use French international settings on Chinese Windows 2000 with an absurd color scheme, DVORAK keyboard, trackball, 640×480 VGA mode, and huge ugly fonts on your development system so that you remember to bake in the code that adjusts for all these things.
Then your application will be buff and strong and it will laugh in the face of wimpy problems like people who use commas instead of dots as the decimal.
Ha. I eat commas for breakfast, your code will say, with a Russian accent.
posts.key_insight: Developing under the strictest possible conditions and rejecting permissive input handling produces software that is genuinely robust, not merely tolerant of errors.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky describes the challenges of making software work across all versions of Windows, using VMWare to test CityDesk on dozens of configurations. He illustrates how subtle API behavior differences between Windows versions cause bugs, using a GlobalSize function change as a case study. He argues against Postel's Law ('be liberal in what you accept'), contending that strict development environments catch problems early. He advocates for deliberately harsh testing conditions during development — unusual fonts, foreign locales, odd settings — so that software becomes robust. The three weeks spent fixing configuration bugs across a one-year development cycle was a worthwhile investment to reach all Windows users worldwide.
key_points: VMWare virtual machines enable testing across dozens of Windows configurations, from Win95 to Win2000 in multiple languages, catching bugs that only appear on specific platforms.

Subtle undocumented changes in API behavior between Windows versions (like GlobalSize returning exact vs. rounded-up sizes) cause real bugs that are nearly impossible to catch without cross-platform testing.
Postel's Law ('be strict in what you emit, liberal in what you accept') has been proven wrong by the evolution of HTML — strict APIs produce more reliable code than lenient ones.

Developers should use deliberately hostile development environments (foreign locales, unusual settings, edge-case configurations) to catch compatibility issues early rather than discovering them in production.
Java's 'write once, run anywhere' promise was appealing but fragile in practice, with threading behavior differences across platforms causing real bugs.
money_quotes: A hard drill makes an easy battle.
Not every programmer studies every line of the documentation for every function they use, and if the code is working, they tend to move on to something else.
The stricter the API is about its input, the more likely the code is going to work in funny situations.

You want your compiler and your development environment to be as strict as possible; you want it to literally generate random return values for GlobalSize so that you don't get into the habit of counting on something that won't be there everywhere.
I think that the evolution of HTML has proven that this isn't such a great idea.
Those of you who develop apps that only have to run on one computer or in a controlled environment have it easy, but you're getting flabby.
Ha. I eat commas for breakfast, your code will say, with a Russian accent.
posts.key_insight: Deliberately strict and hostile development environments catch compatibility bugs early, making software robust — leniency in APIs and testing breeds fragile code that breaks in the real world.

posts.summary: This is a brief holiday greeting post where Joel Spolsky wishes his readers a Happy Thanksgiving. There is no substantive argument or technical content. It reflects the personal, informal nature of blogging in the early 2000s, where bloggers would post short updates to maintain a connection with their audience.
key_points: A simple two-word holiday greeting with no technical or analytical content
money_quotes: Happy Thanksgiving!
posts.key_insight: Even influential technical bloggers occasionally posted brief personal greetings to maintain reader connection.

posts.summary: Joel reports that Fog Creek is in the final quiet days before shipping a product, where incoming bugs have slowed to near zero. He's been finalizing their new website and removing 'Beta' labels from the code. They expect to go live that week, hitting the schedule they set six months prior. The post serves as a brief, satisfied status update emphasizing the value of predictable shipping.
key_points: The final days before a product ships should be calm with very few incoming bugs
Hitting a ship date set six months ago validates disciplined scheduling practices
Removing 'Beta' labels is a symbolic and practical step in the transition to release
money_quotes: The last few days before shipping a product should be extremely quiet, as the rate of incoming bugs approaches zero.
posts.key_insight: A well-managed software project should reach a calm, quiet state in its final days before shipping, with the bug rate approaching zero and the schedule on track.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the shipping of CityDesk, Fog Creek's content management product. He thanks the team, beta testers, families, and blog readers for their support during the development period. He notes the free starter edition can't be downloaded yet because their office T1 line can't handle the expected traffic, and he asks for volunteers to mirror the download. He also highlights a mention in Australian IT news that quoted his philosophy of making products people love.
key_points: CityDesk shipped on time, with a free starter edition and a paid version
Infrastructure limitations (a T1 line) meant they couldn't self-host the free download and needed community help to mirror it
Spolsky's product philosophy centers on usability driving word-of-mouth growth
The post marks a transition from writing about software in the abstract back to reflecting on actually shipping it
money_quotes: CityDesk is shipping! People can buy it! It's a great feeling after a lot of work.
Thanks to you, Joel on Software readers, for bearing with me while I stopped writing about software in the abstract and spent some time doing it.

My number one priority is to make an application that's easy to use so that people like it, so that they tell their friends about it, so that everybody buys it and we have to hire full-time employees just to slit open the envelopes and take out the cheques.
Actually, I think I said 'checks!'
posts.key_insight: Shipping a product is a moment of gratitude and relief, but the real strategy is building something so easy to use that word-of-mouth does the selling.

posts.summary: Joel shares a brief excerpt from an interview where he argues that programmers should strategically decide when to build custom solutions versus using existing libraries. The core principle is to invest engineering effort only where it creates competitive advantage, and use off-the-shelf tools for everything else. He illustrates this with a game development example comparing 3D engines versus storytelling.
key_points: Engineers should use lower-level tools only where they add the most value to the product
Custom solutions should be reserved for your product's major selling point or competitive differentiator
Using libraries and off-the-shelf components for non-core features is the pragmatic choice
money_quotes: Programmers should only use lower level tools for those parts of the product where they are adding the most value.
If you're writing a game where the 3D effects are your major selling point, you can't use an off the shelf 3D engine, you have to roll your own.
If the major selling point of your game is the story, don't waste time getting great 3D graphics — just use a library.
posts.key_insight: Build custom only where it's your competitive advantage; use existing tools for everything else.

posts.summary: Joel announces the availability of a free download for CityDesk Starter Edition, a Fog Creek Software product. The post is a brief promotional notice encouraging readers to try the software.
key_points: CityDesk Starter Edition is now available as a free download
Joel positions it as easy to use with no barrier to trying it
money_quotes: It's free, fun, and easy to use, so there's no reason not to download it and try it out!
posts.key_insight: A brief product announcement offering a free edition of CityDesk to lower the barrier to adoption.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief link post where Joel Spolsky directs readers to a Slashdot discussion about an interview with him by Rich Chapman. He also links to his own reply in the Slashdot comments. The post contains no substantive argument or analysis, serving purely as a pointer to external discussion.
key_points: Joel links to a Slashdot discussion of an interview conducted by Rich Chapman
He provides a direct link to his own reply in the comment thread
posts.key_insight: This is a minimal link post with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post from Joel Spolsky sharing three quick updates: a job opening at Fog Creek Software for a Director of Sales & Marketing, a note that CityDesk (Fog Creek's CMS product) supports XML generation, and a link to a knowledge base article on creating RSS feeds with CityDesk. The post is essentially a micro-blog entry with no substantive argument or analysis.
key_points: Fog Creek Software was hiring a Director of Sales & Marketing, signaling the company's growth phase
CityDesk could generate XML output, including RSS feeds, demonstrating its flexibility as a CMS
The XML generation technique was generalizable to any XML schema, not just RSS
money_quotes: Yes, Virginia, CityDesk can be used to generate XML.
posts.key_insight: A brief product update announcing CityDesk's XML generation capabilities and a hiring need at Fog Creek Software.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post consisting of just a link to Joel's 'Back to Basics' article with the phrase 'Wax on, wax off' — a Karate Kid reference suggesting that mastering fundamentals requires repetitive practice. The post serves as nothing more than a pointer to the longer article.
key_points: Fundamentals matter and require disciplined practice
References the Karate Kid metaphor of building mastery through seemingly mundane repetition
money_quotes: Wax on, wax off.
posts.key_insight: Mastering software development fundamentals requires the same disciplined, repetitive practice as any other craft.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares a brief, informal post linking to a reader's question about e-commerce interface design and then takes a nostalgic dive through his own early internet history. With Google having made 20 years of Usenet archives searchable, Joel digs up his earliest posts dating back to 1988. He traces his online footprint from his first Usenet post as a Penn undergrad, through early open source contributions and GPL software releases, to his time at Microsoft and eventual move to New York.
key_points: Joel highlights a reader's question about e-commerce UI design, praising the quality of his readership's responses
Google's Usenet archive enables a personal archaeology of one's earliest internet presence dating back decades
Joel establishes his open source credentials by showing GPL contributions from 1988, predating many of his critics
The post traces Joel's digital journey across institutions: Penn, Yale, Microsoft, and New York
money_quotes: I have really smart readers.
Google now has 20 years of Usenet online. You can entertain yourself with some scary stuff from my past.
More than a decade before Joel on Software I had things to say. Not very interesting things though.
Now the Slashdot kiddies have to shut up; I was writing GPLed software before they were born, bwa ha ha!
This was rather a difficult trick because Penn didn't officially allow undergrads to post to Usenet in those days (so I transferred out!)
posts.key_insight: Joel uses newly searchable Usenet archives to playfully establish his long internet pedigree, showing that his instinct to share opinions and contribute to open source predated the modern blogging era by over a decade.

posts.summary: Spolsky criticizes programmers who display warning messages telling users not to hit the back or reload buttons, rather than designing interactions that handle these actions gracefully. He uses a Yahoo example where the company's own statistics show 10% of users 'forget' the warning, arguing this proves the design is flawed, not the users. He points out that users inevitably blame themselves when software fails them, which lets lazy programmers off the hook. Spolsky frames this as an ethical issue: choosing to make users feel stupid rather than fixing broken code is a moral decision programmers must own.
key_points: Decent web programmers can always design interactions that handle back/reload gracefully — the techniques predate cookies
Warning text doesn't work because users don't read it; the 10% failure rate proves the design is broken, not the users
Users consistently blame themselves in usability tests when software fails them, which shields programmers from accountability
Choosing user-hostile design over proper engineering is an ethical decision, not merely a technical one
money_quotes: It's not because they forgot. It's because humans are fallable and we're used to hitting refresh when the screen is messed up.
Don't argue with me, Yahoo, when you're the ones quoting statistics to prove it!
Better that 100,000 users should feel stupid than one programmer admit he didn't do a very good job.
Watch any usability test where the product is failing – the users inevitably blame 'their own stupidity.'
Don't let anyone tell you that as a programmer you don't have to make moral or ethical decisions.
Every time you decide that making users feel stupid is better than fixing your code, you're making an ethical decision.
posts.key_insight: Warning users not to use standard browser features is a moral failure by lazy programmers who would rather make 100,000 users feel stupid than fix their own code.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post consisting of a single quote from a commenter named Razib Kahn on the Fog Creek discussion forums. The quote describes how a developer stopped receiving complaint emails after giving up on trying to be original and simply copying Amazon's shopping system design. The post implicitly endorses the idea that users expect familiar interfaces and that convention trumps innovation in UI design.
key_points: Copying established UI conventions (like Amazon's shopping flow) eliminates user confusion
User complaints are often a signal that you've deviated too far from familiar patterns
Sometimes the best design decision is to stop being original and follow what works
money_quotes: So I finally gave up and created a shopping system exactly like amazon.com, and the e-mails stopped at a screeching halt.
posts.key_insight: When users already know how Amazon works, copying that familiar pattern is better UX than inventing something novel.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces two things: a new article about how to effect change in an organization when you lack authority, and a switch from Mailman to WhatCounts for managing his email notification list. He describes the pain points of Mailman, particularly the inability to customize unsubscribe links per recipient, which forced him to manually process unsubscribe requests for over 7,500 subscribers. The new WhatCounts system automates unsubscription but has the trade-off that replies to notifications no longer reach him directly.
key_points: Even managers can't simply decree change in software organizations — managing developers is notoriously difficult
Mailman's inability to include per-recipient information in bulk emails created a painful manual unsubscribe workflow
Switching to WhatCounts solved list management but introduced a new friction: replies to notifications are silently consumed as bounces
Tolerating bad tooling too long is a common trap, even for someone who writes about software management
money_quotes: Managing developers is a lot like herding cats, only not as fun.
Merely saying 'make it so' doesn't make it so.
If you're just a grunt programmer at the bottom of the totem pole, you can't exactly order people to start creating schedules or bug databases.
Why I put up with this for so long is completely beyond me.
Sometimes you don't have the power to create change in your organization by executive fiat.
posts.key_insight: Effecting change in organizations requires more than authority, and tolerating broken tools too long is a trap even experienced practitioners fall into.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky addresses the common frustration of working on a poorly-run software team when you lack the authority to mandate changes. He argues that individual contributors can significantly improve their team's practices through bottom-up strategies rather than top-down mandates. The post outlines six concrete strategies: doing things yourself, using viral adoption, creating pockets of excellence, containing bad programmers, escaping interruptions, and building credibility through excellent output. Spolsky emphasizes that all of these strategies depend on first being an outstanding individual contributor, because process improvements from someone who doesn't ship code will breed resentment. His core message is that grassroots improvement is possible but requires patience, political savvy, and leading by example.
key_points: Many best practices (daily builds, bug tracking, source control) can be adopted unilaterally by a single developer without waiting for organizational buy-in
Viral adoption works: if you consistently use good tools and processes, teammates will gradually adopt them when they see the benefits firsthand
Containing bad programmers through bug reports is more strategically valuable than rewriting their code, because it keeps them occupied and limits damage
Productivity requires protecting your focus — find ways to escape interruptions even when you can't change the office environment

Process improvement credibility depends entirely on first being an excellent, prolific code contributor — without that foundation, you'll be dismissed as someone who cares more about process than output
money_quotes: Managing developers is a lot like herding cats, only not as fun.
Don't have a daily build server? Make one. Set your own machine up with a scheduled job to make builds at night and send out email results.
About the three-thousandth time that you say to people, 'listen, I'd love to fix that, but I'm going to forget. Can you enter a bug in the system?' they'll start using the database.
You've got a perfect opportunity to neutralize this moron for several months. Just keep reporting bugs against their code.

All happy work environments are alike (private offices, quiet working conditions, excellent tools, few interruptions and even fewer large meetings). All unhappy work environments are unhappy in their own way.
There's nothing more deadly to your career than having a reputation of being so concerned with process that you don't accomplish anything.
There's nothing like a flurry of checkins to make you look good to the rest of the development team.
You can make things better, even when you're not in charge, but you have to be Caesar's Wife: above suspicion.
posts.key_insight: You can drive meaningful process improvements from the bottom of an organization, but only if you first establish credibility through excellent individual output — lead by example, adopt practices unilaterally, and let their value spread virally.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post where Joel simply links to a discussion thread about pair programming. He notes that Kevin wants to hear about people's experiences with pair programming and adds that he is curious about it too. There is no substantive argument or analysis presented. The post functions purely as a pointer to a community discussion on Fog Creek's forums.
key_points: Joel signals interest in pair programming as a practice worth discussing
The post directs readers to a community forum thread for shared experiences
Joel positions himself as curious rather than opinionated on the topic
money_quotes: Kevin wants to hear about people's experience with pair programming. (I'm curious too!)
posts.key_insight: Even influential bloggers sometimes use their platform simply to amplify community discussions rather than offer their own analysis.

posts.summary: Spolsky challenges the common argument that software development is an 'immature' industry that will eventually become as predictable as construction. He argues this analogy rests on a fundamental misunderstanding: software is infinitely clonable, meaning once a problem is solved, it never needs to be solved again. Construction workers repeat the same tasks thousands of times, but programmers encode solved problems into reusable libraries. Therefore, everything a programmer works on is by definition novel and cutting-edge, which inherently makes it unpredictable.
key_points: The claim that software is 'immature' compared to construction assumes we just haven't learned the tricks yet for reproducible results
Software's infinite clonability means solved problems get encoded into libraries and never need to be redone
Construction workers repeat identical tasks hundreds of times; programmers by definition work on things never done before
The boring, predictable problems in software have already been solved — what remains is always novel
Software is inherently more like science than construction because every task is on the cutting edge
money_quotes: The unique thing about software is that it is infinitely clonable.
This means that almost everything we do as software developers is something that has never been done before.
In software, the boring problems are solved.
Everything you do is on the cutting edge by definition. So by definition it is unpredictable.
That's why software has more of a science nature than a construction nature.

Implicit in the claim that the software industry is 'immature' is the belief that this is just because we haven't learned all the tricks yet to getting reproducible results. But this idea rests on a falsehood.
posts.key_insight: Software development is inherently unpredictable not because the industry is immature, but because software's infinite clonability means every remaining problem is by definition novel.

posts.summary: This is a minimal New Year's greeting post from early 2002. Joel briefly wishes readers a happy new year and shares a link to a review of his book on user interface design by Petter Hesselberg in Web Developer Magazine. The post contains no substantive argument or analysis, serving purely as a short announcement.
key_points: Joel's UI design book received a review in Web Developer Magazine by Petter Hesselberg
The post illustrates Joel's habit of using his blog for brief link-sharing and announcements between longer essays
money_quotes: Happy new year!
posts.key_insight: A brief announcement post sharing an external review of Joel's book on user interface design, with no substantive commentary.

posts.summary: Joel describes his typical workday pattern of procrastination followed by intense productivity. He spends hours checking email and browsing the web before finally forcing himself to open his code editor. Once he starts coding, he enters a flow state and works nonstop for hours. The core problem he identifies is the mysterious gap between deciding to work and actually starting — a transition he can't reliably make.
key_points: Procrastination follows a predictable, repeating cycle of deciding to start work then defaulting back to low-effort activities like email and web browsing
The hardest part of productive work isn't the work itself but the activation energy required to begin
Once past the starting barrier, flow state takes over and productivity becomes effortless and time-dissolving
money_quotes: Somewhere between step 8 and step 9 there seems to be a bug, because I can't always make it across that chasm.
launch the damn editor and write code nonstop until I don't realize that it's already 7:30 pm
posts.key_insight: The biggest obstacle to programmer productivity isn't the difficulty of the work but the mysterious inability to reliably cross the chasm between intending to start and actually starting.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky reflects on the universal struggle of programmer productivity, noting that even productive developers only get two to three hours of real coding done per day. He identifies that the hardest part isn't sustaining productivity but simply getting started — crossing the chasm between procrastination and flow. Drawing on his experience as an Israeli paratrooper, he introduces the military concept of 'Fire and Motion' as a metaphor for business strategy: you must keep moving forward every day while recognizing when competitors are firing at you just to pin you down. He argues that big companies like Microsoft use constant technology churn as cover fire to keep competitors busy reacting instead of innovating. For small companies, the winning strategy is simply to make progress every day and avoid getting distracted by others' volleys.
key_points: Productive developers typically get only two to three hours of real coding per day, yet still outperform peers who appear to work longer hours
The hardest part of productivity is getting started — once in flow, momentum sustains itself like a loaded bicycle at speed
The military principle of 'Fire and Motion' applies to business: move forward while forcing competitors to react to your volleys rather than advance
Big companies use constant technology churn (ODBC, DAO, ADO, ADO.NET, etc.) as cover fire to keep smaller competitors busy reimplementing instead of innovating
Small companies win by making incremental daily progress and ignoring checkbox features demanded by competitors' sales teams
money_quotes: What drives me crazy is that ever since my first job I've realized that as a developer, I usually average about two or three hours a day of productive coding.
For me, just getting started is the only hard thing. An object at rest tends to remain at rest.
It took me another fifteen years to realize that the principle of Fire and Motion is how you get things done in life.
You have to move forward a little bit, every day. It doesn't matter if your code is lame and buggy and nobody wants it.
The companies that do well are the ones who rely least on big companies and don't have to spend all their cycles catching up and reimplementing.
Microsoft is shooting at you, and it's just cover fire so that they can move forward and you can't, because this is how the game is played, Bubby.
Are you supporting it because your customers need it, or because someone is firing at you and you feel like you have to respond?
We just have to come in every morning and somehow, launch the damn editor.
posts.key_insight: Productivity comes from making incremental forward progress every day and refusing to let competitors' technology churn distract you from building what matters.

posts.summary: This brief post notes two items: a new CityDesk for Geeks page on the Fog Creek website, and a tribute to Dave Thomas, founder of Wendy's, who passed away. Spolsky reflects on how Thomas's personal appearance in hundreds of commercials gave Wendy's a human, approachable identity. He draws a broader lesson about business: customers prefer buying from people they can see and trust rather than faceless corporations.
key_points: Dave Thomas personified his brand by appearing in hundreds of Wendy's commercials, creating trust and warmth
Customers inherently prefer buying from identifiable people rather than anonymous corporations
A founder's personal involvement and visibility can be a powerful competitive advantage
money_quotes: He taught us that nobody, given the choice, prefers to buy from a faceless megacorporation.
posts.key_insight: People prefer to buy from visible, accountable individuals rather than faceless corporations, and a founder's personal presence can be a brand's greatest asset.

posts.summary: This is a joke post consisting entirely of the word 'Dave' repeated dozens of times as hyperlinks, all pointing to Dave Winer's Scripting News blog, ending with a single 'Malkovitch' link. It is a reference to the film 'Being John Malkovich,' where characters can only say 'Malkovich,' applied here as a humorous jab at Dave Winer. The post has no substantive argument or technical content. It appears to be a playful commentary on the early-2000s blogging culture and Joel's relationship with Dave Winer.
key_points: The post is a deliberate joke referencing the movie 'Being John Malkovich'
Every link points to Dave Winer's Scripting News, suggesting an obsessive or satirical fixation
The 'Malkovitch' punchline at the end reframes the entire post as a movie parody
It reflects the informal, personality-driven culture of early blogging
money_quotes: Dave Dave Dave Dave Dave Dave Dave Dave Dave Malkovitch
posts.key_insight: A zero-content joke post parodying 'Being John Malkovich' by replacing every word with links to Dave Winer's blog, illustrating the playful interpersonal dynamics of early-2000s blogging culture.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky describes how FogBUGZ evolved from a personal ASP learning exercise into a successful commercial product, accumulating messy code along the way. Rather than rewriting from scratch—which he famously opposes—he spent three weeks methodically refactoring the entire codebase using simple, mechanical transformations. He changed almost every line of code while keeping the software working at all times, separating concerns, eliminating duplication, and modernizing the HTML.
