The exercise preserved years of accumulated bug fixes and design knowledge that would have been lost in a rewrite, and made the code far more amenable to future development.
key_points: FogBUGZ grew organically from a learning project to a major revenue source, accumulating technical debt at each stage because the team never expected it to become important
Refactoring with strict rules—no new features, always-working code, only mechanical transformations—allowed a complete restructuring in three weeks versus an estimated year for a rewrite
The refactored code preserved embedded bug fixes and design knowledge that would have been lost and likely reintroduced in a ground-up rewrite
Software economics allow you to amortize development costs across many customers, turning a $5000 consulting job into a $200 product sold to thirty people
Programmers who start businesses have an unhealthy tendency to build tools only other programmers want, creating a crowded market of niche developer tools
money_quotes: A lesser company, perhaps one run by an executive from the express-package-delivery business, might have decided to scrap the code and start over.

Adding new features was a hemorrhoid. To add one field to the central bug table would probably require 50 modifications, and you'd still be finding places you forgot to modify long after you bought your first family carplane for those weekend trips to your beach house on Mars.

Programmers who start businesses often have the bad habit of thinking everybody else is a programmer just like them and wants the same stuff as them, and so they have an unhealthy tendency to start businesses that sell programming tools.
I never really had to think very hard, and I never had to design a thing, because all I was doing was simple, logical transformations.
In many of these cases, I realized that had I started from scratch, I would have made the same bug all over again, and may not have noticed it for months or years.
Almost every line of code is different now. Yep, I looked at every line of code, and changed most of them. The structure is completely different.
Those 49 weeks represent knowledge in the design of the code that I preserved intact.
The schedule was entirely predictable. After a week of this, you can calculate exactly how many lines of code you clean in an hour, and get a darn good estimate for the rest of the project.
posts.key_insight: Disciplined refactoring with strict rules—no new features, always-working code, only mechanical transformations—can achieve the benefits of a rewrite in a fraction of the time while preserving years of accumulated design knowledge and bug fixes.

posts.summary: This short post covers three topics: Joel links to a new article about refactoring legacy code instead of rewriting from scratch, announces that Fog Creek built its own e-commerce system to replace Digibuy's expensive 13.9% transaction fees, and asks for affordable alternatives to a $5000 binary patching tool. The rewrite discussion touches on how codebases designed as prototypes grow into unmaintainable messes, leading teams to want to start over rather than incrementally improve.
key_points: Codebases often become unmaintainable because they were originally designed as prototypes or quick experiments, not for long-term use
Rewriting from scratch is tempting but there are better alternatives like incremental refactoring
Fog Creek replaced a third-party payment processor charging 13.9% with their own merchant account at 2.5%, gaining both cost savings and control
Joel questions paying $5000 for binary patching software that implements a well-known algorithm from computer science literature
money_quotes: One reason people are tempted to rewrite their entire code base from scratch is that the original code base wasn't designed for what it's doing.
It was designed as a prototype, an experiment, a learning exercise, a way to go from zero to IPO in nine months, or a one-off demo.

And now it has grown into a big mess that's fragile and impossible to add code to, and everybody's whiny, and the old programmers quit in despair and the new ones that are brought in can't make head or tail of the code.
We got sick of paying the 13.9% transaction charge that Digibuy is charging us for every software download.
That's a lot to pay for what computer scientists call [Aho75].
posts.key_insight: Rather than succumbing to the temptation of a full rewrite when a codebase outgrows its original design, teams should look for incremental approaches to improve the code they have.

posts.summary: Spolsky enthusiastically endorses Six Degrees, a personal knowledge management tool built by former QuarkXPress designers who left after being mistreated by cost-cutting management. He draws a parallel between the crowded knowledge management space and the early search engine era, arguing that Six Degrees has cracked the formula the way Google cracked search. The post also serves as a cautionary tale about Quark's self-destructive decision to replace experienced developers with cheaper offshore labor.
key_points: Software companies' real assets are their people, and replacing experienced developers with cheaper alternatives destroys value, as Quark demonstrated
The former QuarkXPress designers started their own company after being treated as interchangeable, and were acquired by a Vancouver company that gave them autonomy
Six Degrees represents a breakthrough in personal knowledge management, a field Spolsky sees as important but not yet buzzy
Most companies claiming to solve knowledge management will fail, just as most early search engines did before Google found the right formula
money_quotes: When software companies say that 'their capital assets go home every night,' they are serious. Dead serious.

When Quark goes down in flames because the bean-counters pushed out the experienced developers and hired new, cheaper ones 10 time zones away, there will be a great case study in Harvard Business Review to prove it.
When the key designers of QuarkXPress 5.0 got sick of being treated like interchangable code slaves, they started their own shop.
Six Degrees is gonna be Google for the desktop.
Everywhere I look I see people blabbing about how they're going to crack the knowledge management nut.
Only Larry and Sergey figured out the formula to make search actually work.
posts.key_insight: Treating skilled developers as interchangeable commodities drives away the very talent that creates breakthroughs, often straight into the arms of competitors or startups.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post—just a single sentence—where Joel acknowledges that a CityDesk bug fix release broke existing functionality for users. He links to a discussion forum thread about the issue. The post serves as a public acknowledgment of the problem and an implicit apology to affected users.
key_points: Bug fix releases that introduce new problems are worse than the original bugs
Joel publicly acknowledged CityDesk's regression rather than staying silent
The post directs users to the community forum for discussion of the issue
money_quotes: There's nothing worse than upgrading to a supposed "bug fix" release that makes your stuff stop working
posts.key_insight: A bug fix release that breaks existing functionality is worse than the original bug it was meant to fix.

posts.summary: Spolsky highlights a cognitive bias from Shafir's 1994 research showing that most people stop reasoning through a problem when they hit a disjunction—a point requiring more information—even when that information is irrelevant to the outcome. He connects this to programming, arguing that good programmers overcome this mental block. This ability to push past uncertainty points is especially critical in debugging, where wasting time investigating irrelevant areas is costly. Spolsky suggests this cognitive trait may be one of the key properties distinguishing great programmers from average ones.
key_points: Most people halt their reasoning when they encounter a branching point that seems to require unknown information, even when the outcome is the same regardless
This cognitive limitation, documented in Shafir 1994, reflects an inherent difficulty with disjunctive reasoning
Good programmers overcome this bias, enabling them to efficiently eliminate irrelevant areas during debugging
This capacity for reasoning through uncertainty may be a fundamental trait that separates great programmers from others
money_quotes: Certain types of logical thinking are inherently hard.
Most people will stop working through a problem when they get to a disjunction, that is, a point on the decision tree where you need more information.
This becomes especially interesting when you don't actually need the information because the result would be the same in any case.
There is something that makes most people unwilling to go past uncertainty points in problem solving anyway.
Good programmers usually overcome this problem.
We don't want to waste time investigating areas that could not possibly cause the bug.
This might be one of those interesting brain-properties that distinguishes great programmers from everyone else.
posts.key_insight: Great programmers distinguish themselves by their ability to reason through logical disjunctions—pushing past points of uncertainty when the unknown information wouldn't change the outcome anyway.

posts.summary: Spolsky marks the closure of ArsDigita as the official end of the Internet Exuberance era. He examines why ArsDigita failed, noting that while it's tempting to blame VCs who replaced the visionary founder with uninspiring management, the real cause was the collapse of the internet consulting market. He contrasts Fog Creek's survival, attributing it to late entry and retaining proprietary software rather than open-sourcing their core product. He also cynically observes that failed companies prefer acquisition over liquidation purely to polish executives' resumes.
key_points: ArsDigita's demise marks the definitive end of the dot-com Internet Exuberance era
While VCs replacing a charismatic founder with bland management contributed, the real killer was the total collapse of the internet consulting market
Fog Creek survived by starting late enough to avoid overinvesting in consulting and by keeping proprietary software to sell
Companies prefer selling for pennies over liquidation so executives can spin their resumes more favorably
money_quotes: Even though the tendency is to blame it on the VCs, who replaced an exciting, charismatic and visionary founder with grey, grey, grey, the truth is that the Internet consulting market was totally wiped out more than a year ago.

Fog Creek only survived because we started the company late enough in the game that we never had time to build much of a consulting business, and we never open sourced the crown jewels so we had something to sell.
Have you noticed how often companies officially sell themselves off (probably for pennies) rather than simply liquidate? That's just to make the executives' bios look better.
I managed to turn $40 million of VC and a profitable company into a heaping pile of mineral-encrusted fish tanks.
posts.key_insight: Surviving a market collapse depends less on brilliance than on timing and retaining something proprietary to sell when the consulting revenue disappears.

posts.summary: Spolsky reveals what he calls 'The Iceberg Secret': non-technical people judge software progress almost entirely by its visual appearance, not its underlying functionality. Since roughly 90% of software work happens 'under the covers,' showing a polished UI makes stakeholders think a project is nearly done, while showing an ugly but functional prototype makes them think it's barely started. He argues that customers fundamentally don't know what they want, and developers should take ownership of design rather than expecting customers to specify requirements. The post offers practical strategies for managing perceptions, including controlling demo aesthetics, providing detailed schedules, and giving stakeholders harmless visual choices to deliberate over.
key_points: Non-technical stakeholders evaluate software almost entirely by its visual appearance, mistaking pixel-level polish for overall completeness
Customers don't know what they want and never will — developers must own the design process based on domain expertise rather than expecting detailed specifications
Showing a beautiful mockup early creates the dangerous illusion that the project is nearly done, leading to frustration during the long 'under the covers' phase
Developers should strategically manage perceptions by making unfinished parts look unfinished and providing regular quantitative progress updates
Give non-technical stakeholders harmless cosmetic choices to deliberate over so they feel involved without damaging the schedule
money_quotes: Customers Don't Know What They Want. Stop Expecting Customers to Know What They Want. It's just never going to happen. Get over it.

You know how an iceberg is 90% underwater? Well, most software is like that too — there's a pretty user interface that takes about 10% of the work, and then 90% of the programming work is under the covers.
If you show a nonprogrammer a screen which has a user interface that is 90% worse, they will think that the program is 90% worse.
If you show a nonprogrammer a screen which has a user interface which is 100% beautiful, they will think the program is almost done.

People who aren't programmers are just looking at the screen and seeing some pixels. And if the pixels look like they make up a program which does something, they think 'oh, gosh, how much harder could it be to make it actually work?'
A good interior decorator is constantly bringing their client swatches and samples and stuff to choose from. But they would never discuss dishwasher placement with the client.

Don't, for a minute, think that you can get away with asking anybody to imagine how cool this would be. Don't think that they're looking at the functionality. They're not. They want to see pretty pixels.
posts.key_insight: Non-technical people equate visual polish with project completeness, so developers must strategically manage how their work appears to avoid the perception gap between actual progress and visible progress.

posts.summary: This brief teaser post introduces Joel's 'Iceberg Secret' essay through a satirical vignette of a clueless CEO. The CEO mistakes the natural slowdown after an initial prototype phase for laziness, while himself lounging on a golf course. He contemplates firing developers to motivate the team, completely misunderstanding why software projects appear to slow down after early rapid progress. The post sets up Joel's longer essay about the disconnect between visible progress and actual progress in software development.
key_points: CEOs often misinterpret the natural pace of software development after the initial prototype phase as developer laziness
The contrast between the CEO golfing while questioning his team's work ethic highlights management hypocrisy
Early prototypes create misleading expectations about development velocity that persist throughout a project
money_quotes: Things were going so well when we started this project. For the first couple of weeks, the team cranked like crazy and got a great prototype working. But since then, things seem to have slowed to a crawl.
He chooses a Callaway Titanium Driver and sends the caddy to fetch an ice-cold lemonade. "Maybe if I fire a couple of laggards that'll light a fire under them!"
posts.key_insight: The disconnect between executive perception and software development reality leads managers to blame developers for a slowdown that is actually the normal progression from prototype to production.

posts.summary: This is an ultra-brief link post where Joel Spolsky shares that Ev (Evan Williams, creator of Blogger) reviewed Joel's 'User Interface for Programmers' series. The entire post is a single sentence with a thank-you. It serves as a social acknowledgment and cross-blog reference typical of early 2000s blogging culture.
key_points: Joel shares a link to Evan Williams' review of his UI for Programmers series
The post exemplifies the early blogosphere's culture of mutual cross-linking and acknowledgment
At just 6 words of original content, it represents the micro-post format common in early blogs
posts.key_insight: A minimal link post reflecting the early blogging culture of cross-referencing and community engagement among tech bloggers.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that upfront design is essential in software development because nothing is ever as simple as it seems. He illustrates this with a CityDesk usability fix where three minutes of design work on paper saved hours of coding by simplifying a wizard into two menu items. He pushes back against the perception that Extreme Programming advocates zero design, arguing that most programmers use 'no design' as an excuse for laziness that ultimately costs more time. He acknowledges that massive systems must evolve organically, but insists that feature-level design before implementation is non-negotiable for reducing schedule risk and building better software.
key_points: A simple usability fix turned complex when Spolsky almost built an unnecessary wizard — writing a brief spec first revealed a much simpler two-menu-item solution
Nothing in software is as simple as it seems: even basic operations like file copying have dozens of edge cases and failure modes
Schedule risk is dangerous because decisions made assuming short timelines become wrong when features take 20x longer than expected
Extreme Programming doesn't actually advocate zero design, but programmers hear what they want to hear and use it as an excuse to skip planning
Linus Torvalds' skepticism of upfront design applies to massive evolving systems, not to everyday feature development — and he's smarter than most of us anyway
money_quotes: Nothing is as simple as it seems.
Three minutes of design work saved me hours of coding.
You have to design things before you implement them.
You can put wheels on your mama but that doesn't make her a bus.
It's one of those weird forms of laziness where you end up doing more work than you would have done otherwise.
Most programmers are looking for any excuse they can find not to do basic design before implementing features. So they latch onto the 'no-design' idea like flies in a bug zapper.
Linus is much smarter than we are, so things that work for him don't work for us normal people.
When you sit down to write File Copy, or when you sit down to plan the features of the next release of your software, you gotta design. Don't let the sirens persuade you otherwise.
posts.key_insight: A few minutes spent designing on paper before coding consistently saves hours of wasted implementation work, because nothing in software is ever as simple as it first appears.

posts.summary: This ultra-brief post serves as a logical connector between two of Spolsky's recurring arguments. He asserts that since nothing in software is as simple as it seems, and since reducing risk is paramount, the only rational conclusion is that you must design before you implement. The post links to his longer essay 'Nothing Is As Simple As It Seems' for the full argument.
key_points: Software complexity is consistently underestimated — nothing is as simple as it seems
Risk reduction should be a primary driver of development process decisions
Upfront design is the logical consequence of acknowledging complexity and managing risk
money_quotes: The combination of nothing-is-as-simple-as-it-seems and reduce-risk can only lead you to one conclusion: You have to design things before you implement them.
posts.key_insight: Acknowledging that software is always more complex than expected, combined with the imperative to reduce risk, makes upfront design a logical necessity rather than an optional luxury.

posts.summary: Joel briefly mentions two new programming books his publisher sent him: 'Herding Cats' by J. Hank Rainwater about leading programmers, and 'The Career Programmer' by Christopher Duncan about navigating workplace realities. He hasn't read either yet and asks his readers whether they deserve a spot in his recommended reading list.
key_points: Joel recommends two new books aimed at programmers in leadership and career navigation roles
He transparently admits he hasn't read them yet, crowdsourcing opinions from his audience
The post reflects Joel's role as a curator and tastemaker in the software development community
money_quotes: I haven't read them yet. Let me know what you think — do they deserve a place in the hall of fame?
posts.key_insight: A brief book recommendation post where Joel leverages his community to crowdsource opinions on new programming leadership books.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces a revamped recommended reading list for software developers. He has curated the list down to 19 must-read books plus 2 additional books for aspiring programmers. The goal is to create a definitive 'programmers bookshelf' of essential reading. He removed good-but-not-crucial titles and added new ones to sharpen the list's focus.
key_points: Joel maintains a curated list of books he considers essential reading for every software developer
Curation means removing good books that aren't crucial, not just adding more titles
The list distinguishes between books for working developers (19) and those learning to program (2 additional)
money_quotes: I've always wanted this to be a 'programmers bookshelf' listing the books that I think every software developer should read.
I removed some books, good ones but not crucial ones, and I added a bunch of books, and came up with 19 must-read books.
posts.key_insight: A curated reading list for developers should be ruthlessly selective, keeping only the truly essential books rather than accumulating every good recommendation.

posts.summary: Joel announces the release of CityDesk Service Pack 1, a free update shipped three months after the original version. The update fixes approximately 100 bugs and adds frequently requested features. The biggest user complaint was about weird URL generation, which surprised Joel since other CMS tools had the same issue. He highlights their crash reporting system as instrumental in discovering bugs.
key_points: CityDesk SP1 fixed about 100 bugs and added new features as a free service pack release, shipped three months after launch
The most common user complaint was about weird URL names, which Joel found surprising since competing CMS products had the same issue
Built-in crash reporting that automatically transmits crash info to the team proved valuable for finding and fixing bugs
Joel emphasizes shipping a few easy features that he believes will have outsized impact
money_quotes: The biggest complaint about CityDesk was that it generated weird URL names (which actually surprised me, because most other content management systems do the same).
We added a few easy features that I think will go a long way.
And we fixed all known bugs — many of which we found out about because CityDesk catches most crashes and transmits crucial crash info to us.
posts.key_insight: Listening to user complaints and fixing them quickly — even when competitors have the same problems — is how you differentiate a product.

posts.summary: This brief post shares a positive review of CityDesk from the Sydney Morning Herald, which praises it as the best answer for small-to-medium sites transitioning to content management systems. Joel also references a repeated interview with Rick Chapman, reaffirming his longstanding position against rewriting large software products from scratch. The post reinforces two recurring Spolsky themes: promoting his own product and opposing ground-up rewrites.
key_points: CityDesk is praised by the Sydney Morning Herald as the best solution for small-to-medium sites needing a content management system
Joel reaffirms his well-known stance against rewriting large software products from scratch, even for products as problematic as Outlook
The 'Flogging of Dead Horses' label suggests Joel is aware he keeps repeating this anti-rewrite argument but stands firmly by it
money_quotes: I still don't think you should rewrite large software products from the ground up. Not even Outlook.
posts.key_insight: Joel remains unwavering in his conviction that rewriting large software from scratch is a mistake, no matter how flawed the existing codebase.

posts.summary: In this brief post, Spolsky shares his frustration with learning ADO.NET, questioning whether it represents technological progress. He then addresses a more significant concern: developers treating his writings as scripture rather than thinking critically for themselves. He describes receiving emails from teams who quote his articles at each other during disagreements, expecting him to settle their disputes. He concludes that no methodology or external authority can substitute for independent thinking.
key_points: ADO.NET is presented as a possible regression rather than an improvement in data access technology
Developers are using Spolsky's writings as ammunition in team arguments rather than engaging in independent critical thinking
No external authority or methodology can compensate for a team's inability to think through their own problems
Context matters — generic advice from blog posts cannot be blindly applied to every team's unique situation
money_quotes: They are hitting each other over the head with various quotes from me, instead of thinking for themselves.
I haven't yet written the article that says that if you can't think for yourself, no amount of "methodology" is going to save you.
posts.key_insight: No amount of expert opinion or methodology can substitute for a development team's ability to think critically about their own specific problems.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that incomprehensible technology specs are a reliable signal that the technology won't matter. He uses historical examples to show that simplification drives adoption: HTML succeeded where SGML failed, HTTP replaced FTP by being simpler, and within COM, the simple interfaces thrived while the complex ones languished. The core message is that complexity is the enemy of adoption.
key_points: If you can't understand a technology spec, it's likely no one else can either, and it probably won't become important
The most successful technologies are dramatic simplifications of existing ones: HTML from SGML, HTTP from FTP
Even within a single technology, the simpler parts get adopted while the complex parts are ignored
Complexity that should be simple (like COM's IMonikers vs URLs) is a design failure
money_quotes: Whenever somebody gives you a spec for some new technology, if you can't understand the spec, don't worry too much. Nobody else is going to understand it, either, and it's probably not going to be important.
This is the lesson of SGML, which hardly anyone used, until Tim Berners-Lee dumbed it down dramatically and suddenly people understood it.
Even within a given technology some things are easy enough to figure out and people use them, while others are so morbidly complicated when they should be simple that they languish.
posts.key_insight: Incomprehensibility in a technology spec isn't a sign of your ignorance — it's a sign of the technology's irrelevance, because simplicity is what drives adoption.

posts.summary: Spolsky observes a recurring pattern in reviews of lean Microsoft Office competitors: reviewers praise the bloat-free approach but then complain about missing features they personally need, like word count. He argues that the '80/20 myth' — the idea that users only need 20% of Office's features — falls apart because every user needs a different 20%. The market consistently chooses feature-rich software over stripped-down alternatives.
key_points: Reviews of Office competitors follow a predictable script: praise the lean approach, then complain about a missing feature
The 'everyone only uses 20% of features' argument is flawed because each user relies on a different 20%
The market consistently votes for 'bloatware' over minimalist alternatives, regardless of ideological preferences for simplicity
money_quotes: The trouble with the 'everyone only uses 20% of the features' myth is that everybody uses a slightly different 20%.
No matter how much it bothers you neat freaks, the market always votes for bloatware.
posts.key_insight: The '80/20 rule' argument for lean software fails because every user's critical 20% is different, meaning a product must support nearly all features to satisfy its full user base.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky argues that picking a ship date should be done early (even arbitrarily), then used as a forcing function to cut low-priority features rather than slipping the schedule. He outlines three release cadences — frequent small releases for small teams, 12-18 month cycles for commercial software, and 3-5 year cycles for platforms — and explains the tradeoffs driving each. A key insight is that commercial products should avoid shipping too early because first impressions are nearly permanent, using Marimba Castanet as a cautionary tale. He also warns against doing features in order of fun rather than priority, and notes that even web-delivered software shouldn't change too frequently because it undermines usability and user expectations.
key_points: A ship date should be set early and used as leverage to cut low-priority features, not as a moving target that slips with scope creep
Features must be built in priority order, not in order of programmer fun, or you end up with impressive easter eggs but no core functionality

Small teams with few customers should prefer frequent small releases to get fast feedback, while commercial products with many customers should prefer less frequent releases to avoid weak first impressions
First impressions of software are nearly permanent — shipping an anemic 1.0 can permanently damage a product's reputation even if later versions are excellent
Even web-delivered software shouldn't update too frequently, because constant UI changes violate user expectations and reduce usability
money_quotes: If you do this well, you'll soon discover that you don't regret the features that you cut. They're usually kind of dumb.
It's like editing. If you ever want to write a brilliant 750 word essay, start by writing a 1500 word essay and then edit.

If you aren't careful, your programmers are going to do features in order of fun, and you won't be able to ship or cut features because all the programmers were busy writing Karaoke Recalc before they even got the menus to work.
As a customer, the only thing better than getting feature requests done quickly is getting them instantaneously because they're already in the product, because it was designed thoughtfully.
If you ship an anemic commercial program just to get something out the door so you can 'start listening to customers,' what you'll hear those customers saying is 'it doesn't do very much.'
Five years down the line, people will still remember their first impression of 1.0, and it will be almost impossible to get them to reevaluate.
An application is usable if it behaves the way that the user expected it to behave. If you keep changing things around every week, it won't be so predictable, so it won't be so usable.
posts.key_insight: A fixed ship date is a prioritization tool: set it early, build features in priority order, and cut ruthlessly to ship on time — because the features you cut are almost never missed.

posts.summary: Joel announces Fog Creek Software's affiliate marketing program, framing it as a programmer's approach to marketing — writing code instead of making phone calls. The program offers 15-20% commissions for referrals that lead to software purchases, tracked via cookies. He positions this as guerrilla marketing appropriate for a small, bootstrapped software company. The post also briefly introduces a companion article about picking ship dates and using schedules to justify cutting features.
key_points: Programmer-founded companies can leverage code-driven marketing (affiliate programs) instead of traditional advertising
Word-of-mouth and low-profile marketing can be more appropriate than big-budget campaigns for small software companies
Content sites like Joel on Software serve double duty as both thought leadership and sales funnels
Detailed schedules provide political cover for cutting features — framing cuts as schedule necessities rather than personal rejections
money_quotes: As a company founded by programmers, it's no surprise that a lot of what we do for sales and marketing is done by writing code, not by making phone calls or hiring ad agencies!
A little ASP code, some database magic, three thousand pages of lawyer-approved legalese, and hey presto! an automatic sales program that costs very little.
One of the best reasons to make a detailed schedule is because it gives you an excuse to cut features.
posts.key_insight: Programmer-founded companies can turn their technical skills into a marketing advantage by building automated, code-driven sales channels instead of relying on traditional advertising.

posts.summary: Joel comments on Microsoft's abandonment of Hailstorm, their ambitious plan to centralize personal data as an extension of Passport. He identifies three reasons for its failure: other companies refused to participate due to distrust and lack of benefit, consumers rejected trusting Microsoft with personal data, and the project's champion Rick Belluzzo was an outside executive in a company hostile to outside hires. Joel also briefly notes he's published Fog Creek's .NET migration strategy.
key_points: Microsoft's Hailstorm project failed because no other company would participate, driven by both distrust and lack of clear business benefit
Consumer backlash over privacy concerns made centralized personal data storage untenable
Microsoft has a cultural pattern of bringing in outside executives but then undermining them, which doomed Hailstorm's internal champion Rick Belluzzo
money_quotes: Microsoft could never get any other company to go along with it.
Nobody trusts Microsoft any more; more significantly, it's because there's no real benefit to the other companies.
Consumers weren't about to trust Microsoft with all their juicy personal data.
Microsoft doesn't really like executives brought in from outside, even though they keep bringing them in.
posts.key_insight: Ambitious platform plays fail when they offer no benefit to partners, no trust from consumers, and lack durable internal sponsorship.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky outlines Fog Creek's strategy for gradually migrating to Microsoft's .NET framework, explaining why they find it compelling despite his own 'never rewrite from scratch' rule. He praises .NET's productivity gains, consistent class libraries, and C# as a language, crediting Anders Hejlsberg as the key architect. However, he identifies two practical barriers: the team's inexperience with .NET best practices and the large 20MB runtime that users would need to download. The strategy is to learn .NET by using it for internal tools first, then gradually port customer-facing products once the .NET runtime achieves broad adoption.
key_points: ASP.NET represents a massive productivity leap over classic ASP, making form validation and error reporting trivial and dramatically speeding web application development.
.NET's greatest strength is consistency — a single string type, unified date classes, and coherent APIs from top to bottom, solving years of Win32 API fragmentation.
Microsoft violated the 'never rewrite from scratch' rule but succeeded because they had Anders Hejlsberg and massive engineering resources — advantages normal companies don't have.
The migration strategy is deliberately gradual: learn via internal tools, wait for CLR penetration to reach ~75%, then port using conversion tools and incrementally rewrite in C#.
Some old VB.NET code will never be rewritten — if it works, it stays, even with ugly backward-compatibility functions.
money_quotes: .NET appears so far to be one of the most brilliant and productive development environments ever created.
ASP.NET is as big a jump in productivity over ASP as Java is to C.
Remember, just because Microsoft can do something, doesn't mean you can. Microsoft makes their own gravity. Normal rules don't apply to them.
.NET is Delphi 7.0, and 8.0, and 9.0.
I don't care if Japanese is a better language than English. It just doesn't matter.
The quality of .NET code that we can write is not good enough to ship.
When you look at the old Win32 API's, it is simply amazing how many different ways there are to get a string back from a function call.
Many old forms and classes will remain in VB.NET forever as long as they work properly.
posts.key_insight: Even when a new technology platform is clearly superior, the right migration strategy is gradual adoption starting with low-stakes internal projects, not a risky wholesale rewrite of shipping products.

posts.summary: This short blog post covers three unrelated topics: the rise of whitelist spam filters and their impact on mailing lists, a link to an essay about why free software has poor usability, and the announcement of a coupon system for Fog Creek's web shop. Joel notes that whitelist filters are generating auto-replies to his newsletter, creating a practical problem for mailing list operators. He uses the coupon announcement to briefly explain A/B testing pricing strategies.
key_points: Whitelist spam filters are becoming common enough to create operational friction for mailing list operators who can't manually process bounced verification requests
Free/open source software tends to have poor usability, as explored by Mozilla contributor Matthew Thomas
Coupons serve a strategic purpose beyond discounts: they enable A/B testing of different price points to optimize profitability
money_quotes: Whitelist spam filters are probably the last hope for dealing with spam in a meaningful way.
The serious point of coupons is that they let you try different offers on different people and figure out which ones are the most profitable.
posts.key_insight: Even mundane business mechanics like coupons serve as instruments for data-driven pricing experimentation through A/B testing.

posts.summary: Joel shares his experience building an internal business dashboard with ASP.NET, noting how productive the framework is for rapid feature development. He implemented two new features in just 20 minutes. However, he identifies several significant drawbacks: IDE performance issues, the lack of deterministic destructors requiring manual resource management, and the large 20MB runtime requirement. He concludes with a suspicion that Microsoft deliberately optimizes .NET for internal corporate applications rather than shrinkwrap software, since the latter is where Microsoft competes.
key_points: ASP.NET enables rapid development of internal business tools, with Joel adding two features in 20 minutes
The lack of deterministic destructors in .NET's garbage-collected environment forces manual resource cleanup, a step backward from C++/Delphi
The 20MB .NET runtime requirement is a showstopper for commercial shrinkwrap software distribution
Microsoft may be strategically optimizing .NET for corporate/internal use where they don't compete, while leaving it unsuitable for commercial software where they do
money_quotes: Elapsed time to implement both features: 20 minutes.
To be fair not everything about .NET is peaches and cream.

Since destructors aren't called when an object drops out of scope, if you have a SqlConnection object, you positively have to remember to close it because you can't wait for the garbage collector to come around.
That 20 MB runtime is a real showstopper.

I'm starting to suspect that Microsoft doesn't care — they are perfectly happy to have a development environment that is excellent for internal/corporate applications, where they don't compete, but borderline useless for shipping, commercial, shrinkwrap, where they do compete.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's .NET is optimized for rapid internal tool development but its runtime size and resource management limitations make it poorly suited for commercial shrinkwrap software — a gap Joel suspects is deliberate since Microsoft only competes in the latter category.

posts.summary: Spolsky responds to criticism of SOAP by arguing that its wire-format complexity is irrelevant since developers interact through higher-level tools like Intellisense, not raw XML. He compares SOAP critics to those who would reject Pentium processors for having complex instruction sets. However, he then pivots to argue that SOAP's real problems are far more fundamental: it lacks essential remoting features like events and references, putting it a decade behind CORBA and DCOM. He concludes that the biggest issue is that SOAP and Web Services are massively overhyped by trade publications and VCs, and startups chasing the bandwagon will get crushed by the inevitable backlash.
key_points: SOAP's complexity is irrelevant to end users because IDEs and type systems abstract away the wire format, making typed APIs easier to use than untyped alternatives
The industry trend is moving from variant-bound scripting languages toward strongly typed languages with IDE support, which favors SOAP's approach
SOAP is fundamentally inadequate as a remoting system, lacking events and references that CORBA and DCOM had a decade earlier
Web Services are dangerously overhyped by trade publications and VCs, and startups that chase the hype will be destroyed by the inevitable backlash
The firewall-traversal advantage of SOAP is temporary since firewall vendors will eventually close that loophole
money_quotes: The claim that SOAP is bad because the wire format is ugly and hard is like claiming nobody should use Pentiums because their instruction sets are so much more complicated than the instruction set for the 8086.
I think most of the criticism of SOAP can be traced back to a few grumpy language vendors who don't feel like doing the hard work to support it.
It has about 10 years to go before it gets to where CORBA and DCOM were 10 years ago.
And we're supposed to be all excited about this because we can sneak through the firewall. Gee, I wonder what the firewall vendors are working on these days?
You know that something is overhyped when every stupid VC funded startup feels compelled to change their strategy to get behind the latest flavor of the day.
The trouble with jumping on InfoWorld's fake hype bandwagons is that by the time you get there with real products, you'll be smacked in the face by the Tyrannosaurus Rex tail backlash.
It's like the VCs who watched the Lycos IPO and suddenly decided to fund portals. By the time the products came to market, Lycos was over and long gone and everybody was ridiculing portals.
posts.key_insight: SOAP's surface complexity is a red herring — its real failures are missing fundamental remoting capabilities and being a poster child for an overhyped technology cycle that will inevitably collapse on the startups chasing it.

posts.summary: Spolsky discusses the paradox of feature complexity in software design, citing a New York Times article about how 'featuritis' sells products even though it reduces usability. He argues that great design eliminates visible choices rather than adding them, using examples from Windows NT's offline folders, Excel's one-click sorting, and his own work simplifying FogBUGZ's bug-moving feature. The core tension is that making interfaces simpler for users actually requires more complex code underneath.
key_points: Feature bloat sells products because it gives users a feeling of control, but users then complain about complexity
Great design eliminates choices rather than presenting them — the best features hide complexity behind simple interactions
Making interfaces simpler requires significantly more engineering work: more code, more validation, more edge case handling
Users reject simple, rugged designs as 'boring' even though complexity frustrates them
money_quotes: Featuritis sells products, but choices reduce usability.
The really great designs are the ones that appear to eliminate a choice.
You know you're doing your job as a designer when you figure out a way to take a complicated feature and make it simpler.
It usually takes a lot more code to make a simpler interface.
People buy them for a feeling of control, then complain that they are so hard to manage. But show them something simple and rugged, and most of them will call it boring.
posts.key_insight: The best software design hides complexity behind simplicity, but achieving that simplicity requires far more engineering effort than exposing the complexity directly.

posts.summary: Spolsky reconsiders his objection to Extreme Programming's frequent iteration model for shrinkwrapped software. After talking with Kent Beck, he realizes that frequent iterations don't require frequent releases — teams can iterate internally while releasing on a longer schedule. He proposes a model where volunteer customers receive frequent builds for feedback while the broader market gets less frequent releases, allowing design mistakes to be corrected before they're locked in by backwards compatibility constraints.
key_points: XP's frequent iteration model can be decoupled from frequent public releases, solving the shrinkwrap software objection
A volunteer early-access group can provide rapid feedback without burdening the broader customer base with constant updates
Frequent internal iterations allow teams to fix design mistakes before backwards compatibility locks them in
Scaling XP culture requires 'conquer and divide' — get a working team first, then split and grow, rather than adding people too fast
money_quotes: The simple solution is to do frequent iterations but only release on a longer schedule.
You really can't rerelease the embedded software that runs your BMW engine every day or every week.
The fundamental project management question is: we're halfway through, are we halfway done?
It's not divide and conquer, it's conquer and divide.

Building a consistent culture for code development requires that everyone is on the same page. Add people too fast, and you'll find yourself with lots of different cultures, which creates conflict, inconsistency, and problems at the edges.
posts.key_insight: Frequent iteration and frequent release are separable concerns — you can get XP's feedback benefits without the logistical burden of constant public releases by using an internal cadence with selective early-access customers.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky recounts a lunch conversation with Tom DeMarco about reconciling pair programming with private offices for developers. DeMarco explains that people doing similar work don't distract each other, but mixing different work styles (like programmers and sales reps) causes problems. They conclude the ideal setup gives programmers private offices for solo work while providing dedicated labs for pair programming, free from interruptions. Spolsky also briefly criticizes a Salon article for misapplying Brooks's Mythical Man Month to Microsoft's security code review effort.
key_points: Groups doing similar work in the same style don't distract each other, but mixing work styles (e.g., programmers with sales reps) kills productivity
The ideal office layout combines private offices for solo work with shared labs dedicated to pair programming, with no email, phones, or non-programmers allowed in the labs
Pair programming only accounts for 3-4 hours per day; the rest of a developer's work (design, reading, thinking) benefits from private space
Private offices remain important for recruiting top talent who expect professional working conditions
Reviewing existing code for security bugs is easily parallelizable and doesn't suffer from Mythical Man Month coordination overhead
money_quotes: Groups of people who work in the same style on the same kinds of tasks will not distract each other.
You get in trouble, though, when you put a programmer in a room with the sales reps.
No email, phones, or non-programmers would be allowed in the labs.
All the non-programming work you do — design, negotiation, scheduling, reading, thinking, and Tetris — could be done in your private office.
Just try and get Tom Cruise to make your movie if he doesn't get his own dressing room.
Reviewing a large body of existing code to find security problems is easily divided up among huge teams of people, and should not suffer from any of the Mythical Man Month problems.
posts.key_insight: The ideal developer workspace pairs private offices for solo thinking with dedicated, interruption-free shared labs for pair programming — recognizing that coding is only part of what developers do all day.

posts.summary: Spolsky examines why developers choose programming languages, arguing that the decision is almost never about syntax but about practical constraints like runtime size, platform compatibility, and deployment requirements. He observes that each language occupies a specific niche defined by its ecosystem and tradeoffs rather than its curly braces or semicolons. This leads him to question Microsoft's .NET cross-language strategy, which offers a choice of syntax while locking developers into a single runtime and platform. He concludes that .NET provides language choice exactly where it doesn't matter.
key_points: Language choice is driven by practical constraints — runtime size, platform support, deployment targets — not syntax preferences
Each language occupies a niche defined by its ecosystem tradeoffs: C for speed, Java for cross-platform GUI, Perl for portable CLI tools
.NET's cross-language strategy offers syntactic variety while constraining the dimensions developers actually care about
VB.NET and C#.NET are virtually identical except for surface-level syntax, making the 'choice' between them meaningless
money_quotes: I hardly ever choose a language based on syntax.
I wouldn't accept a 20 MB runtime just to get semicolons.
It seems like .NET gives us a "choice" of languages precisely where we couldn't care less about it — in the syntax.
How do I develop a tiny Windows EXE in less than 16K in .NET?
VB.NET and C#.NET are virtually identical except for tiny syntactic differences.
posts.key_insight: Language choice is determined by practical ecosystem constraints like runtime size and platform reach, not syntax — making .NET's multi-language strategy a solution to a problem nobody has.

posts.summary: Spolsky addresses the futility of religious wars in technology debates, using reactions to his .NET commentary as a case study. He shows how readers reduce nuanced positions to binary for/against stances, missing that complex technologies have both positives and negatives. He then extends this to design philosophy, arguing that good design requires resolving genuinely conflicting goals rather than pretending they don't exist. The post concludes with a brief recommendation against Verisign for domain registration and certificates.
key_points: Technology religious wars are counterproductive because participants talk past each other, responding to positions their opponent never took
Complex technologies like .NET inherently have both positive and negative aspects, and acknowledging one doesn't negate the other
Good design requires confronting and resolving conflicting goals, not pretending conflicts don't exist or punting with options dialogs
The trashcan analogy illustrates how real design constraints are genuinely contradictory: light vs heavy, big vs small, easy-in vs hard-out
money_quotes: Anything as complicated as .NET is bound to have many positive aspects and many negative aspects. Duh.

The stupid thing about religious wars is when one person says, 'you don't get as much language choice as you think' and the other person says, 'you are clueless, because you're 4-6x more productive!' What a waste of time.
The World Is Complicated. Get Used To It.
Design is all about making hard choices and, hopefully, sometimes, hitting upon elegant solutions that solve conflicting goals.

When you can't solve conflicting goals, you have to be smart enough to decide which goal to solve, and not just be a lazy punter and pretend that adding a checkbox to the options dialog will solve it.
You have to make it light so it can be emptied easily. You have to make it heavy so it won't blow away.
posts.key_insight: Mature thinking about technology and design requires holding contradictory truths simultaneously rather than collapsing complex tradeoffs into simplistic for-or-against positions.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that software development advice is often given without specifying which type of software it applies to, leading to misunderstandings and misapplied methodologies. He identifies five distinct worlds of software development: shrinkwrap, internal, embedded, games, and throwaway code, each with different constraints around usability, quality, economics, and deployment. He illustrates the problem by examining Kent Beck's claim that Extreme Programming eliminates the need for bug tracking, showing that while this might hold for internal corporate software, it fails for shrinkwrap products that must handle diverse real-world environments like Polish keyboard layouts. The post concludes that methodology advice should always be evaluated in the context of what kind of software you're actually building.
key_points: Software development methodologies are almost always presented as universal truths, but they're usually written for internal corporate development because corporations hire the consultants who write the books.
Shrinkwrap software faces unique challenges: it must handle enormous environmental variation (OS versions, locales, browsers) and demands higher usability standards because users have alternatives.
Games and embedded software share a 'one chance to get it right' constraint that makes their quality requirements fundamentally different from iterative shrinkwrap or internal software.
An empirical review of 106 bugs in CityDesk showed only 5 could have been prevented by XP practices like pair programming or test-driven development — most were field-discovered environmental issues.

The value proposition of development investment varies dramatically: Microsoft can spend $500M on software worth $80/user across millions, while internal software must justify its cost for a single company.
money_quotes: There are several different worlds of software development, and different rules apply to different worlds.
One gets the feeling that they don't actually do anything, they are just an excuse to get an army of consultants in the door billing at $300/hour.

Whenever you read one of those books about programming methodologies written by a full time software development guru/consultant, you can rest assured that they are talking about internal, corporate software development.
Trust me, id software is not about to hire Ed Yourdon to talk about structured analysis.
Out of 106 bugs we fixed for the service pack release of CityDesk, exactly 5 of them could have been prevented through pair programming or test driven design.
We actually had more bugs that we knew about and thought weren't important than bugs that could have been caught by XP methods.
Program crashes on invalid input? Run it again, and this time watch your {${?'s!
When somebody tells you about methodology, think about how it applies to the work you're doing.
posts.key_insight: Software development advice is not universal — methodologies, quality bars, and best practices must be evaluated against which of the five worlds of software development you actually inhabit.

posts.summary: Spolsky introduces his 'Five Worlds' framework, arguing that not all software development is the same and should not be treated as such. He highlights the continuum between internal software, consultingware, and shrinkwrap products. The post traces how software often evolves from internal tools into expensive consulting-driven products when companies try to sell them externally. He argues that companies become addicted to consulting revenue and fail to make the transition to true shrinkwrap software.
key_points: Software development is not monolithic — different contexts (internal, consulting, shrinkwrap) have fundamentally different requirements and constraints
Software products often follow a predictable lifecycle from internal tool to consultingware as companies try to commercialize brittle, assumption-laden code
Companies get addicted to consulting revenue, which disincentivizes making software easier to install and use off-the-shelf
Internal developers typically lack experience making software run in diverse environments, creating a skills gap that prevents the transition to shrinkwrap
money_quotes: Not all software development is the same.

Often products start out as internal products, then the business guys have the bright idea of selling it to other companies, but it is so brittle and makes so many assumptions about its environment that it takes weeks to install it at other customers' sites, which is how consultingware is born.
These companies get so addicted to their consulting revenue they don't see any benefit in making it easier to use off-the-shelf.
Many internal developers have no prior experience with making software run in the wild, so it doesn't.
posts.key_insight: Software naturally drifts from internal tool to expensive consultingware because companies become addicted to consulting revenue and internal developers lack the skills to make software work outside its original environment.

posts.summary: Spolsky highlights Bertrand Meyer's analysis of the multiple language support in Microsoft's .NET platform, endorsing it as superior to his own earlier take. Meyer argues that while .NET claims to support many languages, several of them (C#, VB.NET, Managed C++) are essentially the same language with different syntax, since they all converge on the same underlying object model. Spolsky agrees and predicts that any future Java.NET would follow the same pattern of superficial differentiation.
key_points: Languages that share the same underlying object model aren't truly different languages—they're just syntactic variations on a single theme
C# and VB.NET are essentially one language now that VB has adopted full object-orientation
Managed C++ is much closer to C# than Microsoft's marketing suggests
True language diversity requires genuinely different computational models, not just surface-level syntax differences
money_quotes: Of course, the [.NET] languages involved have their own models, which may differ significantly from the .NET object model.
Otherwise, they wouldn't really be different languages, just a different syntax and minor variations on a single language theme.
Managed C++ is very close to C#, in spite of what the default Microsoft descriptions would have you believe.
And I'm sure when Java.NET comes out it will be too.
One may claim that these two are, deep down, just one language, now that VB has become an OO language.
posts.key_insight: .NET's multi-language support is largely an illusion—most of its languages converge on the same object model, making them syntactic variations rather than genuinely different programming languages.

posts.summary: This post, written by Jim Highsmith and introduced by Joel Spolsky, advocates for creating a clear product vision statement before diving into development. It presents Geoffrey Moore's product vision template from Crossing the Chasm and introduces the 'Design the Box' exercise, where cross-functional teams imagine their product sold in a shrink-wrapped box and must identify the 3-4 key selling points. Spolsky frames the piece by noting that good design requires difficult tradeoffs guided by a vision statement, citing his own CityDesk product as an example. The core argument is that without a shared vision, iterative development degenerates into aimless oscillation.
key_points: A product vision statement acts as a touchstone for making consistent design tradeoffs and deciding what to leave out
The 'Design the Box' exercise forces teams to distill 15-20 features down to the 3-4 that would actually cause someone to buy the product
Every project, even internal IT work, benefits from being treated as a 'product' with product-oriented thinking
The more volatile and time-pressured a project is, the more critical it is to have a clear vision of the desired outcome
Cross-functional teams doing the exercise independently almost always produce surprisingly disparate visions, revealing hidden disagreements
money_quotes: Good design requires making often difficult choices between imperfect options.
Coming up with 15 or 20 product features proves to be easy. It's figuring out which 3 or 4 would cause someone to buy the product that is difficult.
One thing that usually happens is an intense discussion about who the customers really are.
Minus this vision, iterative development projects are likely to become oscillating projects — going round and round in circles because everyone is looking at the minutia rather than the big picture.
Whether the project results involve enhancements to an internal accounting system or a new e-commerce site, product-oriented thinking pays back benefits.
The more critical the delivery schedule and the more volatile the project, the more important it is that the team have a good vision of the final desired outcome.
posts.key_insight: Without a shared product vision that forces hard prioritization decisions, iterative development devolves into aimless oscillation rather than convergent progress.

posts.summary: In this very brief post, Joel Spolsky highlights Jim Highsmith's 'design the box' technique for product vision. The idea is that teams should imagine designing the retail box for their product as a way to clarify what the product is really about. Joel endorses this as a great approach to figuring out the big picture of the products you build.
key_points: The 'design the box' exercise forces teams to distill their product's value proposition into what would fit on a retail package
Joel credits Jim Highsmith as the source of this product vision technique
The post emphasizes the importance of having a clear big-picture view of the products you build
money_quotes: Jim Highsmith has a great idea for figuring out the big picture of the products you build: 'design the box.'
posts.key_insight: Imagining how you would design the retail box for your product is a powerful exercise for clarifying its core value proposition and big-picture vision.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky celebrates the demise of RealNames, a company that attempted to create an alternative namespace system for the internet. He characterizes it as a scam designed to monopolize trademarks similar to Verisign, with a fundamentally flawed 'boil the ocean' business model requiring universal adoption. He mocks the founder's public complaints about the shutdown and extends his criticism to similar company New.net, questioning why anyone would pay for domain names only visible to some internet users.
key_points: RealNames' business model was fatally flawed because it required universal adoption to have any value
Attempting to create monopolies over other people's trademarks is a fundamentally exploitative business strategy
Public complaining by founders after business failure is unprofessional
Domain names or namespaces that only work for a fraction of users are inherently worthless
money_quotes: It was just a dumb scam to try and establish yet another Verisign-like monopoly on everybody else's trademarks.
And it was a 'boil the ocean' business plan, one which required everybody on earth to go along with their stupid scheme.

Why anybody would pay actual money, the stuff that can be used to purchase valuable goods and services, for a domain name that is only visible to a fraction of the Internet is completely beyond my comprehension.
posts.key_insight: Business models that require universal adoption to deliver any value—'boil the ocean' plans—are fundamentally doomed, especially when they attempt to monetize other people's existing assets.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post, essentially a link to a discussion forum question by James Ladd about software license management solutions. Joel adds a parenthetical note that Fog Creek is building their own licensing solution rather than using a third-party product. There is no substantive argument or analysis in this post.
key_points: Joel highlights a community discussion about software license management integration
Fog Creek chose to build their own license management rather than buy an existing solution
The post reflects Joel's practice of surfacing interesting community discussions on his blog
money_quotes: We're rolling our own for Fog Creek's products.
posts.key_insight: Fog Creek opted to build custom license management in-house rather than integrate a third-party solution, consistent with a build-vs-buy philosophy for core business infrastructure.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky puts out a call for volunteers to translate his blog 'Joel on Software' into as many languages as possible. He emphasizes flexibility, allowing contributors to translate as much or as little as they want in any language. He also seeks volunteer copy editors to review incoming translations.
key_points: Joel seeks to expand his blog's reach through community-driven translations
The volunteer model is flexible — contributors choose their own scope and language
Copy editors are needed alongside translators to maintain quality
money_quotes: It would be nice to get Joel on Software translated to as many languages as possible.
You can do as little or as much as you want to any language you want.
posts.key_insight: This is a brief administrative call for translation volunteers, not a substantive blog post with technical or strategic content.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post where Joel Spolsky expresses excitement about his blog content being translated into 15 different languages. The post consists of just a single exclamatory sentence with a link to a language signup page. It reflects the growing international readership of Joel on Software during its early years. The post serves as a milestone marker rather than a substantive essay.
key_points: Joel on Software had been translated into 15 languages by mid-2002, demonstrating significant international reach
Joel actively encouraged community-driven translations of his writing
The post reflects the early-2000s blogging culture of short, informal updates
money_quotes: Wow! 15 languages already!
posts.key_insight: Joel on Software had achieved remarkable international reach by 2002, with community volunteers translating the blog into 15 languages.

posts.summary: Spolsky applies the microeconomic concept of complements to explain corporate technology strategy, particularly around open source software. He argues that smart companies deliberately try to commoditize their products' complements to increase demand and pricing power for their own products. He demonstrates this through examples including IBM commoditizing PC add-ins, Microsoft commoditizing PC hardware, and major corporations funding open source development. He debunks the notion that companies support open source out of idealism, showing it's a rational economic strategy. He also argues that open source software is not exempt from economic laws, and that 'free' code still has real costs in developer time and opportunity cost. The essay concludes by noting that software can easily commoditize hardware, but the reverse is extremely difficult due to switching costs.
key_points: Demand for a product increases when the price of its complements decreases, so smart companies try to commoditize their complements to boost their own profits.

Most corporations investing in open source are doing so as a deliberate business strategy to commoditize software that complements their core business, not out of ideological commitment to free software.
Open source software is not exempt from economics: debugged code has real costs in developer time and opportunity cost, even when no money changes hands.
Software can easily commoditize hardware through abstraction layers, but hardware cannot easily commoditize software because of switching costs and non-interchangeability.
Sun Microsystems is pursuing a contradictory strategy by simultaneously trying to commoditize both software and hardware, leaving itself with no proprietary advantage.
money_quotes: Smart companies try to commoditize their products' complements.
Demand for a product increases when the prices of its complements decrease.

Most of the companies spending big money to develop open source software are doing it because it's a good business strategy for them, not because they suddenly stopped believing in capitalism and fell in love with freedom-as-in-speech.
Debugged code is NOT free, whether proprietary or open source. Even if you don't pay cash dollars for it, it has opportunity cost, and it has time cost.
I'm not very impressed by people who try to prove wild economic things about free-as-in-beer software, because they're just getting divide-by-zero errors as far as I'm concerned.

Without proprietary advantages in hardware or software, you're going to have to take the commodity price, which barely covers the cost of cheap factories in Guadalajara, not your cushy offices in Silicon Valley.
Even when the price is zero, the cost of switching from Microsoft Office is non-zero. Until the switching cost becomes zero, desktop office software is not truly a commodity.
And that's why Bill Gates can buy Sweden and you can't.
posts.key_insight: Companies invest in open source not out of altruism but as a deliberate strategy to commoditize their complements, thereby increasing demand and pricing power for their core products.

posts.summary: Joel announces that his translation project for Joel on Software has attracted 110 volunteers translating into 29 languages. The first translated articles in Swedish and Italian are now live. He used CityDesk's multilingual support features to build the translated site, noting it took about 2 hours to set up initially but adding new languages and articles is now quick. He hasn't yet tackled right-to-left languages or complex character sets.
key_points: The Joel on Software translation project rapidly attracted 110 volunteers across 29 languages, demonstrating the blog's global reach
CityDesk's multilingual features, previously untested, proved effective for managing a multi-language site
The workflow is designed for efficiency: adding a new language takes minutes, and new articles are as simple as copy-paste from email
money_quotes: CityDesk has pretty extensive multilingual support that was designed to make it easy to support a site in multiple languages, but I've never been able to try it out until today.
Adding a newly translated article is usually as simple as cutting and pasting from the email that the copy editor sends me.
posts.key_insight: Building multilingual support into a product before you need it pays off when a community of volunteers organically emerges to translate your content.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky introduces his Strategy Letter V by observing that companies investing heavily in open source software are motivated by business strategy, not idealism. He frames open source through the lens of microeconomic complements, suggesting that companies support open source when it commoditizes products that complement their own revenue-generating offerings. This is a teaser post pointing readers to the full essay.
key_points: Companies invest in open source primarily for strategic business reasons, not ideological ones
The microeconomic principle of complements explains corporate open source investment
Open source advocacy by large companies should be understood as rational self-interest, not altruism
money_quotes: Most of the companies spending big money to develop open source software are doing it because it's a good business strategy for them, not because they suddenly stopped believing in capitalism and fell in love with freedom-as-in-speech.
posts.key_insight: Corporate open source investment is best understood through the economics of complements, not through the lens of ideology or altruism.

posts.summary: This is a very brief announcement post where Joel Spolsky notes that his blog 'Joel on Software' has been translated into Dutch and Indonesian. There is no substantive argument or analysis — it is purely informational. The post consists of a single sentence with links to the translated versions.
key_points: Joel on Software was translated into Dutch and Indonesian, reflecting its growing international readership
money_quotes: Joel on Software in Dutch and Indonesian!
posts.key_insight: This is a minor announcement post with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post noting that Chinese (Simplified) and Hungarian translations of Joel on Software are now available. The post contains no substantive argument or analysis, serving purely as a site update notification.
key_points: Joel on Software expanded to Chinese (Simplified) and Hungarian translations
The post reflects the blog's growing international readership circa 2002
posts.key_insight: This is a minor housekeeping announcement with no substantive technical or editorial content.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that measuring knowledge worker performance inevitably leads to 'measurement dysfunction,' where workers optimize for the metric rather than actual value. He illustrates this with examples from Amazon and Gateway, where customer service reps gamed call-per-hour metrics by hanging up on customers. He extends the argument to software development, where rewarding code output and bug fixes incentivizes writing buggy code, and to CEO compensation, where stock options incentivize short-term stock inflation over long-term company health. Drawing on Robert Austin's research, he concludes that without 100% supervision, incentive pay tied to measurements is fundamentally broken.
key_points: Amazon reps hung up on customers to boost their calls-per-hour metric, and Gateway reps rushed customers off the phone to meet 13-minute call limits, tanking customer satisfaction
Rewarding programmers for writing lots of code and fixing lots of bugs creates a perverse incentive to write buggy code and then fix it, rather than getting it right the first time
CEO stock option compensation incentivizes inflating stock prices temporarily and selling or quitting at the peak, even at the cost of ruining the company
Measurement dysfunction is inevitable whenever workers cannot be completely supervised, which is almost always the case with knowledge workers
Even if you could accurately measure performance, incentive pay itself is a flawed concept
money_quotes: It seems like any time you try to measure the performance of knowledge workers, things rapidly disintegrate, and you get what Robert D. Austin calls measurement dysfunction.
In the absence of 100% supervision, workers have an incentive to 'work to the measurement,' concerning themselves solely with the measurement and not with the actual value or quality of their work.
The best way to get ahead in an organization like this is to check in lots of buggy code and fix it all, rather than taking the extra time to get it right in the first place.

When you try to fix this problem by penalizing programmers for creating bugs, you create a perverse incentive for them to hide their bugs or not tell the testers about new code they wrote in hopes that fewer bugs will be found.
You can't win.
As a result CEOs do everything they can to inflate the price of the stock, even if it comes at the cost of bankrupting or ruining the company.
Terrific. Now the incentive is to inflate the price of the stock temporarily and then quit. You can't win, again.
posts.key_insight: Measuring knowledge worker performance inevitably produces dysfunction because workers will optimize for whatever metric you choose rather than the actual quality of their work, making incentive pay fundamentally counterproductive.

posts.summary: Joel shares and endorses Steven Den Beste's observation about the recurring pattern of Mac fanatics' unrealistic expectations before each MacWorld event. The brief post highlights the cognitive dissonance required to perpetually believe the next Apple announcement will be the one that finally converts the masses. It's a quick link-blog entry rather than a full essay.
key_points: Mac enthusiasts exhibit a pattern of believing each MacWorld will produce a revolutionary, industry-changing product
This cycle of inflated expectations represents a form of cognitive dissonance
The religious language ('heathen,' 'exodus,' 'grace') satirizes the cult-like devotion of Apple fans
money_quotes: The hardcore Mac faithful seem to believe just before each MacWorld that this is going to be the time that the Steve finally announces the killer product which is going to rock the PC heathen back on their heels and begins the great exodus from the dark side into the grace of Steve's love.
posts.key_insight: The Mac faithful engage in a recurring cycle of cognitive dissonance, perpetually convinced the next product announcement will trigger mass conversion from PCs.

posts.summary: Joel reviews Listen.com (Rhapsody), an early legal music streaming service offering unlimited access to music from five major labels for $100/year. He praises its UI superiority over piracy alternatives like Napster and KaZaa, highlighting features like cross-indexing, artist discovery, and reliable playback. He frames it as the recording industry finally providing a reasonable legal alternative to piracy. He also briefly mentions Dave's Google toolbar as a useful command-line interface for the web.
key_points: Legal music streaming at $100/year with major label catalogs represents a viable alternative to piracy
Superior UI and discovery features (cross-indexing, artist influences, reviews) give legal services an advantage over pirate alternatives
Reliable playback without streaming hiccups makes the service practically superior to earlier streaming attempts
The recording industry, 'under extreme duress,' finally created a reasonable way to pay for digital music
money_quotes: For about $100 a year you get unlimited music, legally.
The UI is much better than anything Napster/KaZaa/Gnutella had, it's especially easy to find new things to listen to because everything is nicely cross-indexed and reviewed.
Finally, the recording industry (under extreme duress) has given us a reasonable way to pay for digital music.
Yes, things are missing (Madonna!) but that doesn't mean it's not worth $100 a year for access to 15,000 good albums.
It's the command line for the world wide web.
posts.key_insight: When the recording industry finally offered a legal streaming service with better UI and discovery than piracy tools, it proved that competing with free requires superior user experience, not just legal enforcement.
