posts.summary: Joel announces he will be on vacation from July 19-29 and shares his policy of not checking email or the web while away. He also provides a brief update on the translation project for his articles, noting that if all volunteers follow through, there will be at least 210 translated articles across 38 languages.
key_points: Joel enforces a strict no-email, no-web policy during vacations
The Joel on Software translation project had grown to potentially 210 articles in 38 languages
The post reflects the early-2000s blogging culture of posting even minor personal updates
money_quotes: My new policy is that I don't check email or the web while I'm on vacation.
posts.key_insight: Disconnecting fully from work during vacation is a deliberate policy worth establishing and announcing.

posts.summary: This short post covers two practical problems Joel encountered: providing remote tech support through firewalls and the declining effectiveness of SpamAssassin. For remote support, he needed a way to control customers' computers to fix FogBUGZ installations, but existing tools required complex setup and didn't work behind firewalls. He settled on TightVNC in 'listen' mode as a solution. On spam, he observed that SpamAssassin's effectiveness dropped from 99% to 75% because spammers were actively studying its publicly documented scoring rules to evade detection.
key_points: Remote tech support that takes 3 minutes in person can take over an hour by phone, making remote control tools essential for server products
Existing remote control solutions in 2002 suffered from two problems: complex installation and inability to work behind firewalls
SpamAssassin's transparency about its scoring rules is a design flaw that helps spammers evade it
Arms races in spam filtering are inherently asymmetric when the defender publishes their exact detection methodology
money_quotes: If we could somehow remote-control our customers' computers, it would take us about 3 minutes to get FogBUGZ up and running. Instead we have to try to tell them what to do over the phone, without seeing what's going on, which can take an hour or more.
SpamAssassin has one major design flaw: whenever it decides to flag a message as spam, it includes a detailed reason why, with exact scores.
There's even a web page which might as well be titled Evading SpamAssassin.
It takes about 5 minutes for a spammer to figure out how to avoid the filters.
I'm not sure what's to stop spammers from keeping their versions of SpamAssassin up to date, though…
posts.key_insight: Transparency in adversarial systems is a double-edged sword — publishing your exact detection rules makes it trivial for adversaries to work around them.

posts.summary: This is a brief link-blog post with two unrelated items. Spolsky shares a Bruce Sterling quote criticizing open source software's user experience as ugly and hostile to users. He also announces that Fog Creek has sent out its first affiliate payment checks and notes that CityDesk was mentioned in the New York Times.
key_points: Bruce Sterling compares open source software architecture to a 'Hippie Squat' where anything goes regardless of user experience
Spolsky implicitly endorses the criticism that open source prioritizes developer freedom over user-friendly design
Fog Creek's affiliate program is generating real income for participants, validating the business model
CityDesk received mainstream press coverage in the New York Times
money_quotes: It's the Hippie Squat Model of software architecture.
'Don't like it? Hey, just reconfigure it yourself, don't bother me!'
It's very offensive to user sensibilities and it is as ugly as a sack full of penguin guts.
posts.key_insight: Open source software's 'configure it yourself' ethos can produce experiences that are hostile to ordinary users who just want things to work well.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky advocates for daily builds as an essential software development practice. He argues they prevent individual developers from breaking the team's workflow, enable tracking down regression bugs through binary search of historical builds, and provide a canonical release process. He recommends FinalBuilder as a tool for implementing daily builds on Windows, noting his team has expanded its use beyond builds to routine system administration tasks. The post includes a promotional discount for the tool exclusively for his readers.
key_points: Daily builds prevent individual developers from breaking the build and disrupting the entire team
Historical builds enable binary search debugging to track down when regressions were introduced
A canonical build process from raw checkouts ensures no crucial steps are forgotten during releases
Daily builds provide value even for solo developers, not just large teams
FinalBuilder proved versatile enough to replace scripts for routine system administration tasks
money_quotes: They prevent any one developer from screwing up the rest of the team by checking in something which breaks the build, which makes them crucial for teams with more than about 1 person.
Daily builds give you a way to track down weird bugs that seemed to have slipped in a while ago by binary searching through the historical builds.
By giving you a canonical way to build 'the final bits' from raw checkouts, you can be confident that you never forget some crucial step when you release a new version.
What the heck?! It never did that before! Did it?
It is so good that in addition to daily builds we've pretty much started using it instead of scripts for routine system administration stuff.
posts.key_insight: Daily builds are essential infrastructure for any team because they prevent broken builds from disrupting teammates, enable regression debugging through historical build comparison, and guarantee a reliable, repeatable release process.

posts.summary: This is a very brief administrative post where Joel announces two things: he has been working on an improved online trial for FogBUGZ, and he is moving CityDesk-related news to a separate dedicated site to avoid boring readers who don't use CityDesk.
key_points: Joel built a new online trial experience for FogBUGZ bug tracking software
CityDesk news is being moved to its own dedicated site to keep the main blog focused
Shows awareness of audience segmentation - not all readers care about all Fog Creek products
money_quotes: Rather than post CityDesk news here, where it will surely bore those few remaining non-CityDesk-users to tears, I'm starting a new CityDesk News site.
posts.key_insight: Product-specific news should be separated from a general-interest blog to avoid alienating readers.

posts.summary: This brief link roundup post touches on several topics: Microsoft's .NET marketing failures, small font sizes in web design, and CityDesk product news. Spolsky references CNet's criticism of Microsoft's .NET marketing campaign, noting he had written about the same issue two years earlier. He concedes that while .NET was primarily a marketing exercise rather than a technological revolution, the development tools did make impressive progress. The post also highlights Jakob Nielsen's complaint about tiny text on websites and promotes a new Flash demo for Fog Creek's CityDesk product.
key_points: Microsoft's .NET branding was a marketing campaign gone wrong, not a genuine technological revolution, though the underlying tools did improve significantly
Spolsky's earlier criticism of .NET was about marketing hype, not the technology itself, and was frequently misunderstood
Jakob Nielsen's complaint about tiny text stems from Internet Explorer's inability to resize fonts when designers specify exact pixel sizes in CSS
money_quotes: My beef was that .NET was just marketing gone crazy, it wasn't a criticism of any particular piece of technology that got the .NET moniker stuck on it.

I also claimed that .NET was not revolutionary, but rather a new name for things that were under development anyway, although in retrospect I'm pretty impressed by just what a big step forward the languages and development tools took.
What Jakob is really complaining about is that Windows versions of IE do not give the user the ability to change the font size when the designer has specified an exact pixel size using CSS.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's .NET was primarily a marketing exercise that rebranded existing development efforts, though the underlying technology turned out to be a genuine step forward.

posts.summary: Spolsky comments on Mozilla's post-mortem reflections about open-source UI design challenges. He references Peter Trudelle's admission that Netscape had no model for large-scale UI design in open source, and Matthew Thomas's frustrations with the process. Spolsky argues that design work—whether architectural or UI—suffers enormously when teams are geographically dispersed. He concludes that co-located teams produce vastly better results and declares he would never agree to work with a dispersed team.
key_points: Netscape open-sourced its codebase without any established model for doing large-scale UI design in an open-source context
Netscape management had been cutting UI design resources, with some managers viewing it as mere 'eye candy'
Remote design discussions that could be resolved in five minutes at a whiteboard instead consumed hours of typed debate in Bugzilla, diluted by unhelpful commentary from the public
Spolsky asserts that geographic dispersion fundamentally limits the bandwidth needed for good design work
money_quotes: It's very frustrating doing any kind of design work, architectural or UI, with a dispersed group of volunteers.
Things which Matthew could have persuaded someone in person at a whiteboard in five minutes took hours of typing into Bugzilla, accompanied by no end of useless interjections from the world at large.
There just isn't enough bandwidth to do good design when a team is geographically dispersed.
I'm not saying it can't be done at all, but the results are vastly better when the entire team is physically in the same location.
I'm convinced of this, and will never agree to do software development with a dispersed team.
posts.key_insight: Design work requires high-bandwidth communication that remote and open-source collaboration cannot adequately provide, making co-located teams essential for good results.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky argues that platform success depends entirely on developer adoption. Companies kill their platforms by either failing to recognize they have a platform (treating it as an application) or by being greedy and trying to capture all revenue themselves. The post serves as a brief pointer to a longer essay on platforms.
key_points: Platform success requires massive developer adoption
Making it hard for developers to build on a platform is the fastest way to kill it
Two common platform failures: not recognizing you have a platform, or hoarding revenue
money_quotes: If you want a platform to be successful, you need massive adoption, and that means you need developers to develop for it.
The best way to kill a platform is to make it hard for developers to build on it.
Most of the time, this happens because platform companies either don't know that they have a platform (they think it's an application) or they get greedy (they want all the revenue for themselves.)
posts.key_insight: Platforms die when their owners either fail to recognize they are platforms or refuse to share the economic pie with the developers who make them valuable.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that platform companies must recognize they are platform companies and prioritize developer adoption above all else. He examines historical examples of platform failures (NetWare, Apple, OS/2, IBM PS/2) where vendors either didn't realize they had a platform or got greedy and locked out developers. He contrasts these with successes like Windows and Java, which offered free or cheap runtimes and development tools. The bulk of the post uses Groove as a case study — a technology with compelling P2P architecture that is doomed because it charges for its runtime and treats itself as an application rather than a platform. Spolsky concludes that platforms need free runtimes to achieve the massive developer adoption required for success.
key_points: Platforms must be marketed to developers first, not end users — failing to recognize this distinction is a primary cause of platform failure
Greedy platform companies that try to capture all revenue themselves choke off the developer ecosystem that would make them successful
A free redistributable runtime is essentially mandatory for platform adoption — no developer will build on a platform that adds cost to their product
Groove has compelling P2P technology but is positioning itself as an application rather than a platform, ensuring it will be displaced by a free or open-source alternative
Windows succeeded by never making developers pay for the runtime, starting with a free redistributable and eventually being bundled with every PC
money_quotes: It's really, really important to figure out if your product is a platform or not, because platforms need to be marketed in a very different way to be successful.
The best way to kill a platform is to make it hard for developers to build on it.
Greedy platform companies can't stand the idea of all kinds of unwashed riffraff making money off of their platform, so they make it darn near impossible for anyone to develop for it.
As a platform vendor, you're only as successful as the people who build on you.
Anybody who ever tried to sell software components knows that you have to have a royalty-free runtime or no developer will touch you with a 10 foot pole.
If Notes had a software industry sitting on top of it way back in the 80s, some dormroom startup might have made a compelling hypertext application for it instead and preempted the Web.
Someone else will come along with a P2P architecture and sell it like a component, or make it an open source library, and that's what software developers will use.
posts.key_insight: A platform's success is entirely determined by developer adoption, which requires free runtimes and low barriers to entry — platform vendors who get greedy or mistake their platform for an application are signing their own death warrant.

posts.summary: Spolsky comments on Napster's failure to deliver a legal, paid music service despite having two years and a full development team, when the original Napster was built by one person in three months. He identifies this as a recurring pattern in software development where a single developer builds version 1 quickly, but a larger team fails to deliver version 2 for years.
key_points: Napster and Bertelsmann failed to deliver a legal paid music service despite two years of development
The original Napster was built by a single developer (Shawn Fanning) in just three months
This paradox — one person ships v1 fast, a team of 15 can't ship v2 — is a recurring pattern in software development
Adding people and corporate process to a project can slow it down rather than speed it up
money_quotes: One guy builds version 1 in a few months and then a team of 15 can't build version 2 for years and years. Tell me you haven't seen that movie before.
What I don't understand, exactly, is why they couldn't build this thing that sells Bertlesmann music with two whole years of work and a whole team of developers?
posts.key_insight: A single motivated developer can often outship a large team, and scaling up headcount frequently produces the opposite of the intended effect — a pattern so common in software that it's practically a cliché.

posts.summary: This is a very brief blog post where Joel Spolsky responds to reader questions about his reference to Rick Chapman's upcoming book on stupidity in the software industry. He explains he read the manuscript to write the foreword and promises to announce its release. He also gives a quick shout-out to TopCoder.com as a cool concept for online programming competitions.
key_points: Rick Chapman was writing a book about stupidity in the software industry that Joel found impressive enough to write the foreword for
Joel positions himself self-deprecatingly as 'something of a stupidity maven' to explain why he was asked to write the foreword
Joel highlights TopCoder.com as an interesting new concept for online programming competitions
money_quotes: I just read his manuscript so that I can write the foreword (being something of a stupidity maven, myself).
What a cool idea, online programming competitions!
posts.key_insight: A minor housekeeping post noting an upcoming book on software industry failures and flagging TopCoder as an interesting new platform for competitive programming.

posts.summary: Spolsky examines the business case for developing Mac software in 2002, noting that while Mac shops are moving to OS X-only development, the fundamental economics are challenging. With Mac market share at roughly 3.5%, developers need their product to appeal to 25 times more Mac users proportionally than Windows users just to break even. He acknowledges emotional reasons for Mac development but argues that as a business investment, the math is brutally unfavorable unless developers can demonstrate how they'll overcome that multiplier through less competition or niche dominance.
key_points: Mac software shops moving to OS X-only makes sense because paying customers tend to have newer computers, giving OS X a disproportionate share of wallet-openers versus installed base
Mac's 3.5% market share makes developing Mac-only software comparable to building a Netscape-only website at 3.4% market share
A Mac product must appeal to roughly 25 times more of its addressable market than a Windows equivalent to generate the same revenue
Emotional reasons for Mac development are valid, but investment decisions require demonstrating how to overcome the 25x multiplier through niche positioning or reduced competition
money_quotes: If OS X isn't successful, the Mac is over. It's not like System 9 is getting any more popular.
Developing for the Mac is not a whole lot different than creating a web site that only works on Netscape.
Your product would have to appeal to 25 times more Mac users than Windows users just to break even.
You may want to make an emotional appeal to developing for the Mac. That's fine. If you like Macs and you're doing it for fun, more power to ya.
If you want to make an investment in Mac software be prepared to demonstrate how you're going to overcome that magic 25 multiplier.
posts.key_insight: Platform market share creates a multiplier effect that makes developing for minority platforms a losing business proposition unless you can demonstrate disproportionate appeal within that smaller market.

posts.summary: Spolsky debates whether FogBUGZ 3.0 should support custom fields, a feature he has historically opposed. He argues that custom fields lead to bloated bug entry forms that discourage people from reporting bugs at all, which is far worse than missing some metadata. When he surveyed customers requesting custom fields, he found most wanted fields that already existed in the product or fields that shouldn't be tracked as structured data. Despite his conviction that omitting custom fields is the right design choice, he acknowledges the sales cost of appearing to lack a feature competitors offer.
key_points: Adding fields to bug databases increases ceremony and reduces the number of bugs that get reported, which is worse than missing metadata
Most customers requesting custom fields either want something already built-in or want fields that would be counterproductive as structured data
The primary goal of a bug tracker is to keep track of bugs, not to capture exhaustive structured information about them
Information that's only relevant to a small percentage of bugs should be captured on demand rather than required for every report
Good product design sometimes means telling customers 'no' even when it costs sales
money_quotes: People add so many fields to their bug databases to capture everything they think might be important that entering a bug is like applying to Harvard.
End result: people don't enter bugs, which is much, much worse than not capturing all that information.
The goal of a bug tracking database is to keep track of bugs, which, all else being equal, takes priority over making it easy to find them.

I have heard countless stories of development teams where the bug tracking package was so high-ceremony that people were afraid to enter bugs in the system, because they didn't know what all those fields were.
The real bug-'tracking' happened in email, post-its, and hallway conversations.
Rather than giving our usual answer ('no. on purpose.') over the last few weeks I've been saying, 'can you please tell me what fields you would need?'
It's still rude of me to tell customers that we don't have that feature for their own good, even though it usually is.
I guess we could have a custom fields feature but hide it and make it so hard to use that people don't use it.
posts.key_insight: The best bug tracking tools minimize friction for reporting bugs rather than maximizing the structured data captured per bug, because an unreported bug is far more costly than an under-documented one.

posts.summary: Spolsky reflects on the declining effectiveness of advertising, noting that consumers have built up immunity to ads because advertisers repeatedly lied. He argues this forces companies into editorial and PR placement, where consumer defenses are lower. However, he identifies a tragedy of the commons: just as advertising destroyed its own credibility through overuse and dishonesty, editorial content will suffer the same fate once marketers pollute it with promotional messaging disguised as genuine content.
key_points: Traditional advertising has lost effectiveness because consumers learned not to trust it after years of dishonest ads
Companies are forced to infiltrate editorial content (product placements, PR) where audiences are less guarded
This represents a tragedy of the commons: each medium loses credibility as marketers exploit the trust it holds
The same cycle that destroyed advertising credibility will inevitably destroy editorial credibility too
money_quotes: Nobody believes advertisements any more.
If you have a product to sell you have no choice but to get into the editorial side, where consumers' defenses are lowered.
When advertising first rose to prominence, advertisements did work. We hadn't built up our immunities yet.
As more and more advertisers used the opportunity of the medium to lie, we learned not to trust advertisements.
Once editorial gets polluted by desperate marketers using PR instead of advertising to promote their message, nobody will believe it either.
posts.key_insight: Advertising is a tragedy of the commons where each medium's trustworthiness is destroyed as marketers exploit it, and editorial content is next in line for the same fate.

posts.summary: Spolsky comments on NASA's cancellation of a $273 million launch-control computer replacement project that was over budget and behind schedule. He questions why government agencies like NASA and the FAA continue running 1970s-era systems when the software complexity of those old mainframes was minimal by modern standards. He provocatively suggests that recreating such systems with modern tools would be trivially easy, or failing that, a simple CPU emulator could be written in weeks. The post uses the NASA failure to take a swipe at the mythology surrounding heavyweight software processes like SEI Level 5.
key_points: NASA spent $273 million on a launch-control computer replacement that was ultimately cancelled as over budget, behind schedule, and too expensive to operate
1970s mainframes had roughly a megabyte of memory, meaning the software running on them was less complex than a modern cell phone's code
Spolsky argues that modern development tools make recreating such legacy systems far easier than massive government projects suggest
He proposes a pragmatic alternative: write a CPU emulator for the old hardware rather than undertaking a massive rewrite
The NASA failure undermines the prestige of heavyweight process methodologies like SEI Level 5 that were celebrated in connection with shuttle software
money_quotes: After investing $273 million, NASA is canceling a cutting-edge launch-control computer system for the space shuttle that is over budget, behind schedule and too expensive to operate.
It seems to me as if the most complex piece software that could possibly be run on such systems would pale in comparison to the complexity of the software in a modern CDMA cellular phone.
I know, somebody's going to tell me about how mission critical this stuff is, and how lives depend on it, but heavens, $273 million dollars?
Recreating those old systems with modern tools would probably be a weekend Visual Basic/Access project.
You don't need a zillion dollar Big Science project: you need a four week project writing a CPU emulator for that old mainframe that runs on PCs.
Flipping back and forth between today's news and that old FastCompany hagiography will keep you entertained for hours!
posts.key_insight: Massive government software replacement projects fail not because the technical problem is hard, but because bureaucratic processes inflate simple problems into quarter-billion-dollar boondoggles.

posts.summary: This is a brief link-blog entry where Spolsky references Dave Winer's commentary on his earlier 'Platforms' essay, noting the lesson of General Magic as a cautionary tale about platforms positioned as applications. He also announces a new .NET programming discussion group on Fog Creek's forums, linking to his rationale for creating it and humorously hoping it won't devolve into unhelpful tech support chaos.
key_points: Dave Winer extends Spolsky's platform thesis with the advice that platform owners should never say no to developers
General Magic serves as a cautionary example of a company that had a platform but tried to position it as an application, failing despite massive investment
Spolsky launched a .NET discussion group, signaling Fog Creek's engagement with Microsoft's then-new platform
money_quotes: If you're lucky enough to get a gazillion dollars invested behind your ideas, never say no to a developer.
General Magic had a platform they tried to position as an application.
posts.key_insight: Even massive funding can't save a platform that fails to embrace developers and mistakenly positions itself as merely an application.

posts.summary: Eddie Kessler, Napster's former VP of Engineering and CTO, responds to Joel Spolsky's criticism of Napster's development timeline. He details the enormous technical challenges involved in building a paid music service on peer-to-peer technology, including DRM, acoustic fingerprinting, royalty payment systems, and content identification. Kessler argues the system was actually completed and ready, but deployment was blocked by record labels refusing to license content. He also notes the team was simultaneously forced to modify the existing system under court order while key engineers and he himself were consumed by legal proceedings.
key_points: Building a legal paid music service on P2P technology required solving near-impossible problems like 100% accurate MP3 identification regardless of filename, which is 'not even theoretically possible'
The engineering team was simultaneously forced to comply with court-ordered modifications to the existing system while building the new version, splitting their resources
The system was actually completed and in beta from December 2001 — the holdup was record labels refusing to license content, not engineering failure
The original Napster was not simply Shawn Fanning's 3-month project scaled up — the server code was completely rewritten to handle 2 million simultaneous users
Legal obligations consumed massive engineering leadership time, with the CTO personally involved in briefs, depositions, and meetings with RIAA technical experts
money_quotes: Determine with 100% reliability if any of these files is on a black list of disallowed files, provided by recording companies and music publishers in many formats and with varying degrees of accuracy. This is not even theoretically possible.
The only holdup to full deployment was the record companies' and publishers' refusal to license their content to Napster.
I hope you don't think that the '3 months' Shawn took to write the original version (not true anyway) was the reliable system that scaled to handle 2 million simultaneous users!
Although the Windows client was largely Shawn's, the server code was completely rewritten.
In this case, we were really building a new first version.
posts.key_insight: What appeared externally as engineering failure at Napster was actually a completed system blocked by licensing refusals, built under impossible constraints of simultaneous court-ordered work and near-theoretically-impossible technical requirements.

posts.summary: Joel describes spending four weeks writing the FogBUGZ 3.0 setup program from scratch in VC++ and MFC because no commercial installer toolkit could handle even a quarter of what was needed. He imposed a requirement that every step of the installation wizard be completely reversible, allowing users to back up through the wizard while the installer silently undoes all changes. He acknowledges this may have been overkill but considers the result a great setup program.
key_points: Commercial setup toolkits (Wise, InstallShield, Windows Installer) couldn't handle more than 25% of what the FogBUGZ installer needed
Joel built the entire installer from scratch in VC++ and MFC with full reversibility — every wizard step could be undone including file moves, account creation, permissions, and database setup
The project took longer than expected due to the self-imposed reversibility requirement, which Joel admits was 'a tad overkill'
A side benefit of writing a custom installer was full control over the UI, replacing generic clip-art with a personal photograph
money_quotes: For various reasons none of the commercial setup toolkits (Wise, InstallShield, Windows Installer) could do even 25% of what our setup program needed to do, so I ended up writing it from scratch.

I had this weird conviction that the entire setup process should be completely reversable, and as you go backwards through the wizard it should automatically and silently undo any changes it had already made.

Even if you're looking at the last screen, if you decide you want to change where to extract the files, you can just back up and change it and everything moves and all the old files you overwrote are put back and all the Windows accounts you created are uncreated and all the permissions we granted are ungranted and the SQL database you made is unmade.
The end result is a great setup program, but it may be a tad overkill.
One benefit of writing a setup program from scratch is that I don't have to have someone else's stupid cartoon-of-a-computer-and-floppy-disks in the upper right hand corner.
posts.key_insight: Joel's insistence on full reversibility in an installer illustrates his characteristic attention to user experience even in mundane software components, while honestly acknowledging the diminishing returns of perfectionism.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief announcement post where Joel Spolsky introduces a new discussion group dedicated to Delphi programming questions, hosted on the Fog Creek discussion forums. The post contains virtually no substantive content beyond the link and one-line description. It serves purely as a signal boost for the new forum.
key_points: Joel launched a dedicated Delphi programming discussion group on the Fog Creek forums
The post reflects the era when Joel actively cultivated developer communities around specific technologies
Delphi (Borland's Object Pascal IDE) was still a relevant enough technology in 2002 to warrant its own discussion forum
posts.key_insight: This is a minimal announcement post with no substantive content to analyze beyond the existence of a new Delphi discussion forum.

posts.summary: Spolsky responds to reader feedback about his FogBUGZ Setup program, defending several design decisions. He explains why setup operations should be reversible rather than batched at the end, since failures mid-batch still require rollback. He defends using VC++/MFC over Delphi by arguing productivity depends on what the developer already knows, not abstract language superiority. Most notably, he argues that investing in a good setup program pays off through reduced tech support costs, customer delight that drives upgrades, and better software reviews.
key_points: Setup operations should be reversible because failures can occur at any step, and leaving a system in an indeterminate state is unacceptable — the rollback code needs to exist anyway
Language productivity is personal, not absolute: a developer who knows VC++/MFC deeply will outperform themselves as a beginner in a supposedly more productive language like Delphi
Good setup programs pay for themselves through decreased tech support costs, customer willingness to upgrade, and favorable software reviews
Validating user input immediately at each step is better UX than collecting everything upfront and reporting errors pages later during a long install process
The painful memory of a bad installation experience (like SpamAssassin) can permanently deter users from upgrading, costing real business
money_quotes: Somehow I managed to figure out in high school that language advocacy and religious arguments are unbelievably boring.
Even if Delphi were more productive, the only pertinent question, since I am writing the code, is what is more productive for Joel Spolsky.
I would definitely outcode a completely inexperienced Delphi programmer (which is me), certainly over a short 4 week project.

I'm still using an old version of SpamAssassin that is becoming increasingly ineffective, even though I know the new version is much better, because I just can't bear the thought of another morning wasted.
If SpamAssassin was making money off of upgraders they would have lost my business because they don't have a SETUP program.
A single flaw in setup is guaranteed to be mentioned in every review because every reviewer will see it and say 'Aha!'
It would be extremely rude to leave IIS not running.
posts.key_insight: A polished setup experience is a business investment that reduces support costs, encourages upgrades, and wins reviews — and the engineering decisions behind it (reversibility, immediate validation, familiar tools) should be driven by practical reality, not theoretical ideals.

posts.summary: Joel recommends the new edition of Danny Goodman's Dynamic HTML reference book. He praises the first edition as the best HTML reference despite being outdated, and notes the new 1400-page edition covers what browsers actually do in practice. He considers it essential for anyone working with HTML.
key_points: Danny Goodman's Dynamic HTML reference has been updated after several years
The book's value lies in documenting what browsers actually do, not just what specs say
At 1400 pages, it serves as a comprehensive practical reference for web development
money_quotes: It's been a few years since the first edition, which is still the best reference on HTML despite being severely out of date.
1400 packed pages that actually tells you what web browsers that are actually in use actually do, which makes it invaluable.
posts.key_insight: The most valuable technical references document how things actually work in practice, not just what the specifications say.

posts.summary: Joel provides a brief status update on the community-driven effort to translate Joel on Software articles into multiple languages. He notes that new translations are available in over ten languages and acknowledges that he sometimes delays responding to volunteer translators for up to a week. He puts out a call for copy editors in several languages where translations are stuck in the queue.
key_points: Joel on Software articles are being translated into numerous languages by volunteer contributors
Joel batches translation-related emails and processes them weekly rather than responding immediately
Several translations are blocked in the queue due to a lack of copy editors for specific languages
money_quotes: As my faithful volunteers have learned it sometimes takes me a week to respond to their email; I usually let all the translation-related email pile up and then work through it once a week.
posts.key_insight: A brief housekeeping update on the volunteer-driven internationalization of Joel on Software, notable mainly as an example of community-powered content translation in the early blogging era.

posts.summary: This is a short link-blog post where Joel Spolsky shares three unrelated links he found interesting. He highlights a positive review of his book on usability, a humorous personification of programming languages from Burningbird, and a National Review article criticizing intelligence service leadership. The post is characteristic of early-2000s blogging style where authors curated interesting links with brief commentary.
key_points: Joel's book on usability was praised for its conciseness compared to lengthier alternatives
The Burningbird quote humorously personifies programming languages, with C characterized as rude but respected for its effectiveness
The National Review piece argues intelligence failures stem from insufficient analytical talent rather than lack of effort
money_quotes: I'm sure someone has written the thousand-page tome about usability, but I'll take this one instead.
All C ever said was 'bite me', being a rude language and hard to live with, but still respected because it was such a good worker.
What they didn't have enough of, was brains.
posts.key_insight: A curated linkblog post sharing three brief reading recommendations spanning tech culture, programming humor, and current affairs.

posts.summary: This is a brief promotional post announcing the upcoming release of FogBUGZ 3.0, scheduled for November 4th. Spolsky shares a link to a preview of new features and references an external review by Daniel Berlinger. The post confirms the product mascot (an orangutan) is staying. It's essentially a product marketing update rather than an essay.
key_points: FogBUGZ 3.0 was on track for its November 4th ship date
The product retained its orangutan mascot despite apparent discussion about removing it
External reviewers were already praising the product for bug tracking, customer service, and project management
money_quotes: We're still on schedule to ship the new version on November 4th.
And no, we didn't get rid of the cute orangutan.
posts.key_insight: A short product announcement teasing FogBUGZ 3.0's release, notable mainly as a snapshot of Spolsky's dual role as blogger and product builder.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares a collection of observations on technology topics circa 2002, covering remote desktop software, CPU architecture strategy, Mozilla's XUL framework, and server log analysis. He argues Windows Terminal Services is far superior to VNC for remote access due to its smarter wire protocol. He draws a parallel between Intel's ground-up Itanium redesign and Netscape's rewrite, predicting AMD's incremental Hammer approach will win. He revisits his critique of Netscape's rewrite-from-scratch decision, arguing they should have shipped incrementally while acknowledging XUL as a genuinely valuable outcome. He closes with early-internet-era data questions about .NET adoption and RSS feed usage.
key_points: Windows Terminal Services vastly outperforms VNC because it operates at the GDI layer rather than transmitting pixel bitmaps, making it usable even over modem connections
Intel's from-scratch Itanium approach mirrors the same strategic mistake as Netscape's rewrite — AMD's incremental extension of existing architecture is the smarter bet
Netscape should have incrementally rebuilt their browser in steps (rendering engine, then XUL, then UI port) rather than rewriting from scratch, which cost them four years of market share
XUL represents a genuinely useful cross-platform GUI framework that could let developers target Windows, Mac, and Linux for roughly 110% of the cost of Windows alone
People chose IE over Netscape 4.0 because it was better, not primarily because of Microsoft's anticompetitive practices
money_quotes: Intel's throw-it-all-away-and-start-over Itanium CPU project is turning out to be the Ishtar of the CPU world: way over budget, years late, and terrible.
Getting from a messy architecture to a nice architecture costs time, but it doesn't cost as much as starting from scratch did.
They shouldn't have rewritten from scratch. They should have done this all in steps. Big chunky steps, fine, but steps.
Over the period of time between Netscape 4 and Mozilla 1 they could have had three releases and still be where they are now. No, way ahead.
Being virtually out of the market for 4 years was a catastrophe for Netscape's browser market share and handed Microsoft a huge monopoly.

People used IE instead of Netscape 4.0 over the last four years because they liked it better, not because they were tricked into it or because it was installed on their desktop by default. Give people some credit.
Application developers finally have a way to deliver to all three platforms for perhaps 110% of the cost of Windows alone.
posts.key_insight: Whether in CPU design or browser development, incremental improvement of existing systems consistently beats ambitious ground-up rewrites that sacrifice years of market presence for architectural purity.

posts.summary: Joel provides brief updates on several technical topics he's been discussing. He addresses VNC alternatives, noting TightVNC is faster but still inferior to Terminal Services. He shares data on .NET CLR browser penetration rates (6-8%, lower on consumer sites). He also discusses RSS bandwidth waste, concluding that HTTP spec already has the solution and aggregators need to implement it, praising Dave Winer for quick implementation and calling on others to follow.
key_points: TightVNC improves on VNC but still falls far short of Terminal Services in speed and usability
.NET CLR penetration was only 6-8% in server logs, with consumer-oriented sites seeing as low as 2-3%
RSS bandwidth problems could be solved by properly implementing existing HTTP spec features rather than inventing new solutions
Open source and indie developers responded quickly to the bandwidth concern, with Radio Userland and NetNewsWire fixing the issue within days
money_quotes: It's faster than VNC but nowhere near as fast and usable as Terminal Services.
The conclusion is the best way to reduce this bandwidth is already a part of the HTTP spec, and needs to be implemented by the aggregators.
I don't want to have to hide the feed from bandwidth hogging user agents.
I shall now shut up about this topic because I know it's excruciatingly boring to the rest of the world.
posts.key_insight: The RSS bandwidth problem didn't need a new protocol or standard — the solution already existed in HTTP and just needed aggregator developers to implement it properly.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky revisits a list of prominent Silicon Alley (New York City dot-com) companies he had tracked two years earlier, checking how their stocks performed during the continued tech bust. Nearly every company on the list declined further, with many effectively worthless or acquired at fire-sale prices. He traces the fates of each company — mergers, name changes, lawsuits, and shutdowns — painting a picture of an industry that collapsed under its own hype. He notes with humor that his former employer Juno Online Services was the sole bright spot, having risen in value after replacing its old management.
key_points: Nearly all prominent Silicon Alley companies continued to lose value in the two years following the initial dot-com crash, with many going to near zero
The fates of these companies varied — some were acquired, some pivoted (EarthWeb became Dice), some simply vanished — but almost none thrived independently
The only company that gained value was Juno Online Services, which Spolsky attributes to a complete management overhaul
Spolsky uses the data to implicitly reinforce the wisdom of index fund investing over individual stock picking
money_quotes: Silicon Alley was the term used for New York City's ephemeral dot com industry which lasted for maybe four years before collapsing under the weight of its own overhype.

EarthWeb, which had started out as a Big List of Java Applets in the days when something like that was amusing, changed their name to Dice and dumped the old EarthWeb web site onto retro ubercontent superportal internet.com.
The only reason Agency.com is worth so much is that your share was bought for cash by a big advertising agency before it could go any lower.
Thankfully I personally own only stock index funds, no individual stocks in public companies.

Personally the most interesting thing is that my old company, Juno Online Services, has actually been a great investment during a horrible 2 years. Probably because the old management has been completely replaced.
My leaving probably helped a bit, in fact, shortly after I left the stock surged momentarily to about $80 in celebration.
posts.key_insight: The dot-com bubble's aftermath was even worse than the initial crash suggested, with nearly every hyped Silicon Alley company continuing its decline into irrelevance, acquisition, or oblivion.

posts.summary: Joel provides a brief status update on the upcoming FogBUGZ 3.0 release, scheduled for November 4th. The remaining work includes marketing website updates, minor bug fixes, and online store improvements for selling support contracts and upgrades. It's a short countdown post reflecting the final stretch before shipping.
key_points: FogBUGZ 3.0 is less than a week from its planned ship date of November 4th
Remaining work is scoped to marketing, minor bugs, and online store improvements
The team is expanding their business model to sell support contracts and upgrades online
money_quotes: All that's left is the new marketing stuff for the website, a couple of small bug fixes, and some improvements to the online store so we can sell support contracts and upgrades online.
posts.key_insight: A confident ship-date countdown showing a small team with a tightly scoped punch list heading into launch week.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post, essentially a one-liner sharing a humorous screenshot from FogBugz's sample bug database. Joel links to an image of a sample bug report that contains an amusing complaint about 'Programmer Longhair's attitude.' The post serves as light entertainment and indirect promotion of FogBugz, Fog Creek's bug tracking product.
key_points: Joel shares a humorous example from FogBugz's sample bug database
The post serves as subtle product marketing through entertainment
It highlights how sample data in developer tools can have personality and humor
money_quotes: I would just like to say that Programmer Longhair's attitude is quite inappropriate…
posts.key_insight: Even sample data in developer tools can be an opportunity for personality and subtle product promotion.

posts.summary: Joel announces the release of FogBUGZ 3.0, which expands from simple bug tracking to a broader development management system. The upgrade emphasizes customer feedback integration, allowing companies to handle customer service email through a shared web interface rather than single-user email clients. It supports Extreme Programming-style prioritized feature lists. Joel highlights that FogBUGZ intentionally omits individual performance metrics, arguing that measuring programmers by bug counts causes them to game the system rather than improve quality.
key_points: FogBUGZ 3.0 adds customer feedback integration via email and web, turning customer suggestions into trackable feature items alongside bugs
The email integration component can replace company-wide email aliases with a shared, trackable web interface for customer service
FogBUGZ intentionally omits individual performance metrics because measuring programmers by bug counts causes gaming behavior that makes the tracking system worthless
The product philosophy is to enforce good software development practices by design, including deliberately excluding features that sound useful but reduce effectiveness
money_quotes: As soon as you start measuring people and compensating people based on things like this, they are going to start optimizing their behavior to optimize these numbers, and not in the way you intended.
Every bug report becomes an argument. Programmers insist on recategorizing bugs as 'features.'
Testers are afraid to enter bugs — why antagonize programmers?
The measurements give you what you 'wanted': the number of bugs in the bug tracking system goes down to zero. Of course, there are just as many bugs in the code.
The bug tracking software, hijacked as an HR crutch, becomes worthless for what it was intended for.
We long ago decided that to make FogBUGZ successful, it needs to reflect and enforce as much as we know about good software development practices.
posts.key_insight: Bug tracking tools that provide individual performance metrics undermine their own purpose because developers and testers will game the numbers rather than honestly track defects.

posts.summary: After shipping FogBUGZ 3.0, Spolsky faced unexpected server issues when trial users surged beyond capacity. Rather than lamenting the lack of load testing, he constructs a simple expected-cost analysis showing it was actually rational to skip it. He extends this into a broader argument about using economic models to allocate engineering resources, distinguishing between internal software (where bugginess is acceptable) and commercial software (where incremental quality improvements drive competitive advantage).
key_points: A simple expected-cost calculation shows that skipping load testing was the economically rational choice given the probabilities and costs involved.
Engineering decisions should be driven by economic models, not emotional reactions or blanket 'best practices' pronouncements.

Internal software should rationally tolerate more bugs because fixing them yields steeply diminishing returns, while commercial software in competitive markets gains its edge from fixing progressively smaller bugs.
The cost of failure matters as much as the probability of failure when deciding where to invest engineering effort.
Post-launch support burden scales with sales success — even a small percentage of users needing help becomes significant at volume.
money_quotes: You need some kind of economic model to decide where to spend your limited resources.
You can't make sensible decisions reliably by saying things like 'load testing is a no-brainer' or 'the server will probably survive.' Those are emotional brain droppings, not analysis.
And in the long run we scientists will win.
It's not worth spending an engineering day to fix a problem that wastes 30 seconds of someone's time once a month.
With commercial software in highly competitive markets virtually all your competitive advantage comes from fixing smaller and smaller bugs to attain a higher level of quality.
It is economically rational for internal software systems to be somewhat buggy, as long as they get the job done. After that bug fixes become deadweight.
posts.key_insight: Engineering decisions about quality and testing should be driven by expected-cost analysis rather than blanket best practices, and the calculus differs fundamentally between internal tools and commercial products.

posts.summary: This is a brief teaser post directing readers to Joel's full article 'The Law of Leaky Abstractions.' The post introduces the concept that all abstractions leak — they fail to perfectly hide their underlying complexity. Joel argues this leakage happens pervasively and is a drag on software development. The full argument is developed in the linked feature article, which became one of his most influential pieces.
key_points: All abstractions eventually fail to hide their underlying complexity
Leaky abstractions are pervasive across software development
These failures impose real costs and slow down development
money_quotes: Abstractions fail. Sometimes a little, sometimes a lot.
There's leakage. Things go wrong.
It happens all over the place when you have abstractions, and it's dragging us down.
posts.key_insight: Abstractions inevitably leak, failing to fully hide the complexity beneath them, and this leakage is a fundamental drag on software development.

posts.summary: Spolsky introduces his 'Law of Leaky Abstractions,' arguing that all non-trivial abstractions inevitably fail to fully hide the complexity beneath them. He illustrates this with TCP/IP as a primary example — TCP builds reliability on top of unreliable IP, but network failures still leak through. He extends this to SQL query performance, C++ strings, NFS file systems, ASP.NET, and even driving in rain. The key consequence is that while abstractions let us handle greater complexity, they don't reduce the amount developers must learn, because when abstractions break, you need to understand the underlying layers. Paradoxically, as programming tools become more powerful and abstract, becoming a proficient programmer becomes harder, not easier.
key_points: All non-trivial abstractions are leaky — they fail to completely hide underlying complexity, and the failures manifest as bugs, performance problems, or mysterious behavior.
TCP/IP perfectly illustrates leaky abstraction: TCP builds reliability atop unreliable IP, but network failures, congestion, and severed cables still leak through.
Abstractions save us time working but don't save us time learning — developers must still understand the layers beneath their tools to debug inevitable leaks.
As programming tools become higher-level with better abstractions, becoming a proficient programmer paradoxically becomes harder because there are more layers to understand when things go wrong.
Code generation tools and 'wizzy' productivity shortcuts are especially prone to this problem — when they break, users who skipped learning the fundamentals are completely stuck.
money_quotes: All non-trivial abstractions, to some degree, are leaky.

TCP attempts to provide a complete abstraction of an underlying unreliable network, but sometimes, the network leaks through the abstraction and you feel the things that the abstraction can't quite protect you from.
The abstractions save us time working, but they don't save us time learning.
And all this means that paradoxically, even as we have higher and higher level programming tools with better and better abstractions, becoming a proficient programmer is getting harder and harder.
When you need to hire a programmer to do mostly VB programming, it's not good enough to hire a VB programmer, because they will get completely stuck in tar every time the VB abstraction leaks.
Learn how to do it manually first, then use the wizzy tool to save time.
The Law of Leaky Abstractions is dragging us down.
One day they'll write the code "foo" + "bar", and truly bizarre things will happen, and then I'll have to stop and teach them all about char*'s anyway.
posts.key_insight: Abstractions save working time but not learning time — every layer of abstraction a programmer uses is another layer they must eventually understand when it inevitably leaks.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post noting that Joel on Software has launched a Russian translation, with seven articles initially available. Joel thanks the eleven translators and editors who contributed to the effort and notes that more translated articles are forthcoming.
key_points: Joel on Software launched a Russian language version with 7 translated articles
The translation was a community effort involving 11 volunteer translators and editors
The project demonstrates the international reach and appeal of Joel's writing
money_quotes: Joel on Software in Russian is now live with the first 7 articles.
posts.key_insight: This is a minor announcement post about the Russian translation of Joel on Software, with no substantive technical or business content.

posts.summary: Spolsky addresses the growing spam problem and the collateral damage caused by overzealous spam filtering. He describes how aggressive filters are bouncing legitimate emails, including his own double-opt-in mailing list, because the delivery provider's IP was blacklisted. He advocates for tagging-based spam filters over bounce-based ones, since bouncing risks losing wanted mail. He then proposes a micropayment system where sending an email costs one cent, which would be economically prohibitive for spammers but trivial for legitimate senders.
key_points: Aggressive spam filters that bounce rather than tag emails cause collateral damage by blocking legitimate mail
Even double-opt-in mailing lists operated by reputable providers are getting caught in IP-based blacklists
Tagging spam and letting users sort it is far safer than bouncing it before delivery
A micropayment system (one cent per email) would make spam economically unviable while remaining trivial for legitimate senders
The micropayment model could even redistribute most of the cost to recipients as sending credits, creating a self-sustaining system
money_quotes: My incoming spam ratio is well over 50% by now.
Overzealous system administrators are causing serious damage to the connectivity of the Internet by imposing draconian spam filters.
If you or your ISP is using a spam filter that bounces mail, you're going to lose stuff that you didn't want to lose.
No spammer can afford the penny times the 19 million messages they send.
Eventually, if it caught on, you wouldn't need a spam filter: just put all the free email in a suspect folder, and check it once a week.
posts.key_insight: The real damage from spam isn't the spam itself but the aggressive filtering systems that block legitimate communication — the solution is economic disincentives rather than blunt technical barriers.

posts.summary: Spolsky recommends Eliyahu Goldratt's books 'The Goal' and 'Critical Chain,' explaining how the Theory of Constraints applies to software project scheduling. He describes how padded estimates paradoxically cause schedules to slip due to Student Syndrome, multitasking overhead, and the asymmetry where delays accumulate but advances do not. Goldratt's solution is to use unpadded 50th-percentile estimates for individual tasks and move all buffer time to the end of the project. Spolsky finds this approach compelling for anyone doing project management.
key_points: Padded task estimates paradoxically cause schedules to slip rather than providing safety margin
Student Syndrome means people procrastinate regardless of how much time they're given — extra time is simply wasted
Schedule delays accumulate across tasks but early completions do not, because people waste surplus time rather than starting the next task early
Goldratt's solution: use 50th-percentile estimates per task and aggregate all buffer at the end of the project or milestone
money_quotes: Most people's intuition is to come up with conservative, padded estimates for each task, but they still find that their schedules always slip.
Goldratt shows that the slip is precisely because we pad the estimates for each step.
Student Syndrome – no matter how long you give students to work on something, they will start the night before.

We wanted to be kinder and gentler the next semester. So we gave them two weeks to do the same homework assignment. The first week went by. The students were working on other classes, playing sports on the lawn, going out with friends.
Delays accumulate, while advances do not.
If you have finished this week's work on Friday morning, chances are you will waste time on Friday afternoon rather than starting the next week's work.
Each individual task's estimate should be exactly in the middle of the probability curve, so there is a 50% chance you will finish early and a 50% chance you will be late.
posts.key_insight: Padding individual task estimates causes schedules to slip because delays accumulate while early finishes are wasted; instead, use realistic estimates per task and pool all buffer at the project level.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief announcement post noting that new Spanish translations of Joel's articles have been added to the site. There is no substantive argument or analysis — it is purely a housekeeping update for readers. The entire post is six words plus a hyperlink.
key_points: New Spanish translations of Joel's articles were published
Joel on Software maintained translations in multiple languages, reflecting its international readership
posts.key_insight: This is a minor housekeeping announcement with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: Joel draws a parallel between Lord Palmerston's famous quote about the impossibly complex Schleswig-Holstein question and the state of modern programming. By invoking a 19th-century diplomatic crisis that drove experts mad, he argues that software development has become unnecessarily complicated. The post is essentially a teaser linking to his longer 'Lord Palmerston and Programming' essay.
key_points: Programming complexity has reached a point where even experts struggle to fully grasp it
The historical analogy to an infamously convoluted diplomatic problem suggests the complexity is systemic, not individual
The brevity of the post itself mirrors the bluntness of the complaint: programming is too hard, full stop
money_quotes: Programming has gotten too hard.
posts.key_insight: Programming has accumulated so much accidental complexity that it rivals historically unsolvable problems in its ability to confound even experts.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky outlines Fog Creek Software's plans for 2003, including a printed column for Programmer's Paradise, a second book on management, server migration, new office space, and product updates for CityDesk and FogBUGZ. He emphasizes customer-driven development for CityDesk, focusing on removing specific blockers that prevent adoption. For FogBUGZ, the strategy involves commoditizing complements by supporting open-source databases and expanding into broader development workflow management.
key_points: Customer feedback should drive feature prioritization: focus on removing the specific 'I can't use it because X' blockers rather than speculating about neat features
Fog Creek is seeking office space with windowed private offices, reflecting Joel's strong stance that developers need natural light and private workspaces
FogBUGZ strategy involves commoditizing complements by supporting open-source databases instead of requiring expensive Microsoft SQL Server
Joel candidly admits that much of his 2003 writing will be 'rampant speculation, disguised as hard facts'
money_quotes: I think I've already told you everything I know, so most of what I'll write about during 2003 will be rampant speculation, disguised as hard facts!
Overcoming these x's is the easiest way to improve sales.

The great thing about having customers and listening to them is that we get this kind of information, and we don't have to speculate about what features would be neat, and we don't do things just because some programmer thinks they would be fun.

I ask them how they would feel about living in an apartment where the bedroom didn't have a window. 'You would freak out, right?' And that's for a room you're going to sleep in, at night. For a room where you spend the day it's obviously non-negotiable.
I don't have a technical description of cool. 'I know it when I see it.'
posts.key_insight: The most effective product strategy is listening to customers who say 'I like your product but I can't use it because X' and systematically eliminating those blockers.

posts.summary: This short post covers three topics: a teaser for Spolsky's upcoming column on Test-Driven Development in STQE Magazine, a useful Microsoft DLL database tool, and a book recommendation for Dr. Deming's management philosophy. The Deming discussion is the most substantive, arguing against numeric measurement-based incentive systems. Spolsky highlights two key problems: variability in measurements often reflects systemic issues only management can fix, and individuals will optimize locally for the measured metric at the expense of overall company performance.
key_points: TDD requires writing automated tests first, then writing minimal code to satisfy them, with bugs added to the test suite as they're discovered
Microsoft's DLL Help database catalogs every DLL version shipped across all products, useful for debugging version conflicts
Deming's management philosophy warns against rewarding or punishing employees based on numeric measurements
Measurement variability often stems from broken systems that only management can change, not from individual performance differences
Local optimization of a single metric can harm overall company performance
money_quotes: With TDD, you create an automated test first, and only then write the minimal amount of code that you can get away with to satisfy that test.
You can't improve your team's performance just by picking some numeric measurement and then rewarding or punishing people to optimize it.
The variability in the measurement may be caused by a broken system that only management can change, not by individual performance.
People may optimize locally to improve that one measurement, even at the cost of hurting the performance of the company as a whole.
If you're in a rut constantly trying to figure out how to rejigger your employees' incentive systems, this book will get you out of it.
posts.key_insight: Numeric performance metrics and incentive systems often backfire because they conflate systemic problems with individual performance and encourage harmful local optimization.

posts.summary: Joel shares a brief update on CityDesk development work, covering two topics: the release of internal documentation about entity classes for refactoring database access in VB6, and switching the CityDesk installer from InnoSetup to My Inno Setup Extensions. He notes the practical benefits of the new setup tool and describes eliminating unnecessary steps from the installation wizard, particularly mocking the redundant 'ready to install' confirmation screen.
key_points: Entity classes are being used to refactor CityDesk by isolating database access code behind a clean interface
Switching installer tools was motivated by practical needs like conditional compilation for sharing setup scripts between main install and upgrade
Unnecessary installation wizard steps were eliminated, reflecting Joel's focus on removing pointless user friction
money_quotes: the stupid screen that might as well just say, "OK, we realize you just clicked next, please click next again now, thank you."
posts.key_insight: Good software development means both cleaning up internal architecture through patterns like entity classes and eliminating pointless friction in user-facing experiences like installation wizards.

posts.summary: Joel shares Fog Creek's internal documentation for CityDesk's entity classes, a thin abstraction layer over DAO database access in VB 6.0. The entity classes map to database tables with a naming convention (prefix 'E') and provide standardized Load, Save, and List/GetNext methods for the four most common database operations. The design emphasizes simplicity and consistency while explicitly forbidding inefficient database patterns. The article serves as a practical example of how Fog Creek isolates database access behind simple object interfaces rather than scattering SQL throughout application code.
key_points: Entity classes provide a thin mapping layer between VB objects and database tables, isolating all SQL and DAO code from the rest of the application
The 'Golden Rule' forbids letting the abstraction cause inefficient database usage, such as looping updates instead of using SQL UPDATE statements
Large blob fields use deferred loading via property get/lets to avoid unnecessary data extraction during enumeration
The Save method handles both INSERT and UPDATE transparently based on whether the primary key is -1 (new) or set (existing)
The List/GetNext pattern eliminates the common DAO bug of forgetting MoveNext at the bottom of enumeration loops
money_quotes: About 99% of what we do with databases consists of: select one record, enumerate records, possibly matching a query, change a record, add a new record.
Golden Rule: Never let entity classes cause you to do something inefficient with the database.
A lot of times people using the entity classes forget about what's involved in the underlying query, and end up writing code that's ridiculously inefficient.
These are not implemented as property get/lets, that just makes the code ugly; they are implemented as plain members.
One of the most maddening things about SQL is the numerous, inconsistent and unreliable ways of getting the identity field you just inserted.
A common mistake in writing DAO code is forgetting the MoveNext at the bottom of your loop. This eliminates the need for it.
posts.key_insight: A good database abstraction layer should make the four common operations (select, enumerate, change, add) easy and consistent while never hiding the performance implications of the underlying queries.

posts.summary: Joel briefly describes Fog Creek's search for new office space in New York's Garment District, noting the neighborhood's transition from clothing factories to creative and tech companies. He observes that the post-dot-com-bust real estate market heavily favors tenants. He then highlights Clay Shirky's essay about FedEx's failed ZapMail service, drawing a parallel to the hype around 802.11 wireless and VoIP technologies, suggesting the anticipated business opportunities may not materialize.
key_points: New York's Garment District is undergoing gentrification as software companies and creative firms replace clothing factories, paying double the rent
The post-bubble commercial real estate market gives creditworthy tenants significant leverage with desperate landlords
Clay Shirky's analysis of FedEx ZapMail's failure serves as a cautionary tale for overhyped technologies
The expected huge business opportunities around 802.11 and VoIP are likely illusory, following the same pattern as past technology hype
money_quotes: It's great to be a creditworthy tenant looking for office space; there's so much empty office space in New York that landlords are falling over themselves to do good deals.
Everybody's looking for the huge business opportunities around 802.11 and VoIP and they probably aren't really there.
posts.key_insight: The massive business opportunities people expect from new technologies like 802.11 and VoIP are likely overhyped, following the same pattern as past failed technology bets like FedEx's ZapMail.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky argues that optimizing a single business metric while ignoring others leads to local optimization that harms the overall business. He uses Dell Computer as a case study, showing how their obsessive focus on minimizing inventory has pushed costs onto suppliers and created delivery problems for customers. Joel shares his own frustrating experience ordering a server that was repeatedly delayed due to CPU unavailability. He contrasts Dell's build-to-order model unfavorably with competitors who can overnight ship servers, arguing that Dell's inventory obsession creates a significant competitive disadvantage when supply chain hiccups occur.
key_points: Optimizing a single business metric tends to ruin other unmeasured parts of the business, creating local optimization that harms the whole
Dell's low-inventory strategy doesn't eliminate inventory costs — it pushes them onto suppliers who build warehouses next to Dell plants, and the costs get reflected in goods pricing
The zero-margin-of-error inventory model means every supply chain disruption directly translates into delayed customer orders with no buffer
Competitors who carry inventory can ship overnight, giving them a significant advantage over Dell's build-to-order model when customers need hardware quickly
Dell's leadership optimized for inventory velocity and nothing else, so that's exactly — and only — what they got
money_quotes: If you pick some aspect of your business to optimize, and focus only on that one number, you tend to ruin other parts of the business that you're not measuring as carefully, resulting in a local optimization that actually harms your business as a whole.
The dirty little secret about Dell is that all they have really done is push the pain of inventory up to their suppliers and down to their customers.
Sounds to me like they don't make those servers at all.
When you need a computer right away, you can't call Dell: even when everything is working perfectly they have to build the computer for you and it takes a week or two to get it.
The no-margin-of-error inventory model means that every hiccup in the supply chain automatically results in an angry customer.

Michael Dell never told his employees to optimize for customer satisfaction or to optimize for delivery time, he told them to optimize for inventory velocity and nothing else, and that is what he got.
posts.key_insight: Optimizing a single metric in isolation creates local optimization that harms the business as a whole — you get exactly what you measure, and nothing else.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky argues that software companies should maintain radio silence about products under development rather than pre-announcing features. He contrasts Apple's surprise-driven launches with Microsoft's years-long public development cycles, advocating strongly for the Apple approach. He outlines five reasons for keeping quiet: avoiding broken promises, denying competitors advance notice, managing customer expectations, preserving flexibility, and keeping the policy simple enough for all employees to follow. He dismisses the value of advance buzz by pointing to the Segway's wasted early publicity and argues that early feedback can be gathered from a small group of selected customers rather than the entire world. Spolsky acknowledges that this policy may cost occasional sales but considers it worthwhile to protect reputation and avoid the vaporware trap.
key_points: Pre-announcing features gives competitors a free roadmap and at least a six-month head start on matching your innovations
Customers will always imagine announced features doing far more than intended, guaranteeing disappointment upon delivery
Publicity that drops when nobody can actually buy the product is roughly 90% wasted, as the Segway launch demonstrated
A simple 'radio silence' policy is enforceable across an entire company, while nuanced disclosure policies inevitably lead to leaks
Early feedback can be gathered effectively from a few hundred carefully selected customers rather than opening the floodgates to the entire world
money_quotes: Every word I say, by definition, is a promise.
The best way to avoid breaking promises is not to make any.
If you keep your mouth shut until you ship, you will be guaranteed at least half a year without competition before everyone else matches that cool new feature.

When you tell someone about the upcoming clam steaming feature, no matter how careful you are to delimit what it actually does, they will inevitably spin elaborate fantasies about how it cures baldness and warts and has a telepathic user interface.
I'm inclined to think that publicity that comes out when you can't actually buy the product is 90% wasted.
If your policy is Radio Silence, every employee understands it and can follow it. If your policy is in any way complicated, nobody is sure what to do and things leak.
It's better than selling you something that doesn't fit your needs and having you get pissed off at me, or getting a reputation as being unable to deliver.
posts.key_insight: Software companies should default to complete silence about unreleased products because the costs of pre-announcing — broken promises, competitive exposure, inflated expectations, and lost flexibility — far outweigh the marginal benefits of advance buzz.

posts.summary: This brief post contrasts Apple's secretive product development approach with Microsoft's tendency to talk about products years before they ship. Joel poses the question of whether companies should build buzz by discussing products under development or maintain secrecy until launch. The post serves as a teaser linking to his longer article 'Mouth Wide Shut' where he explores this question in depth.
key_points: Apple surprises people with product launches despite devoted watchers trying to uncover secrets
Microsoft discusses products years before shipping, with external testers using .NET long before release
The core strategic question is whether pre-launch buzz or launch-day surprise is the better approach
money_quotes: When Apple releases a new product, they tend to surprise the heck out of people, even the devoted Apple-watchers who have spent the last few months riffling through garbage dumpsters at One Infinite Loop.
Microsoft, on the other hand, can't stop talking about products that are mere glimmers in someone's eye.
Should you talk endlessly about your products under development, in hopes of building buzz, or should you hold off until you've got something ready to go?
posts.key_insight: Companies face a fundamental strategic choice between building pre-launch buzz through openness and maintaining secrecy for maximum launch impact.

posts.summary: Spolsky recounts a disastrous week at Fog Creek Software where multiple systems failed in cascade: a CVS server crash led to days of backup restoration problems, a Verizon fire knocked out their T1 internet through a chain of outsourced providers, and frozen pipes caused by a faulty furnace installation. Each incident illustrates how backup plans and redundancy measures that seem adequate can fail when tested by real disasters. The central lesson is that weak systems appear healthy until neighboring systems break down, creating cascading failures. Spolsky argues for defense in depth: RAID mirroring plus backups, direct service providers instead of outsourced layers, and fixing all related vulnerabilities at once rather than just the immediate problem.
key_points: Backups alone aren't sufficient — a hard drive failure triggered a multi-day cascade of restore problems despite daily offsite backups, leading Spolsky to mandate RAID mirroring on all non-laptop machines

Outsourced services create accountability gaps: their T1 outage involved Savvis outsourcing to WorldCom outsourcing to Verizon, making a trivial fix take over 36 hours because no one in the chain felt urgency
Weak systems appear perfectly healthy until a neighboring system fails, then hidden vulnerabilities cascade — like hayfever triggering a sneeze that throws out a bad back
The correct response to cascading failures is to fix all the problems at once rather than just the proximate cause, building defense in depth across every layer
