money_quotes: Backups aren't good enough. I want RAID mirroring from now on. When a drive dies I want to spend 15 minutes putting in a new drive and resume working exactly where I left off.
This is not the first time that a hard drive failure has led to a series of other problems that wound up wasting days and days of work.
Honestly, it's like pushing on string.

When you're buying a service from a company that's just outsourcing that service, one level deep, it's difficult to get decent customer service. When there are two levels of outsourcing, it's nearly impossible.
The technical problem was relatively trivial and could have been diagnosed and fixed in minutes if we weren't dealing with so many idiot companies.
Weak systems may appear perfectly healthy until neighboring systems break down.
People with allergies and back problems may go for months without suffering from either one, but suddenly an attack of hayfever makes them sneeze hard enough to throw out their back.
Use these opportunities to fix all the problems at once.
posts.key_insight: Redundancy and backups create an illusion of safety — true resilience requires defense in depth, because cascading failures exploit every weak link simultaneously.

posts.summary: Joel describes using a binary search technique on source control history to find a performance regression in CityDesk's publish times, which had doubled from one minute to two minutes. After a profiler identified general bottlenecks but not the specific regression, he systematically checked out old versions by date and timed them, narrowing down the offending change to a single day. The culprit was an inadvertent change to a cache key that turned 99% cache hits into 100% misses. He credits the technique to a colleague named Gabi at Juno and notes it took about an hour to find the bug.
key_points: A profiler can show general bottlenecks but may not pinpoint a specific regression introduced between releases
Binary search through source control history by date is an efficient way to isolate when a performance regression was introduced
A subtle cache key mismatch caused 100% cache misses instead of 99% hits, doubling publish time
Keeping daily builds around makes this binary search debugging technique practical for larger codebases
money_quotes: The next thing I tried was a method I learned from Gabi at Juno: the old binary search method.
I discovered four things that were changed that day, one of which was a function that DevPartner had told me was kind of slow, anyway.

Within minutes I found the culprit — that function was originally written to cache its results because it's often called with the same inputs, and I had inadvertently changed the cache key in one place and not another, so we were getting 100% misses instead of 99% hits.
Total elapsed time to find this bug: about an hour.
This is as good a reason as any to keep all your old daily builds around.
posts.key_insight: When a profiler can't isolate a specific regression, binary searching through your source control history by date is a fast, systematic way to pinpoint exactly when a bug was introduced.

posts.summary: Joel announces a new column in the Programmer's Paradise catalog where he reviews software tools his company actually uses daily, starting with VMware. He also announces that FogBUGZ now supports MySQL (free) in addition to Microsoft SQL Server (expensive), as well as two additional source control systems. This latter move is framed as practicing what he preached in his earlier essay about commoditizing complements — by supporting free database alternatives, Fog Creek reduces the cost of FogBUGZ's complements to make their own product more attractive.
key_points: Joel distinguishes his software reviews from typical magazine reviews by only covering tools his team uses daily at Fog Creek, promising deeper practical insight.
FogBUGZ added MySQL support alongside SQL Server, directly applying Joel's 'commoditize your complements' strategy by reducing customers' database costs.
Expanding platform support (MySQL, CVSNT, Visual SourceSafe) lowers barriers to adoption by giving customers cheaper and more flexible infrastructure choices.
money_quotes: By the twentieth time I'd installed Windows 2000, I could do it in my sleep, even though I don't know a word of Chinese.

Unlike most software reviewers who write for the magazines, who spend just enough time with a the product to get 750 words worth, I plan to review things that we actually use on a daily basis here at Fog Creek and talk about how we use them.
Smart companies try to commoditize their products' complements.
We decided to take some of our own advice, here, so as of today, FogBUGZ can be run on top of MySQL, which is free, in addition to Microsoft SQL Server, which is expensive.
posts.key_insight: Practicing the 'commoditize your complements' strategy, Fog Creek added free MySQL support to FogBUGZ to lower the total cost of adoption and remove expensive dependencies that weren't adding value to their own product.

posts.summary: Joel describes moving Joel on Software from a T1 line in the Fog Creek office to a colocation facility operated by Peer 1 Network. He explains why the T1 proved unreliable due to layers of outsourced providers (Savvis reselling Worldcom reselling Verizon), making outage resolution painfully slow. He walks through the colocation shopping process, comparing pricing tiers and bandwidth models, and explains why Peer 1 won out — they offered free hosting after seeing his site. The post concludes with a detailed account of physically setting up the Dell 2U server at the downtown Manhattan facility.
key_points: Outsourcing creates chains of responsibility that make problem resolution much harder — when Savvis had to convince Worldcom to convince Verizon to fix a line, it was 'like pushing on a string'
Static HTML generation (via CityDesk) handled 100K-500K daily hits on modest hardware, demonstrating that avoiding dynamic page generation eliminates most scaling concerns

The colocation market in 2003 split into three tiers: overbuilt dot-com-era companies heading for bankruptcy, scrappy small providers, and the rare company that combined quality with reasonable pricing
Colocation gives flexibility to run any software you want, unlike managed web hosting where you're constrained to the host's stack
Physical server setup details — dual power supplies, remote administration cards, packet filtering before going live — reveal the hands-on nature of early-2000s web infrastructure
money_quotes: Outsourcing is problematic that way: when everything works perfectly, you don't notice, but when things go wrong, it's much harder to get them fixed.
We had to convince Savvis to convince Worldcom to convince Verizon to fix it. Somehow this was like pushing on a string.
Since I publish the site using CityDesk which simply generates a bunch of plain HTML files, the server doesn't have to do anything tricky like generate each page from a database on the fly.

These were usually companies from the Dot Com days that built ridiculously fancy offices and have marble bathrooms in their colos and named gigantic sports stadiums after themselves, and are now in bankrupcy court.
If you ever get a choice of whether to deal with Canadians or New Yorkers, I know it's unpatriotic of me to say this, but, go with the Canadians.
Peer 1 is a small enough company that they care about your business, something which the giant bankrupt telcos don't.
We tried www.google.com, which redirected immediately to www.google.ca. Cool! They think we're in Canada!
posts.key_insight: Layers of outsourced infrastructure create accountability gaps where no single provider owns the problem, making colocation with a responsive, right-sized provider more reliable than a theoretically superior but multi-layered ISP arrangement.

posts.summary: Joel announces that he has moved Joel on Software to a new server at a colocation facility operated by Peer 1 Network. He mentions learning about web hosting in the process and links to a separate article describing the experience in detail. The post itself is purely an announcement with no substantive content.
key_points: Joel migrated his blog to a new colocation hosting provider (Peer 1 Network)
He wrote a separate detailed article about what he learned about web hosting
money_quotes: In the process of finding a new home and getting it up and running I've learned quite a bit about how web hosting works.
posts.key_insight: This is a brief announcement post linking to a longer article about web hosting infrastructure.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky responds to Dave Winer's question about why Joel on Software gets dramatically more traffic from Slashdot links than other sites. Spolsky first corrects the numbers, clarifying the difference between hits and page views, reducing the apparent gap significantly. He then explains the multiplier effect: Slashdot posts coincide with his email newsletter to 16,000 subscribers, plus dozens of bloggers linking simultaneously. Finally, he notes that deep back-catalog content keeps new visitors clicking around, inflating page view counts beyond what a single referral source would generate.
key_points: The difference between hits and page views matters enormously when comparing traffic numbers — Spolsky's 500,000 hits translated to about 120,000 page views, with only 90,000 being incremental
Traffic spikes are rarely from a single source; Slashdot links coincided with newsletter emails to 16,000 subscribers and dozens of bloggers linking simultaneously
Content depth creates a multiplier effect — extensive archives encourage new visitors to explore beyond the linked article
Readers who find content that validates their views forward it to colleagues, creating organic viral distribution
money_quotes: I'm not sure what a read is, but a hit is a single file served by the web server.
Even the simplest page on this site consists of four files: the header GIF, the Made with CityDesk GIF, the CityDesk logo, and the article itself.
I almost always get slashdotted on a day when I release a new article. This is coincidentally the same day I send email to 16,000 subscribers telling them about the new article.
Some percentage of those people say, 'Aha! This precisely proves my point!' and forward the URL to their boss or underling to hit them over the head with it. 'See? Nya!'
Joel on Software has enough old content that many new visitors stay a while and click around.
posts.key_insight: Viral traffic spikes are rarely attributable to a single source — they result from compounding effects of multiple channels firing simultaneously plus deep content that retains new visitors.

posts.summary: Joel documents his quest to connect a professional studio microphone to a standard computer sound card for better audio quality in his CityDesk online demos. He details the exact equipment chain needed: a Shure SM58 microphone, a preamp to boost the signal to line level, and the right combination of cables and adapters. He notes that the music store salesperson confidently sold him the wrong setup, missing the preamp entirely. The post serves as a practical guide for others attempting the same thing, and includes before/after audio samples to demonstrate the improvement.
key_points: A professional microphone like the Shure SM58 cannot be plugged directly into a computer sound card — it requires a preamp to boost the signal to line level.
Professional music store staff may sound confident but give incomplete or wrong advice about connecting pro audio gear to consumer computer hardware.
Professional music stores refuse to stock 1/8-inch jack cables, creating an annoying gap between pro audio and consumer computer equipment.
The total setup requires four components: microphone, desk stand, mic cable, preamp, and an adapter cable chain to reach the sound card's mini jack.
money_quotes: The stoner DJ sales dude sounded very confident but he didn't tell me that I needed a preamp, and he gave me the wrong kind of cables.
For some reason the professional music stores like Sam Ash and Zzounds think it is beneath their dignity to stock any parts with 1/8″ jacks, but that is what your sound card needs.
posts.key_insight: Bridging the gap between professional audio equipment and consumer computer hardware requires specific knowledge about signal levels and connectors that even music store staff may not have.

posts.summary: Joel announces that his discussion forum has been running as an experiment with various hidden design decisions to improve discussion quality. He teases an upcoming essay explaining the forum's design choices but says it will only be distributed to email subscribers, not published on the web, due to 'Heisenberg effects' — the idea that explaining the moderation mechanics publicly would undermine their effectiveness.
key_points: Joel's discussion forum incorporates subtle, behind-the-scenes design decisions aimed at improving discussion quality
He references 'Heisenberg effects' — observing/explaining the system would change how people behave within it
The essay is deliberately kept off the public web to preserve the forum's dynamics
He uses exclusive content as an incentive to grow his email subscriber list
money_quotes: Although it may seem simple, there are a lot of subtle design decisions and magic-behind-the-scenes in hopes of improving the quality of discussion that takes place there.
Because it's full of Heisenberg effects, the essay won't appear on this web site, it will only go out via email to email subscribers.
posts.key_insight: The best community moderation systems work precisely because their mechanisms are invisible to participants — explaining them publicly would undermine their effectiveness.

posts.summary: Spolsky draws on Ray Oldenburg's concept of 'third places' and Robert Putnam's 'Bowling Alone' to argue that modern society, especially among software developers, suffers from a critical lack of social gathering spaces. He observes that programmers often flock to online communities out of loneliness, making community software a digital attempt at creating third places. The post argues that design decisions in community software are as consequential as architectural decisions in physical spaces, shaping whether communities thrive or wither. This is a teaser for his longer article 'Building Communities with Software.'
key_points: Humans need a 'third place' beyond work and home for social interaction, but capitalist society has been steadily eroding these spaces
Software developers are particularly affected by the loss of third places, often relocating to new cities and working long hours out of loneliness
Online communities (forums, chat rooms, open source projects) serve as surrogate third places for isolated programmers
Design decisions in community software directly shape behavior and determine whether a community feels welcoming or deserted, just as physical architecture shapes social interaction
money_quotes: For too many people, life consists of going to work, then going home and watching TV. Work-TV-Sleep-Work-TV-Sleep.
People graduate from college, move across country to a new place where they don't know anyone, and end up working 12 hour days basically out of loneliness.
So it's no surprise that so many programmers, desperate for a little human contact, flock to online communities.
In creating community software, we are, to some extent, trying to create a third place.
When you make something easy, people do it more often. When you make something hard, people do it less often.
In this way you can gently encourage people to behave in certain ways which determine the character and quality of the community.
An ersatz third place is less painful than none at all.
In software, as in architecture, design decisions are just as important to the type of community that develops or fails to develop.
posts.key_insight: Community software design decisions function like architectural choices in physical spaces — they subtly shape human behavior and determine whether an online community becomes a thriving third place or a deserted wasteland.

posts.summary: Joel announces his latest article 'Building Communities with Software' was sent to email subscribers but many didn't receive it due to overzealous spam filters. Corporate email filters at Fortune 500 companies rejected the message for containing words like 'taboo' and 'hard core.' He offers a 'sanitized version' online while poking fun at the absurdity of content filtering.
key_points: Corporate spam filters were blocking legitimate newsletter content based on keyword matching, causing widespread delivery failures
The state of email filtering in 2003 was already creating false positive problems that hurt legitimate communication
Joel uses humor to highlight the absurdity of content filters that can't distinguish context
money_quotes: Such is the state of email today.
Some of them complained about "taboo," other's complained about "hard core."
But it still contains the word "taboo" so if that offends you you may want to avert your eyes!
posts.key_insight: Overzealous automated content filtering creates absurd false positives that block legitimate communication, a problem that was already apparent in 2003.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky argues that online community software design decisions profoundly shape the social dynamics and culture that emerge. Drawing on Ray Oldenburg's concept of 'third places' and Robert Putnam's 'Bowling Alone,' he frames online forums as substitutes for vanishing real-world gathering spots, especially for isolated programmers. He examines how specific design choices in platforms like Usenet, IRC, Slashdot, and his own Joel on Software forum either encourage or discourage healthy conversation. Through a detailed Q&A about his forum's deliberately minimalist design, he explains why he avoided features like email notifications, threading, registration, and post confirmation — each omission carefully chosen to foster better community behavior.
key_points: Small software implementation details result in big differences in how online communities develop, behave, and feel — design choices are the primary driver of community culture.

Features that seem obviously useful (email notifications, branching threads, registration) can actually kill community vitality by reducing engagement, fragmenting conversation, or failing to deter bad actors.
Making things harder (no reply button at the top, no quoting, no confirmation step) paradoxically improves community quality by forcing people to read before responding and write more carefully.
Moderation should be invisible — posting explicit rules insults the 99.99% who behave well and doesn't deter the tiny minority who don't.
Online communities serve as 'third places' for programmers who lack real-world social gathering spots, making thoughtful community software design a genuinely important endeavor.
money_quotes: Small software implementation details result in big differences in the way the community develops, behaves, and feels.

When you make something easy, people do it more often. When you make something hard, people do it less often. In this way you can gently encourage people to behave in certain ways which determine the character and quality of the community.
This one feature, so easy to implement and thus so tempting to programmers, is the best way to kill dead any young forum.
It's like those studies they did that showed that it's safer, on twisty mountain roads, to remove the crash barrier, because it makes people scared and so they drive more carefully.
I am pragmatic and understand that a totally uncensored world just looks like your inbox: 80% spam, advertising, and fraud, rapidly driving away the few interesting people.
Posting rules is just a way to insult the majority of the law-abiding citizens and it doesn't deter the morons who think their own poo smells delicious.
The noisiest people (i.e. the people who post the most words while contributing the fewest ideas) are often the long time, hard core members who visit the forum every ten minutes.
posts.key_insight: Community software should be designed like architecture — every feature decision shapes social behavior, and often the best design choice is to deliberately omit features that would undermine the community you're trying to build.

posts.summary: Joel shares an update about inspecting Fog Creek Software's new office space, a sunny loft in Manhattan's garment district. He describes his vision for the space including private offices, communal areas, and entertainment amenities. The post focuses primarily on his infrastructure demands to the architect, particularly his insistence on abundant power outlets and flexible cabling. It reflects his broader philosophy that developer workspace quality directly impacts productivity.
key_points: Private windowed offices are treated as a non-negotiable requirement for the new Fog Creek office
Joel demands dramatically more power infrastructure than typical offices, specifying 4 outlets per foot of wall space
The office design prioritizes both individual workspace quality (private offices) and communal spaces (living room, kitchenette, pool table)
Flexible network infrastructure is a priority, with the ability to run LAN, telephone, fiber, and cable TV wiring even if exposed
money_quotes: Private windowed offices are non-negotiable.
We need three times as many power outlets as anyone would think.
I'm sick of power strips.
I have ten things plugged in right at my desk.
I specified 4 outlets every foot, is that absurd?
posts.key_insight: A great developer workspace requires treating infrastructure like power and networking as first-class concerns, not afterthoughts.

posts.summary: Joel responds to a positive review of FogBUGZ that criticized its lack of customizability. He explains that this was a deliberate design tradeoff: rather than adding features like per-programmer bug count reports that help close sales, they chose to omit features that enable bad management practices. The theory is that happier users become evangelists who recommend FogBUGZ at new jobs, driving long-term growth. He acknowledges this is partly speculation and that design decisions are hard to prove right.
key_points: FogBUGZ deliberately omits customization features that could enable counterproductive management practices like punishing programmers for high bug counts
When managers misuse bug tracking metrics, programmers resist entering bugs, ultimately subverting the entire system
The tradeoff is between features that close initial sales versus features that make existing users love the product and spread word of mouth
FogBUGZ's strategy of building for enlightened users paid off with 200% sales growth driven by user recommendations at new jobs
money_quotes: It's a tradeoff between implementing features that make the sale, versus implementing features that, we think, will make people who use our software love it, which helps in the long term.
Typical bad managers will use that tool to punish programmers with high bug counts or reward programmers with low bug counts.
Eventually the bug tracking system subverts itself.
The current system, in which we expect FogBUGZ users to have enlightened development processes, makes us miss out on initial sales but it makes our existing customers happier.
We've found that anyone who has been using FogBUGZ and moves on to a new job that doesn't have bug tracking will recommend FogBUGZ at their new job.
Design decisions are hard that way.
posts.key_insight: Deliberately omitting features that enable bad management practices sacrifices short-term sales but builds long-term growth through passionate user evangelism.

posts.summary: Joel announces the addition of bidirectional text support to CityDesk, his content management software. This technical improvement enabled the launch of Hebrew and Farsi editions of Joel on Software. The post highlights the ongoing internationalization effort, bringing the total number of language editions to 24.
key_points: CityDesk added bidirectional (RTL) text support, a non-trivial internationalization feature
Hebrew and Farsi editions of Joel on Software were launched as a result
Joel on Software reached 24 language editions, demonstrating significant global readership
money_quotes: I finally got around to adding bidirection text support to CityDesk, so today I can launch Hebrew and Farsi editions of Joel on Software, bringing the total language count up to 24.
posts.key_insight: Proper internationalization support, including bidirectional text, is essential for reaching a truly global audience.

posts.summary: Joel announces that some of his monthly columns for the Programmer's Paradise catalog are now available online, including reviews of VMWare and ERwin. He explains his policy for writing product reviews: he only reviews tools he actually uses in his daily work at Fog Creek Software. He notes that unlike trade magazine reviewers, he still spends about half his time doing actual software development. The post also briefly mentions upcoming projects including integrating an image editor into CityDesk and signing a lease for new office space.
key_points: Joel maintains a strict review policy of only covering products he genuinely uses in his daily work, distinguishing himself from trade publication reviewers
He emphasizes that he still spends roughly 50% of his time doing hands-on software development despite running Fog Creek
The 750-word column format limits depth but forces focus on practical, real-world product experiences
Fog Creek is expanding into new office space with an architect-designed floorplan featuring parallelogram-shaped offices
money_quotes: I do have a policy for writing reviews: I only review products that I actually want to use in my daily work at Fog Creek.
Unlike trade magazine reviewers, I still spend about 50% of my time doing software development, and I'm happy to try the latest toys if I think they will help.
posts.key_insight: Product reviews are most credible when written by practitioners who actually use the tools in real work, not by professional reviewers evaluating them in isolation.

posts.summary: Joel briefly describes his experience implementing a picture editor feature using a third-party library (LEADTOOLS). He notes the actual coding took about four hours, but the surrounding work of figuring out deployment dependencies and reading documentation consumed nearly as much additional time. The post is a micro-update reflecting the realities of integrating third-party components into shipping software.
key_points: Implementing a feature-rich picture editor (crop, resize, rotate, undo, save) took only four hours using a third-party library
Figuring out which OCX/DLL files to ship and integrating them into the installer took an equally long four hours
The hidden costs of third-party component integration—deployment, documentation, packaging—rival the actual development time
money_quotes: Not bad: about four hours of work to implement a picture editor with crop, resize, rotate, undo, and save.
It did take about four hours yesterday to figure out which OCX/DLL files I need to ship and make sure that SETUP included them.
posts.key_insight: The actual coding of a feature is often only half the work; packaging, deployment, and dependency management consume just as much time.

posts.summary: Joel briefly mentions William Whyte's research finding that corporate relocations almost always move the company closer to the CEO's home, and that these companies subsequently declined. He uses this as a segue to mention that Fog Creek Software is relocating offices, and he's learned more about commercial real estate than expected. The post is essentially a teaser for a longer article about finding office space in New York City.
key_points: William Whyte found that Fortune 500 corporate relocations in the 1950s-60s consistently moved companies closer to the CEO's home, despite elaborate relocation committee processes
Companies that relocated from NYC to the suburbs subsequently performed poorly
Joel acknowledges this is his most off-topic post, teasing a longer article about commercial real estate and office hunting
money_quotes: Virtually all corporate relocations involve a move to a location which is closer to the CEO's home than the old location.

They always had big, complicated Relocation Committees which carefully studied all the options and chose, coincidentally I'm sure, to move to within half a mile of the CEO's home in Danbury, Connecticut.
Whyte also showed that these companies all tanked after the relocation.
I try to write only about things I know about, and, recently, I learned a lot more about commercial real estate than I ever imagined I would need to know.
posts.key_insight: Corporate relocation decisions, despite elaborate rational processes, are often driven by the CEO's personal convenience rather than business strategy — and the results tend to be poor.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky provides an exhaustive practical guide to finding commercial office space in New York City, drawn from his experience relocating Fog Creek Software. He covers the full process from temporary options (executive suites and incubators) through finding permanent space, negotiating leases, and hiring lawyers. He warns that the process takes at least nine months and details the hidden costs, misleading metrics like 'rentable square feet,' and negotiation tactics that tenants should know. The post also touches on William Whyte's research showing that corporate relocations driven by CEO convenience tend to harm company performance.
key_points: Allow nine months for the full office search process: two months to find space, a month to negotiate, two months to sign the lease, and four months for design and construction.

Executive suites and incubators charge 5-10x market rates and are terrible for programmers due to cramped conditions, but normal landlords refuse to lease to VC-funded companies because of high failure rates.
Rentable square feet (RSF) is essentially a fictional number inflated by 'loss factors,' so tenants should negotiate based on total monthly cost rather than price per square foot.
Tenant brokers are mostly unnecessary and can actually hurt you—landlord brokers prefer deals where they keep the full commission, giving unrepresented tenants an edge.
Longer leases are generally better for tenants: they lock in low rents, yield more free months and construction money, and landlords will help you relocate or sublet if circumstances change.
money_quotes: Virtually all corporate relocations involve a move to a location which is closer to the CEO's home than the old location.
Normal landlords hate venture capital funded companies. The few landlords in New York that rented to VC startups during the dotcom boom have learned never to do that again.
Incubators are a terrible place for programmers, because they took the baby chick metaphor a bit too far.
RSF is pretty useless. Sign a lease based on the cost, not the square feet, or they'll 'recalculate' their loss factor the next year and raise your rent.
Don't worry. When you find the right place for you, you'll know it.
He reaches for a red pen and crosses out the paragraph. 'But you're not signing it.'
When your lawyer says this, no offense is taken. He's just the lawyer. But if you, the tenant, try to say it, the landlord will say, 'What, are you planning to be late on the rent?'
posts.key_insight: Finding office space in New York is a months-long process full of hidden costs and misleading metrics, but tenants who do their own legwork, understand the game, and hire a good lawyer will get far better deals than those who rely on brokers.

posts.summary: Spolsky makes two observations: first, a humorous jab at the pointless 'conventions used in this document' sections found in every technical manual. Second, he discusses incremental installation tools for software updates, noting that competition from Red Bend Software's vBuild has driven prices down from RTPatch's original $5000, making binary patching more viable for small software companies like Fog Creek.
key_points: Technical writing conventions sections are self-evident wastes of space that exist seemingly to pad page counts
Competition in the incremental installer market (Red Bend vs RTPatch) drove prices down significantly, from $5000 to around $2500-2750
Even advocates of 'best tools money can buy' must weigh tool cost against actual savings — RTPatch at $5000 was disproportionate to bandwidth costs
Spolsky is willing to switch from a free, excellent tool (InnoSetup) to a paid, merely decent one (InstallShield) to gain access to incremental patching
money_quotes: Why does every technical manual and book include a section at the beginning on 'conventions used in this document,' full of ridiculous and useless tips like, 'Tips are indicated by a lightbulb icon in the margin'?
The familiar dot (.) symbol from Internet addresses shall be used on this website to denote the end of a sentence.
Their salesman called me up to give me a hard time for contradicting myself. After all, step 9 in the Joel Test is using 'the best tools that money can buy.' 'Touché,' I said, and hung up.
Still pricey but starting to seem worth it.
Today I'm going to try converting CityDesk's setup from InnoSetup, which is excellent and free, to InstallShield, which is decent and expensive, just to take advantage of vBuild.
posts.key_insight: Competition drives software tool prices down, and pragmatic developers must constantly reassess the cost-benefit tradeoffs of their tooling choices as the market evolves.

posts.summary: Spolsky discusses installation and patching tools for Windows software distribution. He highlights Wise Solutions' installer features that match his wish list from building the FogBUGZ 3.0 installer. He defends the concept of distributing upgrade patches rather than complete files, arguing that modern tools handle edge cases like corrupted files automatically. Despite the appeal of patching technology, he decides to stick with Inno Setup for CityDesk's next release because the bandwidth savings don't justify the switching cost.
key_points: Wise Installation System implemented features Spolsky had wished for during FogBUGZ 3.0 development, including IIS and SQL Server administration
Patching technology saves bandwidth by only distributing diffs rather than complete files, and tools verify file integrity with hash functions before applying patches
Even first-time installs benefit from patching because many system files and runtimes like MSVCRT are already present on most machines
Despite the technical merits of switching installers, the practical cost of migration didn't justify the bandwidth savings for CityDesk
money_quotes: These tools check the version of each file on the user's machine and also compute a hash function to make sure that the file is not corrupted.
Using patching technology saves a lot of bandwidth, even for the first-time installer, because a lot of the bytes you're distributing may be system files and runtimes that users already have.
For consumer software which may well be downloaded over a modem, this makes a big difference.
The bandwidth savings don't quite justify the effort of switching installers.
posts.key_insight: Even when a technically superior tool exists, the practical cost of switching must be weighed against the actual benefits — a classic build-vs-switch tradeoff.

posts.summary: Joel compares coverage of Delta Air Lines' new technology system by two IT publications: InformationWeek and Baseline magazine. He finds InformationWeek's article to be a shallow rewrite of press releases, while Baseline's coverage is detailed and investigative. Notably, Baseline reported a two-hour system crash during a blizzard that InformationWeek ignored entirely. Joel holds up Baseline as an example of what industry press should aspire to and recommends readers get a free subscription.
key_points: InformationWeek's coverage of Delta's new IT system was essentially rewritten press releases with vague generalizations
Baseline magazine provided detailed, investigative journalism that included reporting on a system outage InformationWeek omitted
The IT industry press has historically failed to do real journalism, instead republishing vendor-friendly narratives
Joel personally experienced the Delta system crash, adding credibility to his critique of InformationWeek's omission
money_quotes: The difference: while the InformationWeek article is just a poor rewrite of bland warmed-over press releases and vague generalizations, the Baseline article is detailed, interesting, and specific.
InformationWeek didn't mention the outage.
In fact the whole Baseline article looks like it was written by an investigative journalist.
It's about time the industry press got its act together.
posts.key_insight: The IT trade press too often serves as a press release republishing service rather than doing real investigative journalism, and publications willing to report inconvenient truths deserve support.

posts.summary: This is a brief link-blog entry where Joel Spolsky shares two items of interest. First, he highlights Eric Sink's analysis of leaky abstractions encountered while building Vault, a source control system in .NET, where Sink favorably compares .NET to Java's reliability in later stages of development. Second, Joel mentions William Poundstone's new book about Microsoft's interview practices and puzzle questions.
key_points: Eric Sink found that Java's productivity gains were offset by bugs caused by leaky abstractions late in the development cycle
.NET is presented as a more reliable platform that avoided the abstraction-related failures Sink experienced with Java
Joel continues his interest in the topic of leaky abstractions, a concept he popularized in his own writing
William Poundstone's book on Microsoft interview puzzles touches on hiring practices Joel has written about extensively
money_quotes: I've used Java extensively, and I loved the productivity gains we got during the beginning and middle of the development cycle. But things got ugly at the end.
All those layers of abstraction started contributing to our bug list.
I've been involved in a couple of projects which completely failed because Java was chosen.
This success stands as a testimony to how incredible .NET really is.
posts.key_insight: Platform abstractions that boost early productivity can become liabilities late in development when their leaks manifest as hard-to-fix bugs.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues against software prototypes by recounting two Microsoft cautionary tales. The Excel team spent an entire summer building a drag-and-drop prototype in Visual Basic 1.0, only for the actual feature to take a week to implement. Another team spent a year prototyping what became Microsoft Bob, a legendary flop. He concludes that software prototypes are inherently wasteful: if they replicate full functionality they should just be the product, and if they don't they're useless for real testing. He advocates for paper prototypes as a superior alternative.
key_points: The Excel drag-and-drop prototype took an entire summer to build, while the real implementation took only a week, undermining the time-saving rationale for prototyping
Software prototypes face a fundamental dilemma: they must duplicate enough real functionality to be testable, which makes them nearly as expensive as the real product
Microsoft Bob exemplifies a year-long prototyping effort that produced a product nobody wanted
Paper prototypes solve both the wasted-effort problem and the 'iceberg problem' of hidden complexity
money_quotes: Building the prototype took all summer, because it had to duplicate so much of Excel's real functionality or you couldn't do a real usability test.
The joke is, of course, that the whole point of creating a prototype is to 'save time.'
I've basically given up on software prototypes.
If the prototype can do everything the product can do, it might as well be the product, and if it can't, it's not much use.
Microsoft Bob, the PCjr of the software world. Another wasted prototype.
posts.key_insight: Software prototypes are caught in a paradox where they must be complex enough to test realistically but simple enough to save time, making paper prototypes the superior alternative.

posts.summary: Spolsky analyzes the browser landscape in mid-2003, noting Microsoft's decision to stop developing Internet Explorer as a standalone product after winning the browser war. He celebrates Mozilla Firebird as the first browser to genuinely catch up with IE, making it his new default browser. He argues that real competition could force Microsoft to improve IE again, potentially creating a two-browser world. He also dissects the AOL-Microsoft settlement, pointing out the irony that AOL owns Netscape but still depends on IE's component architecture because Gecko is too hard to embed, and that only one employee is working on fixing this problem.
key_points: Microsoft stopped investing in IE after reaching 90% market share, treating the browser war as won
Mozilla Firebird is the first browser in three years of Mozilla releases that genuinely rivals IE in usability and adds compelling new features like tabbed browsing and incremental search
Mozilla and Opera's progress was delayed by ill-advised ground-up rewrites, giving IE time to dominate
AOL paid $4.2 billion for Netscape but can't use Gecko because it lacks IE's easy COM embedding interfaces, making the acquisition nearly useless
Only one Netscape employee is working—in his spare time—on the COM compatibility layer that could make Gecko actually useful to AOL
money_quotes: There has been virtually no work on the IE web browser for a couple of years now and it looks like Microsoft has no interest in spending resources on a battle they already feel they've won.
Meanwhile the Mozilla and Opera teams were getting bogged down in ground-up rewrites, oops, so there was little or no serious competition.
After downloading virtually every Mozilla release over the last three years, this is the first browser I'm actually going to make my default web browser.
Now with a good code base to build upon, Firebird is likely to soar past IE in functionality and performance.
Considering that AOL spent $4.2 billion to buy Netscape, you'd think somebody would have noticed that they already have a browser component.

The Internet Explorer component is so much easier to embed in applications than Gecko that it probably comes down to the programmers on the AOL client team who just don't want to undergo the pain to embed Gecko.
This poor sod Adam Lock is working in his spare time to save all your jobs. Wake up.
posts.key_insight: When a monopolist stops innovating after winning, the opening for competitors is real—but only if those competitors avoid architectural mistakes like ground-up rewrites and actually solve the practical integration problems that keep users locked in.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky reviews VMware 4.0's new snapshot feature, which allows instant restoration of complete virtual machine states including RAM and disk. He praises the time savings for configuration testing but complains about CMOS changes that broke backward compatibility with existing VMs. The post then pivots to a forward-looking idea: running production servers inside virtual machines. Joel outlines five implications of server virtualization, including instant rollback from security breaches, service isolation without extra hardware, and near-zero-downtime OS upgrades, while acknowledging the CPU overhead cost. He predicts that running servers as VMs will become standard practice for system administrators.
key_points: VMware 4.0's snapshot feature eliminates boot time by storing complete machine state including RAM, making configuration testing significantly faster
Breaking backward compatibility with existing VMs through CMOS changes caused painful Plug 'n' Play rediscovery, especially for non-English OS installations
Server virtualization enables instant rollback from security compromises to a known-good state, though frequently-changing state must be stored separately
Virtual machines allow near-zero-downtime OS upgrades by preparing patched copies offline and swapping them in, with zero risk of failed boots
Joel predicts server virtualization will become standard practice for all but the most CPU-intensive applications
money_quotes: Instead of having a blank Win 98 machine ready to boot up at any time, I have a blank Win 98 machine that is already booted and logged on, ready to restore at any time.

I gave up, throwing away my Win 98 OSes and recreating them from scratch, a serious annoyance (especially since I have VMs running in languages which I don't understand, so I can't really tell what they're yelling at me about.)
If anyone hacks into the machine, installing trapdoors or defacing the website, a single click gets us back to a known-good condition.
You can split up functions among different VMs without buying more hardware. Isolate your mail server from your web server from your DNS server, all on one machine.
You can replace the whole operating system on a live server with only seconds of down time, zero risk that the new OS won't come up, and only one physical box.
Everything runs emulated, so you're paying for all this convenience with a lot of CPU cycles.
I think it's going to be an increasingly standard policy of good system administrators to build servers as VMs for all but the most CPU-intensive applications.
posts.key_insight: Server virtualization enables snapshot-based security rollback, service isolation, and near-zero-downtime OS upgrades, making it an inevitable standard for server administration despite the CPU overhead.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky argues that venture capital is fundamentally misaligned with the goals of most software entrepreneurs. VCs seek high-risk, high-reward moonshots because they diversify across many investments, while founders prefer higher-probability moderate success since they can't diversify their one company. He explains that VC-funded companies are pressured to grow faster than is healthy, causing imbalances between revenue, hiring, PR, and code quality. Additionally, VCs' filtering process and increasingly harsh deal terms (exploding offers, preferential shares, board control) systematically drive away the smartest founders, leaving VCs with a shrinking pool of desperate or naive companies unlikely to contain the next big success.
key_points: VCs and founders have fundamentally misaligned risk preferences: VCs want low-probability massive returns across a portfolio, while founders want high-probability moderate returns on their single company
Healthy company growth requires keeping revenue, hiring, PR, and code quality roughly in sync — VC pressure to accelerate growth throws these curves out of balance, creating fatal problems
VCs' need to reject 999 out of 1000 pitches creates a whack-a-mole filtering process that optimizes for saying no quickly rather than identifying truly great companies
Increasingly harsh deal terms (exploding offers, preferential shares, founder displacement) systematically repel founders with good business sense, leaving VCs with only desperate or naive companies
The best potential investments are often companies too busy building products and serving customers to play the fundraising game
money_quotes: Founders would prefer reasonable success with high probability, while VCs are looking for fantastic hit-it-out-of-the-ballpark success with low probability.
This is just Econ 101; it's the same reason you buy car insurance and Hertz doesn't.
As an entrepreneur, you're going to be forced at gunpoint to bet on three cherries again and again and again.
They spend all this money before anyone has had a chance to learn what the best way to spend money is.
Nothing sends a stronger message that an offer is uncompetitive than refusing to expose it to competition.
All these tough deals are not really protecting the VCs, they're just restricting the VCs' world of possible investments to dumb companies and desperate companies.
Many excellent entrepreneurs feel that their time is better spent pitching products to customers rather than pitching stock to investors.
It seems to me that a company that accepts an exploding offer is demonstrating a remarkable lack of basic business aptitude.
posts.key_insight: The VC model's misaligned incentives, rushed filtering, and increasingly harsh deal terms create a self-defeating cycle that systematically drives away the best founders and companies, leaving VCs fishing in an ever-shrinking pool unlikely to contain breakthrough successes.

posts.summary: This short news post serves as a teaser for Spolsky's essay on why venture capital is a poor fit for entrepreneurship, along with two technical corrections from a previous post. He corrects his characterization of VMware as an emulator (it's actually a virtual machine monitor that runs most instructions on hardware directly), and provides insider context on AOL's decision to use the Gecko rendering engine in their client, driven by management's fear of customer complaints about page rendering.
key_points: Spolsky argues there are fundamental assumptions in the VC world that make venture capital incompatible with entrepreneurship
VMware uses a virtual machine monitor rather than full emulation, running most guest instructions directly on x86 hardware with only supervisor-level instructions being emulated
AOL's adoption of Mozilla's Gecko engine was a management-driven decision motivated by fear of customer complaints about browser rendering quality
AOL overcorrected from having a notoriously substandard proprietary browser to adopting a competitor's rendering engine
money_quotes: There are certain fundamental assumptions about doing business in the VC world that make venture capital a bad fit with entrepreneurship.
Since it's the entrepreneurs who create the businesses that the VCs fund, this is a major problem.
It's only when the guest does something 'supervisor-ish' that we step in, and emulate an instruction or so at a time.

Given how long AOL went with their own substandard browser that was the joke of the Internet industry, I can understand their sensitivity to this issue, but it seems like they've swung a bit too far in the other direction.
posts.key_insight: Spolsky teases his broader argument that venture capital's fundamental assumptions conflict with entrepreneurship, while demonstrating intellectual honesty by publicly correcting his own technical errors.

posts.summary: This is a brief link roundup post where Joel curates reactions and related commentary from other bloggers on topics related to startups, venture capital, and business agreements. He highlights Joi Ito on no-shop agreements, John Ludwig on VC spending advice, Zimran Ahmed on entrepreneurial overconfidence, and Eric Sink on the limitations of VC firms for building lasting companies.
key_points: No-shop agreements can help both parties focus on building a business when a deal requires significant work before closing
VCs don't typically encourage startups to spend more and faster, contrary to popular belief
Entrepreneurs tend to overestimate the certainty of their 'sure thing' opportunities
Most VC firms aren't well-suited for founders who want to build solid, long-lived companies
money_quotes: I don't think I've ever heard anyone at our firm telling a startup to spend more, faster.
…those 'sure things' that Joel mentions exist more often in the mind of the entrepreneur than in reality.
… most VC firms don't have much to offer people who want to build solid, long-lived companies.
posts.key_insight: A collection of outside perspectives challenging common assumptions about venture capital, startup spending, and entrepreneurial confidence.

posts.summary: This is a brief link roundup post where Joel shares several recommendations. He points readers to Steve Pavlina's business tips for shareware developers, notes a compliment about CityDesk from Steven Den Beste, and recommends Ellen Ullman's novel 'The Bug' about a programmer struggling with an elusive software bug. Joel adds a programmer's Easter egg by noting that the novel's critical code actually contains three bugs, not the one the plot focuses on.
key_points: Steve Pavlina's business tips for shareware and games developers are worth reading
Ellen Ullman's novel 'The Bug' is a highly recommended story about a programmer unraveling over an elusive bug
Joel identifies hidden bugs in the novel's code that real programmers would catch — height and width are swapped and there's an additional off-by-one error
CityDesk receives external praise as unbeatable software
money_quotes: Ellen Ullman's new book The Bug is a terrific novel about a programmer coming unravelled because of a killer bug nicknamed The Jester which seems to only appear at the worst possible times.
There's a bug in the book, too, probably left in as a reward for real programmers.
The critical code has 3 bugs, not 1. Height and Width are swapped, and there's another off-by-one bug in the code that the protagonist doesn't fix.
posts.key_insight: Real programmers can't help but debug code even when it's fictional — Joel spots three bugs in a novel's code where the plot only accounts for one.

posts.summary: This is a minimal job posting announcement for a Support Engineer position at Fog Creek Software. The post contains no substantive argument or analysis, serving purely as a brief hiring notice. The position has since been filled.
key_points: Fog Creek Software was actively hiring for a Support Engineer role in mid-2003
posts.key_insight: This is a trivial job posting with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares two brief observations: a warning against believing media narratives that economic downturns are permanent, and detailed advice on writing effective cover letters. He argues that Fortune magazine's claim that job-finding has permanently changed is the same cyclical nonsense as the 'New Economy' hype of the late 1990s. On cover letters, he explains that generic, template-driven letters signal desperation and laziness, while personalized letters that show genuine interest in the specific company signal that the candidate is a top-tier hire with options.
key_points: Economic cycles are inevitable and centuries-old; media claims that 'this time is different' are always wrong, whether during booms or busts
Generic cover letters following standard career-services advice make candidates indistinguishable and signal mass-application desperation
The best candidates demonstrate they have choices and are applying because of genuine interest in the specific company, not out of desperation
Personalizing a cover letter to show knowledge of the company and dropping hints that you're selectively applying puts you in the top 1% of applicants
95% of cover letters Fog Creek receives show no sign of being customized for the job
money_quotes: The sure sign that things are about to change is when the conventional wisdom becomes, 'things will never change.'
Don't take any of the standard career-services-office advice for writing cover letters, or your cover letter will look exactly like everyone else's cover letter.
95% of the cover letters I receive do not include anything about Fog Creek and show no sign that they have been customized in any way for the job in question.
This sends a signal that you are simply spamming your resume to hundreds of jobs, which, in turn, sends a signal that you are both desperate and not willing to work very hard.

The very best candidates have come to realize that they have a choice of where to work, and when they apply for a job, they are applying because there's something intriguing about that particular job.
If you make your cover letter interesting, make it personal, and drop hints that you have choices in the world, you will sound more like one of the top 1% candidates.
posts.key_insight: The best job candidates signal their quality by showing genuine, specific interest in a company rather than broadcasting desperation through generic applications.

posts.summary: This is an ultra-brief post consisting of a single line linking to Salam Pax's photo blog. Salam Pax was the pseudonymous Baghdad Blogger who gained international fame for blogging from Iraq during the 2003 US invasion. Joel simply flags the existence of Salam Pax's new photo blog as a 'scoop' for his readers. There is no argument or analysis — it's a link-sharing post typical of early 2000s blogging culture.
key_points: Joel shares a link to Salam Pax's photo blog as a brief news item
The post reflects the early blogging era's culture of quick link-sharing and cross-promotion
At just 7 words, this is essentially a micro-post predating Twitter-style brevity
money_quotes: Scoop: Salam Pax has a photo blog.
posts.key_insight: A micro-post from the early blogging era, sharing a link to the famous Baghdad Blogger's photo blog with no further commentary.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post where Joel Spolsky asks his readers whether anyone has experience with the IP*Works software components. There is no argument, analysis, or conclusion — it is simply a question posed to the community for discussion. The post consists of a single sentence directing readers to a Fog Creek discussion forum thread.
key_points: Joel solicits community feedback on the IP*Works component library
The post demonstrates Joel's use of his blog as a community Q&A platform
money_quotes: Does anyone have any experience with the quality of the IP*Works components?
posts.key_insight: This is a minimal community query post with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: Spolsky responds to Jason Fried's praise of an OmniWeb dialog box that asks users about permanent redirects. He argues the dialog is unnecessary UI that interrupts users with a technical decision they shouldn't have to make. Since software designers understand permanent redirects better than users, the browser should handle them silently. He contends that developers too often can't resist showing off features instead of making them invisible.
key_points: Permanent redirects should be handled automatically by browsers without user intervention, since designers understand them better than users do
Modal dialogs that interrupt users to brag about developer features are obnoxious and counterproductive
Great UI design is about removing elements, not adding them — the best features are completely invisible
Software developers have a persistent urge to make their work visible, even when invisibility would better serve the user
money_quotes: It's a nice feature that shouldn't be advertising itself, it should just happen automatically and silently.
Why should users be inflicted with the need to make a complicated decision about something they don't understand as well as the software designers?
Unnecessary UIs like this that pop up to brag about a cool feature the developer implemented are a little bit obnoxious.
Too many software developers just can't bring themselves to implement completely invisible features.
They need to show off about what a great feature they just implemented, even at the cost of confusing people.
Really great UI design disappears. It's a matter of taking away, not adding.
posts.key_insight: The best UI features are completely invisible — developers should resist the urge to surface their cleverness in the interface and instead let good design disappear.

posts.summary: This short post combines two unrelated items: a recommendation of Clay Shirky's essay on group dynamics and online communities, and an announcement that Joel has created an experimental Echo (later Atom) feed using CityDesk. The Shirky quote highlights how online community problems are predictable and recurring, yet builders keep repeating the same mistakes. Joel also positions himself as an early adopter of the Echo syndication format, noting its rapidly evolving specification.
key_points: Clay Shirky's insight that group dynamics problems in online communities are predictable and well-documented, yet repeatedly ignored by new builders
Joel created an early experimental Echo/Atom feed using CityDesk 2.0 beta, positioning himself as a first-mover on the new syndication format
The Echo format was still rapidly changing, reflecting the messy early days of web syndication standards
money_quotes: if you don't accept them upfront, they'll happen to you anyway
And then you'll end up writing one of those documents that says 'Oh, we launched this and we tried it, and then the users came along and did all these weird things.'
Even though you didn't read the thing that was written in 1978.
posts.key_insight: The problems that plague online communities and software projects are well-documented and predictable, yet each generation of builders insists on learning them the hard way.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces several product updates in a brief 'new stuff' post. He highlights SourceGear's Vault 1.1 shipping with FogBUGZ integration, CityDesk 2.0 reaching its fourth beta with improved HTML generation and hundreds of bug fixes, and a new plasma TV for the Fog Creek office. He outlines a release strategy for CityDesk: a polishing release (2.0) focused on fixing bugs for existing users, followed by a major feature release (3.0) later in the year.
key_points: SourceGear's Vault 1.1 is the first source control system with built-in FogBUGZ integration
CityDesk 2.0 is a deliberate 'polishing' release focused on fixing hundreds of bugs before adding major new features in 3.0
The CityDesk WYSIWYG editor now generates valid XHTML and preserves whitespace
Joel advocates fixing all small broken things before creating major new functionality
money_quotes: Our goal was to make CityDesk a much more satisfying product for our current users, people who are happy with 1.0 but could be made even happier with some key enhancements.
We've fixed literally hundreds of bugs and annoyances.
We have a compulsive need to fix all the little tiny broken things before we create major new areas of functionality.
After a few minutes I was impressed, after a few days I am in love with it.
But the Playstation is Not Tax Deductible.
posts.key_insight: A polishing release that fixes hundreds of small bugs and annoyances should precede major new feature work, because getting the foundation right makes existing users happier and creates a stronger base for future development.

posts.summary: Spolsky critiques Munich's decision to replace Windows with Linux on 14,000 desktop computers, arguing that while it may work for basic tasks, it will fail for the many employees who depend on Windows-only software. He points out that Munich uses 175 custom Windows applications that would all need porting, plus commercial software with no Linux equivalent. He predicts that employees will quietly buy Windows at full retail price to get their jobs done, ultimately costing Munich more than a negotiated Microsoft deal would have.
key_points: Munich's 175 custom Windows applications represent a massive hidden migration cost that undermines the economic case for switching to Linux
Desktop OS migrations fail when they ignore the long tail of specialized software that individual workers depend on
Politically motivated technology decisions often result in paying twice: once for the mandated solution and once for the workaround that actually gets the job done
Bureaucratic organizations are particularly bad at surfacing migration failures because employees absorb dysfunction silently rather than reporting it
money_quotes: For a secretary who has to do email and type simple documents, that will probably be fine.
What about all the people who need to run software that doesn't run on Linux?
I guarantee you that somewhere out of those 14,000 desktops there's someone using Quark to publish an important report.

Every mid-level bureaucrat will realize they can't do their job because some application they need just doesn't run on Linux, and they'll buy Windows XP at full retail price, burying the costs in expense reports or petty cash or somewhere else.
Munich will start to realize that they're paying twice for software: once for the politically correct shelfware and once for the software they need to get their jobs done.
Either that or they'll simply be unable to do their jobs, and, like bureaucrats everywhere, they won't tell anyone, while the municipality quietly falls apart.
posts.key_insight: Large-scale OS migrations driven by political rather than practical considerations fail because they ignore the long tail of specialized software dependencies, ultimately costing more than the system they replaced.

posts.summary: Spolsky follows up on his previous post about Munich's decision to adopt Linux, noting that reader research revealed the plan actually involves running VMWare with licensed Windows on up to 80% of machines, with most costs going to training and consulting rather than software licenses. He highlights Gartner's analysis that Munich's choice doesn't prove Linux is ready for general desktop use. He also draws an analogy to 1980s telephone deregulation, arguing the real benefit is competitive pressure on Microsoft's pricing, and briefly notes a Slate article about Google's shortcomings in finding unbiased product reviews.
key_points: Munich's Linux migration actually involves running VMWare with licensed Windows on potentially 80% of machines, undermining the narrative of a clean Linux switch
The majority of Munich's migration costs were for training and consulting, not software licenses, suggesting the cost comparison is more nuanced than headlines implied
Competition from Linux deals benefits everyone by pressuring Microsoft on pricing and license terms, similar to how Sprint and MCI forced AT&T to lower prices after deregulation
Google has significant shortcomings in surfacing unbiased professional product reviews despite them existing somewhere on the web
money_quotes: It seems like the answer to my sweet and innocent question about backwards compatibility is that they're actually going to install Linux, but on many machines (as many as 80%?) there will be VMWare running a licensed copy of Windows inside.
Also the majority of the price tag was for training and consulting, not the software licenses per se.

Nobody outside of Redmond is going to be weeping much about the fact that deals like this can put pressure on Microsoft to be more competitive about pricing and more responsive to customers on license terms.
Nobody wanted to use Sprint or MCI, because their service was terrible, but we were sure thankful that reliable old AT&T had to lower their prices in response.
It is, indeed, practically impossible to find unbiased professional reviews of consumer products using Google, although I know they must be out there somewhere.
posts.key_insight: The real value of high-profile Linux adoption deals like Munich's isn't replacing Windows but creating competitive pressure that forces Microsoft to offer better pricing and license terms.

posts.summary: Joel highlights Mailinator, a clever service by Paul Tyma that provides instant disposable email addresses. The service requires no signup — you simply send email to any address @mailinator.com and then check it. Joel uses this as an example of great UI design through simplification, where removing steps (registration, passwords, configuration) creates a better user experience.
key_points: Mailinator provides disposable email addresses with zero setup or registration required
The service eliminates every traditional step of email account creation — no forms, no passwords, no verification
Great UI design is achieved by removing complexity rather than adding features
money_quotes: More proof that great UI design is done by taking away, not adding things.
posts.key_insight: The best user experience design removes steps and complexity rather than adding features and options.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief blog post consisting of three short announcements about Fog Creek Software. Joel mentions a summer intern working on an ASP to PHP compiler, introduces a new support contact for FogBugz, and shows an architect at a construction site for a new office. There is no substantive argument or analysis; it reads as a quick company update or link post.
key_points: Fog Creek had summer interns working on technical projects like an ASP to PHP compiler
FogBugz support was staffed with a dedicated contact person
Fog Creek was building or renovating a new office space
posts.key_insight: This post is a brief company update with no substantive insight; it simply documents day-to-day happenings at Fog Creek Software.

posts.summary: This brief post serves as a double announcement. Spolsky teases his foreword to Rick Chapman's book 'In Search of Stupidity,' which explores the tension between technical and business people in tech companies. He also announces the CityDesk 2.0 release candidate, noting its stability after four rounds of beta testing and signaling a gold release within weeks.
key_points: There is an inherent tension between 'geeks' and 'suits' in every high-tech company
CityDesk 2.0 has reached release candidate status after four rounds of beta testing
Iterative beta testing with real users produces stable software releases
money_quotes: In every high tech company I've known, there's a war going on, between the geeks and the suits.
posts.key_insight: The fundamental tension between technical and business perspectives is a universal dynamic in technology companies.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky reviews Rick Chapman's book 'In Search of Stupidity' by examining why Microsoft dominated the PC software industry while every other top-ten company from 1984 disappeared. He argues that Microsoft's dominance wasn't due to superior marketing or illegal monopoly, but rather that Microsoft was the only company that never made a fatal strategic blunder. Spolsky catalogs the self-inflicted wounds of competitors — Micropro, Lotus, Digital Research, VisiCorp, and Ashton-Tate — showing how non-technical leadership led to catastrophic decisions. He concedes that programmers can make equally destructive mistakes, citing Netscape's ground-up rewrite. His conclusion is that successful software companies need leaders who deeply understand both programming and business.
key_points: Between 1984 and 2001, every company in the top ten PC software publishers except Microsoft disappeared, and Microsoft grew to represent 69% of the top 100's combined revenue.
Microsoft's dominance came not from superior marketing but from being the only company that avoided making fatal strategic mistakes.

Most catastrophic software company failures stemmed from non-technical executives who didn't understand basic technology realities, like John Sculley not knowing handwriting recognition was infeasible.
Programmers can be equally destructive — Netscape's programmer-driven decision to rewrite their browser from scratch cost them the browser market.
Successful software companies require leaders with deep understanding of both programming and business; strength in only one dimension leads to fatal blunders.
money_quotes: Microsoft was the only company on the list that never made a fatal, stupid mistake.
Adobe would double in revenues if they could just get Microsoft's soda pop budget.
The personal computer software market is Microsoft.
No software company can succeed unless there is a programmer at the helm.

Such programmers should be admired for their love of clean code, but they shouldn't be allowed within 100 feet of any business decisions, since it's obvious that clean code is more important to them than shipping, uh, software.
For every other software company that once had market leadership and saw it go down the drain, you can point to one or two giant blunders that steered the boat into an iceberg.
If you want to be successful in the software business, you have to have a management team that thoroughly understands and loves programming, but they have to understand and love business, too.
posts.key_insight: Software companies don't fail because competitors outperform them — they fail because their own leadership makes fatal strategic blunders, usually rooted in not understanding technology or not understanding business.

posts.summary: This short post serves two purposes: announcing a Joel on Software reader dinner in Oslo, Norway, and highlighting the dangers of corporate relocations. Joel draws parallels between Blockbuster's move from Florida to Texas and Boeing's relocation of engineering jobs from California to Texas, noting how both moves resulted in massive loss of institutional knowledge when most employees refused to relocate.
key_points: Corporate relocations beyond a couple of miles cause significant employee attrition and loss of institutional knowledge
Blockbuster's relocation led to years of repeated mistakes as new employees lacked historical context of what had already been tried and failed
Boeing's relocation saw 80% of California engineers refuse to move, with investigators examining whether the resulting knowledge loss contributed to the Columbia disaster
money_quotes: When you relocate more than a couple of miles, some employees' lives would be too disrupted to make the move, so you lose a lot of employees, and all the institutional knowledge, skill, and experience that comes with those employees.
For years and years the business press watched agog as Blockbuster made mistake after inexcusable mistake, re-trying all kinds of ideas that had failed only two years earlier.
About 80% of the California engineers refused to relocate, forcing the company to hire workers in Texas and forfeit much of the experience of its California engineering base.
posts.key_insight: Corporate relocations destroy institutional knowledge because most employees won't move, leaving the company to repeat past mistakes with an inexperienced workforce.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky posts a brief vacation notice announcing he'll be away for a couple of weeks. He teases a mystery icon discussion on the Fog Creek forums. He announces that CityDesk 2.0 will be released immediately upon his return. The bulk of the post highlights a pricing loophole where customers can buy the cheap 1.0 Home Edition and receive a free upgrade to the much more expensive 2.0 Pro Edition, saving $270.
key_points: CityDesk 2.0 is ready to ship and will be released as soon as Joel returns from vacation
A pricing loophole exists: buying CityDesk 1.0 Home for $79 gets you a free upgrade to 2.0 Pro, saving $270
The loophole is intentional — Fog Creek publicly promised free upgrades from any 1.0 edition to 2.0 Pro
money_quotes: There's a loophole the size of a truck going on right now with CityDesk pricing.
So basically while I'm looking at fjords and glaciers, you can buy the 1.0 home edition for $79 and get the 2.0 pro edition free when I get home, saving $270.
posts.key_insight: Spolsky uses his vacation absence as a playful sales tactic, framing a generous upgrade promise as a time-limited pricing loophole to drive last-minute CityDesk 1.0 purchases before the 2.0 launch.

posts.summary: Joel returns from vacation in England and Norway and recommends Petter Hesselberg's book 'Programming Industrial Strength Windows.' He argues it's essential reading for developers building commercial Windows software, as it covers the many small details that collectively determine whether software feels polished or clunky.
key_points: Commercial software quality comes from getting hundreds of small details right, not just a few big things
Each individual polish detail may only affect a small percentage of users, but collectively they define the product's quality
The difference between 'Lexus quality' and 'Yugo clunkitude' is attention to nitty-gritty production concerns
money_quotes: the kinds of things that, individually, only affect a small percentage of people but taken together spell the difference between super solid Lexus quality production code that delights its users, and Yugo clunkitude
posts.key_insight: Software quality is the cumulative result of getting countless small details right, each of which individually seems minor but together define whether a product delights or disappoints.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky reports that during the major 2003 Northeast blackout, his website and company Fog Creek remained online thanks to their colocation provider Peer 1 Network. While many hosting competitors went down, Peer 1 maintained 100% uptime on backup generators. Joel notes that emergencies are when people first discover flaws in their backup plans, and praises Peer 1 for their reliability.
key_points: Emergency backup plans often fail when first tested under real conditions, revealing overlooked dependencies
Peer 1 Network maintained 100% uptime during the 2003 Northeast blackout while competitors failed
Fog Creek continued selling software throughout the blackout thanks to reliable hosting infrastructure
Choosing reliable infrastructure partners matters most during unexpected crises
money_quotes: A once-in-a-generation blackout is the first time most people discover the problems with their emergency backup plans.
Oh, look, we're out of diesel fuel. Or: I didn't know our generator needs electricity to turn on!
We even sold software while the electricity was out.
posts.key_insight: Disaster recovery plans are only as good as their real-world testing, and choosing reliable infrastructure partners pays off when crises actually hit.

posts.summary: Spolsky highlights Raymond Chen's blog series about Windows API history and its hidden complexities. He focuses on the GetWindowText function as a case study in how seemingly simple APIs harbor enormous internal complexity that documentation struggles to convey. Spolsky connects this to his own concept of leaky abstractions, arguing that such hidden complexity is precisely why projects like WINE face near-impossible challenges in achieving full API compatibility. The post also includes an unrelated request for printing recommendations.
key_points: Raymond Chen's Windows history blog reveals fascinating quirks behind everyday Windows behaviors like the Start button and time zone maps
The GetWindowText function exemplifies how a 'simple' API call conceals pages worth of complex behavior that documentation oversimplifies
API emulation layers like WINE face enormous difficulty because achieving 100% compatibility requires replicating undocumented or poorly documented internal behaviors
Leaky abstractions are inevitable when simple interfaces hide complex underlying mechanics
money_quotes: The complexity behind such a simple function is a classic example of an abstraction leaking.

Getting 100% compatibility means emulating all these bizarre internal complexities perfectly, even when they're not completely documented or the documentation doesn't really describe what happens in every scenario.
The documentation tries to explain its complexity with small words, which is great if you don't understand long words, but it also means that you're not getting the full story.
