Meanwhile, web applications reached 'good enough' UI quality, eliminating the need for Windows-specific development. The result is that the new dominant API is HTML, and Microsoft's lock-in through its platform has been broken by the web, not by a competing desktop OS.
key_points: Microsoft's true monopoly power came not from Windows itself but from the Windows API — the vast set of functions developers built against, creating a self-reinforcing ecosystem of applications that kept users on Windows.

Microsoft internally shifted from the 'Raymond Chen Camp' (backwards compatibility at all costs) to the 'MSDN Magazine Camp' (constant reinvention), breaking faith with developers by making VB.NET incompatible with VB6 and planning yet another API replacement with Avalon/WinFX.

The real productivity revolution in programming was automatic memory management, not object-oriented programming — and .NET delivered this, but at the cost of forcing developers to abandon their existing codebases.

Web applications reached approximately 80-95% of rich client UI quality, which proved 'good enough' for most users and developers, making Windows-specific development unnecessary for new applications.
The PC market's saturation meant Microsoft could no longer rely on hardware upgrade cycles to force adoption of new platforms — the strategy that had displaced WordPerfect and Lotus no longer worked.
money_quotes: The goose that lays the golden eggs is not quite dead, but it does have a terminal disease, one that nobody noticed yet.
When you try to unify two opposing forces by creating a third alternative, you just end up with three opposing forces. You haven't unified anything and you haven't really fixed anything.
The real significant productivity advance we've had in programming has been from languages which manage memory for you automatically.
No developer with a day job has time to keep up with all the new development tools coming out of Redmond, if only because there are too many dang employees at Microsoft making development tools!
Venture Capitalists won't invest in Windows applications because they're so afraid of competition from Microsoft.
The new API is HTML, and the new winners in the application development marketplace will be the people who can make HTML sing.
If any application failed to run on Windows 95, I took it as a personal failure. I spent many sleepless nights fixing bugs in third-party programs just so they could keep running on Windows 95.
WinFS, advertised as a way to make searching work by making the file system be a relational database, ignores the fact that the real way to make searching work is by making searching work.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft lost its API dominance not to a competing desktop platform but to the web, accelerated by its own abandonment of backwards compatibility that drove developers away from Windows-specific development.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky enthusiastically recommends Firefox 0.9 as a replacement for Internet Explorer. He argues that Firefox offers superior security through popup blocking, better usability through tabbed browsing, and represents a chance to break Microsoft's browser monopoly. He acknowledges Microsoft won the browser market fairly by making a better product, but criticizes them for stagnating IE development out of fear that web applications would undermine Windows.
key_points: Firefox offers practical security advantages over IE, including popup blocking and resistance to spyware installation prompts
Tabbed browsing represents a genuine usability improvement that keeps users after they initially switch for security
Microsoft deliberately stagnated IE development because web-based applications threatened the Windows platform's relevance
Competition in browsers could eventually make web-based applications competitive with native Windows applications
money_quotes: I switched for the popup blocking but I stayed for the tabbed browsing.

Microsoft took over the browser market fair and square by making a better product, but they were so afraid that Web-based applications would eliminate the need for Windows that they locked the IE team in a dark dungeon and they haven't allowed improvements to IE for several years now.
Now Firefox is the better product and there's a glimmer of hope that one day DHTML will actually improve to the point where web-based applications are just as good as Windows-based applications.
You'll get fewer viruses and you'll get no annoying popups asking you if you want to install lame spyware that will ruin your computer forcing a complete reinstall.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's fear of web applications replacing Windows led them to deliberately stagnate browser innovation, creating an opening for Firefox to become the better product.

posts.summary: This is a very brief teaser post that serves as a promotional link to Joel Spolsky's longer essay 'How Microsoft Lost the API War.' The post consists of a single provocative quote from the essay describing two opposing forces within Microsoft, framed as 'The Raymond Chen Camp' and 'The MSDN Magazine Camp.' It functions as a hook to drive readers to the full article rather than presenting a standalone argument.
key_points: The post is a teaser linking to the full essay 'How Microsoft Lost the API War'
It highlights internal tension at Microsoft between two philosophical camps
Raymond Chen represents backward compatibility and developer trust, while MSDN Magazine represents the push toward new, breaking technologies
money_quotes: There are two opposing forces inside Microsoft, which I will refer to, somewhat tongue-in-cheek, as The Raymond Chen Camp and The MSDN Magazine Camp.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's internal tension between backward compatibility advocates and new-technology evangelists shaped its strategic trajectory in the API wars.

posts.summary: Spolsky calls on web application developers to start demanding browser features that support rich application development, now that graphic designers have gotten their CSS wish list fulfilled. He outlines a detailed list of features he wants to see in browsers, including inline editing, fast asynchronous server queries, standard UI controls, compiled JavaScript, and richer event handling. He explicitly rejects proprietary plugin-based solutions like Java Applets and Macromedia, insisting that improvements must be deeply integrated with DHTML and CSS. He also demands graceful degradation for legacy browsers and dismisses schemes requiring mass user adoption of new plugins. The post is remarkably prescient, essentially describing what would become AJAX, HTML5, and modern web APIs years before they materialized. Spolsky frames this as the application developers' turn to shape the web platform after designers had their era.
key_points: Web platform evolution has served designers (CSS) but neglected application developers who need richer interaction primitives
Browsers need fast asynchronous server communication to enable responsive UIs — essentially describing AJAX before the term existed
Standard UI controls (menus, toolbars, tree views, dialogs) should be built into browsers rather than reimplemented inconsistently by every site
Solutions must integrate with the open web stack (HTML/CSS/JS) rather than relying on proprietary plugins like Flash or Java Applets
Browser development stagnation in the late 90s — caused by Netscape's rewrite and Microsoft's neglect of IE — delayed these capabilities
money_quotes: It's time for us application developers to start clamoring for the features we need to develop great web applications.

It should be possible to have a 300,000 employee directory on the server and create a web app that has a list box where you can type the first few letters of an employee's name and see a filtered list as fast as you can type on the screen.
I want this stuff integrated with DHTML and CSS, deeply in the fabric of the web.
These things would have happened if browser development hadn't ground to a halt in the late 90s due to the misguided Netscape-rewrite-project and the lock-IE-developers-in-a-dungeon project.
Boil the ocean schemes that require 400,000,000 users to install some thingamajig before you get anything useful. Such schemes will not go anywhere.
It's time to make Microsoft play catchup again. Fire and Motion Baby.
It's time for application developers to start clamoring for the next generation of the Web now that the graphic designers got their wish list taken care of.
posts.key_insight: The web platform needed to evolve from a document-presentation medium into a rich application platform through native browser capabilities, not proprietary plugins — a vision that anticipated AJAX, HTML5, and modern web development by several years.

posts.summary: Joel describes his sysadmin week spent upgrading infrastructure for Joel on Software. He visited Peer 1 Networks' colocation facility to install a hot backup server alongside the main one, which had been running for a year and a half with only Windows Update-related downtime. He plans to set up replication using robocopy and SQL log shipping for near-zero-downtime failover. The post also notes Peer 1's rapid growth from 20% to 90% capacity in their data center.
key_points: Windows Update reboots were the primary source of server downtime, and sometimes caused the server to fail to come back up properly
Dell's remote management hardware (RAC) crashed more often than the server itself, defeating its purpose as a remote recovery tool
Joel's backup strategy uses simple, proven tools (robocopy for files, SQL log shipping for databases) rather than complex solutions
Peer 1 Networks grew from 20% to 90% data center capacity in roughly a year, reflecting the mid-2000s hosting boom
money_quotes: Sometimes the server didn't come back up properly after one of the reboots required for patching Windows, so we installed a remote controlled power strip, which has a web interface allowing us to power cycle the server.

There's supposed to be such a thing built into the server itself, something Dell makes called RAC, but it crashes more often than the server, requiring a full power cycle to get it back to life, which defeats the purpose…
Don't get me started.
I couldn't be happier with their hosting services and they're the nicest people, so even though I'm completely tainted since they host my site for free, I most heartily recommend them.
A nice problem to have.
posts.key_insight: Practical server administration often relies on simple, unglamorous solutions like remote power strips and file-copy replication when the sophisticated tools designed for the job prove unreliable.

posts.summary: This short link roundup post from mid-2004 curates several conversations happening across the web about the future of web applications and browser standards. Spolsky highlights voices calling for incremental improvement of web standards with IE compatibility, early AJAX-like frameworks, and the tension between rich client experiences and web-based apps. The post captures a pivotal moment just before the AJAX revolution, when developers were actively debating how to make web apps feel like desktop software. It closes with a humorous note about extending SysAdmin Week to a fortnight.
key_points: Multiple developers and browser vendors were converging on the idea that web standards should evolve incrementally with backward compatibility for IE
Early AJAX precursors like General Interface and phplive were already implementing rich client-like experiences in the browser through DHTML and server-side RPC calls
The web development job market was recovering from the dot-com bust, with growing demand for web application developers
Opera's position that any new web framework must be based on HTML and implementable in IE6 without plugins foreshadowed the direction the web actually took
money_quotes: The best way to help the Web is to incrementally improve the existing web standards, with compatibility shims provided for IE, so that web content authors can actually deploy new formats interoperably.
The recession is over, the slump is ended. Web development is in demand, and the demand is only going to increase.

Our own position was that any successful framework would have to be backwards compatible with the existing Web content, and would have to be largely implementable in Windows IE6 without using binary plug-ins.

It's event driven programming for the web – but the whole page isn't refreshed – whenever you click a button, focus an element, or fire any event that has a handler on the server – an RPC call is dispatched to the server.
I just wanted to announce that SysAdmin Week will hence be known as 'SysAdmin Fortnight.'
posts.key_insight: In mid-2004, the web development community was converging on the idea that rich web applications needed to be built incrementally on existing HTML standards with IE compatibility—laying the intellectual groundwork for the AJAX revolution.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces that his second book is heading to the printer. The book is primarily a collection of reprinted articles from his blog over the previous four years. He humorously lists three advantages of the physical book format over reading online: professional editing, not having to read on a computer, and the ability to physically throw it at coworkers to make a point.
key_points: Spolsky's second book compiles four years of blog articles into printed form
Professional editing improved grammar and style issues present in the original online versions
Physical books retain persuasive advantages over URLs, including the ability to be hurled at someone
money_quotes: You can hurl it at your boss or coworkers to make a point, and the impact will be much more powerful than emailing a URL.
I have finally learned to use semicolons instead of commas to separate complete sentences, and somebody smarter than me has sorted out all the "whiches" and the "thats."
posts.key_insight: A curated, edited physical book still offers tangible advantages over online content, not least the rhetorical force of being able to throw it at someone.

posts.summary: Spolsky criticizes Microsoft's acquisition of Lookout, a popular Outlook search plugin that solved one of Outlook's biggest weaknesses. After the acquisition, Microsoft pulled Lookout from download and announced its technology would be folded into MSN search services rather than improving Outlook. Spolsky questions why Microsoft would buy a product that fixes their email client's search and then kill it without integrating it. He invokes Hanlon's Razor to suggest stupidity over malice. An update reveals the likely explanation: Lookout used an open-source search component Microsoft couldn't redistribute, making it essentially an 'HR acquisition' to hire the developers.
key_points: Outlook's search was so slow it was essentially useless, creating an opportunity for third-party plugins like Lookout
Microsoft acquired Lookout only to discontinue it and redirect the technology toward MSN search rather than fixing Outlook
The acquisition likely had nothing to do with the product itself — Lookout's core search engine was open-source and couldn't be redistributed by Microsoft
The real explanation was probably an 'HR acquisition' where Microsoft bought the company just to hire the people behind it
money_quotes: Searching is not a hard problem, and a lot of plug-ins sprang up to solve this problem.
It just works. Searching five years of accumulated email takes less than a second.
Could Microsoft have possibly bought Lookout just to shut them down?
Even at my most paranoid, I can't for the life of me figure out why Microsoft wants searching in Outlook to be worthless.
Never attribute to malice that which is adequately explained by stupidity.
Methinks this is one of those 'HR Acquisitions,' wherein Microsoft buys a company for a few bucks because it's the only way to hire someone they want.
posts.key_insight: Large companies sometimes acquire small products not for the technology but for the talent, leaving users of the original product worse off as a side effect of corporate hiring strategy.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief news blurb noting that Microsoft featured Lookout (a search tool) on their platform, with a quick correction that it was actually on MSN Sandbox rather than the Download Center. The post captures a minor industry moment with minimal commentary.
key_points: Microsoft promoted the Lookout search tool on MSN Sandbox
Joel initially reported the wrong Microsoft property and issued a quick correction
money_quotes: Scurry, scurry.
posts.key_insight: A trivial news snippet about Microsoft's promotion of the Lookout tool, too brief to convey a substantive argument.

posts.summary: This is a brief news post with two unrelated items. Joel announces a community dinner meetup in Rome for August 2004, continuing a tradition of reader gatherings in various cities. He then shares a quote from RFC 3117 about how Postel's robustness principle paradoxically causes deployment problems, since liberal acceptance of errors masks bugs in new implementations until they encounter stricter systems.
key_points: Joel organized informal community dinners with readers in cities around the world, building an in-person community around his blog
Postel's robustness principle ('be conservative in what you send, liberal in what you accept') can backfire by hiding implementation errors during initial deployment
Tolerant systems create a false sense of correctness that only breaks down when less-liberal implementations are encountered later
money_quotes: Counter-intuitively, Postel's robustness principle ('be conservative in what you send, liberal in what you accept') often leads to deployment problems.

When a new implementation is initially fielded, it is likely that it will encounter only a subset of existing implementations. If those implementations follow the robustness principle, then errors in the new implementation will likely go undetected.
The reader should be able to figure out what happens next.
posts.key_insight: Being too liberal in accepting malformed input paradoxically causes worse interoperability problems down the line by masking bugs during initial deployment.

posts.summary: This is a brief news post where Joel Spolsky shares a feature about Fog Creek Software published on SoftwareCEO.com. The external article highlights Spolsky's contrarian approach to running a software company, noting that he challenges conventional wisdom in the industry. Joel quotes the flattering description and urges readers to check it out before it goes behind a paywall.
key_points: Fog Creek Software was featured on SoftwareCEO.com's home page
The feature highlights Spolsky's skepticism toward received wisdom about running software companies
The article characterizes his views as 'refreshing and thought-provoking' but notes they work in his niche
money_quotes: Spolsky takes a skeptical view of almost every bit of received wisdom he's ever heard about running a software company.
His views are refreshing and thought-provoking, and they certainly work in his niche.
posts.key_insight: A brief self-promotional post sharing external media coverage that frames Spolsky as a contrarian voice in software company management.

posts.summary: In this brief link post, Joel Spolsky endorses Eric Sink's argument that small independent software vendors should focus on hiring solid professionals rather than chasing 'great hackers.' He also recommends Johanna Rothman's upcoming book on hiring technical talent, noting it covers an underserved topic. The post reflects Joel's pragmatic approach to building software teams.
key_points: Small ISVs are better served by hiring reliable professionals than by seeking out legendary 'great hackers'
The topic of hiring technical talent is underserved in industry literature
Joel endorses practical, grounded approaches to team building over hero-worship of elite programmers
money_quotes: It's okay to be in awe of these great hackers. But as a practical matter, small ISVs would be much better off hiring professionals.
posts.key_insight: Small software companies should prioritize hiring dependable professionals over chasing rare 'great hackers.'

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces his annual family vacation to Umbria, Italy and organizes an informal dinner meetup in Rome for readers passing through on August 16th, 2004. He provides logistics for meeting at the Aleph Hotel lobby at 8 PM, noting the challenge of finding open restaurants in Rome during August. The post is a brief, casual announcement typical of early blogging culture, where popular bloggers would organize impromptu reader meetups while traveling.
key_points: Joel organizes an informal reader meetup in Rome during his family vacation to Italy
The meetup reflects the strong community around Joel on Software in the early 2000s blogging era
The casual tone and brevity show how blogs served as personal communication channels with readers
money_quotes: At 8 PM when we see how many people show up, we'll find a good restaurant that's open (not easy in August in Rome, I'm told) and talk about software (and food (and the heat)).
posts.key_insight: A brief travel announcement and reader meetup notice with minimal substantive content about software or technology.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the publication of his second book, a 'best of' collection of 45 articles from his Joel on Software blog. He humorously describes the book's absurdly long official title and pitches it as useful for winning arguments with colleagues by physical force. He notes some misplaced Amazon reviews and asks readers to write accurate ones. The post includes brief postscripts about the book hitting #1 on Amazon in Computers, a reader dinner in Rome, and an unrelated observation about manual vs. automatic transmissions.
key_points: The book is a curated 'best of' collection from the Joel on Software archive, with 45 articles chosen for timelessness plus minor updates and postscripts
Spolsky emphasizes accessibility and affordability, keeping the price under $17 and positioning it as a physical complement to the free website
He leverages his reader community to fix an Amazon review problem where unrelated Oracle book reviews appeared on his listing
The post exemplifies Spolsky's signature style of mixing substantive announcements with humor and random tangential observations
money_quotes: The biggest advantage of the book is that when you throw it at your colleague's head after a very frustrating argument about whether to throw away all your code and start over from scratch, it makes more of an impact than a URL.
So buy several copies and keep them handy for winning arguments at work.
For some reason there are already four reviews of this book up at Amazon.com which don't make any sense; they look like reviews of an Oracle book I'd like to read.

The only case I know of where a manual transmission beats a good automatic transmission is when you're driving on the highway, and you know that sometime soon you're going to have to pass somebody, so you downshift to third gear to get ready to accelerate.
An automatic transmission can't read your mind, so it stays in 4th or 5th, and has to downshift when you floor the accelerator, thus creating a temporary hesitation.
posts.key_insight: Spolsky treats his book launch as an extension of his community-driven blog, using humor and reader engagement rather than conventional marketing to promote a curated collection of his most enduring software development writing.

posts.summary: Spolsky recommends 'The Curious Incident of the Dog in the Night-time' by Mark Haddon, a novel narrated by a fifteen-year-old boy with Asperger's Syndrome. He draws a connection between the character's hyper-logical thinking and the mindset common among programmers and mathematicians. The post uses excerpts from the book to illustrate how extreme literalism and logical reasoning can make everyday human interactions bewildering. Spolsky finds the book both funny and insightful, and warns readers they won't be able to put it down.
key_points: Many people who work with programmers or mathematicians recognize traits of Asperger's Syndrome in their colleagues, including hyper-logical thinking and difficulty reading social cues.
The novel captures the programmer mindset of being 'logical beyond reason' — where rigorous deduction is applied to situations that call for intuition or social awareness.
The book's humor comes from the gap between Christopher's perfectly valid logical reasoning and the social norms he cannot grasp.
Spolsky implicitly suggests the book offers insight into a cognitive style that is common in the tech industry.
money_quotes: Almost everyone who has worked with programmers or mathematicians knows someone with at least a light form of Asperger's Syndrome.
It's funny, but it's also logical, in the irritating way that so many programmers are logical beyond reason.

Christopher numbers the chapters with prime numbers, and can't resist including a mathematical proof as an appendix, but he doesn't know when people are angry with him and hates being touched so much his parents can't hug him.
I must warn you not to start reading it before you go to sleep because nobody I know has been able to put it down without reading through to the end.
Incredibly logical thought processes that make math easy but human relations darn near impossible.
posts.key_insight: The hyper-logical thinking that makes programmers effective at their work is the same trait that can make ordinary human interaction bewildering, and good fiction can illuminate that tension.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky responds to a Microsoft employee who took his 'How Microsoft Lost the API War' article to heart, agreeing that Microsoft's culture of constant paradigm shifts hurts developers. Dare Obasanjo criticized Microsoft's tendency to introduce radically new technologies rather than improving existing ones, echoing Joel's concerns. Joel then makes a candid confession: he delayed publishing his original article because he feared Microsoft might actually listen to his advice. He admits that while as a developer he'd prefer Microsoft to maintain backward compatibility, as a competitor he benefits from Microsoft's self-destructive tendencies.
key_points: Microsoft's internal culture prioritizes creating revolutionary new technologies over improving existing ones that customers already rely on
Forcing developers to rewrite code for each new paradigm shift is a recurring Microsoft problem that frustrates customers
Joel deliberately delayed publishing constructive criticism of Microsoft because he strategically benefits from their mistakes as a competitor
There's an inherent tension between wanting a better ecosystem as a developer and wanting competitors to remain dysfunctional as a business owner
money_quotes: The reason it took me so long to write this article is that I was afraid Microsoft would actually listen to me, even in some small degree.
As a developer, I would much prefer if the Raymond Chen camp won — it sure makes my life easier — but as a competitor to Microsoft, I have to assume that the stupider Microsoft is, the better.
I hate the fact that 'you have to rewrite a lot of your code' is a common answer to questions a customer might ask about how to leverage new or upcoming functionality in a developer technology.
The Microsoft culture is about creating the newest, latest greatest thing that 'changes the world' not improving what is already out there and working for customers.
posts.key_insight: Giving good advice to your competitors creates a strategic dilemma: their improvement as a platform helps you as a developer but hurts you as a business.

posts.summary: Spolsky highlights that Microsoft followed the approach he predicted months earlier, making Avalon available as standalone libraries for existing Windows versions rather than requiring users to upgrade to Longhorn. He frames this as a vindication of Raymond Chen's pragmatic philosophy over the marketing-driven approach of forcing OS upgrades. The post also briefly mentions work on discussion group software with a small but potentially significant feature allowing users to link their names to URLs.
key_points: Microsoft reversed course on making Avalon exclusive to Longhorn, confirming Spolsky's earlier prediction that there was no technical reason to bundle it with the new OS
The decision validates Raymond Chen's philosophy of backward compatibility and practical engineering over marketing-driven platform lock-in
Small features like letting users link their names to URLs can have outsized effects on community behavior and dynamics
money_quotes: There's no technical reason not to do this. But Microsoft needs to give you a reason to buy Longhorn.
It's a small feature that I'm hoping will have a big effect on the anthropology of the discussion group.
Like everything else, it's an experiment.
posts.key_insight: When Microsoft decoupled Avalon from Longhorn, it proved that technical decisions driven by marketing rather than engineering inevitably get corrected.

posts.summary: Joel announces the beta launch of a new discussion group for his website, acknowledging known bugs and missing features like search. The post highlights backend improvements focused on spam prevention and administrative tools. He notes that users can now link their websites to their posts, though the links won't pass PageRank to prevent abuse. The main value proposition is reduced spam and easier creation of new discussion groups in the future.
key_points: The new discussion group is launched in beta with known bugs and a plain design, prioritizing functionality over polish
Backend improvements focus on spam and abuse prevention, reflecting the ongoing challenge of community management
Users can attach URLs to their names to reduce anonymity, but links are nofollowed to prevent PageRank manipulation
Infrastructure improvements reduced the effort to create new discussion groups from an hour to trivial, enabling future expansion
money_quotes: I'm hoping that people who have their own web sites will do so, and that this will make posters a little less anonymous.
These URLs will not be followed by search engines like Google, so they cannot be abused to "steal" PageRank.
Most of the changes are really on the back end, in the adminitrator's interface and in the abuse- and spam-prevention features.
It's now much easier for me to make a new discussion group, something which used to take an hour of work.
posts.key_insight: The most impactful software improvements are often invisible backend changes — like spam prevention and reducing operational friction — rather than flashy user-facing features.

posts.summary: Joel announces the creation of new sub-forums for the Joel on Software discussion group, which had grown too large for productive conversation. He draws a parallel to Usenet's historical tendency to fork newsgroups as they grew, noting his general aversion to forking because it dilutes discussion quality. Despite this, he concedes the practical necessity of splitting into topic-focused groups. He also briefly promotes the second printing of his book.
key_points: Online discussion communities naturally tend to fragment as they grow, following the pattern established by Usenet newsgroups
Forking discussions carries a real cost: smaller groups produce less interesting conversations
Practical necessity sometimes overrides philosophical preferences — too much traffic degrades discussion quality just as much as too little
Joel applies natural selection to his forums: groups that don't generate enough traffic will be killed off
money_quotes: I didn't like forks, because they make discussions less interesting.
It's bad enough there's a comp.software.windows.nt.40.microsoft.notepad, does there have to be a comp.software.windows.nt.40.microsoft.notepad.helpfile.index?
If any of these groups doesn't have enough daily traffic, it'll go the way of the old New Yorkers group and the Delphi group, bless them, in the Joel on Software graveyard.
posts.key_insight: Growing online communities face an inherent tension between keeping discussions consolidated enough to be interesting and splitting them up enough to remain manageable.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces that Eric Sink will host a new Business of Software forum on Joel's discussion site. The brief post serves primarily as an introduction to Eric Sink's credentials, highlighting his extensive career including building the Spyglass web browser, founding the AbiWord project, and running SourceGear. Joel positions Sink as uniquely qualified due to his deep industry experience and generous knowledge-sharing about the software business.
key_points: Eric Sink is taking on hosting duties for a new Business of Software discussion forum
Sink's credentials span decades: early web browsers (Spyglass), open source (AbiWord), and a successful Inc 500 company (SourceGear)
Joel frames Sink simultaneously as friend, teacher, and competitor, suggesting mutual respect in the small software business community
money_quotes: I'm happy to announce that my friend, teacher, and competitor Eric Sink has agreed to host the new Business of Software forum.
He has been extremely generous with his knowledge of software business.
posts.key_insight: This is a brief announcement post introducing Eric Sink as host of a new business-focused forum, notable mainly for how Joel highlights the collegial relationships among independent software entrepreneurs of the era.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that while usability matters at the margins, it's not the most important factor in software success — applications that solve real problems succeed despite poor UI, while perfectly usable but pointless software flops. He introduces the concept of 'social interface design' as the next frontier beyond traditional usability, focusing on how software mediates human-to-human interaction. Using examples like Napster, text messaging, eBay, and Usenet, he shows how small software design decisions profoundly shape social behavior and community dynamics. He argues that social interface design requires thinking like an anthropologist rather than a usability engineer, optimizing for the health of the community rather than individual user success. Spolsky predicts this will become a distinct discipline, much as UI design emerged from ergonomics.
key_points: Software that does something people desperately want will succeed despite terrible usability, while perfectly usable software that solves no real problem will fail
Social interface design — how software mediates human-to-human interaction — is more important than traditional usability for Internet-era software
Small software design decisions (like Usenet's quote-and-reply feature) can create entirely new social behaviors and interaction patterns
The goal of social interface design is to help the society succeed, even if it means individual users must fail — a fundamentally different objective than traditional UI design
Effective anti-abuse strategies work with human psychology rather than against it, such as shadow-banning spam posts rather than displaying error messages that invite workarounds
money_quotes: An application that does something really great that people really want to do can be pathetically unusable, and it will still be a hit.
When you're writing software that mediates between people, after you get the usability right, you have to get the social interface right. And the social interface is more important.
Whereas the goal of user interface design is to help the user succeed, the goal of social interface design is to help the society succeed, even if it means one user has to fail.
Ebay made a big bet on the cultural anthropology of human beings and won. The great thing about ebay is that it was a huge success precisely because it seemed like a terrible idea at the time.
Even though human beings had been debating for centuries, a tiny feature of a software product produced a whole new style of debating.
One of the best ways to deflect attacks is to make it look like they're succeeding. It's the software equivalent of playing dead.
When software implements social interfaces while disregarding cultural anthropology, it's creepy and awkward and doesn't really work.

Software used in teams usually fails to take hold, because it requires everyone on the team to change the way they work simultaneously, something which anthropologists will tell you is vanishingly unlikely.
posts.key_insight: Beyond usability lies social interface design — the discipline of shaping software to support healthy human-to-human interaction, which requires anthropological thinking rather than traditional UI engineering.

posts.summary: This brief news post highlights two topics: Joel's latest article about social interfaces in software, and a community resource about workspace quality for developers. The main intellectual contribution is the argument that internet software has shifted from human-computer interaction to human-human interaction, making social interface design more critical than traditional usability. Joel asserts that even perfect UI cannot compensate for poor social dynamics in software. The workspace link reinforces his ongoing advocacy for quality developer work environments.
key_points: Internet software fundamentally differs from 1980s software because it mediates human-human interaction rather than human-computer interaction
Social interface design is more important than traditional usability for software that connects people
Great UI cannot save software with a poorly designed social interface
Quality workspace design remains an important factor for software development teams
money_quotes: Software in the 1980s, when usability was "invented," was all about computer-human interaction.
But the Internet brings us a new kind of software: software that's about human-human interaction.
When you're writing software that mediates between people, after you get the usability right, you have to get the social interface right.
And the social interface is more important.
The best UI in the world won't save software with an awkward social interface.
posts.key_insight: For internet software that connects people, getting the social interface right matters more than traditional usability — no amount of UI polish can compensate for awkward social dynamics.

posts.summary: This is a brief memorial post published on September 11, 2004, the third anniversary of the 9/11 attacks. Joel Spolsky, based in New York City, posted a simple link to a memorial page. The post contains no analysis or argument — it is a solemn acknowledgment of the date's significance. Its brevity is itself the statement.
key_points: Published on the third anniversary of September 11, 2001
Contains only a single link to a memorial page, with no commentary
Demonstrates that sometimes silence and simplicity are more powerful than words
posts.key_insight: Some moments call for silence rather than commentary, and a simple act of remembrance can be more powerful than any essay.

posts.summary: This post covers three unrelated topics: a link to Rands' piece on completionist vs incrementalist mindsets in software development, Dan Appleman's skepticism about Microsoft's long-term commitment to Windows Forms, and a detailed update on improvements to Joel's discussion group software. The bulk of the post describes new features including full-text search powered by SQL Server, an RSS feed with bandwidth trade-offs, and a Bayesian spam filter inspired by Paul Graham's work. Joel outlines three public anti-spam strategies and hints at undisclosed ones, noting that revealing all countermeasures would help spammers circumvent them. The post reflects the mid-2000s battle against comment spam as spammers shifted from email to web forums to game Google rankings.
key_points: Comment spam became the top priority for spammers as email filters improved, with discussion group spam used to manipulate Google search rankings
Bayesian filtering, closing comments on old posts, and META tags preventing search engine URL following form a three-pronged anti-spam defense
Some anti-spam techniques must be kept secret because revealing them reduces their effectiveness by lowering the cost of spamming
RSS feed design required compromises—full text of topics but not replies—due to bandwidth constraints from frequent RSS reader polling
SQL Server's full-text search had significant limitations, requiring manual index rebuilds scheduled every 15 minutes
money_quotes: If you're going to spend the time to solve a problem, solve it in a manner that you aren't going to be solving it AGAIN in three months.
Can Microsoft categorically promise to maintain a full commitment to development, maintenance and support of Windows Forms for the next 15 years?
If you think comment spam is not a big problem, you haven't moderated a discussion group lately.

This is the number one priority for spammers these days, since email filters are starting to work pretty well and spamming a lot of discussion groups is perceived as a good way to trick Google into giving a site prominent placement.
Although this technique prevents comment spam from working it doesn't prevent it from happening because spammers don't seem to particularly care if a given spam works or not.
By 'we admit to' I imply that there are other things we do which we don't talk about too much because revealing them would make it that much easier for spammers to work around them.
posts.key_insight: Effective anti-spam defense requires multiple layered strategies, some of which must remain secret because transparency about countermeasures reduces the cost of circumventing them.

posts.summary: This is a brief news update post where Joel Spolsky shares links to three recent interviews he gave. He was interviewed by Doug Kaye for IT Conversations (audio), by Dave Walker for the Sydney Morning Herald and The Age (with an extended version online), and by Mary Jo Foley for Microsoft Watch. The post is purely informational with no substantive argument or analysis.
key_points: Joel was gaining significant media attention across multiple outlets and formats
The interviews spanned tech podcasts, mainstream newspapers, and industry newsletters
Joel's influence extended internationally, reaching Australian publications
posts.key_insight: This is a minor housekeeping post linking to external interviews, with no substantive content or argument of its own.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares a brief update post covering two topics: his new book project collecting the best software essays of 2004, and the progress on FogBugz 4.0. He solicits nominations for essays to include in the book, with a November deadline. He also discusses the company's focus on shipping FogBugz 4.0, noting their philosophy of listening to customers over competitors. The post ends with a humorous reflection on regretting the FogBugz name but accepting it as too established to change.
key_points: Joel is curating a book of the best software essays of 2004, soliciting public nominations with full attribution required
FogBugz 4.0 development is consuming the entire company plus four outside consultants, with a beta expected in weeks
Fog Creek's product philosophy centers on listening to customers rather than competitors
Brand names can become entrenched even when founders regret them — the cost of changing exceeds the cost of living with a suboptimal name
money_quotes: This release, I think, will really show the benefits of our policy of listening to our customers, not our competitors.
The goofy name FogBugz is an historical accident which I rather regret, but the brand name is so well known by now that we're not going to change it.
I have actually gotten email from people who say they can't buy FogBugz because the name doesn't sound 'professional.'
Sorry! Get over it, or you may have to use the professionally-named 'Bugzilla' forever!
Writers are encouraged to nominate their own works… don't be embarassed!
posts.key_insight: Brand names become entrenched quickly, so even a regrettable name can be worth keeping once it has recognition — and product success comes from listening to customers, not competitors.

posts.summary: Joel analyzes Ipswitch's failed pricing strategy for their IMail email server, where the company tried to move upmarket to compete with Microsoft Exchange by adding collaboration features and raising prices. He argues they made two fundamental mistakes: underestimating Microsoft's ability to charge more simply by being the market leader, and misframing the value equation by hoping customers would compare their new prices to Exchange rather than to their own previous pricing. Joel uses this as a case study in software pricing psychology, explaining that perceived value is always relative to a baseline comparison. He concludes that small companies should never compete on price against larger rivals who have inherent cost advantages.
key_points: Microsoft can charge more for equivalent software because market leadership lets them capture more consumer surplus through brand trust and a massive sales force
Customers judge new prices against the most natural baseline — usually the previous version's price — not against a competitor the vendor wishes they'd compare to
Ipswitch confused two different markets (large corporate email vs. small ISPs and businesses) that have very different willingness to pay
Small companies should never compete on price against larger rivals who can distribute development costs over more customers and already have sunk marketing costs
Premium-priced 'decoy' editions (like Visual Studio Enterprise Architect) exist primarily to make other editions look like better values by comparison
money_quotes: The price of software has very little to do with cost, and everything to do with perceived value.
Never compete on price when you don't have a price advantage. If you're the small company, you don't have the price advantage.
Consumers don't really know how to judge the value of software so well, so they grope around for nearby products to use as baselines.

IPSwitch was hoping their ISP customers, who have never remotely considered using Exchange due to the extremely high per-user cost, would compare their new price to Exchange, but of course, what they really compared it to was the price of the old version of their product.
They underestimated how much more Microsoft can charge for the same junk simply because Microsoft is the market leader.
My sources inform me that this was a decision made by the MBAs in the company over the strong objections of the developers. It's ironic that the MBAs don't even get the basic MBA stuff right.
posts.key_insight: Software pricing is driven by perceived value relative to a baseline comparison, and companies that try to reframe their value equation by chasing a larger competitor's market will fail when customers compare the new price to the old one instead.

posts.summary: This is a short news-style post covering two unrelated topics. First, Joel highlights Eric Meyer's S5 slide show system built entirely on web standards (XHTML, CSS, JavaScript) as a free alternative to PowerPoint, noting that a CityDesk template makes it even easier to use. Second, he describes an intermittent IIS 6.0 bug he struggled with, which turned out to be a COM+ application identity issue where the IWAM account needed to be changed. The post is characteristic of Joel's blog style of mixing product promotion with technical problem-solving.
key_points: S5 is a standards-based (XHTML/CSS/JavaScript) slide show format that serves as a free, web-friendly alternative to PowerPoint
A CityDesk template for S5 removes the need to know HTML, making standards-compliant presentations accessible to non-technical users
An intermittent IIS 6.0 bug was traced to the COM+ application identity being set to the IWAM_Machinename account instead of the correct user
Joel enlisted his community of readers to help debug the IIS issue, thanking nearly 30 contributors by name
money_quotes: S5 is a slide show format based entirely on XHTML, CSS, and JavaScript. With one file, you can run a complete slide show and have a printer-friendly version as well.
The markup used for the slides is very simple, highly semantic, and completely accessible.
This is a pretty cool free, standards-compliant improvement on PowerPoint that lets you put your presentations on the web.
I am completely at wit's end debugging this strange (apparent) IIS bug.
posts.key_insight: Web standards (XHTML, CSS, JavaScript) can replace proprietary tools like PowerPoint for presentations, while community collaboration remains invaluable for debugging obscure server issues.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post from Election Day 2004, consisting of just three words urging readers to go vote. Joel Spolsky steps entirely outside his usual software development topics to make a simple civic appeal. The post contains no technical content, argument, or analysis whatsoever. It reflects the cultural moment of the 2004 U.S. presidential election between George W. Bush and John Kerry.
key_points: Joel used his popular tech blog platform to encourage civic participation on Election Day 2004
The post demonstrates that even niche technical bloggers sometimes use their audience reach for non-technical messages
At just 3 words, it is one of the shortest posts in the Joel on Software archive
money_quotes: Go vote now!
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the most important thing a person with a platform can say has nothing to do with their area of expertise.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces two updates: a new off-topic discussion group for political conversations (to keep the main forum focused on software), and progress on FogBugz 4.0. He explains his writing hiatus by noting that shipping FogBugz 4.0 has taken priority over blog essays. He highlights the team behind the release, including designer Dave Shea, writer Mike Gunderloy, and tester Jill McFarlane, framing it as proof that investing in great people pays off.
key_points: Politics and software are 'orthogonal' concerns, and Joel deliberately keeps them separated on his blog to avoid alienating readers.
Shipping product takes priority over writing — Joel has paused essays to focus on getting FogBugz 4.0 out the door.

Joel highlights the caliber of individual contributors on FogBugz 4.0 (a famous designer, a prolific author, a 12x-productive tester) as a deliberate counterpoint to outsourcing to the cheapest bidder.
The post subtly reinforces Joel's long-running thesis that hiring exceptional people is the single most important investment a software company can make.
money_quotes: In programming terms, politics are orthogonal to software, and tightly coupling my personal political viewpoint would result in unnecessary arbitrary incompatibilities.
Every time I start writing one I realize that it's much more important to get FogBugz 4.0 out the damn door.
When this thing ships you can tell me if you think having a great team is worth the trouble or if I should have just farmed the development out to the cheapest bidder.
posts.key_insight: Investing in exceptional individual contributors — a world-class designer, a 12x tester, a respected author — is what separates great products from commodity outsourcing.

posts.summary: This is a brief news roundup post where Joel Spolsky shares links about bootstrapping businesses without venture capital, promotes his book project collecting the best software essays of 2004, and advertises summer internships at Fog Creek Software. The post reinforces Joel's well-known skepticism of venture capital by linking to resources about avoiding VCs and bootstrapping. It also serves as a call to action for readers to participate in nominating essays and to refer talented interns.
key_points: Venture capital misaligns incentives between VCs and company founders
Bootstrapping a business without outside funding is increasingly viable
Joel was curating a collection of the best software essays of 2004
Fog Creek Software actively recruited smart CS undergrads and marketing students for internships
money_quotes: VCs do not have goals that are aligned with the goals of the company founders.
There's never been a better time to start a business with no money.
The pay is good, the benefits are great, and they'll get to make a real contribution to shipping software.
posts.key_insight: Founders are better off bootstrapping than taking venture capital, because VC incentives are fundamentally misaligned with founder goals.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post consisting of a single Windows UI tip. Joel shares that pressing Ctrl+C while a Windows popup error message box is displayed copies its text to the clipboard. There is no argument or analysis — it is purely a quick productivity tip shared with readers.
key_points: Ctrl+C copies the text content of Windows popup error message boxes to the clipboard
This is a hidden/lesser-known Windows UI feature that most users don't discover on their own
The post demonstrates Joel's habit of sharing small, practical tips alongside longer essays
money_quotes: Did you know… you can Ctrl+C to copy the text of a Windows popup error message box to the clipboard?
posts.key_insight: Windows has a hidden feature where Ctrl+C copies the text of popup error dialogs to the clipboard, useful for reporting or searching for error messages.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Joel Spolsky shares two pieces of news: his book 'Joel on Software' was named one of Amazon's ten best books of 2004 in the Computers and Internet category, and he announces upcoming speaking engagements at two California conferences in March 2005.
key_points: Joel on Software book was recognized as a top-10 Amazon book of 2004 in Computers and Internet
Joel announced speaking appearances at O'Reilly Emerging Technology Conference and SD West 2005
money_quotes: If you're looking for an interesting conference to get your boss to send you to, both would be excellent choices.
posts.key_insight: A short promotional update highlighting the commercial success of Joel's writing and his growing influence as a speaker in the software industry.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky promotes an upcoming free NetSeminar where he'll present on social software, sponsored by Electric Cloud and hosted by Software Development magazine. He humorously walks through the expected format: his compressed talk, a sponsor demo by tcl creator John Ousterhout, Q&A, and live polls. He transparently discloses the commercial arrangement while genuinely endorsing the sponsor's distributed make technology. The post is essentially a promotional announcement wrapped in Joel's characteristic self-deprecating humor and digressive asides.
key_points: Joel announces a free 60-minute NetSeminar on social software, compressed into a fast-paced 20-minute talk
He transparently discloses the commercial structure: the magazine pays him, Electric Cloud sponsors the event and gets the registration leads
The sponsor, Electric Cloud, was founded by tcl creator John Ousterhout and makes distributed parallel make tools
Joel uses humor to set low expectations for the technical execution of the online event format
money_quotes: tcl (motto: "And you thought lisp had weird quoting semantics")
the nice salespeople at Electric Cloud will quarrel over the registration data, a.k.a. "the Glengarry leads."
Electric Cloud actually has really quite an interesting distributed make technology and you would probably want to check it out even if they weren't paying people to say that.
More likely there will be funny pratfalls, feedback sounds, microphone batteries dying, and in the background of my presentation you will hear glass breaking followed by babies wailing.
I don't know where we'll get the babies. It's not like there are normally babies in my office.
posts.key_insight: Joel turns a routine event promotion into entertaining content by being transparently honest about the commercial arrangement and self-deprecating about the likely technical mishaps.

posts.summary: Joel briefly recaps a seminar he participated in, noting the large audience of 700 people. He felt his segment on social interface design was too short and could have been expanded significantly. He also observes that the Electric Cloud content, while interesting, was thematically disconnected from his own topic, making the overall seminar feel disjointed.
key_points: The seminar drew a substantial audience of about 700 people
Joel felt constrained by time and wanted to explore social interface design in much greater depth
The pairing of unrelated topics (social interface design and Electric Cloud) made the seminar feel incoherent
money_quotes: I could have talked about this social interface design for hours.
The Electric Cloud stuff was interesting enough but admittedly unrelated to my own topic which made the whole seminar kind of out of whack.
posts.key_insight: Combining unrelated topics in a single seminar undermines the coherence of the event, even when individual segments are strong.

posts.summary: Joel shares a quick tip about JPEG file bloat, noting that a small image on his site was 42KB but contained nearly 35KB of useless metadata from a photo editing program. He credits Dennis Forbes for providing a utility to strip the unnecessary data, reducing the image to just 7KB. The post is a brief technical note rather than an essay.
key_points: JPEG files can contain massive metadata blocks ('application blocks') that dwarf the actual image data
In this case, 83% of the file size was useless bloat from photo editing software
Simple tools exist to strip unnecessary JPEG metadata and dramatically reduce file sizes
money_quotes: 34,885 of those bytes were in a useless 'application block' that some photo editing program put there.
posts.key_insight: Photo editing software can silently bloat JPEG files with metadata that vastly exceeds the size of the actual image data.

posts.summary: Spolsky uses the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis as a springboard to argue that some concepts are nearly untranslatable between languages, particularly the Hebrew concepts of 'rosh gadol' (big head — taking broad ownership and initiative) and 'rosh katan' (small head — doing only what you're told). He contends that software methodologies like Microsoft Solutions Framework try to codify the behaviors of 'rosh gadol' thinkers into rules that 'rosh katan' people can follow, but this approach is doomed to fail. The post is a concise, playful lament about the inadequacy of English to express this distinction.
key_points: Some concepts resist translation between languages, and the gap matters for conveying ideas about work culture and software development
The Hebrew distinction between 'rosh gadol' (taking ownership beyond your task) and 'rosh katan' (doing only the minimum asked) is essential to building good teams
Software methodologies like MSF attempt to turn 'rosh gadol' behaviors into repeatable rules, but this is fundamentally misguided
You cannot process-engineer initiative and ownership — these are mindsets, not checklists
money_quotes: MSF is a fraud–an attempt to consolidate all the ראש גדול things Microsoft programmers do in a set of rules which are supposed to work if you force ראש קטן bizonim to implement them. And it's never going to work.
All I wanted to say was that methodologies encourage ראש קטן and I need everyone on my team to be ראש גדול.
I have been trying to translate this simple concept to English for years and am just about ready to give up.
I have been forced to write entire essays simply because I cannot find any other way to convey to English speakers the difference between ראש גדול and ראש קטן.
No matter how debunked Whorf is, I'm still convinced that Israelis are more likely to do things דווקא, simply because they have a word for it.
posts.key_insight: You cannot encode ownership mentality into a methodology — processes designed to make rule-followers behave like initiative-takers will always fail.

posts.summary: Spolsky explores the Israeli military concepts of 'rosh katan' (small head) and 'rosh gadol' (big head) as a framework for understanding developer behavior. Rosh katan describes someone who does exactly what they're told and nothing more, while rosh gadol describes someone who takes initiative and does what's needed, not just what's requested. He connects this to the concept of 'work to rule' labor action, where workers follow instructions so literally they become useless. Spolsky then argues that formal software methodologies like MSF inadvertently encourage rosh katan behavior by reducing experienced developers to compliance monkeys focused on process checkboxes rather than outcomes. He illustrates this with concrete examples of bug-fixing behavior, contrasting initiative-taking developers with those who do the bare minimum. The core argument is that methodologies designed to make average teams consistent actually demotivate talented developers by forcing them into rigid processes that ignore individual capability.
key_points: The Israeli military concept of 'rosh gadol' (big head) vs 'rosh katan' (small head) maps perfectly to the difference between developers who take initiative and those who do only what's explicitly asked
Work-to-rule behavior demonstrates that no matter how many orders you give, someone can remain 100% useless while technically obeying every instruction
Formal methodologies that enforce consistency across all developers treat everyone as interchangeable, wasting the time of experienced developers on tasks like unnecessary unit testing
When talented developers are forced into rigid processes, they become demotivated and start giving inflated estimates and performing the minimum required duties
Best practices extracted from successful teams cannot simply be imposed on less skilled teams to achieve the same results — context and talent matter
money_quotes: Rosh Gadol of course is quite the opposite: taking initiative and doing what is desired, not what is requested.
Someone who is working to rule can always demonstrate that no matter how many orders you give someone, they can probably make themselves 100% useless while still obeying every order you give them.
Methodologies encourage rock stars to become compliance monkeys, and I need everyone on my team to be a rock star.
The Navy is a master plan designed by geniuses for execution by idiots. If you're not an idiot, but find yourself in the Navy, you can only operate well by pretending to be one.

The trouble with MSF is that it starts with a group of successful developers, who are successful because they are resourceful, intelligent, experienced, well-meaning, and have plush private offices with doors that close, and then attempts to claim that if impose some of their 'best practices' on your team of unskilled developers, you will achieve the same results.
It's like Daniel Boulud selling a manual to McDonald's fry cooks.
posts.key_insight: Formal software methodologies that enforce uniform processes inadvertently punish initiative-taking developers by reducing them to compliance monkeys, destroying the very resourcefulness that makes great teams successful.

posts.summary: This is a very brief news post where Joel Spolsky shares a link to an interview he did with Scott Rosenberg for Salon magazine. The post consists of a single sentence and a pull quote from the article that humorously connects software writing to Yiddish humor. It serves primarily as a pointer to external content rather than containing substantive argument or analysis.
key_points: Joel was interviewed by Scott Rosenberg for Salon, a major online publication
The interview positioned Joel as a unique voice connecting software commentary with humor
By 2004, Joel on Software had gained enough prominence to warrant mainstream media coverage
money_quotes: The connection between software and Yiddish humor may not have been evident until Joel Spolsky began writing his Joel on Software essays and blog in 2000.
posts.key_insight: By 2004, Joel on Software had achieved enough cultural influence to attract coverage from mainstream publications like Salon, recognized for its distinctive blend of technical insight and humor.

posts.summary: Joel highlights Google Suggest as a groundbreaking web interface innovation. He notes it uses the XmlHttpRequest technique to fetch data from the server without reloading the page, a capability that existed but was largely ignored by web developers. He argues that the latency of traditional web UIs, which require full page reloads for every interaction, makes them feel clunky compared to native applications. Google is publicly raising expectations for what web interfaces should feel like.
key_points: Google Suggest saves keystrokes but more importantly demonstrates a new paradigm for web interactivity using XmlHttpRequest
The technique of fetching server data while the user interacts with the page had been possible for a long time but was mostly ignored by developers
Full page reloads are the primary reason web UIs feel clunky compared to native GUIs
Google is setting a new public standard for web interface quality that others will need to follow
money_quotes: The latency of web UIs, in which everything you do is a slow round-trip that requires completely refetching and rebuilding the web page, is one of the reason web UIs feel so clunky compared to native GUIs.
Google is very publicly raising the bar on the quality of interfaces that people will expect from web pages.
This has been possible for a long long time, but web developers have been mostly ignoring it.
posts.key_insight: Google Suggest's use of XmlHttpRequest to update the page without reloading publicly demonstrated that web applications could approach the responsiveness of native GUIs, setting new expectations for the entire industry.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Joel Spolsky invites readers to upcoming informal dinner and lunch meetups in Bellevue, WA and Toronto. He asks attendees to RSVP for headcount purposes. The post highlights the community aspect of the Joel on Software blog, where readers could meet in person. There is no substantive technical or editorial content in this post.
key_points: Joel organized informal reader meetups in multiple cities as part of his blog community
The meetups were described as casual 'geek-out sessions' rather than formal events
The post reflects the strong community that formed around the Joel on Software blog in the mid-2000s
money_quotes: These are always very informal geek-out sessions, lots of fun and a chance to meet other readers in your area.
posts.key_insight: This is a minor announcement post with no substantive content beyond scheduling reader meetups.

posts.summary: Joel discusses several topics: seasonal affective disorder as a likely cause of programmer malaise, a usability bug in Google Suggest, and a court ruling on competitor advertising via Google search. His central argument is that Google Suggest, despite its bugs, represents a paradigm shift in web user expectations — users will soon demand responsive, AJAX-style interfaces instead of tolerating full-page reloads. He frames this as 'raising the bar' for all web applications.
key_points: Programmer dissatisfaction with their work may correlate more with seasonal affective disorder than actual problems with the profession.
Google Suggest has a usability bug where the popup dropdown intercepts clicks intended for the search button, violating user expectations.
Google Suggest's real importance isn't search — it's training users to expect highly responsive, interactive web interfaces.
Web applications that rely on full-page reloads for basic interactions like sorting, zooming, or deleting will no longer be acceptable once users experience responsive alternatives.
Competitors advertising on your brand name in Google search is legal but tacky.
money_quotes: Install some bright lights, drink some coffee, take a vacation in Tahiti, and tell me in April if you still think software development is depressing.
It's important not for searching, but because it's going to teach web users to expect highly responsive user interfaces.
They will no longer tolerate the full-page-reload-and-scroll-to-the-top that Mapquest has conditioned them to accept.
If you have an email application, and you show people a list of email and give them a button to delete email as spam, they're going to expect virtually instantaneous response time.
That's what I meant by 'raising the bar.'
You don't see Wal*Mart advertising when you search for Tiffany.
posts.key_insight: Google Suggest's lasting impact isn't better search — it's permanently raising user expectations for responsive, interactive web interfaces and making full-page reloads unacceptable.

posts.summary: This brief post excerpts a key passage from Joel Spolsky's longer essay 'Camels and Rubber Duckies' about software pricing strategy. The core argument is that pricing is a signal, not just a number — it communicates perceived quality to customers. Spolsky argues that pricing your product in the middle of competitors' range signals mediocrity, while pricing significantly higher signals premium quality. The post serves as a teaser linking to the full essay on pricing psychology.
key_points: Pricing is fundamentally a signal about product quality, not just a reflection of cost or value
Mid-range pricing communicates mediocrity — it tells customers your product is unremarkable
Charging significantly more than competitors can paradoxically increase perceived value
Customers use price as a heuristic to judge quality when they lack other information
money_quotes: When you're setting a price, you're sending a signal.

If your competitor's software ranges in price from about $100 to about $500, and you decide, heck, my product is about in the middle of the road, so I'll sell it for $300, well, what message do you think you're sending to your customers? You're telling them that you think your software is 'eh.'
posts.key_insight: Pricing is a signal, not a calculation — charging more than competitors can communicate quality more effectively than pricing in the middle of the pack.

posts.summary: Joel comments on the new FASB accounting rule requiring companies to expense stock options from reported profits starting mid-2005. He dismisses the Silicon Valley objection that stock options are too difficult to value, arguing that accounting routinely handles illiquid assets with uncertain valuations. The post is brief but takes a clear stance in favor of the new expensing requirement, positioning it as basic financial accountability rather than a controversial burden.
key_points: New accounting guidelines will force companies to deduct stock option values from reported profits starting mid-2005
Silicon Valley insiders oppose expensing stock options partly by arguing their value is too hard to determine
Joel argues that accounting for illiquid assets with uncertain values is a routine and unavoidable part of financial accounting
The fact that stock option values change over time does not exempt them from consistent accounting treatment
money_quotes: Just because the value of stock options changes over time or because it is not possible to fix exactly does not mean it shouldn't be accounted for consistently.
posts.key_insight: The difficulty of valuing stock options is not a valid argument against expensing them, since accounting routinely handles uncertain valuations of illiquid assets.

posts.summary: This is a brief news update post marking the fifth anniversary of Joel on Software and sharing miscellaneous announcements. Joel links to PHP upgrade instructions for FogBugz users dealing with a security flaw, mentions a job opening for an office manager at Fog Creek Software, and promotes their summer internship program. The post is a lightweight holiday-season roundup rather than an essay or argument.
key_points: PHP security flaw required FogBugz users to upgrade, and Fog Creek provided instructions
Joel on Software reached its fifth anniversary on December 24, 2004
Fog Creek was actively recruiting both an office manager and summer interns
Joel leveraged the holiday season to encourage readers to spread the word about internships to college-age relatives
money_quotes: Tomorrow is the fifth anniversary of Joel on Software!
posts.key_insight: A brief holiday news post marking five years of Joel on Software and promoting Fog Creek job openings.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief holiday post from December 25, 2004, consisting of nothing more than a link to Paul Auster's 'Auggie Wren's Christmas Story' and a note that it inspired the movie Smoke. At only 14 words, it's essentially a Christmas Day link share rather than a substantive blog post. There is no argument, analysis, or original commentary offered.
key_points: Joel shares a link to Paul Auster's 'Auggie Wren's Christmas Story' as a Christmas Day post
He notes the short story was the inspiration for the 1995 film Smoke
The post contains no original commentary or analysis, serving purely as a holiday recommendation
posts.key_insight: This is a minimal holiday link post with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky introduces his college advice article with a self-deprecating preamble about the unreliability of career advice from older generations in computer science. He notes that college students wisely ignore their elders, whose predictions about technology careers tend to be wildly wrong. He then admits he's equally unqualified to give such advice but proceeds anyway.
key_points: Career advice from elders in computer science is often comically outdated and wrong
College students are better off ignoring most career predictions from previous generations
Self-awareness about one's ignorance doesn't necessarily prevent one from offering opinions anyway
money_quotes: Most college students, fortunately, are brash enough never to bother asking their elders for advice, which, in the field of computer science, is a good thing, because their elders are apt to say goofy, antediluvian things like "the demand for keypunch operators will exceed 100,000,000 by the year 2010."
I, too, have no idea what I'm talking about when I give advice to college students. But that's never stopped me from writing before.
posts.key_insight: Technology career predictions age so poorly that the best qualification for giving advice may simply be the self-awareness to admit you're probably wrong.

posts.summary: This is a brief logistical announcement reminding readers about a Joel on Software community dinner meetup in Bellevue, Washington. The post provides the time and location for the event at Crossroads Mall's food court. It contains no substantive technical or editorial content.
key_points: This is a simple event reminder with no analytical content
Joel on Software community dinners were a recurring series of informal meetups with readers
posts.key_insight: This is a logistical announcement with no substantive insight to extract.

posts.summary: This is a very brief personal update post rather than a substantive essay. Joel comments humorously on Seattle's weather culture, noting the irony of local newspapers treating rain as headline news in a city famous for rain. He also announces an upcoming Joel on Software community lunch meetup in Toronto.
key_points: Seattle newspapers treating rain as newsworthy strikes Joel as absurd given the city's rainy reputation
Joel organized in-person community meetups for his blog readers in various cities
The post reflects the casual, personal blogging style common in the mid-2000s
money_quotes: All the local newspapers are running big headlines about how it's raining. I don't get it!
posts.key_insight: A minor personal update post with a quip about Seattle weather and a meetup announcement, containing no substantive technical or business content.

posts.summary: Spolsky dismantles the common claim among software companies that they 'only hire the top 1%' by exposing a statistical fallacy. He argues that when you reject 199 out of 200 applicants, the 199 rejected candidates don't disappear—they reapply elsewhere, inflating every company's rejection ratio. The best developers rarely appear on the job market because they're already employed and well-compensated. This means the applicant pool is heavily skewed toward a recurring population of unqualified candidates, making selectivity ratios meaningless as a quality metric. Spolsky concludes that companies must proactively seek out top talent rather than passively filtering resumes, which is why Fog Creek aggressively recruits summer interns before they leave the open market.
key_points: Rejected candidates don't exit the job market—they reapply everywhere, artificially inflating every company's apparent selectivity ratio
The best developers rarely apply for jobs because they're already employed, well-paid, and retained by their current employers
A 1-in-200 hire rate says nothing about the quality percentile of the person hired relative to all developers
Passive resume collection systematically over-represents weak candidates and under-represents strong ones
Proactive recruiting (like internship programs targeting top CS students) is necessary to access talent that never hits the open market
money_quotes: Could they all be hiring the top 1%? Where are all the other 99%? General Motors?
Think about what happens to the other 199 that you didn't hire. They go look for another job.
Every employer thinks they're getting the top 0.5% when they're actually getting the top 99.9801%.

Those 200 resumes you got from Craigslist? Those consist of the one guy who happened to be good, but he's only applying for a job because his wife wants to be nearer to her family, and the usual floating population of 199 people who apply for every single job and are qualified for none.
The people who I consider to be good software developers barely ever apply for jobs at all.

When you select 1 out of 200 applicants, the other 199 don't give up and go into plumbing. They apply again somewhere else, and contribute to some other employer's self-delusions about how selective they are.
If you wait around to see who sends you a resume, you're already missing out.
posts.key_insight: Hiring 1 out of 200 applicants doesn't mean you're hiring the top 0.5% of developers—it means you're filtering through the same recycled pool of weak candidates that every other company also rejects.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky documents his real-time planning process for expanding Fog Creek's colocation server infrastructure from a single Dell 2650 to a six-server setup with redundancy and scalability. He outlines the key problems driving the upgrade: insufficient capacity for FogBugz online trials, the need to switch from Windows to Linux email, eliminating single points of hardware failure, and building a scalable architecture. The post takes an unusual 'live article' format where he publishes his plan before implementation, soliciting reader feedback and updating the post with their suggestions.
