He details the specific hardware choices (Dell PowerEdge 2850s), network architecture with separate segments, and open questions about power management, remote control, and RAID compatibility with Unix. The total project budget is around $20K, reflecting Fog Creek's growth from scrappy startup to a company that can afford professional infrastructure.
key_points: Fog Creek's daily revenue has grown enough that 24-hour recovery times and 15-30 minute maintenance windows are no longer acceptable, driving a move toward eliminating single points of hardware failure.
Using standardized, identical server hardware (Dell PowerEdge 2850s) across the rack simplifies maintenance, enables hot-swapping, and reduces costs through volume purchasing.
The architecture separates concerns across six servers: two mirrored web servers, a dedicated SQL Server, a Linux mail server, a routing/firewall server, and a cold backup spare.
Publishing infrastructure plans before implementation and soliciting reader feedback produces better outcomes than the traditional approach of writing about it after the fact.
Dell offers significantly better price-performance than HP (at least 2x cheaper) despite having terrible software and management tools.
money_quotes: In the past I would have upgraded the system, installed it all, and written an article about it. And then people would have emailed me to suggest better ways to do things, but it would be too late.
One of the great things about Fog Creek in its fifth year is that when we need to do something, we have the money to do it.
There will always be single points of failure. The ozone layer, for example.
If something happens to the colo facility, its power supply, or the City of New York, well, we go off the air for a while.
Somehow you always get a lower price than you would have gotten on the website.

I've also been told that if you call during the last week of Dell's fiscal quarter, which I just missed, you get great deals as they spaz out trying to make their numbers for Wall Street in a classic display of American public company mismanagement.
Dell has really, really crappy software skills, and their management software sucks big rocks.

When you call Dell you get a nice, cheerful, competent kid in Austin, Texas who laughs at your jokes, no matter how dumb, and actually has the ability to do sophisticated things like read email that you sent him and then actually reply to it!
posts.key_insight: As a software company grows, infrastructure reliability transitions from a nice-to-have to a revenue-critical concern, and the smartest approach is to plan publicly, solicit expert feedback before committing, and hire specialists rather than improvising with consumer-grade equipment.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces a major server infrastructure upgrade from one server to six at a colocation facility. Rather than following his usual pattern of documenting the process after completion, he plans to write about it in real-time as a 'live' article. This approach allows readers to offer suggestions and share experience before decisions are finalized, rather than sending feedback that arrives too late to be useful.
key_points: Joel on Software and related sites ran on a single server for two years, with a secondary server added later
The upgrade involves moving to six high-end servers at a Peer 1 colocation facility in downtown New York
Joel is experimenting with 'live' documentation—writing about the process as it happens rather than after the fact, inviting reader input in real-time
money_quotes: In the past I would have upgraded the system, installed it all, and written an article about it. And then people would have emailed me to suggest better ways to do things, but it would be too late.
If you have any better suggestions for how to do things, it's not too late for me to learn from your experience.
posts.key_insight: Documenting technical decisions in real-time rather than after the fact turns a one-way writeup into a collaborative process where reader expertise can actually influence outcomes.

posts.summary: Spolsky uses the analogy of a perpetually packed Manhattan restaurant called Isabella's to argue that great software succeeds not through brilliant features but through being 'deeply debugged.' Isabella's thrives despite mediocre food because in ten years of patronage, nothing ever went wrong — every edge case in the dining experience was handled. He extends this to software development, arguing that bug tracking databases are not just scheduling tools but the essential mechanism that makes great software possible. By systematically capturing and fixing every flaw, misunderstanding, and edge case, software evolves from merely functional to truly reliable.
key_points: A restaurant can thrive despite mediocre food simply by never giving customers a reason to stop coming back — the same principle applies to software
Great software is defined not by features but by being 'deeply debugged' — handling every edge case, every misunderstanding, every rare scenario
Bug tracking is not merely a convenience tool but a prerequisite for creating great software, because it captures every flaw and improvement opportunity systematically
With millions of users, even one-in-a-million bugs happen constantly — 'In our business, one in a million is next Tuesday'
Reliability — software that never frustrates its users — is a 'magic' emergent property that comes from relentless bug fixing and triage
money_quotes: Isabella's is thoroughly and completely debugged.
Every time somebody is unhappy, that's a bug. Write it down. Figure out what you're going to do about it. Add it to the training manual. Never make the same mistake twice.
Great products are great because they're deeply debugged. Restaurants, software, it's all the same.
In our business, one in a million is next Tuesday.
A bug tracking database is not just a memory aid, or a scheduling tool. It doesn't make it easier to produce great software, it makes it possible to create great software.
Bug tracking software captures the cosmic rays that cause the genetic mutations that make your software evolve into something superior.
Suddenly it becomes reliable. Reliable, meaning, it never screws up. It never makes its users angry. It never makes its customers wish they had purchased something else.
posts.key_insight: Great software, like a great restaurant, succeeds not through brilliance but through relentless debugging of every edge case until it becomes so reliable that users never have a reason to leave.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the arrival of Mike Gunderloy's new book 'Painless Project Management with FogBugz' at the Fog Creek office. He notes that FogBugz 4.0, which the book covers, will be available for sale by March 1st. Fog Creek plans to sell the book through their online store, marking their first physical inventory item.
key_points: Mike Gunderloy's book 'Painless Project Management with FogBugz' covers FogBugz 4.0 in detail
FogBugz 4.0 was not yet available for sale but expected by March 1st
Selling the book would be Fog Creek's first physical inventory item, requiring changes to their online store
money_quotes: Astute readers will note that FogBugz 4.0 is not quite available for sale yet, but it will be, by March 1st.
posts.key_insight: This is a brief product announcement about a companion book for FogBugz 4.0 and Fog Creek's first foray into selling physical goods.

posts.summary: Joel shares a brief quote from Katie Lucas that satirizes software development methodologies by comparing them to a running race. The quote reduces methodology to absurdly simple steps, implying that most of the ceremony around software processes is unnecessary overhead. The core message is that no amount of planning, preparation, or methodology can substitute for simply being good at the actual work.
key_points: Software methodologies are satirized as overcomplicating what is fundamentally about skill and execution
The analogy to racing suggests that process and preparation are secondary to raw ability
Joel endorses this view by sharing it without commentary, letting the satire speak for itself
money_quotes: Step 1: write about running really fast. Step 2: Go and draw a plan of the racetrack. Step 3: go and buy really tight lycra shorts. Step 4: run really, really, really fast. Step 5: cross line first.
posts.key_insight: No methodology can substitute for simply being excellent at the work itself — most process is just the equivalent of buying tight shorts before a race.

posts.summary: This is a brief news post where Joel Spolsky shares a quote from Jamie Zawinski (jwz) about groupware software design. Zawinski argues that software should be designed around the most visceral, immediate human motivation rather than abstract enterprise use cases. The post is essentially a signal boost for Zawinski's provocative take on product design philosophy.
key_points: Software should be designed around concrete, compelling human needs rather than abstract corporate use cases
Zawinski advocates for narrowing product focus to the most basic user motivation
The quote challenges enterprise groupware thinking by reframing the target user as a college student with primal motivations
money_quotes: Your 'use case' should be, there's a 22 year old college student living in the dorms. How will this software get him laid?
posts.key_insight: The best software design starts by solving the most compelling, immediate human need rather than building for abstract enterprise requirements.

posts.summary: Spolsky critiques Microsoft AntiSpyware on two fronts: a usability blunder where the UI displays 'Detected Spyware on Your System' as a heading before any results have loaded, and the broader strategic problem of Microsoft giving away free antispyware software. He argues the empty-list display reveals classic programmer thinking that confuses users, and that Microsoft's decision to make the product free will destroy the existing antispyware industry while creating a monopoly product plagued by conflicts of interest with companies like Real Networks and media conglomerates.
key_points: The AntiSpyware UI commits a usability sin by showing 'Detected Spyware on Your System' as a list heading before any results exist, alarming users unnecessarily
Programmers think an empty list is self-explanatory, but to users it's one pixel away from communicating the exact wrong message
Microsoft giving away antispyware for free will wipe out a useful competitive industry and replace it with a monoculture
Microsoft has inherent conflicts of interest in running antispyware — they may face pressure from partners and litigants like Real Networks to exempt certain spyware
Corporate deal-making (DRM agreements, lawsuits) could compromise which spyware Microsoft chooses to block
money_quotes: The usual programmer mentality ('it's just a list with 0 elements, what's so hard to understand about that?').
Hey guys, next time don't use a message that's only one pixel away from telling me the exact wrong fact about whether or not there's spyware on my system.
This is not a software category where a monopoly monoculture will be a good thing.
I wonder if Microsoft can run an antispyware product without huge conflicts of interest.
It's a shame that by giving it away free they're likely to wipe out a useful industry and replace it with something that's difficult to trust due to conflicts of interest.
A sheet of tinfoil wrapped tightly around your skull is effective against this zapper, I understand.
posts.key_insight: When a dominant platform company gives away free tools that compete with third-party software, it kills a healthy ecosystem and replaces it with a conflicted monopoly product — and meanwhile, their programmers can't even get the UI right.

posts.summary: Joel announces that FogBugz 4.0 shipped on its planned date after months of work. He notes that the release consumed his attention and he'll be catching up on other things. The caveat is that only the Windows version shipped on time, with Unix and Mac versions still in beta.
key_points: FogBugz 4.0 shipped exactly on its planned date, a point of pride for the team
Major product releases require putting other work on hold, creating a catch-up period afterward
The 'shipped on time' claim comes with a significant asterisk: only the Windows version was ready
money_quotes: FogBugz 4.0 finally went live, on the exact day we planned to ship it quite a few months ago.
The Large Print Giveth and the Small Print Taketh Away: What we shipped today was FogBugz 4.0 for Windows.
posts.key_insight: Shipping on time is worth celebrating, but the fine print often reveals that scope was cut to hit the date.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares a brief update about Fog Creek's operations, noting they've been busy adding physical product shipping capabilities to their online store to sell a FogBugz book. The irony is that they originally built their own store because off-the-shelf e-commerce solutions were too focused on physical delivery, and now they're retrofitting that very functionality. He reflects on the variety of work at a software startup and teases an upcoming series about FogBugz 4.0 development.
key_points: Fog Creek built custom e-commerce software because existing solutions lacked support for digital downloads and licenses, but now must add physical shipping features anyway
Adding physical product fulfillment required substantial new code for package tracking, shipping addresses, inventory, and packing slips
Joel finds appeal in the variety of startup work, where coding monotony can be broken by entirely different operational tasks
money_quotes: The joke is on us, because the reason we wrote our own store code in the first place was because all of the off-the-shelf ecommerce packages were too focused on physical delivery and didn't have any kind of mechanism for selling downloads and licenses.
What I love about a software startup is that when you're bored writing code, you can fool around with stuff like the USPS web site and ordering padded envelopes.
posts.key_insight: Building custom software to avoid one set of limitations often leads to eventually needing to implement the very features you originally bypassed.

posts.summary: This is a short announcement post where Joel Spolsky promotes three upcoming appearances in mid-March 2005: a speech at O'Reilly's Emerging Technology Conference in San Diego, a fireside chat at Software Development West in Silicon Valley, and a pizza and beer reception hosted by Apress in Berkeley. He jokes about his reputation for going off-topic during speeches and humorously describes the uncertain actual content of his talk. The post is essentially a personal events calendar with Joel's characteristic informal humor.
key_points: Joel is speaking at O'Reilly's Emerging Technology Conference but admits the actual topic will diverge from the official one about building communities with software
He acknowledges his reputation for giving speeches that don't stick to the announced topic
He's doing a fireside chat at Software Development West with free expo passes available online before a deadline
money_quotes: I am gaining something of a reputation for giving speeches which are not precisely on topic.
The actual topic of the speech is too hard to pin down. We'll look at pictures, I'll tell some jokes, and if the A/V works right there will be music too.
I don't know if they are actually going to have a fireplace; we might have to burn twigs and promotional literature on stage.
posts.key_insight: This is a lightweight event announcement post with no substantive technical or business content, notable mainly for Joel's self-aware humor about his tendency to go off-topic during talks.

posts.summary: Joel briefly comments on Microsoft's VB.NET breaking changes that alienated VB6 developers, linking to a petition against the changes. He quotes himself from an eWeek article warning that such incompatibilities could erode Microsoft's desktop monopoly. However, he then issues a correction admitting his specific example about short-circuit boolean evaluation was wrong — VB.NET's And/Or operators were not actually changed to short-circuit. He acknowledges real incompatibilities exist but concedes his cited example wasn't one of them.
key_points: Microsoft's breaking changes between VB6 and VB.NET alienated a large developer community
Joel argued these incompatibilities could threaten Microsoft's desktop monopoly
His specific technical example about short-circuit boolean operators was incorrect, and he publicly corrected himself
The willingness to issue a prompt, clear correction demonstrates intellectual honesty
money_quotes: And this is how Microsoft will lose their desktop monopoly: because some bright bulb at Microsoft thought Boolean operations should really short-circuit, no matter what millions of BASIC developers had been doing since the 1960s.
This is a bad example, since the boolean operators I was thinking of (And and Or) were not changed to short circuit in VB.Net.
I have no idea why I've been thinking that they were for so long.
posts.key_insight: Breaking backward compatibility with a massive existing developer base is a strategic risk for platform monopolies, though critics should ensure their specific technical complaints are accurate.

posts.summary: Spolsky discusses the problems Microsoft created by breaking backward compatibility in the transition from VB6 to VB.NET. He recounts his own 1992 attempt to convince the VBA team to remove the Set statement and default properties, noting they rightly refused to break existing code. He argues that when VB.NET forced breaking changes on developers with large codebases, it created a painful dilemma: spend money just to stand still, or consider abandoning the platform entirely. The post suggests Microsoft's strategy of successive breaking changes (VB to VB.NET to WinForms to Avalon) drove developers to seek more stable alternatives.
key_points: The VBA team in the early 1990s correctly prioritized backward compatibility over language cleanup, even for widely disliked features like the Set statement

Breaking changes in VB.NET forced companies with large codebases into a lose-lose choice: stay on an increasingly unsupported VB6 or spend significant money migrating without gaining new functionality
Companies forced to spend money just to stand still will rationally evaluate competing platforms rather than staying locked to a single vendor
Microsoft's pattern of successive platform shifts (VB6 → VB.NET/WinForms → Avalon) compounded developer distrust and accelerated platform abandonment
money_quotes: The Basic team absolutely refused to do anything that would break working code.

If you have a million line code base that's mission critical, as many companies do, and VB suddenly changes, as it did, you have a choice: keep using VB 6 or spend a lot of time (=money) upgrading to VB.NET.
You just spent a lot of money to stand still. And companies don't like to spend a lot of money to stand still.
While you're spending the money, it probably makes sense to consider the alternatives that you can port to that won't put you at the mercy of a single vendor.

As soon as people with large code bases start hearing that they're going to have to work to port their apps from VB to VB.NET with WinForms, and then they start hearing that WinForms isn't really the future, the future is really this Avalon thing nobody has yet, they start wondering whether it isn't time to find another development platform.
posts.key_insight: When a platform vendor forces breaking changes that cost money without adding value, they don't just inconvenience developers — they give them a rational reason to leave the platform entirely.

posts.summary: This is a very brief announcement post where Joel Spolsky shares two pieces of news: FogBugz 3.1 won a Jolt Award in the Defect Tracking Tools category, and his book 'Joel on Software' won a Productivity Award. The post is essentially a celebration of these achievements with no substantive argument or analysis.
key_points: FogBugz 3.1 won the Jolt Award for Defect Tracking Tools
Joel's book 'Joel on Software' won the Productivity Award
money_quotes: Congratulations to the whole FogBugz team on winning the Jolt Award in the category of Defect Tracking Tools for FogBugz 3.1.
posts.key_insight: A brief announcement celebrating industry recognition for both FogBugz and Joel's book with Jolt Awards.

posts.summary: This is a very brief news post where Joel Spolsky shares links to four reviews of his talk at the O'Reilly Etech conference, noting that the reviews ranged from 'off topic' to 'a bit fluffy.' He also promotes a pizza and beer reception at Apress in Berkeley. The post is essentially a quick link roundup with no substantive argument or analysis.
key_points: Joel's Etech talk received mixed reviews, with descriptions ranging from 'off topic' to 'somewhere in between'
Joel curates and shares critical reviews of his own talk with apparent good humor
The post doubles as a promotion for an Apress meetup event in Berkeley
posts.key_insight: This is a minor housekeeping post with no substantive insight, serving only as a link roundup for conference talk reviews and an event announcement.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Joel Spolsky introduces a five-part series covering the development of FogBugz 4.0. He teases that the first installment will discuss major features added based on customer feedback. The post highlights Fog Creek's philosophy of prioritizing customer input over competitor analysis. It's essentially a table of contents entry rather than a substantive essay.
key_points: FogBugz 4.0 development will be covered in a five-part behind-the-scenes series
New features were driven by customer feedback rather than competitive analysis
Fog Creek's mantra is to listen to customers and ignore competitors
money_quotes: Our mantra is to listen to our customers and ignore our competitors.
posts.key_insight: Product development should be driven by customer feedback rather than competitor imitation.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky briefly announces two new FogBugz 4.0 features — Bayesian spam filtering and a 'snippets' feature for canned responses — that emerged from Fog Creek's internal use of their own product for handling company email. The post serves as a teaser directing readers to a longer article about the FogBugz 4.0 development process.
key_points: Fog Creek uses FogBugz internally to handle company email, practicing 'dogfooding'
Internal usage directly motivated two new features: Bayesian spam filtering and message snippets
Dogfooding is presented as a reliable product development methodology for discovering needed features
money_quotes: the process of using FogBugz ourselves ("eating our own dogfood") motivated us to add Bayesian spam filtering, and a "snippets" feature to make it easy to enter common phrases and even entire messages in replies to frequently-asked questions
posts.key_insight: Using your own product internally (dogfooding) is an effective way to discover and prioritize features that real users need.

posts.summary: Spolsky describes two features added to FogBugz 4.0 that emerged from using their own product. First, after discovering that human operators were incorrectly marking real customer emails as spam at a higher rate than Bayesian filters would, they implemented client-side Bayesian filtering directly in FogBugz, then generalized it to automatically sort incoming email into categories. Second, after noticing that terse customer service replies were perceived as rude, they built a 'snippets' feature for inserting canned text fragments to produce warmer, more polished responses efficiently.
key_points: Dogfooding revealed that human operators had a 1% false positive rate classifying spam, far worse than Bayesian filtering's 0.01%, making the case that automated filtering is more reliable than manual sorting for high-volume email
Bayesian filtering must be implemented in the email client rather than the server because it needs to learn what legitimate email looks like for a specific user or organization
The Bayesian classifier was generalized beyond spam detection into a tournament-based document sorter that could categorize email into arbitrary buckets with over 90% accuracy
Terse but technically correct customer service emails are often perceived as rude, and the solution is templated snippets that add warmth without requiring manual effort each time
Both features exemplify how using your own product surfaces real problems that lead to genuinely useful improvements
money_quotes: While everybody is worried about how spam filters might inadvertently delete the proverbial crucial email from a customer, in practice, in the presence of lots of spam, human beings are far more likely to delete a real email than a well-implemented Bayesian filter.

Here I am paying a college graduate to go through email and decide whether or not it's spam, and he's flagging 1% of email incorrectly, when there's this algorithm called Bayesian filtering which in my experience has far fewer false positives.
This paper is the ultimate rebuttal to those grumpy people who email me, barely able to conceal their disgust, saying, 'why do you need to hire such smart people to work on bug tracking software?'
The cool thing about the implementation in FogBugz is that if the sorter makes a mistake, it's OK. You just correct it and get on with your life, and it learns from its mistake.
The moral of the story is that given two email messages with the same semantic content, the terse one is more likely to come across sounding rude.
It's sort of like translating from English to Japanese. You would hear four or five minutes of nonstop Japanese and then the English translation would be 'The train to Osaka is on platform 4.'
posts.key_insight: Using your own product reveals problems that purely analytical thinking misses — in this case, that humans are worse than algorithms at filtering spam, and that technically correct but terse communication fails socially.

posts.summary: Joel briefly announces that Fog Creek built a compiler to automatically convert FogBugz's ASP source code to PHP, enabling the product to run on Unix in addition to Windows. Rather than maintaining two separate codebases or doing a manual one-time port, they chose to build an automated translation tool. The post serves as a teaser directing readers to a longer article detailing this technical approach.
key_points: FogBugz needed to support both Unix and Windows platforms
Rather than manually porting ASP to PHP, Fog Creek built an ASP-to-PHP compiler
The automated approach avoids the maintenance burden of two separate codebases
money_quotes: Rather than do a one-time port, we built a compiler that automatically generates a PHP version from the ASP source code.
posts.key_insight: When faced with cross-platform requirements, building an automated source-to-source compiler can be preferable to maintaining separate codebases or doing a manual port.

posts.summary: Joel describes the creation of Thistle, Fog Creek's ASP-to-PHP compiler, built to produce a Unix version of FogBugz from their existing Windows/ASP codebase. A summer intern named Jimmy prototyped the compiler in Java, which lexes, parses, and generates PHP from VBScript/ASP source code. Joel explains why Thistle is a true compiler (not a translator or regex hack), defends Hungarian notation as a coding convention that enabled key compiler optimizations, and argues the effort was worthwhile despite Unix sales being only 10% of revenue. The post also details how they handled COM objects and runtime libraries through reimplementation.
key_points: Thistle is a genuine compiler with lexer, parser, and AST that outputs PHP instead of machine code, analogous to how Cfront compiled C++ by outputting C

The compiler exploits Fog Creek's Hungarian notation conventions (e.g., 'rs' prefix for recordsets) to resolve ambiguous VBScript expressions that would otherwise require expensive runtime type checks
Thistle was purpose-built for one program (FogBugz) and takes shortcuts based on their coding conventions, making it unsuitable as a general-purpose commercial product
The Unix port cost roughly 20% additional effort but generated 10% of sales, plus gave Windows customers confidence they could switch platforms
Joel strongly advocates Apps Hungarian notation (semantic prefixes) as invented by Simonyi, distinguishing it from the 'grotesque' Systems Hungarian misinterpretation
money_quotes: In computer science jargon a translator IS a compiler. It's exactly the same thing. Those are synonyms.

I've been calling Thistle a 'compiler' sort of to make a point, but mostly because I'm a pedantic windbag who can't resist the opportunity to teach a little lesson to the younguns who think that a compiler has to generate machine code.

Based on your age you will either call this an evil hack (if you're young) or an elegant hack (if you're old); in either case it's a huge optimization made possible by the fact that Thistle only has one program to compile.

Thanks to Hungarian notation, so callously dissed by developers who would not recognize a superb coding convention if it walked up to them on the Shanghai Maglev train and shook their pants leg, every recordset at Fog Creek starts with the letters 'rs'.

I'm especially driven crazy by people who keep blabbing on their blogs that Hungarian makes code 'less' readable. Yes, it's less readable if you don't know the convention, but it takes about 10 minutes to learn the convention.

We had a hard-and-fast requirement that the generated PHP code had to work without further tweaks. That's because the ASP code is a living thing which changes all the time, and we constantly regenerate the PHP code automatically.
It would be a very simple matter to change Thistle to generate the new flavor of the week… be it Ruby.Net, Eiffel, C#, or this Language.
posts.key_insight: Building a purpose-built compiler that exploits your own coding conventions can be a pragmatic way to achieve cross-platform compatibility from a single codebase, even when a general-purpose solution would be impractical.

posts.summary: This is a very brief news post linking to Part Four of a series about FogBugz development. The sole substantive claim is a striking statistic: only 2% of Fog Creek's total effort goes into writing new code that ships to customers. The post uses this dramatic ratio to draw readers into the longer article about the realities of software product development.
key_points: Only 2 out of every 100 calories of effort at Fog Creek go toward writing new shipping code
The vast majority of software development effort is consumed by activities other than writing new code
The post serves as a teaser linking to a deeper exploration of where development effort actually goes
money_quotes: Out of every 100 calories expended by the Fog Creek team, just 2 calories are spent on actually writing new lines of code that ship to a customer.
posts.key_insight: Writing new code that ships is a tiny fraction of the total effort required to develop and maintain a software product.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky breaks down the energy expenditure of shipping commercial software, arguing that writing code is only about 2% of the total effort. The bulk goes to customer service, debugging, marketing, and hiring. He details the non-code work required to launch FogBugz 4.0, including professional graphic design, an online demo, a product movie, a marketing website, a published book, and physical CD-ROMs. The post illustrates how consumers evaluate products based on superficial signals like aesthetics, reputation, and tangible artifacts rather than the software itself. Spolsky argues this explains both why programmer-founded companies often fail and why expensive shelfware succeeds.
key_points: Only about 2% of the energy in shipping commercial software goes to writing new code; the rest is customer service, debugging, marketing, hiring, and non-shipping code.
Consumers evaluate software products through proxy signals like website quality, reputation, community discussion, and aesthetics rather than the product's actual functionality.
People ridiculously overvalue aesthetics when evaluating products, creating an enormous handicap for functional but ugly software.
Making a product 'feel real' requires tangible artifacts like published books, physical CD-ROMs, and professional design, even if few customers use them.
Programmer-founded software companies often fail because programmers only know how to do the 2% (writing code) and lack skills in marketing, customer service, and design.
money_quotes: A ridiculously small portion of the energy it takes to make a commercial software product actually goes into the writing of lines of code.

This, I think, helps explain why so many software companies started by programmers fail: programmers are really good at writing news line of code, they might be good at debugging, but nobody ever taught them how to do marketing or customer service, and they are probably horrible at graphic design.
Oh, and if they have a few minutes left over, they might actually look at your product itself to see if it works.
Human emotions can be really, really superficial. In particular people ridiculously overvalue aesthetics and beauty when evaluating products.

If you have a product that is functional but not beautiful you have a really, really, really, really, really, really, really, really, really, really big handicap to overcome against the product that is beautiful but maybe not so functional.
A real book from a real publishing house is one of those things that makes a product 'feel' real.
I can't tell why but somehow having the option of a physical product makes the software feel more tangible and therefore worth more money.
posts.key_insight: Shipping successful commercial software is overwhelmingly about non-code work — design, marketing, customer service, and creating tangible artifacts that make the product 'feel real' — which is why programmer-founded companies that focus only on code often fail.

posts.summary: This is a minimal announcement post from April 1, 2005, directing readers to the conclusion of Joel's 'The Road to FogBugz' series. The post contains virtually no substantive content of its own, serving purely as a link to another article. At only 9 words, it functions as a blog-era equivalent of a tweet or status update.
key_points: Directs readers to the final installment of 'The Road to FogBugz' series
The word 'shocking' suggests the conclusion contains a surprising reveal or twist
Published on April 1st, which may indicate the 'shocking' label is tongue-in-cheek
money_quotes: Today, the shocking conclusion of The Road To FogBugz.
posts.key_insight: This is a stub announcement post with no independent analytical content, serving only to link readers to the conclusion of a serialized article about FogBugz development.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky recounts the launch of FogBugz 4.0 for Windows on February 23, 2005, after deciding not to hold up the Windows release for the Unix version. Despite expecting to relax after shipping, he was immediately overwhelmed by tripled sales, lit-up phone lines, and a flood of inquiries. He reflects on the lesson that shipping software is the beginning of the work, not the end. The post also surveys Fog Creek's growing plate of responsibilities: large enterprise customers wanting customizations, server infrastructure upgrades, an ambitious summer internship program to build a product from scratch (with a documentary crew filming), and urgent hiring needs.
key_points: Joel decided to ship the Windows version (90% of market) without waiting for Unix, prioritizing the majority of customers over a synchronized launch.
Post-launch demand tripled weekly sales and overwhelmed the team, reinforcing that shipping is the start of a new phase, not the finish line.
He endorsed Eric Sink's hiring philosophy of not filling positions until the need is painfully clear, even though it left them short-staffed during the surge.
Large enterprise customers began requesting customizations with enough money attached to make refusing irrational.
Fog Creek's summer internship program aimed to have four interns build and launch a revenue-generating product in one summer, while being filmed for a documentary.
money_quotes: I should know by now that shipping software is the beginning, not the end.
We promptly got whacked. The phone lines lit up like a New Mexico license plate frame. Our weekly sales tripled.
Don't fill a position until after the need for that position is painfully clear.
I was really hoping to ship FogBugz for Windows and FogBugz for Unix on the same day, but it just wasn't gonna happen.
We're starting to get the kind of gigantic customers who want things just a little bit different, and they're willing to pay so much money for it you'd be insane not to give in.
My David Brent streak is a mile long and will need to be actively surpressed.
posts.key_insight: Shipping software is just the beginning — the real challenge is surviving the surge of demand, growth, and new obligations that follow a successful launch.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces a new job posting discussion group on his website. The group is framed as an experiment in online discussion forums, with the guiding principle that quality of job postings matters more than quantity. This is a brief announcement post with minimal elaboration.
key_points: Joel launched a dedicated job posting discussion group on his site
He frames it as part of an ongoing series of experiments with discussion group formats
The guiding philosophy is that quality matters more than quantity in job listings
money_quotes: Quality is more important than quantity.
posts.key_insight: A curated, quality-focused job board can serve a community better than one that prioritizes volume.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares a brief testimonial from Eric J. Smith, who discovered through reading about FogBugz that it could handle all of his product management needs for CodeSmith. The post serves as a quick endorsement highlighting the marketing value of having quality written content about your product.
key_points: A well-written book about your product serves as powerful marketing that converts readers into customers
FogBugz positioned itself as an all-in-one tool for support, sales, bug tracking, and project management
Third-party testimonials and organic discovery stories are effective social proof
money_quotes: Having a great book about your product, by a great author, pays off in spades.
posts.key_insight: Quality written content about your product can be one of the most effective marketing tools, leading potential customers to discover its value on their own.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief link-sharing post where Joel Spolsky shares a link to JunkFax.org warning that Congress is about to legalize junk faxes with opt-out provisions, similar to what was done with spam legislation. The post contains no original analysis or argument, serving purely as a signal boost for the linked advocacy site. It reflects Joel's practice of using his blog platform to occasionally share news items he found noteworthy or concerning.
key_points: Congress was considering legislation that would legalize junk faxes as long as advertisers include an opt-out notice
Joel draws a parallel between this proposed fax legislation and existing spam legislation that similarly legitimized unwanted commercial email
The post is a pure link-share with no original commentary beyond the quote
money_quotes: Congress is about to do for junk faxes what they recently did for spam: Make it LEGAL as long as a qualified advertiser puts an 'opt out' notice on the faxes!
posts.key_insight: This is a minor link-sharing post with no substantive original content to analyze.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Joel Spolsky promotes two upcoming speeches at CFUNITED, a ColdFusion conference. The first talk will be a keynote about why great products like the iPod succeed despite being more expensive and less capable than competitors. The second talk covers project management using his 'Joel Test' framework, with a demo of FogBugz 4.0. The post is essentially a conference promotion with minimal substantive content.
key_points: Joel will deliver a keynote arguing that product greatness isn't about having the most features or lowest price, using the iPod as a case study
His second talk will cover 'The Joel Test' for evaluating software team quality, with a FogBugz 4.0 demo
The post hints at Joel's recurring thesis that user experience and design trump feature checklists
money_quotes: I'll talk about Really Great Products and why the iPod is so doggone popular even though it's more expensive, and less capable, than a lot of other products that have failed in the marketplace.
Also, as usual, there will be jokes, music, and pictures of Jennifer Aniston.
posts.key_insight: Great products win not by being cheaper or more feature-rich, but by delivering a superior overall experience.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky enthusiastically reviews VMware Workstation 5.0, highlighting its new snapshot tree feature as a major advancement for software testing. He explains how the ability to create branching snapshots with different configurations saves disk space and dramatically speeds up testing workflows. Using FogBugz as an example, he demonstrates how VMware enables testing prerequisite detection code and upgrade paths by quickly reverting to known states. He also notes VMware's role in cross-platform Linux testing and describes Fog Creek's hardware setup for intern projects requiring multi-machine environments.
key_points: VMware 5.0's snapshot tree feature allows branching configurations similar to object-oriented inheritance, saving disk space when testing systems with small variations
Virtual machines are essential for testing installer prerequisite detection by creating environments that deliberately lack specific components
Snapshots enable rapid iteration on upgrade testing by letting developers instantly revert to a clean pre-upgrade state after failures
VMware is critical for cross-platform development, allowing testing across multiple Linux distributions from a single workstation
Fog Creek invested in high-end dual-Xeon workstations with 2GB RAM to support running multiple VMs simultaneously for multi-machine development environments
money_quotes: It has the very slick ability to store multiple snapshots and build up a tree of systems with slightly different configurations, sort of like object-oriented inheritence.
In order to develop and test that code, I need virtual machines that are missing the prerequisites, so I can test all the code paths.
Now I run my upgrade code on that, and, of course, it fails, thanks to Murphy's law, but it leaves FogBugz in a "half-upgraded" state.
The brilliant part of VMware is that I can click to go back to the 2.0 snapshot and in about 30 seconds I'm back to a pristine FogBugz 2.0 installation.
In about one minute I can be running a pristine copy of Windows 2000 that looks just like the day it was installed.
posts.key_insight: Virtual machine snapshots with branching configurations transform software testing by making it trivial to create, destroy, and switch between precisely controlled environments.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces several pieces of Fog Creek news, primarily that a documentary filmmaker will be embedded in their office to film their summer internship program. Four interns selected from over 800 applicants will build a complete software product from concept to paying customers in about ten weeks. The work is divided into three programming chunks and one product marketing role, with the product being something Fog Creek needs internally. Joel frames the project as inexpensive market research that doubles as a real product development effort.
key_points: Fog Creek received over 800 applications for just four summer intern positions, reflecting their highly competitive hiring process
Rather than assigning interns busywork, Fog Creek is having them build a complete software product from scratch in one summer, mentored by experienced developers
The product is scoped to be a minimum viable 1.0 version, with three coding chunks each comparable to a challenging university programming assignment
One intern focuses entirely on product marketing — branding, pricing, website, advertising, and PR — showing Fog Creek treats the business side as equal to engineering
The product serves an internal need, so even if it fails commercially the investment isn't wasted
money_quotes: Instead of wasting their talents giving them the usual dull and unimportant tasks of a typical summer internship, we decided to let the interns create a complete new software product, from beginning to end, over the course of one summer.

With experienced software developers as mentors, the team will design, program, test, and roll out a complete software product over the course of one hectic summer, going from concept to paying customers in about ten weeks.
I'll be the first to admit that this product will be a 1.0 version… the minimum thing that can possibly work.

The product we're building is something we need internally anyway, so it's almost worth the expense even if nobody buys it, but we think it should be useful to a lot of other people, too, so I see this as fairly inexpensive market research.
Our selection process was extremely competitive, with over 800 kids applying for only four positions.
posts.key_insight: The best internships treat interns as real contributors shipping real products, not as cheap labor doing throwaway tasks.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that the highest level of programming skill is deliberately architecting code so that bugs are visually obvious during normal reading. Using a bakery cleanliness analogy, he shows how experienced programmers develop eyes for 'dirty' code, then demonstrates with a web security (XSS) example how naming conventions—specifically Apps Hungarian notation—can make wrong code look wrong on every single line. He rehabilitates Hungarian notation by distinguishing Simonyi's original 'Apps Hungarian' (semantic prefixes like 'us' for unsafe string) from the widely-despised 'Systems Hungarian' (type prefixes like 'dw' for double word), arguing the former was misunderstood and discarded along with the latter. He also critiques exceptions as violating the principle of collocation—keeping all relevant information visible in one place.
key_points: There are four levels of code cleanliness perception: not seeing problems, seeing style violations, smelling subtle issues, and deliberately designing conventions that make bugs visible

Apps Hungarian notation (semantic prefixes indicating the 'kind' of data) was Simonyi's original intent and is genuinely valuable, while Systems Hungarian (data type prefixes) was a misunderstanding that deserved its bad reputation
The core principle is collocation: keeping all information needed to verify correctness physically close together on screen, so your eyes can catch bugs during normal code reading
Naming conventions like 'us' for unsafe strings and 's' for safe strings let you verify XSS safety line-by-line without tracing data flow across files
Exceptions violate collocation by hiding control flow, making it impossible to verify correctness by looking at local code alone
money_quotes: This is the real art: making robust code by literally inventing conventions that make errors stand out on the screen.
Every line of code can be inspected by itself, and if every line of code is correct, the entire body of code is correct.
I'm using the word kind on purpose, there, because Simonyi mistakenly used the word type in his paper, and generations of programmers misunderstood what he meant.

This was a subtle but complete misunderstanding of Simonyi's intention and practice, and it just goes to show you that if you write convoluted, dense academic prose nobody will understand it and your ideas will be misinterpreted and then the misinterpreted ideas will be ridiculed even when they weren't your ideas.

The way to write really reliable code is to try to use simple tools that take into account typical human frailty, not complex tools with hidden side effects and leaky abstractions that assume an infallible programmer.
In general, I have to admit that I'm a little bit scared of language features that hide things.
The more information about what code is doing is located right in front of your eyes, the better a job you'll do at finding the mistakes.
posts.key_insight: The highest form of code quality comes not from external tools or language features, but from deliberately designing naming conventions and code structure so that bugs become visually obvious during normal reading.

posts.summary: This brief post serves as a teaser for Joel's longer essay 'Making Wrong Code Look Wrong.' It consists entirely of a single extended quote from that essay, humorously describing the futile cycle of trying to enforce coding conventions on a team. The passage captures how developers spiral from recognizing inconsistency into bikeshedding arguments, then into unauthorized refactoring, then into managerial scolding, and finally into compromised half-measures before moving on to the next irrelevant obsession.
key_points: Attempts to standardize coding conventions inevitably devolve into bikeshedding arguments about trivial details like brace style
Developers tend to waste time on code aesthetics that have no direct business value, drawing managerial ire
The compromise of 'only reformat when you revisit' leads to inconsistent half-measures that are eventually abandoned
Programmers have a tendency to cycle through irrelevant obsessions (brace style, string classes) instead of focusing on what matters
money_quotes: Blistering Barnacles, we've *got* to get some consistent coding conventions around here!
you spend the next day writing up coding conventions for your team and the next six days arguing about the One True Brace Style
a manager catches you and screams at you for wasting time on something that can never make money
you decide that it's not really a bad thing to only reformat code when you revisit it, so you have about half of a True Brace Style
pretty soon you forget all about that and then you can start obsessing about something else irrelevant to making money like replacing one kind of string class with another kind of string class
posts.key_insight: Developers instinctively gravitate toward cosmetic code consistency battles that feel productive but produce no business value, cycling endlessly from one irrelevant obsession to the next.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Joel congratulates former intern Ben Kamens on graduating and promotes his upcoming cross-country bicycle trip for charity. Joel mentions that Kamens has built a text-message-map-gateway to track the trip's progress. The post is essentially a personal shout-out rather than a substantive essay.
key_points: Joel highlights the career pipeline from intern to future employee at Fog Creek
Ben Kamens built a technical solution (text-message-map tracking) for a non-technical endeavor
The post illustrates Joel's practice of using his popular blog to boost his employees' personal projects
money_quotes: He's rigged up some kind of text-message-map-gateway thingamajig so you can track their progress.
posts.key_insight: A short personal announcement with no substantive technical or business content, reflecting Joel's habit of using his blog platform to support his team's endeavors.

posts.summary: This is a brief news post with two items. First, Joel shares a podcast interview with Dan Bricklin (co-creator of VisiCalc) covering topics like GPL compliance, open source economics, and copyright awareness among developers. Second, Joel shares a short anecdote about being warned about a Wall Street bond scam by his friend Jared, only to fall for it himself shortly after.
key_points: Joel discussed GPL compliance and the importance of developers tracking where their code comes from in an interview with Dan Bricklin
He argued that developers who read Slashdot and Eric Raymond are more copyright-aware than their managers
Joel shared a self-deprecating story about falling for a Wall Street bond scam despite having it explained to him beforehand
money_quotes: developers who read Slashdot and Eric Raymond are more likely to be at least somewhat aware of copyright issues than their managers who don't
Remember that scam I explained to you last month?
Well, you just fell for it!
posts.key_insight: This is a lightweight news post linking to external content rather than making a substantive argument, though it touches on the theme that understanding a risk intellectually doesn't prevent you from falling victim to it.

posts.summary: This is a brief product announcement post where Joel Spolsky announces that FogBugz 4.0 is now available for Unix and Macintosh platforms. The post consists of a single sentence with a link to the product's what's-new page. It signals Fog Creek's expansion beyond Windows-only support. There is no argument or analysis — it is purely a news update.
key_points: FogBugz 4.0 expanded platform support to Unix and Macintosh
The use of 'finally' suggests this cross-platform release was long-awaited by users
Fog Creek was actively broadening its market beyond Windows developers
money_quotes: FogBugz 4.0 for Unix and Macintosh is finally shipping!
posts.key_insight: A minimal product announcement marking FogBugz's expansion to non-Windows platforms.

posts.summary: This is a brief news post linking to a Project Aardvark blog entry by an intern discussing their initial impressions of C#. The linked post describes approaching C# with low expectations based on prior experience with Microsoft languages like Visual Basic, while expressing a preference for dynamic languages like Squeak Smalltalk and Ruby. The post is essentially a signal boost for the Project Aardvark intern blog rather than containing original analysis from Spolsky.
key_points: Project Aardvark interns were working with C# despite personal preferences for dynamic languages
The post reflects a common developer sentiment of the mid-2000s: skepticism toward Microsoft languages from those who preferred dynamic or academic languages
Spolsky used his blog to promote and give visibility to his interns' writing on Project Aardvark
money_quotes: I initially approached C# with a bit of trepidation.
Anyone who knows me well knows that I'd much rather be working in one of the dynamic languages—especially Squeak Smalltalk or Ruby—than in any of the C descendents.
My previous experiences with Microsoft languages (VisualBasic in all of its many varieties) did not exactly raise my expectations, and I was prepared for the worst.
posts.key_insight: This is a lightweight link post promoting an intern's blog entry, reflecting the mid-2000s tension between dynamic language enthusiasts and the Microsoft development ecosystem.

posts.summary: This is a brief news post from Joel Spolsky sharing a quote from Benjamin Pollack about encountering an Internal Compiler Error when trying to build a project that claimed to work fine with Visual Studio .NET 2003. The post also includes a promotional reminder about FogBugz 4.0 introductory pricing ending that day.
key_points: Software that claims to 'build just fine' often doesn't, especially across different environments
Internal Compiler Errors represent the most frustrating class of build failures because they indicate bugs in the toolchain itself, not your code
The post doubles as a promotional reminder for FogBugz 4.0's introductory pricing deadline
money_quotes: I hit F5, the build starts, and then dies with my least favorite error message in the entire world: Internal Compiler Error.
posts.key_insight: Even when documentation promises a smooth build experience, Internal Compiler Errors remain the most dreaded and uncontrollable failures a developer can encounter.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief news post where Joel links to a blog entry from one of his summer interns working on Project Aardvark. The intern describes putting network handshaking code inside a taskbar icon class and tries to justify the decision. Joel's entire commentary is a single sarcastic 'Riiight,' expressing amused skepticism at the intern's architectural choice.
key_points: Joel showcases intern blog posts from Project Aardvark, a documentary project about software development
The intern placed network connection code inside a UI taskbar icon class and tried to rationalize it
Joel's one-word sarcastic response gently mocks the questionable separation of concerns
money_quotes: I initially attached some new connection-specific handshaking code inside a class that controlled the windows taskbar icon for our program.
That probably sounds a bit silly, but it actually made a reasonable amount of sense.
Riiight.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes code architecture decisions that developers convince themselves are reasonable are transparently questionable to everyone else.

posts.summary: This post covers two unrelated topics: Microsoft's recruiting challenges and Fog Creek's network load balancing setup. On recruiting, Spolsky argues that Microsoft's problems attracting talent stem from executive-level decisions — stagnant products, bloated headcount, benefit cuts, and poor developer experience — not from recruiter competence. On the technical side, he describes successfully using Windows Server 2003's built-in Network Load Balancing to apply patches to his web farm with zero downtime, preferring it over hardware load balancers for its lack of a single point of failure.
key_points: Recruiting failures at large companies are caused by executive decisions (product stagnation, benefit cuts, poor work environment), not by recruiter incompetence
Microsoft's enormous market cap means even massive revenue growth translates to modest percentage gains, making stock-based compensation unappealing to recruits
Software-based network load balancing eliminates the single point of failure inherent in hardware load balancers
A drainstop approach to rolling updates allows zero-downtime patching across a web server cluster
money_quotes: The best recruiting department in the world can't make people want to work at a company that's moribund.
Summer interns at Fog Creek have better chairs, monitors, and computers than the most senior Microsoft programmers.
Recruiting has to be done at the Bill and Steve level, not at the Gretchen level.
No matter how good a recruiter you are, you can't compensate for working at a company that people don't want to work for.

You can be the best recruiter in the world and the talent landscape is not going to look very inviting if the executives at your company have spent the last years focusing on cutting benefits, cutting off oxygen supplies, and cutting features from Longhorn.
MSFT can grow by an entire Google every year and still see less than 7% growth in earnings.
posts.key_insight: No amount of recruiting skill can overcome an organization whose leadership has made it an unappealing place to work through stagnant products, bloated headcount, and eroded benefits.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky discusses Microsoft's recruiting challenges, responding to a Microsoft recruiter's candid admission that hiring managers don't understand the competitive talent landscape. He argues that solving recruiting problems requires CEO-level structural changes—like opening offices in desirable cities, spinning off divisions into startup-like units, and giving employees meaningful equity—not just better recruiting tactics. He draws a parallel to Seth Godin's point that marketing is the CEO's job, arguing recruiting is too. The post also touches on his email bankruptcy and Project Aardvark's progress.
key_points: Microsoft's recruiting problems stem from structural and cultural issues that only top leadership (Bill Gates, Steve Ballmer) can fix, not recruiters or hiring managers
To attract top talent, Microsoft should open offices in desirable cities, break into smaller startup-like units, and offer meaningful equity tied to individual products
Recruiting, like marketing, is fundamentally the CEO's job because only the CEO can drive the structural changes that make a company attractive
Even Microsoft's own recruiters acknowledge the company doesn't understand it's competing for talent in a landscape where big-company life in Redmond isn't universally appealing
money_quotes: Recruiting has to be done at the Bill and Steve level, not at the Gretchen level.
A software company has to think of recruiting the right people as its number one problem.
None of the kind of decisions that would make Microsoft an even more attractive workplace are in the hands of the recruiting department or even the hiring managers.
Spin off X-Box so it feels more like a cool gaming startup rather than a big corporate 'General Motors Trying to Sell Hip Things to an Appealing Demographic.'
Marketing is the CEO's job, since that's the only person who can really drive the kinds of changes that the public cares about. And recruiting is the CEO's job, too.
Brilliant software engineers don't grow on trees.
posts.key_insight: Recruiting top software talent is a CEO-level strategic problem that requires structural changes to how a company operates and where it locates, not just better hiring processes.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky introduces his anthology 'The Best Software Writing I' by making a passionate case for better writing in the software industry. He illustrates the power of storytelling through a New York City anecdote and a military leadership story, contrasting vivid narrative with dry, abstract advice. He recounts refusing to endorse a book that was technically correct but fatally boring because it violated the 'show, don't tell' rule. The piece argues that software writing suffers from broken ESL, faux-academic pretentiousness, and a complete absence of narrative, and positions the anthology as a corrective showcase of genuinely good writing about software.
key_points: The 'show, don't tell' rule is the most important principle of good writing, and most software books violate it completely by offering abstract advice without stories or examples.
A vivid 400-word story about a Sergeant Major cleaning a toilet communicates leadership more effectively than any number of platitudes about 'setting a positive example.'
The software development world is drowning in poorly written material: bloated class library books by committee, pretentious academic texts, and juvenile online rants.
Technical correctness and clear prose are necessary but insufficient — without narrative and engagement, a book is unconvincing and unreadable.
Good writing about software exists but is scattered across blogs and articles, and deserves to be collected and showcased.
money_quotes: The author had violated the number one rule of good writing, the 'Show, don't tell' rule. There was not a single story in the book.
It was chock full of sentences like 'A good team leader provides inspiration by setting a positive example.' What the eff?
I'll bet you'd rather sit through ten of those 400 word stories than have to listen to someone drone on about how 'a good team leader provides inspiration by setting a positive example.'
If I have to read another 2000 page book about some class library written by 16 separate people in broken ESL, I'm going to flip out.

If I see another hardback book about object oriented models written with dense faux-academic pretentiousness, I'm not going to shelve it any more in the Fog Creek library: it's going right in the recycle bin.
The software development world desperately needs better writing.

New York is the kind of place where ten things happen to you every day on the way to the subway that would have qualified as interesting dinner conversation in Bloomington, Indiana, and you don't pay them any notice.
posts.key_insight: Software writing fails not because authors lack technical knowledge but because they lack storytelling — showing through concrete narrative is infinitely more persuasive than telling through abstract platitudes.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces his curated anthology 'The Best Software Writing I' by lamenting the poor state of software development writing. He criticizes overly long technical books written by committees in broken English, pretentious academic texts about object-oriented design, and low-quality online commentary. The post serves as both a promotional announcement and a manifesto for better technical writing. Joel positions his anthology as the antidote, promising 29 pieces of brilliant, insightful, and funny writing about software.
key_points: Technical books written by large committees of non-native English speakers produce poor, bloated results
Academic pretentiousness in software writing obscures rather than illuminates
Online commentary (e.g., Slashdot) often lacks the maturity and depth needed for useful technical criticism
The software industry deserves and needs higher-quality writing about its craft
money_quotes: The software development world desperately needs better writing.
If I have to read another 2000 page book about some class library written by 16 separate people in broken ESL, I'm going to flip out.

If I see another hardback book about object oriented models written with dense faux-academic pretentiousness, I'm not going to shelve it any more in the Fog Creek library: it's going right in the recycle bin.
If I have to read another spirited attack on Microsoft's buggy code by an enthusiastic nine year old Trekkie on Slashdot, I might just poke my eyes out with a sharpened pencil.
Stop it, stop it, stop it!
posts.key_insight: The software industry suffers from a glut of poorly written technical content — bloated committee-authored books, pretentious academic texts, and juvenile online commentary — and desperately needs curation of genuinely good writing.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky promotes his curated anthology 'The Best Software Writing I' by sharing positive early reviews and highlighting the diverse range of essays included. He emphasizes the book's breadth by juxtaposing contrasting pieces that address similar themes from different angles — humor alongside serious analysis, technical arguments alongside cultural criticism. The post serves as both a promotional piece and a sampler of the book's contents, name-dropping contributors like Adam Bosworth, Bruce Eckel, danah boyd, and Clay Shirky. Spolsky closes with a lighthearted pitch for the physical book format over reading online.
key_points: The book collects diverse essays on software that range from comic strips to conference talks to cultural criticism
Spolsky curates pieces that address similar themes from radically different angles — humor paired with serious technical analysis
Early reviews praised the collection as timeless and highly recommended
The post highlights essays on social interfaces, type systems, search UX, and data misuse as standout topics
money_quotes: That software which is flexible, simple, sloppy, tolerant, and altogether forgiving of human foibles and weaknesses turns out to be actually the most steel-cored…
Why on earth should we encourage people to perform a mental disorder in the digital world?
Why is a dog asking me questions?
I'll have to take a photo of the printout with my Kodak Funtime digital camera…
If you like to read in the bath, while driving, or in the Himalayas, or you want to preserve your eyesight for that hunting trip you've been planning, you should read the dead-trees version.
posts.key_insight: The best software writing spans a surprising range from comic strips to conference keynotes, and the most valuable curation juxtaposes pieces that illuminate the same problems from wildly different perspectives.

posts.summary: This is a very brief news update post where Joel Spolsky celebrates his book 'The Best Software Writing I' reaching #1 in computer books on Amazon and selling out its first printing in three days. He humorously attributes part of its success to including a contributor's drawing of a cow. He also shares a quote from Queen Elizabeth II complaining about Sony's overly complex remote controls, endorsing her sentiment about poor UI design.
key_points: Joel's curated anthology 'The Best Software Writing I' hit #1 on Amazon's computer books list and sold out its first printing in three days
Humor and personality (like a contributor's cow drawing) can differentiate a tech book from dry competitors
Even Queen Elizabeth II finds Sony's remote controls too complex, illustrating that bad UI design is universally frustrating
money_quotes: Apparently it is completely whuping "PMP Exam Prep (4th Edition)", which is number 2.
They probably didn't think to put Leon Bambrick's drawing of a cow in their book.
I have a lot of trouble with your remote controls. Too many arrows.
posts.key_insight: Good UI design is so universally important that even a queen will call out needlessly complex interfaces.

posts.summary: Joel announces his appearance at CFUNITED, a ColdFusion developers conference in Bethesda, Maryland. He will deliver a keynote and a session on The Joel Test. The Project Aardvark interns will publicly demo their product SidePilot for the first time at the Fog Creek booth. Joel offers free exhibit passes and discounted conference registration to his blog readers.
key_points: Project Aardvark's product SidePilot is functional enough for its first public demo, though still unpolished
Joel is giving a keynote and a session on The Joel Test at CFUNITED
Joel leverages his blog readership to drive traffic to conference booths with free exhibit passes
money_quotes: Yep, it's basically working, although still rather unpolished.
posts.key_insight: This is a brief event announcement promoting the first public demo of Project Aardvark's SidePilot product at CFUNITED, offering little analytical content beyond logistics and reader perks.

posts.summary: This brief post shares a quote about the frustration programmers face when roped into providing free tech support for friends and family. The quote captures the escalation from a seemingly simple fix into hours of remote troubleshooting misery. Spolsky uses it to highlight the social burden placed on technical people and links to the Project Aardvark Midterm Report for further context.
key_points: Remote tech support for non-technical people transforms trivial fixes into multi-hour ordeals
Being a programmer creates an implicit expectation that you serve as unpaid help desk for your social circle
The social and personal costs of free tech support extend beyond time to family relationships and quality of life
money_quotes: Just because you're a programmer doesn't mean you have to be the help desk for a dozen friends, relatives, and the people in the apartment next door. Does it?
Something that could take you 10 seconds to fix in person is about to become a two hour nightmare during which you'll alienate your family, lose sleep, tie up the phone line.
posts.key_insight: The social expectation that programmers serve as free tech support for everyone they know imposes real personal costs that far exceed the technical difficulty of the problems themselves.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky introduces Fog Creek Copilot, a remote desktop assistance product being built by summer interns as 'Project Aardvark.' The product solves the universal pain of trying to help non-technical family members fix computer problems over the phone by providing seamless, firewall-penetrating screen sharing. Joel explains why entering a market with existing competitors doesn't bother him, emphasizing that Fog Creek's advantage lies in making the experience shockingly seamless rather than inventing a new category. The post covers the product's naming process, their first trade show experience, and the compressed schedule to ship to paying customers before the internship ends.
key_points: Fog Creek Copilot uses a reflector server architecture to solve the firewall problem that makes existing VNC-based solutions impractical for casual users helping family members remotely.
Entering a market with existing competitors is fine if you focus on making the experience dramatically better — Fog Creek built its business on bug tracking software it didn't invent.
The product's focus shifted from tech support departments to casual home users after two of four interns independently said they'd use it to help their moms.

Trade shows are cost-ineffective for reach but invaluable for interactive customer feedback — the team learned their best pitch was a relatable scenario, not a technical explanation starting with 'You know VNC?'
The naming criteria prioritized phone-readability above all else, ruling out names with ambiguous spellings or easily confused letters.
money_quotes: Just because you're a programmer doesn't mean you have to be the help desk for a dozen friends, relatives, and the people in the apartment next door. Does it?
Yes, similar services already exist. That never stopped me before.
Fog Creek has been doubling in revenues every year mostly thanks to bug tracking software, and it's not like we invented bug tracking software.
We bought the domain name for more money than we spent developing the first version of FogBugz, oy gevalt.
A trade show is not a very cost-effective way to reach potential customers... I can write an article on my website and get in front of 1000 times as many people.
The best way was to start with a typical remote support scenario. 'Your mom calls you up. She says her screen is half grey. You have no idea what the heck she's talking about.'
I didn't want our interns to go home after 'mostly' finishing the code, leaving us permanent employees to debug until next February.
posts.key_insight: You don't need to invent a new product category to succeed — you need to make an existing solution so seamless that people stop tolerating the painful alternatives.

posts.summary: Joel provides a brief update on the beta launch of Fog Creek Copilot, a remote assistance tool. The product is being rolled out gradually, starting with 50 beta testers and expanding to 150. The team is fixing deployment bugs related to running on a multi-server web farm versus the single-server development environment. Joel highlights the engineering work going into high-availability features like connection draining and automatic failover.
key_points: Fog Creek Copilot's appeal is strong enough that users jokingly worry about being recruited as family tech support if their relatives discover it
The gradual beta rollout (50, then 100 more testers) reflects a deliberate approach to finding bugs before wider release
Moving from single-server development to a multi-server production web farm surfaced small but important deployment issues
High-availability engineering like connection draining and automatic server failover are essential for an online service product
money_quotes: One of the most common reactions we keep getting to Fog Creek Copilot is, "Please don't tell my mom about this!"
Since we're deploying the service on a web farm with two servers, and most of the development has been done on a single server, we found a few tiny details that needed to be fixed.

When we upgrade the reflector part of the service, anybody still using the old reflector can continue to use it until they're done, while the new reflector picks up the new traffic; this is called "draining."
If one of the servers goes down, even while people are using it, the clients automatically reconnect to the other server.
posts.key_insight: Shipping a reliable online service requires solving deployment and high-availability challenges that simply don't exist in single-server development environments.

posts.summary: This is a very brief news update post where Joel links to a report and photos from a Fog Creek Open House event. He also highlights a fellow attendee's website, Deadprogrammer's Cafe, using it to illustrate his observation about the density of interesting experiences in New York City compared to smaller cities.
key_points: Fog Creek held an open house event that was documented by attendee JD with photos and a report
Joel highlights Deadprogrammer's Cafe as a site whose photonarratives capture the richness of daily life in New York City
Joel reiterates his belief that New York provides an extraordinary density of noteworthy everyday experiences that would be remarkable elsewhere
money_quotes: New York is the kind of place where ten things happen to you every day on the way to the subway that would have qualified as interesting dinner conversation in Bloomington, Indiana, and you don't pay them any notice.
posts.key_insight: New York City offers such a density of interesting daily experiences that what would be remarkable elsewhere goes entirely unnoticed by its residents.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that hiring the best programmers isn't just about productivity gains — it's about reaching creative heights that mediocre developers simply cannot achieve. Using data from Yale's CS 323 course, he demonstrates 5:1 to 10:1 productivity variations among programmers, with no correlation between time spent and code quality. But beyond raw productivity, he contends that great software requires stylistic and emotional qualities — like Apple's iPod design or Winamp's irreverent personality — that no number of average developers can produce. The software market is winner-take-all, making 'good enough' a losing strategy. His formula remains: best working conditions attract best programmers, who build best software, which generates profit.
key_points: Data from Yale CS assignments shows 5:1 to 10:1 productivity differences between programmers, with no correlation between time spent and quality of output
You cannot substitute multiple mediocre programmers for one great one — Brooks' Law adds coordination overhead, and mediocre teams never achieve the creative leaps that great individuals can
Software has zero marginal cost of duplication, meaning design adds value faster than it adds cost — the economics favor investing in talent over cutting costs
Great software requires style, emotional appeal, and creative vision (like Apple's iPod design decisions) that cannot be produced by committee or large teams of average talent
The software market is winner-take-all, making 'remarkably good' products the only viable strategy — 'good enough' loses to competitors who hit the high notes
money_quotes: Five Antonio Salieris won't produce Mozart's Requiem. Ever. Not if they work for 100 years.
Design adds value faster than it adds cost.
The quality of the work and the amount of time spent are simply uncorrelated.
The mediocre talent just never hits the high notes that the top talent hits all the time.
It's not just a matter of '10 times more productive.' It's that the 'average productive' developer never hits the high notes that make great software.
You can't afford to be number two, or to have a 'good enough' product. It has to be remarkably good, by which I mean, so good that people remark about it.
