If you threw a bunch of extra programmers onto the Windows Media Player team, would they ever hit that high note? Never in a thousand years.
Nobody hires Dolly Parton to sing at weddings.
posts.key_insight: In software's zero-marginal-cost economics, the difference between good and great programmers isn't just productivity — it's the ability to achieve creative and stylistic breakthroughs that no number of average developers can replicate.

posts.summary: This is a very brief teaser post promoting Joel's longer article 'Hitting the High Notes.' Joel poses rhetorical questions about whether the variation in programmer talent is significant enough to matter. He suggests that while the answer may seem obvious to some, many people still need convincing that the best programmers are dramatically better than average ones.
key_points: There is enormous variation in programmer ability, and it matters significantly
The importance of hiring the best programmers is not universally accepted and needs to be argued
This post serves as a teaser for the full 'Hitting the High Notes' essay
money_quotes: Does it even make sense to talk about having the "best programmers?" Is there so much variation between programmers that this even matters?
Maybe it's obvious to us, but to many, the assertion still needs to be proven.
posts.key_insight: The vast difference in capability between the best and average programmers is a reality that still needs to be demonstrated to many people.

posts.summary: This is a very brief correction note to a previous post. Joel points out that the compression algorithm commonly known as 'LZW' should more properly be called 'ZLW' or 'Ziv-Lempel-Welch,' since the original paper credited Ziv as the primary author. The post is only 35 words and serves as a minor factual addendum rather than a substantive essay.
key_points: The compression algorithm commonly called LZW should properly credit Ziv first, as he was the primary author on the original paper
Joel demonstrates attention to proper academic attribution even in casual blog posts
money_quotes: Although the compression algorithm is commonly called "LZW," the original paper was credited to Ziv first, so I think it should properly be referred to as Ziv-Lempel-Welch or ZLW.
posts.key_insight: Proper attribution matters: the LZW compression algorithm should credit Ziv first, as the original paper did.

posts.summary: Joel shares brief company updates: Fog Creek is outgrowing its office space and hoping to expand next door, the interns have been improving Copilot.com's performance with proxy server support and speed optimizations, the team is attending a showing of Glengarry Glen Ross as sales training, and Joel thanks someone for a Unicode t-shirt.
key_points: Fog Creek Software is growing and needs more office space, eyeing the adjacent office being auctioned off
Interns are doing meaningful product work on Copilot.com, including proxy server support and performance optimization
The company uses creative approaches to sales training, like watching Glengarry Glen Ross together
money_quotes: The contents of the office next door to us are being auctioned off as we speak. I hope that means we'll be able to expand into there soon: we've totally outgrown our existing office space.
Tonight the whole company is going to sales training, in the form of the play Glengarry Glen Ross.
posts.key_insight: A short company update reflecting Fog Creek's growth pains and its culture of giving interns real product responsibility while keeping team activities fun and unconventional.

posts.summary: This is a brief news post linking to Joel's longer article about usability testing with Morae software. The core message is that usability testing is remarkably efficient — even a small number of test subjects in a single day can reveal major disconnects between how a program behaves and how users expect it to behave. Joel frames this as accessible even to someone not at their sharpest, emphasizing the low barrier to gaining valuable insights.
key_points: Usability testing requires surprisingly little investment — just a day and a handful of subjects — to yield significant findings
The primary value of usability testing is discovering where program behavior diverges from user expectations
Usability testing is accessible and forgiving enough that even imperfect execution produces useful results
money_quotes: The great thing about usability tests is with a day of usability testing and handful of subjects, even if you're as senile as I am, you can find the biggest areas where you didn't realize where the program's behavior diverges from the user's expected behavior.
posts.key_insight: Usability testing is a low-cost, high-yield way to discover the gaps between what your software does and what users expect it to do.

posts.summary: Spolsky describes his experience using TechSmith's Morae software to conduct usability testing at Fog Creek, contrasting it with both expensive professional usability labs and cheap hallway testing. He explains how Morae uses webcams to create an affordable middle-ground usability lab setup. During testing of Fog Creek Copilot, they discovered that users were confused by the helper application deleting itself after use, violating the fundamental principle that programs should behave as users expect. Spolsky notes the irony that this violated a principle he himself wrote about in his own book on UI design.
key_points: There's a practical middle ground between expensive professional usability labs ($100K+) and free hallway testing, enabled by software like Morae with cheap webcams
Traditional usability tests suffer from contrived environments where users don't have their own context, leading to artificial confusion that masks real usability issues
Field testing—observing users doing real work at their own desks—produces more authentic results than lab-based task lists
Virtually every usability problem stems from the user's mental model not matching the program's actual model
Even experienced UI designers violate their own usability principles, which is why systematic testing with real users is essential
money_quotes: A user interface is well-designed when the program behaves exactly how the user thought it would.
I've long been an advocate of hallway usability tests and paper prototypes, which often find some of the biggest usability problems long before they occur for about fifty cents.
Instead of giving the subject tasks to do in a highly contrived environment, you conspire to watch the subject doing their own work at their own desk while you hide in a nearby shrub and spy on them.
This is a classic example of the user model not conforming to the program model … most programs don't delete themselves! … which is the source of virtually every usability problem.

The great thing about usability tests is with a day of usability testing and handful of subjects, even if you're as senile as I am, you can find the biggest areas where you didn't realize where the program's behavior diverges from the user's expected behavior.
The program design violated a principle I wrote myself in big bold print in my own book: it didn't do what you expected.
posts.key_insight: Usability testing doesn't require expensive labs—affordable tools and a few real users will reliably expose where your program's behavior diverges from what users expect, even catching mistakes that the designer's own published principles should have prevented.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares the first external review of Fog Creek Copilot, a remote desktop assistance product. The review from FusionAuthority praises the product's high screen quality, near real-time mouse responsiveness, clean uninstallation, and zero-configuration firewall bypassing. Joel closes with a humorous aside about the review site apparently making a joke about Fog Creek's interns.
key_points: Fog Creek Copilot received its first external review, which was highly positive
The reviewer highlighted zero-configuration firewall/router bypassing as the best feature
The product emphasized a clean user experience: no remnants left on the system after disconnection
Performance was praised for high screen resolution with JPG compression and near real-time mouse reactivity over broadband
money_quotes: By far the best feature of the service is the zero configuration required to bypass firewalls/routers and connect to the host computer.
Everyone I demonstrated the service to was impressed.
When we disconnected, the executable was deleted from the desktop, leaving no remnants to clutter the system.
posts.key_insight: Fog Creek Copilot's key differentiator was eliminating configuration friction, particularly around firewall traversal, making remote assistance genuinely effortless.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the launch of Fog Creek Copilot 1.0, a product built entirely by summer interns as part of Project Aardvark. The post is a brief celebration of the shipping milestone, crediting the four interns who developed it over the summer.
key_points: Fog Creek Copilot 1.0 has officially launched
The product was built by summer interns (Ben, Tyler, Yaron, and Michael) as Project Aardvark
The project demonstrates Fog Creek's approach of giving interns real, shippable product responsibility
money_quotes: Fog Creek Copilot 1.0 just went live.
posts.key_insight: Fog Creek trusted summer interns to build and ship a real commercial product, reflecting a culture of giving junior developers meaningful responsibility.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares several news items and recommendations in a brief link roundup post. He highlights a glowing Dr. Dobbs review of the FogBugz book by Mike Gunderloy, comparing it favorably to Kernighan and Ritchie's C book. He mentions an academic paper on programmer performance variation, promotes his own audio interview about 'The Best Software Writing I,' and recommends Michael Kaplan's out-of-print book on internationalization as essential reading for Unicode comprehension.
key_points: The FogBugz book received an exceptional review in Dr. Dobbs, being compared to K&R's 'The C Programming Language' as a standard for documentation quality.
Academic research on student CVS repositories supports Joel's earlier arguments about wide performance variation among programmers.
Michael Kaplan's 'Internationalization with Visual Basic' was critical to making FogBugz and CityDesk handle Unicode correctly, despite being out of print and expensive.
money_quotes: This book is a user guide, but it is to user guides what Kernighan and Ritchie's The C Programming Language is to language manuals.
Every question I had was answered within a paragraph or two of being raised; every explanation made sense.
I really didn't understand Unicode until I read that book, and since it's out of print, it usually costs around $100 on Amazon, but it saved our skin making FogBugz and CityDesk work right.
posts.key_insight: Good technical documentation and deep reference books on topics like internationalization are rare and invaluable — worth celebrating and seeking out even when out of print.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky considers setting up a wiki for translated versions of his Joel on Software articles. He wants to empower volunteer translators to publish directly rather than bottlenecking through him. He asks readers for advice on wiki software, specifying requirements like Wikipedia-style formatting, version history for vandalism control, and a self-organizing community model.
key_points: Joel identifies himself as a bottleneck in the translation publishing pipeline and wants to remove that constraint
He envisions a self-organizing community where volunteers manage translations with minimal oversight
His requirements prioritize Wikipedia-like features: rich formatting, real links, and change history for vandalism protection
money_quotes: The volunteers who translate articles could publish them themselves without emailing them to me and waiting around for me to publish them to the web.
Most importantly, I'd like it to be self-organizing so that, ideally, once we set up the servers, it just sort of magically populates itself with high quality translations.
posts.key_insight: The best way to scale volunteer-driven content is to remove yourself as the bottleneck and let the community self-organize.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares a concrete example from Project Aardvark where designing screens on paper revealed a major simplification: requiring the helper to initiate the process instead of either party. This spec-level change took hours rather than the weeks it would have cost in code. He uses this as evidence to champion Big Design Up Front (BDUF) against Extreme Programming's iterative approach, stating unequivocally that XP proponents are wrong about dismissing upfront design.
key_points: A significant design simplification was discovered during spec writing that would have taken weeks to change in code but only hours in the spec
Joel strongly advocates for Big Design Up Front (BDUF) over Extreme Programming's minimal upfront design approach
Reducing complexity by constraining who can initiate a process made the product radically simpler without sacrificing usefulness
money_quotes: Making this change in the spec took an hour or two. If we had made this change in code, it would have added weeks to the schedule.
I can't tell you how strongly I believe in Big Design Up Front, which the proponents of Extreme Programming consider anathema.
I have consistently saved time and made better products by using BDUF and I'm proud to use it, no matter what the XP fanatics claim.
They're just wrong on this point and I can't be any clearer than that.
Aardvark would be just as useful, and radically simpler, if the helper was required to start the whole process.
posts.key_insight: Discovering design simplifications in a spec costs hours; discovering them in code costs weeks, which is why upfront design consistently produces better products faster.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky publishes the original specification for Project Aardvark (later Copilot.com) as a historical artifact, using it to illustrate the value of writing detailed specs before coding. He acknowledges one major error in the spec regarding SSL proxy server behavior, which added about 10% unplanned work. He argues that specs don't need to be perfect—they just need to minimize surprises during development. Spolsky strongly defends Big Design Up Front (BDUF) against Extreme Programming advocates, citing concrete examples where spec-level decisions saved weeks of coding time. He also notes how usability testing led to a late but inexpensive design change.
key_points: The spec's one major error (assuming port 443 would bypass proxy servers) only added ~10% to overall development effort, demonstrating that specs don't need to be perfect to be valuable
Simplifying the flowchart so only the helper could initiate the process took 1-2 hours in the spec but would have added weeks if discovered during coding
Usability testing revealed users were confused by auto-deletion of the application, leading to a small 2-hour fix that made the product better
Specs are written not to solve every problem in advance but to minimize surprises during development
The team shipped with all planned features and even some version 2 features, validating the spec-driven approach
money_quotes: The reason you write a spec is not to solve every possible problem in advance: the reason you write a spec is to solve as many problems as you possibly can in advance so that you minimize the number of surprises that come up during development.
Making this change in the spec took an hour or two. If we had made this change in code, it would have added weeks to the schedule.
I can't tell you how strongly I believe in Big Design Up Front, which the proponents of Extreme Programming consider anathema.

I have consistently saved time and made better products by using BDUF and I'm proud to use it, no matter what the XP fanatics claim. They're just wrong on this point and I can't be any clearer than that.
This, it turns out, was erroneous, false, and incorrect, and after shipping the first beta we discovered just how wrong it was.
I seriously expected we would have to cut features; instead, we shipped with everything we wanted in version 1 and even did some of the features this spec refers to as version 2 features.
posts.key_insight: Writing detailed specifications before coding is a high-leverage investment because catching design mistakes in a spec costs hours, while catching them in code costs weeks.

posts.summary: This is a brief, somber post from August 31, 2005, written in the immediate aftermath of Hurricane Katrina's devastation of New Orleans. Joel shares a photo from a happier visit to the city in 2003 and quotes from the Book of Lamentations. The post is a moment of pause from his usual software commentary to acknowledge a national tragedy. It reflects the shock and grief felt across the country as the scale of the disaster became clear.
key_points: Joel pauses his regular software commentary to acknowledge the devastation of Hurricane Katrina in New Orleans
He shares a personal connection to the city through his 2003 visit
The biblical quote from Lamentations 1:1 draws a parallel between ancient and modern urban destruction
money_quotes: How doth the city sit solitary, that was full of people!
posts.key_insight: A rare non-technical post where Joel steps away from software commentary to mourn the destruction of New Orleans by Hurricane Katrina, using a biblical lament to capture the tragedy.

posts.summary: This is a brief job posting announcement rather than a traditional blog post. Joel Spolsky announces that Fog Creek Software is hiring Tech Support Engineers in New York City. The role involves supporting FogBugz customers via phone and email, including helping them deploy the software on Windows and Unix servers. Joel highlights the company's good working conditions and benefits.
key_points: Fog Creek Software was actively hiring Tech Support Engineers in NYC
The role centered on supporting FogBugz deployments across Windows and Unix servers
Joel used his popular blog as a recruiting channel, consistent with his emphasis on hiring as a top priority
money_quotes: Great conditions and benefits, of course.
posts.key_insight: Joel leveraged his widely-read blog as a direct recruiting tool, reflecting his belief that hiring is among the most important activities a software company undertakes.

posts.summary: This is a brief news roundup post from Joel Spolsky covering several short items. He shares positive reviews of Fog Creek Copilot's remote support software, announces a keynote at EclipseCon 2006, and promotes an early Ajax development book. The post highlights Fog Creek's growing product line and Joel's speaking profile, while noting the team's increasing interest in Ajax web development techniques.
key_points: Fog Creek Copilot received praise for its simplicity and ability to work through NAT routers without hassle
Joel was gaining prominence as a speaker, landing a keynote at EclipseCon 2006
Ajax was emerging as a key web development technique, enabling rich client-like experiences in the browser
Applications like Gmail, Google Maps, and Flickr were early exemplars of Ajax's potential
money_quotes: It does exactly what it says it does – with gusto!
I've used remote support software before but it's usually hassle getting it through your average NAT router. The copilot service just worked when I tried it.
Ajax applications are highly dynamic web applications that are nearly as feature-rich as their thick client brethren.
All those cool features in GMail, Google Maps, Flickr, and Oddpost that make them feel much more responsive than typical web applications are thanks to Ajax techniques.
posts.key_insight: A lightweight news post capturing a moment when Ajax was transforming web development and Fog Creek was building products focused on removing friction from common tasks like remote support.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky describes the fundamental paradox of custom software development: even when you follow every best practice — getting requirements, writing specs, obtaining sign-off — the customer is still horrified by the result. He argues that the best-case scenario in custom development is that the client simply never uses your work and never calls you again.
key_points: Custom development is inherently risky because customers cannot truly know what they want until they see it built
Following all the 'right' processes (specs, sign-offs, documentation) does not protect you from customer dissatisfaction
The best outcome in custom development is often quiet abandonment rather than a lawsuit
money_quotes: Custom development is that murky world where a customer tells you what to build, and you say, 'are you sure?' and they say yes, and you make an absolutely beautiful spec, and say, 'is this what you want?' and they say yes, and you make them sign the spec in indelible ink, nay, blood, and they do, and then you build that thing they signed off on, promptly, precisely and exactly, and they see it and they are horrified and shocked.
If you're really lucky, the customer will smile wanly and put your code in a drawer and never use it again and never call you back.
posts.key_insight: No amount of process, documentation, or sign-off can bridge the gap between what customers say they want and what they actually need, making custom development an inherently perilous business.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky describes how to prioritize features for a software product, using FogBugz 5.0 as a case study. He first warns against two common prioritization mistakes: building features promised to individual customers (which leads to consultingware) and doing tasks just because they're 'inevitable' (which is procrastination in disguise). He then presents a practical group prioritization method learned from Excel 5 planning: list features on cards, vote to eliminate unpopular ones, assign rough costs, then give everyone fake money to 'buy' the features they care about most. Dividing total spend by cost produces a ranked priority list that reflects collective wisdom. The whole process took three hours and produced a list the team felt genuinely reflected the right priorities.
key_points: Building features promised to individual customers pushes you toward consultingware and away from the profitable shrinkwrap model with zero marginal costs
Doing tasks simply because they're 'inevitable' is procrastination disguised as productivity — importance is analog, not binary
A lightweight group prioritization method using fake money allocation and cost estimates produces surprisingly good results in just a few hours
The prioritization doesn't need to be precise — it only needs to roughly separate small, medium, and large features to guide development order
Letting less-important things go undone is a sign of effective prioritization, not negligence
money_quotes: If you ever find yourself implementing a feature simply because it has been promised to one customer, RED DANGER LIGHTS should be going off in your head.

Custom development is that murky world where a customer tells you what to build, and you say, 'are you sure?' and they say yes, and you make an absolutely beautiful spec, and they sign it in blood, and then you build that thing, and they are horrified and shocked.
I found myself pretending that all non-optional tasks were equally important, and therefore, since they were inevitable anyway, they could be done in any order!
More importantly, I should have realized that 'important' is not a binary thing, it's an analog thing.

If you want to get things done, you positively have to understand at any given point in time what is the most important thing to get done right now and if you're not doing it, you're not making progress at the fastest possible rate.
I've grown to think that keeping your desk clean is actually probably a sign that you're not being effective.
The roughly 50% of the features that we didn't have time to do were really stupid features, and Excel was better because it didn't have them.
Shrinkwrap has no marginal costs for each additional customer, so you can essentially sell the same thing over and over again and make a lot more profit.
posts.key_insight: Effective feature prioritization requires ruthlessly distinguishing what matters most right now from what merely seems inevitable, and a simple group exercise with fake-money allocation beats both sales-driven promises and procrastination-driven busywork.

posts.summary: This post covers two unrelated topics. First, Spolsky critiques Microsoft's Event Log error help system, noting that clicking the built-in help URL rarely provides useful information, while simply Googling the error text almost always does — illustrating Microsoft's failure to appreciate the power of free-form search. Second, he draws a strategic parallel between JetBlue's Boston shuttle launch and Dell's entry into the server market, arguing both are examples of attacking a competitor's profit center to undermine cross-subsidization of competing products.
key_points: Microsoft's hand-curated error help system is inferior to Google search for solving real problems, proving the value of pervasive free-form search over manually maintained links
JetBlue's Boston shuttle isn't primarily about capturing that market — it's about eliminating competitors' excess profits that subsidize routes competing with JetBlue
Dell entered the server market not because they cared about servers, but to stop HP and IBM from using server profits to subsidize cheaper desktop PCs
Attacking a competitor's cash cow to defund their competitive position in your core market is a powerful and underappreciated strategy
money_quotes: For more information, type the text of this error message into Google, because that always gets you useful information as the first result.
Microsoft consistently underestimates the value of pervasive, free form, instantaneous search.

By forcing down prices in the shuttle market, eliminating those excess profits for their competitors, they're going to make it harder for those airlines to accept losses on other routes elsewhere which compete with JetBlue.
Dell's solution was to enter the server market and force those prices down, to force the other server manufacturers to stop subsidizing their PCs.

They didn't originally care much about the server market, but over time, they actually improved their servers, and today the 8th generation PowerEdges are really excellent, well-thought-out servers and usually about 2/3rds the price of their competitors.
posts.key_insight: The smartest competitive move is sometimes attacking not your direct competitor, but the profit center that funds their ability to compete with you.

posts.summary: This post contains two distinct segments. First, Joel shares a Paul Graham quote about how simplicity in UI design requires genuine effort and genius, using a modern stove with confusing buttons as an example of regression from a perfectly functional knob-based design. Second, Joel describes how Fog Creek eliminated paper-based fax handling by chaining a $16 fax modem, $69 fax server software, and FogBugz's email integration to automatically convert incoming faxes into trackable bugs in their system.
key_points: Simplicity in UI design takes deliberate effort; replacing working physical interfaces with digital ones often makes things worse
A three-step integration (fax modem → fax server → email → FogBugz) eliminated paper-based purchase order tracking entirely
Cheap, unsexy technology ($16 fax modem, $69 software) can solve real business workflow problems
Making information accessible to the whole team rather than trapped in one person's office improves customer service
money_quotes: The fact is, simplicity takes effort—genius, even.
You can't just say "Err" to the user of a *stove*.
You should design the UI so that errors are impossible.
The boneheads who designed this stove had an example of such a UI to work from: the old one.
Remember fax modems? The top of the line models these days are about $16 and you probably have boxes of them in your attic.
posts.key_insight: Good solutions often come from respecting what already works — whether that means keeping simple knob-based interfaces or wiring together cheap existing tools to eliminate paper workflows.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky reflects on the vibrant startup scene in Boston after visiting a Y Combinator party, contrasting it with New York's comparatively quiet tech culture. He speculates that New York's high cost of living, distractions, and talent drain to finance and media may explain the gap. The post then shifts to discussing workplace productivity research on interruptions and multitasking, citing Gloria Mark's studies. Joel reinforces his long-held argument that private offices are superior to open floor plans for developer productivity, acknowledging the serendipitous knowledge transfer of open spaces but arguing the interruption costs outweigh the benefits. He shares a concrete example from Fog Creek's intern experience where one interruption cascaded to the entire team. He announces plans to double Fog Creek's office space to ensure every developer gets a private office.
key_points: Boston's startup scene, energized by Y Combinator's seed funding model, appears far more vibrant than New York's, possibly because finance and media absorb top talent
Research by Gloria Mark provides scientific backing for the claim that interruptions are extremely costly, taking an average of 25 minutes to recover from
Open floor plans offer serendipitous knowledge transfer but at a net productivity cost due to cascading interruptions
Private offices provide compounding benefits: higher productivity, better talent attraction and retention, and greater employee happiness
Y Combinator's model of providing seed funding to attract smart young entrepreneurs is presented as remarkably effective community-building
money_quotes: Paul Graham and his crew have done a remarkable job of attracting a first rate flock of smart young entrepreneurs by providing seed funding for a bunch of new startups.
Is it because investment banks, hedge funds, advertising agencies, and media companies suck up all the oxygen?
Dude, come work for Fog Creek… we can waste your talent in a much more laid back environment.

The one advantage of open space which people keep bringing up is all the accidental, serendipitous knowledge transfer that takes place when everyone overhears everyone else. I think it's not worth the productivity loss caused by an increased number of interruptions.

Anything that interrupted one person ended up interrupting everybody — those tiny issues inevitably became conversations with everybody, thus interrupting 3 people who had no reason to be interrupted and who would take an average of 25 minutes to return to what they were doing.

Private offices have remarkable benefits in terms of your ability to attract and retain talent and people's general happiness at work, which leads to increased productivity because you're hiring better people and they're happier.
posts.key_insight: The serendipitous collaboration benefits of open office plans are outweighed by the cascading interruption costs, making private offices a net win for both productivity and talent retention.

posts.summary: Spolsky criticizes Microsoft's approach to database technology, particularly the Jet engine upgrade that won't be redistributable to end users, making it useless for ISVs. He argues that Microsoft's failure to provide native, reliable full-text search in any of its database products is embarrassing for 2005. He contrasts Microsoft's low-level, procedural thinking with Google's higher-level abstractions like Bayesian filtering and internet-scale statistical approaches, warning that Microsoft risks falling further behind.
key_points: Microsoft's new Jet engine upgrade is undermined by not being redistributable, forcing ISVs to stick with the outdated Jet 4.0
SQL Server 2000's full-text search is poorly integrated, unreliable, and falls apart in production environments with frequent database operations
Microsoft lacks a relational database with truly native, well-integrated full-text search despite it being 2005
Google operates at a fundamentally higher level of abstraction than Microsoft, using statistical and probabilistic approaches where Microsoft uses procedural logic
Google's spell checking demonstrates the power of statistical approaches over dictionary-based methods, correcting even proper names Microsoft Word cannot
money_quotes: Our customers want their data in a single file and don't want to have to install and administer SQL Server just to get to that file.
That makes it nearly useless to typical ISVs developing Windows apps, so I guess we're stuck with the creaky old Jet 4.0.

The fact that it's 2005 and I can't buy a relational database from Microsoft that has full text search integrated natively and completely, and that works just as well as 'LIKE' clauses, is really kind of depressing.
Google uses Bayesian filtering the way Microsoft uses the if statement.
Google also uses full-text-search-of-the-entire-Internet the way Microsoft uses little tables that list what error IDs correspond to which help text.
If Microsoft doesn't shed this habit of 'thinking in if statements' they're only going to fall further behind.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's failure to deliver reliable full-text search and its habit of thinking in low-level procedural terms rather than higher-level statistical abstractions reveals a widening gap between Microsoft and Google's engineering philosophies.

posts.summary: This is a very brief announcement post where Joel Spolsky expresses excitement about a preview of the upcoming 'Project Aardvark' movie (later released as 'Aardvark'd: 12 Weeks with Geeks'), a documentary about Fog Creek Software interns building Copilot. The post contains no substantive argument or analysis, serving purely as a teaser for the film.
key_points: Joel previewed the Project Aardvark documentary and found it impressive
The post serves as a brief promotional teaser with no technical or analytical content
money_quotes: Now I'm really excited… I saw the preview of the upcoming Project Aardvark movie and it's simply amazing.
posts.key_insight: A minimal announcement teasing the Project Aardvark documentary, offering no substantive insight beyond Joel's personal excitement.

posts.summary: Joel shares a link to a VentureVoice interview with VC Brad Feld, who discusses the fundamental scaling problem with consulting businesses versus product businesses. Feld discovered that doubling headcount in a consulting firm doubles revenue but keeps profits flat. Joel highlights Feld's observation that transitioning from consulting to products is particularly difficult. The post also recommends a VentureVoice piece about launching companies at the DEMO Conference.
key_points: Consulting businesses have inherent scaling limitations: doubling headcount doubles revenue but not profit
The transition from consulting to product companies is notoriously difficult
Product businesses scale in ways that consulting cannot
money_quotes: We determined that the profit level we were generating at 20 people, well, if we grew to 40 people we'd obviously double the revenue, our profit would probably be exactly the same.
posts.key_insight: Consulting businesses hit a profit ceiling because costs scale linearly with revenue, making products the superior model for building scalable, profitable companies.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky revisits his 'architecture astronaut' concept, arguing that a new wave of meaningless buzzword-stringing has emerged around Web 2.0, tagging, folksonomies, and related concepts. He contends that 'Web 2.0' is not a real concept but rather a vague, nebulous term that makes people feel stupider. He also criticizes the term's implicit condescension toward earlier web practitioners. In a brief aside, he laments Microsoft's increasingly corporate culture where easter eggs are fireable offenses. He pledges to never use the term 'Web 2.0' again on his blog.
key_points: The 'architecture astronaut' phenomenon has resurfaced with Web 2.0 buzzwords like tagging, folksonomies, syndication, Long Tail, and peer production
Web 2.0 is not a meaningful concept — it's empty jargon dressed up as a paradigm shift, just like P2P and messaging were years earlier
The '2.0' version numbering is a deliberate rhetorical move to dismiss earlier practitioners as clueless, echoing the dotcom-era 'they just don't get it' attitude
Microsoft's corporate culture has drained the joy from working there, with easter eggs becoming fireable offenses and legal concerns over soda cans in videos
money_quotes: The term Web 2.0 particularly bugs me. It's not a real concept. It has no meaning. It's a big, vague, nebulous cloud of pure architectural nothingness.
When people use the term Web 2.0, I always feel a little bit stupider for the rest of the day.

That's one sure tip-off to the fact that you're being assaulted by an Architecture Astronaut: the incredible amount of bombast; the heroic, utopian grandiloquence; the boastfulness; the complete lack of reality.
The very 2.0 in Web 2.0 seems carefully crafted as a way to denegrate the clueless 'Web 1.0' idiots, poor children.

Now it's tagging and folksonomies and syndication, and we're all supposed to fall in line with the theory that cool new stuff like Google Maps, Wikipedia, and Del.icio.us are somehow bigger than the sum of their parts.
Boy, am I glad I don't work at Microsoft any more. The joy is gone when adding an easter egg is 'immediate grounds for termination.'
Still, it beats the hell out of listening to one of those podcasts about podcasting or reading one of those blogs about blogging.
posts.key_insight: Buzzword-driven 'paradigm shifts' like Web 2.0 are recycled architecture astronautics — grandiose language papering over a lack of substance, designed more to make predecessors look foolish than to describe anything real.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky offers his theory on why eBay acquired Skype for $2.6 billion, dismissing the official rationale about enabling communication during auctions. He argues that eBay, having been taken over by MBAs too early, became constitutionally incapable of building software internally — as evidenced by the BillPoint vs. PayPal debacle. With excess cash that executives couldn't justify returning to shareholders, eBay had to acquire rather than build. Spolsky warns that this acquisition is fueling bubble thinking, with investors drawing false architectural parallels between unrelated companies like Skype and del.icio.us.
key_points: eBay's official justification for buying Skype (enabling auction communication) was nonsensical since Skype already worked for that purpose without being owned by eBay
Companies with strong engineering cultures (Google, Amazon, Microsoft) build new products in-house, while MBA-dominated companies like eBay must acquire because they can't develop software
The BillPoint vs. PayPal failure proved eBay's inability to build competitive software, even when they actively tried to steer users away from the competitor
The Skype acquisition was fueling irrational bubble thinking, with investors drawing false 'architectural' connections between unrelated companies to justify inflated valuations
money_quotes: eBay somehow got taken over by a swarm of MBAs way too soon and became an organization that was basically incapable of developing software.
If they give it back to the shareholders, the executives at eBay won't be able to justify their ginormous salaries. So that leaves reinvestment.

Since eBay realized they couldn't build things, they have to use their cash to buy things instead. This is what MBAs like to do anyway. They don't completely understand how to make companies but they do enjoy buying and selling them.
A scrappy team of outsiders at PayPal made a system that eBay users preferred 2 to 1, despite the fact that eBay repeatedly tried to force auctioneers into using BillPoint instead.

I don't care if you manage to come up some architectural abstraction that seems to include both. It's not going to make del.icio.us worth $2.6b, because del.icio.us, architecturally, doesn't have that 'free phone call' feature.
posts.key_insight: Companies that lose the ability to build software internally are reduced to acquiring it, and the resulting big-ticket acquisitions distort the market by encouraging investors to draw false parallels between fundamentally different businesses.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the launch of the Fog Creek Software Management Training Program, a three-year intensive program designed to develop the next generation of software management talent. The post acknowledges that Fog Creek has primarily hired programmers up to this point and signals a strategic shift toward building management capacity. The program aims to teach participants everything Fog Creek knows about the software industry.
key_points: Fog Creek recognized a gap in their hiring pipeline: they had focused almost exclusively on programmers and needed to cultivate management talent
The management training program is a three-year intensive commitment, signaling serious investment in developing leaders rather than just hiring them
The program reflects Spolsky's belief that software management is a distinct skill that can and should be systematically taught
money_quotes: Until now, most of our hires have been programmers. That's a start, but we need to start hiring the next generation of management too.
posts.key_insight: Great software companies need to invest in developing management talent with the same intentionality they apply to hiring great programmers.

posts.summary: Joel explains the reasoning behind a minor redesign of his blog's homepage. The key change adds a left column with narrative text aimed at first-time visitors who land on the site with no context. He was motivated by Jakob Nielsen's post on weblog usability. Joel deliberately avoids polished design, preferring the authenticity of a geek-made site over corporate slickness, and notes he even rejected a professional design he'd commissioned because it looked too shiny.
key_points: Most web users have learned to completely ignore sidebar navigation columns, making them ineffective for communicating with new visitors
Blog homepages typically fail first-time visitors by assuming familiarity with the site and its author
Splitting the homepage into two columns — one for new visitors, one for regulars — addresses different audience needs simultaneously
Deliberately amateurish design can signal authenticity and distinguish a personal blog from corporate marketing
money_quotes: Most people on the web today have learned to completely tune out the navigation column; it seems about as exciting as the credits at the end of a movie.
The home page, like the home page of many blogs, wasn't really serving first-time visitors.
The theory here is to divide the home page into two columns, one for first-time visitors and one for everybody else.
I paid a top web designer, Dave Shea, who created a stunningly beautiful design for me to use. It ended up looking a little bit too shiny, though, so I never used it.
It's not some kind of corporate blog developed by the marcomm department at a company that desperately wants to get people to pay attention to their new brand of laundry detergent.
The more it looks like it was designed by a geek, not a graphic designer, the happier I am, because I am a geek.
posts.key_insight: A blog homepage should explicitly address first-time visitors with narrative context rather than assuming everyone already knows who you are and why they should care.

posts.summary: Joel critiques Microsoft's launch of live.com, a beta portal that doesn't work properly in Firefox or even IE, arguing it exemplifies the 'Marimba Phenomenon' where companies spend more on PR than development. He contends that shipping early and often is incompatible with massive PR launches, because a huge audience seeing a half-baked product forms permanently negative impressions. Small companies with modest marketing budgets are better positioned to iterate publicly, since fewer people witness the early bugs. He contrasts Microsoft's botched launch with Fog Creek's Copilot, which fixed serious bugs before most users ever encountered them.
key_points: Live.com's broken back-button behavior and Firefox incompatibility make it unusable as a search engine despite Microsoft's massive launch push
The 'Marimba Phenomenon' occurs when marketing spend outpaces development quality, permanently poisoning first impressions for a large audience
Cash-rich companies face a structural disadvantage with early releases because money buys marketing easily but can't accelerate software quality, which requires time and talent
Small companies with limited marketing are ideally positioned to ship early and often because fewer users witness the inevitable early bugs
Fog Creek Copilot's gradual rollout allowed them to fix serious bugs affecting 5% of customers before the audience grew large
money_quotes: Result: everybody checks out your code, and it's not good yet. These people will be permanently convinced that your code is simple and inadequate, even if you improve it drastically later.
Does ship-early-and-often really work for a huge company doing massive PR pushes that's going to get millions of people checking out their early release? I don't think it does.
It's easy to spend on marketing and PR, since that just takes cash, but it's hard to spend on software development, because that actually takes time and talent.

Cash-rich companies, whether Microsoft or VC-backed startups, run the risk of launching to a bigger audience than they really should have, getting millions of people to be thoroughly unimpressed by version 1.0 and never bothering to come back again.
I don't remember Microsoft ever shipping anything quite this half-baked. Maybe that means that they're moving firmly into the 'agile' camp: ship early and often.
Small companies that don't have great marketing budgets are in a great position to launch early and often.
posts.key_insight: Shipping early and often only works when your audience is small enough that first-impression damage is limited — massive PR launches and iterative development are fundamentally incompatible strategies.

posts.summary: This is a very brief link post where Joel Spolsky points readers to a podcast interview he did with CIO Magazine's Technology Editor Christopher Lindquist. The interview covers three topics: Asterisk (the open-source phone system), Ajax (the web development technique), and 'Astronauts' (likely referring to Joel's concept of 'architecture astronauts'). There is no substantive content in the post itself beyond the link.
key_points: Joel was interviewed by CIO Magazine's Technology Editor about three tech topics
The topics — Asterisk, Ajax, and Architecture Astronauts — were recurring interests in Joel's writing during this period
The post is essentially a pointer to external content with no analysis or argument of its own
posts.key_insight: This is a brief link post directing readers to an external podcast interview, containing no substantive argument or analysis.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces he will be speaking at Webstock, a web conference in Wellington, New Zealand. He notes he hasn't visited New Zealand since 1987 and remarks on the extremely long travel time from New York City. He considers combining the trip with other meetings or conferences in the region to make the journey worthwhile.
key_points: Joel will be speaking at the Webstock web conference in Wellington, New Zealand in May
He hasn't visited New Zealand since 1987, nearly two decades prior
The roughly 24-hour travel time from NYC makes him want to combine the trip with other regional engagements
money_quotes: It's such a long trip from New York City (something like 24 hours, I think) that I really should to try to combine this trip with some other meetings or conferences in the South Pacific / Australia / New Zealand area.
posts.key_insight: A brief travel announcement with no substantive technical or business content.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the DVD release of 'Aardvark'd: 12 Weeks With Geeks,' a documentary filmed during Fog Creek Software's summer internship program. The project was inspired by his frustration with The Apprentice's shallow portrayal of business challenges and the lack of good documentaries about software development. He hired NYU grad Lerone Wilson to film the interns building a product from scratch over one summer, with the filmmaker maintaining editorial control. The documentary features appearances from Dan Bricklin and Paul Graham, and was made available for pre-order at $19.95.
key_points: Spolsky was motivated by the gap between The Apprentice's portrayal of business and actual meaningful work like software development
The filmmaker was given editorial independence rather than being paid to produce a corporate video, lending the project credibility
The documentary captures the full lifecycle of building a software product in a single summer internship
Notable tech figures Dan Bricklin and Paul Graham agreed to appear in the film
money_quotes: I was disappointed by the massive bogusness of the so-called 'business challenges' on the TV show The Apprentice.
Most of the people on the streets handing out flyers are homeless and working for minimum wage.
I thought that the audience that's excited to see inside the business world would be just as enthusiastic to get a view of the software development process at Fog Creek.
Instead of paying for the production ourselves, we provided a minimum subsidy, so the filmmaker could maintain complete editorial control.
The only other recent documentaries I've seen about software development, Code Rush and Startup.com, totally pale in comparison.
posts.key_insight: Spolsky saw an opportunity to document real software development as a counter-narrative to the shallow business theater of reality TV, producing a documentary that showed how products are actually built.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces a new management training program at Fog Creek Software that will span three years and require reading approximately 75 books at a pace of one every two weeks. The program aims to develop managers through a curated reading list covering business classics, software management, and tech company histories. The post serves as a call for suggestions, presenting a first draft of the reading list. Joel emphasizes the program's intensity and long-term commitment to developing management talent internally.
key_points: Fog Creek is investing in a structured three-year management training program, signaling a belief that good management requires serious, sustained education
The reading list spans three categories: best business books, best software management books, and histories of software/computer companies
The pace of one book every two weeks for 75 books reflects a philosophy that management skill is built through broad, deep reading rather than quick training
Joel openly solicits community input on the reading list, treating management education as a collaborative and evolving process
money_quotes: The management training program we're starting up here at Fog Creek will take about three years and will be relatively intensive.
We're working on the theory here of one book every two weeks.

We're trying to collect a combination of the best business books of all time, the best software management books of all time, and every worthwhile history of a software/computer company that we can find.
posts.key_insight: Developing great software managers requires a long-term, structured investment in broad education spanning business fundamentals, software management, and industry history.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces a reading list for Fog Creek's Software Management Training Program and contrasts it with traditional management consulting entry-level positions. He argues that management consulting firms exploit young graduates with grueling travel and grunt work under the guise of 'paying dues,' while Fog Creek offers genuine mentorship and practical software industry education. The post also briefly mentions a podcast interview and updates on the Aardvark'd documentary film. The core pitch is that ambitious young people should choose Fog Creek over consulting firms for a more substantive early career experience.
key_points: Fog Creek's training program targets the same ambitious graduates who would otherwise enter management consulting or technology integrator associate programs
Traditional consulting firms exploit entry-level hires with excessive travel, mundane work, and long hours while providing little actual education
Fog Creek positions itself as a superior alternative by offering real mentorship in the software industry without the grunt work and travel
The Aardvark'd documentary about Fog Creek interns was accepted to both LA and NY independent film festivals
money_quotes: Those programs have a fatal flaw though: they really only care about getting cheap labor that will work hard and can be billed out at high hourly rates.

Life as a recent BA graduate in a management consulting firm consists of tons of travel, never to pleasant places, living out of a suitcase in a hotel somewhere unlovely, and working ridiculous hours on the most mundane aspects of a project, all for the sake of 'paying your dues' in hopes of a better career later.
Only a small fraction of the people that start these programs end up working as management consultants.
As long as you're ready to work hard, why not do it at Fog Creek?

We'll actually teach you everything we know about the software industry instead of giving you grunt work, we won't make you travel, and by the end of three years you'll be far more qualified to lead technology projects and technology companies.
posts.key_insight: If ambitious young people are going to work grueling hours early in their careers anyway, they should choose environments that provide genuine education and mentorship rather than consulting firms that disguise exploitation as 'paying your dues.'

posts.summary: Joel shares the experience of self-fulfilling 2,600 DVD orders for Fog Creek's documentary 'Aardvark'd: 12 Weeks with Geeks,' which far exceeded his expectation of 1,000 orders. He details the logistical lessons learned from a three-day packing ordeal, including the value of barcodes, combining packing slips with shipping labels, and proper counter height for standing work. He then provides a full financial breakdown of the movie's production and distribution costs, showing a final profit of approximately $6,048. Joel notes he originally just hoped to break even, with the real 'profit' being increased visibility for Fog Creek's internship program.
key_points: Fog Creek's DIY culture led them to self-fulfill 2,600 DVD orders instead of using a fulfillment house, resulting in a painful but educational three-day packing ordeal

Practical logistics lessons emerged: barcodes save enormous time even for simple IDs, combining packing slips and shipping labels on one sheet eliminates sequencing headaches, and counter height vs. desk height matters enormously for standing work
The full P&L shows $11.02 per-unit cost against a $19.95 price, with $15,125 in fixed costs requiring 1,694 copies to break even
The movie ultimately netted about $6,048 in profit after accounting for unsold inventory waste, exceeding Joel's break-even expectations
The real strategic value wasn't DVD profit but attracting applicants to Fog Creek's summer internship program
money_quotes: Anyone in their right mind would have found a fulfillment house to actually package and ship these damn things, but here at Fog Creek we seem to have developed a do-it-yourself kind of culture.
The way I justify this silly exercise in hard labor is that I can write it off as a learning experience!
Barcodes are the greatest thing since sliced bread.

The difference between desk/table height and counter height makes a huge difference ergonomically. Three days of working with things at various heights while standing up taught me that lesson in a visceral way.
You can't send Global Priority Mail to Italy. Who knew?
I was hoping to break even on the movie, and make a 'profit' through more applications to our summer internship program from people who saw the movie and were inspired to work for Fog Creek.
posts.key_insight: A software company's DIY documentary project yielded modest financial profit but its real value was as a recruiting tool, and the fulfillment process itself became a crash course in operational logistics.

posts.summary: Joel briefly announces that Fog Creek Software will be attending O'Reilly's Emerging Technology Conference (ETech) in spring 2006. All Fog Creek programmers unanimously chose ETech from a list of about 12 conferences. Joel praises the previous year's conference for its interesting presentations and valuable attendee interactions.
key_points: Fog Creek gives programmers autonomy to choose which conference to attend
ETech was unanimously chosen over 11 other options, suggesting its strong reputation among developers
The best value of conferences comes from interacting with attendees, not just watching presentations
money_quotes: We gave all the Fog Creek programmers a choice of which conference to attend this spring, from a long list of about 12 conferences, and all of them wanted to go to ETech.
most of the presentations, especially the ones that weren't given by the sponsors, were great, but the best part was interacting with the interesting attendees.
posts.key_insight: Letting developers choose their own conferences and valuing peer interaction over sponsored content reflects a developer-centric management philosophy.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief news update from Joel Spolsky about Fog Creek Software's office expansion. The company has acquired a loft space next door to their existing offices and plans to build eleven new offices there. In the interim, the empty space is being used as a ping-pong room. The post is essentially a throwaway status update with no substantive argument or analysis.
key_points: Fog Creek Software was expanding its physical office space with eleven new offices
The company valued giving employees private offices, consistent with Joel's well-known stance on developer workspace
Even unused space gets repurposed for employee recreation (ping-pong), reflecting the company's culture
money_quotes: We have this big empty loft next door where we're about to start construction on another eleven offices.
In the meantime it's serving as a temporary ping-pong room.
posts.key_insight: A brief office expansion update that incidentally reflects Fog Creek's commitment to private offices and employee-friendly workspace culture.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky describes building an in-house shipping system ('FogShip') to handle the overwhelming volume of orders for Aardvark DVDs. The initial ad-hoc process using Mail Merge and manual customs forms took about three minutes per order and required technical skills. After researching tools, he spent three days building a barcode-driven system using Microsoft Access, a Zebra label printer, Endicia for postage, and HP LaserJets for customs forms. The new system reduced shipping time to 35 seconds per order and eliminated the need for SQL expertise, turning a painful process into something anyone could operate.
key_points: Small ergonomic problems compound into massive headaches at scale — signing customs forms, keeping packing slips outside envelopes, and printer toner logistics all became significant bottlenecks when shipping thousands of orders to 60 countries.
Microsoft Access reports, often dismissed as obsolete, turned out to be the perfect tool for generating packing slips and customs forms with precise layout control.
The barcode-scanning workflow eliminated multiple manual steps by combining three actions (marking shipped, displaying order info, printing labels) into a single scan.

Choosing the right physical tools matters enormously: Code 39 barcodes need no checksum code, Zebra printers peel labels automatically, and printing on card stock instead of paper eliminates a folding step.
The three-day investment reduced per-order time from 3 minutes to 35 seconds and removed the skill barrier, making shipping accessible to anyone in the office.
money_quotes: It was amazing how small ergonomic problems turn into huge headaches when you're shipping thousands of orders to 60 different countries.
Worse, it took someone skilled in SQL queries and Mail Merge to create the labels.

I've never, ever used the Access 'report' feature in my life; I always imagined it was intended for old-fashioned people who need to have their data printed out for some obscure reason and can't fathom the idea of looking things up on a computer.
Well, it turns out that Access reports are perfect for generating packing slips. This is what they were born to do.
Without any setup, when you scan a barcode, your scanner will type the letters that barcode represents on the keyboard and press Enter.
I'm pretty shocked that in this day and age HP is still shipping printers that just can't be set up by normal humans.
Shipping an international order now takes about 35 seconds, down from 3 minutes, and can be done by anyone, whether or not they have SQL and Mail Merge skills.
posts.key_insight: Investing a few days in building the right system can turn a painful, skill-dependent manual process into something fast, reliable, and accessible to anyone — the key is obsessing over the small ergonomic details that compound at scale.

posts.summary: This post is essentially a placeholder or stub consisting of a single character 'x' with a title referencing a 'Copilot Beer Pong Table.' There is no substantive content to analyze. It likely accompanied an image or was a quick throwaway post on Joel Spolsky's blog from 2005.
key_points: The post contains no meaningful text content beyond a single character
The title suggests it was likely an image post showcasing a beer pong table related to Fog Creek's Copilot product
Representative of the casual, personality-driven blogging style common in mid-2000s tech blogs
posts.key_insight: This is a content-free stub post, likely originally an image showcase for Fog Creek's office culture, with no analyzable text.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares a curated list of interesting links and media recommendations. He highlights an aviation blog called Enplaned, introduces Reddit as a user-submitted links site, and reviews several podcasts. He recommends TWiT and VentureVoice as the only two podcasts worth regular listening, while criticizing the Gilmore Gang for poor audio quality and Adam Curry's show for being too self-referential about podcasting itself.
key_points: Reddit is introduced as a simple user-submitted links site with a cute alien mascot, notable mainly because its founders appeared in the Aardvark'd movie
Of many podcasts tried, only TWiT and VentureVoice survived Joel's quality filter, suggesting most early podcasts were not worth the time
Joel criticizes Adam Curry's podcast for spending most of its time talking about how great podcasting is rather than demonstrating it through quality content
Production quality matters — Joel dropped the Gilmore Gang solely because of poor audio quality making it inaudible on New York streets
money_quotes: Reddit is a website with a cute alien mascot, featuring user-submitted and user-rated links.
I've subscribed and unsubscribed to a lot of Podcasts trying to keep entertained on the subway, and the bottom line is that there are only two I can tolerate on a regular basis.
TWiT made me understand why Leo Laporte was so beloved on Tech TV, and John C. Dvorak will charm your socks off in his role as the cuddly curmudgeon, channeling Jerry Pournelle.
I had to stop listening to the Gilmore Gang because the sound quality made it inaudible on the streets of New York.
Do they listen to their podcasts after they record them?
I had to stop listening to Adam Curry because all he does is talk about was how great podcasts are. Show us, Adam, don't tell us.
Adam Curry is one of the best DJs in the world, but his show is 10% great DJing, 60% crappy drivel about podcasting, and 30% late-night-infomercial-quality promos for podcasts about garden slugs.
posts.key_insight: Show, don't tell — whether in podcasting or any medium, demonstrating quality through your work is more convincing than talking about how great the medium itself is.

posts.summary: This is a very brief link post where Joel Spolsky points readers to his longer article 'How to Ship Anything,' which describes how Fog Creek Software optimized their physical order shipping process, reducing the labor time from 3 minutes to 35 seconds per order. The post serves as a teaser for the full article about operational efficiency in a software company's fulfillment process.
key_points: Fog Creek dramatically reduced order shipping labor from 3 minutes to 35 seconds per order
Even software companies need to optimize physical logistics and operational processes
Process optimization can yield order-of-magnitude improvements in efficiency
money_quotes: We cut the amount of labor it takes to ship orders from 3 minutes to 35 seconds.
posts.key_insight: Dramatic efficiency gains are possible in mundane operational processes like order fulfillment through systematic optimization.

posts.summary: Joel provides a brief update on Fog Creek's in-house order shipping system, reporting impressive efficiency metrics of one order every 12 seconds with two workers. He uses this as a real-world vindication of his earlier argument against outsourcing critical business functions, quoting extensively from his 2001 essay 'In Defense of Not-Invented-Here Syndrome' about the dangers of losing direct control over fulfillment and customer service.
key_points: Two people working together can ship an order every 12 seconds, yielding throughput of 2,400 orders per day from a single workstation
In-house fulfillment gives direct control over quality and speed, validating the decision not to outsource
Outsourcing critical functions like fulfillment and customer service creates an information gap where you can't even know your customers are unhappy
money_quotes: 2 people working in tandem can ship an order every 12 seconds using the new system.
If you've ever had to outsource a critical business function, you realize that outsourcing is hell.
Without direct control over customer service, you're going to get nightmarishly bad customer service — the kind people write about in their weblogs.
You've set up an outsourced customer service center with the explicit aim of not listening to your own customers.
posts.key_insight: Building critical business operations in-house — even mundane ones like shipping — gives you direct control over quality and customer experience that outsourcing can never match.

posts.summary: Joel announces the Japanese translation of his book 'Joel on Software,' thanking translator Yasushi Aoki and publisher Ohmsha. He notes the book is now available in five languages and praises the translation quality, highlighting how closely the translator worked with him on cultural references and jokes. He shares a personal anecdote about his father's book being translated to Japanese when he was a child, and his attempt to decipher the family name in Japanese characters. A reader correction clarifies the actual Japanese pronunciation of his surname.
key_points: The Japanese edition of 'Joel on Software' joins English, Italian, Korean, and Chinese translations
High-quality translation required close collaboration to handle cultural references and inside jokes
Japanese book manufacturing quality surpassed the English original
Joel connects the experience to his father's academic work being translated to Japanese years earlier
money_quotes: I'm certain the Japanese translation will be excellent because Yasushi-san worked closely with me on many small points of translation and worked very hard to correctly translate all those weird cultural references and inside jokes I always put in my articles.
The quality of the physical book is much nicer than the English original (why do they have such nice paper in Japan?)
I was thrilled that our name in Japanese is usually pronounced sa-po-ra-sa-ki, which sounds like a cross between an alcoholic drink and a rad motorcycle.
posts.key_insight: Good translation of technical writing requires close author-translator collaboration, especially when the writing is full of cultural references and humor.

posts.summary: Spolsky shares programming questions from his freshman midterm exams at Penn, inviting readers to test themselves. He admits that despite being a self-taught programmer who had built two major production systems, he struggled with pointers and recursion on these exams. The post presents four exercises covering functional programming (accumulate/fold), pointer arithmetic in C, and basic recursion in both Scheme and JavaScript. It serves as a humbling reminder that practical programming experience doesn't automatically translate to computer science fundamentals.
key_points: Real-world programming experience (building production systems) doesn't guarantee understanding of CS fundamentals like pointers and recursion
The exercises test core concepts: higher-order functions, accumulate/fold patterns, pointer arithmetic, and string manipulation in C
Spolsky frames the post as a self-assessment tool, encouraging readers to honestly evaluate their own foundational knowledge
The questions bridge multiple languages (Scheme, JavaScript, C) to test conceptual understanding rather than language-specific syntax
money_quotes: By the time I got to Penn for my first year of college, I thought I was already a pretty good programmer.
It took me until the midterm exams to realize I wasn't as smart as I thought.
I completely screwed up some questions, because I still didn't get pointers and I still didn't get recursion.

Completely self-taught, I had written two major systems in Turbo Pascal … one of them was a complete inventory system for a small factory, while the other scheduled all the production at one of Israel's largest bakeries.
Never one to hold a grudge, I share those midterm questions with you… see if you can do better than I did freshman year.
posts.key_insight: Building real software doesn't mean you understand the fundamentals — practical experience and theoretical knowledge are complementary skills that must both be deliberately developed.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky argues that the widespread adoption of Java as the primary teaching language in university CS programs is producing graduates who lack fundamental programming abilities. He contends that pointers and recursion — traditionally taught through C and Scheme — serve as essential 'weed-out' mechanisms that identify students with the mental flexibility required for great programming. By removing these difficult concepts, JavaSchools produce more graduates but fewer truly capable programmers. Spolsky connects this to real-world consequences, noting that Google's invention of MapReduce came directly from functional programming knowledge that JavaSchool graduates would lack. He concludes that these dumbed-down curricula fail both industry and academia.
key_points: Pointers and recursion are the two concepts that most reliably separate great programmers from mediocre ones, and Java curricula effectively bypass both
The shift to all-Java curricula was driven by pressure to reduce dropout rates and increase department budgets, not by pedagogical improvement
Google's MapReduce — trivially obvious to anyone who understood functional programming — illustrates the real-world cost of not teaching these fundamentals
OOP is not a suitable substitute for pointers and recursion as a weed-out mechanism because struggling with OOP still produces working programs
JavaSchool graduates are harder to evaluate in interviews because employers can't distinguish between lack of education and lack of aptitude
money_quotes: Java is not, generally, a hard enough programming language that it can be used to discriminate between great programmers and mediocre programmers.
Pointers and recursion require a certain ability to reason, to think in abstractions, and, most importantly, to view a problem at several levels of abstraction simultaneously.
I have never met anyone who can do Scheme, Haskell, and C pointers who can't pick up Java in two days, and create better Java code than people with five years of experience in Java.
The very fact that Google invented MapReduce, and Microsoft didn't, says something about why Microsoft is still playing catch up trying to get basic search features to work.

When you struggle with an OOP problem, your program still works, it's just sort of hard to maintain. Allegedly. But when you struggle with pointers, your program produces the line Segmentation Fault and you have no idea what's going on.
A JavaSchool that won't teach C and won't teach Scheme is not really teaching computer science, either.

I can't understand why the professors on the curriculum committees at CS schools have allowed their programs to be dumbed down to the point where not only can't they produce working programmers, they can't even produce CS grad students who might get PhDs and compete for their jobs. Oh wait. Never mind. Maybe I do understand.
posts.key_insight: By replacing C and Scheme with Java, CS programs eliminated the intellectual challenges that develop and identify the capacity for multi-level abstract reasoning — the very skill that separates great programmers from adequate ones.

posts.summary: In this foreword to Bob Walsh's book on micro-ISVs, Spolsky pushes back against the term 'MicroISV' while offering practical advice for aspiring software entrepreneurs. He argues the term was coined by Microsoft to diminish independent software companies. He provides a three-point checklist: solve a real pain point, don't go solo, and set realistic revenue expectations. He illustrates revenue growth with a memorable model showing how starting at $364/month and doubling regularly can build a real business over time.
key_points: The term 'ISV' is Microsoft's way of defining software companies in relation to itself, and entrepreneurs shouldn't accept that framing
A startup must solve a clear, specific pain point — most fail because they build solutions to problems nobody actually has
Solo founders are at a disadvantage: having a co-founder provides accountability, idea validation, and resilience when things get hard
First-month revenue for a new software product is typically around $364, but consistent doubling every 12-18 months compounds into a real business
The joy of entrepreneurship is in the gradual nurturing and growth of something you created from nothing
money_quotes: Don't start a business if you can't explain what pain it solves, for whom, and why your product will eliminate this pain, and how the customer will pay to solve this pain.
What they all had in common was that none of them solved a problem, and all of them were as doomed as a long-tailed cat in a room full of rocking chairs.
If you can't even convince one friend that your idea has merit, um, maybe it doesn't?
In the first month, you are going to make, about, $364, if you do everything right.
I prefer 'software company,' and there's nothing wrong with being a startup.
When the going gets tough, which it will, as a one-person operation, you'll just fold up shop. With two people, you'll feel an obligation to your partner to push on through.
There's no reason you can't double your revenues every 12-18 months, so no matter how small you start, you'll soon be building your own skyscraper in Manhattan.

Creating something, all by yourself, and nurturing it and working on it and investing in it and watching it grow, and watching the investments pay off. It's a hell of a journey, and I wouldn't miss it for the world.
posts.key_insight: Starting a software company requires solving a real pain point, having a co-founder for resilience, and accepting that early revenue will be tiny — but patient, steady doubling turns small beginnings into substantial businesses.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky emphasizes that the foundation of any business must be solving a clear, identifiable pain point for customers. He argues that entrepreneurs must be able to articulate what problem they solve, for whom, and how customers will pay for the solution. He illustrates his point with an anecdote about attending six startup presentations where none could clearly explain what pain they addressed. This is excerpted from his foreword to Bob Walsh's book on running a one-person software company.
key_points: Every business must start with a clear pain point it solves for a specific customer
Entrepreneurs need to articulate not just the problem but the payment mechanism — how customers will pay to solve their pain
Most startups fail at the most basic requirement: clearly explaining what problem they solve
money_quotes: Don't start a business if you can't explain what pain it solves, for whom, and why your product will eliminate this pain, and how the customer will pay to solve this pain.
I went to a presentation of six high tech startups and not one of them had a clear idea for what pain they were proposing to solve.
posts.key_insight: A business that cannot clearly articulate the specific pain it solves for a specific customer has no foundation to build on.

posts.summary: This is a very brief promotional post where Joel Spolsky announces that three Fog Creek products and projects — Fog Creek Copilot, FogBugz, and his book 'The Best Software Writing I' — are all finalists for the 2006 Jolt Awards. The post is structured as a simple Q&A riddle. There is no substantive argument or analysis; it is purely an announcement.
key_points: Fog Creek Copilot, FogBugz, and 'The Best Software Writing I' were all nominated as finalists for the 2006 Jolt Awards
The Jolt Awards recognized excellence in software development tools and books
Having three finalists from one company/person across different categories signals broad influence
money_quotes: What do Fog Creek Copilot, FogBugz, and The Best Software Writing I have in common? They're all finalists for the 2006 Jolt Awards.
posts.key_insight: A brief announcement celebrating Fog Creek's three Jolt Award nominations across software and publishing categories.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky introduces a planned series on great UI design by recounting his frustrating experience trying to turn on his cell phone. He argues that while desktop software UI had reached a decent standard by 2000, the explosion of consumer gadgets and the web undid that progress by introducing inconsistent, confusing interfaces. He observes that a few exceptional designers have managed to create truly great products amid the chaos, and announces his intent to explore what separates good design from great design in a series of articles.
