key_points: Consumer gadgets have made everyday appliances like phones and TVs harder to use by importing bad computer UI conventions
Desktop software UI had reached a reasonable standard with Mac and Windows consistency, but the web and gadgets disrupted that progress
A small number of great designers (Apple iPod, TiVo, Google) achieved breakthrough designs that made people happy amid the general decline
There is a meaningful leap between serviceable product design and truly great, history-changing design that deserves exploration
money_quotes: Here's why I'm afraid to turn off my cell phone: because I can't always seem to muster the brain cells necessary to turn it back on.
It turns out it's the red button that turns it on.
Indeed, it's surprising just how many of today's devices and gadgets and remote controls have actually made TVs, stoves, and telephones harder to use.
Suddenly, bad computer user interface design is seeping into the entire world.
Combined with rampant featuritis, these damn devices did more and more things but just figuring out how to do them took a degree in engineering.
A very few, very good designers were, somehow, coming up with truly great designs that were beautiful, easy to understand, fun, and which made people happy.
We're going to look at what it takes to make the leap from a servicable, decent product design to a Mindbogglingly Great, Earth-Shaking, History-Changing product design.
posts.key_insight: While most consumer technology was becoming increasingly incomprehensible through feature creep and inconsistent interfaces, a few great designers proved that beautiful, easy-to-use design was still possible — and understanding that gap is worth serious study.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky introduces a forthcoming piece on great design by using a relatable example: the confusing power buttons on a cell phone. He highlights how even simple devices with just two buttons can be unintuitive, where the 'obvious' choice (green button to turn on) is wrong. This teaser post links to the first draft of his longer work on design principles.
key_points: Even the simplest interfaces with only two buttons can confuse users when design conventions are violated
Color-coded buttons (green/red) create expectations that designers sometimes fail to honor
Icons on devices often fail to communicate their function clearly
Good design should align with users' natural mental models and expectations
money_quotes: Here's why I'm afraid to turn off my cell phone: because I can't always seem to muster the brain cells necessary to turn it back on.
It has two buttons on it, a happy green button and a scary red button. They have funny icons on them that don't mean very much to me.
You might think that the green button turns it on. Green means go, right? Wrong.
posts.key_insight: If a device with only two buttons can confuse users, it demonstrates how easily designers fail at making interfaces intuitive — and why great design demands deliberate attention to user expectations.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares miscellaneous updates about Fog Creek Software, including office construction with a new contractor, the summer internship application deadline, and the Aardvark'd documentary being featured on Google Video. The main substantive announcement is his decision to shut down '?off,' a hidden, uncensored off-topic discussion forum on his site. The forum had devolved from irreverent banter among a small community into genuinely disturbing, psychopathic behavior. Joel frames the closure as a lesson about what happens when you allow completely unmoderated online spaces.
key_points: Fog Creek is expanding its office space with a new contractor after a bad experience with the previous one
The Aardvark'd documentary about summer interns building Fog Creek Copilot is featured on Google Video's home page
Joel shut down '?off,' a hidden, uncensored off-topic forum that had existed on his site, after behavior escalated from obnoxious to psychopathic
Even a tiny community of about ten regulars in an 'anything goes' forum will eventually produce behavior that crosses serious lines
Joel characterizes the experience as having 'learned an important lesson about anarchy'
money_quotes: The last contractor we had was so bad we had a different theory every day as to what his problem was. Depression? Liquidity crunch? Incompetence? Planning to steal the money and flee the country?
It was foul, free-wheeling, and Not Safe For Work.
Some of the participants in the group had probably crossed the line from common obnoxious online behavior to downright psychopathic behavior.
In a discussion group which prides itself on 'anything goes,' this was impossible to control.
At 6 pm today, I closed that discussion group, having learned an important lesson about anarchy.
posts.key_insight: An uncensored, unmoderated online community will inevitably escalate beyond mere offensiveness into genuinely harmful behavior, making anarchy an unsustainable governance model even for small groups.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky defines design as fundamentally an engineering problem about resolving conflicting constraints, not an artistic exercise in decoration. Using the example of designing a city street corner trashcan, he illustrates how real design requirements inherently conflict: light yet heavy, big yet small, open yet closed. He extends this to software, arguing that every feature represents a tradeoff between power users who need it and everyone else who must navigate increased complexity. Spolsky concludes that design is a fixed cost that adds value to every unit sold, making it the single most important factor in a product's success.
key_points: Design is not decoration or art — it is strictly an engineering problem of resolving conflicting constraints
Every new feature is a tradeoff: it serves users who need it while adding complexity that burdens users who don't
The 'just ignore it' argument for adding features is false — unused features still impose cognitive load, take up space, and create failure modes
Design is a fixed cost that adds value to every unit sold, making its return on investment extremely high
Great design requires balancing the competing demands of simplicity and feature richness — neither extreme works alone
money_quotes: Art can enhance design but the design itself is strictly an engineering problem.
Design, for my purposes, is about making tradeoffs.
When you're designing something, you often have a lot of conflicting constraints. In fact, that's a key part of design: resolving all of these conflicting goals.

Even if you think your new feature is all good and can't hurt because 'people who don't care can just ignore it,' you're forgetting that the people who allegedly don't care are still forced to look at your feature and figure out if they need it.
There are two requirements: lots of features and few features. Ah! And that is where the zen-like mystery of design comes in.
One false move, and you fall into the abyss. It's frigging hard to get this right.
Design is something you only have to pay for once for your product. It's a part of the fixed costs in the equation, not the variable costs. But it adds value to every unit sold.
Good design adds value faster than it adds cost.
posts.key_insight: Design is not about aesthetics but about engineering the right tradeoffs between inherently conflicting constraints, and because it is a fixed cost that enhances every unit sold, it is the highest-leverage investment a product team can make.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky draws a sharp distinction between design and decoration using the example of New York City brownstones. He notes that architects would delegate ornamental details to craftsmen by simply writing 'beautiful fretwork' on plans. Spolsky argues this delegation of aesthetic embellishment is decoration, not design. The post serves as a teaser for a longer essay on what design actually means.
key_points: Delegating aesthetic details to craftsmen without specification is decoration, not design
True design requires more than requesting something 'beautiful' — it demands intentional decisions
The distinction between design and decoration is fundamental to understanding what designers actually do
money_quotes: You know those gorgeous old brownstones in New York City? With the elaborate carvings, gargoyles, and beautiful iron fences?

The architect would often just write something like "beautiful fretwork" on the drawing, and leave it up to the artisan, the old craftsman from Italy to come up with something, fully expecting that it will be beautiful.
That's not design. That's decoration.
posts.key_insight: Design is not about making things beautiful — that's decoration; true design requires deliberate, specified decisions rather than delegating aesthetics to someone else's taste.

posts.summary: Joel briefly highlights complaints from Jeff Jarvis and Robert Scoble about the conference industry. Jarvis claims too many conferences are poor quality, while Scoble points out that scaling conferences to 1,000+ attendees causes costs to rise exponentially due to convention center price gouging. The post is essentially a link roundup with minimal original commentary.
key_points: Many conferences deliver poor value to attendees
Convention center costs scale poorly, with absurd markups like $1000 for an urn of coffee
The conference business model may be fundamentally broken at scale
money_quotes: Too many conferences suck.
Costs per attendee start going up exponentially.
posts.key_insight: The conference industry suffers from both quality problems and perverse cost structures that punish scale.

posts.summary: This post serves as a table of contents for Joel Spolsky's planned series on 'Great Design,' organized into three sections: Introduction, Good Design, and Great Design. Many articles are derived from his book 'User Interface Design for Programmers.' The series covers fundamentals like user models, affordances, and usability testing, then advances to deeper topics like emotional design, social interfaces, and the tension between user happiness and user success. Most entries are listed but not yet written, making this more of an outline than a finished work.
key_points: The series is structured as a progression from basic design principles ('Good Design') to more nuanced, harder-to-define qualities ('Great Design')
Good Design topics focus on practical UI concerns: user models, affordances, modes, usability testing, and designing for user limitations
Great Design topics venture into aesthetics, emotional design, community building, and knowing when to remove features rather than add them
The framework distinguishes between user happiness and user success as potentially competing goals
money_quotes: Articles in gray are still being written.
Users don't read
Users can't control the mouse
Users can't remember anything
Shipping is a Feature
Taking Things Away
User happiness vs. user success
posts.key_insight: Great design goes beyond usable interfaces into emotional resonance, community dynamics, and the discipline of removing features — a progression from competence to artistry.

posts.summary: Spolsky introduces his 'Great Design' series by distinguishing between good design and great design. He argues that the best products in every category succeed despite obvious flaws, not because they're flawless. Good design — usability, features, price optimization — can only get you to #2 in a market. Great design is an ineffable quality that makes people fall in love with products despite their shortcomings. He uses examples like the iPod, Aeron chair, and Brad Pitt to illustrate how market leaders dominate not through superior specs but through something harder to define. The series will cover usability fundamentals first, then explore the 'black magic' of what elevates good design to greatness.
key_points: The dominant product in every category tends to be popular despite obvious design flaws, not because it lacks them
Good design — optimizing usability, features, and tradeoffs — is necessary but can only get you to #2 in a market
Creative's ZEN players beat the iPod on nearly every measurable metric yet hold single-digit market share, proving that specs alone don't win
Great design is an ineffable quality that inspires love despite flaws, and it requires talent beyond just hard work
Usability is important but not sufficient — a perfectly usable singles bar would lose to Coyote Ugly Saloon
money_quotes: The blue chip product in every category can usually be thought of as being popular despite obvious design flaws.

Getting every aspect of the design perfect, making a usable product, making the right tradeoffs between price and functionality, between flexibility and ease of use, between weight and battery life, etc., etc., etc., is all really important, but the most it can possibly get you is to #2.
It's that ineffable quality that certain incredibly successful products have that makes people fall in love with them despite their flaws.

You get the feeling that if Mr. Nielsen designed a singles bar, it would be well lit, clean, with giant menus printed in Arial 14 point, and you'd never have to wait to get a drink. But nobody would go there; they would all be at Coyote Ugly Saloon pouring beer on each other.
You can have a gigantic broken nose, or be completely lacking in eyebrows, or have a giant gap between your two front teeth, and still be People Magazine's Sexiest Whatever of the Year.
Despite having products that are better than the iPod by just about every reasonable metric, they are unable to even come close to Apple iPod's dominant market share.
posts.key_insight: Great design isn't about eliminating flaws or optimizing every metric — it's an ineffable quality that makes people love a product despite its shortcomings, and no amount of good design alone can replicate it.

posts.summary: Spolsky introduces a distinction between 'good design' and 'great design,' arguing that market-leading products succeed despite obvious flaws rather than because of flawless execution. He uses examples like the iPod, Aeron chair, and Brad Pitt to show that dominance in a category comes from an ineffable quality that makes people fall in love with products despite their shortcomings. He contrasts this with competitors like Creative's ZEN players, which beat the iPod on specs but can't capture market share. The post serves as a roadmap for a series that will first cover usability fundamentals, then explore the 'black magic' that separates good from great.
key_points: Blue-chip products in every category tend to be popular despite obvious design flaws, not because they lack them
Optimizing every measurable design dimension — price, features, usability — can at best get you to #2 in a market
Certain markets are winner-take-all, making the difference between #1 and #2 existential rather than incremental
Usability is necessary but insufficient — a perfectly usable product can still fail to inspire love or loyalty
Great design involves an ineffable quality that requires talent, not just hard work and attention to detail
money_quotes: The blue chip product in every category can usually be thought of as being popular despite obvious design flaws.

Getting every aspect of the design perfect, making a usable product, making the right tradeoffs between price and functionality, between flexibility and ease of use, between weight and battery life, etc., etc., etc., is all really important, but the most it can possibly get you is to #2.
It's that ineffable quality that certain incredibly successful products have that makes people fall in love with them despite their flaws.

You get the feeling that if Mr. Nielsen designed a singles bar, it would be well lit, clean, with giant menus printed in Arial 14 point, and you'd never have to wait to get a drink. But nobody would go there; they would all be at Coyote Ugly Saloon pouring beer on each other.
You can have a gigantic broken nose, or be completely lacking in eyebrows, or have a giant gap between your two front teeth, and still be People Magazine's Sexiest Whatever of the Year.
Despite having products that are better than the iPod by just about every reasonable metric, they are unable to even come close to Apple iPod's dominant market share.
posts.key_insight: Perfecting every measurable aspect of design can only make you second-best; true market dominance comes from an ineffable quality that makes people love a product despite its flaws.

posts.summary: This is a brief introductory post linking to the third part of Joel Spolsky's 'Great Design' series. It teases the question of what separates best-in-class products from their competitors, using examples like the iPod, Aeron chair, and Brad Pitt. The post itself is essentially a pointer to the longer essay rather than a standalone argument.
key_points: Every product category has standout leaders that define excellence
The post sets up an inquiry into what common qualities make certain products great
This is part of a larger series exploring principles of great product design
money_quotes: Just about every product category has its blue-chip, gold-plated stars.
posts.key_insight: Understanding what separates the best products from the rest requires examining the common qualities shared by category-defining leaders.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief link post where Joel Spolsky shares that someone named Andy created a mashup integrating FogBugz (Fog Creek's bug tracking software) with TechSmith's SnagIt screenshot tool. The post contains virtually no analysis or commentary beyond a single word of approval. It serves as a quick signal boost for a community-built integration with Joel's product.
key_points: A developer named Andy built an integration between FogBugz and SnagIt
Joel used his blog to promote third-party tools built around his product
The post reflects the early blogging era's norm of very short link-sharing posts
money_quotes: Cool!
posts.key_insight: Joel used his blog platform to amplify community-built integrations with FogBugz, fostering a developer ecosystem around his product.

posts.summary: Spolsky reviews several Ajax-based calendar applications and finds none of them adequate for his real-world scheduling needs. He identifies specific failures around time zones, flight entry, notifications, printing, and delegation. He argues that shipping premature products and getting press coverage backfires because users who try a half-baked product rarely return. He theorizes that these calendar startups aren't building for actual users but are hoping to be acquired by Yahoo!, which he criticizes for buying companies instead of hiring programmers. The post warns that building a product for a single acquirer is a fundamentally risky business strategy.
key_points: None of the reviewed Ajax calendars handle basic real-world needs like flight scheduling across time zones, flexible notifications, or printable itineraries.
Premature publicity for unfinished products creates the 'Marimba phenomenon' — users try it once, find it lacking, and never return.
The proliferation of Ajax calendars is driven not by user demand but by the hope of being acquired by Yahoo!, which was buying startups instead of building in-house.
Building a product aimed at a single potential acquirer rather than real end users is an inherently risky business strategy.
The 'ship early and often' philosophy fails when it means shipping something so incomplete that first impressions permanently alienate potential users.
money_quotes: I know that everybody is saying that the cool thing to do these days is Ship Early and Often, but when you ship half-baked ajax calendars that don't do much and then get Scoble to go nuts about how great they are, well, you're going to have a lot of people like me checking it out and realizing that, for example, no thought whatsoever has gone into printing.

When you get premature publicity, lots of people check out your thing, and it's not done yet, so now most of the people that tried your thing think it's lame, and now you have two problems: your thing is lame and everybody knows it.
People aren't really building calendars to sell to people like me who need calendars: they're building calendar companies to sell to Yahoo!

Yahoo! has given up on the old concept of hiring programmers to write code, and is going with this new age concept of buying entire companies on the hopes that they might contain a good programmer or two, which, by the way, is a sure sign of trouble for a technology company.
How many Ajax Calendar Companies do you think Yahoo! is gonna buy? You don't build a product for one customer. It's just too risky.
Flights from New Zealand to Los Angeles arrive before they departed. It's confusing but it's true and if I can't enter them properly on my calendar I'm back to typing itineraries in Word.
posts.key_insight: Premature publicity for incomplete products permanently alienates the very users you're trying to attract, and building a product solely to be acquired by one company rather than to serve real users is a losing strategy.

posts.summary: Joel announces that VMware Server is now available for free, highlighting it as a significant development. He references his long-standing enthusiasm for VMware Workstation and his desire for an affordable server virtualization option. Previously, VMware's server products (GSX and ESX) were expensive and complicated, but this free release changes the landscape. He endorses the product as mature and production-ready.
key_points: VMware Server is now available at no cost, removing the price barrier for server virtualization
VMware's previous server products (GSX and ESX) were prohibitively expensive and complex for many users
Joel sees this as a market-changing move that democratizes server virtualization technology
money_quotes: The basic idea is that it lets you run a whole PC inside a window, with its own operating system and hard drive.
This is great stuff, and it's not half baked.
posts.key_insight: Making server virtualization free removes a major barrier to adoption and represents a transformative shift in how developers and companies can manage server infrastructure.

posts.summary: Joel follows up on his previous calendar software evaluation to highlight Airset, an online calendar he overlooked. He finds it significantly better than the hyped alternatives he previously reviewed. He admits he was deterred from trying it because the website lacked screenshots and easy trial access, and because a reviewer had chosen a competitor. He acknowledges this was a mistake and notes he still has feature requests for the product.
key_points: Airset is a superior online calendar compared to the overhyped 'proof-of-conceptware' others were excited about
Poor product marketing (no screenshots, no easy trial) can cause potential users to skip even good products
Letting a single reviewer's opinion substitute for personal evaluation is a mistake
Even knowledgeable users take shortcuts in product evaluation that lead to missed opportunities
money_quotes: It's much, much better than the proof-of-conceptware that people are hyperventilating about.
I went to the website, and couldn't find any screenshots or specific technical information, or an easy way to try it without signing up, so I gave up.
Dang, that was dumb.
posts.key_insight: A product's marketing and onboarding experience matters as much as its quality — even savvy users will skip great software if they can't quickly see what it does or try it without friction.

posts.summary: Spolsky describes Fog Creek's rigorous process for evaluating 400+ summer internship applications for five positions. The process involves objective screening against six criteria, followed by subjective evaluation by three senior developers, phone screens, and in-person interviews with all expenses paid. He defends the process against accusations of elitism by pointing out that hiring top talent enables the company to build sophisticated products and maintain high revenue-per-employee. However, he acknowledges a fundamental tension: the resume-based system discriminates against non-traditional candidates, and some of the most brilliant programmers look terrible on paper.
key_points: Removing job listings from Monster.com cut unqualified applications in half while increasing the quality of applicants, suggesting that friction in the application process is a useful filter
Fog Creek uses a multi-stage hiring funnel: objective six-criteria screening, subjective scoring by three senior developers, phone screens, then expensive in-person interviews with full hospitality
Hiring superstars pays for itself through higher revenue-per-employee, which funds better working conditions that in turn attract more talent — a virtuous cycle
The traditional resume-based application system has a fundamental flaw: it discriminates against non-traditional candidates who may be brilliant but look bad on paper
Adding more application materials like writing samples and professor recommendations provided no additional signal beyond what resumes already showed
money_quotes: When you post an internship to Monster or eRecruiting, candidates can apply for that job simply by clicking one link. As a result, last year we got flooded with seriously unqualified candidates.
The system is designed to be flexible, so we don't just rule people out because they didn't go to the right school or get the right grades.
When you're looking at a small pile of resumes sometimes you just know who the great ones are and who the good-on-paper ones are.
Once all those students applied, it's the least we can do to consider each application with the utmost care and fairness.

When you hire superstars you make a lot of money in terms of revenue-per-employee. That means we can have cushy private offices and high-end workstations and Aeron chairs without running out of money.
Many of the most brilliant programmers and software developers I know just look awful on paper.
I don't really think we can reinvent the application process.
posts.key_insight: A rigorous, multi-stage hiring process is worth the investment because top talent drives outsized returns, but the resume-based system remains fundamentally flawed in its inability to identify brilliant non-traditional candidates.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky outlines his busy March 2006 conference schedule, which takes him across California and Texas for multiple speaking engagements. He'll be presenting on UI design and usability testing at ETech, participating in a startup panel at SXSW, screening the Aardvark'd documentary at SDWest, and appearing at EclipseCon. The post is a brief, informal travel update peppered with his characteristic dry humor and offhand jabs at conference culture and Java.
key_points: Joel observes that conferences disproportionately cluster in March, joking about a 'conference organizing school' that mandates the timing
He takes a shot at the value of startup conferences, suggesting founders should work on products instead of attending 'interactive festivals'
He parenthetically questions Java's relevance with the aside 'Is Java the new Cobol?'
money_quotes: Somewhere in the world, there's a conference organizing school, and they must teach you there that all conferences have to be held during March.
Here's a good startup decision: work on your products instead of hanging out at "interactive festivals" in Texas.
Is Java the new Cobol?
posts.key_insight: Even while promoting his own conference appearances, Joel can't resist pointing out the irony that founders would be better off building products than attending startup panels.

posts.summary: This is a very short teaser post where Joel Spolsky introduces a revised excerpt from his book 'User Interface Design for Programmers.' He uses dramatic examples from aviation safety to hook readers into thinking about usability design. The post frames usability as a life-or-death concern, using the concept of CFIT (Controlled Flight Into Terrain) to illustrate how poor interface design has real consequences. Joel notes he is in the process of extensively rewriting and expanding his earlier book on UI design.
key_points: Poor usability in critical systems like aircraft controls can directly cause fatal accidents (CFIT)
Usability failures exist on a spectrum of severity, from fatal crashes to minor injuries to mere inconvenience
Joel was actively revising and expanding his book 'User Interface Design for Programmers' at the time of writing
money_quotes: Bad usability in the design of aircraft controls can result in what is cheerfully referred to as CFIT: Controlled Flight Into Terrain.
If you're lucky, the mistakes you make in usability design will merely cause people to lose limbs, or, heck, even just thumbs. No biggie!
posts.key_insight: Usability design has real-world consequences that range from inconvenience to catastrophe, and treating it as an afterthought is dangerous.

posts.summary: Joel announces that two Fog Creek products won Software Development magazine's 16th Annual Productivity Awards. Fog Creek Copilot won in the Utilities category, while FogBugz 4.0 won in the Defect Tracking category. He credits the interns who built Copilot in a single summer. He also notes that Software Development magazine is merging with Dr. Dobb's Journal, pointing to the decline of developer print media.
key_points: Fog Creek Copilot, built by interns in one summer, won a Productivity Award in the Utilities category
FogBugz 4.0 won a Productivity Award in the Defect Tracking category
Productivity Awards are runners-up to the more prestigious Jolt Awards
Software Development magazine merging with Dr. Dobb's Journal signals the decline of developer print magazines
money_quotes: All credit for this cool award goes to the interns who developed it in one summer: Tyler, Ben, Michael, and Yaron!
Eric Sink's blog post about the eventual death of developer magazines seems prescient.
posts.key_insight: A team of interns can build award-winning software in a single summer given the right environment, while the print media ecosystem that celebrates such achievements is itself fading away.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces a custom Reddit community at joel.reddit.com, designed as a link-sharing and voting platform for his blog readers. The site lets users post interesting links from around the web, with the best content rising to the top through community voting. He positions it as a solution to the flood of 'check out my thing' emails he receives daily, redirecting self-promotion to a meritocratic community filter. He encourages readers to use their voting power to surface quality content and bury spam.
key_points: Joel launched a dedicated Reddit community for Joel on Software readers to share and vote on interesting links
The Reddit serves as a filter for the daily flood of 'check out my thing' emails Joel receives, letting the community decide what's worth attention
The meritocratic voting system means interesting content rises and uninteresting content gets a clear signal to improve
This is an early example of a prominent blogger creating a branded community space using Reddit's then-new subreddit infrastructure
money_quotes: If you wrote something interesting that you want to publicize, just put it up on the reddit. If it really is interesting, other people will vote it up and it will get noticed.
If it isn't interesting, go work on making it more interesting and post it again.
Every day I get at least a half dozen emails of the form, 'check out my thing!' with a URL for some startup or widget or interesting article that someone wrote.
I love 'em but I don't always have time to read them and it's vanishingly unlikely that I will ever link to them, because that's not really what this site is all about.
Use your voting power to vote for things you found interesting (and vote down spam, duplicates, and boring things).
posts.key_insight: Community-driven voting systems can solve the problem of content curation at scale, replacing a single person's bottleneck with collective filtering.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces a call for nominations for 'The Best Software Writing, Volume II,' a follow-up to his successful first anthology of great software development writing from around the web. He recaps his motivation: the software world is drowning in poorly written technical books and low-quality online commentary. The first volume was a hit, validating his belief that the community needs and wants better writing about software. He invites readers to nominate their favorite articles via a discussion group before the April 15th deadline.
key_points: The first volume of 'The Best Software Writing' was successful enough to warrant a sequel
Spolsky identifies three categories of bad software writing: bloated multi-author technical books, pretentious academic-style OOP books, and low-quality online rants
The anthology model curates the best writing from across the web rather than commissioning new content
Community participation drives the selection process through open nominations
money_quotes: The software development world desperately needs better writing.
If I have to read another 2000 page book about some class library written by 16 separate people in broken ESL, I'm going to flip out.

If I see another hardback book about object oriented models written with dense faux-academic pretentiousness, I'm not going to shelve it any more in the Fog Creek library: it's going right in the recycle bin.
If I have to read another spirited attack on Microsoft's buggy code by an enthusiastic nine year old Trekkie on Slashdot, I might just poke my eyes out with a sharpened pencil.
Stop it, stop it, stop it!
posts.key_insight: The software industry produces vast amounts of writing but very little of it is good, and curating the best examples serves both as a reward for quality and a rebuke of the mediocre norm.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky writes the foreword to Eric Sink's book on the business of software by telling a personal anecdote about selling donuts at age fourteen during a summer institute. He bought donuts wholesale and sold them at a markup, watching his small business grow organically over the summer, only to have the department chairman confiscate his profits for a party he couldn't attend. Spolsky uses this story as a springboard to describe the thrill of watching a business grow incrementally — from a few customers a day to unmanageable sales reports. He compares growing a software business to gardening, emphasizing the joy of organic, plant-like growth over time.
key_points: The excitement of entrepreneurship comes from watching organic, incremental growth — small daily gains compounding over time
Growing a business is like gardening: plant a seed, tend it daily, and watch it grow from a sprout into something substantial
Key milestones in a software business are mundane but meaningful, like turning off the email notification for each sale because there are too many
Spolsky's donut story illustrates both the natural joy of building something and the injustice of having your work appropriated by those with more power
money_quotes: There is something incredibly exciting about watching a new business grow.
People love growing businesses for the same reason they love gardening.
Last week you made $24. This week you made $26. By this time next year you might be making $100.
One day, you'll turn off the feature that emails you every time someone buys your software. That's a huge milestone.
As you look at the revenues from your business, you'll say, 'gosh, it's only 3:00, and we've already had nine customers! This is going to be the best day ever!'
The moral of the story? Um, there is no moral.
I couldn't believe it!
posts.key_insight: The deepest satisfaction in building a software business comes from watching it grow organically, day by day, from something tiny into something you can barely keep up with.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Joel Spolsky advertises a paid summer internship position in graphic design or web design at Fog Creek Software in New York City. The post asks readers to spread the word to any students who might be interested. There is no substantive argument or analysis — it is purely a job listing promotion.
key_points: Fog Creek Software was offering a paid summer internship in graphic/web design
The internship was located in New York City
Joel used his blog's readership as a recruiting channel
money_quotes: If you know a student in graphic design or web design looking for a paid summer internship in New York City, tell them about Fog Creek!
posts.key_insight: Joel leveraged his popular blog as a direct recruiting tool for Fog Creek internship positions.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that the primary job of management in a software company is to build a 'development abstraction layer' that shields programmers from all non-coding concerns — infrastructure, sales, administration, office logistics — so they can focus purely on writing code. He illustrates this through a parable of a talented programmer who builds flawless software but fails as a solo entrepreneur because he lacks the organizational support layer that turns code into products. Spolsky contrasts the traditional command-and-control management model with his proposed model where management exists to serve developers, not direct them. He argues that companies succeed when they are driven by programmers but organized with excellent support infrastructure beneath them, and that the best abstraction is one that disappears completely.
key_points: A brilliant programmer working alone will fail because writing great code is insufficient — you need an entire organization to turn code into a product that reaches customers
The command-and-control management model is outdated for software companies; management should exist to serve programmers, not to issue orders down a hierarchy
The 'development abstraction layer' means programmers should only think about code while management handles everything else — from office logistics to sales to infrastructure

Companies fail in two symmetric ways: sales-driven companies that never build version 2.0, and programmer-driven companies that polish code in obscurity — the winning model combines programmer leadership with strong organizational support
Roughly 80% of a software company's payroll should be support functions, similar to military ratios of support staff to soldiers
money_quotes: When uttered by a software developer, the term 'marketing' simply stands in for all that business stuff: everything they don't actually understand about creating software and selling it.
Any successful software company is going to consist of a thin layer of developers, creating software, spread across the top of a big abstract administrative organization.
Your first priority as the manager of a software team is building the development abstraction layer.
Management, in a software company, is primarily responsible for creating abstractions for programmers. We build the yacht, we service the yacht, we are the yacht, but we don't steer the yacht.

Microsoft does such a good job at creating this abstraction that Microsoft alumni have a notoriously hard time starting companies. They simply can't believe how much went on below decks and they have no idea how to reproduce it.
Like teaching a pig to sing, it wastes your time and it annoys the pig.

If only 20% of your staff is programmers, and you can save 50% on salary by outsourcing programmers to India, well, how much of a competitive advantage are you really going to get out of that 10% savings?
The command-hierarchy system of management has been tried, and it seemed to work for a while in the 1920s, competing against peddlers pushing carts, but it's not good enough for the 21st century.
posts.key_insight: Management's job in a software company is not to command programmers but to build a seamless support infrastructure that creates the illusion that writing code is all it takes to ship a product.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky posts a brief tribute to novelist Muriel Spark upon her death, sharing a single extended passage from her novel 'The Finishing School.' The excerpt features a creative writing teacher instructing students on scene-setting, advising restraint and patience before introducing emphasis or point-of-view. The post is purely appreciative, offering the quote without commentary, letting Spark's prose speak for itself as a demonstration of her craft.
key_points: The post is a literary tribute rather than a technology essay, showing Spolsky's breadth of interests beyond software
The Spark passage illustrates the principle of restraint in creative work: set the scene before making your point
The excerpt's lesson — don't add emphasis too early — parallels good technical writing and design, where clarity precedes style
money_quotes: You begin by setting your scene. You have to see your scene, either in reality or in imagination.
Don't make any emphasis as yet. It's too soon.
You are setting your scene. You don't want to make a point as yet.
posts.key_insight: Good craft — whether in writing or any creative discipline — requires the discipline to establish context before asserting a point.

posts.summary: Joel announces the screening of 'Aardvark'd,' a documentary about Fog Creek's summer interns, at the New York International Independent Film and Video Festival. He describes how the film is used as a recruiting tool for new intern candidates. He also highlights the scale and perks of their internship program, which received hundreds of applications and offers generous benefits including free housing, high-end equipment, and cultural events.
key_points: Fog Creek uses the Aardvark'd documentary as a recruiting tool, giving it to interview candidates to showcase the internship experience
The internship program is highly competitive with hundreds of applicants, and they expanded to include graphic design and program management roles
Fog Creek offers lavish intern perks including free housing, Aeron chairs, high-end workstations, and weekly cultural outings to attract top talent
money_quotes: Despite the filmmaker's fondness for stories about tomato plants and cockroaches, the movie works great as a recruiting tool.
We had hundreds of applications, and we had so many good candidates we only interviewed about half of the candidates we wanted to before the new office space filled up.
Interns get free housing, free lunch, Aeron chairs, dual-Xeon workstations with 30″ LCD monitors, and weekly cultural events like Broadway shows, a Yankees game, and tours around New York City.
posts.key_insight: Investing in generous intern perks and employer branding through media like documentaries creates a self-reinforcing recruiting advantage that attracts top talent.

posts.summary: Joel uses a frustrating experience with Cingular's customer service to illustrate how unhandled exceptions in software mirror broken organizational systems. When software fails with a generic 'call customer service' message, and customer service has no idea what went wrong, it creates an impossible loop. He argues this is a systemic management failure where outsourced call centers are disconnected from outsourced development teams, making feedback impossible. He contrasts this with Fog Creek's approach where bug reports go directly to developers and customer service sits next to programmers.
key_points: Generic error messages that say 'call customer service' are the software equivalent of an unhandled exception — they punt the problem without providing useful information
When software development and customer service are outsourced to different companies, there is no feedback loop for bugs to reach the people who can fix them
US wireless companies are universally broken because incompetent management has built brittle, over-complicated systems with too many outsourced moving parts
The solution is direct channels between customers, support staff, and developers — not layers of organizational indirection
money_quotes: Of course they don't. It's a friggin' unhandled exception.
One of the hallmarks of a broken system is when there's just no possible way that the programmer who is writing code that talks to customers can ever get feedback from those customers about bugs.
Everyone is trying their hardest to do their job but management has set it up so that it's impossible.

All of them suffer from hard working shlubs who are stuck in a situation created by incompetent managers, who have built esoteric mountains of complicated and brittle systems with a million moving parts, 3/4's of them outsourced, where it's simply impossible to get anything done.
What does your code do to contact you when things go badly wrong?
But even if you call our customer service line you get a human who is sitting right next to the programmer.
posts.key_insight: When the people who write the code are organizationally disconnected from the people who hear about its failures, the system is fundamentally broken — no amount of hard work by individuals can compensate for a management structure that prevents feedback from reaching developers.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief announcement post noting that FogBugz 5.0 has shipped. Joel acknowledges that the release is more of an incremental update (closer to version 4.5) but they chose to call it 5.0 to avoid confusing fractional version numbers. The post is essentially a one-liner linking to a longer article about the release.
key_points: FogBugz 5.0 shipped, though Joel admits it's more like a 4.5 release in scope
Version numbering is a pragmatic marketing decision, not strictly a reflection of change magnitude
Joel uses humor to deflect expectations about the size of the update
money_quotes: What's shipping today is really something like FogBugz 4½, but we're calling it 5.0 anyway, because life is confusing enough without fractions.
posts.key_insight: Version numbers are practical marketing tools, not precise measurements of change, and sometimes simplicity in naming trumps accuracy.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky briefly reacts to the announcement that Bill Gates will step down from Microsoft. He notes that Microsoft's stock is surprisingly stable despite the news, argues it should be declining, and offers his assessment that Ray Ozzie, while smart, is not in Gates' league. He concludes with the provocative claim that Steve Ballmer is the one who really needs to leave.
key_points: Microsoft's stock price showed surprising stability despite the major leadership announcement
Ray Ozzie is capable but not a comparable replacement for Bill Gates
Steve Ballmer, not Gates, is the real leadership problem at Microsoft
money_quotes: Ozzie is smart but not in the same class as Bill Gates.
And it's really Ballmer that needs to go.
posts.key_insight: Gates stepping down was less concerning for Microsoft's future than the continued leadership of Steve Ballmer.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky responds to Paul Boutin's article about struggling with remote desktop assistance software, which took most of an hour to configure. Joel recommends Copilot (Fog Creek's own product) as a simpler alternative that is secure by default, works through firewalls, and takes only minutes to set up.
key_points: Remote desktop tools shouldn't require an hour of installation and configuration
Good software should be secure out of the box without complex setup
Products that 'just work' through firewalls and NAT are superior to those requiring network configuration
money_quotes: Secure out of the box, works through all firewalls, and never takes more than a couple of minutes to get working.
posts.key_insight: Software that requires extensive configuration to accomplish a basic task represents a design failure — the best tools just work out of the box.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky recounts his experience as a Program Manager at Microsoft in the early 1990s, where he was tasked with designing what became Visual Basic for Applications (VBA) in Excel. He describes the technical decisions behind the language design, including adding Variants, late binding, For Each, and With statements. The centerpiece of the story is his BillG review, where Bill Gates had read his entire 500-page spec overnight and asked increasingly difficult questions, culminating in a question about date functions that Joel could answer only because he'd stumbled onto the Lotus 1-2-3 leap year bug the day before. The post uses this anecdote to argue that technical founders who understand their products deeply are far superior to MBA-type managers who treat management as a generic skill.
key_points: Joel designed key features of what became VBA for Excel, including Variants, late binding (IDispatch/COM Automation), For Each, and With statements
The infamous 1900 leap year bug in Excel was deliberately inherited from Lotus 1-2-3 for compatibility, while Visual Basic used a different epoch to be technically correct
Bill Gates personally read a 500-page spec overnight and wrote margin notes on every page, demonstrating extraordinary technical engagement from a CEO
Gates's review strategy was to ask progressively harder questions until someone admitted ignorance — Joel happened to be the first person to answer the hardest question
The post argues that non-technical leaders running software companies is fundamentally dysfunctional, comparing it to someone who can't surf trying to surf
money_quotes: Bill doesn't really want to review your spec, he just wants to make sure you've got it under control. His standard M.O. is to ask harder and harder questions until you admit that you don't know, and then he can yell at you for being unprepared.
Nobody was really sure what happens if you answer the hardest question he can come up with because it's never happened before.
Watching non-programmers trying to run software companies is like watching someone who doesn't know how to surf trying to surf.
He didn't meddle in software if he trusted the people who were working on it, but you couldn't bullshit him for a minute because he was a programmer. A real, actual, programmer.
The cult of the MBA likes to believe that you can run organizations that do things that you don't understand.
Is Ballmer going to be another John Sculley, who nearly drove Apple into extinction because the board of directors thought that selling Pepsi was good preparation for running a computer company?
Four, announced the f*** counter, and everyone said, wow, that's the lowest I can remember. Bill is getting mellow in his old age.
posts.key_insight: Software companies need leaders who are deeply technical enough to understand the product at every level, because you cannot effectively manage what you do not understand.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares Nick Gravgaard's 'elastic tabstops' proposal as a solution to the perennial tabs-vs-spaces debate in programming. The post is extremely brief, essentially just a link to Gravgaard's idea that tab characters should be treated as delimiters between table cells rather than fixed-width spacing. By reframing tabs as structural delimiters rather than visual formatting, the proposal attempts to sidestep the holy war entirely.
key_points: The tabs-vs-spaces debate can potentially be resolved by redefining what a tab character means
Elastic tabstops treat tabs as table cell delimiters rather than fixed-width cursor movements
The post is a brief endorsement-by-sharing of Nick Gravgaard's concept
money_quotes: Rather than saying that a tab character (a "hard tab") will move the cursor until the cursor's position is a multiple of N characters, we should say that a tab character is a delimiter between table cells
posts.key_insight: The tabs-vs-spaces debate might be resolved by reconceptualizing tabs as structural table cell delimiters rather than fixed-width spacing characters.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces an open house event at Fog Creek Software's newly expanded office space. He invites readers to visit, meet the team and summer interns, and enjoy snacks and wine. The post is a brief, informal invitation with event details including date, time, and address in midtown Manhattan.
key_points: Fog Creek Software completed a major office expansion
The company hosts summer interns as part of its talent pipeline
Joel uses his popular blog as a direct channel to invite the tech community to company events
money_quotes: If you'd like to check it out, meet the Fog Creek team and this year's crop of bright-eyed-and-bushy-tailed summer interns, please come to our open house.
posts.key_insight: Fog Creek leveraged its blog audience and office culture as recruiting and community-building tools, turning a simple office expansion into a public event.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky describes how system administration tasks have consumed his time at Fog Creek Software, with a simple task like creating an email address taking an entire day due to the complexity of their infrastructure. He announces that Fog Creek needs to hire a dedicated full-time system/network administrator. The post doubles as a job listing, detailing the mixed-platform environment and extensive technology stack the role would manage. Joel uses the opportunity to showcase Fog Creek's developer-friendly benefits and culture.
key_points: Even a technically skilled CEO can become bottlenecked by sysadmin work that should be delegated, with 75% of Joel's to-do list consumed by infrastructure tasks.
Fog Creek runs a complex mixed environment spanning Windows, Linux, NetBSD, and Macintosh with a wide array of services from DNS to SQL Server.

The company's hiring pitch emphasizes quality-of-life benefits like private offices, Aeron chairs, free lunch, and four weeks vacation — reflecting Joel's philosophy that great workplaces attract great talent.
Joel values practical skill over credentials, explicitly stating they don't care about certifications.
money_quotes: Yesterday, the net sum of what I managed to accomplish consisted, approximately, of creating one new email address.
The combination of Postfix + Cyrus + ActiveDirectory authentication over LDAP is a little bit too much for my rapidly shrinking brain to handle.
But still, it's gotten to the point where my personal to do list now consists of 75% system administration tasks.
There's a big stack of seven Dell PowerEdge 2850 servers outside my office that need to be configured and installed somewhere. This is getting ridiculous.
We don't care about certifications.
posts.key_insight: When a company founder's time is consumed by operational tasks outside their core role, it's a clear signal to hire a specialist rather than continuing to do it yourself poorly.

posts.summary: Joel describes his experience setting up a Google Mini search appliance to power search on his blog. He finds the setup process mostly straightforward but notes several rough edges, including an infuriating IP configuration procedure and a clunky software upgrade process. He's impressed that the device ranks results well despite not using Google's full infrastructure, though a later correction notes it doesn't actually phone home for PageRank data. The post is essentially a casual product review that touches briefly on the broader concept of hardware appliances versus software solutions.
key_points: The Google Mini produced good search rankings entirely from local crawling, without accessing Google's PageRank infrastructure
Setup was reasonable but marred by frustrating procedures for IP configuration and software upgrades that didn't match the 'appliance' promise
The administration UI was complex and geeky rather than polished and Google-friendly
Joel hints at a broader question about whether appliance-based solutions are preferable to pure software solutions
money_quotes: An hour or two to get the appliance racked up, most of which was spent following an infuriating procedure to get the IP address on the thing set up.
Hey, Google, it's on the network, why can't it upgrade itself?
I tried to make it so that when you search for "mindless complexity" you get the search results for ".NET", because I am the KING of witty.
The administration UI is, well, not pretty or GoogleFriendly. It's more High Geek.
What do you think about the idea of appliances instead of software?
posts.key_insight: Even Google struggles to make a true 'appliance' experience — the gap between appliance promise and software reality shows up in clunky setup procedures and administration interfaces that betray the underlying complexity.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky excoriates Travelers Insurance for running an ad in Inc. Magazine that stereotypes geeks as threats and criminals. The ad depicted a poorly-dressed person and warned business owners about 'high-tech criminals,' implying geeks are dangerous. Spolsky argues the ad is offensive on multiple levels: it demonizes the people who build technology, it reflects high-school bully mentality, and it insults the very audience reading Inc. Magazine. He points out the irony of insurance salesmen claiming to protect against geeks, and concludes by announcing he's taking his business elsewhere.
key_points: The ad perpetuates harmful stereotypes by equating geeks with criminals and threats to business
Insurance salesmen mocking geeks is ironic given their own actuaries are geeks, and they're unqualified to 'protect' anyone from technical threats
The ad fundamentally misreads its audience, since Inc. Magazine readers are largely the tech founders and geeks being mocked
Anti-intellectual attitudes have become normalized, extending from politics into corporate advertising
Consumers who feel insulted by a company's advertising will take their business elsewhere
money_quotes: Am I nuts, or is this ad really offensive?
What is this, high school? With the bullies who fail all their classes have such an inferiority complex they have to make fun of the geeks?
I can't really think of anyone less qualified to 'protect' you from 'geeks' than a bunch of insurance salesmen.
Have you looked at your own actuaries? Have you noticed how insurance people dress?
Can you imagine anyone more harmless than a geek?

Maybe the current Bush presidency has given you the idea that it's ok to make fun of the scientists, inventors, researchers and programmers who are creating the future, finding cures for your diseases, building the spreadsheets you use to figure out how much commission you're making.
You probably forgot that most of the people that read Inc. are geeks. And we buy insurance. Lots of insurance.
And in fact I used to buy it from you. But not any more.
posts.key_insight: Companies that insult their own customers in advertising deserve to lose them, and mocking the technical people who build the modern world reflects a deeply misguided anti-intellectual culture.

posts.summary: Spolsky responds to Microsoft's 'Workplace Advantage' initiative, which reclassifies employees into four types to determine optimal workspace configurations. He acknowledges that private offices aren't ideal for every role, but firmly argues they remain essential for programmers. Drawing on his own experience with Project Aardvark interns, he describes how open spaces destroy programmer concentration by turning every two-person conversation into a group interruption. He reports that switching to more private workspaces for summer interns improved productivity without hurting communication.
key_points: Microsoft's Workplace Advantage categorizes employees into four types (travelers, orchestrators, concentrators, providers) to match workspace to work style
Private offices aren't right for every role, but remain the most productive environment for programming work
Open spaces eliminate the possibility of private conversations—every interaction becomes a group interruption that breaks flow
Formal methods like weekly status emails and informal ones like shared lunches can keep people in the loop without destroying concentration
Switching Project Aardvark interns from open space to private niches improved focus without noticeably hurting communication
money_quotes: There's not much debate that this is the most productive environment for programmers, but not everyone at Microsoft is a programmer.
Don't fall for it. They also want M&Ms for breakfast and a pony.
Open space is fun but not productive.
Every time I went out there to talk to one of them, it became a conversation with all of them.
Although this slightly helps keep everyone 'in the loop,' it also knocks programmers out of flow causing them to lose their concentration and devastating productivity.
I prefer to keep people in the loop using more formal methods, like weekly email status reports, and through informal methods like eating lunch together every day.
posts.key_insight: Programmers who say they prefer open offices are wrong about what makes them productive—private spaces preserve flow state, and communication can be maintained through deliberate mechanisms like shared meals and status updates.

posts.summary: Spolsky revisits his long-standing complaint that Dell's website forces customers to identify their 'segment' (home, small business, large business) before shopping, creating a frustrating experience. Dell's own blog acknowledged customers hate this but defended it as creating 'greater value.' Spolsky argues the real reason is price discrimination — charging business customers more for the same products. He points out that competitors like Apple sell to everyone at the same price without segmentation. The negotiation-heavy purchasing process means Dell loses easy online sales because customers have been trained to haggle. He concludes that the website designers lack the authority to fix what is fundamentally a top-down business strategy decision.
key_points: Dell's customer segmentation on their website is really about price discrimination, not about providing better service or relevant products
Customers resent feeling ripped off more than they resent high prices — uniform pricing (like Apple's) feels fairer even when expensive
Dell's negotiation-heavy sales process costs them easy online revenue and forces customers into adversarial purchasing behavior
Website UX problems often can't be fixed by designers when they stem from fundamental business model decisions made at the top
Competitors like Apple and Lenovo successfully sell computers online without requiring customers to identify their 'type'
money_quotes: This business about creating 'greater value' is a bit of a whitewash. We all know exactly what's going on. They're trying to charge business customers more.
You feel less ripped off buying a way-overpriced Apple MacBook Pro because everyone else is paying the same price.
By the time we place our order the price we pay is about 20% to 30% less than the price advertised on the web, and we're still not sure if we could have paid less.

I'd love to just order the damn servers from their website, clicking on the links to configure it. Dell would have my money sooner and wouldn't have to pay any sales people to talk to me on the phone.
You've trained me to negotiate every time if I don't want to pay the sucker price, so now I have no choice.
The poor designers working on fixing the website clearly don't have the authority to change the way Dell does business.
posts.key_insight: When a website's UX problems are actually manifestations of the company's business model — like price discrimination through customer segmentation — no amount of design improvement can fix them without changing the underlying business strategy.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky walks through a progressive tutorial on first-class functions in JavaScript, starting from simple code deduplication and building up to anonymous functions, map, and reduce. He argues that languages supporting first-class functions enable developers to find more abstraction opportunities, producing smaller, more reusable code. The post connects these functional programming concepts directly to Google's MapReduce, arguing that abstracting away looping enables massive parallelization. He uses this as evidence for why CS students need exposure to functional programming, not just Java. The post culminates in the claim that the most productive programming environments allow working at different levels of abstraction.
key_points: First-class functions let you pass behavior as arguments, enabling more powerful abstractions than simple value parameterization
Anonymous inline functions eliminate the friction of declaring functions elsewhere, making functional patterns practical and readable
Map and reduce are fundamental abstractions that separate what to do from how to iterate, enabling implementation-level optimizations like parallelization
Google's MapReduce was obvious to anyone with functional programming training, illustrating how CS education shapes what engineers can invent
Languages that force workarounds for first-class functions (C's function pointers, Java's functors) impose enough friction to discourage the abstractions entirely
money_quotes: Now you need a way to pass an argument to the function which itself is a function. This is an important capability, because it increases the chances that you'll be able to find common code that can be stashed away in a function.
Jeez, that is handy. Notice that I'm creating a function there on the fly, not even bothering to name it, just picking it up by its ears and tossing it into a function.
If your programming language requires you to use functors, you're not getting all the benefits of a modern programming environment. See if you can get some of your money back.
By abstracting away the very concept of looping, you can implement looping any way you want, including implementing it in a way that scales nicely with extra hardware.
Without understanding functional programming, you can't invent MapReduce, the algorithm that makes Google so massively scalable.

The very fact that Google invented MapReduce, and Microsoft didn't, says something about why Microsoft is still playing catch up trying to get basic search features to work, while Google has moved on to the next problem: building Skynet the world's largest massively parallel supercomputer.
Programming languages with first-class functions let you find more opportunities for abstraction, which means your code is smaller, tighter, more reusable, and more scalable.
The most productive programming environments are the ones that let you work at different levels of abstraction.
posts.key_insight: First-class functions aren't just syntactic sugar — they enable abstractions like map and reduce that fundamentally change what's possible, from cleaner code to massively parallel computation.

posts.summary: Joel posts two brief corrections to previous statements. First, he clarifies that his criticism of FORTRAN not allowing functions applied to very old versions, not modern ones, acknowledging he last used WATFOR 27 years prior. Second, he corrects his claim that the Google Mini communicated with Google for PageRank data, noting that Google's product managers confirmed the device deliberately avoids 'phoning home' to protect intranet customers' privacy.
key_points: Old FORTRAN (like WATFOR) had significant limitations that newer versions of the language addressed
The Google Mini was designed to be fully self-contained and does not communicate back to Google, specifically to serve intranet customers
Joel demonstrates willingness to publicly correct factual errors in his writing
money_quotes: They are very religious about NOT phoning home to Google under any circumstances.
posts.key_insight: Public corrections, even minor ones, are worth issuing promptly to maintain credibility and accuracy.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky explains why there won't be a second edition of 'The Best Software Writing' in 2006. After reviewing nominations, he found insufficient quality material for a full book, with half of what he found coming from a single author. He identifies five reasons for the shortage: good writing is shifting to fragmented blog formats that don't work as essays, there's too much bad writing to sift through, programming magazines have declined in quality, and he lacks the patience to find hidden gems.
key_points: Blog-native writing increasingly relies on hypertext elements (links, quotes, comments) that don't translate well to book form, making the traditional essay format increasingly rare
The decline of programming magazines has reduced the pool of polished, edited technical writing
The signal-to-noise ratio in software writing is extremely low, making curation exhausting
Joel redirects the community to Reddit as a crowd-sourced alternative to his personal curation
money_quotes: When I was done, I was extremely depressed to discover that there wasn't enough material for a whole new book, and what I did find was 50% Stevey.
The old idea of an essay with a beginning, a middle, and an end is increasingly rare.
I'm pretty crabby and have high standards, and slogging through gallons of bad writing just to find the occasional gem is something I don't think I'm going to live long enough to do.
A lot of programmers' magazines have disappeared and those that are left, like MSDN, have deteriorated to the point where I'd rather read the average Oracle knowledge base article.
There's a lot of terrible writing out there.
posts.key_insight: The shift from polished essays to fragmented blog-native hypertext was already eroding the quality and coherence of software writing by 2006, foreshadowing broader challenges with online content curation.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky introduces a series on three management methods by framing the fundamental challenge of leadership: getting people to move in the same direction despite having different personal agendas. He illustrates this with examples from startups, government, and the military, showing how individual incentives naturally diverge from organizational goals. He sets up three approaches he'll explore in subsequent posts: Command and Control, Econ 101, and the Identity Method. The post serves as a teaser that names the frameworks without yet explaining them.
key_points: The core problem of management is alignment: getting people with different personal agendas to move in the same direction.
Misaligned incentives exist everywhere — startups, government, military — not just in any one type of organization.
Individual motivations (money, interesting work, comfort, safety) naturally pull against collective goals.
There are three common management approaches: Command and Control, Econ 101, and the Identity Method.
No single method is universally best; each has pros and cons worth examining.
money_quotes: If you want to lead a team, a company, an army, or a country, the primary problem you face is getting everyone moving in the same direction, which is really just a polite way of saying 'getting people to do what you want.'
As soon as your team consists of more than one person, you're going to have different people with different agendas.
The problem of getting people to move in your direction (or, at least, the same direction) is not unique to startups, of course.
The mayor wants to make sure that it's easy to get city approval of a new building project. The city building inspectors want to keep getting the bribes they have grown accustomed to.
They might want a team of soldiers to charge at the enemy, even when every individual soldier would really just rather cower behind a rock and let the others do the charging.
posts.key_insight: The fundamental challenge of all leadership is aligning people who have divergent personal incentives toward a common direction.

posts.summary: Spolsky examines the Command and Control management style borrowed from the military and explains why it fails in software development. He identifies three key problems: developers resent being ordered around, managers lack bandwidth to micromanage individual contributors doing different tasks, and the people closest to the work have the most information to make good decisions. He then explains why the military actually uses this method — to override individual self-preservation instincts in life-or-death situations like charging through a minefield — and argues that this rationale simply doesn't apply to software teams where developers can leave anytime.
key_points: Command and Control creates 'hit and run micromanagement' where managers briefly derail developers then disappear, leaving them to reassemble their disrupted work
In technical organizations, individual contributors always have more information than managers, making top-down technical decisions inherently inferior
The military uses Command and Control not because it's optimal management, but because it's the only way to override soldiers' rational self-interest in suicidal situations
Software developers can work anywhere they want, which removes the coercive leverage that makes Command and Control functional in the military
money_quotes: In software development teams everybody is working on something else, so attempts to micromanage turn into hit and run micromanagement.

Effectively, all you accomplish is to knock your poor programmers off the train track every once in a while, so they spend the next week finding all their train cars and putting them back on the tracks.

When the boss wanders into an office where two developers have been arguing for two hours about the best way to compress an image, the person with the least information is the boss, so that's the last person you'd want making a technical decision.

This forced people to debate the issue on the merits and issues were always resolved in favor of the person who was better at arguing, er, I mean, issues were always resolved in the best possible way.
The military uses Command and Control because it's the only way to get 18 year olds to charge through a minefield, not because they think it's the best management method for every situation.
In software development teams where good developers can work anywhere they want, playing soldier is going to get pretty tedious and you're not really going to keep anyone on your team.
posts.key_insight: Command and Control management exists to override rational self-interest in life-or-death situations, a condition that never applies in software development, making it both unnecessary and counterproductive for technical teams.

posts.summary: Spolsky presents 'The Identity Method' as the superior management approach for knowledge workers, contrasting it with the previously discussed Command and Control and Econ 101 methods. The technique works by making employees identify with organizational goals, creating intrinsic motivation rather than subverting it. He illustrates this with concrete examples from Fog Creek: shared daily lunches that build family-like bonds, an internship program designed to make people identify as New Yorkers, and sharing financial information so employees can make good decisions autonomously. The key mechanism is twofold: build a cohesive team identity, then give people the information they need to steer correctly. Spolsky argues this is harder than other methods but produces better results, noting that in six years nobody has quit Fog Creek.
key_points: Identity Management creates intrinsic motivation by making employees identify with organizational goals, unlike Econ 101 management which subverts it
Building team identity requires concrete rituals like shared meals, social activities, and creating a family-like sense of loyalty and commitment
The second essential component is information sharing: giving employees enough context about business realities that they can make good decisions autonomously
Sharing information is superior to incentives or commands because it allows employees to adapt their decisions as circumstances change
Organizational goals that are perceived as virtuous help create identification, even if the virtue is somewhat superficial
money_quotes: The problem with Econ 101 management is that it subverts intrinsic motivation. The Identity Method is a way to create intrinsic motivation.
It's hard to understate what a big impact this has on making the company feel like a family, in the good way, I think. In six years, nobody has ever quit.
The point being that by sharing information, I can get Brett to do the right thing for Fog Creek even if circumstances change.
If I tried to push him around by offering him a cash reward for every day before April that he ships, his incentive would be to dump the existing buggy development build on the public tonight.

To be an Identity Method manager, you have to summon all the social skills you have to make your employees identify with the goals of the organization, so that they are highly motivated, then you need to give them the information they need to steer in the right direction.
I've identified three major styles: two easy, dysfunctional styles and one hard, functional style.
posts.key_insight: The most effective management creates intrinsic motivation by building team identity and then sharing enough business context that employees can autonomously make good decisions, even as circumstances change.

posts.summary: Joel describes a practical problem at Fog Creek: their office library has grown so disorganized that it's easier to buy a new copy from Amazon than find a book they already own. He's looking for software that can scan ISBN barcodes, look up catalog information online, and print spine stickers with Library of Congress numbers so books can be properly shelved. The post is essentially a public request for software recommendations, directed at his readership.
key_points: Fog Creek's office library has grown large enough that finding books is harder than reordering them from Amazon
Joel wants a barcode-scanning workflow that automates cataloging: scan ISBN, fetch metadata, print spine labels
The post illustrates how even simple organizational problems at small companies can become friction points that waste money
money_quotes: It's actually easier to order a new copy of a book from Amazon than to find a book we already have!
posts.key_insight: When finding something you already own costs more effort than buying it again, you have an organizational problem worth solving with software.

posts.summary: Spolsky reviews two Macintosh library management applications, Delicious Library and Booxter, for cataloging a physical book collection. While Delicious Library has charming visual design and easy barcode scanning, it lacks Library of Congress integration, which Spolsky considers essential for organizing books by topic. Booxter provides this LOC integration but neither app can print spine labels, creating a workflow gap. The post illustrates how a visually appealing product can lose to a plainer competitor that solves the actual problem.
key_points: Delicious Library wins on visual design but lacks Library of Congress catalog number import, making it insufficient for proper book organization
Library of Congress classification is valuable because it groups books by topic, enabling browsable shelves rather than arbitrary ordering
Booxter has a simpler UI but solves the core problem by connecting to the Library of Congress
Neither app supports printing spine labels at scan time, which would be the natural workflow for actually shelving books correctly
