money_quotes: Delicious Library does not have any way to import data from the Library of Congress.
When you do this, books on similar topics tend to wind up near each other on the shelves.
Somebody has gone to a lot of trouble choosing a card catalog number that put similar books next to each other.

I've always found that open-shelved libraries with books in order according to some reasonable card catalog system are far superior to libraries with books in order according to acquisition date, size, color, IQ, or IDENTITY column.
Printing a spine label at scan time would make for a vastly easier workflow.
posts.key_insight: A visually delightful application that misses the user's core workflow need will lose to a plainer one that solves the actual problem.

posts.summary: Joel observes that online services almost never include instructions for canceling accounts, treating it like a superstition that hiding cancellation will retain customers. He argues this is the result of misapplied A/B testing: companies can easily measure that telling people how to cancel increases cancellations, but they can't measure how many potential customers never sign up because they fear cancellation will be difficult. Joel shares Fog Creek's approach of offering unconditional money-back guarantees and easy cancellation, noting it has cost only 2% of revenue over six years.
key_points: FAQ pages for online services systematically omit cancellation instructions, as if hiding the option will prevent churn
A/B tests can measure the visible cost of easy cancellation but cannot measure the invisible cost of lost signups from customers who fear being trapped
This is a classic case of measuring benefit while ignoring cost — or vice versa — leading to poor business decisions
Fog Creek's unconditional 90-day money-back guarantee has cost only 2% of revenue, including chargebacks and fraud
Reducing perceived risk for new customers is more valuable than trapping existing ones
money_quotes: It's like they're all subject to the same bizarre superstition … if you don't tell people how to cancel, maybe they'll lose interest and keep paying you.
If you want to attract new customers, you have to give them the confidence that there is no risk in signing up or converting from whatever they were using before.
What's not easy to test is how many people never sign up in the first place because they're afraid that canceling is going to be a nightmare.
So what you have here is a somebody doing a cost/benefit analysis where they are measuring the benefit while ignoring the cost.
You may cancel at any time, on the Web, without hassle.
The moneyback guarantee has cost us precisely 2% of revenues, which also includes chargebacks, credit card fraud, and people who accidentally ordered twice.
I'm only measuring the cost, because the benefit is too hard to measure!
posts.key_insight: Companies that hide cancellation options are optimizing based on measurable churn while ignoring the unmeasurable cost of potential customers who never sign up because they fear being trapped.

posts.summary: This is a brief link post where Joel Spolsky highlights two notable observations from other writers. David Pogue criticizes LG's poorly designed control system that requires seven warnings in the manual, implying the design itself is flawed. Eric Sink challenges the conventional wisdom around word-of-mouth marketing, arguing that most advice simply states that buzz is good without explaining how to generate it. Joel curates these links without additional commentary, letting the quotes speak for themselves.
key_points: If a product's interface requires extensive warnings in the manual, the design itself needs to be reconsidered rather than documented around
Most marketing advice about word-of-mouth is circular — it explains why buzz is good but not how to create it
Eric Sink draws an analogy between buzz advice and telling people what rich people can buy — stating obvious outcomes without explaining how to achieve them
money_quotes: Listen up, LG dudes: I'm sorry, but if your primary control system requires seven warnings in your manual, maybe you should reconsider your system.
Everybody knows how powerful word-of-mouth and buzz can be.
This is a little bit like explaining the nice things that rich people can buy. It is obvious that good results are good. We don't need to be told that.
What we need to know is how to make those good results happen.
posts.key_insight: Good design shouldn't need extensive warnings, and good advice shouldn't just tell you that good outcomes are desirable — both should focus on how to get things right.

posts.summary: Spolsky briefly references a Harvard Business School piece asking whether it's better for leaders to be loved or feared. Rather than engaging with the question itself, he uses it as a springboard to criticize HBS for producing vague, non-committal conclusions. He quotes their wishy-washy takeaway about 'expanding self-awareness' as an example of saying nothing bold or useful.
key_points: Harvard Business School research tends to produce safe, non-committal conclusions that avoid taking a real stance
Academic management advice often dresses up obvious truisms in complex language to appear insightful
Bold, clear thinking is more valuable than hedged, inoffensive pronouncements
money_quotes: The annoying thing about everything that comes out of Harvard Business School is that the conclusion is so infuriatingly wishy-washy.
"Expanding your self-awareness, situational awareness, and ability to adapt your leadership style increases your overall range of effectiveness as a leader," they say. What a bold thought.
posts.key_insight: Academic management research often produces conclusions so hedged and obvious that they offer no actionable insight whatsoever.

posts.summary: Joel shares his three essential Firefox extensions: IETab for rendering IE-only sites within Firefox, Chris Pederick's Web Developer Extension for testing websites, and Adblock for removing flashing advertisements. He praises IETab for solving compatibility issues with Microsoft sites and the Web Developer Extension as indispensable for web development. His discussion of Adblock leads to a nuanced take on web advertising — he doesn't oppose ads ideologically but objects to flashing ads that exploit evolutionary visual reflexes to cause headaches.
key_points: IETab solves the IE compatibility problem by embedding Internet Explorer's rendering engine directly inside Firefox tabs
The Web Developer Extension is presented as essential for web development, offering quick access to testing tools like screen resizing, disabling JavaScript/CSS, and removing form constraints
Flashing advertisements exploit an evolutionary reflex — human vision is wired to attend to movement in the periphery — making them literally painful to ignore
Joel draws a distinction between acceptable advertising and hostile advertising, arguing the problem isn't commerce but disrespect for the reader's experience
money_quotes: It is impossible to imagine developing web software without this.
I don't have some kind of ideological opposition to commerce or advertising.
Human eyes and brain has evolved together so that your attention is drawn to things that move.
Everything about vision is designed to force you to look at things in the periphery, like lions and snakes and stuff, that might be a threat.
When you're trying to read a page with flashing crap in the corners, you can literally get a headache trying to drag your eyes back to the content that's not flashing.
You're trying to defeat millions of years of evolution and it's painful.
If websites and advertisers have so little respect for me that they are willing to subject me to headaches just to sell a few more dancing hamsters, well, to hell with 'em.
posts.key_insight: Flashing web advertisements are not merely annoying but fundamentally hostile to users, exploiting millions of years of visual evolution to hijack attention at the cost of physical discomfort.

posts.summary: Spolsky addresses the common question of which technology stack to choose for enterprise web applications. He argues that the debate between platforms like .NET, Java, and PHP is largely a waste of time because all three (plus Python as a half-entry) are proven at scale and equally capable of delivering success. The real differentiator is team expertise, not language superiority. He warns against choosing unproven or niche languages for mission-critical work, citing Ruby on Rails as risky and Lisp as a cautionary tale. He concludes that the best language is whichever one your team knows best, and that time spent debating language choice is time wasted.
key_points: Three and a half platforms (C#, Java, PHP, and half-Python) are all equally viable for serious enterprise web development, and nobody fails because of choosing among them.
Language debates are entertaining but ultimately fruitless — the choice should be driven by your team's existing expertise, not theoretical superiority.
Unproven or niche languages like Ruby on Rails and Lisp are risky for mission-critical systems due to limited ecosystem maturity, scaling unknowns, and hiring challenges.
Having at least one experienced architect who deeply knows your chosen platform matters far more than which platform you choose.
Spolsky reveals FogBugz runs on Wasabi, a private in-house language — an ironic twist given his advice to stick with mainstream choices.
money_quotes: People all over the world are constantly building web applications using .NET, using Java, and using PHP all the time. None of them are failing because of the choice of technology.

There are three and a half platforms (C#, Java, PHP, and a half Python) that are all equally likely to make you successful, an infinity of platforms where you're pretty much guaranteed to fail spectacularly when it's too late to change anything.
These debates are enormously fun and a total and utter waste of time.
The only real difference is which one you know better.

I know that typically on new projects there's a long evaluation period where you decide what technology to use, along with lots of debates that include some crazy person actually wasting quite a lot of time evaluating Squeak and Lisp and OCaml.

Paul told you that he made his app in Lisp and then he made millions of dollars because he made his app in Lisp, but honestly only two people ever believed him and, a complete rewrite later, they won't make that mistake again.
The benefit was that not one minute was spent debating the merits of programming languages, a fruitless debate if I've ever seen one.
posts.key_insight: Technology stack debates are a waste of time because all mainstream platforms work fine at scale — the only thing that matters is how well your team knows the one you pick.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky explains why Fog Creek built Wasabi, a custom programming language and compiler for FogBugz. Because FogBugz is deployed on customers' own servers across diverse environments, they needed to target multiple platforms (VBScript on Windows, PHP4/PHP5 on Unix) without maintaining separate codebases. Wasabi compiles a single codebase—backwards-compatible with VBScript but with modern features like closures and lambdas—to all target platforms. Joel argues that building a custom compiler is only about two months of work for one skilled developer, and the benefits of domain-specific languages extend well beyond their particular use case.
key_points: FogBugz's on-premise deployment model forces targeting the lowest common denominator across diverse server environments, making a cross-compiling language practical
Wasabi is backwards-compatible with VBScript but adds modern language features like closures, lambdas, and embedded SQL
Writing a single-purpose compiler is far less work than people assume—about two months for one developer—because it only needs to compile one codebase
The same code can compile to both server-side and client-side (JavaScript) targets, eliminating duplication for features like Ajax previews
Domain-specific languages are broadly useful whenever your problem domain is sufficiently different from what general-purpose tools serve well
money_quotes: Since we don't want to program in VBScript or PHP4 or even PHP5 and we certainly don't want to have to port everything to three target platforms, the best solution for us is a custom language that compiles to our target platforms.
Since we are not blub programmers, we like closures, active records, lambdas, embedded SQL a la LINQ, etc. etc. and so those are the kinds of features we put into Wasabi.
Most people don't realize that writing a compiler like this is only about 2 months work for one talented person who read the Dragon book.
Since the compiler only has one body of code to compile, it is much easier to write. It doesn't have to be a general-purpose compiler.

We have the ability to add any feature to the language that we want easily… this is the same power Paul Graham talks about in On Lisp, the power to invent new language features that suit your exact application domain.
There's a good chance that the domain you're working in could really use a domain-specific language.
Should you write your own compiler? Maybe, if you're doing something that's different enough from the mainstream and if there's no good off-the-shelf technology for your problem.
posts.key_insight: Building a custom compiler for a specific codebase is far more practical than most developers assume, and the ability to target multiple platforms from a single source while adding domain-specific features can be well worth the investment.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post, not a substantive blog entry. Joel Spolsky simply notifies readers about an upcoming speech he is giving at Cornell University. The post contains no argument, analysis, or insight — just logistical details: the time (5pm) and location (Phillips 203). It appears to have been written as a quick update for readers who might want to attend.
key_points: Joel was giving a speech at Cornell University, his alma mater
The post serves purely as a logistical announcement with time and room number
No substantive content or argument is present in this post
posts.key_insight: This is a logistical announcement with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the launch of jobs.joelonsoftware.com, a niche job board designed to connect great companies with great programmers. He argues that giant job boards like Monster and Craigslist fail both employers and job seekers by producing floods of undifferentiated resumes and vague job listings. His board requires companies to post their actual name, charges $350 per listing to maintain quality, and offers a 90-day money-back guarantee. The goal is a curated, exclusive marketplace where his readership of skilled developers can find transparent, high-quality job listings.
key_points: Niche job boards serve both employers and job seekers better than massive generic boards by creating targeted, like-minded communities
Requiring companies to post their actual name filters out vague recruiter listings and gives developers the transparency they need to make informed decisions
Charging $350 per listing with a 90-day money-back guarantee keeps quality high while reducing risk for employers
The traditional resume-and-cover-letter approach gives employers insufficient information to distinguish qualified candidates
The site was built in roughly one week of actual work as a deliberate MVP to test market demand before investing further
money_quotes: A niche job board has modest goals, no grander than to bring a dozen or so companies that are great places to work together with a dozen or so great programmers.
The traditional way of applying for a job, sending a cover letter and a resume, just does not give the employer enough useful information to separate the 'maybes' from the 'maybe nots.'
Every good software developer I know has a choice of where to work. They don't want to work for a 'TOP INVESTMENT BANK'.
If you want to hire the right people, you have to go where the right people hang out.
Some investment banks are really nice places to work. Others are sweatshops. Some are ethical. Many are ethically challenged.
Recruiters who don't want to reveal the company name are really barking up the wrong tree. Try Craigslist, shugah.
posts.key_insight: The best hiring marketplaces are small, transparent, and community-driven — quality matching beats mass reach for both employers and developers.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky argues that the best software developers are essentially never on the open job market because they get hired through connections, internships, and reputation before they ever need to send out resumes. This creates a paradox where traditional recruiting methods like job boards yield piles of resumes dominated by unqualified candidates, with very few worth considering. He proposes three strategies: going where great developers congregate (conferences, niche communities), building a robust internship pipeline that identifies talent before graduation, and creating a developer community around your company. He also warns against over-reliance on employee referrals, especially with cash incentives, which tend to lower hiring quality rather than improve it.
key_points: Great developers apply for roughly four jobs in their entire career and are almost never available on the open market, while unqualified candidates apply to hundreds or thousands of positions, creating a terrible signal-to-noise ratio in resume piles.

Recruiting should shift from 'gather and filter resumes' to 'track down winners and make them talk to you' by going to conferences, niche job boards, and places where talented developers already congregate.

Internship programs serve as extended auditions that let companies identify great talent before they enter the job market, justifying higher starting offers because the employer has better information about the candidate's abilities.

Employee referral bonuses create perverse incentives where employees coach friends through interviews and pressure hiring managers, ultimately lowering the quality of hires rather than improving them.
Building a developer community around your company creates a self-selecting audience of talented people you can recruit from whenever openings arise, though this strategy is difficult to replicate.
money_quotes: The great software developers, indeed, the best people in every field, are quite simply never on the market.
Instead of thinking as recruiting as a 'gather resumes, filter resumes' procedure, you're going to have to think of it as a 'track down the winners and make them talk to you' procedure.
The corollary of that rule—the rule that the great people are never on the market—is that the bad people—the seriously unqualified—are on the market quite a lot.
One good way to snag the great people who are never on the job market is to get them before they even realize there is a job market: when they're in college.

We've already auditioned these kids, and there's no risk that they won't be great. We know what they can do. So when we hire them, we have more information about them than any other employer who has only interviewed them.
I feel like I'm giving you advice that says, 'to win a beauty pageant, (a) get beautiful, and (b) enter the pageant.'
Nobody wants to persuade their friends to apply for a job at their company only to get rejected. It sort of puts a damper on the friendship.
Suddenly your entire workforce is trying to get you to hire someone's useless college roommate.
posts.key_insight: The best developers never enter the job market, so effective recruiting means proactively finding them through conferences, internships, and community-building rather than passively filtering through resume piles.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Joel Spolsky introduces a new job board on his site and announces a series of articles called 'The Guerrilla Guide to Hiring.' He links to the first article in the series, 'Finding Great Developers,' and mentions an upcoming speech at Cornell. The post itself contains no substantive argument, serving purely as a pointer to other content.
key_points: Joel launched a job board on joelonsoftware.com
He began a series called 'The Guerrilla Guide to Hiring'
The first installment focuses on finding great developers
posts.key_insight: This is an announcement post linking to Joel's longer article on finding great developers, with no standalone insights of its own.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that attracting top programming talent requires more than good interview processes — companies must create environments developers actually want to work in. He covers the key factors developers evaluate: private offices (despite Silicon Valley's open-plan obsession), quality physical workspaces and equipment, social dynamics including respect and autonomy, interesting work, and company identity. He contends that money is surprisingly low on developers' priority lists as long as compensation is fair, and that the real differentiators are workplace quality, intellectual respect, and organizational culture.
key_points: Private offices dramatically improve both productivity and recruiting appeal, yet most companies resist them due to VC pressure and cultural norms favoring open plans
Investing in quality equipment (Aeron chairs, large monitors, books) costs trivially little compared to developer salaries but sends a powerful signal about how the company values its programmers
Developers prize autonomy, meritocracy, and freedom from dysfunctional politics — they want to win arguments by being right, not by being well-connected
Company identity and mission matter for recruiting: developers want meaningful work they can identify with, and smart companies create ideological narratives around their products
Money is not the primary motivator for developers — complaints about salary usually signal deeper dissatisfaction with working conditions or respect
money_quotes: The world of programming is very just and very strictly ordered and a heck of a lot of people go into programming in the first place because they prefer to spend their time in a just, orderly place, a strict meritocracy where you can win any debate simply by being right.

So upgrading them to an Aeron chair literally costs the same amount as you're spending on their toilet paper, and I assure you that if you tried to bring up toilet paper in the budget committee you would be sternly told not to mess around, there were important things to discuss.

They don't care about money, actually, unless you're screwing up on the other things. If you start to hear complaints about salaries where you never heard them before, that's usually a sign that people aren't really loving their job.
Nothing is more infuriating than when a developer is told to use a certain programming language, not the best one for the task at hand, because the boss likes it.
If you're going to hire smart people, you're going to have to let them apply their skills to their work.

When I complained about MacroMan, my boss told me, 'Nothing's gonna derail that train. Give up.' But I kept arguing, and arguing, and arguing—I was fresh out of college, about as unconnected as anyone could be at Microsoft—and eventually people listened to the meat of my arguments and the MacroMan project was shut down.
Wanna rewrite that whole trading app in Lisp? Whatever. Just get me a goddamned cheeseburger.
The job involves making nuclear weapons out of baby seals.
posts.key_insight: Top developers choose workplaces based on respect, autonomy, quality environment, and meaningful work — not primarily salary — so companies that treat programmers as interchangeable cogs will lose the talent war regardless of what they pay.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky outlines what makes a workplace attractive to top software developers, arguing that recruiting great programmers requires creating an environment they actually want to work in. He covers private offices, physical workspace quality, equipment, social dynamics, interesting work, and company identity as key factors. He emphasizes that developers care deeply about autonomy, meritocracy, and being treated as professionals rather than interchangeable cogs. The post argues that many of these investments (like good chairs and monitors) are surprisingly cheap relative to their recruiting and retention impact. Spolsky notes that money is actually low on developers' priority lists as long as compensation is fair, and that workplace culture and environment matter far more. The piece serves as practical advice for companies struggling to attract engineering talent.
key_points: Private offices are both more productive and a crucial recruiting tool, yet most companies resist them due to VC pressure and cultural norms around open offices
Physical workspace investments like Aeron chairs and large monitors cost trivially little relative to developer salaries but send powerful signals about how the company values its engineers
Developers prize autonomy, meritocracy, and freedom from dysfunctional politics — they want technical decisions made on technical merits, not organizational power
Company identity and ideology matter to developers — they want to feel their work has meaning and want to identify with their employer's mission
Money is surprisingly unimportant to developers as long as compensation is fair; complaints about salary are usually symptoms of deeper workplace problems
money_quotes: If the great programmers don't want to work for you, they ain't gonna come work for you.
Upgrading them to an Aeron chair literally costs the same amount as you're spending on their toilet paper.

The world of programming is very just and very strictly ordered and a heck of a lot of people go into programming in the first place because they prefer to spend their time in a just, orderly place, a strict meritocracy where you can win any debate simply by being right.
When a programmer complains about 'politics,' they mean — very precisely — any situation in which personal considerations outweigh technical considerations.
If you start to hear complaints about salaries where you never heard them before, that's usually a sign that people aren't really loving their job.
Stars go first class. Get used to it.
If you're going to hire smart people, you're going to have to let them apply their skills to their work.
The job involves making nuclear weapons out of baby seals.
posts.key_insight: Attracting top developers is less about offering high salaries and more about creating a workplace that respects their autonomy, provides quality tools and space, operates as a meritocracy, and gives them meaningful work they can identify with.

posts.summary: Spolsky addresses the first policy violation on his new job board: a recruiter posting without disclosing the company name. He explains why recruiters prefer anonymity (protecting their commission) and why job seekers find this frustrating. He argues there's a place for both recruiters and transparent job markets, but acknowledges the business dilemma of potentially alienating headhunters who are the biggest spenders on job boards. He opens the question to his community for discussion.
key_points: Recruiters hide company names to protect their commission and build their candidate pipeline, not to serve job seekers' interests
Top developers want transparency about who they'd work for, not generic descriptions of anonymous companies
There's a fundamental tension between what job seekers want (transparency) and what pays the bills (recruiter ad spending)
An open market where candidates can research companies directly serves developers better than recruiter-mediated listings
money_quotes: Job seekers are sick of looking at generic lists of seemingly anonymous companies.

Top developers have a choice of where to work, and they're not very enchanted with the prospects of working for a "leading developer of software products" that needs a "C/C++/XML/HTTP/HTML" to fill a slot.
Headhunters are probably the biggest single spenders on job boards, so I might be antagonizing my biggest potential audience of paying customers.
I think there's also a place for a more open market where people can look directly at jobs, then jump to the company's website and decide if that's the kind of company they might want to work for.

The reason recruiters don't like to post company names is because they don't get a commission unless they refer the employee, so they don't want to take any risk that the candidate will go directly to the company and cut them out of their commission.
posts.key_insight: Job boards that prioritize recruiter revenue over candidate transparency end up serving neither side well, because top talent won't engage with anonymous listings.

posts.summary: Spolsky announces that the Joel on Software job board will maintain its policy of requiring company names on all job postings, banning anonymous recruiter posts. He notes overwhelming developer consensus against anonymous job listings from commission-driven recruiters. The policy allows recruiters only if they disclose the actual hiring company. He also announces a new feature displaying Joel Test scores on job listings.
key_points: Developers universally hate job boards filled with anonymous recruiter postings designed to fish for resumes and commissions
Requiring company name disclosure filters out low-quality recruiter spam and retained-only searches survive the policy
Transparency in job postings (company name, Joel Test scores) serves as a quality signal for developers
money_quotes: I have never seen such developers all over the world so unanimous on any issue.
If anything, I underestimated how much people hate job boards clogged with anonymous posts put up by commission- and resume-fishing recruiters.
We will continue to require a company name and take down posts that don't disclose the actual company.
Recruiters can use it if they post the name of the company that is actually hiring.
Thanks, summer intern Noah!
posts.key_insight: Requiring transparency in job postings — specifically company name disclosure — is a simple policy that dramatically improves job board quality by filtering out resume-fishing recruiters.

posts.summary: Joel reports that Windows Vista RC1 fails to install properly in VMWare Workstation due to a graphics mode switching issue. He finds it suspicious that Microsoft's new OS doesn't work on a competitor's virtualization product while Microsoft sells the competing Virtual PC. He provides a workaround involving forcing VGA mode in the VMX configuration file, and speculates that Vista's Aero/Glass UI may be fundamentally incompatible with VMWare's virtual graphics.
key_points: Vista RC1 appears to hang during installation in VMWare because it silently switches to graphics mode in a way VMWare can't render
Joel suspects Microsoft's failure to test with VMWare is deliberate anticompetitive behavior, given that Microsoft sells the competing Virtual PC product
A workaround exists: forcing 640x480 VGA mode in the VMX config file allows installation to proceed
Vista's Aero/Glass UI demands on the graphics card may make full-featured VMWare testing impossible
money_quotes: Since Microsoft makes a competitive product, Virtual PC, it is Highly Suspicious that they come out with a major new test release of an operating system that just happens to not work on VMWare Workstation.
Shabby and slimy, Microsoft.
They're probably testing Windows Vista with tens of thousands of applications; not testing with VMWare is inexcusable.
VMWare Workstation, something which is practically the de facto standard for developers testing new operating systems.
I'm assuming that the Aero/Glass UI's heavy demands on the graphic card may mean that it can't be tested under VMWare, but I'd love to be wrong.
posts.key_insight: When a platform vendor also sells competing products, compatibility failures with rivals' tools look less like accidents and more like anticompetitive strategy.

posts.summary: Joel announces that Fog Creek Software is now accepting summer internship applications with a changed process. Previously, they waited until a February deadline to review all applications at once for fairness, but this meant top candidates had already accepted offers elsewhere (notably Microsoft). This year they're switching to a first-come, first-served review process, urging students to apply immediately rather than wait.
key_points: Batch-processing applications for fairness cost them top candidates who accepted other offers while waiting
Speed of hiring decisions matters more than procedural fairness when competing for talent
Switching to rolling/first-come-first-served admissions to avoid losing candidates to competitors like Microsoft
money_quotes: It was fair, but it also meant that by the time we even looked at the resumes, some great applicants had already accepted summer jobs at Microsoft.
If you're a college student looking for an internship, you pretty much should just apply now, instead of puttering around indecisively.
posts.key_insight: Optimizing a hiring process for fairness at the expense of speed can cost you the best candidates to faster-moving competitors.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky argues that resumes are an inherently weak signal for evaluating programming candidates, but offers a systematic framework for sorting them fairly. He outlines seven criteria—Passion, Pickiness, English, Brains, Selectivity, Hard-Core, and Diversity—used not as hiring decisions but as a way to prioritize who gets interviewed first. He warns strongly against keyword-matching on technologies, arguing that good programmers can learn any language quickly and that recruiters' fixation on acronyms is counterproductive. The only exception is hiring a lead architect who needs deep experience in a specific stack. He also cautions against adding extra hoops like programming quizzes to the application process, since top candidates with many options will simply skip you.
key_points: Resumes provide only faint signals about candidate quality and should be used to screen out and sort, never to make hiring decisions directly.
Seven sorting criteria (Passion, Pickiness, English, Brains, Selectivity, Hard-Core, Diversity) create a fair, objective ordering for who to interview first.
Keyword-matching on specific technologies is the worst way to evaluate developers, since good programmers can learn new languages quickly and resume keyword sections are gamed.
Adding extra application hoops like programming quizzes tends to scare away the best candidates who have plenty of alternatives, leaving you with desperate applicants.
The one exception to ignoring technology keywords is when hiring a lead architect who must have deep experience in the specific stack being used.
money_quotes: The standard job application of cover letter plus resume is a phenomenally weak way to introduce a candidate.
Programmers who can communicate their ideas clearly are going to be far, far more effective than programmers who can only really communicate well with the compiler.
A disorganized resume rife with grammatical errors where nothing lines up is a pretty big red flag for a disorganized thinker or just general sloppiness.

The entire industry of professional headhunters and recruiters is bizarrely fixated on the simple algorithm of matching candidates to positions by looking for candidates that have the complete list of technology acronyms.
For someone who is basically a good software developer, learning another programming language is just not going to be a big deal. In two weeks they'll be pretty productive.
By inventing artificial hoops and programming tests and whatnot simply to apply, you're just as likely to scare away good programmers as weak programmers.
The more diverse your team is, the more likely that someone on the team will have some experience in their background that allows them to come up with a different solution.
I'm more impressed by someone who has done work in OCaml than someone who has worked in Java.
posts.key_insight: Resumes are too weak a signal to hire on, but a systematic scoring framework across passion, communication, brainpower, and diversity can at least help you interview the most promising candidates first—while keyword-matching on technologies remains the worst possible approach.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky criticizes the technical recruiting industry's over-reliance on keyword matching when screening developer resumes. He argues that recruiters lack understanding of the technologies they filter for, leading to absurd disqualifications of qualified candidates. The post highlights how this mechanical approach to hiring fails both employers and programmers, using concrete examples of recruiter ignorance like demanding impossible years of experience with new technologies or treating synonymous terms as different skills.
key_points: Technical recruiters rely on shallow keyword matching rather than understanding candidate capabilities
Most recruiters don't understand the technologies they screen for, leading to nonsensical filtering decisions
The keyword-matching approach produces absurd results like requiring 5 years of experience with a technology that hasn't existed that long
Synonymous technologies (Win32 vs Windows API) are treated as different skills due to recruiter ignorance
money_quotes: The entire industry of professional headhunters and recruiters is bizarrely fixated on the simple algorithm of matching candidates to positions by looking for candidates that have the complete list of technology acronyms that the employer happens to be looking for.
It becomes especially infuriating to realize that most of these recruiters have no idea what any of these technologies are.
At least when real estate agents prattle on about Subzero refrigerators and Viking stoves, they at least know what these things are.
The easiest way to catch-out a technical recruiter is when they inevitably insist on 5 years of experience with Ruby on Rails.
To top programmers, the most maddening thing about recruiters is their almost morbid fascination with keywords and buzzwords.
posts.key_insight: Recruiting based on keyword matching rather than understanding of technical capability is fundamentally broken because the people doing the filtering don't understand what they're filtering for.

posts.summary: Spolsky describes his experience using TextMate with Markdown on a MacBook Pro to compose long blog articles, since his usual tool CityDesk is Windows-only. He found the combination surprisingly pleasant for generating clean HTML with curly quotes. However, he criticizes OS X's font antialiasing as inferior to Windows ClearType and reports severe system freezes triggered by WiFi disconnections. He ultimately concludes that Word 2007 on Windows XP remains the superior writing experience.
key_points: TextMate with Markdown mode provides a smooth writing experience on Mac with clean HTML output and readable source
OS X font antialiasing with monospaced fonts was noticeably worse than Windows ClearType at the time
Mac OS X had a critical stability issue where brief WiFi drops caused complete system freezes requiring hard reboots every two hours
Despite trying Mac alternatives, Word 2007 on Windows XP was still the better overall writing experience due to autocorrection and display quality
money_quotes: The combination I found that made me happiest was TextMate in Markdown mode. It was a surprisingly good experience.
Even Markdown source is quite clean and still highly readable, useful if you need to post the same content to Usenet or use it in plain text somewhere.
I don't understand all these people who say that Macs never crash.
On Windows, it just comes back, while on the Mac, I get a spinning colored ball and everything is frozen. Everything. Forever.
If I try to wait it out the beachball will still be spinning the next morning.
It was like a Windows 3.1 deja vu all over again thing.
posts.key_insight: Even beloved tools have rough edges — Spolsky's pragmatic evaluation of Mac writing tools in 2006 challenged the prevailing narrative of Mac superiority while giving early recognition to Markdown as a clean authoring format.

posts.summary: Joel shares a link to Avi Bryant's technique for optimizing duck-typed method calls, expressing appreciation for the clever approach. He then delivers a dry one-liner noting that Ruby remains slow despite such optimizations.
key_points: Avi Bryant developed a technique to make duck-typed method calls faster
Joel finds the optimization technique noteworthy and clever
Despite clever optimizations, Ruby's overall performance remains poor
money_quotes: Ruby's still slow. Film at 11.
posts.key_insight: Clever micro-optimizations for dynamic languages don't solve the fundamental performance problems those languages have.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky clarifies that his performance concerns are with the Ruby language itself, not the Rails framework, citing benchmark data showing Ruby is dramatically slower than alternatives. He argues that Ruby's duck typing and late binding make it fundamentally difficult to optimize method calls down to efficient CPU instructions. Using FogBugz as a concrete example, he describes computation-intensive features that would be impossible to implement in Ruby. He pushes back against the 'developer cycles vs CPU cycles' talking point as an oversimplification that dismisses legitimate performance concerns, noting that even simple CRUD apps eventually need compute-intensive features like Bayesian spam filtering.
key_points: Ruby's duck typing and late binding prevent the compiler from optimizing method calls to simple CPU instructions, creating a fundamental performance ceiling
The 'developer cycles vs CPU cycles' tradeoff is a bumper-sticker slogan that dismisses real-world performance constraints where applications hit computational walls
Even simple CRUD applications eventually need compute-intensive features, making language performance relevant to nearly all projects over time
FogBugz has specific features requiring millions of calculations per page render and Bayesian filtering that would be infeasible in Ruby
When you need 100 servers instead of 10, the economics of developer time savings quickly reverse
money_quotes: I understand the philosophy that developer cycles are more important than cpu cycles, but frankly that's just a bumper-sticker slogan and not fair to the people who are complaining about performance.
In FogBugz 6 there's one place where we need to do literally millions of calculations to display a single chart on a single web page.
With a duck-typed function call I really don't think we could do it before the web browser gave up and timed out and the sun cooled down a couple of degrees.
Even classic, simple CRUD applications often discover somewhere down the line that there's something enormously computationally intensive that they want to do.
If your language of choice is 10x slower than the competition, you may be simply unable to add that feature.
Even if you aren't doing compute-intensive things, if you find yourself needing to buy 100 servers instead of 10 servers, you may suddenly revisit the whole developer cycle vs. CPU cycle equation.
I stand by my claim that it's not appropriate for every situation.
posts.key_insight: Language performance isn't just about current needs — even simple applications eventually hit compute-intensive requirements, and a language that's orders of magnitude slower can make those features impossible to implement.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky delivers a scathing review of Sprint's LG Fusic phone and Power Vision Network service, which Sprint sent to bloggers hoping for positive coverage. He systematically dismantles every aspect of the product: the cheap plastic build quality, the confusing menu system with gratuitous animations, two separate MP3 players that are both broken in different ways, and overpriced content services offering public domain movies on a tiny screen. The review builds to a broader critique of telecom companies trying to be content gatekeepers instead of embracing the open internet. He concludes with a satirical theory that the phone must be designed for four-year-olds, since nothing else explains the toy-like design and unusable interface.
key_points: The LG Fusic phone has catastrophically bad industrial design, resembling a Fisher Price toy rather than a premium device, with cheap plastic construction and dangling rubber plug-caps
The phone contains two separate MP3 players, neither of which works properly — one requires a network connection even for local files, and the other stops playing when you close the clamshell
Sprint's content services (music at $5/3 songs, movies at $7/month) are overpriced ripoffs offering public domain garbage that nobody would watch even for free

Telecom companies keep repeating the same mistake of trying to be content gatekeepers and toll collectors instead of enabling open internet access, a pattern Spolsky first wrote about with WAP in 2000
No amount of blogger outreach or tipping point marketing strategy can overcome a fundamentally terrible product
money_quotes: There's no better way to make me not want to write about something than to ask me to write about it.
Believe me, it is hard to make black clash with anything, but LG did it.
Thank you Sun Microsystems for bringing programming language advertisements into consumer electronics.

There are two bad MP3 players on this device, neither one of which remembers where you're up to, neither one of which can be used on the subway with the phone folded in my pocket, neither one of which has a fast-forward feature.
This service is literally as much of a scam as those X-Ray glasses they used to advertise in comic books to steal a few bucks from some little kids.

Cell phone companies keep failing to insert themselves as toll collectors because they're so darn clueless about how the Internet works, and about the value of many-to-many networks instead of broadcast networks.

All the 'tipping point' theories in the world won't protect Sprint from the basic truth that the LG Fusic user interface could basically serve as an almost complete textbook for a semester-long course in user interface design, teaching students of usability exactly what NOT to do.
No amount of pampering bloggers and calling them Ambassadors is going to get around the fact that you're sending us plastic junk phones that look like bath toys.
posts.key_insight: No marketing strategy — not even giving free products to influencers — can compensate for a fundamentally broken user experience and a business model built on exploiting customers rather than serving them.

posts.summary: Joel provides a step-by-step tutorial for integrating Markdown and SmartyPants text processors with EditPad Pro on Windows. The post walks through installing Perl, downloading the necessary scripts, creating a batch file to chain the two processors together, and configuring EditPad Pro's tools menu to run the pipeline. The end result is a one-click workflow that converts a text file to HTML with proper typography and opens it in a browser. It's a brief, purely practical how-to post with no broader argument or commentary.
key_points: Markdown converts plain text formatting conventions to HTML, while SmartyPants handles typographic niceties like curly quotes
EditPad Pro is recommended as a fast, feature-rich Notepad replacement for Windows users
The two Perl scripts can be chained together via a simple batch file to produce well-formatted HTML output
EditPad Pro's configurable Tools menu allows integrating external scripts into a one-click workflow
money_quotes: EditPad Pro is a very respectable text editor for Windows. It's fast and contains scrillions of useful features.
It's not the fanciest thing in the world, but if you're still using Notepad for the occasional bits of text, it's a fine drop-in replacement.
posts.key_insight: Chaining Markdown and SmartyPants through a batch file integrated into a text editor's tools menu creates a simple, effective plain-text-to-HTML authoring workflow on Windows.

posts.summary: Joel follows up on his earlier complaint about his MacBook Pro freezing with spinning beach balls. After running diagnostics that found nothing, a reader suggested checking the console logs, which revealed repeated DNS lookup failures. The root cause turned out to be a known bug in Apple's lookupd process where a crash leaves the process in a zombie state, hanging any application that tries to do DNS resolution. Joel notes that Apple has left this bug unfixed for over two years despite it being filed in their bug tracker.
key_points: Console logs revealed repeated NetInfo connection failures for lookupd, corresponding exactly to the freezing behavior
The bug involves lookupd crashing but not being properly terminated by CrashReporter, preventing mach_init from respawning it
A third-party developer created a workaround daemon (Unlockupd) to watch and restart lookupd when it gets stuck
Apple had known about the bug (rdar://3632865) for over two years without fixing it
money_quotes: Lookupd has a bug in its cache cleanup code that causes it to randomly crash.
CrashReporter, the system crash log agent, does not properly handle lookupd crashes, and as a result, when lookupd crashes, the process is not terminated.
From this point, any application that attempts to access lookupd, either directly or indirectly, will hang.
It's odd that Apple hasn't fixed this bug in over two years.
Suddenly any app that tried to do a DNS lookup of any sort would go into permanent beachball mode and never recover.
posts.key_insight: A seemingly mysterious system-wide freeze turned out to be a known but unfixed OS bug, discoverable through console logs — a reminder that checking system logs should be an early debugging step, and that even Apple leaves critical bugs unfixed for years.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares a brief link post highlighting Steve Yegge's essay on Agile programming. The post centers on Yegge's colorful and scathing characterization of Agile as a fad embraced by naive programmers. Spolsky implicitly endorses Yegge's skepticism by featuring the quote prominently and recommending a book critical of Extreme Programming. The post reflects the mid-2000s backlash among certain influential developers against Agile methodologies.
key_points: Spolsky signals agreement with Agile skepticism by amplifying Yegge's critical take without adding caveats
Yegge frames Agile as a marketing scam exploiting programmers who haven't studied software engineering history
The post recommends 'Extreme Programming Refactored' as further reading against XP/Agile practices
The brief format suggests Spolsky felt Yegge's quote spoke for itself and needed no elaboration
money_quotes: it's an idiotic fad-diet of a marketing scam making the rounds as yet another technological virus implanting itself in naive programmers who've never read 'No Silver Bullet'
the kinds of programmers who buy extended warranties and self-help books and believe their bosses genuinely care about them as people
the kinds of programmers who attend conferences to make friends and who don't know how to avoid eye contact with leaflet-waving fanatics in airports
who believe writing shit on index cards will suddenly make software development easier
posts.key_insight: Spolsky uses Yegge's provocative takedown to express deep skepticism that Agile methodologies offer any genuine improvement over disciplined software engineering fundamentals.

posts.summary: Joel announces a new search feature for the Joel on Software job listings board, which had grown to hundreds of listings and needed filtering. The feature includes location-based search with RSS subscription, allowing job seekers outside major tech hubs to monitor opportunities in their area. He describes the implementation details, including ZIP code databases and great circle distance calculations. The post is a casual product update that mixes feature announcement with technical anecdotes about geodesic math.
key_points: The job board grew from a simple browsable list to 420 listings in the first month, necessitating search functionality
RSS integration lets job seekers passively monitor for new opportunities in their geographic area
50% of job listings clustered around New York and Palo Alto, highlighting tech industry geographic concentration even in 2006
When implementing geographic distance search, doing it correctly with great circle math is roughly as complex as doing it incorrectly with flat-earth approximations
money_quotes: In the first month, though, we got about 420 listings; there are 174 live right now: too many to scan.
Right now 50% of the job listings are for jobs within 100 miles of either New York, NY or Palo Alto, CA.

When you're looking for points that are within 20 miles of a given longitude and latitude, it's not that big a deal to pretend the Earth is flat and just use the Pythagorean Theorem, but of course, then you have to convert degrees to miles and suddenly it's just as hard to do it the wrong way as it would be to do it the right way.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes doing things the mathematically correct way is no harder than taking shortcuts, so you might as well do it right.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the Russian translation of his book 'Joel on Software.' The post is extremely brief, consisting of just two sentences: one announcing the book's availability and one humorously noting he doesn't recognize the people depicted on the cover. It's a lighthearted promotional post with no substantive technical content.
key_points: The Russian translation of 'Joel on Software' was published at a price of 250 rubles
Joel humorously distances himself from the cover design choices, noting he doesn't know who the people on the cover are
The post reflects the international reach of Joel's writing and brand
money_quotes: I have no idea who those people are on the cover.
posts.key_insight: A brief announcement post about the Russian translation of Joel's book, notable mainly for its dry humor about the cover art.

posts.summary: Spolsky reviews Bruce Tate's 'Beyond Java' through the lens of Brooks' 'No Silver Bullet' essay, which distinguishes between accidental and essential complexity in programming. He traces five major advances in eliminating accidental difficulties (assemblers through memory-managed languages) and asks what the sixth will be. The book argues that experienced Java programmers are migrating to Python and Ruby, and Spolsky identifies explicit type declarations as the central accidental difficulty that these newer languages eliminate. He concludes that the book makes a compelling case worth reading, while maintaining his characteristic skepticism toward programming language hype.
key_points: Brooks' 'No Silver Bullet' distinguishes accidental difficulties (solvable by better tools) from essential complexity (inherent to software), predicting diminishing returns from new technologies

There have been only five major advances in eliminating accidental programming difficulties since the 1950s: assemblers, algebraic languages, structured languages, declarative languages, and memory-managed languages
The central thesis of 'Beyond Java' is that explicit type declarations are the key accidental difficulty driving experienced Java programmers toward Python and Ruby
Dynamic languages like Python, Ruby, and JavaScript solve real accidental difficulties rather than being mere hype
money_quotes: Programmers under the age of thirty probably don't realize why Aged Senile Programmers like me are so slow to adopt exciting new programming languages the day they come out.

Programming consists of overcoming two things: accidental difficulties, things which are difficult because you happen to be using inadequate programming tools, and things which are actually difficult, which no programming tool or language is going to solve.

Improvements in programming languages can eliminate accidental difficulties, but after you've done that, you're left with the actual complexity of software development, so the No Silver Bullet theory basically warns us to expect diminishing returns from new technologies.
To a historian, it's starting to look like type declarations are one of those accidental difficulties that good programming languages can eliminate.
An example of an accidental difficulty is manual memory management, e.g. 'malloc' and 'free,' or the singleton classes people create in Java because they don't have top level functions.
posts.key_insight: Viewed through Brooks' essential vs. accidental complexity framework, explicit type declarations appear to be the next major accidental difficulty that programming languages can and should eliminate.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky describes his three-part phone screening process for programmer candidates, designed to efficiently filter out poor fits before costly in-person interviews. The first part explores career history to assess problem-solving ability and passion, drilling into both technical depth and how candidates navigated organizational challenges. The second part poses an open-ended design question to evaluate algorithmic thinking through conversation rather than rote coding. The third part lets candidates ask questions, which doubles as a test of preparation and genuine interest. Spolsky argues phone screens are not only cheaper but fairer than in-person interviews, since they reduce snap judgments based on appearance. Even with this filter, only about one in three candidates who pass the phone screen ultimately get hired.
key_points: Phone screens eliminate about half of candidates who look great on paper, saving significant time and money before in-person interviews.
Phone interviews reduce appearance-based bias by forcing interviewers to evaluate what candidates actually say rather than how they look.
Drilling into technical details and project roles uncovers exaggeration, while trick questions test whether candidates have genuine affinity for technical truth.
Open-ended design questions are superior to rote coding questions for phone screens because they enable natural conversation about algorithms, tradeoffs, and problem-solving approaches.
The interview is bidirectional: smart candidates have choices, so the process must also sell them on the company.
money_quotes: I've had people with great resumes tell me a pointer should fit in one byte.
With a phone interview, because you can't see the person, it's easier to focus on the quality of what they're saying rather than other external factors not relevant to their job.
Smart programmers have a certain affinity for the truth, and they'll call you on it.

The bottom line in my interviewing technique is that smart people can generally tell if they're talking to other smart people by having a conversation with them on a difficult or highly technical subject.
The interview question is really just a pretext to have a conversation on a difficult subject so that the interviewer's judgment can form an opinion on whether this is a smart person or not.
If you find yourself explaining everything three times, either you're terrible at explaining things, or you're talking to someone who is not that smart.
Failing to do even the most basic homework before the interview does not give me a great deal of confidence in the candidate's ability to be smart or to get things done.
The interview process is as much a way for the candidate to decide if they want to work for us as it is a way for us to decide if we want to hire the candidate.
posts.key_insight: Phone screens are both a cost-saving filter and a fairness mechanism that forces interviewers to evaluate candidates on substance rather than appearance, using open-ended technical conversations as a proxy for general intelligence and problem-solving ability.

posts.summary: This is a brief pointer post directing readers to Joel's full article about phone screening candidates. The core premise is that phone screens serve as an efficient filter to avoid wasting time and money on in-person interviews with candidates who clearly aren't qualified. The post itself contains only a single quote and a link to the detailed article.
key_points: Phone screens should be used as a pre-filter before investing in full in-person interviews
The primary goal is efficiency: avoiding wasted time and money on obviously unqualified candidates
Phone screens are a standard part of a structured hiring pipeline
money_quotes: Before moving on to a full-fledged in-person interview, we usually use a phone screen to make sure that we're not wasting time and money on someone who is just seriously not smart.
posts.key_insight: Phone screens are an essential efficiency tool in hiring, filtering out clearly unqualified candidates before committing to costly in-person interviews.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky presents his comprehensive guide to interviewing programmers, arguing that hiring is the most critical factor in software project success. He contends that interviewers should evaluate candidates on exactly two criteria: being smart and getting things done. The guide advocates for a rigorous process with multiple interviewers, strict hire/no-hire decisions with no maybes, and a strong bias toward rejecting uncertain candidates. Spolsky recommends using open-ended coding problems (especially involving recursion and pointers) to assess aptitude rather than trivia questions or brain teasers. He emphasizes that rejecting a good candidate is far less costly than hiring a bad one, and that interviewers should look for passion, clear communication, and leadership in candidates' discussions of past work.
key_points: Hiring decisions should be binary (Hire or No Hire) with no maybes — any uncertainty should mechanically translate to No Hire, because a bad hire is far more costly than a missed good candidate.
The only two qualities that matter in a programming candidate are being Smart and Getting Things Done — people who have only one of these traits are net negatives for a team.
Interview coding problems should test aptitude (recursion, pointers, multiple levels of abstraction) rather than trivia knowledge or specific technology skills, which become obsolete quickly.
Multiple interviewers (at least six, mostly peers) should evaluate each candidate independently to avoid groupthink and bias, with any two no-hires being sufficient to reject.
During open-ended discussion, interviewers should look for passion, ability to explain concepts clearly to non-experts, and evidence of past leadership and initiative.
money_quotes: The trick is telling the difference between the superstars and the maybes, because the secret is that you don't want to hire any of the maybes. Ever.
It is much, much better to reject a good candidate than to accept a bad candidate.
If you can't tell, that means No Hire. It's really easier than you'd think. Can't tell? Just say no!
Smart does not mean 'knows the answer to trivia questions.' Software teams want to hire people with aptitude, not a particular skill set.
For some reason most people seem to be born without the part of the brain that understands pointers.
If the basic concepts aren't so easy that you don't even have to think about them, you're not going to get the big concepts.
People who Get Things Done but are not Smart will do stupid things, seemingly without thinking about them, and somebody else will have to come clean up their mess later.
I want my ER doctor to understand anatomy, even if all she has to do is put the computerized defibrillator nodes on my chest and push the big red button.
posts.key_insight: Great software teams are built by hiring only people who are both smart and get things done, with a deliberate bias toward rejecting uncertain candidates rather than risking a bad hire.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares a brief note about Fog Creek Software's summer internship recruiting season. He references a Boston Globe article that quoted him on how good employers build relationships with professors to find exceptional students. The post serves primarily as a call to action, asking readers to recommend capable students for Fog Creek's internship program. It reflects Joel's ongoing emphasis on hiring top talent as a core business strategy.
key_points: Good employers build relationships with professors to identify exceptional students early
Proactive recruiting through personal networks yields better intern candidates than passive job postings
Joel uses his blog's readership as a recruiting channel, leveraging his audience to source talent
money_quotes: The good employers have relationships with professors and they forward students who seem exceptional.
posts.key_insight: The best hiring pipelines are built on relationships with people who can identify exceptional talent early, such as professors recommending their top students.

posts.summary: Spolsky shares statistics from the geographic search feature added to his job board, revealing where Joel on Software readers are looking for work. He uses the data to answer a common question from companies outside major tech hubs about whether advertising on the board is worthwhile. He explains how RSS-based job subscriptions create a passive but engaged audience of employed-but-open-to-opportunities developers. The post also serves as a transparent explanation of how the statistics are collected and their limitations, including double-counting caveats.
key_points: Geographic search data helps companies outside major tech centers decide whether to post jobs, with concrete numbers like 80 Italy seekers and 174 Austin seekers in 21 days
Joel on Software readers are characterized as smart people who already have good jobs but passively monitor opportunities via RSS feeds
The job board's money-back guarantee removes risk for employers, making it easy to justify posting even in smaller markets
Statistical transparency matters: Spolsky openly explains the double-counting caveat and cookie-based tracking limitations rather than inflating numbers
money_quotes: My theory of Joel on Software readers is that they're pretty smart, ergo, they already have good jobs, for the most part, but thanks to the power of RSS, a lot of people have set up subscriptions to find out about new jobs in their area.
We're still (as far as I know) the only job board that gives them their money back if they're unsatisfied, so there's really no risk at all.
Someone who is willing to work either in Chicago or Boston should show up in the Chicago count and the Boston count.
The number of job seekers in the last 21 days is 307,764 with one major caveat.
If you place an ad for a job in Italy, it's fair to guess that about 80 people who are specifically searching for jobs in Italy will see it.
posts.key_insight: Providing transparent, geographic job-seeker statistics transforms a common sales objection into a data-driven decision, making it easy for companies in any location to justify posting on a niche job board.

posts.summary: Spolsky recounts trying to sign up for a website where his apostrophe-containing secret answer triggered a raw MySQL error, revealing a SQL injection vulnerability. He explains how the site's programmers were concatenating user input directly into SQL queries, a common but dangerous practice. He walks through exactly how an attacker could exploit this to execute arbitrary SQL, such as deleting an entire database table. He notes that many programming tutorials teach this flawed pattern, and cites research showing 11.3% of web applications had SQL injection vulnerabilities at the time.
key_points: The site exposed raw MySQL error messages to users, immediately revealing its vulnerability to SQL injection
Many programming books and tutorials teach the dangerous pattern of concatenating user input directly into SQL queries
SQL injection allows attackers to close a string, terminate the original query, and execute arbitrary SQL commands
Research found that 11.3% of web applications had SQL injection vulnerabilities, making it an extremely common problem
money_quotes: This means that the programmers are in the habit of taking strings that they got from the user (i.e. GET or POST parameters) and concatenating them together with other bits and pieces of SQL to generate SQL statements.
I'm not surprised that they are in the habit of doing this; a lot of programming books, tutorials, and documentation use examples like this.
Unfortunately it's a gigantic security hole called SQL injection.
The user, if malicious, can close the string that you opened, finish your select statement, put in a semicolon (the SQL statement separator), and then type any SQL code they want, and it will run.
This is an extremely common problem: Michael Sutton did a little research project and found that 11.3% of web applications have SQL injection vulnerabilities.
posts.key_insight: SQL injection remains pervasive because the vulnerable pattern of concatenating user input into queries is what most tutorials teach, making the insecure approach the default one developers learn.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post where Joel shares a testimonial from a user named Kiere who praises FogBugz as a help desk tool. The quoted user reports positive experiences after handling about 100 tickets and 45 knowledge base articles. The post is essentially a customer endorsement with no additional commentary from Joel. It serves as marketing content for Fog Creek's FogBugz product.
key_points: FogBugz was being used successfully as a help desk system, not just a bug tracker
The user highlights speed, productivity, and simplicity as key strengths
Joel used his blog as a channel to amplify customer testimonials for his own product
money_quotes: After using my new help desk system for about 100 tickets and 45 knowledge base articles, I can honestly say this has been my favorite tool yet.
posts.key_insight: FogBugz found adoption beyond bug tracking as a general-purpose help desk tool, which Joel promoted through customer testimonials.

posts.summary: Spolsky dissects the management consulting industry's approach to measuring software developer productivity, arguing it's fundamentally fraudulent. He walks through a step-by-step scenario where consultants sell bogus productivity metrics to large companies, programmers game those metrics, and consultants then use the inflated numbers to sell more engagements to competitors. He argues that knowledge work productivity metrics are trivially gameable, making the entire exercise a pointless wealth transfer from corporations to consulting firms. The kicker is that the consultants themselves don't even realize the fraud they're perpetuating.
key_points: Software development productivity cannot be meaningfully measured with metrics like lines of code or function points
Programmers will inevitably game any productivity metric imposed on them, such as inflating function point counts through unnecessary XML round-trips
The consulting industry creates a self-perpetuating cycle by using one client's gamed metrics to scare competitors into buying the same services
Performance metrics in knowledge organizations are completely trivial to game, making consultant-driven productivity improvement an illusion
The consultants themselves are often unaware they're participating in a fraud because they lack the technical knowledge to see through the gaming
money_quotes: The whole fraud is only possible because performance metrics in knowledge organizations are completely trivial to game.
I think it was something about measuring performance in software organizations? aHA! YES! I know all about that. You can't do that. Quit it. Stop it.

You can triple the number of function points in your code simply by round tripping everything through an XML file. Big waste of time, prone to bugs, does nothing, but each file you touch adds a function point.
All they've done is caused a fairly pointless transfer of wealth from ExxonMobilConoco to BainMcKinseyGartner's senior partners.
The stunningly good-looking, bright, earnest chipmunks with 4.0s in Russian Lit from Harvard who work for these companies, have absolutely no way of knowing this.
They get all the way through the 2-year associate program on their way to MBA school without even realizing that they haven't done a goddamn thing about productivity.
posts.key_insight: Software productivity metrics are so easily gamed by developers that any attempt to measure and optimize them through external consulting becomes a self-perpetuating fraud that enriches consultants while accomplishing nothing.

posts.summary: Spolsky enthusiastically reviews the Sonos multi-room music system, describing it as the best piece of technology he's ever bought. He traces his journey from collecting 45s as a kid to having access to 2.5 million songs via Rhapsody, and explains how Sonos wirelessly distributes that music throughout his home. The bulk of the piece praises Sonos's zero-configuration setup and exceptional user interface design, which he contrasts favorably with the pain of setting up printers or Bluetooth. He closes with a technical deep-dive from Sonos's Director of Software Engineering, detailing their cross-platform C++/Linux architecture and build process.
key_points: Sonos achieves true zero-configuration setup through WiFi auto-discovery, making it dramatically easier than comparable tech like printers or Bluetooth
Great UI design means eliminating decisions users don't care about (WEP vs WPA) while enabling decisions they do care about (which version of a song to play)
Sonos uses a shared C++ code layer across Linux-based ZonePlayers and platform-native UI layers (WTL on Windows, Cocoa on Mac) to balance portability with native feel
The combination of unlimited streaming (Rhapsody) with whole-home distribution fundamentally changes the music listening experience compared to physical media
Cross-platform portability is enforced through daily builds on all platforms using native compilers for each
money_quotes: What I never imagined is that when I grew up, I'd be able to expand my record collection to about 2,500,000 songs for just under $10 a month.
It's about ten times easier than installing a printer. It's fifty times easier than getting Bluetooth to work.
The Sonos developers obviously put a huge amount of work into making setup and configuration completely painless.
Every time you provide an option, you're asking the user to make a decision. That means they will have to think about something and decide about it.
Users don't want to decide if the WiFi network should use WEP or WPA. They do want to decide whether to listen to the Big Mountain version of Baby, I Love Your Way or the Peter Frampton version.

WTL succeeds in taking away a lot of the drudgery of programming directly against the metal without hiding how Windows actually works to the point of being dangerous because developers forget how to fly without the autopilot.
posts.key_insight: The best technology products invest heavily in eliminating unnecessary decisions and configuration, letting users focus only on the choices they actually care about.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky critiques a blog post that argues programmers should never be interrupted during their planned iterations, even for urgent customer needs. He agrees that context switching is costly but argues the original post only examines one side of the cost/benefit equation. Spolsky contends that truly agile development should mean the ability to respond to changing priorities, not rigid adherence to two-week plans. He illustrates his point with a real example from Fog Creek, where the Copilot team interrupted their 2.0 development to fix a crash caused by an IE 7 bug. Despite the cost of context switching, fixing the customer-facing crash was the right call. A good manager weighs both the costs of interruption and the costs of inaction.
key_points: Context switching is genuinely harmful to programmer productivity, but that cost must be weighed against the business value of responding to urgent needs
Agile development that rigidly refuses to deviate from two-week plans is not truly agile — it merely swaps one set of bureaucratic procedures for another
Management's job is to weigh both sides of the cost/benefit equation, not just protect developers from all interruptions
Real-world example: Fog Creek's Copilot team correctly interrupted their 2.0 development to fix customer-facing crashes caused by IE 7, despite the productivity cost
Programmers are well-compensated precisely because they can handle mentally difficult tasks like context switching when the situation demands it
money_quotes: Agile is not supposed to be about swapping out one set of bureaucratic, rigid procedures for another equally rigid set of procedures that still doesn't take customer's needs into account.
Being able to do mentally difficult things like context switching makes our product better. This Is Why Programmers Get The Big Bucks.

The whole reason you gave them Aeron chairs, unlimited M&Ms, free catered lunches, and the kickass computers with the 30″ LCDs is so they can deal with new bugs Microsoft introduced in their code by messing up a DLL that used to work.
Every decision has pros and cons and when I hear a manager who is just talking about the cons without considering the pros, that manager is not doing their job.
Agile development is supposed to be about agility. It's supposed to mean that you can change plans quickly.

He's laid out a very convincing argument why Sarah should not interrupt her carefully planned two week iteration, but he hasn't even mentioned arguments for the other side: the important sale that will be lost.
posts.key_insight: True agility means weighing the real costs of context switching against the real costs of ignoring urgent customer needs, not dogmatically shielding developers from all interruptions.

posts.summary: Spolsky criticizes Windows Vista's shutdown menu for offering fifteen different ways to turn off a laptop, arguing that excessive choice creates unhappiness rather than satisfaction. Drawing on Barry Schwartz's 'The Paradox of Choice,' he demonstrates how most shutdown options could be merged or eliminated. He walks through a systematic reduction from nine menu options down to a single 'b'bye' button that handles locking, sleeping, hibernating, and powering off intelligently. The post concludes that both Microsoft and the open source movement share a flawed design philosophy of equating more options with better software.
key_points: Windows Vista presents users with up to 15 different ways to shut down a laptop, creating unnecessary cognitive burden every time they leave their computer
Most shutdown options (sleep, hibernate, lock, switch user, log off, restart) can be logically merged into a single intelligent 'away' mode that handles transitions automatically
The paradox of choice applies directly to software design: more options don't make users happier, they make users more anxious and confused
Both Microsoft and open source share a consensus-driven design philosophy that conflates 'making everybody happy' with offering every possible option
