Each individual option seems defensible in isolation, but the aggregate effect is a confusing experience for non-technical users
money_quotes: Every time you want to leave your computer, you have to choose between nine, count them, nine options: two icons and seven menu items.
The more choices you give people, the harder it is for them to choose, and the unhappier they'll feel.
iPods don't even have an on/off switch.
If you've spoken to a non-geek recently, you may have noticed that they have no idea what the difference is between 'sleep' and 'hibernate.'
Inevitably, you are going to think of a long list of intelligent, defensible reasons why each of these options is absolutely, positively essential. Don't bother. I know.
Each additional choice makes complete sense until you find yourself explaining to your uncle that he has to choose between 15 different ways to turn off a laptop.

This highlights a style of software design shared by Microsoft and the open source movement, in both cases driven by a desire for consensus and for 'Making Everybody Happy,' but it's based on the misconceived notion that lots of choices make people happy, which we really need to rethink.
posts.key_insight: Good software design means ruthlessly reducing choices rather than exposing every possible option, because each individually defensible option contributes to an collectively overwhelming experience.

posts.summary: Spolsky follows up on his criticism of Windows Vista's shutdown menu by citing insider Moishe Lettvin's revelation that 24 people were involved in implementing that single feature. He uses this as evidence that Microsoft has become hopelessly entangled in bureaucracy, overstaffing, and management layers. He argues that Microsoft's rapid hiring forced them to lower their standards significantly. The irony, he notes, is that Microsoft once looked at IBM's bloated OS/2 team as a cautionary tale, only to become the same kind of bloated monster that takes five years to ship an incoherent product.
key_points: A single shutdown menu feature required 24 people to implement, illustrating massive organizational dysfunction
Microsoft's growth forced it to lower hiring standards significantly to fill positions
The company became consumed by bureaucracy, management layers, and endless meetings
Microsoft ironically became the same kind of bloated organization (like IBM's OS/2 team) it once used as a cautionary example
money_quotes: Every piece of evidence I've heard from developers inside Microsoft supports my theory that the company has become completely tangled up in bureaucracy, layers of management, meetings ad infinitum, and overstaffing.
The only way Microsoft has managed to hire so many people has been by lowering their hiring standards significantly.

In the early nineties Microsoft looked at IBM, especially the bloated OS/2 team, as a case study of what not to do; somehow in the fifteen year period from 1991 – 2006 they became the bloated monster that takes five years to ship an incoherent upgrade to their flagship product.
posts.key_insight: Organizations that grow too fast inevitably lower their hiring bar and drown in bureaucracy, becoming the very bloated monsters they once mocked.

posts.summary: Joel briefly highlights Arno Gourdol's commentary on the design of the shutdown feature in Mac OS X, specifically the insight that physical actions like closing a laptop lid eliminate the need for software sleep commands. The post is extremely brief, essentially serving as a link to Gourdol's deeper analysis with minimal added commentary. Joel also uses the post to plug remaining copies of the Aardvark'd documentary about Fog Creek internships.
key_points: Physical hardware interactions (closing a laptop lid) can replace software UI commands for common actions like sleep
Good OS design minimizes unnecessary user decisions around system power states
The post is primarily a curated link to Arno Gourdol's more detailed analysis rather than original commentary
money_quotes: How often do you need to manually set your computer to Sleep? I just close the lid of my MacBook and it goes to sleep: a simple mechanical, physical interaction: no need for a software command.
posts.key_insight: The best interface for common system actions is no software interface at all — physical interactions like closing a laptop lid are simpler and more intuitive than navigating shutdown dialogs.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky examines how the Windows team at Microsoft organized their source control to manage thousands of developers working on a massive codebase. He shares a Windows engineer's account of how the pre-Longhorn approach of seven shared branches failed to scale, leading to a restructured system of tiered feature branches with quality gates at each merge point. The post argues that this hierarchical branching model, where teams own sub-branches and QA owns the merge points, is the right way to handle source control at scale. Spolsky endorses the pattern of giving developers private branches for frequent check-ins, with code only merging upward when it meets quality bars.
key_points: Shared branches with thousands of developers lead to either frequent build breakage or infrequent check-ins, both of which are harmful
The solution is a hierarchical branch structure mirroring the team organization, with sub-feature branches rolling up to aggregation branches and then to main
Quality gates at each merge point are essential — they're costly (days to run) but without them the OS would never have shipped
Teams that were more disciplined about quality could reverse-integrate faster and more frequently, creating a virtuous cycle
The ideal model gives every developer a private branch with QA owning the junction points between merge levels
money_quotes: Of all the things broken at Microsoft, the way they use source control on the Windows team is not one of them.
You check in frequently, and there's a very high chance of either breaking the build, or breaking functionality in the OS.
These gates were essential in upholding the quality of the main branch, and had they not existed, the OS would have never shipped.
Generally, the more disciplined teams were about quality, the faster and more frequently they'd RI.
From what you know about the varying levels of stability/quality of components of the OS, it's pretty easy to map that back to RI velocity and so forth, since it all goes hand-in-hand pretty nicely.
So they can check in as often as they want, only merging up when they feel that their code is stable.
Your QA department owns the junction points above each merge.
posts.key_insight: At massive scale, source control must mirror organizational structure with hierarchical branches and quality gates at each merge point, so developers can check in freely while QA ensures only stable code flows toward the trunk.

posts.summary: Spolsky critiques the recurring media narrative that new programming tools will make software development as easy as snapping together Lego bricks. He traces this claim from a 1991 BusinessWeek cover story about object-oriented programming to a 2006 piece by Om Malik about new development platforms, showing how the same promise keeps resurfacing without ever materializing. He contrasts these optimistic predictions with Fred Brooks' 1987 argument in 'No Silver Bullet' that the inherent difficulty of software lies in conceptual design, not in the mechanics of coding. The post implies that journalists consistently misunderstand what makes programming hard.
key_points: Media coverage of programming tools repeatedly falls for vendor claims that new technology will make coding trivially easy, often using the Lego metaphor
The same 'snap-together programming' narrative appeared in 1991 with OOP and again in 2006 with web platforms, yet the fundamental difficulty of software persists
Fred Brooks identified in 1987 that the hard part of software is specification, design, and conceptual integrity — not the labor of writing code
Journalists conflate making syntax easier with making programming easier, missing that complexity is inherent to the problem domain
money_quotes: Frequently, the mainstream media, reporting on computer programming tools, gets the story horribly wrong.

What happens is that some kind of vendor of programming technologies has come up with some product they are claiming makes programming easier. The journalists don't really understand. What they hear is 'programming is going to be easier.'
Not only are there no silver bullets now in view, the very nature of software makes it unlikely that there will be any.

I believe the hard part of building software to be the specification, design, and testing of this conceptual construct, not the labor of representing it and testing the fidelity of the representation.
If this is true, building software will always be hard. There is inherently no silver bullet.
posts.key_insight: The media perennially falls for the claim that new tools will make programming as easy as assembling Lego bricks, ignoring Brooks' insight that software's difficulty is inherent in the conceptual design, not in the mechanics of coding.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky argues that simplicity in software is overrated and often misunderstood as a product strategy. He contends that the 80/20 rule fails because everyone uses a different 20% of features, making 'lite' products frustrating for most users. While he acknowledges simplicity works as a bootstrapping strategy for resource-constrained startups, he argues it fails as a long-term approach because competitors can easily clone simple products and users ultimately want more features. He distinguishes between simplicity as usability or aesthetic minimalism (both valid) versus simplicity as deliberately lacking features (a losing strategy), citing his own experience at Fog Creek where new feature releases consistently drove the most revenue growth.
key_points: The 80/20 rule for features fails because every user relies on a different 20%, so any 'lite' product will frustrate most of its potential audience
Simple products work as bootstrapping strategies for resource-constrained startups, but this is a happy coincidence of necessity rather than a proven long-term approach
Products like the iPod and 37signals succeed not because of simplicity per se, but because of beauty, usability, emotional appeal, and design — which are themselves features
At Fog Creek, nothing increased revenue more than releasing new versions with more features — not marketing, not press coverage, not advertising
Simplicity as 'easy to use' or 'clean aesthetic' is valid; simplicity as 'deliberately fewer features' is a misattribution of what makes products successful
money_quotes: 80% of the people use 20% of the features. So you convince yourself that you only need to implement 20% of the features, and you can still sell 80% as many copies. Unfortunately, it's never the same 20%.
So you sell 'simple' as if it were this wonderful thing, when, coincidentally, it's the only thing you have the resources to produce.

I think it is a misattribution to say, for example, that the iPod is successful because it lacks features. If you start to believe that, you'll believe, among other things, that you should take out features to increase your product's success.
Nothing we have ever done at Fog Creek has increased our revenue more than releasing a new version with more features. Nothing.
The Hummer is aesthetically appealing precisely because it's ugly and complicated.
The flow to our bottom line from new versions with new features is absolutely undeniable. It's like gravity.

If you think simplicity means 'not very many features' or 'does one thing and does it well,' then I applaud your integrity but you can't go that far with a product that deliberately leaves features out.
posts.key_insight: Simplicity is often confused with usability and good design, but deliberately reducing features is not what makes products successful — users want all the features, they just want them to be well-designed and easy to use.

posts.summary: Spolsky explores the concept of elegance in software design, drawing from Alain de Botton's comparison of two bridges to argue that the best software accomplishes its task with grace and economy rather than brute force. He connects this to his earlier discussions of choices and simplicity, arguing that software should be invisible to users who are just trying to accomplish real-world goals. Good choices support the user's task directly, while bad choices intrude on what they're actually trying to do. He distinguishes between simplicity as elegance (making powerful features feel effortless) and simplicity as feature reduction, arguing the former is desirable while the latter limits a product's market.
key_points: Elegance in software means accomplishing powerful things with grace and economy, making difficulty look effortless—like Maillart's bridge versus Brunel's heavier construction
Users don't care about software itself; they're using it as a tool for real-world goals, and the more they notice the software, the more annoyed they become
Choices are good when they directly support the user's task, but bad when they intrude with decisions the user doesn't care about, like unsolicited upgrade prompts
Simplicity-as-elegance (searching all fields at once like iTunes vs. forcing a choice like Rhapsody) creates power through economy, and is a genuine feature
Simplicity-as-feature-reduction shrinks the product's ability to solve real problems and limits market potential
money_quotes: Unless they're software reviewers for a living, they don't really care about the software itself, and the more they notice it, the more annoyed they're going to be.
You just cannot imagine how little I want to spend even three seconds of my life thinking about whether or not to install that new JVM.
If the entire collective wisdom of the Java development team doesn't know if it's going to break something, how am I supposed to know?
Economy means power, in this case, and it's a feature.
If you're using simplicity to mean a lack of power, a lack of features, that's fine, if you want to be in the paper clip business, good luck with that.
Choices are bad when they represent an intrusion into the user's actual DNA-replication goals.
iTunes doesn't give you any choice: it just searches all fields, which works just as well and is easier.
posts.key_insight: True software elegance is not removing features but making powerful capabilities feel effortless, so users never have to think about the software standing between them and their goals.

posts.summary: Joel announces a new Visual Studio add-in for FogBugz that lets developers access their bug tracker from within the IDE. He also introduces the FogBugz API version 1.0, which enables third-party integrations. The API currently supports basic functionality like listing open cases, with more features planned for FogBugz 6.0. He mentions the add-in will eventually become a paid premium feature but is free during its initial launch.
key_points: FogBugz for Visual Studio integrates bug tracking directly into the IDE as a tool window
The new FogBugz API 1.0 was created to make the add-in possible, with expanded functionality planned for version 6.0
The add-in is offered free initially but will become a paid premium feature
Third-party developers are invited to request API features they need for their own integrations
money_quotes: In the long run, I think, FogBugz for Visual Studio will be available as a premium feature available for a small extra charge, but for the time being, since it's brand new, you can download it for free.
Because FogBugz has been shipping for a while without an API, we provide a couple of files you have to install on your FogBugz server to make the API work.
posts.key_insight: Shipping a product API after initial launch enables an ecosystem of integrations, and offering new add-ins for free at first is a practical way to drive adoption before monetizing.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky notes that Fog Creek's internal FogBugz database has passed 500,000 case numbers. He quickly clarifies that the vast majority are spam, faxes, customer emails, to-do items, and other non-bug entries. The actual breakdown reveals only 66,443 real cases, 7,303 fixed bugs, and 909 implemented features. The post serves as a lighthearted showcase of how FogBugz is used as an all-purpose tracking system, not just a bug tracker.
key_points: FogBugz hit 500,000 case numbers, but the vast majority are spam that gets auto-assigned a case number before deletion
The tool is used for far more than bugs: faxes, customer email, to-do lists, job applications, phone messages, sysadmin notifications, and crash reports
Out of 500,000 cases, only 66,443 are real cases, with 7,303 actual bugs fixed and 909 features implemented
815 bugs could not be reproduced — a wry closing detail that any developer will recognize
money_quotes: Last night, at some point, we passed case number 500,000 in the Fog Creek in-house FogBugz database.
We actually have 66,443 real cases and 489,022 historical events.
We have replied to 33,692 customer emails using FogBugz, fixed 7,303 bugs, and implemented 909 features.
815 bugs could not be reproduced.
Incoming spam gets assigned a case number, even though it usually lands in a spam folder and gets deleted after seven days, so most of those cases are spam.
posts.key_insight: A good issue tracker becomes the central nervous system of a software company, handling far more than just bugs — and the raw case count tells you almost nothing about actual engineering work.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky writes a promotional year-end post encouraging readers to improve their careers in the new year. He highlights three paths: applying for Fog Creek's Management Training Program (which combines work experience with a Columbia Masters degree), browsing interesting job listings on his jobs board, or posting openings if you already work at a great company. The post features a testimonial from a current trainee and lists several compelling companies hiring through the board. It doubles as an advertisement for Fog Creek's jobs board with a discount coupon for employers.
key_points: Fog Creek's Management Training Program combines paid work experience with a funded Masters degree in technology management from Columbia
The jobs board features diverse, mission-driven companies working on surgical robots, mobile media, virtual worlds, and education
Joel frames career improvement as a concrete, actionable resolution rather than an abstract aspiration
The post subtly reinforces Joel's standards for good workplaces by contrasting them with dysfunctional environments
money_quotes: I woke up in the middle of the night the day after I read about it, knowing I had to apply.

No matter what I do here — learning FogBugz to do sales, learning SQL Server to do customer support, or even reconciling the credit card bill — I feel empowered in a way I've never felt at another workplace.
Once I'm done with my Fog Creek training and my degree from Columbia, I'll be a superlatively hireable package.

If you know of companies that are noisy, fluorescent, cubicle farms working 90 hour weeks, where the CTO refuses to use source code control because he's afraid people will blame him for the horrible code he writes, please don't tell them about this offer.
posts.key_insight: Career improvement is best pursued through workplaces that invest in employee growth and maintain high standards for working conditions.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky provides an elaborate, Talmud-style commentary on a blog post by tech pundit Steve Gillmor, annotating every obscure reference and name-drop to make it comprehensible. Spolsky explains Gillmor's criticisms of Sun CEO Jonathan Schwartz's advocacy for 'thick clients' and Dave Winer's commentary on JSON and web APIs. Through three hours of research and annotation, Spolsky demonstrates that Gillmor's central arguments collapse under scrutiny: Schwartz was actually arguing for Ajax-style rich clients (which Gillmor also favors), and Winer's JSON discussion was a simple misunderstanding he'd already corrected. The post serves as both a public service translation and an implicit critique of punditry that relies on impenetrable insider references and fails to verify its own premises.
key_points: Steve Gillmor's writing is nearly incomprehensible due to unexplained insider references, name-drops, and jargon that require extensive annotation to parse
Gillmor's critique of Jonathan Schwartz's 'thick client' advocacy is based on a misreading — Schwartz was actually arguing for Ajax/JavaScript as 'rich clients,' a position Gillmor himself holds
Tech pundits frequently extrapolate wildly about nascent trends as if they've already conquered the world, like assuming porting Microsoft Office to the web is just 'flipping the switch'
PR firms attempting to infiltrate the blogosphere with traditional press release tactics are training bloggers' spam filters and generating ill-will toward their clients
After three hours of careful research, Gillmor's entire thesis about old dogs needing new tricks 'completely collapses in a puff of smoke'
money_quotes: Nobody I know can understand a thing Steve Gillmor is talking about, mainly because he makes so many obscure references without explaining them.
It took me over three hours to research and explain all this, and, as you'll see if you follow closely, Gillmor's entire argument fell apart under scrutiny.
One of the frequent failings of pundits is a strong tendency to extrapolate wildly about trends which haven't even begun yet, and assume that they will take over the world.
There was no recognition that web-based word processors and spreadsheets, while amusing, have merely a sliver of the capabilities of Windows-based apps.

These releases are sent by completely clueless PR professionals who have absolutely no idea what's going on; not only are they clueless, but they actually think they're clever by adding a few bloggers to their spam directories.
What they're really doing is training bloggers' bayesian spam filters to ignore the company they're supposedly promoting, and generating ill-will towards their clients by spamming on their behalf.
Gillmor's saber rattling is endearing but completely impotent.
posts.key_insight: Punditry that relies on impenetrable jargon and insider references often collapses entirely when someone takes the time to actually research and verify its claims.

posts.summary: Spolsky expresses excitement about Scott Rosenberg's forthcoming book 'Dreaming in Code,' which chronicles the troubled development of Chandler, an open-source personal information manager. He praises Rosenberg as the journalist who best understands software development, citing a previous interview experience. Spolsky notes the irony that Chandler was hyped as an 'Outlook killer' in 2003 but four years later had only reached version 0.7alpha4. The post also briefly touches on the ethics of accepting review copies as a blogger.
key_points: Scott Rosenberg stands out among technology journalists for his deep understanding of software development
Chandler's development serves as a cautionary tale: hyped as an 'Outlook killer' in 2003, it was still in early alpha four years later
Being hyped by the press early in development is 'almost certainly a bad sign' for a software project
The question of why software projects fail or stall remains fascinating and worth studying through narrative journalism
money_quotes: Of all the journalists covering technology and especially software development I think Scott understands it the best.
I've been watching Chandler closely ever since it was probably doomed by being breathlessly hailed by Wired Magazine as 'The Outlook Killer' in January, 2003.
Well, it's January 2007 now, Outlook isn't dead, and Chandler is up to 0.7alpha4.
Being hyped by Wired Magazine is almost certainly a bad sign.
I've been wondering what the heck went so wrong on the Chandler team, or perhaps it's just a matter of good software taking ten years?
posts.key_insight: Early media hype is a warning sign for software projects, and understanding why ambitious projects like Chandler stall is one of the most important questions in software development.

posts.summary: Spolsky reviews Scott Rosenberg's 'Dreaming in Code,' which chronicles the Chandler project's failure to ship meaningful software despite years of effort and millions of dollars. He uses a brilliant analogy about how human eyes create an illusion of complete vision through rapid page-faulting to explain why software teams overestimate how well they understand their own grand visions. He argues Chandler failed because its design was described in adjectives ('revolutionary') rather than concrete specifics, and because the team hired programmers before doing real design work. Spolsky also critiques the project's key ideas—peer-to-peer sync, 'no silos,' and natural language dates—as either too hard, already done, or fundamentally flawed from a usability perspective.
key_points: Your mind tricks you into thinking you have a complete picture of a software project when you actually only have vague impressions, leading teams to skip critical design work
Hiring programmers before completing design is a compounding mistake—designing software as a team in meetings is far harder than one person working alone with paper
Specs described in adjectives ('revolutionary,' 'extremely cool') rather than concrete specifics are a red flag that real design hasn't happened
The 'No Silos' concept of merging email and calendar appeals to architecture astronauts but destroys usability by eliminating real-world metaphors users rely on
Open source volunteers don't materialize for apps that don't do anything yet—they contribute to working software where they can scratch their own itches
money_quotes: One of the unfortunate side effects is that your mind gets into a bad habit of overestimating how clearly it understands things. It always thinks it has The Big Picture even when it doesn't.
The only thing harder than trying to design software is trying to design software as a team.
Did you ever try to write poetry in a committee meeting? It's like a bunch of fat construction guys trying to write an opera while sitting on the couch watching Baywatch.
I don't know about you, but I can't code 'revolutionary.' I need more details to write code.

Whenever the spec describes the product in terms of adjectives ('it will be extremely cool') rather than specifics ('it will have brushed-aluminum title bars and all the icons will be reflected a little bit, as if placed on a grand piano') you know you're in trouble.

The way you make users understand your program model is with metaphors. When you try to combine two very dramatically different real-world items into the same kind of thing in the user interface, usability suffers because there's no longer a real-world metaphor that applies.
When you have an app that doesn't do anything yet, nobody finds it itchy. They're not using it. So you don't get volunteers.
posts.key_insight: Software projects fail when teams mistake a vague, adjective-driven vision for actual design, diving into code before anyone has worked out the concrete details of what they're building.

posts.summary: Joel announces version 2.1 of the FogBugz Add-in for Visual Studio, which fixes several bugs. The update improves position persistence across sessions and better handles SSL and authenticated installations. He thanks specific community members for their detailed bug reports that helped identify and resolve the issues.
key_points: FogBugz for Visual Studio 2.1 released with multiple bug fixes
Key improvements include remembering window position and better SSL/authentication support
Community bug reports were instrumental in identifying and fixing problems
money_quotes: Thanks to Olivier, James, Matt, Jason, Alan, and Miguel for detailed bug reports which helped me track down and fix all these problems.
posts.key_insight: A brief product update announcement highlighting bug fixes and thanking community contributors for their detailed reports.

posts.summary: Joel announces the release of Fog Creek Copilot 2.0, a remote desktop support tool that requires zero configuration and works through firewalls. The update adds Mac support, a clever direct connect feature that silently upgrades relay connections to peer-to-peer when possible, and file transfer capabilities. The price for day passes was also cut from $10 to $5, with the reasoning that lower friction will drive more users and ultimately more corporate subscriptions. Joel highlights that the product was originally built by four interns in 12 weeks and continues to be developed by two of them who joined full-time.
key_points: Copilot 2.0 adds Mac OS X support, file transfer, and a direct connect feature that transparently upgrades relay connections to peer-to-peer for better performance
The product was originally built entirely by four interns in a 12-week summer internship, demonstrating what a small team with a good spec and open source foundations can accomplish
The day pass price was cut from $10 to $5, betting that lower prices will drive higher volume and more corporate subscriptions
The direct connect feature is architecturally clever: it guarantees 100% initial connectivity through relay servers, then silently optimizes to peer-to-peer in the background
The core value proposition is eliminating all configuration friction—no firewall rules, no port forwarding, no installation required
money_quotes: Fog Creek Copilot is a remote tech support service that lets one person control another computer remotely, much like VNC or RDC, with the advantage that it requires zero configuration, works through firewalls, and installs nothing.
One of the reasons they were able to accomplish so much in one summer is that they used open source software as a starting point.
Ben, by the way, is the only person I know who writes code in C, C++, C#, and Objective C all in the same day, while writing a book about Smalltalk at night.

It sets up the initial connection through our servers, so you get connected right away with 100% reliability. But then once you're all connected, it quietly, in the background, looks for a way to make a direct connection.
We don't want anyone to have an excuse not to use Fog Creek Copilot.

To avoid paying $10, you might actually be crazy enough to try to just talk your mom into uninstalling Norton Utilities, punching the appropriate holes in the Windows firewall, and setting up appropriate port-forwarding rules on her broadband router… but for $5, why go through the trouble?
All you need to tell your mom, or your customer, is 'Go to copilot.com, type in this number, and download and run the program you find there.' And to know that it will Just Work.
We're betting that the lower price will lead to more users, which will lead to more corporate subscriptions, which will lead to higher total revenues.
posts.key_insight: The best technical products eliminate friction at every level—from zero-configuration networking to pricing low enough that no one bothers with workarounds.

posts.summary: Joel shares three positive press reviews of Fog Creek Copilot 2.0's new Mac support. The reviews highlight Copilot's ease of use for remote tech support, particularly for helping non-technical family members. The post serves as a brief product announcement through the lens of external validation, with reviewers praising the zero-configuration approach and the significance of Fog Creek finally supporting Macs.
key_points: Copilot 2.0 added Mac support, which was a notable gap in Fog Creek's product lineup
The product's key value proposition is zero-configuration remote access, making it ideal for helping non-technical users
Copilot positioned itself against more expensive and complex alternatives like Apple Remote Desktop
money_quotes: Up to now Fog Creek was 'the best software company that doesn't make Mac products.' Now I guess I'll have to drop that qualification.

Copilot, like similar other 'remote tech support' systems out there, does not require any sort of configuration on the part of the client, making it easy for you to have someone install it and you accessing their computer in no time.
I'm not the only person who knows the joys of trying to talk a frustrated parent through some troubleshooting technique.
posts.key_insight: Fog Creek's Copilot 2.0 Mac launch addressed a significant platform gap, with reviewers emphasizing that zero-configuration remote support solves a universal pain point of helping non-technical users.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief link post where Joel Spolsky shares that he was interviewed by Bob Walsh and Michael Lehman for The MicroISV Show on Channel 9. The post contains no substantive argument or analysis, serving purely as a pointer to external content. It reflects Joel's practice of cross-promoting his media appearances on his blog.
key_points: Joel was interviewed for The MicroISV Show, a program focused on micro independent software vendors
The interview was hosted by Bob Walsh and Michael Lehman on Microsoft's Channel 9 platform
The post is a minimal link-only entry with no editorial commentary
posts.key_insight: This is a brief promotional link post directing readers to an external interview about micro-ISV topics, with no substantive content of its own.

posts.summary: This is a very brief teaser post linking to Joel Spolsky's longer article 'Seven Steps to Remarkable Customer Service.' The post uses a personal anecdote about a frustrating experience with a locksmith to hook the reader into clicking through to the full article. The squiggly-lines-of-anger imagery conveys the emotional state of a dissatisfied customer. The post serves as a promotion for the longer piece rather than containing substantive argument itself.
key_points: Uses a relatable customer frustration story as a hook for a longer article on customer service
Illustrates the emotional toll of repeated service failures from the customer's perspective
Demonstrates that even loyal customers have a breaking point after repeated poor experiences
money_quotes: I was tempted just to give up on him. But I decided to give this loser one more chance.
posts.key_insight: Repeated customer service failures push even patient customers to the brink of abandoning a provider entirely.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky outlines seven (plus one bonus) principles for delivering customer service so good that customers remark about it. Drawing from Fog Creek's experience as a bootstrapped company, he argues that fixing problems at their root (not just symptom-level), taking blame gracefully, offering generous refund policies, and hiring highly qualified support staff creates fanatically devoted customers. He uses vivid anecdotes—a locksmith, a diner owner, Lands' End—to illustrate how emotional dynamics in customer interactions matter as much as the technical fix. The core thesis is that investing more upfront in quality support is cheaper long-term than handling the same problems repeatedly with cheap outsourced labor.
key_points: Fix every support problem twice: once for the customer and once in the product, treating each incident like an NTSB crash investigation to eliminate entire classes of problems permanently.
Generous refund policies (90-day no-questions-asked) cost Fog Creek only 2% of revenue while eliminating chargebacks entirely and removing customer anxiety that leads to hostility.
Taking blame with phrases like 'it's my fault' instantly defuses angry customers—avoiding lawsuits comes from not having angry people, not from never admitting fault.
Customer service staff must be highly qualified and co-located with developers, with a real career path, because once common problems are eliminated only hard debugging remains.
Treat angry customer interactions like puppetry: the customer is yelling at the business, not at you personally, so focus on what response will make them happy.
money_quotes: We treat each tech support call like the NTSB treats airliner crashes.
When customers have a problem and you fix it, they're actually going to be even more satisfied than if they never had a problem in the first place.
The cheap call centers have no mechanism for getting problems fixed; indeed, they have no incentive to get problems fixed because their income depends on repeat business.
We were able to increase our sales tenfold while only doubling the cost of providing tech support.
The way to avoid getting sued is not to have people who are mad at you.
You will never win an argument with your customer.
Our customers are nice because they're not worried. They're not worried because we have a ridiculously liberal return policy.
Say 'It's my fault' a hundred times one morning in the shower, until it starts to sound like syllabic nonsense. Then you'll be able to say it on demand.
posts.key_insight: Investing in high-quality, root-cause-fixing customer service costs more per incident but dramatically less overall, while turning customers into vocal advocates.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky recounts how he publicly boasted about Fog Creek's 0% chargeback rate, only to receive their first-ever chargeback shortly after. He confirms the rate was literally zero, not rounded. The proof came from custom reconciliation software that screen-scrapes their credit card processor and matches transactions with QuickBooks. When the first chargeback appeared, the software crashed because it had no code path to handle that case. They had to modify the software to accommodate the previously impossible event.
key_points: Fog Creek had a literal 0% chargeback rate, not a rounded figure, which is extraordinarily rare for any business
Joel had built custom software to reconcile credit card transactions with QuickBooks via screen-scraping
The software's inability to handle a chargeback served as independent proof that one had never occurred
The first chargeback arrived suspiciously soon after Joel publicly boasted about having none
money_quotes: Some people speculated that I was rounding, and really meant, say, 0.45%. Nope, I really meant zero. None.
As soon as I wrote this, some wise guy decided to dispute a charge.
So today, for the first time in the history of that app running, it finds a chargeback on the statement, and doesn't know what to do with it.
Unrecognized entry: I don't know what account VISA CHARGEBACK should be posted to.
And that's how I know we've never had a chargeback… until today!
posts.key_insight: Software that fails on an edge case can serve as unintentional proof that the edge case has never occurred before.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky discusses Fog Creek's need for more office space, sharing the formulas and considerations involved in calculating office space requirements. He breaks down square footage allocations per employee and common areas, explains the difference between carpetable and rentable square footage, and humorously critiques landlord practices around loss factors. He also explores strategies for handling growth during long-term leases, including a novel idea of subletting excess space to software startups as premium programmer-friendly offices.
key_points: Individual offices of 70-100 sqft per employee plus 30% circulation overhead form the basis of office space planning
Rentable square footage advertised by landlords includes significant unusable space like elevator shafts, mechanicals, and common areas, making it misleading for tenants
Growing companies face a tension between long-term leases (5-10 years) and unpredictable space needs, with no perfect solution
Fog Creek considered an early version of coworking: subletting excess space to bootstrapping startups in programmer-optimized offices at ~$1500/month
money_quotes: It would be really nice if office space listings were given in carpetable square feet, because that's what the tenant cares about.
The building owner's house in Queens which if you tried to go near a goon would shoot you in the knees (despite his very fetching daughter trying to seduce you from her bedroom window).

In reality, this just gets you in the ballpark, and every space is different (long narrow spaces need more circulation space) so there's no alternative to having your architect take the floor plans and actually figure out how many offices will fit in there.

Getting too much space and renting it out in small chunks to software startups that need an office or two while they're bootstrapping, and who would appreciate a working environment designed for programmers rather than a coffee shop.
The trouble is that it would end up costing something on the order of $1500 a month for a private, Fog Creek-quality office, which might be out of the range of bootstrapped startups.
posts.key_insight: Office space planning for growing software companies is a messy optimization problem where landlord metrics obscure reality and long-term leases conflict with unpredictable growth.

posts.summary: Joel announces that FogBugz 5.0.31 now ships with German language support, making it the product's first multi-lingual version. Rather than creating a separate German edition, both languages are built into the default product, with per-user language selection that defaults to browser settings. The team uses a Pig Latin translation step to ensure all strings are localizable before handing them to German translators. Fog Creek has also established sales and support operations in Munich.
key_points: FogBugz ships its first multi-lingual version supporting English and German, with language selection per user or auto-detected from browser settings
Rather than maintaining separate product editions for different languages, localization is built into the single default product
The team uses Pig Latin as an intermediate translation step to verify all strings are properly localizable before real translation
Fog Creek established local sales and support in Munich with German-language customer service
money_quotes: Rather than have a different edition of FogBugz, the default edition now just supports both languages.
To ensure that every string is localizable, our developers translate everything to Pig Latin first before handing it off to the German translators.
posts.key_insight: Building localization into the default product rather than maintaining separate editions, and using a pseudo-translation step like Pig Latin to catch unlocalizable strings, is a practical approach to internationalization.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky posts a job listing seeking a technical writer to update the FogBugz project management book for version 6.0. The original author Mike Gunderloy is unavailable, and the revision requires about 40% new content plus refreshed screenshots due to a major UI overhaul. Joel highlights the book's value as both documentation and a software project management introduction. An update notes that Gunderloy ultimately found time to do the revision himself.
key_points: FogBugz 6.0 introduced enough new features and UI changes to require a substantial book revision with 40% more content
The book served a dual purpose as both product documentation and a general introduction to software project management
The update revealing Mike Gunderloy's return suggests a preference for continuity of authorship over bringing in someone new
money_quotes: More than just a manual, this book is a great introduction to software project management.
I think we'll all be better off if this book will become the standard against which other end-user documentation will be measured.
posts.key_insight: Good product documentation should transcend mere feature descriptions and serve as a genuine introduction to the discipline the product supports.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces plans to expand his job board to serve the Indian market, noting strong readership from India but minimal job postings. Rather than making decisions unilaterally, he crowdsources advice from his readers on localization details including domain naming, pricing, payment methods, and scope. The post is essentially a public product requirements gathering exercise.
key_points: The Joel on Software job board has been successful but has almost no listings for India despite significant Indian readership
Spolsky openly crowdsources product decisions from his community rather than guessing at international market needs
Key localization concerns include domain naming conventions, payment methods beyond credit cards, pricing, and whether to include H1B visa sponsorship listings
money_quotes: The job board, jobs.joelonsoftware.com, has been rather successful.
I was thinking about setting up a separate job board specifically focused on jobs in India, since there are so many Joel on Software readers there and I've only seen three jobs (in Bangalore) posted.
What should I do to localize the job board for India?
posts.key_insight: When expanding a product into an unfamiliar market, asking your existing users in that market what they need is more valuable than guessing.

posts.summary: Joel documents the challenges of finding office space in New York City that provides both private offices and natural daylight. He explains the geometry problem: the distance from outer windows to the building core determines how many offices can receive light, with roughly 30 feet being the maximum usable depth. Manhattan's building stock is optimized for large open floorplates suited to trading floors and cubicle farms, making private offices with daylight nearly impossible to find.
key_points: Private offices with natural daylight require the outer wall-to-core distance to be 30 feet or less, limiting viable buildings
Manhattan's standard block dimensions (264 feet apart) produce buildings with 60-70 foot depths, double what works for private offices
Wall Street's demand for large unobstructed trading floors has driven NYC developers to optimize for exactly the opposite of what software teams need
Smaller buildings that don't fill a block often lack windows on all sides, pushing the core to windowless walls and worsening the depth problem
money_quotes: Our desire to have (a) private offices with (b) lots of daylight is very hard to accomodate in New York City.
If the distance from the outside window to the core is more than, say, 30 feet, you either have to waste space or give someone a dark office.
Those big office buildings you see that take up an entire city block might have floorplans that are 200 x 200 feet.
If you're building a sea of cubicles, this is very efficient. If you want 100% private offices, this doesn't work well at all.
The ideal trading floor is a huge square, the bigger the better, without a single column. This is exactly the opposite of what software developers want.
posts.key_insight: Manhattan's commercial real estate is geometrically optimized for open floorplans and trading floors, making it structurally hostile to the private-office-with-daylight layout that benefits software developers.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the launch of a job board specifically for positions in India, hosted at jobs.joelonsoftware.co.in. He prices job listings at $50 (about 2200 rupees) as an introductory rate to help fill the board. He differentiates his board from major Indian job sites like Monsterindia and Naukri.com by promising fewer but higher-quality applicants, with a money-back guarantee.
key_points: Launch of a region-specific job board targeting the Indian market
Introductory pricing of $50 per listing to attract early adopters
Value proposition centered on quality over quantity of applicants, differentiating from mass-market job boards
money_quotes: Unlike the huge boards, Monsterindia, Naukri.com, etc., a job posted to Joel on Software will get far fewer resumes, but they will be of much higher quality.
posts.key_insight: A niche job board targeting a self-selected audience of engaged readers can deliver higher-quality candidates than mass-market alternatives, even in a large market like India.

posts.summary: This is a brief Friday link post where Joel Spolsky directs readers to watch his interview on The Scoble Show with Robert Scoble. He offers both a full hour-long version and a 6-minute edited highlights version. The post contains essentially no substantive content beyond the links themselves.
key_points: Joel was interviewed by Robert Scoble for The Scoble Show, a popular tech video series
The interview runs just over an hour with a 6-minute edited version available
The post is a casual Friday link drop with minimal commentary
money_quotes: It's Friday, and you weren't going to get anything done anyway, so go watch Robert Scoble interview me for The Scoble Show.
posts.key_insight: A throwaway link post pointing readers to an external video interview, with no substantive argument or analysis.

posts.summary: This is a brief link roundup post where Joel shares three items: a review calling the Grand Canyon Skywalk a ripoff, a podcast interview he did about customer service and user experience, and a travel anecdote about a miserable experience changing planes at Paris Charles de Gaulle airport via Air France. The post is extremely short and informal, serving more as a personal update than a substantive essay.
key_points: The Grand Canyon Skywalk is characterized as a tourist trap
Joel was interviewed about customer service for a UX podcast, suggesting his interest in user experience topics
Charles de Gaulle airport is singled out as a terrible experience for connecting flights, with Joel noting that even routine scheduled arrivals seem to surprise the ground crew
money_quotes: Nothing surprises the ground crew at CDG more than the arrival of the daily, scheduled flight from Kennedy.
Every single thing about that airport is broken and it's the worst place in the world to change planes.
posts.key_insight: A lightweight link post with no central thesis, though Joel's CDG rant reflects his characteristic intolerance for broken systems and poor user experiences.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky discusses the financial and practical challenges of building out office space, particularly the tax disadvantage of leasehold improvements which can't be meaningfully deducted. He explains how his company outgrew their current office and faces the prospect of spending millions on construction for a new 15,000 square foot space. He reveals why companies build cubicle farms instead of real walls: office furniture depreciates faster and can be leased, making it tax-deductible. His proposed solution is manufactured, modular wall systems from Haworth that qualify as furniture rather than leasehold improvements while still providing private offices.
key_points: Leasehold improvements are barely tax-deductible (depreciated over 39 years), while office furniture depreciates over 7 years and can be leased with fully deductible payments
The tax code creates a perverse incentive where companies choose cubicle farms over private offices not because they're cheaper to build, but because of how they're classified for depreciation
Manufactured modular wall systems offer a workaround: they provide real private offices but qualify as moveable furniture rather than permanent construction
Building out office space is also environmentally wasteful since the next tenant will likely demolish everything and start over
money_quotes: This is why companies build cubicle farms instead of walls, even though the dollar cost is comparable.
You have $0 in the bank, but the IRS thinks you made a $20 profit.
To build that much office space could cost a couple of million dollars. With the lack of deductability, your bank account goes down by three million dollars.
All in all, it's a big waste of money and environmentally unsound.
Please do not take tax advice from me. I am morbidly unqualified to give tax advice.
posts.key_insight: The US tax code's distinction between leasehold improvements and office furniture is the hidden reason companies build cubicle farms instead of private offices, but modular wall systems offer a clever workaround that provides real offices with better tax treatment.

posts.summary: Joel recounts the saga of email search in Microsoft Outlook, which was so poor that a third-party tool called Lookout became essential. Microsoft acquired Lookout, removed it from the market, and then Outlook 2007 actively blocked it from running while offering an inferior built-in search powered by Windows Desktop Search. The built-in search was roughly 100 times slower than Lookout had been. A subsequent patch from Microsoft finally improved search performance enough to make Outlook 2007 usable, though still not as fast as the original Lookout.
key_points: Outlook's native search was so bad that users relied on third-party tools like Lookout, which used the open-source Lucene engine for sub-second full-text search
Microsoft acquired Lookout, removed it from the market, and Outlook 2007 specifically blocked it from running — while offering a far slower replacement
The Windows Desktop Search integration made searches take about 30 seconds instead of less than one second, a 100x regression
A later patch fixed the worst performance issues for users with large PST files, making Outlook 2007 finally adequate but still not as fast as the tool it killed
money_quotes: Search used to be horrible in Outlook. It was so bad that for all intents and purposes, you couldn't search your old email.
Microsoft did the only thing that made sense: they bought Lookout (the company) and took the product off the market.
People complained.
When Outlook 2007 runs, it checks to see if Lookout was running and disabled it if it was.
The only possible explanation is that someone on the Outlook team is getting paid a bonus for convincing people to switch to Gmail.
Now I can search old email quickly enough that I don't forget what I was searching for by the time the results come up.
posts.key_insight: When a platform owner acquires and kills a superior third-party tool, they take on an obligation to match its quality — and Microsoft's failure to do so with Outlook search turned an upgrade into a downgrade.

posts.summary: Spolsky describes a clever method for deriving demand curves by exploiting exchange rate fluctuations as a natural price experiment. Since FogBugz is priced in US dollars but sold to UK customers, the falling dollar effectively created varying price points in pounds sterling without deliberate price changes. The data reveals that large customers buying volume licenses are price-insensitive, while small customers exhibit classic downward-sloping demand curves. He acknowledges significant limitations in the data but suggests it as a practical technique for software companies selling internationally.
key_points: Exchange rate fluctuations create a natural experiment for testing price sensitivity without deliberately changing prices
Large business customers buying volume licenses show no correlation between price and sales volume, suggesting price insensitivity
Small customers (startups and small ISVs) exhibit classic downward-sloping demand curves and respond to psychological pricing (prices ending in 9)
The technique has significant confounds: the dollar's steady decline means lower prices correlate with later dates, and customers may not track actual exchange rates
money_quotes: The more you learn about pricing, the less you seem to know.

To set prices well, you need to be able to plot your customers' demand curves, and it's almost impossible to figure out what your demand curve is, because it's so hard to charge different customers different amounts and get any kind of reliable data.
Large customers are not price sensitive. These are larger businesses that are used to spending money on things, and it's not their money, anyway.
Small customers are price sensitive. They're startups and small ISVs that are used to watching their budget.
They exhibit a classic downward-sloping demand curve as economics would predict, and they like prices that end in 9 a bit better than prices around it.
posts.key_insight: Exchange rate fluctuations against your pricing currency create a natural experiment that can reveal demand curve segments for international customers without requiring deliberate price discrimination.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces a guest editorial he wrote for Inc. magazine about recruiting and internships. He expresses pride at being invited to contribute to a publication he reads cover-to-cover. He humorously notes that professional editing made his writing feel more polished than his usual blog style, and shares a photo of Fog Creek's previous summer interns.
key_points: Joel was invited to write a guest editorial for Inc. magazine on recruiting and internships
He considers Inc. a valuable resource for entrepreneurs and reads every issue thoroughly
Professional editing changed his voice slightly, making it feel more polished than his typical blog writing style
money_quotes: Inc. (the magazine) is a great resource for entrepreneurs. For the last several years, I've been reading it cover-to-cover the day it arrives.
Thanks to professional editing, it feels a little bit polished compared to my usual style.
posts.key_insight: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to Joel's Inc. magazine guest editorial on recruiting and internships, offering little substantive content beyond the referral itself.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky traces the history of VBA from its origins as Excel's macro language through its strategic role as a lock-in mechanism for Microsoft Office. He explains how Microsoft deliberately built VBA and its complex object model as a barrier to entry that competitors couldn't perfectly emulate. The irony, he argues, is that Microsoft's decision to drop VBA from Mac Office 2008 undermines their own lock-in strategy, forcing loyal users to reconsider their platform choices. He draws parallels to the VB6-to-VB.Net transition and the Windows XP-to-Vista upgrade, arguing that Microsoft has abandoned the backwards-compatibility philosophy that made them dominant. Spolsky concludes that breaking your own lock-in features is self-defeating when your business depends on upgrade revenues.
key_points: VBA was designed not just as a user convenience but as a strategic lock-in mechanism, since competitors could never perfectly emulate the vast, undocumented API surface
Dropping VBA from Mac Office forces users to reevaluate their platform choice entirely, turning Microsoft's own lock-in into an exit catalyst
Microsoft has abandoned its backwards-compatibility religion across Office, Windows, and Basic simultaneously, threatening three core businesses that depend on upgrade revenue
API emulation is fundamentally brittle — subtle undocumented behaviors mean you don't get partial credit when trying to replicate a large programming interface
Each recent Microsoft release requires hacks and workarounds just to maintain prior functionality, creating upgrade fatigue among loyal users
money_quotes: In the brittle world of programming, such imperfections often mean your program crashes long before it does anything useful. You don't get partial credit when you try to emulate an API.

Microsoft has managed to hoist itself by its own petard. By locking in users and then not supporting their own lock-in features, they're effectively making it very hard for many Mac Office 2004 users to upgrade.
When Microsoft lost the backwards-compatibility religion that had served them so well in the past, they threatened three of their most important businesses.

Much as professional programmers sneer at the Basic programming language, market research unambiguously showed that about 2/3rds of the kinds of accidental programmers who develop macros preferred Basic to other languages and perceived it to be easy.
Despite complicated technical explanations, every development decision like this is based on a cost/benefit analysis.

It is extremely frustrating how much you have to run in place just to keep where you were before with Microsoft's products, where every recent release requires hacks, workarounds, and patches just to get to where you were before.
posts.key_insight: When a platform vendor abandons backwards compatibility with the very lock-in features that kept users captive, they don't free users — they push them toward competitors.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces an upcoming speaking engagement at Web.Start in Zagreb, Croatia. He also issues a correction and apology for previously referring to the United Kingdom as 'England' in a data analysis post. He explains the mix-up came from their order form dropdown listing, and offers to buy Edinburgh readers a pint when he visits on May 19th.
key_points: Promoting his upcoming talk at Web.Start conference in Zagreb, Croatia on May 17th
Correcting a previous post where he mistakenly referred to the UK as 'England'
Using humor and self-deprecation to acknowledge the geographic gaffe, with playful links to the Falkland Islands and Disney World's UK pavilion
money_quotes: Inadvertently, I referred to the UK as 'England.'
By way of apologies, if you're in Edinburgh when I pass through there on May 19th, let me buy you a pint.
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted travel announcement and correction post, notable mainly for Spolsky's characteristic self-deprecating humor in handling a geographic faux pas about the UK.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky continues a discussion about elevator usability, sharing two anecdotes that illustrate how deeply ingrained habits affect user interaction with systems. He describes how users of modern floor-selecting elevators sometimes stand passively in their home building's traditional elevators, expecting the system to already know their destination. He also shares a classic story about solving perceived elevator slowness by installing mirrors rather than speeding up the elevators.
key_points: Users carry mental models from one system into another, leading to errors when interfaces differ between similar products
Elevators are an ideal usability case study because everyone uses them yet their design is full of subtle pitfalls
Sometimes the best engineering solution addresses the user's perception of a problem rather than the technical problem itself
money_quotes: Elevators, actually, are a great usability subject, because so many people are familiar with them, and they seem like they should be so simple, but pitfalls are everywhere.

People who work in buildings with the new elevators but live in buildings with traditional elevators report that when they get home at night, they sometimes get into the elevator and then just stand there, expecting the elevator to know their floor already.
They had the hotel install mirrors in the hallway where the elevators were. By the time the visitors were done preening, the elevator had arrived. Human engineering at its finest.
posts.key_insight: The best usability solutions often address human perception and habit rather than optimizing the underlying system itself.

posts.summary: This is a brief logistical announcement about a drinks reception in Edinburgh on May 19, 2007. Joel provides the venue details (Radisson SAS hotel, St. Giles room) and encourages people to sign up. He notes the room can easily accommodate everyone. The post is purely informational with no substantive argument or analysis.
key_points: Announcement of a community meetup/drinks reception in Edinburgh on May 19, 2007
Venue is the Radisson SAS hotel in the St. Giles room
Joel encourages sign-ups and asks people to remove their names if they can't attend
posts.key_insight: A short logistical announcement about a community meetup with no broader insight or argument.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky makes a brief promotional post tying Mother's Day to a common tech experience: fixing your mom's computer. He suggests removing spyware, installing Firefox, and cleaning up toolbars, then announces that Fog Creek Copilot (their remote desktop product) will be free for the day to make remote tech support easy.
key_points: Frames a product promotion around a universally relatable experience for tech-savvy people: being the family IT support
Identifies the common PC problems of the era: spyware, adware, unwanted toolbars, and browser issues
Promotes Fog Creek Copilot by making it free for Mother's Day, lowering the barrier to trying the product
money_quotes: This Sunday is Mother's Day. Why not fix your mom's computer?
You know: remove the spyware and adware, install Firefox, and make it so that weird toolbar toast doesn't pop up every 15 seconds.
posts.key_insight: A clever product promotion that leverages the universal experience of being your family's unpaid IT department to drive trial usage of a remote desktop tool.

posts.summary: Joel announces a small new feature for the Joel on Software discussion groups: registered user accounts with visual indicators and article favoriting. The feature is built on an early version of FogBugz 6, which they are dogfooding internally before its beta release. The post uses a playful FAQ format to address basic questions about the new accounts. It ends with a teasing reference to a mysterious 'kiwi' mascot.
key_points: Registered accounts are optional and free, with a green checkbox displayed next to logged-in usernames
The discussion group software is an early internal build of FogBugz 6, being used as dogfooding before public beta
Joel links back to his own essay on the importance of eating your own dogfood as a development practice
money_quotes: The software running the discussion groups is our in-house, under-development, unannounced version of FogBugz 6, which will be going to beta later this summer, and we're rolling it out now as a part of our continuing efforts to eat our own dogfood.
posts.key_insight: Fog Creek practiced dogfooding by running their community discussion forums on a pre-release version of FogBugz 6 to test it in production before public beta.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky highlights a Best Buy employee's confession about being taught to scam customers into unwanted subscriptions, using it as evidence of a systemic problem he calls 'Econ 101 management.' He argues that major consumer-facing companies rely on crude metrics rather than thoughtful training, which incentivizes frontline workers to deceive customers. The post connects this specific scandal to a broader pattern across American consumer electronics, ISPs, telecoms, and cable companies.
key_points: Best Buy employees were actively taught by managers to trick customers into signing up for subscriptions like MSN
Companies substitute simplistic metrics for genuine investment in training frontline workers
When low-wage, high-turnover workers are given targets without proper guidance, they inevitably resort to scamming customers
This pattern is systemic across American consumer electronics, ISPs, telecoms, credit card companies, and cable companies
money_quotes: Instead of having smart people figure out how to train their frontline customer service workers to serve customers well and profitably, they make up metrics that sound good and let the low wage, high-turnover customer service people come up with their own systems, which, inevitably, involve scamming customers and ripping them off.
posts.key_insight: When companies substitute crude metrics for thoughtful training of frontline workers, the inevitable result is that employees game the system by scamming customers.

posts.summary: This is a brief promotional post tied to Mother's Day 2007. Joel uses the holiday as a hook to remind readers that Copilot (Fog Creek's remote desktop assistance tool) is free for the day. He suggests readers use it to help their mothers switch to Firefox and install security patches remotely. The post is essentially a lighthearted product plug wrapped in a holiday greeting.
key_points: Fog Creek's Copilot remote assistance tool was offered free on Mother's Day as a promotional event
Joel frames tech support for family members as a relatable, universal experience
The post implicitly advocates for Firefox over Internet Explorer, reflecting mid-2000s browser preferences
money_quotes: Copilot is free today, so here's your chance to switch your mom over to Firefox and install all the latest patches.
posts.key_insight: Joel leveraged holidays as natural promotional moments for Fog Creek products by tying them to relatable experiences like providing remote tech support to family.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares a satirical excerpt from a Meetup recruiting page that parodies Google's famously insular corporate culture. The brief post lets the absurd quote speak for itself, mocking how Big Tech companies create self-contained bubbles where even mundane services like haircuts are branded and elitist. The implication is that this kind of environment is more cult-like than appealing.
key_points: Big tech companies can create insular cultures where employees rarely need to interact with the outside world
The obsession with credentialism extends to absurd extremes when even haircutters are evaluated by IQ percentiles
Corporate perks can cross the line from attractive benefits into parody-worthy excess
money_quotes: At Google, you get your free Google-approved haircut at the Googleplex by a Googler with an IQ in the 99% percentile of all haircutters.
The average Googler Haircutter has over 45 RSS feeds in their Google Reader.
The walls are very clean, and you will not need to interact with non-Googlers.
posts.key_insight: When a company's culture becomes so insular that even getting a haircut is branded and credentialed, it has crossed from workplace perk into self-parody.

posts.summary: This is a brief product announcement for Fog Creek Copilot, a remote support tool. Joel announces that subscribers can now cobrand the Copilot interface with their own logo. The feature is aimed at tech support providers who want a seamless, branded experience for their clients. The post is essentially a marketing update with no substantive argument or analysis.
key_points: Fog Creek Copilot added a cobranding feature for subscribers
The feature allows companies to display their own logo in the support tool
The target audience is tech support providers serving external clients
money_quotes: Subscribers to Fog Creek Copilot can now cobrand Copilot with their own logo to provide seamless tech support to their clients.
posts.key_insight: White-labeling and cobranding features help B2B tools feel seamless for end clients.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the publication of his book 'Smart and Gets Things Done,' a concise guide to finding and hiring the best technical talent. The book compiles and reorganizes material from his Joel on Software blog articles into a short, readable format of about 180 pages. He highlights the illustrations by Terry Colon as a standout feature and walks through the chapter structure covering topics from finding developers to interviewing to fixing teams. The post is essentially a promotional announcement with Joel's characteristic self-deprecating humor about the book's brevity.
key_points: The book distills Joel's hiring and talent management blog posts into a concise, reorganized format
It covers the full hiring pipeline: finding developers, sorting resumes, phone screens, interviewing, and fixing teams
The content originated as Joel on Software articles but was rewritten for book-quality coherence
Joel emphasizes that he writes first and foremost for the web, with books as a secondary format
money_quotes: I write first and foremost for the web, so most of this material originated as Joel on Software articles.
Since it's such a short book, you should be able to do that during the commercials of a typical half-hour sitcom.
posts.key_insight: Joel packages his influential web writing on technical hiring into a deliberately concise book covering the full pipeline from finding developers to fixing underperforming teams.

posts.summary: Spolsky uses a personal anecdote about tracking down a neighbor's malfunctioning clock radio to illustrate a broader point about software quality. The clock radio's poor UI defaults—resetting its alarm to midnight after a power outage—caused a week of lost sleep, a problem that a single better design decision would have prevented. He connects this to the mindset required in the final debugging phase of software development, where developers become hypercritical and hunt for every small flaw. Quoting Dave Winer, he argues that great commercial software is built by fighting for every inch—thousands of tiny improvements that individually seem insignificant but collectively create a product that feels magical. The post concludes by celebrating the FogBugz 6.0 team's beta release as an example of this inch-by-inch craftsmanship.
key_points: Poor default settings in everyday products (like a clock radio alarming at midnight) cause real user pain and represent exactly the kind of small design failure that compounds
Building great software requires a mindset of constant, obsessive criticism—finding fault with everything until every small flaw is addressed
Commercial software quality is the accumulation of thousands of tiny improvements, not a few big features or lucky breaks
Good UI design means anticipating how real users (like an elderly neighbor) will interact with a product and choosing sensible defaults
The difference between decent software and great software is whether even the obscure, rarely-visited corners of the product are polished
money_quotes: Commercial software—the kind you sell to other people—is a game of inches.
To create a usable piece of software, you have to fight for every fix, every feature, every little accommodation that will get one more person up the curve. There are no shortcuts.

Every day you make a tiny bit of progress. You make one thing just a smidgen better. You make the alarm clock default to 7:00am instead of 12:00 midnight. A tiny improvement that will barely benefit anyone. One inch.
It takes a mindset of constant criticism to find them. You have to reshape your mind until you're finding fault with everything.
The inches add up to feet, the feet add up to yards, and the yards add up to miles. And you ship a truly great product.
Even the janitor's closets of your software have marble floors and solid core oak doors and polished mahogany wainscoting.
The UI was terrible. Your average little old lady didn't stand a chance.
And there's no reason to reset the alarm time to midnight after a power outage. 7:00 am would be a completely civilized default.
posts.key_insight: Great software is not built through grand strokes but through the relentless accumulation of thousands of tiny improvements, each one fighting for another inch of quality.

posts.summary: Joel responds to a reader asking whether companies should pay for interview travel expenses. Rather than directly answering, he advises the candidate to batch multiple interviews into a single trip to the UK, minimizing personal cost while maximizing opportunities. He then explains how mentioning competing employers triggers psychological principles of social proof and scarcity, making the candidate appear more desirable and potentially motivating companies to cover travel costs.
key_points: Batch multiple interviews into one trip to spread costs and increase your odds of landing a job
Mentioning other potential employers triggers social proof, making you seem more desirable as a candidate
The principle of scarcity means employers will worry about losing you to competitors, driving up your market value
The strategic framing of your situation matters more than directly asking for expense reimbursement
money_quotes: I'd be happy to interview in person. I'm planning a trip to the UK for all the interviews in two months. See you then.
Worst case scenario, you don't spend very much money on the trip, you almost certainly get a job, and you probably even have your choice of employers.
There's a good chance the mere mention of other potential employers will make these guys realize you're actually in heavy demand.
When they think that other employers are competing for your services, you'll seem like a more desirable candidate. This is the principle of social proof.
Opportunities seem more valuable to us when their availability is limited.
The combination will drive up your market value, and probably even motivate the hiring company to pay for the flight.
posts.key_insight: Rather than asking employers to pay for interview travel, candidates gain more leverage by batching interviews and letting the implied competition between employers do the negotiating for them.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky criticizes Apple's release of Safari for Windows, noting its extremely slow launch time of 57 seconds compared to 2-3 seconds for competitors. He broadens this into a critique of Apple's pattern of dishonest performance marketing, citing their PowerPC-to-Intel switch and iPhone network speed ads. However, he repeatedly updates the post as Safari's launch time improves with subsequent runs, turning the piece into a humorous self-correction. He ultimately concedes he was wrong about the launch speed while maintaining his broader critique of Apple's marketing dishonesty.
key_points: Safari for Windows took 57 seconds to launch on first run, compared to 2-3 seconds for Firefox and IE, though this improved dramatically on subsequent launches
Apple has a pattern of dishonest performance marketing, from PowerPC vs Intel claims to iPhone network speed ads that edit out download times
Page rendering speed benchmarks are largely irrelevant when network throughput is the actual bottleneck for web browsing
The post becomes an unintentional case study in the dangers of blogging complaints before fully investigating an issue
Safari's one practical benefit for Windows developers: reduced need to buy Macs for compatibility testing
money_quotes: Apple is and always has been severely dishonest in all their advertising when it comes to performance.
This is the company that spent years telling us that the PowerPC was faster than Intel, only, suddenly, to change their claims midsentence without an explanation when reality caught up with them.
These products may be cool, but I don't understand why Apple should be allowed to lie so blatently in their advertising about performance and get away with it.
Another case where the big print giveth and the fine print taketh away, it adds up to one thing: a lie.
When Apple's marketing team goes around making untrue claims about performance, they make the company look like liars, not innovators.
Safari even managed to bring the inferior font rendering of the OS X platform to Windows, no easy trick.
It's this kind of epistemological, reality-shifting shit that makes me not want to blog any more.
We are at war with Eastasia. We were always at war with Eastasia. 2+2=5, and I love Steve Jobs.
posts.key_insight: Apple's marketing consistently makes performance claims that don't hold up to real-world testing, prioritizing narrative over honesty in ways that undermine their credibility as innovators.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky examines the fundamental philosophical difference between Apple and Microsoft's approaches to font rendering on screens.
