Apple prioritizes preserving the typeface designer's intent even if text appears slightly blurry, while Microsoft prioritizes on-screen readability by snapping letters to pixel boundaries even if it distorts the typeface. This difference stems from Apple's desktop publishing heritage versus Microsoft's pragmatic focus on screen reading. Joel argues that user preferences in this debate are largely driven by familiarity rather than objective quality, with each platform's users predictably preferring what they're accustomed to.
key_points: Apple's font rendering preserves typeface design fidelity at the cost of on-screen blurriness, rooted in its desktop publishing legacy
Microsoft's ClearType hammers fonts into pixel boundaries for sharper on-screen reading, even distorting the original typeface design
Microsoft pragmatically designed entire font families like Georgia and Verdana around pixel boundaries, which look great on screen but lack character in print
User preference surveys on font rendering mostly reflect familiarity bias rather than informed aesthetic judgment, with each platform's users preferring their own system
Windows users experiencing Apple's rendering through Safari for Windows perceive it as blurry, but this reaction is driven by unfamiliarity rather than objective inferiority
money_quotes: Apple generally believes that the goal of the algorithm should be to preserve the design of the typeface as much as possible, even at the cost of a little bit of blurriness.
Microsoft generally believes that the shape of each letter should be hammered into pixel boundaries to prevent blur and improve readability, even at the cost of not being true to the typeface.

Microsoft pragmatically decided that the design of the typeface is not so holy, and that sharp on-screen text that's comfortable to read is more important than the typeface designer's idea of how light or dark an entire block of text should feel.

Typically, Apple chose the stylish route, putting art above practicality, because Steve Jobs has taste, while Microsoft chose the comfortable route, the measurably pragmatic way of doing things that completely lacks in panache.
To put it another way, if Apple was Target, Microsoft would be Wal-Mart.
In most matters of taste, when you do preference surveys, you'll find that most people don't really know what to choose, and will opt for the one that seems most familiar.
Whoa! That's different. I don't like different. Why don't I like these fonts? Oh, when I look closer, they look blurry. That must be why.
posts.key_insight: The Apple vs. Microsoft font rendering debate reveals that most user preferences about design are driven by familiarity rather than objective quality, and both approaches represent legitimate but fundamentally different philosophies about whether screens should serve print fidelity or on-screen comfort.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the launch of fogcreek.de, Fog Creek Software's first international distributor based in Munich. The site offers full-service sales and support for FogBugz in German. He describes this as a major milestone for the company and encourages German-speaking users in Germany, Austria, and Switzerland to reach out to their local contact Branimir.
key_points: Fog Creek launched its first international distributor in Munich, Germany
FogBugz now supports both English and German language options based on user preference
The distributor covers the DACH region: Germany, Austria, and Switzerland
money_quotes: This is our first real international distributor, and a major milestone for Fog Creek Software.
posts.key_insight: Fog Creek Software reached a growth milestone by establishing its first international distribution presence in the German-speaking market.

posts.summary: Joel announces the upcoming release of FogBugz 6.0 and his plan to do a world tour of live demos. He invites readers to fill out a survey indicating their location so he can decide which cities to visit. The demos will be free, lasting 30-45 minutes with open Q&A about FogBugz and software development in general. He self-deprecatingly notes his inability to learn that travel is miserable.
key_points: FogBugz 6.0 is in beta and will ship within a couple months
Joel plans a world tour of free live demos with open Q&A sessions
He's crowdsourcing city selection via a survey to gauge demand
The Q&A scope extends beyond FogBugz to general software development topics
money_quotes: Because I was born without the part of the brain that allows normal people to learn, over time, that Travel is Hell, I thought it would be fun to travel to a few cities around the world and demo it myself, in front of a live audience.
It's cool. It's very cool. It has a kiwi.
posts.key_insight: Joel uses his personal brand and direct audience engagement to market FogBugz, crowdsourcing tour logistics from his readership.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky posts a brief job listing seeking a freelance web designer in New York City to redesign Fog Creek Software's websites. He specifies requirements including strong XHTML/CSS skills, a portfolio of modern web designs, and some print experience. The post is essentially a classified ad rather than an essay.
key_points: Fog Creek was redesigning its web properties including fogcreek.com and copilot.com
Joel preferred hiring local freelancers in New York rather than remote or full-time designers
The required skill set combined web standards (XHTML/CSS) with print design experience
money_quotes: We need someone with strong xhtml/css skills, a history of making great looking, modern web sites, and some print experience.
posts.key_insight: A brief job posting seeking a New York-based freelance web designer to redesign Fog Creek's web properties, notable mainly as a snapshot of mid-2000s web design hiring expectations.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky reviews three management books, enthusiastically recommending two and panning the third. He praises Robert Townsend's 1970 classic 'Up the Organization' for its still-relevant advice on corporate management, consultants, and marketing. He equally recommends Michael Lopp's 'Managing Humans' for its insightful take on software engineering management and team dynamics. However, he gently but firmly criticizes 19-year-old Ben Casnocha's 'My Start-Up Life' for being thin on substance and padded with platitudes, while acknowledging the author's intelligence and potential.
key_points: Robert Townsend's 1970 management advice remains surprisingly relevant, including his warnings about conglomerates (now rebranded as big tech acquirers) and management consultants
Marketing should be driven by company leadership through intensive retreats, not delegated to staff departments of 'experts'
Michael Lopp's framework of 'Incrementalists vs. Completionists' captures a fundamental tension in how technical teams approach problem-solving
Youth and intelligence don't substitute for experience when writing about business management — platitudes that feel profound at 19 become embarrassing with hindsight
Self-awareness about one's own writing is a lifelong struggle, even for established writers in their 40s
money_quotes: Conglomerate is just an old word for what you call Yahoo, Microsoft, and Google.
They are people who borrow your watch to tell you what time it is and then walk off with it.
He's the quintessential Silicon Valley middle manager. I hope he doesn't find that term insulting: he's probably the best Silicon Valley middle manager there is.
He's the Doogie Howser, MD of software startups, except for the fact that he probably has no idea who Doogie Howser is, given that the show went off the air when he was 4 years old.
His book, unfortunately, tells you almost nothing about starting a company. It's really, really thin on stories of what the actual company did and how things worked.
You're going to reach the ripe old age of 23, and you're going to look back on this book you wrote, and you're going to say, how on earth did anyone let me publish such self-important crap.
I'm in my 40s, and I'm still morbidly embarrassed by the pompous, arrogant, self-important crap I write on this site here, up to and including this very sentence.
posts.key_insight: The best management wisdom is timeless and experience-tested, which is why a 1970 corporate management book can still outshine a young entrepreneur's freshly published startup memoir.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces a new wiki dedicated to collecting information about the business of software. The post is extremely brief, essentially just linking to the wiki and quoting its mission statement. In a characteristically playful move, Joel demonstrates the wiki's editability by correcting the awkward phrasing of the wiki's own description in real time. The post serves as both an announcement and a live demonstration of how wikis work.
key_points: A new wiki has been created to consolidate information about the business of software across all segments, from microISVs to enterprise
Joel demonstrates the wiki concept by editing the wiki's own description to improve its phrasing
The post doubles as an invitation for readers to contribute by editing the wiki themselves
money_quotes: The point of this wiki is to bring under one roof as much useful, quality information as possible about the business of software, whether it's microISVs selling desktop software, Web 2.0 sites or even the big enterprise kind of outfits.
Hmm, gee, that phrasing is a little bit awkward.
That's better!
You can edit it too.
posts.key_insight: The best way to demonstrate a collaborative tool is to use it live, turning an announcement into a participatory invitation.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares a brief, lighthearted note that his book 'Joel on Software' makes a cameo appearance in the horror movie 1408, starring John Cusack and Samuel L. Jackson. The post is essentially a fun factoid with no technical content.
key_points: Joel's book 'Joel on Software' appears as a prop in the 2007 horror film 1408
The post is self-aware about being trivial, filing itself under 'interesting but ultimately useless facts'
money_quotes: There's a scene in the movie 1408 that features a stack of Joel on Software (the book) along with John Cusack and Samuel L. Jackson.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes a blog post is just a fun personal aside — Joel's book made it into a Hollywood movie.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces FogBugz On Demand, a hosted version of their web-based project management tool that was previously only available as a download. He explains that Fog Creek was previously too small to reliably host customer data, but spent the past year building infrastructure including hiring a sysadmin, setting up high-end servers, and establishing two live data centers. The post details their infrastructure decisions, including choosing Windows/SQL Server over LAMP for familiarity despite higher cost, and using Peer 1 Network for both New York and Los Angeles data centers. He describes their warm backup strategy using SQL transaction log shipping between cities, acknowledging it's not instantaneous failover but provides solid disaster recovery.
key_points: Fog Creek transitioned from selling downloadable web software to offering a hosted SaaS model, requiring significant infrastructure investment for a small company.

They chose expensive but familiar Windows/SQL Server stack over cheaper LAMP, reasoning that per-customer cost is inconsequential when spread across many users, though they'd switch to LAMP at larger scale.
Both data centers run as fully live production environments rather than active/passive, ensuring the backup facility is always verified working.
They abandoned a Chicago data center provider after discovering a six-hour outage and inadequate reliability standards, consolidating on Peer 1's backbone.
The warm backup system uses SQL transaction log shipping between cities, with plans to reduce the recovery point from one day to 15 minutes.
money_quotes: Selling web software has always been a slightly strange aspect of the way Fog Creek operates. Since FogBugz is a web-based project management tool, why should customers have to download it and install it on their own servers?
I can't tell you how scary it is to be responsible for our customers' mission-critical data, so I didn't want to have any single point of failure, no matter how fortified it is.

Rather than setting up Los Angeles as a mere backup, we decided it would be completely live. Half our customers will be hosted from Los Angeles, and half from New York. That way we know at any time that both data centers are working and set up correctly, and we don't have to wait until a massive failure to discover the problems with the backup data center.
Their concept of building reliable systems did not use the same definition of 'reliable' as we do.
The first FogBugz 1.0 trial server, believe it or not, was a Thinkpad laptop with a broken screen plugged into our office T1!
We've been running IIS and Microsoft SQL Server here for six years without data loss, so that's what we know and trust.
posts.key_insight: When transitioning to hosted SaaS, prioritize proven reliability and active-active infrastructure over cost optimization, because earning customer trust with their mission-critical data demands higher standards than internal systems.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky highlights Maxim Shemanarev's work on font rasterization, sharing a quote that emphasizes how great engineering results come from applying publicly available knowledge with engineering intuition and common sense rather than breakthrough innovations. The brief post serves as a pointer to Shemanarev's detailed technical article, with Joel's single-word reaction ('Wow!') conveying deep admiration for the craftsmanship on display.
key_points: Great engineering often requires no novel breakthroughs — just careful application of existing knowledge
Engineering intuition and common sense can produce remarkable results without patents or rocket science
Craftsmanship in software is about patience and attention to detail, not flashy innovation
money_quotes: The amazing thing is there is no rocket science! Nothing to patent!
You only need to use a bit of your engineering intuition plus common sense.
posts.key_insight: Outstanding software craftsmanship comes not from proprietary breakthroughs but from patiently applying engineering intuition and common sense to publicly available knowledge.

posts.summary: Joel responds to a reader's confusion about Fog Creek's server infrastructure, clarifying that they run a mixed environment with both Linux and Windows servers. Linux handles network services like DNS and routing, while Windows servers run the web front ends (IIS) and database back ends (MS SQL 2005) for FogBugz On Demand. He emphasizes their strategy of standardizing on common hardware to enable easy server swaps and spare parts management.
key_points: Fog Creek runs a mixed OS environment: Linux for network infrastructure, Windows for application hosting
Hardware standardization across servers enables quick failover and parts interchangeability
Practical infrastructure decisions trump ideological purity about OS choices
Even with standardization goals, real-world vendor changes (like Dell's SCSI-to-SAS transition) create unavoidable inconsistencies
money_quotes: The Linux servers tend to be used for routers and assorted network services like DNS. The Windows servers are either working as web front ends (IIS) or database back ends (MS SQL 2005).
We try very hard to stick to a single hardware architecture so any server can be swapped with any other in case of problems, and parts can be moved hither and yon as needed.
In fact, one backup server even has an extra NIC in it so it can replace the router if it comes to that.
posts.key_insight: Standardizing server hardware for interchangeability and easy failover matters more than committing to a single OS platform.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces Fog Creek Software's annual summer open house event in New York City. The post invites software developers to visit their offices on July 19, 2007 to meet the team, interns, and management trainees. He uses the invitation as an opportunity to subtly showcase Fog Creek's developer-friendly work environment, mentioning private offices, large monitors, and premium chairs.
key_points: Fog Creek held annual summer open house events as a tradition to engage with the local developer community
The company maintained a developer-friendly office environment with private offices, 30-inch LCDs, and Aeron chairs — unusual enough to be worth showing off
Joel encourages attendees to bring their bosses, implicitly arguing that investing in developer workspace is both feasible and worthwhile
money_quotes: Bring your boss to prove how private offices, 30″ LCDs, and Aeron chairs are possible.
posts.key_insight: Investing in premium developer workspaces is not only possible but worth showing off as a recruiting and retention advantage.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky briefly explains why Fog Creek Software chose to reimplement SQL Server Mirroring rather than use Microsoft's built-in solution. He links to their systems administrator's detailed technical explanation of why the off-the-shelf technology didn't fit their needs. Joel reinforces this with his long-held philosophy that great teams often need to build their own solutions rather than relying on third-party tools. The post is essentially a short endorsement of the 'Not Invented Here' syndrome when practiced by strong engineering teams.
key_points: SQL Server Mirroring sounded ideal on paper but failed to meet Fog Creek's actual operational needs
Great engineering teams sometimes need to build custom solutions rather than adopt existing tools
Joel connects this specific technical decision back to his broader 2001 philosophy about self-reliance in software development
money_quotes: When you're working on a really, really good team with great programmers, everybody else's code, frankly, is bug-infested garbage, and nobody else knows how to ship on time.

When you're a cordon bleu chef and you need fresh lavender, you grow it yourself instead of buying it in the farmers' market, because sometimes they don't have fresh lavender or they have old lavender which they pass off as fresh.
Even though the SQL Server Mirroring technology sounds like an ideal fit at first, it is easy to see how it doesn't really suit our needs.
posts.key_insight: Strong engineering teams are justified in rebuilding existing solutions when off-the-shelf tools don't truly fit their needs, even if it looks like reinventing the wheel from the outside.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares logistics updates for the upcoming FogBugz World Tour, a multi-city product demo event spanning the US, Canada, New Zealand, Australia, and Europe. He describes the planning challenges including booking hotel meeting rooms across roughly 20 cities and notes the surprising lack of internet-based booking for hotel event spaces. The post is primarily a gear-shopping request, asking readers for recommendations on projectors, PA systems, and subnotebooks for six weeks of road travel.
key_points: The FogBugz World Tour will cover approximately 20 cities across multiple continents, requiring about six weeks of travel
Hotel event coordination is still heavily fax-based, which Joel identifies as a business opportunity ripe for internet disruption
Joel is crowdsourcing gear recommendations from his readers, treating his blog audience as a trusted advisory network
The practical constraints of a road show shape technology choices: weight, brightness, portability, and durability all matter
money_quotes: There's a huge business opportunity there–getting hotel event coordinators onto the Internet.
I'm looking at about six weeks on the road.
Today she faxed around about 80 RFPs to hotels to get meeting rooms lined up.
Is there such a thing as a video projector which connects to the PC using Cat-5 instead of video cable, so I don't have to carry a 50 foot video cable around?
Does anyone have any experience (good or bad) with the new flash-drive based notebooks?
posts.key_insight: Even in 2007, significant parts of the business world like hotel event booking remained stubbornly offline, representing overlooked opportunities for internet disruption.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky answers two reader questions about software startups. On launch timing, he advises against generating too much early publicity for an underwhelming product (the 'Marimba Phenomenon'), but notes most small startups won't have this problem and should ship early. On equity for a developer co-founder, he recommends standard time-based vesting over 4-7 years rather than milestone-based vesting, and explains why reverse vesting is preferable for tax reasons.
key_points: Premature publicity for an unfinished product can permanently turn off potential customers — the 'Marimba Phenomenon'
Most small startups lack the attention problem, so shipping a 1.0 to real customers early and iterating is the right move
Equity for developer co-founders should vest over time (4-7 years), not be contingent on software milestones, since 'finishing' software is too hard to define contractually
Reverse vesting (shares granted upfront with a repurchase right that evaporates over time) is preferable to normal vesting for tax reasons, converting income into lower-taxed capital gains
money_quotes: You want to avoid the Marimba Phenomenon, where you get so much publicity in the early days that everybody checks out your underwhelming offering and decides that you're never going to have something worth looking at.
Most companies with a 1.0 product can certainly get real customers with their earliest usable versions and build from there.
The standard solution is to vest over time — anywhere from four to seven years — with unvested shares being forfeited if he leaves for any reason.
If the software doesn't get done, you fire him and he loses the unvested shares — it's not necessary to make the vesting contingent specifically on finishing the software.
"Finishing" software would be too hard to define in a contract.
Reverse vesting is preferable for tax reasons, because at the time you give him the shares, they're worth a lot less, so there's less income and more capital gains, which are taxed at a lower rate.
posts.key_insight: Equity for startup co-founders should vest over time rather than being tied to software milestones, because 'finishing' software is impossible to define contractually and time-based vesting already handles the failure case through termination.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky invites readers to Fog Creek Software's annual open house event in New York City. The brief announcement highlights the company's developer-friendly workplace perks including private offices, large monitors, and premium chairs. He encourages visitors to bring their bosses to demonstrate that such workplace investments are feasible.
key_points: Fog Creek held annual open houses to connect with the developer community in person
The company showcased its developer-friendly perks (private offices, 30-inch LCDs, Aeron chairs) as proof they're achievable
The invitation to 'bring your boss' reflects Joel's ongoing advocacy for better developer working conditions
money_quotes: Bring your boss to prove how private offices, 30″ LCDs, and Aeron chairs are possible.
posts.key_insight: Joel used Fog Creek's open house as both community engagement and a living argument that investing in developer workspace quality is practical and worthwhile.

posts.summary: Spolsky endorses Dave Winer's philosophy that blog comments are counterproductive to free expression rather than enabling it. He argues that allowing comments beneath posts creates a torrent of anonymous noise, hate speech, and off-topic arguments that degrades the quality of discourse. Using vivid examples from a real estate blog where comments devolved into death threats and geopolitical flame wars, he makes the case that anonymity removes accountability and incentivizes the worst behavior. His proposed solution is that people should express disagreement on their own blogs, where they must take ownership of their words and earn attention through the quality of their ideas.
key_points: Blog comments infringe on the writer's freedom of expression rather than enabling free discourse — readers should get their own platform instead
Anonymity is the root cause of toxic comments, as people say things they would never say if they had to take ownership of their words
The quality of online discussion would dramatically improve if anonymous posting were eliminated entirely
The marketplace of ideas works better when people must earn attention through compelling writing and PageRank rather than piggy-backing on someone else's audience
Even innocuous topics like real estate devolve into hate speech and flame wars when anonymous commenting is allowed
money_quotes: You don't have a right to post your thoughts at the bottom of someone else's thoughts. That's not freedom of expression, that's an infringement on their freedom of expression.

Get your own space, write compelling things, and if your ideas are smart, they'll be linked to, and Google will notice, and you'll move up in PageRank, and you'll have influence and your ideas will have power.

What you get is a bunch of interesting ideas, carefully constructed, followed by a long spew of noise, filth, and anonymous rubbish that nobody … nobody … would say out loud if they had to take ownership of their words.
It's not fun, freewheeling freedom of expression, yay first amendment! It's mostly anonymous hate speech.
He's not even human to me, he's anonymous. What web browser he uses doesn't amount to a hill of beans.
The way to give people freedom of expression is to give them a quiet place to post their ideas.

Thoughtless drivel written by some anonymous non-entity who really didn't read the article very carefully and didn't come close to understanding it and who has no ability whatsoever to control his typing diarrhea if the site's software doesn't physically prevent him from posting.
posts.key_insight: Blog comments don't enable free expression — they undermine it by letting anonymous voices drown out the carefully constructed ideas of the author, and true freedom of expression means giving people their own space to write, not a comment box on someone else's work.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky follows up on his earlier post about blog comments by highlighting two thoughtful responses from Clay Shirky and Dave Winer, posted on their own blogs rather than in his comment section. He uses their replies as evidence that distributed conversation across individual blogs produces higher-quality discourse than centralized comment sections. He points to aggregation tools like Techmeme and Bloglines as mechanisms that can reconstruct these distributed conversations automatically. The post serves as a self-reinforcing example of his argument that the best responses come from people with their own platforms and established reputations.
key_points: Responses posted on authors' own blogs carry more weight because their identity and reputation provide context and accountability
Clay Shirky's point that comment quality problems are primarily a scale issue — small sites can maintain good comments, large ones cannot
Aggregation tools like Techmeme can algorithmically reconstruct distributed conversations across multiple blogs, solving the discoverability problem
Dave Winer's approach of making comments hard to find naturally filters for higher-quality contributors who are genuinely invested in the discussion
money_quotes: There is a lot more value to them than the comments about this post on my own discussion forum.

Because I know who Clay is, I've met him, he wrote A Group Is Its Own Worst Enemy, which, to this date, is the most important, insightful, and brilliant understanding of group dynamics in online communities.
Great content, in their own spaces.
The sites that suffer most from anonymous postings and drivel are the ones operating at large scale.
I find this ups the quality enormously — people don't generally comment here to embarass anyone or to provoke a fight — there isn't enough traffic to interest those people.
posts.key_insight: The best online discourse happens when respondents post on their own platforms with their reputations attached, and aggregation tools can reconstruct the conversation without needing centralized comment sections.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky describes the logistical planning for the FogBugz 6.0 World Tour, a 32-city product demo tour spanning North America, Oceania, and Europe. He details the cities planned for both rounds and breaks down the surprisingly high cost of in-person demos versus webinars. Despite the $64 per-attendee cost covering flights, hotels, catering, and swag, he maintains that live demos are worth the investment.
key_points: The FogBugz 6.0 World Tour will cover 32+ cities across multiple continents with live product demos
In-person product demos cost approximately $64 per attendee when accounting for all logistics
Joel believes live demos justify their high cost compared to cheaper webinar alternatives
The tour is structured in two rounds: North America first, then Oceania and Europe
money_quotes: This is basically about the same logistical complexity as planning a family trip to DisneyLand, times 32.
it's costing us $64 to put on a live demo of FogBugz for each person who attends.
No wonder people do those crappy webinar things instead. I still think it's worth it, though.
posts.key_insight: In-person product demos are expensive and logistically complex, but the personal connection they create with potential customers makes them worth the investment over cheaper alternatives like webinars.

posts.summary: Joel announces a 21-city North American demo tour for FogBugz 6.0, explaining the business reasoning behind physically visiting cities rather than just posting a video online. He breaks down the surprisingly high costs of business hotel events while noting the per-person cost of $60-70 is actually cheap compared to traditional enterprise sales forces that cost $50,000 per sale. Since FogBugz is priced too low ($21/month) to support a conventional sales team, the demo tour serves as an alternative go-to-market strategy. The events will feature product demos, general software development discussion, Q&A, and overpriced hotel coffee.
key_points: Business hotels gouge event organizers with absurd charges for basic amenities like coffee ($15/person), projectors ($500), and other fees, making demo tours expensive
FogBugz's low price point ($21/month) makes traditional enterprise sales forces economically impossible since those cost around $50,000 per sale
At $60-70 per attendee, the demo tour is actually a cost-effective alternative to a commission-based sales team
The tour format mixes product demos with general software development discussion and generous Q&A time, making it valuable beyond just a sales pitch
money_quotes: Business hotels all charge ridiculous amounts for catering ($15 per person for coffee), audio visual equipment ($500 to rent a projector), and as many additional gougy-charges as they can think of. $50 for sneezing. $120 to have the window shut.
That kind of sales force costs $50,000 to make one sale. With prices in the $21/month range, that model just doesn't work for us.
When you think about it this way, $60 to reach one person is cheap!

We have inside sales people who handle incoming phone calls (very rare) and incoming email (we get a lot of it), but our software is just priced too cheaply for the traditional enterprise software sales system.
There will be some really, really expensive coffee and maybe a muffin or something if the hotel in particularly cheap.
posts.key_insight: When your product is priced too low for traditional enterprise sales, creative alternatives like demo tours can reach customers at a fraction of the cost of a conventional sales force.

posts.summary: Spolsky recounts his frustrating experience trying to open the new Office 2007 retail packaging, using it as a springboard to critique Microsoft's broader redesign philosophy. He argues the packaging violates basic industrial design principles by lacking proper affordances and requiring users to Google how to open it. He extends this critique to Office 2007's ribbon interface, which forces users to relearn tasks they already knew, and condemns Vista as having no redeeming qualities worth the compatibility problems it causes. His recommendation is to stick with Windows XP.
key_points: The Office 2007/Vista packaging is a failure of industrial design with false affordances that mislead users about how to open it
Office 2007's redesign forces users to waste time relearning tasks they already mastered, with no compelling reason to upgrade
Vista offers no benefits that justify the compatibility headaches it introduces, and users should stick with Windows XP
Even rudimentary usability testing should have caught that people can't figure out how to open the box
money_quotes: It represents a complete failure of industrial design; an utter F in the school of Donald Norman's Design of Everyday Things.
A box that many people can't figure out how to open without a Google search is an unusually pathetic failure of design.

Wasting five minutes trying to get the goddamned box open is just the first of many ways that Office 2007 and Vista's gratuitous redesign of things that worked perfectly well shows utter disregard for all the time you spent learning the previous versions.
It's extremely frustrating every time I have to spend several minutes finding something that I knew exactly how to find in the previous version.
Even though there's no reason to upgrade to Office 2007, if you're setting up a new system, it's just as good as the previous version, even a little better in some places.
I've been using Vista on my home laptop since it shipped, and can say with some conviction that nobody should be using it as their primary operating system.
Vista simply has no redeeming merits to overcome the compatibility headaches it causes.
posts.key_insight: Gratuitous redesigns that force users to relearn what they already know — from product packaging to software interfaces — represent a fundamental disrespect for users' invested learning.

posts.summary: In this brief post, Joel Spolsky shares a quote from his interview with ACM Queue comparing small and large companies. He uses a hotel analogy: big companies are like the Bellagio — nice but impersonal — while small companies are like bed and breakfasts, offering a more personal experience with both upsides and downsides. The analogy captures how small companies can be wonderfully intimate or disturbingly eccentric, with no middle ground.
key_points: Large companies offer polish and reliability but lack personal connection — nobody remembers your name
Small companies provide a more intimate, personalized experience where relationships matter
The downside of small companies is unpredictability — the quirks can be charming or deeply uncomfortable
Company culture at small scale is a high-variance bet compared to the standardized big-company experience
money_quotes: Basically a small company has a flavor to it, whereas a big company is sort of like checking into the Bellagio in Las Vegas.
It's a nice hotel but it has 5,000 rooms, so don't expect anybody to remember your name.
A small company is more like a bed and breakfast. You're going to have a great time because you get along with people and it's a much friendlier experience.
You don't really mind that the bathroom is down the hall because the people made a special vegetarian meal for you and then showed you around town.
On the other hand, you might be at a bed and breakfast where they have weird leather implements and lots of cats.
posts.key_insight: Small companies are high-variance experiences — they have a distinctive flavor that can be either wonderfully personal or uncomfortably eccentric, unlike the reliable but anonymous uniformity of large companies.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky recaps the first stop of the FogBugz World Tour in Vancouver, where he demoed FogBugz 6.0 to about 120 attendees. The demo hit a snag when Monte Carlo simulations overloaded the laptop's IIS 5.1 server, requiring a restart mid-presentation. Despite the technical hiccup, audience feedback was enthusiastic, with some attendees deciding to purchase FogBugz on the spot. The post also covers practical air travel tips for frequent business travelers, including advice on timing flights, using smaller airports, and leveraging airline loyalty programs to avoid delays.
key_points: FogBugz 6.0 was publicly demoed for the first time in Vancouver, with the official launch planned for the following Monday
A demo crash caused by IIS 5.1 limitations on a laptop running XP highlighted the gap between demo environments and production servers
Audience feedback ranged from immediate purchase decisions to one attendee admitting FogBugz was far more impressive than the 'glorified Excel spreadsheet' they had assumed
Joel shares seven detailed air travel tips for business travelers, emphasizing mid-morning flights, backup flight options, and leveraging premium airline services to avoid disruptions
money_quotes: it's amazing what I can come up with when I am not encumbered by facts
I expected to be bored about FogBugz and enraptured by fascinating tidbits of Joel Spolsky wisdom. In reality, the opposite was true.
My tidbits aren't that fascinating.
Make sure you're never on the last flight of the day if you really need to get somewhere on schedule. If something happens to the last flight, you're in trouble.
don't wait in line with the crowds for the single, overworked airline representative. Get on the phone to your airline's frequent flyer priority number.
posts.key_insight: Even polished product demos can go wrong when the demo environment doesn't match production, but genuine audience enthusiasm matters more than a flawless presentation.

posts.summary: Spolsky describes his Seattle demo event and introduces a framework for understanding software development as three distinct phases: design, development, and debugging. Each phase requires fundamentally different scheduling approaches—design is art that's hard to estimate for new projects, development is engineering that can be scheduled precisely with good specs, and debugging is science where prediction is nearly impossible. He argues that skipping the design/spec phase doesn't save time but actually slows you down, since designing on paper is roughly 10x faster than designing in code. The post also promotes FogBugz 6.0's new Evidence-based scheduling feature using Monte Carlo simulation.
key_points: Software development has three phases—design (art), development (engineering), and debugging (science)—regardless of methodology, and each requires fundamentally different scheduling approaches
Development is the only phase that can be scheduled with precision, but only when starting from a detailed specification or blueprint
Bug-finding rates are too noisy to extrapolate shipping dates from, since they reflect testing capacity and tester pool size more than code quality
Skipping specs and designing while coding is like driving with the handbrake on—designing on paper is 10x faster than designing in code, so iterating on specs lets your design improve 10x faster
For brand-new projects, the design phase genuinely cannot be estimated, and that's acceptable
money_quotes: Software development is a cycle with three distinct phases: design, development, and debugging.
Can you imagine Safeco Field filled to the brim with software developers? And that's just the Vista Shutdown Menu Team.
Design is the art phase, where you're doing new, creative work.
Debugging is the science phase. Science is difficult to schedule because you're looking for things, and predicting when you're going to find them is remarkably difficult.
Unless you know in advance how many bugs you're going to find, you don't have an ice cube's chance in the Sahara to work out a detailed estimate of how long this phase will take.
Designing a feature by writing a thoughtful spec takes about 1/10th as much time as writing the code for that feature—or less.
If you ever started a new project by writing code, and you thought you'd 'design as you went along,' what you're doing is driving around with the handbrakes on.
Your product's design can only get better at 1/10th the speed that my product's design can.
posts.key_insight: Each phase of software development—design, development, and debugging—is fundamentally different in nature and requires its own scheduling approach, and trying to skip the design phase by coding first makes your design iterate 10x slower.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky recounts a FogBugz demo event in Chicago where the choice of a cheap hotel — the Congress Plaza — backfired badly. The hotel was shabby, the staff was on strike forcing attendees to cross a picket line, and the depressing environment likely hurt the perception of the product being presented. He reflects on how the physical environment of a business presentation psychologically colors the audience's impression of the product itself. He also mentions speaking at the Inc. 500 Conference about Fog Creek's hiring practices and promotes his upcoming FogBugz 6.0 launch in New York.
key_points: Choosing a cheap hotel for a business demo created a negative atmosphere that likely hurt the product's perception among attendees
The physical environment of a presentation psychologically influences how the audience perceives the product being presented
Hotel quality indicators can be counterintuitive — shabby hotels sometimes have plasma TVs as a facade of modernity while genuinely nice hotels stick with older equipment
The Congress Plaza Hotel staff was on strike, forcing demo attendees to cross a picket line — a situation Joel hadn't thought to check for when booking
Joel spoke at the Inc. 500 Conference about Fog Creek's hiring process, linking to his well-known series on finding and interviewing developers
money_quotes: Psychologically, I think that people tend to associate the environment they're in with the presentation.
When a demo is in a modern, new, shiny business hotel, it's like a little one hour vacation in luxuryland.

But when you go to a demo at the Congress Plaza and the rug is stained and there are fluorescent lights everywhere and the bathroom looks like LaGuardia airport, some of that general depressing aura of shabbiness will rub off on the product being presented.
I guess shabby hotels just treat their employees shabbily.
The nice chains, inexplicably, have old fashioned big-ass tube TVs. The ancient rotting edifices have 32" plasmas.
The usual nice words you might use to describe such a hotel would be 'threadbare' or 'shabby.' Other words ('maccabre,' 'Barton Fink,' and 'scuzzy') come to mind.
posts.key_insight: The physical environment where you present your product psychologically shapes how the audience perceives that product, so cutting corners on venue quality is a false economy.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky briefly promotes the Business of Software 2007 conference, noting that it features exactly the kind of speakers he would invite if he organized his own conference. He mentions Eric Sink and Rick Chapman as examples of people whose insights on the software business he values.
key_points: Joel considered organizing his own software business conference but found an existing one that fills the niche
He highlights Eric Sink and Rick Chapman as exemplary speakers on the business of software
The post serves as an endorsement of the Business of Software 2007 conference
money_quotes: Occasionally I think about organizing my own conference, but I would just invite people like Eric Sink and Rick Chapman, anyway, so the Business of Software 2007 conference has eliminated the need to do that.
posts.key_insight: A brief endorsement of the Business of Software 2007 conference as gathering the right people to discuss the software industry.

posts.summary: Joel reflects on returning from a trip and realizing that a web design he had been approving incrementally now looked terrible. He uses Gerald Weinberg's 'sesame seed' parable to explain how incremental small changes can gradually degrade quality without anyone noticing at each step. The design had lost its elegance through rounds of tweaking, pixel-width compromises, and platform rendering differences. Joel decided to scrap the work and start over rather than try to salvage it, acknowledging that sometimes you need to go through the entire process to realize you've built the wrong thing.
key_points: Incremental approval processes can blind you to cumulative degradation — each small change seems fine, but the end result is far from the original vision
Stepping away and returning with fresh eyes is one of the most effective ways to evaluate design work honestly
Technical differences between platforms (Mac antialiasing vs. others, bitmap mockups vs. real HTML) can make an approved design look completely different in practice
It's better to start over than to salvage a fundamentally flawed result, even if significant work has been invested
money_quotes: I did the one thing that drives web design firms completely crazy: I suddenly took a look at the new web design they've done for us, which I've been approving every step of the way, and didn't like it any more, so I told them we had to start over.

Every year or two, the new management trainee looking for ways to save money proposes removing a sesame seed or two, until eventually, they're shipping hamburger buns with exactly three sesame seeds artfully arranged in a triangle, and nobody buys their hamburgers any more.
We've been removing sesame seeds from the initial design they did in hopes of making things better, and, lo and behold, at some point the design flipped from being good to being bad.
But a week later, the same basic design just looked terrible.
It's better to have something we're both proud off than to try and salvage the work done so far.
Sometimes you have to go all the way through the design process before you realize that you've built the wrong thing, but it's ok, it's a learning experience.
posts.key_insight: Incremental design refinements can silently accumulate into overall degradation, and sometimes stepping away is the only way to see that you need to start over.

posts.summary: Spolsky draws parallels between the PC software era of the 1980s and the state of web development in 2007, identifying three recurring dynamics: hardware constraints that developers waste time optimizing for, portability problems across platforms, and the lack of standardized UI conventions. He argues that just as DOS applications were made obsolete by Windows providing a standard SDK, Ajax web applications will eventually be supplanted by a new SDK that provides a portable compiled-to-JavaScript language, standardized UI components, and cross-application interoperability. Using Lotus 1-2-3's loss to Microsoft Excel as a cautionary tale, he warns that dominant web apps like Gmail could become legacy software if they fail to adopt whatever new platform SDK wins developer mindshare.
key_points: Developers who waste effort optimizing for current hardware constraints (bandwidth, JS compile time) will lose to those who focus on features, because constraints always disappear with time

Browser incompatibilities will be solved not by sandboxes (which are slow and limited) but by a new portable language that compiles to native JavaScript for each browser, analogous to how C solved portability across instruction sets
Ajax applications lack standardized UI conventions and interoperability (like cut-and-paste between apps), mirroring the pre-Windows era of inconsistent DOS applications
Whichever SDK provides both a portable language and standardized UI/interop features will gain the same competitive stronghold that Microsoft Windows had through its API
Dominant incumbents like Google Gmail are vulnerable to displacement by a new platform SDK, exactly as Lotus 1-2-3 was displaced by Windows-based Excel
money_quotes: Assembler programmers don't have groupies.
The developers who ignored performance and blasted ahead adding cool features to their applications will, in the long run, have better applications.
Sandboxes are penalty boxes; they're slow and they suck, which is why Java Applets are dead, dead, dead.
HTML is CICS with fonts.

And while you're not paying attention, everybody starts writing NewSDK apps, and they're really good, and suddenly businesses ONLY want NewSDK apps, and all those old-school Plain Ajax apps look pathetic.
The NewSDK will be the second coming of Microsoft Windows; this is exactly how Lotus lost control of the spreadsheet market.
Their nail polish has more than 2GB.
And you laugh at them, for their NewSDK is a honking 232 megabytes of JavaScript, and it takes 76 seconds to load a page.
posts.key_insight: History shows that platform shifts happen when someone builds a standard SDK that solves portability and UI consistency, and today's dominant web apps will be displaced just as DOS-era software was displaced by Windows — the only unknown is who builds it.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky explains a bug in Excel 2007 where multiplying 77.1 by 850 displays 100,000 instead of 65,535, drawing on his experience as a former Excel team member. He clarifies that the bug is purely in the display formatting code, not in the actual calculation engine — downstream calculations using the value produce correct results. The root cause involves IEEE 754 floating point representation, where 0.1 has no exact binary representation, causing the product to be extremely close to but not exactly 65,535, which triggers a rare formatting bug affecting only 12 out of all possible floating point numbers. Spolsky argues the bug's real-world impact is negligible but uses it as a springboard to discuss floating point arithmetic, testing limitations, and takes a parting shot at the current Excel team's competence.
key_points: The Excel bug is purely a display formatting issue — the underlying binary value is correct and produces accurate results in further calculations and charts
IEEE 754 floating point cannot exactly represent 0.1 in binary (it's a repeating binary fraction), so 77.1*850 produces a value extremely close to but not exactly 65,535
The bug only affects 12 out of 18,446,744,073,709,551,616 possible floating point numbers, making it nearly impossible to catch with black-box testing
Automated VBA-based testing wouldn't catch this because VBA receives the correct binary value before display formatting occurs
Arbitrary-precision arithmetic could solve floating point issues but has historically been too slow for spreadsheet use, though modern CPUs might handle it
money_quotes: 0.1 has no exact representation in binary… it's a repeating binary number. It's sort of like how 1/3 has no representation in decimal.
If you tried to do 3*1/3, and you didn't have time to write 3's forever, the result you would get would be 0.99999999, not 1, and people would get angry with you for being wrong.
The chance that you would see this in real life calculations is microscopic. Better worry about getting hit by a meteorite.
With a bug like this that only happens on 12 out of 18446744073709551616 possible floating point binary numbers, it's unlikely that any set of black-box tests would cover this case.
The Microsoft Excel support team has spent the last 20 years defending IEEE 754, and it's not surprising that they've started to believe in it.

Do you really want the bright sparks who work there now, and manage to break lots of perfectly good working code — rewriting the core calculating engine in Excel? Better keep them busy adding and removing dancing paper clips all day long.
posts.key_insight: The Excel 2007 display bug illustrates how IEEE 754 floating point's inherent imprecision can interact with formatting code to produce visually alarming but computationally harmless errors that are nearly impossible to catch through testing.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces his new monthly column 'How Hard Could It Be?' in Inc. Magazine, focused on the business of software startups. He teases the first installment with a humorous anecdote about the absurdity of trying to print signatures onto already-printed forms, illustrating the kind of mundane operational headaches entrepreneurs face. The post is primarily a brief announcement rather than a substantive essay, serving to direct readers to the full column in Inc. Magazine.
key_points: Joel launched a monthly Inc. Magazine column called 'How Hard Could It Be?' about the business side of software startups
The teaser anecdote illustrates how seemingly simple office tasks (like signing forms) can spiral into frustrating technical problems
Joel positions himself as both a practitioner and student of entrepreneurship, noting he's learned a lot from Inc. Magazine over the years
money_quotes: Once a sheet of paper has been through a laser printer, the next time you try to print on it, it's going to fight back, jamming the printer and resulting in the deaths of three other pages, and you're going to spend five minutes with a putty knife cleaning up the bloodshed.
I didn't relish the carpal tunnel syndrome that would result from signing all these forms.
posts.key_insight: Even the most mundane operational tasks in running a startup — like signing paperwork — can become unexpectedly complicated misadventures.

posts.summary: Spolsky deconstructs the brilliance of Apple's 'Designed by Apple in California' slogan, explaining how each word triggers deep cultural and emotional associations that make consumers feel good about their purchase. He contrasts this with Microsoft's Zune tagline 'Hello from Seattle,' which he argues demonstrates a complete failure to understand the cultural codes that make Apple's branding work. The post uses this comparison to illustrate how branding success comes from understanding deep emotional resonance, not from superficial imitation. He closes with a promotional plug for his FogBugz World Tour stops across California.
key_points: Apple's 'Designed by Apple in California' works because each word triggers layered cultural associations — the Beach Boys, the Beatles, opportunity, escape, and utopian idealism
The word 'designed' rather than 'made' is a deliberate choice that elevates the product while quietly acknowledging that manufacturing has moved to China
Microsoft's 'Hello from Seattle' on the Zune reveals a desperate desire to imitate Apple without understanding why Apple's branding resonates
Effective branding taps into deep cultural codes and emotional memory, not surface-level mimicry
money_quotes: You think of California, not the actual state, with its endless dismal boulevards full of muffler shops and donut stores, but the California of memory: the Beach Boys, the Summer of Love, and the beatniks, a utopian land of opportunity.
It's not made. It's designed. In California. Like a surfboard. Or a Lockheed XP-80.
Of course, it might distract your attention from the fact that we no longer make things like this in America. We design them, but they pretty much have to be made in China.
The iPod slogan Designed by Apple in California triggers a flood of emotional responses that just make you happy to have selected this MP3 player.
Hello from Seattle? That has, I'm afraid, none of the same resonance. It evokes nothing. Boeing and rain, maybe. Kurt Cobain's unhappiness.
But really it's just a desperate desire to be like Apple, without even a smidgen of understanding of the culture code that makes Designed by Apple in California work.
Anyway, we already did 'Hello.' 'Hello' was charming once. In 1984. Now it's just old.
posts.key_insight: Great branding works by tapping into deep cultural codes and emotional associations, and companies that imitate the surface without understanding the underlying resonance only expose their own tone-deafness.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares a brief travelogue from the FogBugz World Tour stop in San Francisco and the Bay Area. He describes a pleasant weekend driving around Marin County, a well-attended Monday demo with about 110 people, and a tour of Pixar's offices in Emeryville. He notes the contrast between Steve Jobs' messy private office and Pixar's impressive public spaces, and mentions that while many employees get private offices, space constraints mean some work in cubes.
key_points: The FogBugz World Tour demo in San Francisco drew approximately 110 attendees
Pixar's offices feature impressive public spaces but face space constraints, with a mix of private offices and cubicles
Steve Jobs' office was notably messy, contrasting with his public image of minimalist Zen aesthetics
Joel was demoing the Developer Ship Dates feature in FogBugz 6.0
money_quotes: Steve Jobs' office was, uh, kinda messy. Not Zen at all.
The public spaces are really incredible; a lot of people get private offices, but they're kind of short on room so there are a lot of cubes, too.
posts.key_insight: A lightweight travelogue from the FogBugz product tour, notable mainly for the amusing observation that Steve Jobs' private office didn't match his minimalist public persona.

posts.summary: Spolsky shares a quick travel hack for determining whether a flight will actually be on time. He noticed Continental Airlines' gate display showed an on-time departure, but there was no plane at the gate. By using FlightAware to track the inbound aircraft, he accurately predicted the flight would be 39 minutes late, which proved exactly correct.
key_points: Airline gate displays can be unreliable or overly optimistic about departure times
Tracking the inbound aircraft via FlightAware gives a more accurate picture of actual departure time
The technique proved precisely accurate, predicting a 39-minute delay that matched reality
money_quotes: Being suspicious, I notice that there's no plane at the gate.
Arriving 3:21. A bit later than the display at the gate is showing, and a total of 39 minutes late. And indeed, we took off almost exactly 39 minutes late.
posts.key_insight: You can outsmart misleading airline departure displays by tracking the inbound aircraft on FlightAware to get a realistic arrival and departure estimate.

posts.summary: Joel highlights the longstanding frustration of trying to stop unwanted paper catalog deliveries, noting that catalog companies historically made it nearly impossible to opt out. He recommends Catalog Choice, a nonprofit service that contacts catalog merchants on your behalf to stop deliveries. He praises both the concept and the implementation of the service, noting its clean Ajax UI and graphic design.
key_points: Opting out of paper catalogs was historically futile — companies either couldn't remove you or would re-add you within months
Catalog Choice (a .org nonprofit) solves this by acting as an intermediary to contact merchants on your behalf
Joel praises the service for combining a great idea with excellent technical execution
money_quotes: Most of them had no way of doing that, and even if you got off the list, you'd always find your way back on again two months later.
Great idea, excellent implementation (very nice Ajax UI and great graphic design), and I'll let you know if it helps.
posts.key_insight: The best solutions combine a genuinely useful idea with polished execution, as demonstrated by a nonprofit tackling the mundane but universal problem of unwanted catalogs.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky provides a brief travel update for his FogBugz world tour, listing upcoming cities across the US and Europe. He encourages waitlisted readers to sign up, noting they'll likely be accommodated. He mentions that the Austin event will be streamed online using NewTek's TriCaster portable broadcast studio. The post is essentially a logistical announcement with no substantive argument or insight.
key_points: The FogBugz world tour is visiting multiple US cities including Arlington, Atlanta, Dallas, Austin, and Boulder
Waitlisted attendees are encouraged to sign up as most are being accommodated
The Austin event will be broadcast online using NewTek's TriCaster portable broadcast technology
European dates in Dublin, London, Cambridge, Amsterdam, and Copenhagen are planned for November
money_quotes: We've been doing a pretty good job of getting everyone in, so don't worry if you're waitlisted… we'll probably fit you in.
posts.key_insight: A brief logistical update on Joel's FogBugz world tour schedule with no substantive technical or business content.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky provides a brief travel update from his World Tour promoting FogBugz. He reports on a 150-person turnout in Arlington, Virginia, and previews an upcoming live-streamed demo in Austin, Texas. He notes the absurd cost of hotel internet and mentions a minor travel mishap with Delta's automated check-in machines.
key_points: The FogBugz World Tour drew about 150 attendees in Arlington, Virginia
A live stream of the Austin demo was being arranged in partnership with NewTek, highlighting the logistical challenges of early streaming technology
Hotel internet pricing ($100 for a meeting room connection) was already a pain point for business travelers in 2007
money_quotes: To get a high-speed internet connection in the meeting room, we have to pay the hotel an extra $100. After all the other hotel bills, that actually sounds kind of reasonable, believe it or not.

After the demo we managed to break the automated check-in machines at the Delta airlines check-in counter, trying to move to an earlier flight. Eventually a nice human at the counter straightened it out.
posts.key_insight: A lightweight travel diary from Joel's product tour, offering a snapshot of the logistical realities of software promotion and business travel in 2007.

posts.summary: Joel recaps a speaking event at the Hyatt Regency in Atlanta where about 110 people attended and asked great questions. He highlights meeting long-time Fog Creek community member Terry Kearns in person. He also notes that downtown Atlanta is quiet after 5pm and recommends Yelp as increasingly useful for finding restaurants, having used it to discover a nearby restaurant called Room.
key_points: About 110 people attended the Atlanta event, with some traveling from neighboring states
Meeting long-time online community members in person strengthens the relationship between a company and its supporters
Yelp was emerging as a more useful restaurant discovery tool than Chowhound
money_quotes: Downtown Atlanta is one of those places where, to put it politely, the sidewalks roll up at 5pm.
I checked Yelp, which is fast surpassing Chowhound in usefulness.
posts.key_insight: A brief travelogue noting the value of in-person community meetups and the rise of Yelp as a local discovery platform.

posts.summary: Spolsky recounts a frustrating experience at Hertz DFW airport where a computer system failure brought the entire rental operation to a halt. With employees forced to fill out agreements by hand and managers sitting idle, the cascading failure left him stranded in suburban Dallas without transportation. He speculates that the entire outage might have been caused by something as simple as a DOS prompt waiting for a date entry. The post illustrates how over-reliance on automated systems without adequate fallback procedures creates spectacular failures.
key_points: Hertz's automated systems failed completely, and the company lacked enough trained staff to handle manual operations as fallback
The cascading failure spread beyond Hertz as displaced customers booked out every other rental agency at the airport
Technology-driven staffing reductions create brittleness: the fancy computers justified fewer employees, but left no capacity for when those computers failed
Simple technical problems (like a DOS box waiting for a date input) can bring down entire operations when no one present understands the underlying systems
money_quotes: Thanks to the spiffy new computers, they don't have nearly enough people working there to handle the loads when the computers break down.
There were still three Hertz employees sitting around in the manager's office shooting the breeze despite the lines of angry customers out the door.

If there was just one person working for Hertz who could actually read, that person would have typed in the date on time on that old DOS box, which then would have booted up and the whole system would have come back.
We're stuck in the middle of Addison, north of Dallas, without a car, in the least pedestrian-friendly spot of the known universe.
I think I'm going to have to buy a car, because Southwest Airlines is booked solid all day. I guess in a pinch I can always charter a jet ($4673).
posts.key_insight: Automating operations without maintaining fallback capacity and human competence creates systems that fail catastrophically rather than gracefully degrading.

posts.summary: Joel announces the official shipping of FogBugz 6.0, highlighting its major new features including an integrated Wiki, API, overhauled search engine, and Ajax improvements. The centerpiece feature is Evidence-Based Scheduling (EBS), which uses Monte Carlo simulation to generate probability distributions for ship dates rather than single-point estimates. Instead of trusting developer estimates at face value, EBS looks at historical accuracy of each developer's past estimates to simulate multiple possible futures. The post doubles as a product announcement and a sales pitch with introductory pricing.
key_points: FogBugz 6.0 ships with an integrated Wiki, API, revamped search engine, and Ajax-driven UI improvements
Evidence-Based Scheduling uses Monte Carlo simulation (bootstrapping) to replace single ship dates with probability distribution curves
EBS works by comparing developers' past estimates against their actual delivery speeds, rather than taking estimates on faith
The system simulates 60 equally probable futures to generate a shipping probability curve
money_quotes: Instead of adding up everyone's estimates—instead of taking them on faith—FogBugz does a Monte Carlo simulation looking at what speeds developers worked at in the past, vis-à-vis their estimates.
What you get, instead of a date, is a probability distribution curve that shows the probability that the product will ship on such-and-such a date.
You use that same distribution of probabilities that you had in the past and run a simulation of 60 futures each of which will occur with equal probability.
EBS is interesting enough that I'll devote a whole article to explaining it as soon as I get a minute of free time.
Go make yourself a free online trial, what are you waiting for?
posts.key_insight: Software schedule estimates become far more useful when treated as probability distributions derived from historical data rather than taken at face value.

posts.summary: This is a very brief post where Joel Spolsky shares the background music used in the FogBugz 6.0 launch materials. He credits the song 'Sound Scientist' by a San Diego band named 'bill' and provides a download link for the MP3. The post serves as a quick promotional aside during the FogBugz 6.0 launch campaign.
key_points: FogBugz 6.0 launch included a demo movie and world tour
The launch music was 'Sound Scientist' by the band 'bill' from San Diego
Joel shared a direct MP3 download link for the song
money_quotes: The great music we've been using to launch FogBugz 6.0—in the demo movie and the world tour—is a song called Sound Scientist by a San Diego band named bill.
posts.key_insight: A minor promotional post crediting the music used in the FogBugz 6.0 product launch, with no substantive technical or business content.

posts.summary: Joel announces the availability of a 70-minute video recording of his FogBugz demo from Austin, Texas. He describes the technical setup used by NewTek's crew with their TriCaster portable broadcast studio, which allowed the demo to be streamed live to about 150 viewers. The post details the hosting challenge of a 264 MB Flash video file and how Peer 1 Network's Content Delivery Network was used to distribute it across 17 cities for faster downloads.
key_points: NewTek's TriCaster enabled a full broadcast-quality live stream from a relatively compact portable setup, taking only 30 minutes to deploy
The live demo attracted around 150 concurrent viewers, showing early adoption of live-streamed software demos
Hosting large video files in 2007 required creative solutions like CDNs, which Peer 1 Network provided by distributing content across 17 cities
Joel highlights the evolution of Fog Creek's hosting from a free colocation arrangement to two paid racks across LA and New York
money_quotes: The demo itself was broadcast live from the Hyatt Regency in Austin by the crew from NewTek, who came for the opportunity to play around with their TriCaster, which is, essentially, a broadcast studio in a backpack-sized box.
The idea is that your huge files are spread out on servers in 17 different cities, and everybody downloads it from whichever one is closest.
posts.key_insight: This is a brief promotional announcement for a FogBugz demo video, notable mainly as a time capsule of 2007-era challenges around live streaming and hosting large video files before modern platforms like YouTube and Twitch made such tasks trivial.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky argues that incompetent teams are the primary cause of software project failures, but this truth is systematically hidden in postmortems because people are too polite to blame their colleagues. He points out that across all industries, official failure analyses avoid the uncomfortable reality that the people involved simply weren't skilled enough. The post is a teaser for a longer Inc. Magazine article on five common failure modes.
key_points: Bad teams are the #1 cause of software project failures, but postmortems almost never say so
Social norms and politeness prevent honest assessment of team competence in failure analyses
No amount of showing up to work compensates for lack of talent or skill
money_quotes: Even though a bad team of developers tends to be the No. 1 cause of software project failures, you'd never know it from reading official postmortems.
In all fields, from software to logistics to customer service, people are too nice to talk about their co-workers' lack of competence.
You'll never hear anyone say 'the team was just not smart enough or talented enough to pull this off.'
The simple fact is that if the people on a given project team aren't very good at what they do, they're going to come into work every day and yet—behold!—the software won't get created.
posts.key_insight: Postmortems systematically lie about the most common cause of project failure—team incompetence—because social norms make it unacceptable to say the team wasn't good enough.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky introduces Evidence-Based Scheduling (EBS), a method that uses developers' historical timesheet data to produce probabilistic ship date predictions via Monte Carlo simulation. The system requires breaking tasks into small units (under 16 hours), tracking actual time spent, and computing each developer's velocity history. Rather than producing a single unreliable ship date, EBS generates a confidence distribution curve showing the probability of shipping by any given date. Spolsky argues this approach automatically compensates for developer optimism, interruptions, and individual estimation patterns without requiring obsessive time tracking. He also discusses broader scheduling wisdom: only programmers should estimate their own work, managers shouldn't pressure developers into shorter estimates, and realistic schedules force beneficial feature cuts.
key_points: Tasks must be broken into units of 16 hours or less, which forces developers to actually design features in detail before estimating them.
Each developer's historical velocity (estimate divided by actual time) creates a personal correction factor that automatically accounts for optimism, interruptions, and work style.
Monte Carlo simulation using historical velocities produces a probability distribution of ship dates rather than a single unreliable date, giving honest confidence levels.
The system works even when developers don't track interruptions separately—leaving the clock running on the current task naturally incorporates interruption probability into the velocity data.
Realistic schedules force teams to cut low-value features, which paradoxically produces better products that ship sooner.
money_quotes: When you haven't thought about what you're going to do, you can't know how long it will take.
If you have a hand-wavy three week feature called 'Ajax photo editor' without a detailed design, I'm sorry to be the one to break it to you but you are officially doomed.
It compensates precisely, based exactly on this developer's actual, proven, historical optimism.
When I'm behind schedule, I feel doomed and depressed and unmotivated. When I'm working ahead of schedule, I'm cheerful and productive. The schedule is not the place to play psychological games.
When you think of writing code without thinking about all the steps you have to take, it always seems like it will take n time, when in reality it will probably take more like 4n time.
A schedule is a box of wood blocks. If you have a bunch of wood blocks, and you can't fit them into a box, you have two choices: get a bigger box, or remove some blocks.
You can never get 4n from n, ever, and if you think you can, please email me the stock symbol for your company so I can short it.
The process of culling features to fit a schedule was the best thing we could have done.
posts.key_insight: By feeding developers' actual historical estimation accuracy back into Monte Carlo simulations, you can transform unreliable gut-feel schedules into honest probability distributions that automatically correct for individual biases and interruptions.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky curates positive feedback from prominent tech bloggers about Evidence-Based Scheduling (EBS), the flagship feature of FogBugz 6.0. The post collects endorsements from Jeff Atwood, Rafe Colburn, Scott Rosenberg, and Reg Braithwaite, each highlighting different aspects of the feature. The testimonials praise EBS for improving estimation accuracy, encouraging focus through time tracking, and being a fully shipped product rather than just a concept. Spolsky also links to his earlier article on EBS published in Better Software Magazine.
key_points: Jeff Atwood praised EBS as a crucial feature unmatched by other software lifecycle tools for producing good estimates
Rafe Colburn found the time tracking feature useful not just for estimation but as a tool for maintaining focus and avoiding multitasking
Reg Braithwaite emphasized the critical difference between having a prototype of an idea and actually shipping it as a polished product
The post serves as a curated collection of social proof from respected voices in the software development community
money_quotes: I'm not aware of any other software lifecycle tools that go to such great lengths to help you produce good estimates.
When you kick off the timer on a task, you don't want to jump around and multitask because it will just throw off the timer.
Prototypes are not shipping products. FogBugz is a shipping product. My prototype was not. And that makes all the difference.
posts.key_insight: Evidence-Based Scheduling in FogBugz 6.0 earned praise from the developer community for turning estimation theory into a shipped, practical tool that also encouraged better work habits like sustained focus.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky briefly recounts experiencing his first earthquake, a 5.6 magnitude event centered 9 miles away while staying at a hotel. He describes the shaking lasting about 15 seconds and observes lights flashing in a nearby Adobe building. He compares the experience to airplane turbulence.
key_points: Joel experienced his first earthquake, a 5.6 magnitude event near his hotel
The Adobe building across the road had lights flashing on and off during the shaking
He compares the sensation to being on an airplane during turbulence
money_quotes: the whole hotel shook for about 15 seconds
I guess it's sort of like being on an airplane during turbulance
posts.key_insight: A brief personal anecdote about experiencing a first earthquake, with no broader technical or professional insight.

posts.summary: Spolsky recounts the experience of being the client for the first time when hiring Happy Cog Studios to redesign the Fog Creek and FogBugz websites. He discovers firsthand why clients are always disappointed with custom development: specs can't reveal everything, and designs deteriorate through committee meddling. After several failed iterations of tweaking designs, he realized the solution was to separate the FogBugz and Fog Creek identities into distinct sites, and to give the designer artistic freedom rather than micromanaging. The post illustrates how iterative delivery and trusting skilled practitioners leads to better outcomes than design-by-committee.
key_points: Clients are inevitably disappointed with custom software because specs can't capture the full experience of using a product — only working prototypes reveal real problems.
Iterative delivery of working software with frequent feedback loops is more effective than building in isolation for months, which is the core value of agile approaches.
Design-by-committee makes things progressively worse — the more a non-designer tweaks a professional's work, the worse the result becomes.
Separating distinct product identities (FogBugz vs. Fog Creek) into separate websites solved a fundamental design constraint that was causing confusion and wasted space.
The best outcome came from trusting the designer's artistic license rather than meddling, even though the instinct to control was strong.
money_quotes: Custom development is that murky world where a customer tells you what to build, and you say, 'are you sure?' and they say yes, and you make an absolutely beautiful spec, and say, 'is this what you want?' and they say yes, and you make them sign the spec in indelible ink, nay, blood, and they do, and then you build that thing they signed off on, promptly, precisely and exactly, and they see it and they are horrified and shocked.

A spec can't reveal everything. Clients can't use the spec for their real work, and they'll never notice just how many keystrokes it takes to do basic operations until you get, at least, a UI prototype working.
The first delivery should be the minimum thing that could possibly be useful.
This should not be seen as an excuse to write code without designing it, first. All code is going to be designed eventually.

If you try to design it first in a programming language, the designing process is vastly slower than if you were designing it with pencil and paper and descriptive paragraphs in the English language, so you're wasting time.
Somehow we ended up with a design that just got worse and worse and worse the more we tweaked it.
