I thought that we could try and meddle in his design, tweaking things left and right, and get another bad design-by-committee, or we could just tell him we trusted him and whatever he delivered would be exactly what went live.
posts.key_insight: The best results come from frequent iteration with working prototypes and trusting skilled practitioners with creative freedom, rather than over-specifying requirements or micromanaging designs by committee.

posts.summary: This is a very brief news post with two items: an announcement about Joel speaking at the Irish Java Technology Conference in Dublin, and a link to Stefan's article series about Wasabi, Fog Creek's in-house programming language. The post is mostly notable for the humorous aside about how Wasabi got its name, having been renamed from 'FogBasic' after an internal naming contest. The contest came down to 'Bone Crusher 3000' vs 'Wasabi,' with Wasabi winning despite the appeal of typing 'bc3k' into the command line.
key_points: Joel announced a speaking engagement at the Irish Java Technology Conference in November 2007
Fog Creek's in-house language Wasabi was originally called 'FogBasic' before being renamed via an internal contest
The naming contest used instant run-off voting, with the final two candidates being 'Bone Crusher 3000' and 'Wasabi'
money_quotes: During the summer of 2006, we realized that I was a complete dork for naming it FogBasic, so we had a naming contest.
The final two were Bone Crusher 3000 and Wasabi. As much as I wanted to be typing bc3k into the command line, Wasabi won out.
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted news update that gives a glimpse into Fog Creek's playful engineering culture through the story of how their in-house language got its name.

posts.summary: This is a brief link-blog post written while Joel is traveling in Dublin. He shares Chris Lomont's reverse-engineering analysis of the 2007 Excel floating point bug, mentions Stefan's series on Wasabi's 'picture functions' feature, and notes that a Dublin meetup room is larger than expected, allowing more attendees.
key_points: The Excel 2007 floating point bug was traced to a formatting routine update from older 16-bit assembly code
Wasabi's 'picture functions' are functions whose source code visually resembles their output, blending code and template
Joel's Dublin event had more capacity than expected, allowing the waiting list to be cleared
money_quotes: The bug seemed to be introduced when the formatting routine was updated from older 16-bit assembly code used in previous versions of Excel…
A 'picture function' is a function that looks like a picture of what it generates.
posts.key_insight: A brief travel update linking to technical curiosities: a deep-dive into how legacy assembly code migration caused the Excel 2007 floating point bug, and Fog Creek's novel approach to templating with 'picture functions.'

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky recounts lessons learned from a 26-city demo tour for FogBugz 6.0, covering practical tactics for venue selection, staging, presentation design, and follow-up sales. He discovers the power of social proof when a fellow attendee converts a skeptic more effectively than he could. The post argues that demos should be structured as stories with suspense and resolution, not boring feature lists or PowerPoint slides. He emphasizes continuous improvement through live iteration, treating each demo like a comedian testing material. Joel also distinguishes marketing (creating demand) from sales (fulfilling it), admitting he failed to plan adequate sales follow-up despite the tour's success.
key_points: Social proof from peers is more persuasive than any amount of pitch from the seller — having existing customers visible in the audience changes the dynamic entirely
Demos should be structured as narratives with a protagonist, a problem, suspense, and resolution rather than as feature walkthroughs or slide decks
Venue quality, room size, and staging details (coffee, music, dress code) create subconscious impressions that shape how people perceive your product
Live demos improve through iteration with real audiences — practice only gets you so far, and each performance reveals what works and what doesn't
Marketing creates demand but without a sales process to follow up, leads go cold — both push and pull are necessary to close deals
money_quotes: The minute a second person said something, BLING! It was like triangulation. Now he's seeing it from two different angles. It's 3D. Must not be an optical illusion.
The only interesting way to design a demo is to make it a story. You have a protagonist, and the protagonist has a problem, and they use the software.
He was in 'I object!' mode, groping around for some reason not to buy FogBugz, even if he couldn't come up with anything rational.
Your prospective customers start out with no visual image to associate with your company. The demo is the one image that's going to stick in their head. It has to be nice.
You'd much rather have a packed room with people standing in the back and the hotel staff rushing to set up a few more rows of chairs. This is far better than a half-empty room.
Coffee contains caffeine, which makes people cheerful. If you're lucky, they'll attribute their cheeriness to your software instead of the caffeine.
Those nice little touches you put so much hard work into are not the meat of the demo, so don't talk about them, just act nonchalant.
The official definition is that marketing creates demand, while sales fulfils demand. Giving demos is marketing, not sales.
posts.key_insight: A great software demo combines social proof, narrative storytelling, and meticulous staging to create an emotional experience that no feature list or slide deck can match.

posts.summary: Joel shares a walkability scoring website created by his friend Mike Mathieu. He compares the walkability scores of Microsoft's campus (31), Google's campus (34), and Fog Creek's New York City office (98 out of 100). The post is a lighthearted plug for Fog Creek's urban office location, concluding with a tongue-in-cheek recruitment pitch.
key_points: Suburban tech campuses like Microsoft and Google score poorly on walkability (31 and 34 respectively)
Fog Creek's Manhattan office scores 98 out of 100 for walkability
Urban office locations offer lifestyle advantages over suburban corporate campuses
money_quotes: Conclusion: Ditch your car, work at Fog Creek.
posts.key_insight: Fog Creek's urban New York location offers a walkable lifestyle advantage over the suburban campuses of tech giants like Microsoft and Google.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky recounts his experiences at Yale's CS department and connects them to broader lessons about software quality. He describes how a frustrating Dynamic Logic class convinced him to abandon graduate school, which he considers one of his best decisions. This leads to his central argument: programmers habitually redefine problems to be algorithmically solvable, discarding the aspects that matter most to users. He argues that automated approaches to software quality can only verify narrow technical correctness, while ignoring aesthetics, usability, and polish. He uses Windows Vista as a cautionary tale of automated testing replacing human judgment. The talk also touches on the Unix vs. Windows cultural divide and how it maps onto academic hierarchies.
key_points: Formal proofs of program correctness are impractical because the proof is more likely to contain errors than the programmer's intuition about trivial programs, and a complete spec requires just as much information as the program itself.
Programmers redefine quality problems to be automatable, discarding unmeasurable but crucial dimensions like aesthetics, usability, consistency, and user satisfaction.
Windows Vista suffered because Microsoft replaced human testers with automated scripts, catching technical bugs but missing UI inconsistency, polish, and user experience problems.

The Unix vs. Windows cultural divide maps onto a highbrow/lowbrow academic hierarchy, with Ivy League schools teaching Unix and functional programming while less selective schools teach specific vendor tools.
A complete specification that could mechanically verify a program must contain the same Shannon entropy as the program itself, making spec-writing exactly as hard as programming.
money_quotes: The best thing about CS323 is it teaches a lot of people that they just ain't never gonna be programmers.

It occurred to me that when you have a proof that goes on for pages and pages, it's far more likely to contain errors in the proof as our own intuition about the trivial statements that it's trying to prove.
The computer software most people use most of the time is of ridiculously high quality compared to everything else in their lives.
The quality of software is defined by how much it increases my bonus this year.
If the spec defines precisely what a program will do, with enough detail that it can be used to generate the program itself, this just begs the question: how do you write the spec?
This is the software engineering equivalent of a perpetual motion machine.
It turns out that nobody wrote the automated test to check if Vista provided users with a compelling reason to upgrade from XP.

A righteous Unix program starts out with a command-line interface, and if you're lucky, someone else will come along and write a pretty front end for it, with shading and transparency and 3D effects, and this pretty front end just launches the command-line interface in the background, which then fails in mysterious ways.
posts.key_insight: Automating software quality measurement forces you to discard the dimensions of quality users care about most—aesthetics, usability, and polish—because they can't be reduced to mechanical verification.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky recounts his career journey from Microsoft to Viacom to Juno Online Services, using each experience to illustrate lessons about where programmers should work. He argues passionately against in-house software development, where programmers are cost centers producing 'good enough' software with no incentive to improve it. He contrasts this with product companies where better software directly drives revenue and programmers are valued accordingly. He also critiques command-and-control management styles where inexperienced managers override technical experts, advocating instead for the Silicon Valley model where managers exist to clear obstacles for talented people.
key_points: In-house software development, which accounts for roughly 80% of programming jobs, is a career dead end where you're never allowed to do things right and must stop improving software once it's 'good enough'
At product companies, programmers' work directly generates revenue, which means better offices, benefits, promotion paths, and the freedom to polish and refine software
Non-tech companies treat programmers as low-status cost centers, regardless of how strategically important technology is to the business
Good management means clearing obstacles for smart people, not making technical decisions — managers typically have the least information about technical issues
Hit-and-run micromanagement, where managers swoop in to override qualified technical people on minor decisions then disappear, is deeply destructive
money_quotes: You never want to do in house software.
Once the core functionality is there, the main problem is solved, there is absolutely no return-on-investment, no business reason to make the software any better.

You're going to churn out embarrassing junk, and then, you're going to rush off to patch up last year's embarrassing junk which is starting to break down because it wasn't done right in the first place.
When you're working on products, you can keep refining and polishing and refactoring and improving.
A programmer is never going to rise to become CEO of Viacom, but you might well rise to become CEO of a tech company.
If there's one thing I know, it's that managers have the least information about every technical issue, and they are the last people who should be deciding anything.
Managers exist to get furniture out of the way so the real talent can do brilliant work.
posts.key_insight: Programmers should seek product companies where their work directly drives revenue and quality is rewarded, rather than in-house roles where they're cost centers building disposable software under managers who lack the information to make good technical decisions.

posts.summary: In this final part of his Yale talk, Spolsky recounts founding Fog Creek Software out of frustration with how programmers were treated at other companies, and reflects on the most valuable skills he gained from his CS education. He describes starting Joel on Software as a writing platform that grew to reach hundreds of thousands of readers. He credits a gut-level writing course (CS115 Algorithmic Thinking) taught by Roger Schank as the most useful class he took, arguing that clear technical writing is what separates leaders from individual contributors. He concludes that CS degrees don't teach software development, but may build useful mental muscles.
key_points: Spolsky founded Fog Creek Software because he couldn't find a company that treated programmers as talent rather than typists, committing to respect, quality, and customer-first policies.
Joel on Software succeeded not through original ideas but through clear writing and accessibility, reaching 100,000 to a million readers per article.
A writing-intensive course dismissed by the CS faculty as a gut class turned out to be the most professionally valuable part of his CS education.
Clear technical writing is the key differentiator between grunt programmers and leaders—Microsoft's corporate strategy shifted based on a single well-written email.
CS degrees don't actually teach software development, though they may build mental foundations that help later.
money_quotes: I despaired of finding a company to work for where programmers were treated like talent and not like typists, and decided I would have to start my own.
The site consists of fairly unoriginal thoughts, combined with jokes. It was successful because I used a slightly larger font than the average website, making it easy to read.
Being able to write clearly on technical topics is the difference between being a grunt individual contributor programmer and being a leader.
The people who get to decide the terms of the debate are the ones who can write.
The C programming language took over because The C Programming Language was such a great book.

The main thing you don't learn with a CS degree is how to develop software, although you will probably build up certain muscles in your brain that may help you later if you decide that developing software is what you want to do.
He wanted to talk about whether computers could think and all undergrads wanted to talk about was why their paper got a B instead of an A.
posts.key_insight: The ability to write clearly about technical topics is the most important career skill a programmer can develop, more valuable than any specific technical knowledge from a CS degree.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that profitable businesses are built by solving difficult, unpleasant problems that competitors avoid. Using the analogy of factory work where every job has its 'gnarly problem,' he explains why FogBugz offers installable software despite the pain of supporting diverse customer environments. He responds to 37signals' Jason Fried, who prefers hosted-only software, by noting that FogBugz's installable option generates five times the sales. Spolsky contends that many startups feel insubstantial because they avoid solving hard problems, while acknowledging that great design itself is a legitimate gnarly problem to solve. He concludes that business growth comes from progressively solving more gnarly problems, with each solution amplifying the others.
key_points: Every profitable job involves dealing with at least one difficult, unpleasant problem — the market pays for solutions to hard problems, not easy ones
FogBugz's installable option outsells hosted 4-to-1, generating five times the revenue despite costing extra salaries in tech support
Many startups feel insubstantial because they're simple web apps with no barriers to entry that haven't solved any genuinely difficult problems yet
Great design is itself a gnarly problem that provides surprisingly sustainable competitive advantage, even though it looks easy to copy
Business growth comes from progressively expanding what you're good at — each solved problem (design, features, marketing, sales, support) amplifies the others
money_quotes: The trouble is, the market pays for solutions to gnarly problems, not solutions to easy problems.

The one thing that so many of today's cute startups have in common is that all they have is a simple little Ruby-on-Rails Ajax site that has no barriers to entry and doesn't solve any gnarly problems.
People pay for solutions to their problems.
Good design seems like the easiest thing to copy, but, watching Microsoft trying to copy the iPod, turns out to be not-so-easy.
Great design is a gnarly problem, and can actually provide surprisingly sustainable competitive advantage.
The only way to keep growing–as a person and as a company–is to keep expanding the boundaries of what you're good at.
Boy, I would love nothing more than to scrap installable FogBugz and run everything on our servers… but we'd be making so much less money we'd be out of business.
posts.key_insight: Sustainable business advantage comes from solving the gnarly, unpleasant problems that competitors avoid — the market pays for muck, not elegance.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky vents his frustration with Microsoft Outlook 2007's built-in search functionality, which he finds slow, inaccurate, and inferior to the third-party add-in Lookout that it replaced. He recounts how Microsoft acquired the small company behind Lookout, discarded the technology, and then shipped a worse search feature in Outlook 2007 that also disabled Lookout. Joel shares his relief at finding instructions from Lookout's original author to get the add-in working again on Outlook 2007, restoring near-instant search speeds.
key_points: Outlook 2007's built-in search was painfully slow (30 seconds per search) and returned low-quality results with roughly 50% irrelevant matches
Microsoft acquired Lookout's maker in what Joel characterizes as an HR acquisition, then threw away the superior Lucene.NET-based search technology
Outlook 2007 disabled Lookout and claimed built-in search was an adequate replacement, which Joel found to be false
Lookout's original author posted a workaround to restore the add-in on Outlook 2007, delivering sub-second search performance
money_quotes: The search feature in Microsoft Outlook 2007, frankly, sucks big time.
I regularly get 50% garbage results mixed in that have nothing in common with my search terms, and the message I am looking for often doesn't come up.
It must have been one of those HR acquisitions, because the Lookout technology was thrown away.
When Outlook 2007 came out, it disabled Lookout, and allegedly this wasn't supposed to be a big deal because Outlook 2007 has search 'built in.'
Last week I had finally had enough. I can't work like this.
posts.key_insight: When a large company acquires a small tool and replaces it with an inferior built-in alternative, users suffer — a pattern Joel illustrates through Microsoft's bungled handling of Outlook search.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post that simply links to an Inc.com article where Joel shared travel tips from his World Tour. The post promises advice on avoiding air travel problems and details about equipment used. There is essentially no substantive content in the blog post itself, as it serves purely as a pointer to external content.
key_points: Joel conducted a 'World Tour' involving significant travel
He published detailed travel tips through Inc.com rather than his own blog
The tips covered avoiding air travel problems and equipment recommendations
money_quotes: You'll learn how we completely avoided air travel snafus, what equipment we brought along, and more.
posts.key_insight: This is a brief link post pointing readers to Joel's travel tips published on Inc.com, with no substantive content of its own.

posts.summary: Joel shares a brief excerpt from Clive Thompson's New York Times piece about voting machine bugs. The post highlights a specific Diebold voting machine defect where machines running Windows CE would crash every few hundred ballots. The root cause was that voters accidentally dragging their finger while tapping 'cast vote' triggered an unanticipated drag-and-drop event. The programmers had never accounted for this OS-level behavior, leaving the machine unable to cope. Joel presents this without commentary, letting the absurdity speak for itself.
key_points: Diebold voting machines running Windows CE crashed intermittently when voters cast their ballots
The bug was caused by the OS interpreting a slight finger drag as a drag-and-drop command that the software never handled
The failure illustrates how building critical systems atop general-purpose consumer platforms introduces unexpected failure modes
Even a simple UI interaction can become catastrophic when edge cases in the underlying platform aren't understood
money_quotes: their voting software runs on top of Windows CE, and if a voter accidentally dragged his finger downward while touching 'cast vote' on the screen, Windows CE interpreted this as a 'drag and drop' command
The programmers hadn't anticipated that Windows CE would do this, so they hadn't programmed a way for the machine to cope with it. The machine just crashed.
posts.key_insight: Building mission-critical systems on top of general-purpose platforms without fully understanding their behavior invites catastrophic, hard-to-diagnose failures.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that Computer Science education has declined by adopting Java and dumbing down curricula, failing to attract students passionate about programming rather than theory. He proposes creating a BFA-style undergraduate program focused on software development as a craft, modeled after art schools like Juilliard. The program would combine practical studio work building real software with experienced industry practitioners as teachers, supplemented by liberal arts courses. He points to programs like Waterloo and Rose-Hulman as early examples of this approach succeeding where traditional CS departments struggle to attract students. He also laments that universities once produced important software (Unix, X Window, AFS) but no longer do.
key_points: CS departments are dumbing down curricula with Java adoption but failing to attract students who genuinely love programming
Software development is an art that should be taught through intensive studio practice, not primarily through theory like NP completeness and Quicksort
A BFA-style program with 2/3 software development and 1/3 liberal arts, taught by industry practitioners, would be a magnet for talented young programmers
Programs like Waterloo's Software Engineering and Rose-Hulman already prove this model attracts qualified students while Ivy League CS departments struggle for majors
Universities once contributed landmark software (Berkeley Unix, MIT's X Window, CMU's AFS) but have largely stopped producing important code
money_quotes: I think the solution would be to create a programming-intensive BFA in Software Development–a Julliard for programmers.

When I said BFA, Bachelor of Fine Arts, I meant it: software development is an art, and the existing Computer Science education, where you're expected to learn a few things about NP completeness and Quicksort is singularly inadequate to training students how to develop software.
The studio operates like a software company.
These programs have no problem attracting lots of qualified students at a time when the Ivy League CS departments consider themselves lucky if they get a dozen majors a year.
Think about how many computer science departments earned their reputation by writing an important piece of code.
What's the best college for a high school senior who really loves programming but isn't so excited about lambda calculus?
posts.key_insight: Software development should be taught as a practical craft through intensive studio work with experienced practitioners, not as an academic discipline focused on theory.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces his co-sponsorship of the Business of Software 2008 conference, organized by Neil Davidson of Red Gate Software. He praises the previous year's edition in Cambridge, England as the best conference he attended, highlighting its strong speaker lineup and lack of filler content. The 2008 edition will be rebranded as 'Business of Software 2008: A Joel on Software Conference' and is tentatively planned for Boston in the fall. The post is essentially a brief endorsement and promotional announcement.
key_points: The 2007 Business of Software Conference in Cambridge, England featured notable speakers like Guy Kawasaki, Eric Sink, and Tim Lister
Joel valued the conference for its substance and lack of fluff, calling it the best conference he attended that year
Joel is co-sponsoring the 2008 edition, which will carry his brand name alongside the existing conference
money_quotes: It had a great lineup of speakers, a great collection of real software businesses in attendance, and almost no fluff.
It's exactly the kind of conference I would organize if I were organizing a conference about the software business, which, thankfully, I'm not, but Neil is, and he's doing a bang up job.
posts.key_insight: The best software business conferences focus on substance and real practitioners rather than fluff and marketing.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky recounts a 3 AM network outage at Fog Creek's data center caused by a switch left in auto-negotiate mode, and uses it to explore why traditional SLAs are statistically meaningless for measuring reliability. He argues that internet outages are 'black swan' events — rare, unpredictable outliers that defy normal statistical analysis. Rather than chasing expensive ultra-high uptime guarantees, he advocates for finding a 'sweet spot' where all expected failures are handled. Fog Creek adopted Toyota's 'Five Whys' root-cause analysis method, which revealed the real issue was a missing checklist for production configurations. Instead of an SLA, they launched a public status blog documenting outages, post-mortems, and preventive actions as a continuous improvement process.
key_points: SLAs are statistically meaningless when outages are rare — with only two or three data points per year, the numbers don't predict future reliability
Internet outages are 'black swan' events: extremely low-probability surprises where normal statistical methods like mean time between failure don't apply
There's a sweet spot between unreliable and impossibly expensive six-nines availability, where all 'expected unexpecteds' are handled but you accept that 'unexpected unexpecteds' will still occur
Toyota's Five Whys method traces symptoms to root causes — in this case, the root cause wasn't a misconfigured switch but the absence of a written checklist for production deployments
Transparent post-mortems and continuous improvement processes are more valuable than contractual uptime guarantees with trivial penalties
money_quotes: It's the unexpected unexpecteds, not the expected unexpecteds, that kill you.
If your six nines system goes down mysteriously just once and it takes you an hour to figure out the cause and fix it, well, you've just blown your downtime budget for the next century.
Measuring the number of minutes of downtime per year does not predict the number of minutes of downtime you'll have the next year.
A black swan is an outlier, an event that lies beyond the realm of normal expectations.
Documentation is often thought of as an aid for when the sysadmin isn't around or for other members of the operations team, whereas, it should really be thought of as a checklist.

Rather than imposing a statistically meaningless measurement and hoping that the mere measurement of something meaningless would cause it to get better, what we really needed was a process of continuous improvement.
SLAs can be a little bit meaningless that way, and given how low the penalties are, a lot of network providers just started advertising 100% uptime.

Michael knew this could be a problem, but when he installed the switch, he had forgotten to set the speed, so the switch was still in the factory-default autonegotiate mode, which seemed to work fine. Until it didn't.
posts.key_insight: Instead of chasing statistically meaningless uptime metrics, apply root-cause analysis like Five Whys and invest in continuous improvement processes — checklists, post-mortems, and transparency — to systematically eliminate the preventable failures.

posts.summary: Spolsky draws a parallel between Google's new 2D barcode initiative for print ads and the failed :CueCat barcode scanner from the dot-com era. He acknowledges that technology has changed since his original dismissal — people now have camera phones with web browsers — but argues the fundamental problems remain: typing URLs isn't hard, the chicken-and-egg problem persists, and consumers won't install special software for marginal benefit. He frames it as a generational tension between experienced elders who remember past failures and enthusiastic youngsters who think they've invented something new.
key_points: Google's 2D barcode scheme for print ads is essentially the same failed concept as the :CueCat from the dot-com era
While technology has evolved (camera phones with browsers), the core problems — URL typing is easy, chicken-and-egg dynamics, no consumer value — remain unchanged
There's a recurring tension between experienced people who remember why ideas failed and newcomers who don't know the history
Sometimes the world changes enough to make old failures viable, but sometimes the elders are simply right
money_quotes: The number of dumb things going on here exceeds my limited ability to grok all at once.
Sometimes when the elders say to the youngsters, "don't do that, we tried that, it failed," it's just because they're failing to notice that the world has changed.
But sometimes the elders are right, and the youngsters really are too young to know the history of the idea they think that they've just invented.

Typing URLs is not hard, this is a monumental chicken and egg problem, and this doesn't provide any value to the consumers who are expected to install new software on their phones to go along with this ridonculous scheme.
It doesn't say much for the quality of those 150 people Google hires every week that they're now chasing some of the worst of the bad ideas of the fin de siecle.
posts.key_insight: Not every failed idea deserves a second chance just because technology has improved — sometimes the fundamental value proposition was always broken.

posts.summary: Joel criticizes Microsoft's corporate communication style, specifically their announcement about SQL Server 2008 delays. Instead of simply saying the product would be late, Microsoft issued a jargon-filled statement full of meaningless phrases like 'feature complete CTP during the Heroes Happen Here launch wave.' Joel argues the announcement is deliberately obfuscatory and calls on the author to just be honest. He notes that nobody would have been upset by a straightforward delay announcement since SQL Server 2005 works fine.
key_points: Microsoft used impenetrable corporate jargon to disguise the fact that SQL Server 2008 was delayed
Honest, direct communication about delays is always better than obfuscation, especially when customers don't care that much
Corporate-speak erodes trust more than the bad news it tries to hide
money_quotes: Have some guts. Just say it's late.
We really don't care that much. SQL Server 2005 is fine.
A feature complete CTP during the Heroes Happen Here launch wave? What on earth does that mean?
Don't piss on my leg and tell me it's raining.
posts.key_insight: Companies destroy trust not by delivering bad news, but by burying it under corporate jargon instead of communicating honestly.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky enthusiastically recommends TripIt.com, a travel organization service that works by simply forwarding travel confirmation emails. The service automatically parses booking details from airlines, hotels, and car rental agencies to create organized itineraries with maps, weather, and confirmation numbers. Joel contrasts this with the tedious manual process of entering travel data into a calendar, which requires multiple fields and is error-prone. He holds up TripIt as an exemplar of what he calls the 'Figure It Out' school of UI design, where the software handles complexity instead of burdening the user.
key_points: TripIt eliminates manual data entry by parsing travel confirmation emails automatically, requiring zero user effort beyond forwarding an email
The service exemplifies 'Figure It Out' UI design: no registration required, no forms to fill out, the software infers everything from existing data
Manual travel data entry is surprisingly complex — a simple round trip requires airline, flight number, four times, four time zones, and a confirmation number
The parsing problem is tractable because the travel industry has a finite number of players sending standardized confirmation emails
money_quotes: You don't have to sign up for an account. You don't have to log on. You just forward those emails.
TripIt is a beautiful example of the Figure It Out school of user interface design.
Why should you need to register? TripIt figures out who you are based on your email address.
All in all it takes a few minutes and is very error prone.

Everyone gets email from the same 4 online travel agencies, 100-odd airlines, 15 hotel chains, 5 car rental chains… it's pretty easy to just write screen scrapers for each of those to parse out the necessary data.
posts.key_insight: The best user interfaces don't ask users to provide information the software can figure out on its own — great design means doing the hard parsing work so the user doesn't have to.

posts.summary: This is a brief travel announcement where Joel Spolsky lets readers know he is heading to Tokyo for the Shoeisha Developers Summit. The post contains no substantive argument or analysis — it is purely logistical. It reflects the casual, personal blogging style common in the mid-2000s where authors would share travel plans with their audience.
key_points: Joel is traveling to Tokyo for the Shoeisha Developers Summit
The post serves as a brief status update to readers rather than substantive content
posts.key_insight: A minimal travel announcement with no substantive technical or business content.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky explains why Microsoft Office binary file formats are extraordinarily complex, arguing that this complexity isn't the result of deliberate obfuscation or bad programming. The formats were designed for performance on 1990s hardware, relied on Windows libraries, weren't built for interoperability, and had to represent every feature accumulated over decades of development. He uses the '1904' date epoch record in Excel as a case study showing how even the simplest format detail requires deep historical context to implement correctly. He concludes that the released specs won't meaningfully help competitors, and offers practical workarounds: using Office's COM automation to let Office do the heavy lifting, or writing to simpler formats like CSV, RTF, or HTML that Office can still open.
key_points: Office binary formats prioritized performance on 1990s hardware (1MB RAM, 20MHz CPUs), using binary blitting instead of parsing and optimizations like Fast Save that appended changes rather than rewriting files.

The formats were never designed for interoperability — they were internal serialization of application state, and the complexity reflects thousands of developer-years of accumulated features and backwards compatibility.

Even the simplest record in the Excel spec (the 1904 date epoch flag) requires understanding Mac/Windows history, Lotus 1-2-3 compatibility, and a deliberate leap-year bug — illustrating how the full spec is essentially equivalent to the source code itself.

Publishing the binary specs doesn't meaningfully help competitors because a file format is just a 'concise summary of all the features an application supports' — implementing the format means reimplementing the application.
Practical workarounds exist: use Office's COM Automation API to let Office itself do the work, or write to simpler interchange formats (CSV, RTF, HTML, WK1) that Office opens natively.
money_quotes: A normal programmer would conclude that Office's binary file formats are deliberately obfuscated, are the product of a demented Borg mind, were created by insanely bad programmers, and are impossible to read or create correctly. You'd be wrong on all four counts.

There are thousands of developer years of work that went into the current versions of Word and Excel, and if you really want to clone those applications completely, you're going to have to do thousands of years of work.
A file format is just a concise summary of all the features an application supports.
At no point in history did a programmer ever not do the right thing, but there you have it.
All of these subtle bits of behavior cannot be fully documented without writing a document that has the same amount of information as the Excel source code.
The binary file specification is, at most, going to save you a few minutes reverse engineering a remarkably complex system.
You can't just implement the most popular 20% and expect 80% of the people to be happy.
posts.key_insight: Office file format complexity isn't malice or incompetence — it's the inevitable result of decades of feature accumulation, performance optimization for ancient hardware, and the fact that a file format is really just a serialization of the entire application's capability.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky corrects numerous factual errors in a wire service article that mischaracterized his comments about Microsoft's interoperability announcement. The journalist incorrectly claimed Spolsky had 'failed' to build a remote desktop product over 10 years ago, when in fact Fog Creek Copilot was a profitable, actively-developed product less than three years old. Spolsky clarifies that Copilot uses the open VNC protocol, not Microsoft's Remote Desktop protocol, and that Microsoft's announcement had no bearing on Copilot's development. He does agree with the broader point that Microsoft's protocol disclosure could benefit the ecosystem by enabling third-party competition in areas like remote desktop clients.
key_points: Wire service journalism under deadline pressure led to a cascade of factual errors about Spolsky's products and statements
Fog Creek Copilot was a profitable, actively-developed product using the open VNC/RFB protocol, completely independent of Microsoft's Remote Desktop protocol
Microsoft's interoperability announcement could genuinely benefit the Windows ecosystem by enabling third-party developers to build better drop-in replacements for built-in features
The lack of published Remote Desktop protocol specs meant no competitive RDP clients existed besides Citrix's cross-licensed version, leaving users stuck with Microsoft's rudimentary client
Microsoft stands to gain from open protocols since features like Remote Desktop are free and built into Windows — better third-party options strengthen the platform
money_quotes: That's the nature of the wire reporters on deadline.
We've always known how the protocol works, and how it transmits GDI commands for performance reasons, which is neither rocket science nor a trade secret.
The X Window server worked this way before Windows was even invented, so if there is a patent for this GDI business, it ain't Microsoft's.
Copilot doesn't use the remote desktop protocol, full stop, and we don't plan to.
Its relative simplicity is demonstrative of how there's been no competitive offerings that would have forced Microsoft to make a better widget.
It's bound to open up lots of opportunities in hundreds of areas where third-party developers will be able to develop better drop-in versions of various pieces of the Microsoft software stack.
Microsoft isn't making a dime off of RDC because it's free and built into Windows… a few competitive options with more features can only help the Windows business in the long run.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's decision to publish its protocol specifications could strengthen the Windows ecosystem by enabling third-party competition in areas where Microsoft has no revenue incentive to innovate.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares a brief announcement about the upcoming Business of Software Conference. He links to Neil Davidson's preliminary speaker list, which includes Joel himself alongside notable figures like Richard Stallman, Jason Fried, and Eric Sink. The post is essentially a quick promotional note with no substantive argument or analysis.
key_points: The Business of Software Conference speaker lineup includes Joel Spolsky, Eric Sink, Richard Stallman, Dharmesh Shah, Jason Fried, Steve Johnson, and Paul Kenny
The post is a brief promotional announcement linking to Neil Davidson's blog for details
money_quotes: So far I've lined up (in no particular order) Joel Spolsky, Eric Sink, Richard Stallman, Dharmesh Shah, Jason Fried, Steve Johnson and Paul Kenny.
posts.key_insight: A brief promotional post announcing the speaker lineup for the 2008 Business of Software Conference.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief blog post that serves as a teaser for Joel Spolsky's February column in Inc. Magazine. The post contains virtually no content beyond a single quote about bureaucratic inertia, referencing Newton's first law of motion as a metaphor. The full argument and context exist in the linked Inc. Magazine column rather than in the blog post itself.
key_points: Bureaucratic organizations tend to resist or ignore the momentum of existing systems and processes
Newton's first law serves as a metaphor for organizational inertia — things already moving should be left to continue
The post is essentially a promotional pointer to Spolsky's Inc. Magazine column
money_quotes: The bureaucrats in Washington had forgotten Newton's first law: An object in motion tends to remain in motion.
posts.key_insight: Bureaucratic interference often disrupts systems that would function better if left to their existing momentum.

posts.summary: This is a very brief teaser post directing readers to Joel's monthly Inc. magazine column. The core advice is about not imposing startup-era frugality on your successful employees. Joel argues that as a company grows, you should let star performers travel and work in comfort rather than forcing the scrappy cost-cutting that founders endured in the early days. The implication is that penny-pinching on employee perks damages morale and retention.
key_points: Company spending norms should evolve as the business matures beyond the bootstrap phase
Imposing founder-era austerity on star employees signals a lack of respect for their contributions
Employee morale is connected to how generously and professionally they are treated in their work conditions
money_quotes: Don't tell your star salespeople to take the bus and stay with relatives when they make that call in St. Louis, even though that's what you did when you started the company.
posts.key_insight: As your company grows past the bootstrap phase, clinging to founder-era frugality with your top performers undermines morale and signals you don't value them.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces three new job openings at Fog Creek Software, using the occasion to articulate his hiring philosophy and company culture. He describes positions for a junior system administrator, a Linux guru, and a Windows internals expert, each with detailed explanations of why the role exists and what makes it interesting. Spolsky addresses the tension between wanting experienced hires and giving smart people a chance to learn, particularly in system administration. He also tackles the apparent contradiction of a small company hiring more people, arguing that The Mythical Man-Month's lessons are more nuanced than 'small teams always win.' The post doubles as both a job listing and a window into how Fog Creek thinks about team growth, emphasizing that they add people only where it won't slow things down.
key_points: Experience matters enormously for system administration, unlike many other roles where smart inexperienced people can learn on the job, creating a catch-22 for aspiring sysadmins
Fog Creek's solution is to pair an inexperienced but smart second hire with an experienced first hire, providing mentorship without compromising quality
The Linux guru role is designed so the person taking support calls is the same person maintaining the setup code, creating a natural incentive to fix root causes rather than repeat manual fixes
The Mythical Man-Month's actual claim is narrower than commonly believed—it says adding people to a late project makes it later, not that small teams are always better
Fog Creek grows by finding areas where adding people enables parallelism rather than adding communication overhead to existing teams
money_quotes: Normally, I'm pretty happy to hire inexperienced but bright people and let them learn on the job. Even for fairly important jobs, like, say, President of the United States.
You don't want your new system administrator to learn about how to create secure and robust online services by building something insecure and unrobust and learning from experience.
If everybody is asking for x years of experience, how are you supposed to get that experience?
At Fog Creek when you need equipment you order it. That's really all there is to it.

My pet theory is that if the person who takes the call when a customer is missing, say, the Pear Mail module, if this person is the same person who maintains the setup code, then they will eventually get sick of sshing into customers' servers and typing 'pear install Mail' for them and they'll just fix it in the setup code once and for all.
And what MMM claimed was only that adding people to a late project makes the project later.
Our too-small team is forced to do things in serial that we could do in parallel with a couple more people.
posts.key_insight: Smart hiring means finding roles where new people enable parallelism rather than adding communication overhead, and pairing inexperienced but talented hires with experienced mentors solves the catch-22 of requiring experience for experience-dependent roles.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky briefly announces that while Fog Creek's summer internships are filled, his marketing mentor Seth Godin is offering paid marketing internships. The internships focus on creative projects using digital tools like online video, blogs, copywriting, and design. Joel emphasizes that these are substantive roles with no menial work involved.
key_points: Fog Creek's own summer internships were already at capacity
Seth Godin's internships are paid and focused on creative digital marketing projects
The internships promise meaningful work with no menial tasks like cold calling or stapling
money_quotes: no scut work, no cold calling, stapling, collating or errands
posts.key_insight: Good internships should offer meaningful creative work rather than menial tasks.

posts.summary: Spolsky examines the IE8 team's dilemma over whether to default to strict web standards mode or maintain backward compatibility with IE7's rendering behavior. He frames this as an instance of a universal tension between idealists who want strict standards compliance and pragmatists who prioritize not breaking existing websites. Through an extended analogy about Martian headphone jacks, he explains how markets evolve from one-to-one to many-to-many relationships, making backward compatibility increasingly painful but necessary. He argues there is genuinely no right answer: idealists are correct in principle that strict standards would improve the web, but pragmatists are correct that shipping a browser that breaks half the internet is suicidal. He predicts the IE team will ultimately revert to pragmatic backward compatibility despite publicly championing standards.
key_points: Markets evolve through stages (one-to-one, one-to-many, sequence-to-many, many-to-many), and each stage makes interoperability harder because there are more combinations to test and more legacy behavior to preserve.

Jon Postel's robustness principle ('be liberal in what you accept') created a tragedy of the commons: forgiving browsers encouraged sloppy HTML, which now means billions of pages depend on non-standard behavior.

Web standards are a platonic ideal, not a practical reality, because there is no reference implementation to test against and the specs themselves are confusing enough that even careful developers make mistakes.
The IE8 standards-vs-compatibility debate is structurally identical to the Windows Vista backward compatibility problem: users blame the new platform, not the broken applications.
Both the idealist and pragmatist positions are simultaneously correct in their own domains, making this a genuinely unsolvable dilemma rather than a case of one side being wrong.
money_quotes: It's not a fine line. It's a line of negative width. There is no place to walk. They are damned if they do and damned if they don't.

In the real world where people are imperfect, you can't have a standard with just a spec–you must have a super-strict reference implementation, and everybody has to test against the reference implementation.

Just because you all agree to follow one book doesn't ensure compatibility, because the laws are so complex and complicated and convoluted that it's almost impossible to understand them all well enough to avoid traps and landmines, and you're safer just asking for the fruit plate.
The consumer is not an idiot. She's your wife. So stop laughing.
They don't give a flicking flick about your stupid religious enthusiasm for making web browsers which conform to some mythical, platonic 'standard' that is not actually implemented anywhere.
As usual, the idealists are 100% right in principle and, as usual, the pragmatists are right in practice.
posts.key_insight: In many-to-many markets like the web, standards without a strict reference implementation are an unenforceable fiction, leaving platform developers trapped between the idealists who are right in principle and the pragmatists who are right in practice.

posts.summary: This is a very brief teaser post promoting Joel's April column in Inc. Magazine. It opens with a colorful anecdote about parachuting from a plane, referencing his military experience. Joel promises this will be the last time he tells 'shaggy dog stories' about his army days. The post is essentially a plug for his Inc. Magazine column with minimal standalone content.
key_points: Joel was writing a regular column for Inc. Magazine aimed at small business owners
He frequently drew on his military service as a source of analogies and stories for business lessons
The post acknowledges his tendency toward lengthy, roundabout anecdotes ('shaggy dog stories') and promises to move on from military references
money_quotes: If the flight attendants on the JAL 747 from Tokyo I'm on right now were to, in a remarkable lapse of Japanese standards of service, throw me off the plane with a parachute, I could do a pretty nice roll when I hit the ground.
posts.key_insight: This is a lightweight teaser post directing readers to Joel's Inc. Magazine column, notable mainly for its characteristic storytelling style and self-aware humor about his reliance on military anecdotes.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the first-ever Joel on Software conference, 'Business of Software 2008,' to be held in Boston in September. The post serves as a promotional announcement listing nine speakers from across the software and business world, including Seth Godin, Richard Stallman, Jason Fried, Jessica Livingston, and Spolsky himself. He highlights Boston as an ideal September destination with perfect weather and cultural attractions. The tone is light and promotional, with Spolsky's characteristic self-deprecating humor on display in his own speaker bio.
key_points: The conference lineup bridges software development and business, featuring founders, marketers, sales trainers, and open source advocates
The speaker list reads as a who's-who of late-2000s software industry thought leaders, from Richard Stallman to Jason Fried to Jessica Livingston of Y Combinator
The event is positioned as a 'Business of Software' conference rather than a purely technical one, reflecting Spolsky's interest in the intersection of code and commerce
Spolsky uses humor and personality to sell what is essentially a conference announcement, a style that defined his blogging voice
money_quotes: Over 250,000 college students have just arrived, full of completely unjustifiable hope and optimism.
There are plenty of coffee shops that aren't NASDAQ-listed.
Joel Spolsky, aka, 'me,' noted DJ, has over 600 karma points on the social news site 'Reddit.'
posts.key_insight: Spolsky leveraged his blogging influence to curate a conference bridging software development and business strategy, assembling a speaker lineup that represented the full spectrum of late-2000s tech entrepreneurship thinking.

posts.summary: Joel announces the second episode of the Stack Overflow podcast with Jeff Atwood, covering logistics and listener engagement. The episode discussed the rationale for doing a podcast, addressed listener questions, and featured a debate about whether programmers should learn C. Joel outlines improvements including RSS subscription support, a community wiki for transcripts, and plans to improve audio quality via Skype. The post is primarily administrative, directing listeners to subscribe and contribute.
key_points: The podcast debated whether programmers should learn C, a recurring Spolsky theme about understanding fundamentals
Community volunteers stepped up to transcribe episodes, making content accessible to hearing-impaired readers and search engines
Joel set up infrastructure for ongoing engagement: RSS feeds, a dedicated blog, and a listener voicemail system
money_quotes: Today we talked about why we're doing a podcast in the first place, took some questions/suggestions from listeners, and got into a fight over whether programmers should learn C.
A couple of people volunteered to help by typing up transcripts for the hearing-impaired, the pressed-for-time, and search engines.
If you have any comments, ideas, or suggestions record a short MP3 and email it to podcast@stackoverflow.com.
posts.key_insight: The early Stack Overflow podcast prioritized community participation and accessibility from the start, inviting listener contributions through transcripts, voicemail questions, and open discussion.

posts.summary: This is a very brief teaser post directing readers to Joel's column in Inc. Magazine about Fog Creek Software's search for office space. The post highlights the frustration of a failed real estate deal with dry humor, noting that a floor they wanted to expand into turned out not to be available. It also promotes the upcoming Business of Software conference with an early registration reminder.
key_points: Fog Creek Software's office expansion plans were delayed when a deal for additional space fell through
Joel was writing a regular column for Inc. Magazine about running a software company
The post serves primarily as a pointer to external content rather than a standalone essay
money_quotes: We lost some time because a deal to expand at our current location fell through — it turned out that the extra floor we wanted wasn't actually, to use the real estate jargon, 'available.'
posts.key_insight: Even well-run software companies face mundane operational setbacks like failed real estate deals that can slow growth plans.

posts.summary: Joel describes the process of selecting desks for Fog Creek's new office. Starting from ergonomic principles—feet flat on the floor, arms horizontal while typing—he explains why they chose motorized height-adjustable desks. They settled on the Details adjusTables Series 7 with custom Steelcase desk surfaces featuring a knife-edge profile for a sleeker appearance.
key_points: Proper ergonomic setup requires adjusting seat height first, then ensuring arms are horizontal while typing, which necessitates adjustable-height work surfaces
Most adjustable keyboard trays are flimsy and limiting, so full-surface height adjustment is preferable
Standing desks with electric motors remove the friction of switching between sitting and standing throughout the day
Aesthetic details matter even for functional furniture—they ordered custom desk surfaces to avoid the default 'blah' look
money_quotes: Most of the adjustable height keyboard trays are extremely annoying… they're floppy, flimsy, and limit the keyboard to one location.
A lot people praise the benefits of standing up for a part of the day, even if you spend the whole day at a computer.
If you are going to be standing up and sitting down it's best to have a desk with a pushbutton, electric motor so you don't get lazy about doing it.
posts.key_insight: Good office furniture decisions start from ergonomic principles and should minimize friction—motorized height-adjustable desks make healthy work habits effortless.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues against the common practice of disabling menu items when they can't be used. He contends that disabled menu items leave users confused and helpless, unable to figure out what they need to do to enable the functionality. Instead, he recommends keeping menu items enabled and showing an explanatory message when the action can't be completed, giving users actionable information.
key_points: Disabling menu items became a widespread UI convention, but it's a poor practice
Disabled menu items frustrate users by showing what they want but giving no guidance on how to get it
Menu items should remain enabled and display explanatory messages when the action can't be performed
money_quotes: Users see the disabled menu item that they want to click on, and are left entirely without a clue of what they are supposed to do to get the menu item to work.
Instead, leave the menu item enabled. If there's some reason you can't complete the action, the menu item can display a message telling the user why.
posts.key_insight: Disabled menu items tell users what they can't do without telling them why or how to fix it — an explanatory message is always more helpful than a grayed-out option.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces Fog Creek Software's annual open house event, scheduled for July 17, 2008 at their Manhattan office. He notes that they normally decline tour requests because they prioritize giving programmers quiet working conditions. The company is about a month away from moving to a larger downtown space but chose to hold the event at the old office anyway. Visitors will get to meet interns, MBA program participants, sales staff, and developers working on FogBugz, Copilot, and Wasabi.
key_points: Fog Creek regularly declines office tour requests to protect programmers' quiet working conditions
The annual open house is the one exception to their no-tours policy
The company was preparing to move to a larger downtown office space
The event showcased their various teams including interns ('Dingos'), MBA participants ('SMTPs'), and product developers
money_quotes: Here at Fog Creek Software we get a lot of requests for a tour of the office, which we usually have to decline: we have this unusual obsession with giving programmers quiet working conditions.
Some kind of food-like snack will be served. Tiny cheddar-cheese-flavored crackers in the shape of fish, maybe. Don't skip lunch.
posts.key_insight: Fog Creek's willingness to decline tour requests year-round reflects their commitment to prioritizing developer productivity over PR opportunities, with the annual open house as a carefully bounded exception.

posts.summary: Joel announces the adoption of the Pecha Kucha presentation format for the Business of Software conference. The format strictly limits speakers to 20 slides at 20 seconds each, totaling exactly 6 minutes and 40 seconds. He explains how the rigid constraints force speakers to rehearse carefully, edit ruthlessly, and deliver polished presentations. Eight finalists were selected from 45 applicants to present in this format alongside the main speaker lineup.
key_points: The Pecha Kucha format enforces exactly 20 slides at 20 seconds each, with automatic advancement and a hard cutoff
Rigid constraints on presentation time force better preparation, tighter editing, and more polished delivery
The format was chosen to add variety and dynamism to the conference by bringing in additional voices for brief talks
The competitive application process (45 applicants narrowed to 8) ensured high-quality short presentations
money_quotes: Each one is shown for exactly 20 seconds and then flips automatically. At the end, even if you're almost done and just have one more thing, the mic cuts off and you sit down.
Speakers have to plan very carefully and rehearse repeatedly to make sure their speech is going to synchronize correctly with the slides, which makes for a more polished speech.
They have to edit mercilessly to boil their subject matter down to 400 seconds, which makes it more interesting and dynamic.
And if they suck, well, you don't have to wait very long for them to go away!
posts.key_insight: Imposing strict constraints on presentations forces speakers to prepare better, edit ruthlessly, and deliver more engaging talks than open-ended formats allow.

posts.summary: This is a brief teaser post promoting Joel Spolsky's Inc. magazine article titled 'Good System, Bad System.' The post opens with a vivid, humorous observation about the density of Starbucks locations on his walk to work in midtown Manhattan, using it as a hook to draw readers to the full article. It also promotes his second book, 'More Joel on Software.' The actual substance of the argument about good vs. bad systems is in the linked Inc. article, not in this post itself.
key_points: The post serves primarily as a teaser for an external Inc. magazine article about systems
Spolsky uses the absurd density of Starbucks in Manhattan as a comedic opening hook
He observes how New Yorkers micro-optimize their walking routes out of habit, never exploring alternatives
The post also promotes his second book 'More Joel on Software'
money_quotes: I pass six Starbucks every morning on my walk to work.
For most New Yorkers, micro-optimizing the walk to work is a matter of habit, and I have no reason to cross the street.
For all I know, the other side of Eighth Avenue consists of nothing but pachinko parlors and flea circuses. Wouldn't surprise me one bit.
posts.key_insight: This is a promotional teaser post whose brief excerpt illustrates Spolsky's signature style of using witty everyday observations as entry points into broader business and systems thinking.

posts.summary: Joel briefly updates listeners about StackOverflow Podcast episode 18, his first conversation with Jeff Atwood since the beta launched. He expresses genuine enthusiasm about the beta's performance, noting it exceeds his highest expectations. The hybrid system combining wiki, Q&A, discussion, and Reddit-like features effectively surfaces the best answers. He highlights that the ability to edit questions and answers creates a continuous improvement loop.
key_points: The StackOverflow beta exceeded even Joel's highest expectations in terms of performance and usability
The hybrid wiki/Q&A/discussion/Reddit model effectively bubbles the right answers to the top
Editability of old questions and answers creates a flywheel where content improves over time
Even with a small beta audience, users were getting great answers quickly
money_quotes: The bizarre wiki/q&a/discussion/reddit hybrid system does a great job of bubbling the right answers to the top.
The ability to edit old questions and answers means that answers just get better and better.
posts.key_insight: StackOverflow's hybrid model combining wiki editability with Q&A, discussion, and voting creates a system where answer quality continuously improves over time.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky reviews the Nokia E71 as a serious alternative to the iPhone 3G, having picked one up from Nokia's Fifth Avenue boutique after growing frustrated with iPhone availability and early bug reports. He finds the E71 excels in build quality, keyboard, call quality, and battery life, while acknowledging the iPhone's superior screen and simpler app experience. He details his specific use cases—MP3 playback, Exchange sync, and tethered internet—finding the E71 adequate with third-party software filling gaps in Nokia's built-in apps. He concludes that while the iPhone pushed the state of the art, the game isn't over for competitors, and the E71 is the best phone he's ever owned.
key_points: The Nokia E71 emerged as a credible iPhone 3G alternative, with superior build quality, keyboard, call quality, and battery life despite Apple's revolutionary design
Third-party apps were essential to make the E71 fully functional—DataViz RoadSync for proper Exchange sync, Opera Mini for browsing, and Google Maps replacing Nokia's buggy built-in maps
The iPhone vs. E71 tradeoff comes down to user type: geeks prefer the Nokia's functionality and multitasking, while less technical users prefer the iPhone's simplicity
Apple makes building an MP3 player look easy, but the E71's music player reveals just how many small details Apple gets right that competitors miss
Nokia's built-in software was often inadequate (Exchange sync, browser, maps), but the platform's openness to third-party solutions compensated
money_quotes: If the iPhone competed in the Olympic swimming tournaments, Michael Phelps would have just retired on the spot and given up swimming for life.
I thought that it meant 'game over' for all the other handset makers. But Nokia is a fantastic company and they weren't going to give up that easy.

It's amazing how something as simple as playing MP3s is so fraught with minor problems… Apple makes it look easy to build an MP3 player, so when someone else tries, it's always surprising to see just how hard it is to get right.

In general I think that geeks will prefer the Nokia for its functionality, while the iPhone is totally the phone for people who are less technical and don't want to spend any time setting up their phone and downloading software to get it exactly the way they want it.
After years of using junky phones I literally did not know cell phone calls could be this good.
Why don't desktop word processors have autocomplete yet?

The volume control has exactly ten choices. It reminded me of those old AT&T public telephones with three amplification choices for the hearing impaired. You have to choose between too soft and too loud.
posts.key_insight: Even when a competitor creates a revolutionary product like the iPhone, there remains room for alternatives that serve power users willing to trade polish for functionality and configurability.

posts.summary: This is a brief teaser post directing readers to Joel's Inc. magazine column about the value of middle managers. The post includes a quote about employee unhappiness and complaints affecting productivity, suggesting the column explores how Joel came to appreciate the role middle managers play in organizations. The framing implies Joel initially held the common programmer-culture skepticism toward middle management before changing his view.
key_points: Employee unhappiness can become self-reinforcing, with complaining replacing productive work
Joel's perspective on middle managers evolved from skepticism to appreciation
The post serves as a teaser for his Inc. magazine column on organizational management
money_quotes: I thought you should know that people are really unhappy, and it's starting to make it so that people just complain all day, instead of doing their work, and that's not good.
posts.key_insight: Middle managers serve a vital function in organizations by addressing employee unhappiness before it spirals into a culture of complaint that undermines productivity.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky recaps StackOverflow Podcast episode 20, highlighting a deadlock bug caused by a third-party library that delayed the public beta launch. He uses this as evidence for his long-held skepticism of third-party dependencies and references the Excel team's decision to build their own compiler. The post also thanks attendees of the Business of Software conference and humorously solicits sponsors for the podcast.
key_points: A small bug in a third-party library caused a deadlock that was the final blocker for StackOverflow's public beta
Joel reaffirms his bias against third-party libraries, citing the risk of hard-to-diagnose bugs outside your control
The Excel team at Microsoft built their own compiler to avoid external dependencies
The Business of Software conference was a success with strong speakers and attendees
money_quotes: Caused by a very small bug in third party libraries, which is exactly why I've always had a bias against using third party libraries.
I tell an interesting story about why the Excel team had their own compiler.
And I explain to a listener why Jeff never listens to me.
If your company might be interested in sponsoring the podcast and becoming a hero to developers worldwide, or at least the eight developers who listen to the podcast, please email me.
posts.key_insight: Third-party library bugs that block your critical path validate the case for minimizing external dependencies, even when the bugs are small.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces that Fog Creek Software is finally moving into their new office space after over a year of planning and construction since March 2007. The new office is twice as large, in a better neighborhood, and features premium amenities including adjustable-height desks, aquariums, and a marble-lined shower. He notes the frustrating delays typical of office build-outs, including moveable walls from Haworth that won't arrive until six weeks after move-in. The post is a brief, lighthearted update teasing more details to come.
key_points: Fog Creek's new office took over 18 months from planning to move-in, illustrating how long commercial build-outs take
The company is investing heavily in workspace quality — 5x rent increase for better finishes, ergonomic desks, and premium amenities
Even with careful planning, vendor delays (Haworth moveable walls) forced them to move in without walls and doors
Joel continues his philosophy that developer workspace quality matters, investing in ergonomics and environment
money_quotes: These things take FOREVER.
costing about 5 times as much in rent
50% more aquarium
a private marble-lined shower
adjustable-height desks (for developer ergonomics, of course)
we're literally going to have to move in without walls and doors, which will arrive about 6 weeks late
posts.key_insight: Fog Creek's willingness to pay 5x more rent for a premium developer workspace reflects Joel's long-held belief that investing in work environment is essential to attracting and retaining top talent.

posts.summary: Joel presents a simple three-step system for managing passwords across multiple computers using Dropbox and PasswordSafe. The approach combines Dropbox's file synchronization with an encrypted password file that works across Windows, Mac, and Linux. He argues this setup is secure, free, and practical because it doesn't expose passwords to any internet site while generating unique, random passwords for every account. The post is notably a product of its era, predating modern cloud-based password managers.
key_points: Combining Dropbox's file sync with an offline password manager creates a cross-platform password solution without trusting a cloud password service
Every site should have its own unique, long, randomly-generated password rather than reused simple passwords
The encrypted password file is worthless without the master password, making Dropbox sync safe enough for sensitive data
The solution was notable at the time for being free, cross-platform, and not requiring trust in any third-party internet service
money_quotes: Now that DropBox is shipping, there's finally a good way to manage all your passwords.
This gives you a folder on your computer that can, magically, be synchronized onto every computer you use.
The file itself is encrypted… if someone gets their hands on it, it's worthless without the master password you created for it.
Log on to all your bank accounts and change that "abcd" password to some long 16 digit, unique, secure password that PasswordSafe makes up for you.
It doesn't expose your passwords to any internet site (whether or not you trust it).
posts.key_insight: Before dedicated cloud password managers existed, combining file synchronization with an offline encrypted password vault was the first practical way to use unique, strong passwords across multiple computers.

posts.summary: This is a very brief announcement post for Episode 21 of the Stack Overflow podcast. Joel mentions that he and Jeff Atwood discussed how to handle abusive users in online communities. He uses a chess analogy to describe users who disrupt the intended purpose of a platform. The post also notes that Stack Overflow was about to launch publicly the following Monday.
key_points: Stack Overflow podcast episode 21 covered the challenge of abusive users in online communities
The core problem is framed as some users deliberately undermining the purpose of a platform while others try to use it constructively
Stack Overflow was days away from its public launch at the time of this post
money_quotes: You've got a bunch of people playing Chess, but certain people want to play 'throw the chess pieces all over the park…'
posts.key_insight: Online communities must grapple with the fundamental tension between users who want to use a platform as intended and those who want to disrupt it.

posts.summary: Spolsky proposes a simple but practical product idea: a desk-mountable power strip that combines electrical outlets, a network hub, and a USB hub in one unit. He notes that an existing product by Mockett comes close but requires users to hardwire their own ports rather than offering built-in connectivity. The core frustration is having to crawl under desks to plug in laptops and peripherals.
key_points: The best product ideas often come from everyday frustrations like crawling under desks to plug things in
Combining existing technologies (power strip + network hub + USB hub) into one integrated product can solve real workflow problems
Products that come 'tantalizingly close' to solving a problem but require DIY assembly miss the point of convenience
money_quotes: This would make it easy to plug in laptops, USB peripherals, and all your rechargers at your desk without crawling around on the floor.
posts.key_insight: Great product opportunities exist in combining existing technologies into integrated solutions that eliminate everyday annoyances.

posts.summary: This is a very brief announcement post directing readers to Stack Overflow Podcast #24, where the full Stack Overflow team gathered at Fog Creek's New York City office for a roundtable discussion about the future of StackOverflow.com. The post is essentially a pointer to the podcast episode hosted on the Stack Overflow blog. Joel closes with a Rosh Hashanah greeting in Hebrew.
key_points: The Stack Overflow team met in person at Fog Creek's new NYC office for a roundtable discussion
The discussion focused on the future direction of StackOverflow.com
The substantive content lives in the linked podcast episode rather than the blog post itself
money_quotes: The whole Stack Overflow team got together in person at Fog Creek's shiny new office in New York City for a roundtable discussion about the future of StackOverflow.com.
posts.key_insight: This is a brief link post announcing a Stack Overflow podcast episode recorded at Fog Creek's NYC office, with no substantive analysis in the post itself.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky details the technical evolution of the Stack Overflow podcast's recording setup, from low-quality phone recordings through Skype with Pamela to a professional hardware-based studio configuration. The post walks through the specific equipment used — a Sennheiser headset, DJ mixer, Fostex recorder, and M-Audio USB preamps — and explains how they're wired together to record his voice locally while keeping the Skype conversation separate. He describes the post-production workflow using Audacity and The Levelator for volume normalization. Joel acknowledges the setup is an over-engineered hardware kludge that could theoretically be done in software, but he couldn't get the software solutions to work despite trying.
key_points: Recording a remote podcast at high quality requires separating local voice capture from the VoIP conversation to avoid dropout issues when speakers talk over each other
The Skype + Pamela setup had a critical flaw: simultaneous speech caused both voices to drop from the recording, forcing an unnatural conversational style
The hardware solution routes audio through separate channels — local voice and computer sounds on one path, remote voice on another — mixed together only at the recording stage
The Levelator automatically normalizes volume levels across an entire podcast, eliminating the need to monitor levels during recording
Joel admits the elaborate hardware setup is a kludge born from his inability to configure software-based virtual audio routing, despite being comfortable with physical XLR cables
money_quotes: If Jeff and I spoke at the same time, the recording would, for some reason, drop both of us.
We had to learn to be careful not to speak over each other and not to interrupt each other. This made the conversation sound stilted, and sometimes interesting things got lost.
The Levelator takes an entire podcast and adjusts the volumes automatically so that every speaker comes out at the same volume. It's pretty much magic.
All the cables and analog mixers seem like a ridiculous way to set this up.

There are a lot of little apps that cost $20 that claim to allow you to create virtual sound cards and virtual cables between them, all in software. I couldn't figure any of them out, but I am pretty handy with an XLR cable, so this is the big hardware kludge I came up with.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes a hardware kludge you understand beats a software solution you can't figure out — pragmatism over elegance gets the podcast shipped.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces Stack Overflow Podcast episode 25, featuring guest Steve Yegge. The episode covers working at Google, marketing ideas, and programming languages.
