Joel notes the episode ran long in homage to Yegge's famously lengthy blog posts. He also describes fixing persistent Skype audio quality issues with new podcasting equipment.
key_points: Steve Yegge appeared as a guest to discuss Google, idea marketing, and programming languages
The episode intentionally ran 15 minutes long as a nod to Yegge's verbose blogging style
Joel invested in new podcasting gear to fix recurring Skype audio dropout problems
money_quotes: In the spirit of Steve's extremely long blog posts, we ran about 15 minutes long this week.
posts.key_insight: This is a brief promotional post for a podcast episode with Steve Yegge, offering little substantive argument beyond announcing the guest and noting audio equipment improvements.

posts.summary: This brief post teases Joel's Inc. magazine column about the perverse effects of commission-based sales incentives. Using the example of a shoe store cashier pushing useless silicone spray, Joel sets up an argument about how commission structures lead to bad customer experiences. His father adds a parallel to education policy, noting that measurable incentives like No Child Left Behind create similar distortions.
key_points: Commission-based incentives drive employees to push products that don't serve customers
Up-selling bogus products erodes customer trust and experience
The perverse incentive problem extends beyond sales to areas like education policy (No Child Left Behind)
money_quotes: It's supposed to make sneakers shiny and waterproof, but it doesn't seem to do anything.
The same problem arises when you set measurable incentives (money for better test results) in educational policies like No Child Left Behind.
posts.key_insight: Commission and incentive structures often produce perverse outcomes, pushing people to optimize for the metric rather than the actual goal.

posts.summary: This is a minimal announcement post for Stack Overflow Podcast episode 26, where Joel Spolsky and Jeff Atwood answer listener questions. The post contains virtually no substantive content beyond a link to the podcast episode. It serves purely as a cross-posting notification on Joel's blog to direct readers to the Stack Overflow blog where the full podcast notes reside.
key_points: Episode 26 of the Stack Overflow podcast focused on answering listener questions
The post is a brief cross-promotion linking to the Stack Overflow blog
Joel and Jeff Atwood co-hosted the podcast as part of building the Stack Overflow community
money_quotes: Jeff and I devote the episode to questions from listeners.
posts.key_insight: This is a stub announcement post with no substantive content to analyze beyond its role as a podcast cross-promotion.

posts.summary: This is a brief podcast announcement post for Stack Overflow Podcast #27, featuring Reddit founders Steve Huffman and Alexis Ohanian as guests. Joel also shares a technical warning about Microsoft SQL Server 2008's full text search being significantly slower than the 2005 version due to a new architecture. The post serves primarily as a pointer to the podcast episode and a cautionary note for developers considering a database upgrade.
key_points: Stack Overflow Podcast #27 featured Reddit co-founders Steve Huffman and Alexis Ohanian as guests
Microsoft SQL Server 2008 introduced a new full text search architecture that performs significantly worse than SQL Server 2005
Software upgrades don't always mean improvements — newer versions can regress in specific areas
money_quotes: Jeff recently upgraded the database server from Microsoft SQL Server 2005 to 2008, and found pretty conclusively that 2008 has a new architecture for full text search which is significantly slower than it was in 2005.
posts.key_insight: Database version upgrades can introduce performance regressions, as demonstrated by SQL Server 2008's slower full text search compared to its predecessor.

posts.summary: This is a brief teaser post directing readers to Joel's Inc. magazine column called 'The Unproven Path.' The post hints that Joel deliberately violated seven of his own long-held principles about business and software engineering, and was surprised to find that nothing bad resulted. It suggests a reflective examination of whether established best practices are truly necessary or merely comfortable assumptions.
key_points: Joel abandoned seven long-held principles about business and software engineering simultaneously
The expected negative consequences of breaking these principles never materialized
The post suggests that even deeply held professional convictions may be worth questioning
money_quotes: I abandoned seven long-held principles about business and software engineering, and nothing terrible happened.
posts.key_insight: Long-held principles about business and software engineering may be less essential than we assume, and breaking them doesn't necessarily lead to disaster.

posts.summary: This is a very brief, lighthearted post promoting Stack Overflow Podcast #28. Joel shares a humorous review by a listener named Corey who noted Joel's conversational dominance over co-host Jeff Atwood. Joel playfully taunts Jeff and acknowledges he hasn't been blogging as much because the podcast has become his primary content outlet. He teases the episode's discussion topics with a cryptic reference to colors like Azure and Orange.
key_points: Joel's podcast with Jeff Atwood had become his primary content output, replacing regular blog posts
A listener observed that Joel dominated conversations on the podcast, outpacing Jeff Atwood
The post serves mainly as a promotional pointer to Stack Overflow Podcast episode 28
money_quotes: Jeff just couldn't keep up with the pace of Joel's conversational tennis.
Ha! Take that, Jeff "Atwood," if that's even your real name, you homicidal maniac!
As Corey discovered, the action is all on the podcast.
posts.key_insight: Joel's blogging frequency declined as the Stack Overflow podcast became his primary medium for sharing ideas and engaging with the developer community.

posts.summary: This is a minimal stub post linking to Stack Overflow Podcast #29, where Joel Spolsky and Jeff Atwood discuss video games, programming languages not in English, and hiring great programmers. The post contains essentially no substantive content beyond a brief description of the podcast topics. It serves purely as a promotional pointer to the podcast episode hosted on the Stack Overflow blog.
key_points: The podcast covers video games, non-English programming languages, and hiring great programmers
This is a cross-promotional stub post directing readers to the Stack Overflow blog
The post contains no substantive analysis or argument of its own
money_quotes: Jeff and I talk about video games, programming languages that aren't "in" English, and hiring great programmers.
posts.key_insight: This is a stub post with no substantive content, serving only as a pointer to Stack Overflow Podcast #29.

posts.summary: This is a minimal announcement post directing readers to Stack Overflow Podcast episode 30, featuring Richard White of UserVoice as a guest. The post contains virtually no substantive content beyond the link itself.
key_points: Stack Overflow Podcast episode 30 featured Richard White of UserVoice as a guest
posts.key_insight: This is a link-only announcement post with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: Spolsky warns college students about 'exploding offers' — a pressure tactic where companies impose artificially tight deadlines to prevent candidates from interviewing elsewhere. He explains that second-rate companies use this technique because they know they can't compete for top talent in a fair comparison. The post provides a three-step strategy: schedule interviews close together, push back on deadlines, and if necessary accept verbally while continuing to interview. Spolsky argues that unethical recruiters exploit students' high ethical standards and inexperience to lock them into suboptimal positions.
key_points: Exploding offers are a deliberate pressure tactic used by companies that know they're not candidates' first choice, exploiting the power imbalance between experienced recruiters and first-time job seekers.
Campus career centers try to prevent exploding offers but have limited enforcement power — the worst penalty is losing on-campus recruiting access, which companies easily work around.
Pushing back on deadlines rarely burns bridges; hiring managers actually want candidates more when they realize those candidates are in demand elsewhere.

Students should schedule interviews close together, push back on deadlines, and as a last resort accept verbally while continuing to interview — reneging is justified when a company refused to give reasonable time.
Unethical recruiters deliberately exploit students' strong sense of personal ethics and promise-keeping to gain a recruiting advantage.
money_quotes: You're going to spend several years of your life in some cold dark cubicle with a crazy boss who couldn't program a twenty out of an ATM, while some recruiter somewhere gets a $1000 bonus because she was better at negotiating than you were.

There's not a single hiring manager in the world who wants to hire you but would get mad just because you're considering other offers. It actually works the other way. When they realize you're in demand, they'll want you more.
They know that they're a second-rate company: good enough, but nobody's dream job, and they know that they can't get first-rate students unless they use pressure tactics like exploding offers.
Campus recruiters count on student's high ethical standards.

Unethical recruiters that don't care about your future and don't want you to compare different companies are going to take advantage of your ethics so they can get their bonus. And that's just not fair.
Since we instituted that 13th amendment thing, they can't force you to work for them.
posts.key_insight: Exploding offers are a cynical tactic by second-rate companies to exploit students' inexperience and ethics, and the right response is to push back on deadlines rather than accept a suboptimal job out of misplaced obligation.

posts.summary: This is a very brief blog post that serves as a teaser for Joel Spolsky's Inc. magazine column on servant leadership. Joel shares a vivid quote describing a military sergeant major who ruled through fear, inspections, and impossibly high standards—implicitly contrasting this authoritarian style with his own approach to leadership. The post uses the military anecdote as a foil to set up his argument for servant leadership in the full column.
key_points: Traditional command-and-control leadership often manifests as demanding impossible standards through fear and intimidation
The contrast between authoritarian military discipline and servant leadership is presented as a central tension
Joel uses the Inc. column format to explore management philosophy beyond his typical software-focused writing
money_quotes: He was a terror to everyone in the battalion.

Demanding impossibly high standards of order and cleanliness in what was essentially a bunch of tents in the middle of the desert—tents that were alternately dust-choked or mud-choked, depending on the rain situation.
posts.key_insight: Effective leadership is better served by supporting your team than by terrorizing them into compliance with impossible standards.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to Stack Overflow Podcast #32. Joel mentions the episode covers software piracy, site performance improvements, handling criticism, and programmer office spaces. The post contains virtually no substantive content beyond linking to the podcast episode. It serves purely as a cross-promotion notice on Joel's blog.
key_points: The podcast episode covers software piracy, performance improvements, criticism, and programmer offices
This is a link-only announcement post with no original analysis or argument
posts.key_insight: This post is a bare podcast announcement with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: Joel recounts discovering that a timesheet reporting feature he opposed was being built for FogBugz 7, only to find video evidence that he had previously approved it himself. He explains his belief that timesheet-based performance tracking leads to falsified data and undermines statistical ship-date predictions. The post becomes a self-deprecating confession about being the kind of boss who approves things vaguely and then objects when he sees the details. He concludes that his team should stop listening to him because they've thought more deeply about their features than he has.
key_points: Using timesheets as a performance metric creates perverse incentives that produce falsified data, destroying the value of time tracking for estimation purposes
Leaders who approve things in vague, general terms often fail to recognize the implications until implementation reveals the details
Good management means empowering teams to make their own decisions rather than deferring to the boss who has less context
There's a deliberate product design philosophy of not making harmful features easy or pretty, even if the raw data is available
Self-awareness about one's own limitations as a decision-maker is a rare and valuable leadership quality
money_quotes: Using timesheets as a performance metric can lead to only one thing: bad data in timesheets.
The first time your boss comes into your office and gives you grief because it looks like you only did 7 hours of work yesterday, you're going to make sure that never happens again.
Go ahead, hang yourself, but we're sure as heck not going to make it easy for you with a pretty report all tied up with a bow that you might click on by accident.
Thou shalt not put a stumbling block before the blind.
I'm turning into one of those crazy bosses that approves things, and then gets upset when you do them.
The thing is described to me in general, vague terms and it sounds great, and I say, 'sounds great!' and then I see the thing a little bit closer, and it's awful.
STOP FRIGGIN' LISTENING TO ME. I don't know what I'm talking about.
We probably hired you because you're smarter than I am.
posts.key_insight: The best leaders recognize that their team members have more context on specific decisions and should trust them to override even the boss's opinions.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief announcement post where Joel Spolsky introduces a redesign of his 'Joel on Software' blog. The entire post consists of a single sentence linking to a separate, more detailed explanation of the redesign rationale. There is no substantive argument or analysis contained in the post itself, which totals only 7 words of actual content beyond the metadata.
key_points: Joel redesigned the Joel on Software blog in December 2008
The rationale for the redesign was published in a separate linked post
The post itself is essentially a redirect notice with no standalone content
money_quotes: I've redesigned Joel on Software.
posts.key_insight: This is a minimal announcement post serving only as a pointer to a fuller explanation of the blog's redesign, containing no substantive analysis on its own.

posts.summary: Joel describes debugging CSS rendering issues on his redesigned blog, particularly with IE6's unpredictable behavior. He details fixing RSS feed display problems by inlining styles, and notes that image pixelation issues are caused by IE's poor image scaling compared to Firefox and Safari. The post is a casual, lighthearted update mixing technical gripes with office culture.
key_points: IE6 CSS rendering bugs are so unpredictable that random mutations to stylesheets are the most practical debugging approach
RSS readers aggressively strip CSS, forcing developers to inline styles for proper rendering
Browser quality directly affects user experience—Firefox and Safari handle image scaling far better than IE
money_quotes: The best way to fix CSS problems with IE6 is to generate random mutations on the style sheet until it looks fixed.
CSS is nondeterministic, and many better minds than mine have gone completely stark raving mad trying to understand the rhyme and reason of IE6 rendering bugs.
If you use a better browser, you get better results.
posts.key_insight: IE6's CSS rendering was so broken that debugging it was effectively a random process, and browser choice materially affected the quality of the web experience.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Joel Spolsky mentions he will be speaking at the Future of Web Apps conference in Miami in February 2009. He invites readers to suggest topics for his talk via email. The post contains no substantive argument or analysis, serving purely as a event notification to his audience.
key_points: Joel was invited to speak at the Future of Web Apps (FOWA) conference in Miami
He crowdsourced talk topic ideas from his blog readers
posts.key_insight: A simple conference announcement with no substantive technical or business content.

posts.summary: Joel highlights a CSS fix shared by a Flickr developer that improves image scaling quality in Internet Explorer. The fix requires adding a non-standard CSS property to enable bicubic interpolation for image resizing. Joel points out that every other browser already uses bicubic interpolation by default, making IE's choice of a lower-quality default an obvious mistake. He uses this as a small example of poor default choices in software.
key_points: IE required a non-standard CSS extension (-ms-interpolation-mode:bicubic) to achieve smooth image scaling that other browsers provided by default
Choosing the wrong default setting can degrade user experience across an entire product, even when the correct behavior is technically supported
Browser inconsistencies force web developers to add workarounds for what should be standard behavior
money_quotes: All the other browsers use bicubic interpolation for scaling by default, because that's the only thing that make sense, but IE requires a non-standard CSS extension.
posts.key_insight: Choosing poor defaults forces users and developers to work around problems that should never have existed in the first place.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky addresses early criticism of Stack Overflow by drawing a parallel to Wikipedia's early days. Critics who point out wrong answers are making the same mistake journalists made when criticizing Wikipedia articles that were already fixed by the time their columns went to print. He emphasizes that Stack Overflow is fundamentally a wiki where content improves over time through voting and editing. With 81% of traffic coming from search engines, the real value is in long-term answer quality, not initial responses.
key_points: Stack Overflow reached 8.3 million page views per month within three months of launch
Criticism of Stack Overflow mirrors early Wikipedia criticism: pointing out errors that are already being corrected by the community
81% of Stack Overflow traffic comes from search engines, meaning most value is delivered long after questions are first asked
The wiki model means answers improve over time through editing and voting, making point-in-time criticism misleading
money_quotes: Most of the criticism I've heard of Stack Overflow reminds me of the early criticism of Wikipedia: 'I went to this article and it was wrong.' By the time you read the criticism, the article has been fixed.
By the time their column appeared in print, the Wikipedia article was corrected, making a liar out of the journalist.
Most topics get most of their traffic not in the first few days, but by the Google traffic that comes in for people searching for the same exact problem.
Answers DO get better. If they don't, it's a wiki: fix them.
Instead of complaining about good answers with few votes, vote down the top answer and vote up the better answer.

My criterion for whether Stack Overflow works: when you type your question into Google, and you're happy to see a Stack Overflow result rather than a result at another one of those Q&A sites where you have to sign up and pay a monthly fee to see the answer.
posts.key_insight: Stack Overflow's value should be judged not by the accuracy of initial answers but by the long-term quality of content that improves through wiki-style community editing and voting.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky describes Fog Creek Software's move to a new, larger office at 55 Broadway in New York City after outgrowing their previous 'Bionic Office.' The company invested roughly half a million dollars beyond the landlord's construction to create an ideal workspace for software developers, featuring private offices for every developer, motorized standing desks, Herman Miller Aeron chairs, and numerous amenities. Spolsky reiterates his philosophy that private offices with doors are essential for developer productivity, preventing the interruptions endemic to open plans and cubicle farms. The post serves as both a showcase of the new space and a practical argument that investing heavily in developer workspace pays dividends through increased productivity and stronger recruiting.
key_points: Every developer, tester, and program manager gets a private office with a door, and nearly all have direct exterior windows for natural light.
Great office space serves dual purposes: it increases developer productivity by reducing interruptions, and it strengthens recruiting by impressing job candidates.
Finding pre-built office space configured for private offices is nearly impossible, so companies committed to this model must do custom interior construction.
The office includes ergonomic details like motorized height-adjustable desks, 30-inch monitors, straight desks for pair programming, and Herman Miller Aeron chairs.
Fog Creek invested about $500,000 of their own money beyond the landlord's construction budget to upgrade the space to their standards.
money_quotes: At Fog Creek our goal is building the best possible place for software developers to work.
If I wasn't intimately involved in every detail of the construction, we'd end up with the kind of life-sucking dreary cubicle hellhole made popular by the utopian workplace in 'Office Space.'

Private offices with doors that close prevent programmers from interruptions allowing them to concentrate on code without being forced to stop and listen to every interesting conversation in the room.
The nice offices wow our job candidates, making it easier for us to attract, hire, and retain the great developers we need to make software profitably.
It's worth it, especially in a world where so many software jobs provide only the most rudimentary and depressing cubicle farms.
Our ideal of giving every developer a private office is unusual, so it's almost impossible to find prebuilt office space set up that way.
posts.key_insight: Investing heavily in private offices and high-quality workspace amenities for developers pays for itself through increased productivity and a decisive recruiting advantage over companies that settle for cubicle farms.

posts.summary: Joel briefly reviews Animoto, a web service that automatically creates video presentations from uploaded photos and music. He praises its simplicity, particularly how easily it imports photos from popular sharing services. He notes the pricing tiers and mentions that rendering times are slow enough to discourage iterative tweaking.
key_points: Animoto turns photos and a soundtrack into a video presentation with minimal user effort
Integration with popular photo sharing services makes importing pictures nearly frictionless
The freemium pricing model offers 30-second videos free, with paid upgrades for length and resolution
Slow rendering times limit the ability to iterate and refine the output
money_quotes: you just point it at one of the five most popular online photo sharing services, and it shows you a list of your albums on that service. One click and all your pictures are imported
I was pretty impressed by the simplicity of the whole thing.
unless you have all day, you can't make very many adjustments before you get tired of fooling around
posts.key_insight: A tool that reduces friction by integrating with services users already use can deliver a compelling experience despite significant limitations like slow rendering.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky advises software developers applying to startups to emphasize hands-on coding experience over management credentials. He argues that startup CTOs immediately dismiss resumes filled with management buzzwords like 'architected,' 'optimized business processes,' and 'managed team of 25.' Instead, candidates should highlight concrete individual coding accomplishments. Spolsky notes that the VP and middle-management roles candidates aspire to simply don't exist at small companies, where the only things that matter are writing code and calling customers. He suspects many candidates overstate their management experience while understating their coding contributions, which backfires when applying to startups.
key_points: Startup CTOs want to see evidence of hands-on coding, not management experience or architectural oversight
Middle management roles don't exist at startups, and founders view extra managers as added expense without more code getting written
Many candidates overemphasize management and leadership on their resumes at the expense of their actual coding contributions
Startups fundamentally need only two things: code to be written and customers to be called on the telephone
Tailoring your resume to the company size and culture is essential — what impresses at a Fortune 500 company will get you rejected at a startup
money_quotes: Spare me, that's all we need, somebody running around trying to manage and optimize and architect when we just need someone who isn't afraid to write code.
The only thing we REALLY need is code to be written, and customers to be called on the telephone.
To a startup founder, middle managers just seem like added expense without more code getting written.
Those VP jobs just don't exist at startups, and the few VPs they have are the founders and a key early hire or two. Not you.
For startups, everything about your resume has to scream getting your own hands dirty.

Your resume makes you look like you're looking for the kind of job where you can call meetings that take people away from coding all day long, which, to a startup, is about as useful as a one-legged man in a butt-kicking contest.
You're kind of enjoying the nice parking space and the personal assistant and stuff, and coding? not so much.
posts.key_insight: When applying to startups, your resume must emphasize hands-on coding accomplishments over management credentials, because small companies need people who write code, not people who manage people who write code.

posts.summary: Joel announces the release of Copilot OneClick for Macintosh, a feature that lets users preinstall remote support software on frequently accessed computers for one-click connections. The post explains that Fog Creek Copilot is a simple, inexpensive remote desktop tool used for tech support, helpdesks, and even coding interviews. The new OneClick feature eliminates the need to set up a connection each time, and now works cross-platform between Windows and Mac. Joel highlights the product's ease of use, security, and low price point.
key_points: Copilot OneClick allows preinstalling remote support software for instant one-click connections to frequently helped computers
The Mac version now enables full cross-platform remote control between Windows and Mac machines
The product works through firewalls, proxies, and NATs without configuration, secured by 128-bit SSL
Pricing is accessible at $19.95/month for unlimited use, with free weekend access
money_quotes: It's perfect for ad-hoc tech support, and used extensively by helpdesks, software companies providing telephone support, and people helping their friends and families with computer problems.
Every time your dad calls up needing help with the accounting software running his Ponzi scheme, you just click one link and you're logged onto his computer.
As usual, it works through all kinds of firewalls, proxies, and NATs without any configuration.
It's cheap, by which I mean, inexpensive—I don't mean that you can just buy it two drinks and take it back to your apartment and expect to be taking a bubble bath with it.
It's even free on weekends when we have lots of unused bandwidth.
posts.key_insight: A product announcement showcasing Fog Creek's philosophy of making software that just works across platforms without requiring technical configuration from end users.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares Fog Creek Software's goals for 2009, inspired by a Seth Godin post about the importance of having goals. He lists six concrete company objectives including shipping FogBugz 7.0, launching a new product built by summer interns, and putting on the Business of Software conference. The post is notably brief and action-oriented, treating goal-setting as a straightforward exercise rather than overthinking it.
key_points: Goals are uncomfortable but essential for people who make an impact, as Seth Godin argues
Fog Creek's 2009 goals span product shipping, intern projects, conferences, and content creation
The intern goal is notably ambitious: build a new product and earn revenue before they return to school
Not having goals means never worrying about missing them — which is exactly the problem
money_quotes: Having goals is a pain in the neck.
The people who get things done, who lead, who grow and who make an impact… those people have goals.
Build a kick-ass new product from the ground up with the summer interns, and get the first dollar of customer revenue before they go back to school.
If you don't have a goal, you never have to worry about missing it.
Put on a fantastic Business of Software conference.
posts.key_insight: Setting concrete, public goals forces accountability and drives impact, even though it means risking the discomfort of missing them.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky critiques the Java installer's dialog box for filling space with meaningless, repetitive text that mentions 'Java' six times without explaining what it actually does. He argues that just because there's room on a dialog box doesn't mean you need to fill it. Referencing his earlier writing that people don't like to read, he suggests the entire dialog could be replaced with a humorous one-liner without any loss in functionality.
key_points: Empty space on a dialog box is not an invitation to fill it with meaningless copy
The Java installer repeats 'Java' six times without conveying any useful information about what Java actually is
Users don't read dialog text, so verbose filler copy is doubly wasteful
If you can't think of anything meaningful to say, say nothing — or say something brief and honest
money_quotes: Just because there's room on the dialog box, doesn't mean you have to put something there.
The Java installer starts off with a dialog box that manages to repeat the word Java six times without really telling you what it is, just that it's "everywhere."
If you can't think of anything to say, maybe you should just shut up.
This whole damn dialog could read Java Loves You—Please Wait without any loss in functionality.
posts.key_insight: UI text that says nothing useful is worse than no text at all — brevity and silence both outperform meaningless verbosity.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares a brief link to research from Australian scientists showing that open-plan offices lead to lower productivity and higher worker stress. The post reinforces his long-held position that private offices are essential for knowledge workers. By citing scientific research, he adds empirical weight to arguments he has made repeatedly throughout his blog's history. The brevity of the post suggests he views the finding as self-evident validation of his stance.
key_points: Australian scientists reviewed global research and found open-plan offices reduce productivity and increase stress
The post serves as a 'told you so' moment, reinforcing Spolsky's well-known advocacy for private offices
Scientific evidence now backs what Spolsky has argued from practical experience at Fog Creek Software
money_quotes: Australian scientists have reviewed a global pool of research into the effect of modern office design, concluding the switch to open-plan has led to lower productivity and higher worker stress.
posts.key_insight: Scientific research confirms what Spolsky has long argued: open-plan offices harm productivity and worker well-being.

posts.summary: This very brief post links to Joel Spolsky's Inc. magazine column about recognizing employee contributions. The core idea is that visible, direct contributions to revenue don't necessarily represent more value than indirect contributions from other team members. Spolsky challenges the common tendency to disproportionately reward or credit the person whose work most visibly translates into money. The post argues for a more equitable understanding of how value is created in teams.
key_points: Visible, direct contributions to revenue can overshadow equally valuable indirect contributions from other team members
Individual programmer ideas that generate money don't diminish the value added by the rest of the team
Fair recognition requires looking beyond direct revenue attribution
money_quotes: Simply because one programmer's idea translated visibly and directly into a lot of money didn't mean that the other team members weren't adding just as much value to the business, albeit in a less direct way.
posts.key_insight: The visibility of a contribution's impact on revenue is not a reliable measure of its actual value to the business.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces an open house event at Fog Creek Software's new office in New York City. After sharing photos of the new office online, many people expressed interest in visiting. The open house is scheduled for January 30th with free food and drinks. The post is a brief logistical announcement with the office address and time constraints.
key_points: Fog Creek's new office photos generated enough public interest that people asked to visit in person
The open house is a one-hour event with a strict cutoff time, reflecting organized event management
Fog Creek's office at 55 Broadway, 25th floor in New York served as a point of pride for the company
money_quotes: After I posted pictures of the new Fog Creek office, a lot of people wrote to ask if they could visit it.
posts.key_insight: A well-designed office can become a recruiting and brand-building asset that draws genuine public interest.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky points to a discussion about large companies that demand custom legal agreements for small software purchases. He highlights the absurdity of bureaucratic procurement processes that cost far more in legal overhead than the software itself. The core argument is that companies obsessive about contracts are the least likely to pirate software, making elaborate legal protections pointless for small purchases.
key_points: Big companies often demand custom legal agreements even for trivially cheap software purchases
The legal overhead of custom contracts far exceeds the value of the software being purchased
Companies obsessive about contracts are inherently the lowest piracy risk, making the process self-defeating
money_quotes: Lawyer-infested companies that are obsessive-compulsive about contracts do not pirate software and they CERTAINLY don't try to trick you into signing a contract 'allowing' them to pirate your software. This theory is laughable.
posts.key_insight: Companies that demand custom legal agreements for cheap software are the least likely to pirate it, making the entire exercise a waste of everyone's time.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky responds to a factually incorrect news story by journalist Paul Boutin in the Industry Standard, which falsely attributed an anonymous forum comment to Spolsky himself. Spolsky clarifies he does not use Google Apps and did not write the comment in question. He identifies three contributing factors: his discussion forum's visual similarity to his main blog, anonymous posting capabilities, and declining standards in tech journalism. He announces that anonymous posting will be disabled immediately.
key_points: A journalist falsely attributed an anonymous forum comment to Spolsky, building an entire story on the misattribution
The visual similarity between Spolsky's discussion forums and his main blog contributed to the confusion
Anonymous posting on the forums enabled the misattribution and will be turned off immediately
Spolsky signals broader concerns about declining standards in tech journalism, which he defers to a future post
money_quotes: A very irresponsible journalist, Paul Boutin, wrote a whole story in the Industry Standard which appears to me made up from whole cloth.
I am not using Google Apps, I did not write the comment in question, basically none of the things that Boutin said I did are true.
A part of the problem is the design of my discussion groups, which look too much like the main Joel on Software site.
Another part of the problem is The New Tech Journalism, which will have to remain a rant for another day.
posts.key_insight: Poor UX design choices—like making a discussion forum visually indistinguishable from the main site—can create real-world problems when combined with anonymous posting and careless journalism.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky and Jeff Atwood discuss the diminishing returns of strict unit testing and overly rigid object-oriented design principles like SOLID. Joel argues that 100% unit test coverage creates a maintenance burden that grows disproportionately as codebases evolve, since design changes routinely break a constant percentage of tests. He also criticizes the SOLID principles, particularly Interface Segregation and Single Responsibility, as bureaucratic over-engineering that produces millions of tiny classes nobody needs. Both conclude that what ultimately matters is delivering value to customers quickly, and that dogmatic adherence to any methodology can become counterproductive friction.
key_points: Unit test maintenance costs grow disproportionately as projects get bigger, because design changes routinely break a constant percentage of tests that must then be rewritten.
Unit tests are most valuable for stable, low-churn codebases like compilers or public APIs, where the design is fixed and breakage is catastrophic.
The SOLID principles, taken literally, lead to an explosion of tiny classes and interfaces that cost enormous time to build but provide little practical benefit for internal code.
Pragmatic software development means building imperfect things that work well, then fixing problems as they arise rather than engineering defensively against hypothetical future changes.
The ultimate measure of software quality is how quickly you can respond to real business needs and deliver value to customers, not adherence to design principles.
money_quotes: The whole idea of agile programming is not to do things before you need them, but to page-fault them in as needed.

The amount of investment you'll have to make in maintaining those unit tests, keeping them up-to-date and keeping them passing, starts to become disproportional to the amount of benefit that you get out of them.
People that say things like this have just never written a heck of a lot of code.

You're spending a lot of time in advance writing code that is just not going to be relevant, it's not going to be important. It could, theoretically, protect you against things, but, how about waiting until those things happen before you protect yourself against them?
The way real software works is that you create these very imperfect things, and they work great.
The unit of measure that really matters to me is, how quickly you can respond to real business needs with your code.
The worst thing that happens is that you get people that just stop thinking about what they're doing.
Quality really doesn't matter that much, in the big scheme of things.
posts.key_insight: Dogmatic adherence to unit testing and OO design principles like SOLID creates an ever-growing maintenance burden that, for most internal codebases, costs more than it saves — pragmatic developers should invest in engineering rigor proportionally to actual risk rather than following rules for their own sake.

posts.summary: Joel describes Fog Creek's experiment with Akamai's IP Application Accelerator service to improve the performance of Copilot, their remote desktop product. He was initially skeptical that routing packets through Akamai's network could meaningfully improve speed for a real-time application that can't use caching. After testing, they found roughly 100% throughput improvement on cross-country connections and noticeably better usability. A particularly impressive test from a hotel in Munich with terrible internet showed Copilot performing better than the hotel's native connectivity, suggesting Akamai's network routing around poor infrastructure genuinely works.
key_points: Default internet routing is not necessarily efficient — packets can travel slow, congested paths analogous to the Cross-Bronx Expressway
Akamai's IP Application Accelerator routes traffic through their private network of global nodes, improving speed even for non-cacheable real-time applications
Copilot saw roughly 100% throughput improvement on Boston-to-LA connections and usability jumped from 'tolerable' to 'pretty snappy'
The most compelling evidence came from a Munich hotel with terrible connectivity, where Akamaized Copilot outperformed the hotel's native internet access
Joel emphasizes the experiment is early and the final verdict will come from customer feedback
money_quotes: Something I knew: if you just put traffic on the Internet, it's not necessarily going to go by the most efficient route.
The default routes can be slow, clogged, and high latency. Think Cross-Bronx Expressway.
I have to admit to being extremely skeptical. Isn't that what the Internet is supposed to do anyway?
Performance is the biggest complaint about Copilot, so we were ready to try anything that increased the 'speed of light.'
Our exhaustive scientific experiments using beakers and chemicals and graph paper and slide rules proved that the usability of Copilot jumped from 'tolerable' to 'pretty snappy.'
Copilot's speed and reliability doing remote desktop was actually better than the native internet access in the hotel.
posts.key_insight: Internet routing is surprisingly inefficient by default, and services that optimize packet paths through private networks can dramatically improve real-time application performance even when caching isn't possible.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares a New York Times feature covering Fog Creek Software's new office space. The brief post highlights the NYT's recognition that Spolsky, despite being a software designer rather than an architect, demonstrated genuine design knowledge in creating the office. Spolsky links to additional resources including his own detailed write-up and a 49-picture slideshow for readers wanting more details about the space.
key_points: The New York Times featured Fog Creek's new office in their commercial real estate section
The NYT noted that Spolsky's design knowledge exceeded expectations for a software industry client
Spolsky had previously documented the office design process in detail on his blog
money_quotes: A client who claims to know something about design might be an architect's worst nightmare. But it turns out that Joel Spolsky, a software designer, author and blogger, actually knows a lot about it.
posts.key_insight: Fog Creek's investment in thoughtful office design earned mainstream media recognition, reflecting Spolsky's belief that workspace quality matters for software companies.

posts.summary: Spolsky shares a single data point from Fog Creek's sales figures suggesting the tech recession may be easing. FogBugz and Copilot sales dropped 20% during October-December 2008 but bounced back to pre-recession levels starting January 5, 2009. He notes the Joel on Software job board is down from its peak but still showing healthy activity. He acknowledges this could be a fluke and invites readers to share their own experiences.
key_points: Fog Creek saw a 20% sales decline in Q4 2008 followed by a sharp recovery in early January 2009
The Joel on Software job board dropped from ~100 to ~50 listings but still showed meaningful demand for developers
Spolsky is transparent about the limitations of drawing conclusions from a single company's data
money_quotes: October-December 2008 were terrible—sales were 20% lower than usual—but starting January 5th, we saw a significant bounce *back* to the same level of sales as we had before this recession started.
The Joel on Software job board is holding steady at about 50 jobs listed, down from a peak around 100, but there are still a significant number of openings for great developers.
posts.key_insight: Even during a broad economic downturn, demand for software tools and great developers can prove more resilient than the general market suggests.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky publishes Fog Creek Software's professional ladder, a transparent framework for determining base salaries based on three dimensions: experience, scope, and skills. Experience counts years of relevant full-time work but explicitly excludes school and penalizes repetitive tasks. Scope measures the breadth of responsibility from intern to multi-project owner. Skills measure capability independent of current role, from supervised beginner to someone critical to shipping world-class products. The final level combines all three using a formula that averages scope and skills, then cross-references with experience.
key_points: Salary levels are determined by a transparent, formulaic combination of experience, scope of responsibility, and skill level — removing subjectivity from compensation decisions
Experience is strictly defined: school doesn't count, and repetitive work like rote tech support gets collapsed into a single year regardless of actual time spent
Scope and skills are evaluated independently — you can have high skills but low scope, or vice versa — and they're averaged together to determine level
The skill progression culminates in delivering complete, fully-baked products end-to-end, not just writing code
The ladder applies uniformly across developers, QA, and sysadmins with role-appropriate criteria at each level
money_quotes: You don't have three years of experience, you have the same year experience three times over.

A developer at skill level 5 delivers complete, fully-baked products, from specs and prototypes, complete with documentation, management interfaces, polished user interfaces, automatic build routines, marketing collaterals, etc.
Has been critical to shipping a world-class product. Takes total ownership for all aspects of their project and makes many unique contributions.
Decisions have a significant impact on Fog Creek's profitability and overall well-being.
Has consistently had major success during their participation in all aspects of small and large projects and has been essential to those projects' successes.
Innovative, consistent, and contributes beyond the assigned tasks. Mentors others. Actively seeks accountability.
posts.key_insight: A transparent, formulaic career ladder that separates scope from skills and penalizes repetitive experience creates a fair compensation system while clearly communicating what growth looks like at each level.

posts.summary: This is a very brief blog post that serves as a teaser for Joel Spolsky's Inc. magazine column called 'Start-up Static.' The post consists almost entirely of a humorous quote describing the cocky phase of startup founders, where early success goes to their heads. It links out to the full column for the complete article.
key_points: Startup founders go through a phase of extreme overconfidence after initial validation
Success in startups can breed arrogance that spills into personal relationships
The post is primarily a promotional teaser for Joel's Inc. magazine column
money_quotes: At this point, you're so dang cocky that you have too much wine at Thanksgiving dinner and pointedly remind your mother-in-law about how rude she was to dismiss your start-up idea and how, when you're making millions of dollars, there will be nothing for her — she can bloody well eat frozen government cheese.
posts.key_insight: Startup founders pass through a phase of irrational cockiness where premature success inflates their ego far beyond what's warranted.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky explains the role of program manager, tracing its origin from Charles Simonyi's failed überprogrammer concept to Jabe Blumenthal's reinvention at Microsoft as a user advocate who designs UIs, writes functional specs, and coordinates teams. Drawing on his own experience as a program manager on Excel's VBA feature, Spolsky argues that the role's power comes from the creative tension between program managers and developers as peers — neither side should have authority over the other. He defends functional specifications against agile critics, arguing that iterating on a written spec is far cheaper than iterating on code. The post concludes that companies' number one mistake is letting the manager of programmers also design the product, eliminating the healthy debate that produces better designs.
key_points: Program managers must be peers with developers, not their bosses — the best designs emerge from structured debate between equals, not top-down mandates
The PM's job is to figure out what customers need and translate that into a functional spec describing user-visible behavior, not to solve technical implementation problems
Functional specs are not anti-agile — they let you iterate over designs far more cheaply than iterating over code, and force you to discover problems before writing a single line
Program managers earn developer respect through technical competence, intellectual honesty, and willingness to change their mind when evidence warrants it
The biggest organizational mistake is having the development manager or CEO also write the specs, because the design never gets a fair adversarial review
money_quotes: Lacking a program manager, your garden-variety super-smart programmer is going to come up with a completely baffling user interface that makes perfect sense IF YOU'RE A VULCAN (cf. git).
The number one mistake most companies make is having the manager of the programmers writing the specs and designing the product.
Writing a functional specification is at the very heart of agile development, because it lets you iterate rapidly over many possible designs before you write code.
You have absolute control over the state of each and every bit in the final product. What else do you need? A tiara?
If a program manager says dumb things, the programmer might flip the bozo bit on them.
My job wasn't necessarily to solve problems, it was to figure out what customers needed and make sure that programmers figured out how to solve them.
Compared to code, a written spec is trivial to change.

It's a little bit like courts of law: we don't allow a lawyer for one side to be the judge, and we work on the theory that the truth is most likely to be uncovered through a process of debate between equals.
posts.key_insight: Great software design emerges from structured adversarial debate between program managers and developers who are peers, not from a single authority dictating both the spec and the implementation.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces a live recording of the Stack Overflow podcast at Microsoft's MIX 09 conference in Las Vegas. He and Jeff Atwood will record the podcast in front of a live audience at The Venetian. The post is a brief event announcement with logistical details including date, time, and location. Attendees need conference registration but not workshop registration.
key_points: Stack Overflow podcast will be recorded live at MIX 09 conference in Las Vegas
The event is scheduled for March 17, 2009 at The Venetian
Conference registration is required but workshop registration is not
money_quotes: It's 'live' in the sense that it will be recorded in front of a live audience… you!
posts.key_insight: A brief event announcement promoting a live Stack Overflow podcast recording at MIX 09, notable mainly as a snapshot of the early Stack Overflow community-building era.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky describes his experiment with replacing traditional hard drives with Intel X25-M solid state drives to speed up developer productivity. After a developer requested weeks of engineering time to parallelize their compiler, Joel decided to try throwing money at the problem first. The SSDs dramatically improved boot times and application launching, but failed to improve the 30-second compile time since the compiler was CPU-bound rather than IO-bound. He concludes the SSD upgrades were still worthwhile for overall snappiness but acknowledges they'll still need to invest developer time in parallelizing the compiler.
key_points: When developer time is expensive and scarce, try solving performance problems with hardware upgrades before investing in engineering effort
SSDs produced dramatic improvements in boot times (2:11 to 0:34) and application launches, but had zero impact on CPU-bound compilation
Drive cloning tools are unreliable — Clonezilla was too time-consuming, and Ghost/Acronis both failed on ThinkPads due to MBR quirks
Understanding whether a bottleneck is IO-bound or CPU-bound is essential before choosing a solution, as throwing hardware at the wrong bottleneck wastes money
money_quotes: He asked if it would be OK if someone spent a few weeks looking for ways to parallelize and speed it up, since we all have multiple CPU cores and plenty of memory.
I thought it might be a good idea to just try throwing money at the problem first, before we spent a lot of (expensive and scarce) developer time.
There's an open source app called Clonezilla, which, I have to say, is only free if your time is worthless.
This little laptop is now the fastest computer I've ever used.
But… compile time. Hmm. That wasn't much better. I got it down from 30 seconds to … 30 seconds.
Our compiler is single threaded, and, I guess, a lot more CPU-bound than IO bound.
posts.key_insight: Before investing expensive developer time in optimization, try throwing money at the problem with hardware upgrades — but first understand whether the bottleneck is IO-bound or CPU-bound, or you'll solve the wrong problem.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky briefly announces that he has published a description of Fog Creek Software's compensation system for Inc. magazine. He also shares the Fog Creek professional ladder, which determines employee levels and base salaries. The post itself is essentially a pointer to two external resources rather than a substantive essay.
key_points: Fog Creek uses a structured professional ladder to determine employee levels
Base salary is computed from an employee's level on the professional ladder
Spolsky believes in transparency around compensation systems, publishing details publicly
money_quotes: I also posted the Fog Creek professional ladder which is used to compute everyone's level and thus determines their base salary.
posts.key_insight: Fog Creek uses a transparent, level-based professional ladder to systematically determine employee compensation.

posts.summary: This is a brief job posting rather than a traditional blog post. Joel Spolsky announces that Fog Creek Software is actively seeking an experienced Win32 C/C++ developer to work on their Copilot product. The post leverages Joel's blog audience as a recruiting channel, appealing directly to skilled systems programmers.
key_points: Fog Creek is continuously hiring good programmers, suggesting a permanent talent search mentality
The specific need is for Win32 (C/C++) expertise on the Copilot team, a remote desktop assistance product
Joel uses his popular blog as a direct recruiting tool, bypassing traditional job boards
money_quotes: We're always looking for good programmers at Fog Creek, but right now we could really, really use a top notch Win32 (C/C++) developer to join the Copilot team.
posts.key_insight: A well-known tech blog doubles as a powerful recruiting channel, letting companies reach exactly the kind of skilled developers they need.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the speaker lineup for the Business of Software 2009 conference. He opens with an anecdote about seeing Kathy Sierra speak at Webstock in New Zealand and being blown away. He then lists the impressive roster including Geoffrey Moore, Don Norman, Paul Graham, Heidi Roizen, and others, emphasizing that every speaker is worth attending on their own. The post is essentially a promotional piece for the conference, positioning it as a uniquely high-quality, single-track event.
key_points: Kathy Sierra's talk at Webstock was the most amazing speech Joel had ever heard, and she's headlining the 2009 Business of Software conference
The conference philosophy is to have every speaker be worth the trip, rather than one or two keynotes padded with filler
The lineup includes influential figures like Geoffrey Moore (Crossing the Chasm), Don Norman (Design of Everyday Things), and Paul Graham (Y Combinator)
The conference is a single-track format over two and a half days, priced at $1995 with an early-bird discount
money_quotes: Once in a while, someone gets up and just blows me away. I'm absolutely riveted and I start scribbling notes furiously. One or two good speakers like that, and you've got a conference that's worth going to.

Neil and I share a philosophy that instead of doing a conference with one or two keynote speakers and a lot of filler, we want to have a conference where every speaker is worth going out of your way to hear.
You won't find many successful startup founders that haven't read and learned from Moore.
With any one of these speakers, it'd be worth going to this conference. With all of them, it's a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity.
posts.key_insight: A great conference should have no filler — every speaker should independently justify attendance.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post consisting of a single sentence linking to a video of Joel Spolsky's talk at Google about Stack Overflow. The post serves purely as a pointer to external content rather than containing any substantive argument or analysis. No details about the talk's content, themes, or takeaways are provided in the post itself. It functions as a simple announcement for his audience.
key_points: Joel gave a talk at Google about Stack Overflow
The post is a minimal link-sharing announcement with no substantive written content
posts.key_insight: This post is a simple pointer to an external video and contains no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: Joel announces Stack Overflow DevDays, a series of one-day developer conferences in multiple cities priced at just $99. With Stack Overflow reaching 3.5 million monthly visitors in six months, the events aim to bring the online community together in person. Each city gets six speakers covering diverse topics from iPhone development to ASP.NET MVC to Python, deliberately crossing technology tribal lines. Joel partnered with Ryan Carson's Carsonified to keep costs radically low during the recession, when traditional $1500 conferences were being cancelled. The events sold out across all initial cities, prompting expansion from five to ten locations.
key_points: Stack Overflow hit 3.5 million unique monthly visitors in just six months, demonstrating massive developer community demand
DevDays deliberately rejected single-technology tribalism in favor of cross-platform, ecumenical programming content
The $99 price point was a direct response to the recession killing traditional $1500 developer conferences like SD West
All five original cities sold out immediately, validating both the community hunger and the low-price high-volume model
The format prioritized hardcore programming content—speakers writing code—over the typical conference fluff
money_quotes: We've starting thinking about how to get that great tribe of developers together in the real world.
This is not going to be just a Java conference or a .NET conference or a Ruby conference. This will be completely ecumenical.
Sadly, Byte disappeared, to be replaced by Mac-only magazines, IBM-PC only magazines, even Microsoft SQL Server-only magazines.
The conference is for programmers. The conversation is going to be hard core. Speakers are going to be writing code.
Conference centers can charge you $1000 for one urn of coffee.
You can bring your whole team for less than the cost of a normal conference.
posts.key_insight: By pricing developer conferences at $99 instead of $1500 and mixing topics across technology tribes, you can tap into massive latent demand that traditional conferences miss—especially during economic downturns.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the public beta launch of Server Fault, a Q&A site for system administrators built on the same engine as Stack Overflow. He explains that while Stack Overflow was designed specifically for programmers, the underlying Q&A platform with voting, editing, and tagging was clearly applicable to other fields. System administration was chosen as the first expansion because programmers frequently do sysadmin work themselves, and the domain is full of obscure, hard-to-discover solutions that benefit from community knowledge sharing.
key_points: Server Fault extends the Stack Overflow Q&A engine to system and network administration
System administration was chosen as the first expansion because it overlaps with programming and relies heavily on tribal knowledge
Stack Overflow accounts carry over but reputation, badges, and tags are kept separate per site
The post illustrates sysadmin pain points with concrete examples like finding processes holding files open
money_quotes: The engine behind the site, the Q&A engine with voting, editing, and tagging, could obviously be used in a lot of other professions.
It's the perfect domain for a Q&A engine… there are a million detailed problems that depend highly on lore to get right.
There's no way to accidentally discover aspnet_regiis.exe -I until someone shows you the trick.
As programmers, we often end up doing system administration ourselves.
I am sorry to inform you that you may be a system administrator or IT professional.
posts.key_insight: System administration is an ideal domain for community Q&A because its solutions depend on obscure tribal knowledge that is nearly impossible to discover independently.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the expansion of Stack Overflow DevDays from five cities to ten due to overwhelming demand. The initial five-city event sold out quickly with over 2,388 registrations, including 800+ in London alone. New cities added include Boston, Austin, Los Angeles, Cambridge UK, and Amsterdam. The post details the schedule, pricing, and speaker lineup including Joel himself, Jeff Atwood, Scott Hanselman, and Jon Skeet, covering topics like Android, Python, iPhone development, and jQuery.
key_points: Stack Overflow DevDays sold out its initial five-city run so quickly that five additional cities were added to meet demand
Over 2,388 developers registered worldwide, demonstrating the strength of the Stack Overflow community's desire to connect in person
The conferences were priced accessibly at $99 US with discounted student tickets, positioning them as affordable developer events
The event doubled as a launch platform for FogBugz 7 and a new Fog Creek intern project, blending community building with product marketing
money_quotes: We've got 2388 people booked worldwide so far, including over 800 in London.

It's clear that with just five events we weren't going to be able to accomodate all the people who want to spend a day meeting online Stack Overflow friends in real life and learning a little something about some hot new programming topics.
This will be the launch event for FogBugz 7 and a new product now under development by the Fog Creek summer interns, who just arrived and are already coding away earnestly.
At the current rate, it's pretty clear these conferences will sell out long before the events themselves, so don't wait until the last minute to decide.
posts.key_insight: The rapid sellout of Stack Overflow DevDays demonstrated that online developer communities create genuine demand for in-person connection, validating Stack Overflow's community-building approach.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky explains why New York City is not included in the StackOverflow DevDays conference tour. The two main reasons are prohibitively high costs for venues, catering, and hotels, and surprisingly low attendance from New York's tech community. He cites specific examples like $23 per person just for a coffee break and hotel rooms at $600-700 per night. Despite New York having a large software development community, Joel notes that turnout for tech events there has consistently disappointed compared to other cities.
key_points: New York venue and catering costs make a $99 one-day conference financially impossible, with even basic coffee breaks costing $23 per person.
New York tech community attendance at events is paradoxically low despite the city's large developer population — London drew three times the attendees for the same FogBugz event.
The city's tourist attractions actively compete with conference sessions for attendees' attention.
High hotel costs ($600-700/night) compound the problem by making conferences expensive for both organizers and attendees.
money_quotes: It's simply impossible to do a $99 one-day event in New York.
I don't know why, but techies in New York just don't turn out for events at the same level as other cities.
When we did the FogBugz world tour we had three times as many attendees in London as New York.
And half of your attendees wander off to visit the Statue of Liberty when they should be in your Python tutorial meeting.
posts.key_insight: New York's combination of exorbitant costs and paradoxically low tech event attendance makes it one of the worst cities for affordable developer conferences.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky examines the failure of Clear, an airport security line-skipping service that paid for expensive background checks even after the TSA refused to let pre-screened travelers bypass security screening. Clear's original business model was to let vetted travelers skip security entirely, but when that proved impossible, they kept doing costly background checks for a service that only let people cut in line. Spolsky argues this was organizational dysfunction — the inability to adapt a business model to changed circumstances. He notes the irony that Clear might have been profitable if they had simply dropped the unnecessary background checks.
key_points: Clear's intended business model (bypassing security screening entirely) was never approved by the TSA, yet they continued operating as if it would be
The TSA's security theater is absurd — screening pilots for shampoo bombs when they already control the aircraft
Organizations can fail by refusing to re-evaluate their business model when key assumptions prove false
Clear could likely have survived as a profitable line-skipping service if they had dropped the expensive, now-purposeless background checks
Thoughtless optimism — continuing to execute a plan everyone knows is broken — is a distinct form of organizational dysfunction
money_quotes: You still went through the X-ray machine and had to remove your shoes, belt, pocket contents, laptops, and plastic quart ziplock bag of toiletries. You just got to cut to the front of the line.

The TSA doesn't even trust pilots, who go through the same screening as the rest of us to make sure they're not bringing something extraordinarily dangerous onto a plane like a 3.5 oz bottle of shampoo.

With a little bottle of shampoo, they could make a bomb, which they could use to fly the plane they are piloting into a building, something that is impossible for mere pilots sitting at the controls of the jet.
At this point, a rational businessperson would say, 'Well, does the Clear idea still make sense if we can't actually let you skip the screening?'
The environment changed. It turns out that Clear's business model of prescreening wasn't going to be possible. But they kept doing it anyway.
What kind of organizational dysfunction does it take to completely ignore the changed circumstances and keep at a money-losing business?
Clear could probably have been profitable if they had just skipped the one unnecessarily stupid part of their business model: the detailed background checks on all their customers.
They had a business model, the business model wasn't actually possible, everybody knew it, and they still plugged away at it. Thoughtless optimism.
posts.key_insight: When a key assumption in your business model proves false, continuing to execute the original plan unchanged isn't perseverance — it's organizational dysfunction.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares a brief post linking to Mencius Moldbug's analysis of why Wolfram Alpha fails as a product. The core argument, conveyed entirely through the quoted passage, is that users cannot form accurate mental models of what Wolfram Alpha can and cannot do. Because the system is built on a million lines of constantly changing code, users' attempts to predict its capabilities are inherently inadequate and error-prone. Joel lets the quote speak for itself without additional commentary.
key_points: Users fail with Wolfram Alpha because they cannot build accurate mental models of what the system understands
The gap between a complex system's actual capabilities and users' expectations creates a fundamentally broken user experience
A system that requires users to guess what it can do — and constantly re-learn as it changes — imposes an unreasonable cognitive burden
money_quotes: They create an incomplete model of the giant electronic brain in their own, non-giant, non-electronic brains.
Since the giant electronic brain is a million lines of code which is constantly changing, this is a painful, inadequate and error-prone task.
posts.key_insight: A product fails when users cannot form a reliable mental model of what it can do, forcing them to guess at its capabilities through painful trial and error.

posts.summary: Joel announces the release of FogBugz 7, describing how the team recovered from a disorganized development process by resetting plans and writing a focused vision statement in August 2008. The vision centered on three pillars: 'fruity treats' (small customer-requested improvements), customization (a plugin architecture), and 'supersonics' (performance improvements via .NET/Mono). The team used Evidence Based Scheduling to ship on time within one year. Joel credits the team rather than himself and highlights how a concise, shared vision statement made prioritization easy by defining what was out of scope as much as what was in.
key_points: A concise vision statement that defines what's NOT in the product is as valuable as defining what IS in it, enabling sharp prioritization across a year-long project
Evidence Based Scheduling worked well on a large one-year project, giving the team confidence to both cut features and add new ones like Scrum support
The team recovered from a failed strategy of splitting FogBugz into two products by merging teams and refocusing on a single, comprehensible release
The plugin architecture replaced the unsatisfying answer of 'you get the source code, so you can modify it' with a sustainable extensibility model
Joel positions himself as increasingly removed from hands-on development, crediting the growing professional team for the product's quality
money_quotes: One of the best things we did as a development team was to write a short, concise, comprehensible vision statement that got everybody exactly on the same page about what we were going to do over the course of a year.
The vision statement made it easy to prioritize. Instead of just telling us what was in the product, it also gave us a way to know what was out.
No more HALF-ASSED features (I'm looking at you, logo customization in FogBugz 6.1).
If it's NOT ON THAT LIST it's NOT IN THE PRODUCT. Get used to it.
They know perfectly well that if they modify our source code, terribobble tragedies will occur the minute we release a service pack.

I wish I could take more credit for it, but the truth is, Fog Creek has grown. We have a very professional team with testers, program managers, and developers, and I just sort of sit here agog at what a brilliant job they're doing.
I'm just the Michael Scott character who wastes everybody's time whenever I venture out of my office.
posts.key_insight: A sharp, concise vision statement that explicitly defines what's out of scope is the most effective tool for keeping a development team aligned and on schedule over a long project.

posts.summary: This is a very brief announcement post where Joel links to Brett Kiefer's article about Evidence Based Scheduling 2.0 on the FogBugz blog. The update adds support for strict dependencies and start dates to FogBugz's scheduling system. Joel quotes a humorous example using Back to the Future references to illustrate the dependency feature. The post is essentially a product update pointer with no substantive argument.
key_points: EBS 2.0 adds strict task dependencies and start dates to FogBugz's scheduling system
The post is a brief link to the FogBugz blog rather than an original essay
Evidence Based Scheduling was a key feature of FogBugz that Joel had previously written about extensively
money_quotes: EBS 2.0 gives you the vocabulary of strict dependencies and start dates.
posts.key_insight: Evidence Based Scheduling evolved to support task dependencies and start dates, making project scheduling more realistic.

posts.summary: This is a very brief teaser post directing readers to Joel's Inc. magazine column where he retells the early story of Fog Creek Software. The post opens with a satirical quote mocking dot-com era optimism about how every industry would be disrupted and startups would make everyone rich. The column apparently covers the founding narrative of Fog Creek during a turbulent period for the software industry. The post itself is too short to contain a full argument, serving primarily as a pointer to the longer Inc. piece.
key_points: The post satirizes dot-com bubble enthusiasm where every industry was supposedly going to be revolutionized by the internet
Joel frames the founding of Fog Creek Software against the backdrop of an industry upheaval or downturn
The title 'The Day My Industry Died' suggests the column covers the dot-com bust and its impact on software businesses
money_quotes: Every single industry was going to be turned upside down! New industries would be created! Start-ups would make people rich! Which is really nice, because it's awesome to be rich! And, bonus: It'll never be winter again!
posts.key_insight: The dot-com era's boundless optimism about startups and disruption was absurdly overhyped, and building a real software company meant surviving the inevitable crash that followed.

posts.summary: This is a brief promotional post where Joel Spolsky congratulates Bob Walsh on publishing his book 'Web Startup Success Guide,' for which Joel wrote the foreword. He also highlights an interview with David Allen (GTD Guru) that appears as chapter 8 of the book and is available to read online. The post is essentially a book recommendation and signal boost for a colleague's work.
key_points: Joel wrote the foreword for Bob Walsh's 'Web Startup Success Guide'
The book includes an interview with GTD expert David Allen as chapter 8
Joel uses his platform to promote work by people in his professional network
money_quotes: Congratulations to Bob Walsh on publishing his Web Startup Success Guide (to which I wrote the foreword).
posts.key_insight: A short endorsement post promoting Bob Walsh's book on web startups, notable mainly for Joel's involvement as foreword author.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky promotes Seth Godin's keynote from the 2008 Business of Software conference, highlighting Godin's insight that mass-market advertising leads to mediocre products. He emphasizes Godin's reputation as a transformative speaker whose seminars command premium prices and sell out instantly. The post shares a link to watch the full hour-long talk online.
key_points: Mass-market advertising forces companies toward average products for average people
Seth Godin's speaking ability commands premium pricing ($1650) with instant sellouts
The Business of Software conference made Godin's keynote available for free online
money_quotes: If you're going to interrupt everybody with an ad, it better be something everybody wants to buy. So what do you end up with? Average products for average people.
posts.key_insight: Mass-market advertising pushes companies toward building average products for average people rather than remarkable products for specific audiences.

posts.summary: Joel announces that Red Gate Software has launched a startup incubator in Cambridge, England, offering free office space, internet, room, board, advice, and pocket money. Unlike typical incubators, Red Gate isn't taking equity in the companies it helps. Joel is one of the advisors involved. The incubator's philosophy is based on building relationships with smart people, with the belief that long-term benefits will naturally follow.
key_points: Red Gate Software launched a no-strings-attached startup incubator in Cambridge, England
The incubator provides comprehensive support (office space, internet, room, board, advice, pocket money) without taking equity
Red Gate's strategy is relationship-driven: they believe proximity to smart people doing interesting things will create future opportunities naturally
The model rejects transactional incubator norms in favor of long-term relationship building
money_quotes: For a first, it's really free; Red Gate isn't taking stock in the companies it helps.
We think that getting to know smart people doing interesting things will, in the long term, be good for Red Gate.
Ultimately, all deals come down to relationships. So we want to build them.
posts.key_insight: The most valuable thing an established company can do for startups is invest in relationships without demanding equity, trusting that proximity to smart people will create its own returns.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces a half-day startup workshop attached to the Business of Software 2009 conference in San Francisco. The workshop features small-group sessions moderated by successful software founders including Red Gate's Neil Davidson and Simon Galbraith, Fog Creek's Joel Spolsky and Michael Pryor, and HubSpot's Dharmesh Shah. The format is informal, focused on Q&A and problem-solving for early-stage software founders. The event concludes with a live taping of Jason Calacanis's 'This Week in Startups' podcast. While the workshop itself is free, attendees must register for the main conference.
