key_points: The workshop is structured as three 90-minute small-group sessions with experienced software founders, limited to 72 participants total
All three moderating teams represent bootstrapped or lightly-funded software companies, emphasizing practical non-VC startup experience
Red Gate's Springboard incubator is highlighted as a novel model that provides resources to startups without taking equity
The event is positioned as accessible and conversational rather than lecture-based, targeting founders who feel 'dazed and confused'
money_quotes: This would be a terrific event to attend if you've recently started a software company and feel dazed, confused, or just want to bounce ideas off of someone who's been there.
It was founded with no VC and little debt.
The format is very open. It's a chance to chat, bounce ideas around, ask questions, solve specific problems, get feedback, and learn from each other.

Bootcamp is where you run around in circles for 20 weeks without getting more than four hours of sleep a night while drill sergeants barely a year older than you foam at the mouth and berate you endlessly.
This will be more of a friendly conversation with successful software startup founders. Not bootcamp.
posts.key_insight: Early-stage software founders benefit most from small-group, informal access to experienced founders who have built companies without relying on venture capital.

posts.summary: Joel announces a simple programming challenge for the Stack Overflow community: create a full-screen countdown timer application for use during StackOverflow DevDays conference intermissions. The app needs to display on the main screen to warn attendees when sessions are about to resume. He frames it as both a practical need for keeping the packed conference schedule on time and as a way to showcase the community's programming talent. The contest is hosted on Meta Stack Overflow.
key_points: StackOverflow DevDays was a series of one-day mini-conferences held in ten cities, requiring tight schedule management
Joel turned a practical conference need into a community engagement opportunity by posting it as a contest
The challenge demonstrates Joel's philosophy of leveraging community talent for real-world problems
money_quotes: I thought this would be a great opportunity to showcase the StackOverflow community's programming talent, so I posted a little contest over on meta.
posts.key_insight: Even mundane operational needs like a countdown timer can be turned into community engagement opportunities that showcase developer talent.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that computer science programs fail to teach practical software development skills like version control, teamwork, and project management. He highlights UCOSP, a consortium of Canadian universities organizing cross-university capstone projects on open source software. He then diagnoses why student projects consistently fail to deliver useful results: students have 16 years of experience procrastinating and no experience with long-term team schedules. The solution, he argues, is weekly deadlines and milestones, since long-term deadlines without short-term checkpoints are rarely met — a problem that affects professional programmers just as much as students.
key_points: CS departments have become divorced from real-world software development by prioritizing theory over practical skills like version control, teamwork, and debugging
Student capstone projects consistently fail because students have zero experience managing long-term, team-based schedules and default to last-minute cramming
The 'interesting' academic portion of code is only 10% of the work — the other 90% needed to make software survive real users is never taught
Long-term deadlines without short-term milestones are rarely met, whether by students or professional programmers
The key difference between student teams and professional teams isn't discipline — it's that professional teams have managers who set frequent intermediate deadlines
money_quotes: Many universities have managed to convince themselves that the more irrelevant the curriculum is to the real world, the more elite they are.
College students in their final year have about 16 years of experience doing short projects and leaving everything until the last minute.
Until you're a senior in college, you're very unlikely to have ever encountered an assignment that can't be done by staying up all night.

The other 90% of the work that it takes to bring code up to the level of 'useful, real-world code' is never expected from undergrads, because it's not 'interesting' to fix bugs and deal with real-world conditions.
It appears to be a permanent part of the human condition that long term deadlines without short term milestones are rarely met.
Students have exactly zero experience with long term, team-based schedules. Therefore, they almost always do crappy work when given a term-length project and told to manage their time themselves.

The only reason the real world gets this right where all-student college teams fail is because in the real world there are managers, who can set deadlines, which a team of students who are all peers can't pull off.
posts.key_insight: Long-term deadlines without frequent short-term milestones fail universally — for students and professionals alike — and the real value of management is imposing that cadence of regular deliverables.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares a brief link post pointing readers to Adam Bosworth's writing about standards design. He highlights Bosworth's core principle that successful standards prioritize simplicity over complexity. Joel endorses the linked piece as 'required reading,' signaling strong agreement with the argument. The post is essentially a curated recommendation rather than an original essay.
key_points: Successful standards succeed through simplicity, not comprehensiveness or complexity
Joel endorses Adam Bosworth's perspective as essential reading for technologists
The post reflects Joel's long-held preference for simplicity in software design and standards
money_quotes: All successful standards are as simple as possible, not as hard as possible.
posts.key_insight: The best technical standards win by being as simple as possible rather than as comprehensive as possible.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky describes Fog Creek Software's nine-year journey to articulate its mission, inspired by Kathy Sierra's framework of 'help your users be awesome at something.' He recounts how the company started with the goal of being a great workplace for developers in New York City, but eventually realized that everything they did successfully—the blog, Stack Overflow, FogBugz, conferences—had one common thread: helping software developers make better software. This clarity of mission helped explain past failures (CityDesk flopped because it wasn't for developers) and guided future product decisions like adding source control and code review to FogBugz.
key_points: Kathy Sierra's framework—'We help $TYPE_OF_PERSON be awesome at $THING'—is a powerful litmus test for whether a company will create passionate users.
Fog Creek's original mission of being a great workplace was accomplished but made for a terrible elevator pitch and didn't inspire customers.
Every successful Fog Creek initiative (Joel on Software, Stack Overflow, FogBugz, DevDays) shared a common thread: helping developers make better software.
A clear mission statement retroactively explains both successes and failures—CityDesk flopped because it had nothing to do with developers.
Mission clarity makes product roadmap decisions straightforward: if it helps developers make better software, it belongs in FogBugz.
money_quotes: If you can't explain your mission in the form, 'We help $TYPE_OF_PERSON be awesome at $THING,' you are not going to have passionate users.
The tagline was 'building the company where the best software developers want to work.' It was, to say the least, awkward.
Abercrombie and Fitch: building the apparel store where the hottest teenagers will want to work. Who cares? Not the hot teenagers, I'll tell you that.
Everything we've done successfully has one thing in common: It's all about helping software developers be awesome at making software.
I'm not writing to promote our products. You don't have to buy our products to get the benefit of reading about my experience designing them and building them and selling them.
Our focus on helping developers explains why one of our early products, CityDesk, flopped: it had nothing to do with software developers.
It took almost ten years, but I think we finally got the mission for the next ten nailed.
posts.key_insight: A company's mission should be framed around helping a specific type of person become awesome at something, and that clarity retroactively explains past failures and guides future decisions.

posts.summary: This is a brief teaser post directing readers to Joel Spolsky's Inc. magazine column about the challenges of growing a sales team. Joel admits that despite his expertise in hiring programmers, he has struggled to build out the sales side of his business. The post hints at a broader discussion about whether slow company growth is a fatal problem. The self-deprecating humor suggests the Inc. column explores the tension between technical founders' comfort with engineering hiring versus their discomfort with sales hiring.
key_points: Technical founders often struggle with building non-engineering teams, particularly sales
Expertise in hiring programmers doesn't translate to expertise in hiring salespeople
Slow growth in sales capacity is a real concern for software companies
money_quotes: For a guy who wrote a book on how to hire great programmers, it's mortifying how incompetent I've been at enlarging the sales team, which, right now, consists of one terrific account executive and a dog.
I'm just kidding. There's no dog.
posts.key_insight: Technical founders who excel at hiring engineers can be surprisingly bad at building sales teams, and that gap can threaten a company's growth.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces Stack Overflow Careers, a job platform that inverts the traditional hiring model by letting employers browse developer CVs rather than having developers search job listings. He motivates the launch by observing that at DevDays conferences, only 25% of elite programmers report being truly satisfied with their jobs, which he considers unacceptable given how productive top developers are. The platform ties into Stack Overflow profiles so developers can demonstrate their skills through community contributions rather than traditional resumes. Joel argues that software hiring should work like Hollywood talent scouting, not like union hiring halls, because developers are not interchangeable.
key_points: At DevDays, only 25% of elite programmers reported being truly satisfied with their jobs, while another 25% actively hated theirs
Stack Overflow Careers inverts the job market: employers browse developer CVs rather than developers browsing job listings
A developer's Stack Overflow contributions and peer reputation are more meaningful hiring signals than traditional resumes
The platform deliberately targets only the top 25,000 active Stack Overflow contributors, not the millions of visitors
Top developers produce ten times as much work as average developers, so employers should compete harder to attract them
money_quotes: I've been saying for ten years that the top developers have a choice of where to work, and the top employers need to work harder to attract them, because the top developers get ten times as much work done as the average developers.
And yet, I still keep meeting ridiculously productive developers working in shitholes.
This is a talent market. Developers are not even remotely interchangeable. Therefore, recruiting should work like Hollywood, not like union hiring halls of the last century.
In a union hiring hall, downtrodden workers line up like cogs, hoping to make it to the front of the line in time to get a few bucks for dinner.

To a hiring manager, the fact that you took the time to help a fellow programmer with a detailed answer in some obscure corner of programming knowledge, and demonstrated mastery, is a lot more relevant than the Latin Club you joined in school.
It's not for the 5.2 million people who visit Stack Overflow; it's for the top 25,000 developers who participate actively.
posts.key_insight: The best developers are chronically underserved by the job market, and hiring should be flipped so employers compete for talent by browsing developer portfolios rather than developers submitting resumes into a void.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the employer side of Stack Overflow Careers, a programmer search engine he's long wished for. He argues that the core hiring problem isn't evaluating programmers but finding them in the first place. The platform lets employers search for candidates by skill, location, and relocation willingness, with Stack Overflow reputation as a signal of programming ability. He acknowledges the chicken-and-egg challenge of building a two-sided marketplace and notes that early beta employers have already found promising candidates. The post serves as both a product launch announcement and a demonstration of the search functionality's practical value.
key_points: The hardest part of hiring programmers is finding candidates, not evaluating them in interviews
Stack Overflow Careers differentiates from traditional job boards by showing only active job seekers with verifiable programming skills via Stack Overflow reputation
Two-sided marketplaces face a chicken-and-egg problem requiring simultaneous critical mass of both employers and candidates
The platform reduces friction by letting employers search and see candidate counts before paying
Location flexibility and relocation willingness dramatically expand the candidate pool for any given search
money_quotes: The problem I've had with hiring programmers was not interviewing them or deciding if they're smart—it's been finding them in the first place.
What I've dreamed about is a programmer search engine.
If you've ever emailed someone based on a resume you found through a traditional search engine, you've probably discovered that they're not actually on the market.
It would show you something about their programming skills besides the usual resume blahblah.
It's like a junior high school dance, with the boys on one side of the gym and the girls on the other side, and for a while you just sit there holding your breath to see if anyone will dance.
posts.key_insight: The biggest bottleneck in hiring programmers isn't the interview process but the discovery problem—finding qualified candidates who are actually looking for work—which a purpose-built search engine tied to a developer community can solve.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky reflects on the limits of hands-off management, noting that while it works well with trusted internal teams of smart, dedicated people, it fails when working with outside vendors. He and his co-founder Ryan discovered this during a trip, prompting them to reconsider when micromanagement is actually appropriate. The post serves as a teaser for his full Inc. magazine column on the topic.
key_points: Hands-off management works well with smart, dedicated internal staff but breaks down with external vendors
Even experienced entrepreneurs must continuously learn and adapt their management style to different contexts
Micromanagement, usually seen as negative, has legitimate use cases depending on who you're managing
money_quotes: We're both used to hiring very smart and dedicated people who will get things done to a high standard if you give them some general direction and set them free.
This style of hands-off management, which works so well with our own staffs, just wasn't working when we had outside vendors involved.
posts.key_insight: The right management style depends on context: hands-off leadership that empowers internal teams can fail completely when applied to external vendors, making selective micromanagement a necessary skill.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares statistics about Stack Overflow's growth and its relationship to Stack Overflow Careers. He notes that higher-reputation users are disproportionately likely to submit CVs, which validates the platform's pitch to employers. Stack Overflow has reached 6 million monthly unique visitors, making it arguably the largest programming site in the world. Despite this scale, the site struggles to attract major advertising deals because it refuses to run animated or aggressive ad formats, a trade-off Joel embraces as worth the lost revenue.
key_points: Higher Stack Overflow reputation correlates strongly with participation in Stack Overflow Careers, validating the platform's value proposition to employers
Stack Overflow reached the 740th ranked site globally with 6 million monthly uniques and steady growth
Stack Overflow appears to have more traffic than any other programming site, including MSDN
The site refuses animated ads on principle, costing significant advertising revenue from agencies running major campaigns like the Visual Studio 2010 launch
Advertising agencies prefer aggressive, animated ad formats and are uninterested in sites that only offer static placements
money_quotes: When you search for CVs on Stack Overflow, you are looking at some pretty gosh darn good programmers.

We don't accept any kind of animated ads on Stack Overflow, because, well, they're evil, so we lose a lot of revenue from advertising agencies who are looking for the most aggressive possible ways to get in people's faces.
Whatever. Don't care. We hate animated ads and I'm pretty sure our users do, too.
Agencies really don't like us. Now if we go to video units with fly-over… oh they'll start loving us!
Are there any sites out there for programmers with more traffic than Stack Overflow? I haven't found any.
posts.key_insight: Stack Overflow's willingness to sacrifice advertising revenue by refusing animated ads reflects a user-first philosophy that, paradoxically, helped build the massive engaged audience that makes the platform valuable in the first place.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that the industry's focus on 'backups' sets the bar too low for data protection. He contends that having backups is meaningless if you can't actually restore from them. Through several real-world failure scenarios, he demonstrates how backups can fail silently at restore time. He proposes reframing the conversation entirely: instead of asking whether you have backups, ask whether you've successfully performed a restore.
key_points: Backups are necessary but insufficient — the real test is whether you can restore from them
Many backup failures are only discovered at restore time, such as encryption keys stored on the lost machine, silent file truncation, or missing configuration data
The minimum bar for a reliable service should be demonstrating a full restore to new infrastructure without accessing the original data center
Reframing the question from 'do you back up?' to 'do you restore?' changes the standard of practice
money_quotes: Doing a backup is too low a bar. Any experienced system administrator will tell you that they have a great backup plan, the trouble comes when you have to restore.
The minimum bar for a reliable service is not that you have done a backup, but that you have done a restore.
Let's stop asking people if they're doing backups, and start asking if they're doing restores.
The backed-up files were encrypted with a cryptographically-secure key, the only copy of which was on the machine that was lost
Your backups were on an LTO drive which was lost with the data center, and you can't get another LTO drive for three days
posts.key_insight: A backup you've never restored from is just a hope — the true measure of data protection is proving you can rebuild everything from scratch on new infrastructure.

posts.summary: Spolsky mocks a Microsoft job posting filled with incomprehensible internal jargon and acronyms, arguing it reveals how insular the company has become. He notes the irony that Microsoft needs a team to compete against open-source alternatives, yet can't even communicate clearly with the outside world. He draws a parallel to IBM's notorious habit of using proprietary terminology for common concepts, which Microsoft itself used to ridicule. The core problem isn't that technical people speak in jargon—it's that even Microsoft's marketing people have become unintelligible and don't realize it.
key_points: Microsoft's job postings are so laden with internal acronyms (KT, WHI, v-team, Steve B) that outsiders cannot parse them
The insularity of a 93,000-person company creates a self-reinforcing bubble where nobody notices they've stopped communicating clearly
Microsoft has become the very thing it once mocked IBM for—an organization with its own incomprehensible private vocabulary
The real danger isn't technical jargon among engineers, but marketing people who don't realize they're speaking a language only insiders understand
money_quotes: The whole reason Microsoft even needs a v-team of 13, um, "V DASHES" to compete against Open Office is that they've become so insular that their job postings are full of incomprehensible jargon and acronyms which nobody outside the company can understand.

With 93,000 employees, nobody ever talks to anyone outside the company, so it's no surprise they've become a bizarre borg of "KT", "Steve B", "v-team", "high WHI," CSI, GM, BG, BMO (bowel movements?) and whatnot.
When I worked at Microsoft almost two decades ago we made fun of IBM for having a different word for everything.
Everybody said, "Hard Drive," IBM said "Fixed Disk." Everybody said, "PC," IBM said "Workstation."
But at Microsoft you can't even understand the marketing people, and, what's worse, they don't seem to know that they're speaking in their own special language, understood only to them.
posts.key_insight: When a company grows so large and insular that even its marketing people communicate in impenetrable internal jargon without realizing it, the organization has lost the ability to connect with the outside world.

posts.summary: Joel argues that as companies grow and employees specialize, managers often misdiagnose their communication problems. They think they need more coordination and alignment meetings, when the real issue is that too many people are inserting themselves into decisions that already have capable owners. The post is a brief pointer to his Inc. magazine column expanding on this idea.
key_points: Company growth leads to specialization, which creates perceived communication gaps
Managers often misdiagnose the problem as needing more alignment when they actually need less interference
Having smart people already working on something means others should step back, not pile on
money_quotes: As companies expand, the people within them start to specialize.
Some managers will conclude that they have a 'keep everyone on the same page' problem.
But often what they actually have is a 'stop people from meddling when there are already enough smart people working on something' problem.
posts.key_insight: The real scaling problem in growing companies isn't insufficient communication — it's too many people meddling in work that already has competent owners.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky briefly recommends Steve Krug's new book 'Rocket Surgery Made Easy,' a follow-up to the usability classic 'Don't Make Me Think.' He describes it as a concise and fun guide to running simple hallway usability tests. The post serves as a short book endorsement rather than a detailed review. Spolsky highlights the book's practical focus on improving software and website usability through lightweight testing methods.
key_points: Steve Krug's 'Rocket Surgery Made Easy' is a sequel to the influential 'Don't Make Me Think'
The book focuses on running simple 'hallway' usability tests as a practical approach to improving usability
Spolsky endorses the book as concise, fun, and highly recommended
money_quotes: Rocket Surgery Made Easy is a terrific, short, concise, fun guide to running simple "hallway" usability tests to improve the usability of your software and websites.
posts.key_insight: Simple hallway usability testing, as outlined in Steve Krug's latest book, remains one of the most practical approaches to improving software and website usability.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that testers serve a critical role by providing immediate feedback to programmers, drawing an analogy to dog training where quick reinforcement is essential for learning. He contends that testers should not simply be failed programmers or automation script writers, but smart, methodical people who evaluate software holistically. The post pushes back against the trend of making testers write automated test suites, arguing this crowds out their real job of evaluating code and providing both positive and negative feedback. Spolsky concludes that testing is an underappreciated career path that suits people who love software but don't necessarily love programming.
key_points: Fast feedback loops are essential for programmer improvement, and dedicated testers provide this better than waiting months for customer responses
Positive reinforcement from testers is just as valuable as bug reports — programmers need to know when they've done something right
Over-emphasizing automated test suites causes testers to miss UI issues, bad design choices, and inconsistencies that only human evaluation catches
Hiring failed programmer candidates as testers poisons the testing team — you need smart people, not people who couldn't cut it elsewhere
Testing is a distinct skill set requiring scientific thinking and puzzle-solving aptitude, not programming ability
money_quotes: For programmers, getting better at what you do requires quick feedback, positive and negative, on what you've just done.

There is no better way to improve a programmer's morale, happiness, and subjective sense of well-being than to have dedicated testers who get frequent releases from the developers, try them out, and give negative and positive feedback.
If you put too much emphasis on those scripts, you won't notice misaligned text, hostile user interfaces, bad color choices, and inconsistency.
Worse, you'll have a culture of testers frantically working to get their own code working, which crowds out what you need them to do: evaluate someone else's code.
A particularly terrible idea is to offer testing jobs to the programmers who apply for jobs at your company and aren't good enough to be programmers.
If you spend long enough acting like a tester is just an incompetent programmer, eventually you're building a team of incompetent programmers, not a team of competent testers.
Since testing can be taught on the job, but general intelligence can't, you really need very smart people as testers, even if they don't have relevant experience.
posts.key_insight: Testers exist primarily to give programmers fast, human feedback — both positive and negative — and treating testing as either failed programming or pure automation misses the point entirely.

posts.summary: Spolsky reflects on how the optimal ratio of developers to sales/marketing staff changes as a software company matures. In the early days, hiring salespeople is wasteful because the product isn't good enough to sell. But as product quality improves, sales and marketing efforts multiply in effectiveness, eventually leading mature software companies to employ far more salespeople than developers. He uses a simple mathematical model where revenue equals quality times reach to explain why this ratio shifts, and argues this also explains why offshoring development yields minimal cost advantage for product companies.
key_points: Early-stage startups get little value from sales hires because the product quality multiplier is too low to make marketing efforts worthwhile
Programmers and salespeople multiply each other's value: better code makes each salesperson more effective, and more salespeople make each improvement in code more profitable
Large mature software companies rationally end up with 5-20 salespeople per developer because the product quality is high and the bottleneck shifts to reach
Offshoring development saves little for product companies because development is only ~10% of total costs, making a 50% labor savings just a 5% overall advantage
Marketing is not a substitute for code quality — it's a multiplier, and multiplying by zero still yields zero
money_quotes: Programmers multiply salespeople, and vice-versa.

I entertained the quixotic and, retrospectively, stupid idea of requiring every employee at Fog Creek to be a programmer… even the receptionist would have to have done some BASIC programming in high school to qualify.
It is not the case (as commonly believed by nerds) that marketing is a substitute for code quality. The best marketing in the world cannot force people to pay for a useless product.
If everybody in the world knew about your software and was encouraged to evaluate it, the number that would buy it would be (Earth population) x Quality.
If you hire sales and marketing staff too early, they don't really get much traction, and you may start to think that sales and marketing are a waste of time.
Double the quality, and the same sales effort yields double the revenue.

If a developer in Russia, India, or China costs 50% as much as a developer in Seattle, San Francisco, or Boston, but software development is only 10% of your costs, you can only get a 5% advantage from offshoring development.
posts.key_insight: Software quality and sales effort are multipliers of each other, not substitutes, which means the optimal headcount ratio shifts dramatically from developer-heavy to sales-heavy as a product matures.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky explains why Stack Overflow is seeking venture capital despite his long-standing advocacy for bootstrapped, organic growth. He revisits his earlier framework of 'Ben and Jerry's' vs 'Amazon' style companies, acknowledging that while Fog Creek thrived as the former, Stack Overflow's situation is fundamentally different. He lays out six indicators that a company should take VC (land grab opportunity, proven repeatable concept, publicity value, investor expertise, big exit potential, founders willing to cede control) and seven counter-indicators. He concludes that Stack Overflow, with 6 million monthly uniques and ambitions to change how everyone gets answers online, is one of those rare companies where VC genuinely makes sense.
key_points: Spolsky distinguishes between 'Ben and Jerry's' companies (organic, careful growth) and 'Amazon' companies (capital-fueled land grabs), arguing both models are valid for different situations
He provides a concrete checklist of six positive indicators for taking VC and seven counter-indicators, creating a practical framework for the decision
Stack Overflow's proven traction (6 million monthly uniques in 18 months) and ambition to expand beyond programmers to all Q&A on the internet justify the need for outside capital
He emphasizes that choosing a VC is like choosing a spouse — the relationship matters as much as the money, and founders should avoid jumping at the first offer

The post implicitly addresses criticism from readers who expected him to remain anti-VC, showing that intellectual consistency means applying principles to circumstances rather than holding rigid positions
money_quotes: By staying profitable and growing carefully, we've managed to survive two big downturns and we've grown into a stable, 34-person company that's a great place to work and is likely to remain stable, and a great place to work, for a long time.
The business is in a new field with no competition, but the field has proven itself, and is obviously going to get very crowded very soon, so the faster you can grab territory, the better.
If the product is immature and unproven, in which case, expensive marketing efforts will be wasted proving to the world how bad your product is.
The proverbial dentist, who is happy to give you a half million bucks, but doesn't know the first thing about CPMs and CPCs and CTOs, so you might as well not bother.
Now we're biting off the bigger goal of changing the way everyone gets answers to their questions on the Internet, and that's something we can't do alone.
If there is any other way to raise the kind of money you need, for example, by selling actual products to customers.
posts.key_insight: The decision to take venture capital isn't ideological — it's situational, and even a committed bootstrapper should recognize when a proven product facing a land grab opportunity genuinely needs outside capital to fulfill its potential.

posts.summary: Joel briefly announces a new feature on Stack Overflow Careers that allows users to import their job and education history from LinkedIn or Facebook. The feature was built by developer Kevin Montrose, whose Stack Overflow reputation score Joel highlights. The post serves as a short product update rather than an essay.
key_points: Stack Overflow Careers added LinkedIn and Facebook profile import functionality
The feature reduces friction in CV creation by reusing existing professional data
Joel highlights the developer's Stack Overflow reputation score as a credential
money_quotes: New StackOverflow developer Kevin Montrose (6,878 reputation) added a neat feature to the career site that makes it a zillion times easier to file a CV if you've already put in your job and education history on LinkedIn or FaceBook.
posts.key_insight: Reducing friction in user onboarding by importing existing data from other platforms dramatically improves the experience.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces his retirement from traditional blogging on the 10-year anniversary of Joel on Software. He reflects on his experience writing for Inc. magazine, where editorial rewrites made pieces feel unlike his own voice. He declares the opinionated tech essay genre—Hacker News fodder—has become boring to him personally, though he acknowledges it remains a valid format for others. He calls for a shift from recycled conventional wisdom toward more objective, measurable knowledge that fills in the 'long tail' rather than repeating the same ideas. He also announces ending the StackOverflow podcast's current format, closing the Joel on Software discussion group, and sharply curtailing speaking engagements.
key_points: The opinionated tech essay format, pioneered by Greenspun and Winer, has been so widely adopted that it now feels exhausted and repetitive
The tech community keeps recycling the same ideas (fail fast, NDAs are worthless, execution over ideas) instead of producing genuinely new, objective knowledge
Professional editing at Inc. improved his writing technically but stripped away his authentic voice, making the columns feel like someone else's work
The next decade should focus on measurable truth rather than anecdotes, and on filling the long tail of knowledge rather than rehashing familiar arguments
Twitter is dismissed as a medium that reduces complex thought to context-free sentence fragments
money_quotes: After a decade of this, the whole genre of Hacker News fodder is just too boring to me personally.
We need something that is more objective (based on measurable truth and falseness rather than just lists of anecdotes about successful projects and failed projects).

We need to stop rewriting the same things again and again (fail fast! NDAs are worthless! Execution matters, not ideas! Use the right tools for the job!). Instead we should start filling in the long tail of knowledge.
It creates a mentally stunted world in which the most complicated thought you can think is one sentence long.

Logging on gives you a page full of little hand grenades: impossible-to-understand, context-free sentences that take five minutes of research to unravel and which then turn out to be stupid, irrelevant, or pertaining to the television series Battlestar Galactica.
It's as if somebody kidnapped me and replaced me with an indistinguishable imposter who went to Columbia Journalism School.

I would write an essay describing why Twitter gives me a headache and makes me fear for the future of humanity, but it doesn't deserve more than 140 characters of explanation, and I've already spent 820.
posts.key_insight: The tech blogging world has saturated itself with recycled conventional wisdom dressed as insight, and what's needed now is objective, measurable knowledge that fills gaps rather than repeating familiar mantras.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky publicly reverses his earlier dismissal of distributed version control systems, admitting he was wrong on a podcast where he criticized DVCS. After his team at Fog Creek switched to Mercurial and built a product (Kiln) around it, he studied the technology deeply and came to understand that the key insight isn't the 'distributed' part but that DVCS thinks in terms of changes rather than versions. This mental model shift makes branching and merging vastly easier, which he calls possibly the biggest advance in software development technology in a decade. He urges Subversion users to switch immediately and links to his Mercurial tutorial, HgInit. The post also serves as his farewell essay on Joel on Software.
key_points: The most important aspect of DVCS is not distribution but the shift from thinking about versions to thinking about changes as first-class objects
This change-oriented mental model solves the historically painful problem of merging branched code, making branching strategies practical rather than terrifying
Approaching Mercurial with a Subversion mindset will cause confusion because the underlying program model is fundamentally different
Many teams using centralized VCS developed workarounds like if-statement feature flags instead of branches, treating a tool limitation as a virtue
Joel considers easy merging and branching possibly the biggest advance in software development technology in the decade he spent writing
money_quotes: With distributed version control, the distributed part is actually not the most interesting part.
The interesting part is that these systems think in terms of changes, not in terms of versions.
If you come at Mercurial with a Subversion mindset, things will almost work, but when they don't, you'll be confused, unhappy, and unsuccessful, and you'll hate Mercurial.
When you manage changes instead of managing versions, merging works better, and therefore, you can branch any time your organizational goals require it, because merging back will be a piece of cake.
We tried to branch our code, and that worked fine. But when it came time to merge back, it was a complete nightmare and we had to practically reapply every change by hand, and we swore never again.
I'd go back to C++ before I gave up on Mercurial.
Subversion = Leeches. Mercurial and Git = Antibiotics. We have better technology now.
This is possibly the biggest advance in software development technology in the ten years I've been writing articles here.
posts.key_insight: The revolutionary insight of distributed version control is not distribution itself but the paradigm shift from managing versions to managing changes, which finally makes branching and merging practical and painless.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post announcing the release of a new book by Fred Brooks, author of The Mythical Man Month. Joel simply links to 'The Design of Design' on Amazon. There is no analysis, review, or commentary — just a book announcement. The post consists of only 19 words of original content.
key_points: Fred Brooks, author of The Mythical Man Month, has published a new book called The Design of Design
Joel considers this noteworthy enough to announce but offers no review or commentary
money_quotes: There is a new book out by Fred Brooks (author of The Mythical Man Month)!
posts.key_insight: A minimal announcement post highlighting Joel's respect for Fred Brooks as an influential software engineering author.

posts.summary: Joel announces major changes to Stack Exchange, pivoting from a paid white-label hosted product to a free, community-driven platform. The original Stack Exchange model let anyone create a Q&A site, but didn't produce enough high-quality sites. The new model makes Stack Exchange free, introduces a democratic community process for creating new sites, and places all content under Creative Commons licensing rather than private ownership.
key_points: Stack Overflow's ambitions grew from programming Q&A to wanting to cover every topic
The paid white-label Stack Exchange model failed to produce the expected flood of high-quality community sites
The pivot to Stack Exchange 2.0 makes the platform free, community-governed, and publicly licensed under Creative Commons
Existing Stack Exchange site owners are assured their sites won't be disrupted during the transition
money_quotes: Like the small-town mayor who suddenly finds herself running an entire state, our ambitions for Stack Overflow keep growing.
Programming questions? We asked. Why just programming questions? Why not every question under the sun?
We thought that other people would create awesome sites on every imaginable topic. Some people did (yay!), but it wasn't the flood of high quality sites we were hoping for.
Stack Exchange will now be free.
The content of these new, community-created Stack Exchange sites will be publically owned under a Creative Commons license, instead of being owned by individuals or businesses.
posts.key_insight: When a paid platform model fails to generate sufficient community-driven content, making it free and community-governed can be the necessary pivot to achieve scale and quality.

posts.summary: Spolsky explains the philosophy behind Stack Exchange 2.0's expansion into new Q&A communities. He argues that successful Q&A sites must target experts and professionals, not casual enthusiasts. The key insight is that expert-focused sites naturally attract enthusiasts, but enthusiast-focused sites fail to attract experts. He uses proposed Firearms and Law sites as examples, noting that easy questions have already been answered elsewhere, while millions of hard, long-tail questions remain unserved. The core mission is making the internet better for expert answers, not duplicating what Yahoo Answers already offers.
key_points: Expert-focused Q&A sites naturally attract enthusiasts, but enthusiast sites don't attract experts — the hierarchy only works one direction
The real value of Stack Exchange is in the millions of hard, long-tail questions that only professionals can answer, not the 200 easy ones already covered elsewhere
Stack Overflow's core mission is making the internet a better place for expert answers, which requires attracting real professionals to each community
What makes a community great is great people — experts want to hang out with other experts
money_quotes: The power of the Stack Exchange platform is detailed, expert answers to extremely rare, 'long-tail,' highly technical questions.
To get expert answers, you need experts. To attract experts, you need a site where people are asking very interesting and hard questions.
The pro sites WILL attract the enthusiasts, but not the other way around.
There are only 200 easy law questions, and they've all been asked 100 times on Mahalo and Yahoo!Answers.

There are 20,000,000 detailed, difficult, long-tail questions that only professionals can answer, and we'd be doing a REAL service to the Internet by creating a place where you can find answers to the 20,000,000 hard questions, not the 200 easy ones.
Make the Internet a better place to get expert answers to your questions.
What makes a community great is great people… that is, real experts. And the experts want to hang out with other experts.
posts.key_insight: Expert-focused communities naturally attract enthusiasts too, but enthusiast-focused communities never attract experts — so you must build for the pros first.

posts.summary: This is an ultra-short humorous post written from the perspective of Joel's dog Taco, thanking TechSmith for sending pet photos and toys including an iPhone. It's essentially a playful thank-you note that serves as lighthearted filler content on the blog. The post has no substantive technical or business content.
key_points: A playful thank-you note written from the perspective of Joel's dog Taco
TechSmith apparently sent gifts and pet photos to Joel/Fog Creek
The humor comes from a dog writing a formal letter and claiming to enjoy an iPhone
money_quotes: I especially like the iPhone.
posts.key_insight: This is a whimsical non-technical post written as a joke letter from Joel's dog, with no substantive takeaway.

posts.summary: This ultra-brief post announces Stack Overflow's Series A venture capital financing. Joel links to the official announcement on the Stack Overflow blog with a single quote explaining the reasoning: they decided to make the product free and find VC funding to support it. The post marks a significant turning point for Stack Overflow's business model and growth trajectory.
key_points: Stack Overflow raised its Series A round of venture capital funding
The decision to make Stack Overflow free necessitated external funding to cover costs
Joel frames the VC decision as a pragmatic solution rather than a grand strategy
money_quotes: And the best answer we could come up with was, let's make the damn thing free, and get some VC somewhere to pay for it.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the best business strategy is to make the product free and figure out funding separately.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares his enthusiasm for Kathy Sierra's talk at the Business of Software 2009 conference. He notes that Sierra made fun of Stack Overflow's VC-backed business model during her presentation, including a reference to the site's unicorn meme. The post is essentially a brief recommendation to watch her talk, with a plug for the upcoming Business of Software conference.
key_points: Kathy Sierra delivered a memorable talk at the Business of Software 2009 conference
Sierra humorously critiqued Stack Overflow's VC-backed business model during her presentation
Joel promotes the next Business of Software conference in Boston
money_quotes: Her talk at the Business of Software 2009 was amazing, even though, in retrospect, I realize now that she had a slide making fun of Stack Overflow's terrific new VC-backed business model.
posts.key_insight: This is a brief promotional post recommending Kathy Sierra's conference talk, notable mainly for Joel's good-natured acknowledgment that she mocked Stack Overflow's business model.

posts.summary: Joel announces the release of FogBugz 7.3, describing it as a significant update despite its incremental version number. The post is a straightforward product announcement listing new features including case editing, bulk email reply, resizable UI, IMAP support, and faster backlog editing. It serves primarily as a marketing update for Fog Creek's bug tracking product.
key_points: FogBugz 7.3 is positioned as a larger release than its version number suggests, with daily-use features
Key additions include case event editing, bulk reply for emails, and IMAP support
Agile/scrum workflow improvements were made with faster project backlog editing
money_quotes: FogBugz 7.3 is a pretty huge release, despite the modest version number.
posts.key_insight: A brief product announcement for FogBugz 7.3 highlighting incremental but practical feature improvements for daily use.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky briefly introduces Paul Kenny, a sales training expert who regularly speaks at the Business of Software conference. He notes that both Red Gate and Fog Creek Software hire Kenny to train their sales teams. The post primarily serves to share a video of Kenny's 2009 conference talk and promote the upcoming 2010 Business of Software conference in Boston.
key_points: Paul Kenny is a recurring speaker at the Business of Software conference specializing in high-tech sales
Both Red Gate and Fog Creek rely on Kenny for sales team training and organizational development
The post promotes the next Business of Software conference scheduled for October 2010 in Boston
money_quotes: He's an expert on sales, especially high tech sales, and both Red Gate and Fog Creek hire him all the time to train our sales teams and build our sales organization.
posts.key_insight: Even deeply technical software companies like Fog Creek invest heavily in professional sales training and organizational development.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky briefly recommends a resource for setting executive compensation in tech companies. His friend Noam at Harvard Business School conducts annual surveys of executive compensation. The surveys help founders benchmark pay and equity for executive roles. The data can be purchased for $999 or accessed free by participating in the survey.
key_points: Annual executive compensation surveys from Harvard Business School provide benchmarking data for tech companies
The surveys help answer practical questions like VP of Sales salary or whether a CTO equity grant is fair
Participating in the survey grants free access to results, creating an incentive for broad participation
money_quotes: The surveys are a great way to figure out how much to pay that VP of Sales you were thinking of hiring, or whether your equity grant as CTO is fair.
posts.key_insight: Compensation benchmarking surveys are a valuable tool for tech founders making executive hiring and pay decisions.

posts.summary: Joel announces that Area 51, the community-driven platform for proposing new Stack Exchange sites, has entered beta. He addresses a Hacker News commenter's criticism that the site seems overly complicated and questions its business model. Joel humorously deflects the business model question with a Saturday Night Live reference. He draws a parallel to early skeptics of Stack Overflow itself, who predicted it wouldn't last a year, and notes that Area 51 is intentionally designed for committed users rather than casual visitors.
key_points: Area 51 launched in beta as the community-driven process for creating new Stack Exchange sites
Early critics dismissed Stack Overflow's UI and predicted it wouldn't survive a year, proving spectacularly wrong
Products designed for committed communities don't need to be immediately accessible to everyone
Joel deflects the monetization question with humor rather than a serious answer
money_quotes: The answer is simple. Volume.
I'm not one to take Internet chat board comments seriously.
If you don't know what it's for, or why it's going to work, or you can't figure it out, it's not, actually for you.
posts.key_insight: Products built for committed communities can afford to be complex and uninviting to casual visitors — early skepticism from outsiders is often a sign you're building for the right audience.

posts.summary: Joel shares a video of Don Norman speaking at the Business of Software 2009 conference. He highlights Norman's memorable example about a two-button remote control for PowerPoint presentations, where half the audience presses up and half presses down to advance slides, yet everyone believes their choice is obvious. Joel uses this as a brief plug for the upcoming Business of Software 2010 conference.
key_points: Don Norman demonstrates that even simple two-button interfaces can produce completely split user behavior
People's conviction that their choice is 'obvious' reveals a fundamental blind spot in how we think about design intuition
The post serves primarily as a conference promotion wrapped around an interesting UX anecdote
money_quotes: Your remote control has two buttons. They are unmarked, but one button points up and one button points down. Which button do you press?
It turns out half the people press up, half the people press down, and everybody thinks their choice is obvious.
posts.key_insight: Even the simplest design choices can split users evenly while each side remains convinced their interpretation is the only logical one.

posts.summary: Joel announces the launch of Web Applications Stack Exchange, a new Q&A site for web app questions like exporting Gmail or deleting Facebook accounts. It's the first site created through the community-driven Area 51 process, where proposed sites must demonstrate they can reach critical mass before being approved. The post is essentially a brief promotional announcement for the expanding Stack Exchange network.
key_points: Web Applications Stack Exchange has entered beta as a Q&A site for consumer web app questions
It is the first site created through Area 51, Stack Exchange's community-driven site creation process
New Stack Exchange sites must demonstrate they can reach critical mass before being officially created
money_quotes: It's a part of the Stack Exchange network, so it has the clean, elegant design that made Stack Overflow a phenomenal success.
There are more great sites in the pipeline, but they have to demonstrate that they can reach critical mass or we won't create them.
posts.key_insight: Stack Exchange's growth strategy required new community sites to prove demand through a structured proposal process before being created.

posts.summary: In this very brief post, Joel Spolsky explains the decision to give each Stack Exchange site its own unique domain name and brand identity rather than using generic Stack Exchange subdomains. He argues that individually-branded sites feel more authentic and trustworthy to their communities. The post links out to a longer explanation on the Stack Overflow blog.
key_points: Individual branding for community sites creates a stronger sense of identity and belonging than generic numbering schemes
Authenticity and trust are enhanced when sites have their own domain names and visual identities
Community coherence matters more than corporate brand consistency when building network sites
money_quotes: We decided that individually-branded sites felt more authentic and trustworthy.
Nobody wants to say that they live in Housing Block 2938TC. They want to live in Colonial Manor.
posts.key_insight: Giving community sites their own unique brand identities rather than generic labels fosters authenticity, trust, and a stronger sense of belonging among users.

posts.summary: Joel briefly announces the launch of three new Stack Exchange community sites: Gaming, Cooking, and Pro Webmasters. He teases three more launches the following week. The post is essentially a short promotional update with no substantive argument or analysis.
key_points: Three new Stack Exchange sites launched: Gaming, Cooking, and Pro Webmasters
Stack Exchange was expanding rapidly beyond its programming Q&A origins into diverse topic areas
The pace of expansion was aggressive, with three more sites promised the following week
money_quotes: We launched three new Stack Exchange sites this week!
posts.key_insight: Stack Exchange was aggressively expanding its Q&A platform model into non-technical domains like gaming and cooking.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky briefly muses that a viable business model might be to take 37signals' deliberately simple products and create more complex, feature-rich versions of them. The post is essentially a teaser for a conference talk he gave at the Business of Software conference, where he presumably explored the tension between simplicity and feature complexity in more depth. He also promotes his upcoming appearance at the next year's conference.
key_points: There is a market tension between the simplicity 37signals champions and users who want more features and complexity
Copying a simple product and adding complexity could be a viable business strategy
The simplicity-vs-choice debate is significant enough to warrant a full conference talk
money_quotes: Sometimes I think a pretty good business model would be to copy the applications that 37signals makes, but make them more complex.
posts.key_insight: The deliberate simplicity that companies like 37signals champion leaves a market opening for competitors willing to offer the complexity and features that many users actually want.

posts.summary: Spolsky addresses the apparent redundancy of launching new Stack Exchange sites that cover topics already served by existing communities, using WordPress as a case study. He argues that there's no rule requiring only one Q&A site per topic, and that Stack Exchange's technology provides a fundamentally better experience than traditional forums. The post highlights specific advantages like voting, reputation systems, wiki-style editing, and lower barriers to participation. He predicts these features will eventually become standard everywhere, but until then Stack Exchange will continue expanding.
key_points: New Stack Exchange sites deliberately duplicate existing communities' subject matter, and that's fine because there's no rule limiting one Q&A site per topic
Stack Exchange's core advantages over traditional forums are voting, reputation, wiki-style editing, and lower participation barriers
Stack Overflow succeeded not by finding uncovered topics but by providing better technology for the same topics
Network effects naturally consolidate activity onto the better platform, as demonstrated by jQuery Q&A migrating to Stack Overflow organically
money_quotes: There's no rule that says that there needs to be exactly one Q&A website per topic.
WordPress.org's forums don't have voting, so you have to read through every answer and decide for yourself which one might solve your problem.
They don't have reputation, so there's no way to see whether you're getting an answer from someone who knows what they're talking about.
Stack Overflow is simply better than traditional forums, which is why it largely replaced proprietary forums.
Ultimately it didn't matter that much, because most of the jQuery Q&A activity happens on Stack Overflow anyway.
One day, the features that are standard on Stack Exchange will be copied everywhere.
posts.key_insight: Winning in Q&A isn't about finding unserved topics — it's about providing superior technology for topics already being discussed elsewhere.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post that simply links to a Stack Overflow Blog entry about forking in the Unix community. Joel references Freud's concept of 'the narcissism of minor differences' to explain why the Unix world loves to take sides and fork projects. The post is essentially a pointer to content published on the Stack Overflow blog rather than a standalone essay.
key_points: The Unix community has a tendency to fork projects and take sides over minor disagreements
Joel draws a parallel between open source forking culture and Freud's 'narcissism of minor differences'
The post redirects readers to the Stack Overflow blog for the full discussion
money_quotes: The Unix world loves to take sides.
I don't have to blog about this; Freud already did, in 1930. He called it 'the narcissism of minor differences'
posts.key_insight: The tendency of open source communities to fork and factionally divide mirrors what Freud identified as the narcissism of minor differences — fighting hardest over the smallest distinctions.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky briefly flags a growing problem with Stack Exchange's Area 51 site-proposal platform: an explosion of overly niche, overlapping, and redundant community proposals. He points readers to a longer discussion on the Stack Overflow blog about how the team plans to address this by merging similar proposals. The post serves as a redirect rather than a full essay, signaling that community fragmentation was becoming a real operational concern for the Stack Exchange network.
key_points: Area 51 was generating thousands of proposals for new Stack Exchange sites, many of which were too narrowly focused to sustain viable communities
Overlapping and redundant proposals were fragmenting potential user bases across similar topics
The Stack Exchange team recognized the need to actively consolidate and merge proposals rather than let organic growth continue unchecked
money_quotes: A pretty clear pattern has started to worry us: too many ridiculously niche proposals, overlapping proposals, and proposals that are already covered by an existing site.
posts.key_insight: Unchecked community-driven site proposals lead to fragmentation, and platform operators must actively consolidate to maintain quality and critical mass.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief announcement post about Stack Overflow introducing new PNG-based flair (user profile badges/widgets that can be embedded on other sites). The post has essentially no substantive content beyond the title, serving as a simple product update notification. It reflects the later period of Joel's blog where posts became shorter announcements rather than the long-form essays he was known for.
key_points: Stack Overflow introduced PNG-based flair as an alternative format for user profile widgets
The post is a minimal announcement with no elaboration or discussion
posts.key_insight: This is a bare product announcement with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces major new releases of FogBugz 8.0 and Kiln, Fog Creek's bug tracking and version control products. He credits the team and ten summer interns for the upgrades. To promote the releases, he announces a 20-city world tour featuring live demos and a free one-hour course on distributed version control. The post serves as a brief promotional announcement encouraging readers to sign up quickly before events fill up.
key_points: FogBugz and Kiln received major upgrades, partly built by ten summer interns
Fog Creek is launching a 20-city world tour to demo FogBugz 8.0 in person
The tour includes a free one-hour introduction to distributed version control (DVCS)
The events are free but historically fill up quickly
money_quotes: Thanks to the hard work of the Fog Creek team, including ten great summer interns, we have just released amazing new upgrades to FogBugz and Kiln.
I'll be flying to 20 (yes, twenty) different cities to demo FogBugz 8.0 to anyone who wants to come see it in person.
If you've been wondering what all the fuss is about, this is a painless way to learn the basics of the new generation of version control.
posts.key_insight: Fog Creek used ambitious in-person world tours as a grassroots marketing strategy for developer tools, combining product demos with educational content on emerging technologies like distributed version control.

posts.summary: Joel announces the launch of the first Stack Exchange site to complete the community creation process and exit beta. The site, initially called nothingtoinstall.com, focuses on helping users with web application questions. He provides example questions covering common web app tasks like emailing large files, deleting Facebook accounts, and backing up WordPress blogs. The post includes an update noting the site was quickly renamed to webapps.stackexchange.com because the original name was unpopular.
key_points: The first community-created Stack Exchange site successfully graduated from beta to full launch
The site focuses on practical how-to questions about consumer web applications
The original branding (nothingtoinstall.com) was abandoned within days due to community feedback, showing willingness to iterate on naming
money_quotes: The first Stack Exchange site to make it all the way through the community creation process is now live and out of beta!
Only days after launching, we changed the name from "nothingtoinstall.com" to "webapps.stackexchange.com" because nobody really liked that name!
posts.key_insight: The successful launch of the first community-driven Stack Exchange site validated the Area 51 process for growing the Q&A network beyond programming, even if naming decisions needed quick course correction.

posts.summary: Joel promotes the FogBugz World Tour 2010, noting that unexpected demand led them to secure larger venues in some cities. The free event features demos of FogBugz 8.0 and Kiln 2.0, plus a one-hour introduction to distributed version control. He encourages last-minute signups and reassures waitlisted attendees that cancellations typically open spots.
key_points: The FogBugz World Tour 2010 attracted over 3,154 signups, exceeding expectations and requiring larger venues
The event combines product demos with educational content on distributed version control
Waitlisted attendees historically get in after last-minute cancellations
money_quotes: Due to unexpected demand we've been working with the venues for the upcoming FogBugz World Tour 2010 to find more room.
We'll give you a one hour introduction to distributed version control… how it works, how it's different than the version control you know and love, and how to set it up to make your life easier.
posts.key_insight: A brief promotional post urging readers to sign up for the free FogBugz World Tour 2010, which had exceeded expected demand.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky draws a playful parallel between the Stack Exchange Q&A format and the structure of the Talmud, arguing that Stack Exchange is a natural platform for questions about Jewish observance. He notes that the Talmud similarly presents a question followed by multiple ranked answers. The post serves as a brief promotion for a proposed Jewish Life and Learning Stack Exchange site.
key_points: The Talmud's structure of questions followed by multiple ranked answers mirrors Stack Overflow's format
Stack Exchange is well-suited for questions about Jewish observance due to this structural parallel
The post promotes a proposed Jewish Life and Learning community on Stack Exchange's Area 51 platform
money_quotes: Most of the Talmud reads just like Stack Overflow: a question, followed by multiple answers, usually with the highest ranking answer appearing first.
The number of questions is infinite.
posts.key_insight: The ancient format of Talmudic discourse — question, multiple answers, ranking — maps surprisingly well onto modern Q&A platforms like Stack Exchange.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post that serves as a pointer to a longer article on the Stack Overflow blog. Joel highlights the problem of recurring questions on Stack Overflow and directs readers to a post about how the platform handles frequently asked 'long tail' programming questions. The post itself contains essentially no original content beyond a single quoted sentence and a link.
key_points: Recurring questions are a persistent challenge for Q&A platforms like Stack Overflow
The 'long tail' concept applies to programming questions — many niche questions recur across users
Joel used his personal blog as a distribution channel to drive traffic to the Stack Overflow blog
money_quotes: Have you ever noticed how certain questions come up again and again on Stack Overflow sites?
posts.key_insight: Stack Overflow needed a strategy for handling the inevitably recurring programming questions that form the long tail of developer knowledge.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky recaps Stack Overflow's 2010 growth, highlighting the company's expansion from 7 million to 16.6 million monthly visitors, raising $6 million in venture capital, and growing from 3 to 27 employees. He emphasizes the launch of Stack Exchange as a network of 33 Q&A sites beyond programming, and touts the platform's high answer rates as its key differentiator. The post also covers the company's new New York office and development team accomplishments. It's essentially a celebratory year-in-review that doubles as a vision statement for Stack Exchange's expansion into new subject areas.
key_points: Stack Overflow grew 131% in traffic during 2010, reaching 16.6 million monthly unique visitors and 72.8 million monthly page views
The key quality metric is percentage of questions answered (over 80%), which Spolsky considers more important than raw traffic numbers
Stack Exchange launched as a democratically-driven network of Q&A sites beyond programming, growing to 33 sites with 1.5 million monthly visitors in six months
The Area 51 process ensures new sites only launch when they have critical mass of knowledgeable users, and underperforming sites get shut down
The company scaled from 3 remote developers to 27 employees with a physical office, funded by $6 million in VC from Union Square Ventures
money_quotes: The expert Q&A model that Stack Overflow pioneered is really working.
The true measure of success for any Internet company is how often people come up to me in swank hotel lobbies and offer to buy me meals, let me use their corporate jet, etc.
When was the last time you met a programmer who didn't use 'El Stack'?
All this TRAFFIC isn't worth a thing if people aren't getting answers to their questions.
We learned a long time ago that the only way to get questions answered promptly is to have a critical mass of knowledgeable users.
If a proposed site doesn't have critical mass, we just won't create it. Even if it does get created, it has to maintain a certain level of traffic and quality or we'll close it down.
Does your company have a Star Trek Couch? I didn't think so.
posts.key_insight: Stack Overflow's real competitive advantage isn't traffic but its answer rate — the expert Q&A model works because it ensures critical mass of knowledgeable users before launching any new community.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to a YouTube video of Joel Spolsky's FogBugz/Kiln world tour presentation. The post contains virtually no substantive content beyond the link and a tip about viewing quality. It serves purely as a promotional notice for Fog Creek Software's product demo tour.
key_points: FogBugz/Kiln world tour presentation was recorded and posted to YouTube
Joel recommends the 720p option for clearer screen visibility
posts.key_insight: This is a minimal promotional post with no substantive analysis or insight — purely a product demo announcement.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky addresses the common question of whether employers own software created by programmers in their spare time. He notes widespread misinformation on the topic and frames employment at a tech company as a decision to sell one's intellectual output. The post is essentially a brief pointer to his longer answer on answers.onstartups.com, where he elaborates on intellectual property rights for employee side projects.
key_points: There is significant misinformation about employer ownership of side projects created on personal time
Working at a tech company whose product is intellectual property means you've chosen to sell your intellectual output
The full answer is hosted on answers.onstartups.com rather than elaborated in the blog post itself
money_quotes: There's a surprising amount of misinformation out there about whether software companies own the work that a programmer does in their spare time.
Being an employee of a high tech company whose product is intellectual means that you have decided that you want to sell your intellectual output.
posts.key_insight: Programmers at tech companies should understand that employment fundamentally involves selling their intellectual output, which has implications for side project ownership that are widely misunderstood.

posts.summary: Spolsky announces the launch of Careers 2.0 by Stack Overflow, framing it as a generational shift in how programmers find jobs. He satirizes the traditional hiring process with its resumes, recruiters, and whiteboard interviews as an outdated ritual that fails to assess actual programming ability. The post positions Careers 2.0 as the modern replacement for this broken system.
key_points: Traditional resumes contain information that is useless for determining programming ability
The conventional hiring process is an 'esoteric ritual' focused on superficial signals like formatting and font choices
Whiteboard interviews test mundane tasks rather than real programming skill
Stack Overflow's Careers 2.0 represents a fundamental rethinking of developer hiring
money_quotes: It included all kinds of silly information required by the esoteric resume ritual (foreign languages spoken, whether or not you play ultimate Frisbee, Microsoft-veteran status).

This so-called "information" was utterly useless at determining whether you could program or not, but if you spelled everything right and used suitable fonts, you could come in for a day of interviews at which you would be asked to perform mundane programming tasks on a whiteboard.
posts.key_insight: The traditional resume-and-whiteboard hiring process is a broken ritual that evaluates superficial signals rather than actual programming ability.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky briefly announces that the company formerly known as Stack Overflow has been renamed to Stack Exchange. The post is essentially a teaser that directs readers to the Stack Overflow blog for the full explanation. It includes a photo of the snack room at the newly-named Stack Exchange office. The post is minimal in content, serving more as a pointer than a standalone article.
key_points: The parent company behind Stack Overflow rebranded to Stack Exchange
The post redirects readers to the official Stack Overflow blog for details on the name change
The announcement is made casually, referencing a snack room upgrade as the visual hook
money_quotes: The snack room at Stack Exchange got a wee upgrade today.
posts.key_insight: Stack Overflow the company rebranded to Stack Exchange to reflect its expansion beyond a single Q&A site into a network of communities.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the return of the podcast he co-hosts with Jeff Atwood, formerly the Stack Overflow Podcast, now rebranded as the Stack Exchange Podcast. The post provides links to subscribe via iTunes, SoundCloud, or the Stack Exchange Blog. He credits Alex Miller as the new producer and promises technical improvements to come.
key_points: The Stack Overflow Podcast has been rebranded to the Stack Exchange Podcast, reflecting the platform's expansion beyond Stack Overflow
The podcast is available across multiple platforms including iTunes, SoundCloud, and the Stack Exchange Blog
A new producer, Alex Miller, has been brought on to improve the podcast's technical quality
money_quotes: Jeff Atwood and I have resumed our weekly podcast, formerly known as the Stack Overflow Podcast, now known as the Stack Exchange Podcast!
SoundCloud is sort of like a sound version of Twitter, a very cool way to subscribe to audio.
posts.key_insight: The rebranding from Stack Overflow Podcast to Stack Exchange Podcast signals the company's strategic evolution from a single Q&A site into a broader network of communities.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky argues that eating lunch together as a team is critically important for workplace culture and shouldn't be left to chance. He observes that even at companies with cafeterias like Google and Microsoft, many employees end up eating alone, which he sees as a failure. Drawing a parallel to the social dynamics of school cafeterias, he contends that communal meals are fundamental to human connection. At Fog Creek and Stack Exchange, he deliberately designed the lunch experience with long shared tables and expectations that newcomers join the group.
key_points: Even at companies with free or cheap cafeterias, many employees eat alone, often masking their discomfort with phones or taking food back to their desks
The social dynamics of workplace lunch mirror junior high school cafeteria politics, tapping into deep psychological needs around belonging
Intentional design choices like long tables instead of small round ones can structurally encourage communal eating
Fog Creek and Stack Exchange deliberately share meals across company boundaries despite being separate organizations
Building a great workplace requires treating social rituals like lunch as deliberate design decisions, not afterthoughts
money_quotes: I've been on teams that eat together every day, and it's awesome. I've been on teams that don't, and lunch every day is, at best, lonely.
Excuse me, I'd love to introduce myself to you, but it's very important that I update my cabbage.
The importance of eating together with your co-workers is not negotiable, to me. It's too important to be left to chance.
That's why we eat together at long tables, not a bunch of little round tables.
Eating together is a critical part of what it means to be human and what it means to have a humane workplace, and that's been a part of our values from day one.
There's a lot of stuff that's accidental about Fog Creek and Stack Exchange, but lunch is not one of them.
posts.key_insight: Communal lunch is too important to workplace culture to be left to chance — it must be deliberately designed into the company's daily routine.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces planning for the next series of Stack Overflow DevDays conferences scheduled for fall 2011. The two-day conferences focus on providing intensive introductions to emerging technologies that developers want to learn. He references the original DevDays format of high-bandwidth sessions on topics like iPhone development, Python, and jQuery. For the 2011 edition, the team is crowdsourcing topic suggestions from the community, with early interest in DVCS, HTML5, and Node.js.
key_points: Stack Overflow DevDays conferences are returning as a series of two-day events in fall 2011
The conference format focuses on intensive introductions to technologies developers want to learn but don't yet use professionally
Topic selection is being crowdsourced from the Stack Overflow community via Meta Stack Overflow
Early community interest centers on DVCS, HTML5, and Node.js as top requested topics
money_quotes: The idea for the original DevDays was to have high-bandwidth, intensive introductions to a wide variety of new technologies.
The kinds of technologies that everybody wants to learn but doesn't necessarily need to use on a project right now.
posts.key_insight: The best developer conferences focus on technologies people want to learn but haven't yet needed professionally, and letting the community choose topics ensures relevance.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky discusses Stack Exchange's approach to growing its network of 51 online communities, arguing that algorithmic approaches alone fail at community building. He observes that each community has unique needs requiring human judgment rather than one-size-fits-all software solutions. To address the challenge of scaling community cultivation, he announces plans to build a team of 'super-evangelists' who will learn community-building skills through intensive daily training. The post doubles as a job listing for this new community evangelism team.
key_points: Algorithmic approaches to community building fail because each community has distinct needs and values that require human judgment
Many technology companies mistake building software for building communities, then wonder why people don't show up
Critical mass—the point where a community grows organically—requires active cultivation, not just a platform
Stack Exchange plans to train community evangelists through an intensive daily program combining hands-on work with study of marketing and community-building literature
