The skill of turning a dead internet site into a thriving community is a distinctly 21st-century profession that doesn't yet have an established training path
money_quotes: Pouring a big fat algorithm in equal measures on top of 51 different groups of people does not always work the way you might hope it would work.
Maybe that's why the super-algorithm companies (like Google) tend to suck when they try to build social applications.

There are an awful lot of technology companies, founded by programmers, who think they are building communities on the Internet, but they're really just building software and wondering why the community doesn't magically show up.
You can't use a robot to train a puppy. Every puppy is different.
You need smart human beings to use smart human judgment and cultivate each community individually.
Critical mass is that magic moment when the community has enough activity that it grows by itself.
Everyone who joins the program (and survives for a year) will come out with an almost supernatural ability to take a dead, lifeless site on the internet and make it into the hottest bar in town.
posts.key_insight: Building online communities is fundamentally a human endeavor requiring individual judgment and cultivation, not an engineering problem solvable with algorithms.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post where Joel Spolsky acknowledges the passage of New York State's Marriage Equality Act in June 2011. The post contains no substantive text beyond the title, serving as a simple marker of the event. It represents a rare instance of Joel using his software-focused blog to note a political and social milestone. The lack of commentary suggests he viewed the headline as speaking for itself.
key_points: Joel marked the passage of NY's Marriage Equality Act on his software blog with no additional commentary
The post signals Joel's support for marriage equality through the choice to highlight it on his platform
It represents a departure from Joel's typical software development and business topics
posts.key_insight: Sometimes a headline is the entire statement, and choosing to amplify a moment on your platform is itself a form of commentary.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the return of Stack Overflow DevDays, a developer conference series focused on hands-on coding tutorials of emerging technologies. The conferences are expanding to four cities across three continents, with two-day formats instead of one. The format emphasizes live coding over slides, covering technologies like DVCS, HTML5, Node.js, and Hadoop. An update notes the events were ultimately cancelled before they took place.
key_points: DevDays focuses on live coding demonstrations rather than PowerPoint presentations, assuming attendees are developers who want rapid introductions to new technologies
The conference format is designed as a high-bandwidth firehose: ten technology tutorials starting from basics, each with code written from scratch on stage

The 2011 edition expanded significantly from previous years — four cities (San Francisco, Sydney, London, Washington DC), two days instead of one, with additional events like a High Scalability conference and an open source hackathon
The events were ultimately cancelled before taking place, as noted in the post's update
money_quotes: The stuff the cool kids are all talking about on the playground while you're stuck in the basement somewhere grinding away on Java Enterprise Visual Basic.
Every presenter launches an editor and writes code from scratch and shows you what it does.
There are almost no prepared PowerPoint slides with ten bullet items each containing 10 words explaining the ROI benefits of some new technology.
It's the best possible way to spend two days and learn as much as you would learn in two years of reading Twitter.
Each tutorial starts at the ground level and gives you a whirlwind tour through a technology by showing you actual code.
posts.key_insight: The most effective developer conferences replace slide decks and bullet points with live coding, giving experienced programmers rapid, hands-on exposure to new technologies.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces Trello, a new product born from Fog Creek Software's effort to keep employees motivated by creating small teams to launch new products. The idea originated from Joel's frustration with never knowing what his team was working on. An early concept called 'Five Things' limited everyone to exactly five tasks, but evolved into Trello. Built by a small team following lean startup principles, Trello was dogfooded from just 700 lines of code and quickly proved to be an incredibly useful collaboration tool.
key_points: Fog Creek created four two-person teams to launch new products, motivated by employee retention and excitement rather than pure business strategy
The lean startup approach guided development: ship early, listen to customers, and pivot until you find product-market fit
The origin problem was simple: Joel never knew what anyone was working on, and half the time disagreed with their priorities
The 'Five Things' concept — limiting everyone to exactly five tasks — was a precursor that helped crystallize the idea behind Trello
Dogfooding from just 700 lines of code validated the product early and revealed they had a hit
money_quotes: If we want to keep our employees excited and motivated for another ten years, we were going to need some new things to work on.
After ten years in management I still never knew what anyone was supposed to be working on.
Once in a while I would walk around asking everyone what they were doing, and half the time, my reaction was 'why the hell are you working on THAT?'
Nobody was ever allowed to have SIX things assigned to them. If you have too many things on your to-do list, your motivation tends to sag.
It had to be super simple and friction-free so that everyone would use it, but it had to be powerful, too.
We started dogfooding the product when it was only 700 lines of code, and even in that super-simple form, we found it incredibly useful.
posts.key_insight: Great products can emerge from solving your own mundane management frustrations — not knowing who's working on what — when you combine that irritation with lean development and a relentless focus on simplicity.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky evaluates whether startups should launch products at tech conferences like TechCrunch Disrupt, Launch, or Demo. He examines the two main promises—VC exposure and press coverage—and finds both require significant qualification. VC introductions only help if your company is already fundable, and press coverage requires proactive outreach rather than passive hope. Drawing from launching Trello at Disrupt, he argues that a polished product combined with pre-briefed journalists can generate massive viral traction. He also makes a case against lean startup incrementalism, advocating for building something genuinely good before exposing it to 131,000 eyeballs. The overlooked benefit of conference launches, he concludes, is the hard deadline and morale boost they give your team.
key_points: Conference launches only accelerate VC connections if your company is already fundable—no conference can fix a fundamentally unfundable company
Press coverage at conferences requires proactive journalist outreach under embargo; passive discovery from 200 attending reporters yielded zero unprompted stories for Trello
Startups get only 2-3 newsworthy moments ever (launch, funding, traction), so each must be maximized with coordinated, simultaneous coverage
Spolsky argues against premature lean launches: Trello waited 9 months instead of 3, using closed beta for customer development while polishing the product for a big public debut
The most underrated benefit of conference launches is the hard deadline's effect on team morale, decision-making, and the shared experience of shipping
money_quotes: There's always going to be a 'Netflix for Cabbage' or a 'Second Life for Facebook' that the judges fall in love with.

If you're knocking on VC's doors and they all seem to be closed, you're not fundable. You're beautiful, you're smart, and you're going to change the world, but you happen to be non-fundable, so just stop.
There was not a single reporter, from the 200 that were registered, at Disrupt who saw our presentation and said, 'Oh cool, I'm going to write about that.'

I couldn't stop thinking that you never have a second chance to make a first impression. We got 131,000 eyeballs on 9-month-old Trello when we launched, and it was AWESOME. I don't want 131,000 eyeballs on MEH.
The news cycle is 12 hours, tops. If you call journalists the day after you release your product, it's not news.
If your product is really good, launching at one of these conferences is an incredible catalyst. If your product is 'meh,' it won't help.
It's like sex, with clothes on.
Good software developers invent the future.
posts.key_insight: Conference launches are only valuable if your product is genuinely excellent—the real benefit isn't VC access or press, but the hard deadline that forces your team to ship something great and experience the thrill of real users.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky explains how Trello differs from Fog Creek's previous products by being a horizontal tool rather than vertical software aimed at developers. Drawing on his experience at Microsoft where he discovered most Excel users just made tables rather than doing calculations, he argues that great horizontal applications succeed by providing useful data structures — and Trello provides a 'list of lists.' He also outlines Trello's strategy: continuous delivery, public development, free pricing to maximize adoption, API-first architecture, and cutting-edge technology to attract developers.
key_points: Horizontal products serve all users but are harder to monetize and riskier than vertical products targeting specific professions

The most successful horizontal applications are fundamentally fancy data structures: Excel is a table, Word is wrapped text, PowerPoint is an array of full-screen images, and Trello is a list of lists
Microsoft discovered most Excel users never used formulas — they just needed tables — which explained why the calculation-focused Lotus Improv failed
Trello's 'Get Big Fast' strategy prioritizes removing all adoption friction, including making the product free and building an API-first architecture
Features not yet delivered to users are inventory — dead weight that costs money without earning anything
money_quotes: The great horizontal killer applications are actually just fancy data structures.

Spreadsheets are not just tools for doing 'what-if' analysis. They provide a specific data structure: a table. Most Excel users never enter a formula. They use Excel when they need a table. The gridlines are the most important feature of Excel, not recalc.
Horizontal means that it can be used by people from all walks of life. Word processors and web browsers are horizontal. The software your dentist uses to torture you with drills is vertical.

A feature that you built and tested, but didn't deliver yet because you're waiting for the next major release, becomes inventory. Inventory is dead weight: money you spent that's just wasting away without earning you anything.
In the long run, we think it's much easier to figure out how to extract a small amount of money out of a large number of users than to extract a large amount of money out of a small number of users.
On the Trello team, any feature that can be provided by a plug-in must be provided by a plug-in.
There have been outliners, but outlines are, IMHO, one of the great dead ends in UI design: so appealing to programmers, yet so useless to civilians.
posts.key_insight: The most successful horizontal software products succeed not by being the best tool for a specific task, but by providing a universally useful data structure that people adapt to countless purposes.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the creation of The Academy for Software Engineering, New York City's first public high school dedicated to training students in software development. The school, spearheaded by CS teacher Mike Zamansky and backed by VC Fred Wilson, will accept any interested ninth grader regardless of grades. Spolsky argues this is crucial because the US education system is failing to produce enough programmers to meet industry demand, and the existing pipeline draws from too narrow a demographic pool. He emphasizes that the school will maintain rigorous academics while not being a traditional vocational school.
key_points: The school uses 'limited, unscreened' admissions, meaning any interested student can apply regardless of grades or attendance, which Spolsky considers its best feature
The US education system is producing far too few programmers to meet industry demand, with starting salaries for top programmers approaching $100,000
Elite colleges draw CS majors from a narrow pool of mostly white and Asian males, and an unscreened public school could significantly improve diversity in tech
The school maintains rigorous academics and college preparation while acknowledging that college isn't the only path for talented programmers
money_quotes: A lot of kids are just not interested enough in other academic subjects to get good grades, but they would make great software engineers.
A school that accepts a cross-section of students is bound to be more enriching than a school that only accepts academic superstars.
The US post-secondary education system is massively failing us: it's not producing even remotely enough programmers to meet the hiring needs of the technology industry.
Starting salaries for smart programmers from top schools are flirting with the $100,000 mark. Supply isn't even close to meeting demand.
One of the reasons the elite US colleges seem to turn out so few computer science majors every year is that they are only drawing from a narrow pool of mostly white and asian males.
College is not for everyone—many of the best programmers I know were just not interested enough in a general four year degree and went straight into jobs programming.
posts.key_insight: The best way to address the software engineering talent shortage and diversity gap is to open the pipeline earlier with unscreened public schools that welcome any interested student, not just academic high-performers.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky criticizes young startup founders who emulate Steve Jobs' abrasive personality traits, mistaking correlation for causation. He argues that Jobs' difficult temperament wasn't the source of his design genius, and that copying his worst qualities is misguided. The post is a brief, pointed observation about the cargo-cult mentality in Silicon Valley's startup culture.
key_points: Young startup founders are deliberately cultivating toxic personality traits in imitation of Steve Jobs
Emulating Jobs' worst qualities confuses correlation with causation (cum hoc ergo propter hoc)
Design genius and abrasive personality were coincidental traits in Jobs, not causally linked
money_quotes: The saddest thing about the Steve Jobs hagiography is all the young 'incubator twerps' strutting around Mountain View deliberately cultivating their worst personality traits because they imagine that's what made Steve Jobs a design genius.
Cum hoc ergo propter hoc, young twerp. Maybe try wearing a black turtleneck too.
posts.key_insight: Imitating a genius's worst personality traits is cargo-cult leadership — being difficult doesn't make you visionary.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky shares a brief link to UserVoice founder Richard White's detailed account of how UserVoice uses Trello throughout their development process. The post serves as a promotional mention for Trello, which was built by Spolsky's company Fog Creek Software. It contains no original analysis or argument, functioning purely as a signal boost for a third-party endorsement of Trello.
key_points: UserVoice adopted Trello extensively in their development workflow
The post directs readers to UserVoice's own blog for the detailed account
Spolsky uses his platform to amplify third-party validation of his product Trello
money_quotes: The folks over at UserVoice are using Trello quite extensively throughout their development process.
posts.key_insight: This is a minimal link post where Spolsky leverages his blog's readership to promote Trello adoption by highlighting a customer success story.

posts.summary: Spolsky draws an analogy between manufacturing inventory and software development, arguing that accumulated work-in-progress — feature backlogs, bug databases, and undeployed features — represents costly inventory that slows teams down. He identifies three key accumulation points where software 'inventory' piles up and provides concrete suggestions for constraining each. The post argues that excessive backlogs waste time on features that will never ship, bloated bug databases divert effort from the product itself, and undeployed features forfeit competitive advantage. He connects this thinking to the origin of Trello, which was designed to make inventory visible and discourage accumulation. The core message is that lean principles from manufacturing apply directly to software teams, and constraining inventory at every stage is essential for shipping faster.
key_points: Software development has inventory accumulation points analogous to manufacturing: feature backlogs, bug databases, and undeployed code all represent costly work-in-progress.
90% of feature backlog items will never be implemented, so time spent grooming, speccing, or discussing them is largely wasted.
Bug databases tend toward bankruptcy — thousands of entries that represent months of wasted effort on reports nobody will fix.
Undeployed features are the most expensive inventory because they could be earning money but instead sit idle while competitors ship.
Making inventory visible (as Trello does) helps teams identify bottlenecks and resist the urge to accumulate more work than they can ship.
money_quotes: 90% of the things in the feature backlog will never get implemented, ever.
The backlog, in fact, should be seen as a list of things you are not allowed to talk about or work on.

When I hear about product teams that regularly have 'backlog grooming' sessions, in which they carefully waste a tiny amount of time and mental energy every day or every week thinking about every single feature which will never be implemented, I want to poke my eyes out.
At some point you realize that you've put too much work into the bug database and not quite enough work into the product.
Don't worry, the severe bugs will come back.
This is one of the most expensive forms of inventory: unshipped feature inventory. It could be earning you money, but it's sitting on the shipping dock of your factory.
The 'cost' of code inventory is huge. It might add up to six or twelve months of work that is stuck in the assembly line and not yet in customers' hands.
posts.key_insight: Software teams should treat accumulated backlogs, bug databases, and undeployed features as costly inventory to be ruthlessly constrained, not carefully maintained.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces Kiln Harmony, a major new version of Fog Creek's distributed version control system that lets developers use either Git or Mercurial interchangeably on the same repository. He argues that DVCS is the most important developer tool advancement of the decade and explains why commercial products like Kiln and GitHub add value on top of raw open-source tools. He positions Kiln as serving the corporate market (code reviews, access control) while GitHub serves open source (social networking, forking). The key technical achievement is that Kiln stores repos in both formats with perfect bidirectional translation, letting teams migrate gradually or mix tools freely.
key_points: Distributed version control is the single biggest improvement in developers' lives in the past decade, even though it's harder to use than centralized systems
Commercial DVCS products succeed by wrapping raw open-source tools in enterprise features — they're Cadillacs to open source's dune buggies
Kiln targets corporate workflows (code review, permissions, search) while GitHub targets open-source sociology (forking, pull requests, social profiles)
Fog Creek originally chose Mercurial as '1% better' than Git, but GitHub's ecosystem growth made Git support essential
Kiln Harmony stores every repo in both Git and Mercurial formats with perfect 1:1 reversible translation, letting teams mix tools freely
money_quotes: DVCS is such an important improvement over the previous generation of centralized version control (Subversion, CVS, etc.) that it's a required upgrade, even though it's honestly a bit harder to use.
They're dune buggies. Powerful, yes, and sufficient for a college project, but as it turns out, people buy Cadillacs, not dune buggies, to drive around in.
I think of Kiln as the corporate Lincoln Town Car, while GitHub is kind of a VW Minibus.
Since internal corporate projects have a very different sociology than open source projects, Kiln is very different than GitHub.
We decided Mercurial was about 1% better than Git and that's the way we went.
GitHub has taken the world by storm, creating an ecosystem around Git that more than makes up for its minor failings.
We could have done it the lazy way: support both kinds of repositories and make you choose which one to use. But we are not lazy.
If Joe in Accounting refuses to move, it doesn't matter. He can keep using Mercurial.
posts.key_insight: Commercial developer tools succeed not by reinventing open-source technology but by wrapping it in the workflow-specific features that match how different communities — corporate teams versus open-source contributors — actually collaborate.

posts.summary: Spolsky argues that patent trolls operate a protection racket indistinguishable from organized crime. They buy vague software patents, send threatening letters to small developers, and profit when victims pay up rather than face million-dollar legal costs. He condemns the practice of paying trolls as complicit and calls on developers to band together through organizations like the EFF, Stack Exchange's Ask Patents, and the Application Developers Alliance to fight back and reform the patent system.
key_points: Patent troll lawsuits have more than tripled since 1999, making it one of the fastest-growing industries in the US
The troll business model exploits the asymmetry between cheap threat letters and million-dollar legal defense costs
Paying patent trolls is morally wrong because it funds their next round of attacks on other developers
Collective action through organizations like the EFF and Ask Patents is the civilized response to what amounts to organized crime
money_quotes: It's a great business model.

The recipients of the letter need to think that it's a threat to sue if they don't pay up, but in court, the letter has to look like an invitation to license some exciting new technology. In other words it has to be just on this side of extortion.
It is organized crime, plain and simple. It is an abuse of the legal system, an abuse of the patent system, and a moral affront.
In the face of organized crime, civilized people don't pay up.
When you pay up, you're funding the criminals, which makes you complicit in their next attacks.
If you pay the trolls, giving them money and comfort to go after the next round of indie developers, you're not just being 'pragmatic,' you have actually gone over to the dark side.
Life is a bit hard sometimes, and sometimes you have to step up and fight fights that you never signed up for.
Civilized people don't pay up. They band together, and fight, and eliminate the problem.
posts.key_insight: Patent trolling is a protection racket that thrives because individual developers rationally choose to pay rather than fight, making collective resistance the only viable solution.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces Trello Business Class, the first paid tier for Trello, after nearly two years of offering the product completely free. He explains the monetization strategy: build a free product that helps millions, then charge the 1% of users who derive the most value. The team identified medium-to-large organizations with many active users as the target paying audience and built enterprise features around security, permissions, and user management. Business Class launches at $25/month per organization, while core Trello remains free.
key_points: Trello grew to 1.5 million members in under two years with a completely free product, no freemium gate or trial period
The monetization strategy targets the top 1% of users who get the most value, rather than extracting money from all users
Enterprise features like security controls, bulk user management, and observer permissions are the natural paid tier because they only matter at organizational scale
Common features useful to anyone will always remain free; only features that serve large organizations will be monetized
money_quotes: What we really wanted to do was make a free product that helps millions of people, and then find some way to get paid by the 1% of those people who get the most value out of it.
The 1% are delighted to pay. They actually email us asking if there is some way they can pay us.
Some have commented that this business model might actually be just a few fries short of a Happy Meal.
Not "free trial," not "freemium," but just plain old free.
It's a classic example of a feature that is only useful when you're in that class of Trello users who get the most value out of it, so paying should be a no-brainer.
Anything that is a common feature, useful to anyone, will be free.
posts.key_insight: The best freemium strategy is to give the product away to everyone, then charge only the power users who derive so much value they're already asking how to pay you.

posts.summary: Spolsky celebrates the first successful patent rejection achieved through Ask Patents, a Stack Exchange site where developers submit prior art to block bad software patent applications. He explains three techniques companies use to get weak patents approved: obfuscating language, using non-standard terminology to evade prior art searches, and deliberately overreaching with broad claims. He then walks through his personal experience finding prior art for a Microsoft patent application in about 15 minutes, which led to the USPTO rejecting all twenty claims. He argues that software patents are of uniformly poor quality and remarkably easy to challenge, calling on developers and even competing companies to systematically use Ask Patents to reduce the flood of bad patents.
key_points: Companies exploit three techniques to push weak patents through: obfuscating language, using non-standard terminology to defeat keyword searches, and deliberately overreaching with broad claims hoping examiners won't catch everything.
The America Invents Act enabled public submission of prior art during patent examination, which led to the creation of Ask Patents on Stack Exchange.
Spolsky found prior art for a Microsoft patent application in roughly 15 minutes using a simple Google search, and the USPTO subsequently rejected all twenty claims based heavily on his submission.
Software patent applications are of uniformly poor quality, making them surprisingly easy to challenge with prior art that is often hiding in plain sight on the public internet.

Spolsky envisions competing tech giants using Ask Patents to systematically pick off each other's weak applications, creating a mutually assured destruction dynamic that would reduce bad patents overall.
money_quotes: Software developers don't actually invent very much. The number of actually novel, non-obvious inventions in the software industry that maybe, in some universe, deserve a government-granted monopoly is, perhaps, two.
The other 40,000-odd software patents issued every year are mostly garbage that any working programmer could 'invent' three times before breakfast.

Many patent owners, especially the troll types, don't really want you to avoid their patent. Often they actually want you to infringe their patent, and then build a big business that relies on that infringement, and only then do they want you to find out about the patent.
The harder the patent is to read, the more likely it will be inadvertently infringed.
What you're seeing is just a long shot lottery ticket, and you have to look deep into the narrower claims to see what they really expect to get.
Software patent applications are of uniformly poor quality. They are remarkably easy to find prior art for.
How cool would it be if Apple, Samsung, Oracle and Google got into a Mexican Standoff on Ask Patents?
posts.key_insight: Bad software patents thrive on obscurity and examiner overload, but crowdsourcing prior art through platforms like Ask Patents can defeat them with surprisingly little effort.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces that Trello has been spun out of Fog Creek Software into its own independent company, Trello Inc., with outside investment from Index Ventures and Spark Capital. He recounts Trello's origin as one of four internal prototype projects in 2011, growing from a two-person team to 4.6 million users over three years. While Fog Creek didn't need outside money, the investment accelerates growth plans for features, sales, and marketing. Spolsky emphasizes that all three companies he's involved with—Fog Creek, Trello, and Stack Exchange—share the same 'operating system' of treating developers well and putting people first.
key_points: Trello grew from a two-person prototype team to 4.6 million users and its own company in three years, validating Fog Creek's internal incubation approach of running multiple small teams on product ideas

Trello succeeded as a non-developer product because its visual board/card metaphor made it instantly understandable to civilians, and it spreads virally as people adopt it from one context (reading group) into another (workplace)
Spolsky chose investors who already had stakes in Stack Exchange to avoid conflicts of interest, and only took outside money after achieving traction and revenue to maintain founder control

All three companies (Fog Creek, Trello, Stack Exchange) run on the same 'operating system' centered on private offices, developer autonomy, and treating people humanely—principles Spolsky spent a decade advocating on his blog
money_quotes: We peeled off eight developers. The idea was that they would break into four teams. Two people each. Each team would work for a few months building a prototype or MVP for some product idea. Hopefully, at least one of those ideas would turn into something people wanted.
It's a gas that expands to fill all available space. Somebody finds out about Trello from their reading group and adopts it at work; pretty soon their whole company has hundreds of Trello boards.
We didn't really need the money; Fog Creek is profitable and could afford to fund Trello development to profitability.

We didn't take any outside investment until we already had traction and revenue, so we could choose the investors that we thought were the most entrepreneur-friendly, and we kept control of the company.

They are all running basically the same operating system, based on the original microprocessor architecture known as 'making a company where the best developers want to work,' or, in simpler terms, treating people well.
Y'all don't listen, so I'm just going to have to build company after company that runs my own wacky operating system, and eventually you'll catch on.
It's OK to put people first. You don't have to be a psychopath or work people to death or create heaps of messy code or work in noisy open offices.
posts.key_insight: The best way to grow successful products is to build companies that treat people well, take outside money only from a position of strength, and let great teams organically discover what users want.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces Stack Exchange's $40 million funding round led by Andreessen Horowitz. He recounts the company's origin story, from building Stack Overflow in 2008 to fix programmer Q&A, to expanding into 133 community-driven sites across diverse topics. The post emphasizes Stack Exchange's obsessive focus on serving programmers, including building Stack Overflow Careers as a developer-first job board. Spolsky frames the fundraise as necessary to continue investing in growth rather than slowing down to reach profitability, and stresses that investors are chosen to align with the company's mission.
key_points: Stack Exchange grew from a single programmer Q&A site to 133 communities and a top-50 US website by traffic, growing 25% annually
The company's core philosophy is fanatical, single-minded service to programmers, which drove every product decision from banning animated ads to prohibiting contingency recruiters
Stack Overflow Careers represents a major business bet, employing over half the company's 200+ staff, built as a developer-first job board rather than a recruiter tool
The $40m raise is positioned as a growth investment to build features faster rather than slowing hiring to reach profitability
Stack Exchange expanded beyond programming because programmers themselves demanded quality Q&A for their other interests, and the model proved transferable
money_quotes: We don't even allow animated ads, even though they are totally standard on every other site on the Internet, because it would be disrespectful to programmers to strain their delicate eyes with a dancing monkey.
And we're OBSESSED, so 98% is like, we might as well close this all down and go drive taxis in Las Vegas.
What works for developers should work for a lot of other people, too, as long as they're willing to think like developers, which is the best way to think.
Programmers often have crappy jobs, and their bosses often poke them with sharp sticks.
We banned the scourge of contingency recruiters even if they have big bank accounts and are just LINING UP at the Zion Gate trying to get into our city to feed on programmer meat.
Software is eating the world and writing software is just writing a script for how the future will play out.
We could just slow down our insane hiring pace and get profitable right now, but it would mean foregoing some of the investments that let us help more developers.

We only take outside money when we are 100% confident that the investors share our philosophy completely and after our lawyers have done a ruthless job of maintaining control so that it is literally impossible for anyone to mess up our vision.
posts.key_insight: Stack Exchange's growth strategy is built on the premise that fanatical, uncompromising service to a core community of programmers creates a platform that naturally expands to serve broader audiences.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces HyperDev, a new product from Fog Creek Software that provides an instant development environment for building full-stack web apps. The product eliminates all setup friction: visiting the URL immediately gives you a running server, an IDE, and a live URL with no accounts, git, hosting, or deployment required. Changes deploy instantly as you type, and real-time collaboration is built in without version control. Spolsky positions HyperDev not as a replacement for professional tools but as a way to remove barriers for simple projects, new programmers, and quick prototyping that would otherwise never get built due to administrative overhead.
key_points: HyperDev gives users a running server, IDE, and public URL instantly upon visiting the site with zero setup, accounts, or configuration required
All code changes deploy instantly as you type, eliminating the save-commit-push-deploy cycle entirely
Real-time collaboration works like Google Docs, with multiple developers editing the same codebase simultaneously without version control
The product targets simple projects and new programmers who are overwhelmed by modern development tooling, not professional teams who should continue using Git
Spolsky argues that useful code never gets written because developers dread the administrative overhead of setting up environments, repos, and servers
money_quotes: HyperDev is going to be the fastest way to bang out code and get it running on the internet.
Boom. Your new website is already running.
Literally every change you make is instantly saved, uploaded to the server, the server is restarted with the new code, and your browser is refreshed, all within half a second.
It's a magical kind of team programming where everything shows up instantly, like Trello, or Google Docs.
Professional software development teams will continue to use professional, robust tools like Git and that's great.
Useful little bits of code never get written because you dread the administration of setting up a new dev environment, source code repo, and server.
New programmers and students are overwhelmed by the complexity of distributed version control when they're still learning to write a while loop.
You can have all the 8's you want.
posts.key_insight: The friction of modern development tooling — accounts, version control, hosting, deployment — prevents useful software from being built, and eliminating that friction entirely can unlock creativity for simple projects and new programmers.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces that he has migrated his blog 'Joel on Software' to WordPress. The entire post consists of a single sentence acknowledging the platform change and warning readers about potential bugs. It's a minimal, utilitarian announcement with no substantive discussion.
key_points: Joel migrated his blog to WordPress
He acknowledges the migration may have introduced bugs
money_quotes: I've moved to WordPress. There may be some bugs!
posts.key_insight: A brief housekeeping announcement about a platform migration with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky explains why software companies need to own all intellectual property created by their employees, including side projects. He walks through the legal mechanics of copyright assignment and 'work for hire' agreements, explaining why the default assumption that 'work done at home is mine' is dangerously wrong. He discusses how state laws like California's provide some protection for employees working on unrelated projects on their own time and equipment, but notes the 'related to employer's line of work' clause is ambiguous enough to create a chilling effect. The practical conclusion is that all software companies will try to own everything employees create, and the real variable is how aggressively management chooses to enforce those rights.
key_points: Under US default copyright law, contractors retain copyright on code they write even when paid for it — which is why all software companies require invention assignment agreements
Companies need blanket IP assignment not just for control but for due diligence in financing, mergers, and acquisitions — one unsigned agreement from a past employee can hold up a deal

California and some other states override contracts to protect employee side projects done on their own time, with their own equipment, unrelated to the employer's work — but 'unrelated' is dangerously ambiguous
The ambiguity in 'related to employer's line of work' creates a chilling effect that effectively discourages side projects, which is arguably the employer's real goal
The practical difference between companies isn't in the contracts (which are all similar) but in management's stance on enforcement
money_quotes: Most developers think that the work they do at work belongs to their employer, but anything they work on at home or on their own time is theirs. This is wrong enough to be dangerous.
I don't just want to buy your 9:00-5:00 inventions. I want them all, and I'm going to pay you a nice salary to get them all.

That ONE GUY from 1998 who didn't sign the agreement is going to be a real jerk about signing it now, because he knows that he's personally holding up a $350,000,000 acquisition and he can demand a lot of money to sign.
If my employer is Google, they do everything. They made a goddamn HOT AIR BALLOON with an internet router in it once. Are hot air balloons related?
This ambiguity is meant to create enough of a chilling effect on the employee working in their spare time that for all intents and purposes it achieves the effect that the employer wants.

The employee doesn't bother doing any side projects that might turn into a business some day, and the employer gets a nice, refreshed employee coming to work in the morning after spending the previous evening watching TV.
The only way to gain independence is to be independent.

Being an employee of a high tech company whose product is intellectual means that you have decided that you want to sell your intellectual output, and maybe that's OK, and maybe it's not, but it's a free choice.
posts.key_insight: Software companies universally claim ownership of all employee inventions not because they're greedy but because IP assignment is a structural requirement for investment and acquisition, and the real question for developers isn't what the contract says but how aggressively management will enforce it.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky recounts the technological history of his blog, from Dave Winer's Manila platform in 1999 through his own CityDesk product to its final migration to WordPress. CityDesk was built on the bet that people would prefer desktop WYSIWYG blogging on Windows, which proved wrong as web-based platforms won out. The VB 6.0 codebase gradually stopped working on modern Windows, forcing Joel to maintain a Windows XP virtual machine just to publish. After years of persuasion from WordPress co-founder Matt Mullenweg, Joel finally migrated the site, crediting a team of developers who preserved 16 years of content and over 1000 links.
key_points: CityDesk was built on the assumption that desktop WYSIWYG editing would beat web-based blogging—a bet that proved monumentally wrong
Technology choices compound over time: VB 6.0 and Microsoft Jet created an increasingly unmaintainable system that eventually required a Windows XP VM to run
The early blogging ecosystem was DIY—cool kids wrote their own platforms, ran servers in closets, and 512MB of RAM was enough to survive Slashdot
Migrating 16 years of web content while preserving links, formatting, and structure is a monumental undertaking requiring significant human effort
money_quotes: In what turned out to be a monumentally wrong bet, I thought that people would want to blog on Windows, with all the slick WYSIWYG editing goodness that wasn't yet available in early versions of HTML.
The first time Slashdot linked to me it melted down. We upgraded the memory from 256M to 512M and it recovered.
In those days all the cool kids wrote their own blogging platforms.

So until recently, I had a virtual machine set up with Windows XP running in there, and a copy of CityDesk. That virtual machine runs on a modern Windows box which I don't use for any other purpose, so it lives under a desk in a cave somewhere and I have to remote desktop into it.
Matt Mullenweg over at WordPress has been trying to get me to move Joel on Software over to WordPress for so long it's not even funny.
posts.key_insight: Technology bets that seem reasonable at the time—desktop over web, VB 6.0 over newer platforms—can become compounding liabilities that eventually force painful migrations.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky explains why he quit Twitter and Facebook, arguing that both platforms inadvertently evolved into engines of outrage and addiction. Twitter's 140-character limit strips nuance from communication, turning complex thoughts into inflammatory bumper stickers that spark flame wars. Facebook's machine learning algorithm, optimized purely for engagement, discovered that intermittent reinforcement—burying useful friend updates among garbage content—keeps users compulsively returning. Spolsky argues these selfish algorithms are destroying civil society by creating partisan echo chambers and replacing meaningful action with slacktivism. He concludes that software designers bear responsibility for the social consequences of their design decisions, and notes Facebook's announced pivot toward meaningful connections as a sign the company is finally accepting that responsibility.
key_points: Twitter's character limit strips nuance from communication, accidentally creating a 'subtlety-free indignation machine' that thrives through natural selection among social apps
Facebook's ML-driven feed uses intermittent reinforcement—the same principle dog trainers use—to keep users addicted by burying occasional valuable updates among garbage content
Both platforms' algorithms optimized solely for engagement have divided users into partisan echo chambers and replaced meaningful civic action with performative slacktivism
Software designers bear direct responsibility for the social consequences of their design decisions because they are literally creating the future
Facebook's announced pivot to sacrifice engagement metrics for meaningful connection signals the company finally understanding its social responsibility
money_quotes: We've taken a medium, text, which is already bad at conveying emotion and sentiment and high-bandwidth nuance, and made it even worse, and the net result is a lot of outrage and indignation.

In a world where the tech industry was cranking out millions of dumb little social applications, this one happens to limit messages to 140 characters and that happens to create, unintentionally, a subtlety-free indignation machine, which is addictive as heck.
Facebook mixed a new, super-potent active ingredient into their feed called Machine Learning.
The only thing the ML algorithm was told to care about was addiction, or, as they called it, engagement.

9 out of 10 things in my feed are complete garbage—last week's newspaper lining the birdcage with the droppings already on it—but then once every two weeks I find out my niece is engaged or my best friend got a great new job.
The lesson here is that when you design software, you create the future.

As software developers and designers, we have a responsibility to the world to think these things through carefully and design software that makes the world better, or, at least, no worse than it started out.
posts.key_insight: Social platforms' design decisions—Twitter's character limits and Facebook's engagement-maximizing algorithms—accidentally created addiction machines that destroy nuanced discourse, and software designers must accept responsibility for these societal consequences.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky reflects on Stack Overflow's reputation system as a 'dusting of gamification' — lightweight game mechanics that drive good behavior without being the core product. He traces the lineage from Slashdot's karma to Reddit's karma to Stack Overflow's reputation points, explaining how simple numerical scores signal community norms and reward contributors. He acknowledges the dark side: downvotes feel punishing, and the system can feel unwelcoming, particularly to underrepresented groups. He concludes that gamification is a weak force — useful for nudging people toward things they already want to do, but incapable of driving behavior on its own.
key_points: Stack Overflow's reputation system was intentionally minimal — a 'dusting' of gamification rather than a core game mechanic, primarily rewarding helpful answers with upvotes worth 10 points while making downvotes cost the voter 1 point to discourage abuse.
The lineage of reputation systems runs from Slashdot (functional anti-spam karma) to Reddit (visible but meaningless karma) to Stack Overflow (reputation that signals community norms and standards).
The psychological power of reputation comes from a basic human need to know you've made a difference and been appreciated for your contribution.
Downvotes create a significant negative emotional impact that outweighs the positive feeling of upvotes, driving people away — disproportionately affecting underrepresented groups.
Gamification is a weak force that can nudge existing motivation but cannot manufacture it — nobody learns French for Duolingo points, but streaks keep already-motivated learners opening the app.
money_quotes: What reputation and karma do is send a message that this is a community with norms, it's not just a place to type words onto the internet.
We don't really exist for the purpose of letting you exercise your freedom of speech. You can get your freedom of speech somewhere else. Our goal is to get the best answers to questions.
To be honest, it was initially surprising to me that you could just print a number after people's handles and they would feel rewarded. Look at me! Look at my four digit number!
You toil away in the hot kitchen all day and when you serve dinner it's nice to hear a compliment or two.
Stack Overflow's reputation system serves to recognize that you're a human being and we are super thankful for your contribution.
It's very nice to get an upvote, but getting a downvote feels like a slap in the face.

Nobody's going to learn French just to get the Duolingo points. But if you are learning French, and you are using Duolingo, you might make an effort to open the app every day just to keep your streak going.
posts.key_insight: Gamification is most effective as a light touch that reinforces existing motivation and signals community norms, but it's too weak a force to drive behavior on its own and can actively harm inclusivity when it punishes rather than rewards.

posts.summary: Spolsky reflects on Stack Overflow's tension between maintaining quality standards and welcoming newcomers to programming. He draws parallels between debugging techniques like rubber duck debugging and the site's question-asking requirements, arguing that these rules serve a purpose even when they frustrate beginners. Using the history of comp.lang.c as a cautionary tale of a community that drove away newcomers by catering only to experts, he explains why Stack Overflow was designed to welcome beginner questions. However, he acknowledges that the site's rules and community snarkiness create real obstacles for new developers, particularly those from underrepresented groups, and commits to improving the experience.
key_points: Stack Overflow's question-asking rules mirror proven debugging techniques like rubber duck debugging and divide-and-conquer, helping askers solve their own problems before posting
The comp.lang.c newsgroup serves as a cautionary tale: communities that only cater to experts eventually languish and die from irrelevance
Stack Overflow was intentionally designed to welcome beginners, with the understanding that advanced users might get bored and leave, which was considered acceptable
Community rudeness and snark toward newcomers is a bigger problem than the formal rules, actively driving away potential future contributors and harming diversity in tech
The site's ultimate value proposition is being a trusted universal resource for developers, which requires balancing openness with quality standards
money_quotes: Every community has lots of rules and when you join the community they either seem strange and delightful or, if you're just desperately trying to get some code to work, they are strange and maddening.
Our general rule is that if the correct length of an answer is a whole book you are asking too much.
The newsgroup languished because it catered only to the few people that had been there for a decade.
Stack Overflow doesn't have to be a lifetime commitment. You're welcome to get bored and move on.

All the flak that Stack Overflow throws in the face of newbies trying to become developers is actively harmful to people, to society, and to Stack Overflow itself, by driving away potential future contributors.
Programming is hard enough; we should see our mission as making it easier.
The average programmer, in the world, has been helped by Stack Overflow 340 times.
posts.key_insight: Community gatekeeping rules serve necessary quality purposes, but when combined with hostile enforcement they drive away the newcomers a community needs to survive and remain relevant.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces Stack Overflow for Teams, a paid service that creates private Q&A spaces on Stack Overflow for companies and organizations. He argues that development teams have long struggled to capture institutional knowledge in searchable, written form. He dismisses wikis as feeling like homework that produces stale content, and chat archives as capturing conversations rather than knowledge. The Q&A format works better because questions are answered for someone who needs help immediately, and the results are searchable and structured. The product integrates directly into stackoverflow.com with team-specific tags, notifications, and clear public/private separation.
key_points: Every development team struggles to get institutional knowledge out of people's heads and into written, searchable form
Wikis fail because writing documentation speculatively feels like homework, doesn't get updated, and produces content of questionable usefulness
Chat archives capture conversations-in-amber rather than actual knowledge, making them poor repositories for institutional knowledge
Q&A works because you answer questions that help someone immediately rather than writing documentation hoping it might help someone someday
The same dynamics that made Stack Overflow beat discussion forums on the public internet apply to proprietary team knowledge
money_quotes: All the questions are already answered!
Every development team since the beginning of time has been trying to figure out how to get institutional knowledge out of people's heads and into written, searchable form where everyone can find it.
It just feels like a bunch of homework to write a bunch of wiki documentation about your code when you don't know if it will ever help anyone.
Even if that works, all you really find is the history of some conversation people had. It might have clues but it's not knowledge.
Unlike wikis, you don't write documentation in the hopes that one day it might help someone. You answer questions that are going to help someone immediately.
Unlike chatrooms, searching actually works. It finds you a question and its answers, not a conversation-captured-in-amber.
You can stop asking your teammates questions in email (where they help nobody else) or in chatrooms (where they are impossible to find) and start building your own private knowledge base.
posts.key_insight: Q&A systems beat wikis and chat for capturing institutional knowledge because they create documentation at the exact moment someone needs it, producing structured, searchable answers rather than speculative docs or conversation archives.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces he is stepping down as CEO of Stack Overflow and moving to Chairman of the Board. He reflects on the platform's growth from a fledgling site where only a third of conference attendees had heard of it to one of the top 50 websites in the world with 100 million monthly visitors. He highlights the company's business success, including profitability, $70m in revenue, and 200% annual growth in SaaS products. Spolsky argues that Stack Overflow's next chapter requires a different type of leader focused on making the community more diverse, inclusive, and welcoming to newcomers. He emphasizes that as the developer population grows rapidly, Stack Overflow must remove obstacles for new programmers rather than driving them away with rudeness and rigid rules.
key_points: Stack Overflow grew from a niche developer tool to one of the top 50 websites globally with 100 million monthly visitors, achieving profitability with $70m in revenue.
The SaaS products (Teams and Enterprise) were growing at 200% annually, signaling a shift toward enterprise business alongside the free community platform.
Spolsky believes Stack Overflow's culture of rudeness and strict rules actively harms newcomers and drives away potential contributors, which he sees as the central challenge for the next CEO.
The number of software developers grew 64% in a decade and demand still outstrips supply, making it critical for Stack Overflow to welcome new developers rather than gatekeep.
Spolsky frames the CEO search as specifically seeking candidates from under-represented groups who are deeply committed to diversity and inclusion.
money_quotes: It's like asking, 'Does anyone use gravity? Raise your hand if you use gravity.'
Oh and—hey!—we do not make you sign up or pay to see the answers.
It's going to take a different type of leader to get us through that work.

All the flak that Stack Overflow throws in the face of newbies trying to become developers is actively harmful to people, to society, and to Stack Overflow itself, by driving away potential future contributors.
Programming is hard enough; we should see our mission as making it easier.
Software and the internet are not just tools; they are shaping the future of society.
There's always someone in the community who can answer the questions I can't.
posts.key_insight: Spolsky frames his departure as recognition that Stack Overflow's next challenge is not technical or commercial growth but cultural transformation—making the platform welcoming to the rapidly expanding and diversifying population of new developers.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces Prashanth Chandrasekar as Stack Overflow's new CEO, effective October 1st, 2019. The search process evaluated nearly 200 candidates before selecting Chandrasekar, who previously built a new business unit at Rackspace. Spolsky emphasizes that Stack Overflow needs experienced leadership to accelerate its enterprise business, particularly Stack Overflow Teams. He notes the company's goals around diversity, inclusivity, and expanding into proprietary knowledge management for organizations. Spolsky will remain as Chairman of the Board.
key_points: Stack Overflow selected Prashanth Chandrasekar from nearly 200 candidates to serve as the company's new CEO
Chandrasekar's key qualification was building Rackspace's Global Managed Public Clouds Business from scratch, successfully pivoting the company's business model
Stack Overflow is positioning itself as a hyper-growth enterprise software company, particularly through its Teams product
The company's strategic priorities include making Stack Overflow more diverse and welcoming, and expanding into proprietary organizational knowledge management
money_quotes: We were looking for that rare combination of someone who could foster the community while accelerating the growth of our businesses, especially Teams, where we are starting to close many huge deals and becoming a hyper-growth enterprise software company very quickly.
This is not something I'm particularly good at, and I thought it was time to bring on more experienced leadership.
It speaks to how well respected a company Stack Overflow is that we found ourselves in the rare position of having plenty of highly qualified executives who were excited about the opportunity.
Under his leadership, Rackspace successfully pivoted from a leading managed hosting company to a cloud services company.
We want to make it possible for knowledge workers everywhere to use Stack Overflow to get answers to the proprietary questions that are specific to their organizations and teams.
posts.key_insight: Spolsky recognized that scaling Stack Overflow's enterprise business required a different kind of leadership than what a founder-CEO could provide, leading him to hand off the CEO role while staying involved as Chairman.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky provides an update on his life after stepping back as CEO of Stack Overflow, describing his current role as chairman of three companies. He discusses the transition to new CEO Prashanth Chandrasekar, the evolution of Fog Creek into Glitch under Anil Dash, and his newest venture HASH, an open-source agent-based simulation platform. He frames this period as a sabbatical rather than retirement, noting he's also spending free time on LED electronics hobby projects.
key_points: Spolsky views the best outcome of his CEO transition as the new CEO proving how much better the company can be run, showing unusual self-awareness about founder limitations
Glitch targets the 'quiet majority' of developers who don't need complex tooling like git branches — they just want to write code and have it run
HASH builds agent-based simulations that work where closed-form models and AI/ML approaches fail, especially with limited training data
Spolsky critiques the AI hype, arguing that many startups train ML models on too little data and just reproduce flawed human decision-making
Despite chairing three companies, he's spending free days on hardware hobby projects with programmable LEDs
money_quotes: It's really satisfying to realize that the best possible outcome for me is if he proves what a bad CEO I was by doing a much better job running the company.

In every era there has to be some kind of simplified programming environment for the quiet majority of developers who don't need fancy administration features for their code, like git branches or multistep deployment processes; they just want to write code and have it run.

There are a lot of startups out there trying to train machine learning models with way too little data. Sometimes the models they create just reproduce the bad decision making of the humans they are trained on.
In many cases a model with simulated agents running a white box algorithm is going to be superior.
This kind of modeling is incredibly computationally intensive, but it works even when you don't have a closed-form formula for how individual agents' behavior affects overall outcomes.
Rest assured that I am probably not going to burn down the apartment because I am very careful with the soldering iron almost every time.
posts.key_insight: A founder's greatest success can be stepping aside and watching someone else run the company better, while redirecting energy toward new problems — from agent-based simulation platforms to hobby electronics.

posts.summary: Joel introduces HASH, a free online platform for agent-based simulation modeling. He explains the concept by contrasting simple mathematical relationships with complex systems where inputs and outputs aren't linearly related. Using a warehouse staffing example, he shows how adding a fifth employee can produce zero additional throughput due to workers getting in each other's way. He argues that even when you can't model a system mathematically, you can simulate individual agent behaviors to understand emergent outcomes.
key_points: Simple systems can be understood with basic math, but complex systems with emergent behavior cannot
Agent-based simulation lets you model individual behaviors and observe system-level outcomes
Complex systems often have non-linear relationships between inputs and outputs, like diminishing returns from adding workers
HASH provides a free platform for building these kinds of simulations
money_quotes: Sometimes you're working on something that's just too complicated for that, and you can't even begin to guess how the inputs affect the outputs.
When you hit five employees, they get in each others' way so much that the fifth employee effectively does no additional work.
You may not understand the relationship between the number of employees and the throughput of the warehouse, but you definitely know what everybody is doing.
If you can imagine writing a little bit of JavaScript code to simulate the behavior of each of your workers, you can run a simulation and see what actually happens.
You can tweak the parameters and the rules the employees follow to see how it would help, and you can really gain some traction understanding, and then solving, very complex problems.
posts.key_insight: When systems are too complex for mathematical modeling, simulating individual agent behaviors lets you understand and optimize emergent outcomes.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces that Stack Overflow is being acquired by Prosus, a holding company, while reflecting on the slow pace of programming progress over decades. He argues that despite new frameworks and tools, building web applications remains roughly as difficult as it always was, with the notable exception of Stack Overflow itself, which genuinely changed how developers learn and get help. He reassures the community that Stack Overflow will continue to operate independently with the same team and mission. He also briefly introduces his new venture, HASH, which aims to make web data more structured and programmatically accessible.
key_points: Programming tools evolve slowly, and despite decades of progress, building CRUD web apps takes roughly the same effort it always has
Tool makers love adding features but hate removing them, leading to ever-increasing complexity without proportional productivity gains
Stack Overflow was a rare exception that genuinely changed programming overnight by transforming how developers learn and help each other
The acquisition by Prosus is structured to keep Stack Overflow operating independently with the same team, plan, and business practices
Spolsky's new venture HASH aims to solve the problem of unstructured web data that requires painful scraping before it can be used programmatically
money_quotes: COM was like Gödels Theorem: it seemed important, and you could understand it all long enough to pass an exam, but ultimately it is mostly just a demonstration of how far human intelligence can be made to stretch under extreme duress.
If there is one thing we have learned, it's that the things that make it easier on your brain are the things that matter.

I found that it took approximately the same amount of work to make a CRUD web app as it always has, and that there were some things that were, shockingly, still just as randomly difficult as they were in VBScript twenty years ago.
The biggest problem is that developers of programming tools love to add things and hate to take things away.
Six-to-eight weeks before that, Stack Overflow was only an idea. Six-to-eight weeks after that, it was a standard part of every developer's toolkit.
The company grew faster than any individual's skills at managing companies, especially mine.
Stack Overflow will continue to operate independently, with the exact same team in place that has been operating it, according to the exact same plan and the exact same business practices.
posts.key_insight: Despite decades of programming tool evolution, genuine productivity leaps are extraordinarily rare — Stack Overflow was one, and preserving its independence and mission through the Prosus acquisition matters more than the financial event itself.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky announces the Block Protocol, an open standard for making web content blocks interchangeable across different editors, blogging tools, and content management systems. He observes that while many web editors have converged on the concept of insertable blocks (paragraphs, tables, kanban boards, etc.), each platform implements them from scratch with no interoperability. The Block Protocol aims to let any block work in any embedding application, so developers can write a block once and have it usable everywhere. He emphasizes the protocol is open, free, and non-proprietary, and highlights that blocks can carry typed, structured data making web content machine-readable without scraping.
key_points: Web editors have converged on the block metaphor (even standardizing on the / key) but every implementation is proprietary and incompatible
The Block Protocol is an open, free standard that lets any block be embedded in any conforming application, eliminating redundant reimplementation
Blocks can carry typed schemas, making structured data on the web machine-readable without screen scraping or manual data exports
The project is being released very early (v0.1) to attract community feedback and contributions before the protocol matures
money_quotes: We have seem to have standardized on one thing: the / key to insert a new block. Everything else, though, is completely proprietary and non-standard.
Until now, every app that wants blocks has to implement them from scratch. Want a calendar block? Some kind of fancy Kanban board? Something to embed image galleries? Code it up yourself, buddy.
As a result of the non-standardization of blocks, our end-users suffer.
I thought, wouldn't it be cool if blocks were interchangeable and reusable across the web?
Have you ever seen one of those websites where there's a link to 'download the data set in .XLS format'? Yeah, say goodbye to that.
Want to create a block that shows the Great Circle routing for a flight between two airports? Write the code for the block once and it can be embedded anywhere.
posts.key_insight: Web editors have all converged on blocks as a UI paradigm but with zero interoperability, and an open protocol could eliminate redundant work while making structured web content machine-readable by default.

posts.summary: Joel Spolsky argues that the Semantic Web vision from 1999 has failed because adding structured markup to web content is too much work for publishers. He proposes the Block Protocol as a solution: an open protocol that lets developers create reusable, structured content blocks (like 'book' or 'address') that any web editor can embed, automatically generating semantic markup behind the scenes. The key insight is that structured data will only become widespread if adding it is easier than not adding it. He announces a WordPress plugin that brings Block Protocol blocks to the 43% of the web powered by WordPress, making any block immediately widely usable. The post frames this as the practical first step toward finally delivering on the Semantic Web's original promise.
key_points: The Semantic Web failed not because the idea was wrong, but because adding semantic markup is extra homework that publishers skip when there's no immediate payoff.
People will only add semantic markup if doing so is easier than not doing it — the cost must be zero or negative.

The Block Protocol is an open protocol allowing any developer to create structured content blocks usable across any conforming web editor, eliminating the need for each platform to build every block type independently.
A WordPress plugin makes Block Protocol blocks immediately available to 43% of the web, providing critical initial reach without requiring mass coordination.
The protocol is designed to be 100% free, open, and public, with no impediment to commercial or open-source block development.
money_quotes: People will only add semantic markup to their web pages if doing so is easier than not.
The cost of adding semantic markup has to be zero or negative, or this whole project is not going anywhere.
Then you stir in some CSS, which adds some pretty decorations to the structure, saying things like: make those paragraphs have tiny gray sans-serif text! And then people think you are hip.
That was 1999, and not much progress has been made and there is very little of this semantic markup in the wild.
Human progress depends on getting more and more information in formats that are readily accessible, both by regular humans, their dumb A.I. li'l sibs, and your more traditional computer programs.
So I guess I gotta wait around for someone at WordPress to develop all the blocks I want to use. And then someone at Notion, and then someone at Trello, and then someone at Mailchimp.
If you build a block for the Block Protocol, it'll be widely usable right away.
posts.key_insight: Structured semantic data will only succeed on the web when adding it becomes easier than not adding it, which requires an open block protocol that makes rich content insertion effortless for authors while generating markup automatically.
